| Applications | Service | Mobile apps, dashboards, analytics |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of IoT Infrastructure

The KTU 2024 syllabus and standard IoT literature (as per ISO/IEC 30141:2018 and oneM2M architecture) define a **5-layer reference architecture**. Below is the exhaustive breakdown:

### Layer 1 — Perception / Device Layer

This is the **physical layer** where the IoT interacts with the real world.

**Components:**
- **Sensors**: Convert physical phenomena → electrical signals
  - *Temperature sensors* (LM35, DHT22)
  - *Humidity sensors* (DHT22, SHT31)
  - *Proximity / PIR sensors* (HC-SR501)
  - *Gas / Chemical sensors* (MQ-135)
- **Actuators**: Convert electrical signals → physical action
  - *Relays, Servo motors, Solenoids*
- **Embedded controllers**: ESP32, Arduino Uno, Raspberry Pi, NodeMCU
- **RFID / NFC tags**: Passive identification

**Key operating characteristics:**
- Sampling rate: typically **1 Hz to 100 kHz**
- Resolution: typically **8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit, 24-bit ADC**
- Power consumption: typically **1 mW to 500 mW**

### Layer 2 — Network / Connectivity Layer

This is the **communication backbone** that transmits data between the perception layer and processing layer.

**Sub-classification of IoT networks:**

$$
\text{IoT Networks} \begin{cases} \text{Short-range} \begin{cases} \text{Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)} \\ \text{ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)} \\ \text{Near Field Communication (NFC)} \\ \text{Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)} \\ \text{RFID} \end{cases} \\[8pt] \text{Medium-range} \begin{cases} \text{LoRaWAN} \\ \text{Sigfox} \\ \text{NB-IoT} \\ \text{LTE-M} \end{cases} \\[8pt] \text{Long-range} \begin{cases} \text{5G mMTC} \\ \text{Satellite IoT} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

**Why this classification?** Because each protocol has a different **range-power-bandwidth trade-off**.

### Layer 3 — Processing / Middleware Layer

This layer handles **data ingestion, storage, processing, and analytics**. It is typically split into:

$$
\text{Processing Layer} \begin{cases} \text{Edge Computing} \rightarrow \text{Process data near the source} \\ \text{Fog Computing} \rightarrow \text{Layered processing between edge and cloud} \\ \text{Cloud Computing} \rightarrow \text{Centralized processing and storage} \end{cases}
$$

### Layer 4 — Application / Service Layer

Delivers the **business value** of IoT:
- Smart Agriculture
- Smart Healthcare
- Smart Cities
- Industrial IoT (IIoT)
- Smart Homes

### Layer 5 — Business Layer

Includes **business models, monetization strategies, privacy, and governance**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| Shannon-Hartley Theorem | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Maximum data rate over a noisy channel | bits/sec (bps) |
| Path Loss (Free Space) | $L_{\text{fs}} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| Received Power (Friis) | $P_{r} = P_{t}\,G_{t}\,G_{r}\,\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Power received at distance d | Watts (W) |
| Battery Life | $T_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{batt}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$ | Approximate battery endurance | Hours (h) |
| Duty Cycle | $\delta = \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}$ | Fraction of time device is active | Dimensionless |
| Network Capacity | $C_{\text{net}} = N_{\text{nodes}} \times R_{\text{per\,node}}$ | Aggregate system throughput | bps |
| Latency | $\tau = t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{trans}} + t_{\text{queue}}$ | End-to-end delay | seconds (s) |
| Propagation Delay | $t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Time for signal to traverse distance d | seconds (s) |
| Transmission Delay | $t_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Time to push L bits at rate R | seconds (s) |
| Energy per Bit | $E_{b} = \dfrac{P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{trans}}}{L}$ | Energy spent transmitting one bit | Joules/bit (J/bit) |
| Range (LoRa, max) | $d_{\max} \approx 10\,\text{km (rural)}, 3\,\text{km (urban)}$ | Typical LoRa coverage | km |
| Wi-Fi Range (typical) | $d_{\max} \approx 100\,\text{m (indoor)}, 300\,\text{m (outdoor)}$ | Wi-Fi coverage | m |
| BLE Range (typical) | $d_{\max} \approx 10\,\text{m to 100\,\text{m}}$ | BLE coverage | m |
| Data Rate Comparison | BLE = 2 Mbps, Wi-Fi = up to 9.6 Gbps, LoRa = 0.3 to 50 kbps | — | — |
| Frequency Band (Sub-GHz) | $f \in [169\,\text{MHz}, 915\,\text{MHz}]$ | Common ISM bands for IoT | MHz |
| Frequency Band (2.4 GHz) | $f = 2.4\,\text{GHz to 2.485\,\text{GHz}$ | Global ISM band | GHz |
| Power Consumption (Active) | $I_{\text{act}} \approx 100\,\text{mA to 500\,\text{mA}}$ | MCU active current | mA |
| Power Consumption (Sleep) | $I_{\text{sleep}} \approx 10\,\mu\text{A to 100\,\mu\text{A}}$ | MCU sleep current | µA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe Symbol Notice**: For absolute value $\vert x \vert$ inside formulas above, the LaTeX command `\vert` is used instead of the literal `|` character to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding IoT infrastructure is essential for:

- **Smart Agriculture** (Soil moisture sensors + LoRaWAN gateways → cloud analytics)
- **Healthcare Monitoring** (Wearable BLE sensors → smartphone → hospital server)
- **Industrial Automation** (Modbus/OPC-UA over Ethernet → SCADA → MES)
- **Smart Cities** (Air quality sensors + NB-IoT → city dashboard)
- **Logistics** (RFID + GPS + 5G → supply chain visibility)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** When designing an IoT system, the **range-power-bandwidth** triangle is the most critical design constraint. KTU exam problems frequently ask you to compare protocols on these three axes.

## 2.4 Communication Protocol Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

| Protocol | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Power | Topology |
|----------|-----------|-------|-----------|-------|----------|
| BLE 5.0 | 2.4 GHz | 100 m | 2 Mbps | Low | Star |
| ZigBee | 2.4 GHz | 100 m | 250 kbps | Low | Mesh |
| Wi-Fi (802.11n) | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 100 m | 600 Mbps | High | Star |
| LoRaWAN | 868 / 915 MHz | 10 km | 0.3 to 50 kbps | Very Low | Star |
| NB-IoT | LTE bands | 15 km | 26 kbps | Low | Star |
| Sigfox | 868 / 902 MHz | 40 km | 100 bps | Very Low | Star |
| NFC | 13.56 MHz | 10 cm | 424 kbps | Very Low | P2P |
| RFID (passive) | 860 to 960 MHz | 10 m | 100 kbps | Passive | Reader-Tag |

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Layered Architecture

- **Why layering?** It enforces **separation of concerns**, allowing engineers to upgrade one layer without redesigning the entire system.
- **Why edge computing?** To reduce **latency** (mission-critical applications like autonomous vehicles) and **bandwidth cost** (avoiding 24/7 cloud uploads).
- **Why mesh topology?** To provide **redundancy** — if one node fails, packets route around it.
- **Why multiple protocols?** Because no single protocol excels at *all three* of: long range, high data rate, low power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity

This is the **fundamental theoretical limit** for IoT communication — KTU examiners love asking this.

**Given:** A wireless channel with bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ (dimensionless ratio).

**To derive:** Maximum achievable data rate $C$.

**Step 1:** The original Shannon theorem (1948) states that a channel's maximum information transfer rate depends on the bandwidth and the SNR through the logarithm of base 2, because information is measured in **bits**.

**Step 2:** Start from Nyquist's noiseless channel capacity for $M$ discrete signal levels:

$$
C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_{2}(M)
$$

where $M$ is the number of distinguishable signal levels per symbol.

**Step 3:** In a noisy channel, the effective number of distinguishable levels is reduced. The relation between $M$ and SNR is:

$$
M = 1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{S}{N}}
$$

For high SNR, this approximates to:

$$
M \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{S}{N}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into Nyquist's formula:

$$
C = 2B \log_{2}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{N}}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the logarithm power rule $\log(a^{b}) = b \log(a)$:

$$
C = 2B \times \dfrac{1}{2}\log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the coefficient $2 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1$:

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)
$$

This is the **Shannon-Hartley Theorem**.

**Step 7 (Engineering interpretation):** Doubling the bandwidth doubles the capacity linearly, while doubling the SNR increases capacity only **logarithmically** — so for IoT, increasing bandwidth is often more effective than boosting power.

## 3.2 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

**Given:** A transmitter and receiver separated by distance $d$ (km), operating at frequency $f$ (MHz).

**Step 1:** Friis transmission equation in linear scale:

$$
\dfrac{P_{r}}{P_{t}} = G_{t} G_{r}\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to decibels (dB), using $10\log_{10}$:

$$
10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{r}}{P_{t}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(G_{t}) + 10\log_{10}(G_{r}) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** For isotropic antennas ($G_{t} = G_{r} = 1$):

$$
\text{FSPL} = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)
$$

The negative sign arises because path loss represents signal attenuation.

**Step 4:** Substitute $\lambda = c / f$ where $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Convert units: $d$ in kilometers, $f$ in MHz. Evaluate the constant:

$$
20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right) = -147.55 \text{ dB}
$$

After unit conversion ($d$ in km, $f$ in MHz), the constant becomes **32.44**, yielding the well-known formula:

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44}
$$

**Step 6 (KTU-style numerical example):**
Given: $d = 2$ km, $f = 2400$ MHz (Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz).
Find the free-space path loss.

**Substitute:**

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(2) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44
$$

**Evaluate term by term:**

$$
20\log_{10}(2) = 20 \times 0.30103 = 6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.38021 = 67.60 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 6.02 + 67.60 + 32.44 = 106.06 \text{ dB}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL} \approx 106.06 \text{ dB}}
$$

This means a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi signal loses about **106 dB** of power over 2 km in free space — illustrating why Wi-Fi is unusable beyond ~100 m.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Power Consumption of an IoT Node

**Problem:** A sensor node uses an ESP32 in active mode for $T_{\text{on}} = 200$ ms and in deep-sleep for $T_{\text{off}} = 4.8$ s, drawing $I_{\text{act}} = 160$ mA when active and $I_{\text{sleep}} = 10$ μA in deep-sleep. The battery has $C_{\text{batt}} = 2000$ mAh.

**Step 1:** Calculate the average current drawn:

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{act}} \cdot T_{\text{on}} + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{off}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values (convert all times to consistent units, e.g., seconds):

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{160 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 + 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.8}{0.2 + 4.8}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerator:

$$
160 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = 0.032 \text{ A·s}
$$

$$
10 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.8 = 4.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A·s} \approx 0.000048 \text{ A·s}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.032048 \text{ A·s}
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator:

$$
0.2 + 4.8 = 5.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5:** Divide:

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{0.032048}{5.0} = 0.0064096 \text{ A} \approx 6.41 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 6:** Battery life:

$$
T_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{batt}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \dfrac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{6.41 \text{ mA}} \approx 312 \text{ hours} \approx 13 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 7 (Insight):** Reducing $T_{\text{on}}$ to 50 ms (instead of 200 ms) and increasing $T_{\text{off}}$ to 8 s dramatically extends life to several months.

## 3.4 Numerical Matrix: Comparing BLE and LoRaWAN Throughput

| Metric | BLE 5.0 | LoRaWAN (SF=7) |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| Symbol rate | 1 Msps | 125 ksps |
| Spreading factor | 1 | 7 |
| Bit rate | 2 Mbps | 5.5 kbps |
| Range (typical) | 100 m | 5 km |
| Energy per bit | 50 nJ/bit | 100 µJ/bit |
| Use case | Wearables | Smart Agriculture |

This illustrates the fundamental trade-off: BLE is fast but short-range; LoRaWAN is slow but long-range.

## 3.5 Derivation: Aggregate Network Capacity

For a star-topology LoRaWAN network with $N$ nodes, each transmitting at rate $R$:

$$
C_{\text{net}} = N \times R
$$

For LoRaWAN, channels are time-multiplexed, so the actual aggregate is reduced by the duty cycle $\delta$:

$$
C_{\text{net,eff}} = N \times R \times \delta
$$

With $N = 1000$, $R = 5.5$ kbps, $\delta = 0.01$ (1% duty cycle):

$$
C_{\text{net,eff}} = 1000 \times 5500 \times 0.01 = 55000 \text{ bps} = 55 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Interpretation:** LoRaWAN can support thousands of low-rate sensors, but aggregate bandwidth is limited.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: IoT 5-Layer Infrastructure Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[PERCEPTION LAYER] --> B1[NETWORK LAYER]
    B1 --> C1[PROCESSING LAYER]
    C1 --> D1[APPLICATION LAYER]
    D1 --> E1[BUSINESS LAYER]

    A1a[Sensors: Temperature Humidity Gas Motion] --> A1
    A1b[Actuators: Relays Motors Valves] --> A1
    A1c[RFID Tags and Readers] --> A1

    B1a[Short Range: BLE ZigBee Wi-Fi NFC] --> B1
    B1b[Medium Range: LoRaWAN Sigfox NB-IoT] --> B1
    B1c[Long Range: 5G mMTC Satellite IoT] --> B1
    B1d[Gateways and Routers] --> B1

    C1a[Edge Computing] --> C1
    C1b[Fog Computing] --> C1
    C1c[Cloud Computing: AWS Azure GCP] --> C1
    C1d[Data Analytics and AI ML] --> C1

    D1a[Smart Agriculture] --> D1
    D1b[Smart Healthcare] --> D1
    D1c[Smart Cities] --> D1
    D1d[Industrial IoT] --> D1

    E1a[Business Models] --> E1
    E1b[Privacy and Governance] --> E1
    E1c[ROI and Monetization] --> E1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Data Path from Sensor to Cloud Dashboard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Sensor Node 1 DHT22] --> node2[ESP32 MCU]
    node2 --> node3[BLE TX]
    node2 --> node4[Wi-Fi TX]
    node3 --> node5[BLE Gateway]
    node4 --> node6[Home Router]
    node5 --> node7[Smartphone App]
    node6 --> node8[Cloud Server]
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9[Database Storage]
    node8 --> node10[Analytics Engine]
    node10 --> node11[Dashboard Web App]
    node11 --> node12[End User]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: IoT Protocol Stack Compared to OSI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Application Layer MQTT CoAP HTTP] --> L6
    L6[Presentation TLS DTLS Encryption] --> L5
    L5[Session LoRaWAN MAC BLE L2CAP] --> L4
    L4[Transport UDP TCP] --> L3
    L3[Network IPv6 6LoWPAN RPL] --> L2
    L2[Data Link IEEE 802.15.4 BLE MAC Wi-Fi MAC] --> L1
    L1[Physical Sub-GHz ISM 2.4 GHz UHF RFID]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Edge-Fog-Cloud Continuum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Sensor] --> node2[Edge Node Filters and Aggregates]
    node2 --> node3[Fog Node Local Analytics]
    node3 --> node4[Cloud Server Big Data Storage]
    node4 --> node5[Decision Support System]
    node5 --> node6[Action via Actuator]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Range vs Power vs Data Rate Trade-Off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[NFC] -->|0.1 m| node2[BLE]
    node2 -->|10 m| node3[ZigBee]
    node3 -->|100 m| node4[Wi-Fi]
    node4 -->|5 km| node5[LoRaWAN]
    node5 -->|15 km| node6[NB-IoT]
    node6 -->|40 km| node7[Sigfox]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define IoT Infrastructure. List any four components.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Infrastructure** is the integrated set of hardware, software, communication networks, protocols, and platforms that enable physical devices to sense, communicate, process, and actuate within an IoT ecosystem.

**Four Components:**

1. **Sensors and Actuators** (Device/Perception Layer) — e.g., DHT22, MQ-135
2. **Communication Networks and Protocols** (Network Layer) — e.g., Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN
3. **Edge/Fog/Cloud Computing Platforms** (Processing Layer) — e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub
4. **Applications and Services** (Application Layer) — e.g., Smart Agriculture Dashboard

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between BLE and LoRaWAN in terms of range and data rate.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BLE 5.0 | LoRaWAN |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Range | Up to **100 m** | Up to **10 km (rural)** |
| Data Rate | Up to **2 Mbps** | **0.3 to 50 kbps** |
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 868 / 915 MHz |
| Power | Low | Very Low |
| Use Case | Wearables, indoor | Smart Agriculture, smart cities |

**Key Difference:** BLE offers high data rate over short range, while LoRaWAN offers long range at low data rate — a classic **range-bandwidth trade-off**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) Explain the 5-layer architecture of IoT with a neat diagram. Describe each layer's role with examples. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b) Compare and contrast Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE, and LoRaWAN protocols on the basis of range, data rate, power consumption, frequency band, and typical IoT applications. (7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer for Part (a):**

The IoT reference architecture is organized into **5 layers** as per ISO/IEC 30141:2018 and KTU 2024 syllabus:

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Device Layer):**
This is the physical layer that interacts with the environment. It includes **sensors** (temperature, humidity, motion, gas), **actuators** (relays, motors), **RFID tags**, and **embedded microcontrollers** (ESP32, Arduino). Its primary role is to **sense** and **actuate**.

**Layer 2 — Network Layer (Connectivity Layer):**
This layer handles **data transmission** between devices and the processing layer. It uses protocols like **Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, and 5G**. Gateways and routers belong here.

**Layer 3 — Processing Layer (Middleware Layer):**
This layer performs **data ingestion, storage, and analytics** through **edge computing, fog computing, and cloud platforms** (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT).

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (Service Layer):**
This layer delivers **business value** through applications such as smart agriculture, smart healthcare, smart homes, industrial automation, and smart cities.

**Layer 5 — Business Layer:**
This topmost layer focuses on **business models, ROI, governance, and privacy policies**.

### **Model Answer for Part (b):**

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Frequency | Application |
|----------|-------|-----------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Wi-Fi | ~100 m | Up to 9.6 Gbps | High | 2.4 / 5 GHz | Smart homes, indoor |
| ZigBee | ~100 m | 250 kbps | Low | 2.4 GHz | Home automation, mesh networks |
| BLE | ~100 m | 2 Mbps | Very Low | 2.4 GHz | Wearables, beacons |
| LoRaWAN | ~10 km | 0.3 to 50 kbps | Very Low | 868 / 915 MHz | Smart agriculture, smart cities |

**Key Insights:**
- Wi-Fi trades power for bandwidth.
- BLE and ZigBee target low-power indoor scenarios.
- LoRaWAN sacrifices speed for ultra-long range.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the components of an IoT infrastructure and describe how data flows from a sensor to a cloud-based application. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(b) Explain the range-power-bandwidth trade-off in IoT communication protocols. Discuss why no single protocol is suitable for all IoT applications. (7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer for Part (a):**

A typical IoT infrastructure consists of the following components connected in sequence:

1. **Sensor Node**: e.g., DHT22 captures temperature and humidity.
2. **Microcontroller**: e.g., ESP32 reads sensor data via ADC/I2C.
3. **Wireless Transmitter**: ESP32 sends data via Wi-Fi (HTTP/MQTT).
4. **Gateway / Router**: Forwards data to the Internet.
5. **Cloud Server**: Receives data via MQTT broker, stores in database.
6. **Analytics Engine**: Processes data, applies ML models if needed.
7. **Application Dashboard**: Visualizes data for end users (mobile/web).

**Data Flow Sequence:**

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{MCU} \rightarrow \text{Transmitter} \rightarrow \text{Gateway} \rightarrow \text{Cloud} \rightarrow \text{Database} \rightarrow \text{Dashboard}
$$

### **Model Answer for Part (b):**

The **range-power-bandwidth trade-off** is the fundamental design constraint in IoT protocols.

- **Long-range protocols** (LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT) achieve km-level coverage but operate at **very low data rates (kbps or bps)** to conserve power.
- **Short-range high-bandwidth protocols** (Wi-Fi, BLE) operate at high data rates but only over **tens of meters**.
- **Mesh protocols** (ZigBee, Thread) balance range and bandwidth but increase **complexity and power**.

**Why no single protocol suffices:**

- A smart agriculture field in Kerala needs **kilometers of coverage** with low-power soil sensors → LoRaWAN is ideal.
- A wearable fitness tracker needs **high bandwidth** for heart-rate and motion data over a few meters → BLE is ideal.
- A factory floor needs **deterministic, low-latency communication** → 5G URLLC is ideal.

Hence, IoT deployments use **multi-protocol architectures**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in IoT Infrastructure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing layers of architecture** — Students often mix up *Perception* with *Network* layer. Remember: Perception = physical sensors/actuators; Network = protocols/routing.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — Path loss is in dB, NOT in watts. Use $10\log_{10}$ for power ratios.
> 3. **Ignoring duty cycle in battery calculations** — Always use **average current**, not active current.
> 4. **Forgetting to state protocol characteristics explicitly** — When asked to "compare," always give a **tabular comparison**, not just prose.
> 5. **Confusing BLE with classic Bluetooth** — BLE = Bluetooth Low Energy, designed for IoT; classic Bluetooth is for audio.
> 6. **Missing the "Why"** — Don't just list components; explain their **role and interaction**.
> 7. **Wrong formula constant** — The constant **32.44** in FSPL applies when $d$ is in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for IoT Infrastructure:**

- ☐ IoT Infrastructure = 5 layers: **Perception → Network → Processing → Application → Business**.
- ☐ Key range-power-bandwidth triangle governs all protocol choices.
- ☐ BLE: 2.4 GHz, 100 m, 2 Mbps, low power, wearables.
- ☐ ZigBee: 2.4 GHz, 100 m, 250 kbps, mesh topology.
- ☐ Wi-Fi: 2.4 / 5 GHz, 100 m, up to 9.6 Gbps, high power.
- ☐ LoRaWAN: 868 / 915 MHz, 10 km, 0.3 to 50 kbps, ultra-low power.
- ☐ NB-IoT: licensed LTE spectrum, 15 km, 26 kbps.
- ☐ Sigfox: 868 / 902 MHz, 40 km, 100 bps.
- ☐ NFC: 13.56 MHz, 10 cm, contact-based.
- ☐ RFID: 860 to 960 MHz, 10 m, passive tags.
- ☐ Shannon Capacity: $C = B\log_{2}(1 + S/N)$.
- ☐ FSPL: $L = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44$.
- ☐ Edge vs Cloud: Edge = low latency, local; Cloud = big data, centralized.
- ☐ Duty cycle is critical for battery life calculations.
- ☐ Always remember: **IoT infrastructure = the roads, vehicles, and destinations** of the data journey.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Exam Aspirants:** Practice numerical problems on **Shannon capacity**, **path loss**, and **battery life** — these are the most common 14-mark question types in PBITT604.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networks and Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Networks and Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Networks and Communication" in the IoT Context?

In the KTU 2024 PBITT604 syllabus, **Networks and Communication** forms the foundational backbone of the entire Internet of Things ecosystem. It is the discipline concerned with how *things* (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, edge devices) are interconnected, identified, and how the data generated by them is transported reliably, securely, and efficiently across heterogeneous media — ranging from short-range personal area networks to global, IP-based infrastructures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PBITT604 / Module 1):**
> *Network and Communication in IoT refers to the set of protocols, topologies, physical media, and architectural layers that enable the seamless exchange of data between sensing/actuation devices, edge gateways, cloud platforms, and end-user applications, ensuring interoperability across constrained and unconstrained devices.*

Mathematically, the entire field rests on the classical **Shannon-Hartley theorem**, which defines the theoretical upper bound of a noise-limited communication channel:

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\; \text{bits/second}$$

where:
- $C$ = **channel capacity** (bit rate ceiling, in bps)
- $B$ = **bandwidth** of the channel, in **Hz**
- $S/N$ = **signal-to-noise ratio** (dimensionless, a power ratio)

For IoT, $B$ is typically small (often **< 2 MHz** for sub-GHz ISM bands such as 868/915 MHz) and $S/N$ is poor because of low transmit power, battery constraints, and harsh radio environments. This is precisely *why* low-power wide-area network (LPWAN) protocols and short-range mesh protocols dominate the IoT networking stack.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" of IoT

Think of an IoT network as the **national postal service of a country**:
- **Things (sensors/actuators)** are *houses* that generate or receive letters (data packets).
- **Communication protocols** are the *postal rules* — addressing format, stamp size, maximum weight.
- **Network topologies** are the *road layouts* connecting cities (star roads, ring roads, interconnected grids).
- **Gateways/Routers** are the *post offices* that sort and forward mail.
- **The Cloud/Server** is the *central sorting hub* (e.g., Ernakulam Head Post Office) that stores and distributes mail to recipients.
- **IoT Applications (mobile apps, dashboards)** are the *recipients* reading the letters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Module 1 explicitly expects students to *identify and compare* network types (PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN), understand *layered communication architectures* (OSI & TCP/IP mapped to IoT), and evaluate *wireless communication technologies* used for connecting constrained devices.

---

## 1.3 Scope of "Networks and Communication" in PBITT604

The KTU 2024 Module 1 lays down five sub-themes under this umbrella:

1. **Network Classification** — PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN, BAN, CAN, NAN.
2. **Network Topologies** — Point-to-Point, Star, Tree, Mesh, Hybrid.
3. **Layered Communication Protocols** — OSI 7-layer model, TCP/IP 4-layer model, IoT-specific adaptations.
4. **Wireless Communication Technologies** — Wi-Fi, Bluetooth/BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT, LTE-M, RFID, NFC.
5. **Addressing & Identification** — IPv4, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, MAC addressing, MQTT/HTTP/CoAP as application layer enablers.

These five pillars are exactly what the rest of Module 1 builds upon, leading into Module 2 (IoT Architectures) and Module 3 (Protocols in depth).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Logical View of Communication

Every communication system — whether a WhatsApp call, a satellite link, or a soil-moisture sensor uploading data to AWS — is decomposed into **layers**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to map the classical **OSI 7-layer model** and the **TCP/IP 4-layer model** to IoT devices.

### 2.1.1 The 7-Layer OSI Model and IoT Mapping

| OSI Layer | Classical Function | IoT-Specific Implementation / Protocol | Real-World Example in IoT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **7. Application** | User-facing services | **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP, WebSocket** | Dashboard reading temperature |
| **6. Presentation** | Encryption, encoding, compression | **TLS/DTLS, CoAP, JSON, CBOR, Protobuf** | AES-128 payload encryption |
| **5. Session** | Dialog control, checkpoints | **CoAP confirmable messages, MQTT sessions** | Re-establish after Wi-Fi drop |
| **4. Transport** | End-to-end reliability | **TCP (HTTP), UDP (CoAP, MQTT-SN)** | Reliable vs best-effort delivery |
| **3. Network** | Logical addressing & routing | **IPv4, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL** | Sensor assigned `2001:db8::1` |
| **2. Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing | **IEEE 802.15.4, BLE MAC, Wi-Fi MAC, LoRaWAN MAC** | CSMA/CA channel access |
| **1. Physical** | Bit transmission over media | **Radio (RF), IR, Optical, Twisted Pair** | 2.4 GHz ISM band transmission |

### 2.1.2 The 4-Layer TCP/IP Model and IoT

| TCP/IP Layer | Function | IoT Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Application** | Combines OSI 5-7 | MQTT broker, CoAP server |
| **Transport** | Host-to-host | TCP / UDP |
| **Internet** | Packet routing | IPv6 + 6LoWPAN |
| **Network Access** | Combines OSI 1-2 | IEEE 802.15.4, BLE |

---

## 2.2 Network Topologies — The Geometric Skeleton

A **network topology** defines *how nodes are geometrically/logically connected*. The choice of topology directly impacts *reliability, latency, power consumption*, and *scalability* — all critical in IoT.

### 2.2.1 Star Topology
- All nodes connect to a **central hub/coordinator** (e.g., a Wi-Fi router, ZigBee Coordinator).
- Simple, low latency to hub, but **single point of failure**.
- Used in: **Home Wi-Fi networks, ZigBee Home Automation profiles.**

### 2.2.2 Mesh Topology
- Every node can forward packets for every other node (full mesh) or selected nodes (partial mesh).
- **Self-healing**: if one link fails, traffic re-routes automatically.
- High redundancy, but increased power consumption due to multi-hop relay.
- Used in: **ZigBee, Thread, BLE Mesh, LoRaWAN (with repeaters).**

### 2.2.3 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology
- A **root node** connects to multiple branch nodes, which further connect to leaves.
- Common in **smart agriculture** (Field → Greenhouse → Cluster → Cloud) and **building automation (BAS)**.
- Easy to expand, but root failure isolates entire sub-trees.

### 2.2.4 Bus / Linear Topology
- A single backbone (cable or shared wireless medium) terminated at both ends.
- Used in: **Classical Ethernet, RS-485 industrial buses (Modbus RTU).**

### 2.2.5 Hybrid Topology
- A combination of two or more topologies (e.g., *Star-of-Stars*, *Star-Mesh*).
- **Most real-world IoT deployments** are hybrid.

---

## 2.3 Network Classification by Geographic Span

| Network Type | Approximate Span | Typical IoT Use-Case | Example Tech |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PAN / BAN** | $< 10$ m | Wearable, body sensors | BLE, ANT+ |
| **LAN** | $\leq 1$ km | Smart home, lab | Wi-Fi, ZigBee |
| **CAN** | Campus | University, factory | Wi-Fi, Ethernet |
| **MAN** | City | Smart traffic | WiMAX, LTE |
| **WAN** | Country / Global | Asset tracking, agri | LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Satellite |
| **NAN** | Neighborhood | Smart meter, streetlight | LoRa, NB-IoT |
| **VLAN / VPN** | Logical | Secure corporate IoT | Tunnelled LTE/Wi-Fi |

---

## 2.4 Wireless Communication Technologies — The IoT Radio Zoo

The KTU 2024 Module 1 expects familiarity with the following:

| Technology | Frequency Band | Standard | Range | Data Rate | Power Profile |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Wi-Fi** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax | $\sim 50$ m | up to **9.6 Gbps** (Wi-Fi 6) | High |
| **Bluetooth / BLE** | 2.4 GHz | IEEE 802.15.1 | $\sim 10$ m | 1–2 Mbps | Very low |
| **ZigBee** | 2.4 GHz | IEEE 802.15.4 | $\sim 100$ m | 250 kbps | Low |
| **Thread** | 2.4 GHz | IEEE 802.15.4 + IPv6 | $\sim 100$ m | 250 kbps | Low |
| **LoRaWAN** | 433/868/915 MHz | LoRa Alliance | 2–15 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Ultra-low |
| **NB-IoT** | Licensed LTE bands | 3GPP | $\sim 10$ km | $\leq 200$ kbps | Low |
| **LTE-M (Cat-M1)** | Licensed LTE | 3GPP | $\sim 10$ km | 1 Mbps | Low |
| **RFID (passive)** | 125 kHz – 5.8 GHz | ISO/IEC 14443, 15693 | $\le 10$ m | $\le 100$ kbps | Zero (harvested) |
| **NFC** | 13.56 MHz | ISO/IEC 18092 | $\le 10$ cm | 106–424 kbps | Very low |
| **Z-Wave** | 908/916 MHz | Z-Wave Alliance | $\sim 30$ m | 100 kbps | Low |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the most frequently tested quantitative results in PBITT604 Module 1. Memorize the variables and the units — KTU exam answers are penalized if units are missing.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units | Engineering Use in IoT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | $C$ = capacity, $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = SNR | bps, Hz, ratio | Sets upper data rate of any IoT link |
| 2 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ | $d$ = distance, $f$ = frequency, $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | dB | Predicts LoRa/BLE range |
| 3 | **Link Budget** | $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{TX} - \text{FSPL} - L_{RX} + G_{RX}$ | $P_{RX}$, $P_{TX}$ in dBm, $G$ = antenna gain, $L$ = loss | dBm / dB | Determines if a link closes |
| 4 | **Received Power (Friis)** | $P_R = P_T G_T G_R (\lambda/4\pi d)^2$ | $P_T$ = transmit, $G$ = gain, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $d$ = distance | W | Used in range estimation |
| 5 | **Maximum Nodes in IEEE 802.15.4** | 2^16 = 65,536 | – | – | 16-bit short address space |
| 6 | **IPv6 Address Space** | 2^{128} | – | addresses | Effectively unlimited for IoT |
| 7 | **CoAP Message Overhead** | 4-byte base header + TKL + token | TKL = 0–8 bytes | bytes | Explains why CoAP suits constrained nodes |
| 8 | **MQTT QoS Delivery** | QoS 0: at most once, QoS 1: at least once, QoS 2: exactly once | – | – | Reliability vs overhead trade-off |
| 9 | **Number of Hops vs Latency** | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{hop,i}$ | $T_{hop}$ = per-hop delay, $n$ = number of hops | seconds | Mesh network latency modelling |
| 10 | **Power for one bit transmission** | $E_{bit} = V \cdot I \cdot t_{bit}$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $t_{bit}$ = 1/data rate | Joules | Battery-life estimation |

**Note:** In all formulas above, the absolute-value delimiter has been rendered using `\vert` / `\mid` to keep markdown tables safe from pipe-character conflicts.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture (Kerala context):** A LoRaWAN sensor node at a cardamom plantation in Idukki transmits soil moisture every 15 minutes. The **link budget** equation tells the engineer whether one gateway on the hill is sufficient or two are needed.
- **Smart Health (Hospital Wards):** A BLE star-of-stars mesh transmits vitals. **Shannon's equation** is used to size the bandwidth needed to support 50 patients/ward with $\leq 5$-second latency.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** A Modbus/RTU over RS-485 bus uses **bus topology**. The student must understand termination resistance and signal reflection.
- **Smart City (Kochi):** NB-IoT smart-parking sensors in Marine Drive use **3GPP licensed spectrum** to avoid interference with consumer Wi-Fi.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) for an IoT Link

We derive the **Friis free-space equation** from first principles, because it is a *guaranteed 3-to-7-mark derivation* in KTU ESE.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Isotropic Radiator

An **isotropic antenna** radiates power uniformly in all directions. At a distance $d$, this power is spread over the surface of an imaginary sphere of area $4\pi d^2$.

Therefore, the **power flux density** (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_T}{4\pi d^2}
$$

> *Explanation:* $P_T$ is the total transmitted power in watts; the denominator is the surface area of the sphere — this is the standard inverse-square law of radiation.

### 3.1.2 Capture by a Real Antenna

A real receiving antenna with **effective aperture** $A_e$ captures a portion of this flux. The aperture of an antenna is related to its gain $G_R$ and wavelength $\lambda$:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_R \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

> *Explanation:* Higher-gain antennas physically "look bigger" in terms of cross-section; shorter wavelengths (higher frequencies) make antennas physically smaller for the same gain.

### 3.1.3 Received Power

Multiplying flux density by effective aperture:

$$
P_R = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_T}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_R \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

If the transmit antenna is *also* directional with gain $G_T$, we add that factor in:

$$
P_R = P_T \, G_T \, G_R \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^{\!2}
$$

> *This is the **Friis transmission equation** — the bedrock of all wireless range calculations in IoT.*

### 3.1.4 Conversion to dB Form

We take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides to express in decibels (the form KTU examiners prefer):

$$
P_R \text{ [dBm]} = P_T \text{ [dBm]} + G_T \text{ [dBi]} + G_R \text{ [dBi]} - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

Since $c = f\lambda$, we can substitute $\lambda = c/f$. The standard form of FSPL in dB is:

$$
\text{FSPL [dB]} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

Substituting $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and converting:

$$
\text{FSPL [dB]} = 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The constant 32.44 dB comes from** $20 \log_{10}(4\pi / c)$ when distance is in km and frequency in MHz. It is **the single most-tested constant in IoT wireless problems.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Link Budget for a LoRa Node

> **KTU Typical Question:** *A LoRaWAN end-device transmits at +14 dBm with a 2 dBi antenna. The gateway antenna gain is 6 dBi. Distance = 8 km, frequency = 868 MHz. Cable losses: 1 dB at TX, 1.5 dB at RX. Compute the received power.*

**Solution (Step-by-Step — as expected by a KTU examiner):**

**Step 1:** Identify all given quantities and convert to dB/dBm.

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| TX power | $P_T$ | +14 | dBm |
| TX antenna gain | $G_T$ | 2 | dBi |
| RX antenna gain | $G_R$ | 6 | dBi |
| TX cable loss | $L_T$ | 1 | dB |
| RX cable loss | $L_R$ | 1.5 | dB |
| Distance | $d$ | 8 km | – |
| Frequency | $f$ | 868 MHz | – |

**Step 2:** Compute FSPL.

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(8) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44
$$

$$
= 20 (0.9031) + 20 (2.9385) + 32.44
$$

$$
= 18.06 + 58.77 + 32.44 = 109.27 \;\; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the link-budget equation.

$$
P_R = P_T + G_T - L_T - \text{FSPL} + G_R - L_R
$$

$$
P_R = 14 + 2 - 1 - 109.27 + 6 - 1.5
$$

$$
P_R = (14 + 2 + 6) - (1 + 109.27 + 1.5) = 22 - 111.77
$$

$$
\boxed{P_R \approx -89.77 \;\; \text{dBm}}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

A typical LoRa SX1276 receiver sensitivity at SF12/125 kHz BW is around **–137 dBm**. Our $P_R = -89.77$ dBm is **~47 dB above sensitivity**, so the link closes with a massive fade margin. A KTU examiner will accept an *engineering interpretation* sentence as the final 1–2 marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice the *dominant loss term* is the FSPL. Doubling the distance adds only **6 dB** of loss; doubling the frequency also adds **6 dB**. This is why sub-GHz bands (868/915 MHz) are preferred for long-range IoT.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Wireless Technology

Use this KTU-recommended comparison table to select a wireless technology for a given IoT problem.

| Criterion | Wi-Fi | BLE | ZigBee | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Rate** | High | Med | Low | Very Low | Low |
| **Range** | Low | Very Low | Low | Very High | High |
| **Power** | High | Very Low | Low | Ultra-low | Low |
| **Mobility** | Excellent | Good | Poor | Poor | Excellent |
| **Cost / Node** | Med | Very Low | Low | Low | Med |
| **IP-native** | Yes | No (with 6LoWPAN — no) | No (with Thread — yes) | No | Yes |
| **Best IoT Use-Case** | Video, firmware update | Wearable, beacon | Home automation | Smart agri, metering | Smart city, asset tracking |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating *bandwidth* with *data rate*. KTU questions often test: "Higher bandwidth ≠ always better." For a soil-moisture sensor sending 4 bytes/hour, LoRaWAN's tiny bandwidth is the *correct* choice — Wi-Fi would be wildly inefficient.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — IoT Communication Stack (OSI vs TCP/IP)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sens1["Sensor Node MCU"] --> phys1["L1 PHYSICAL IEEE 802.15.4 RF 2.4 GHz"]
    sens1 --> dl1["L2 DATA LINK CSMA-CA 16 bit MAC"]
    sens1 --> nw1["L3 NETWORK 6LoWPAN IPv6"]
    sens1 --> tp1["L4 TRANSPORT UDP"]
    sens1 --> app1["L7 APPLICATION CoAP DTLS"]

    phys1 --> nw1
    dl1 --> nw1
    nw1 --> tp1
    tp1 --> app1

    app1 --> gw1["EDGE GATEWAY"]
    gw1 --> mqttbr["MQTT BROKER"]
    mqttbr --> cloud["CLOUD SERVER"]
    cloud --> user["USER DASHBOARD"]

    classDef iot fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e;
    classDef net fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350f;
    classDef app fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d;
    class sens1,dl1,phys1,nw1,tp1,app1 iot;
    class gw1,mqttbr net;
    class cloud,user app;
```

> *Reading guide:* the left column stacks the OSI layers within a single IoT sensor; the right column shows the journey of a packet *from the device to a human user*. KTU examiners love this "layered + end-to-end" combined view.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Network Topologies Used in IoT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY"]
        h1[Coordinator Hub]
        n1a[Node 1]
        n1b[Node 2]
        n1c[Node 3]
        n1d[Node 4]
        h1 --- n1a
        h1 --- n1b
        h1 --- n1c
        h1 --- n1d
    end

    subgraph MESH["MESH TOPOLOGY"]
        m1[Node A]
        m2[Node B]
        m3[Node C]
        m4[Node D]
        m5[Node E]
        m1 --- m2
        m2 --- m3
        m3 --- m4
        m4 --- m5
        m5 --- m1
        m1 --- m3
        m2 --- m4
    end

    subgraph TREE["TREE TOPOLOGY"]
        r1[Root Gateway]
        b1[Branch 1]
        b2[Branch 2]
        l1a[Leaf 1a]
        l1b[Leaf 1b]
        l2a[Leaf 2a]
        l2b[Leaf 2b]
        r1 --- b1
        r1 --- b2
        b1 --- l1a
        b1 --- l1b
        b2 --- l2a
        b2 --- l2b
    end

    classDef starCls fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#451a03;
    classDef meshCls fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d;
    classDef treeCls fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e;
    class h1,n1a,n1b,n1c,n1d starCls;
    class m1,m2,m3,m4,m5 meshCls;
    class r1,b1,b2,l1a,l1b,l2a,l2b treeCls;
```

> *Reading guide:* The **subgraph** names are the *only* allowed source of descriptive text; the **node IDs** are kept strictly alphanumeric (`h1`, `m1`, `r1`, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard. Notice how **mesh** has more interconnections, **star** has fewer, and **tree** is hierarchical — this is the visual signature KTU students must recognize instantly.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End IoT Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["THINGS LAYER Sensor Actuator RFID Tag"] --> B["EDGE NETWORK Gateway Router Bridge"]
    B --> C["ACCESS NETWORK LPWAN Cellular WiFi"]
    C --> D["CORE NETWORK Internet Backbone"]
    D --> E["CLOUD LAYER Storage Analytics AI"]
    E --> F["APPLICATION LAYER Dashboard Mobile Alert"]
    F --> G["USER End Consumer Operator"]

    A -.bidirectional.-> F

    classDef thing fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d;
    classDef edge fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,stroke-width:2px,color:#7c2d12;
    classDef access fill:#fef08a,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713f12;
    classDef core fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d;
    classDef cloud fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e3a8a;
    classDef app fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#3b0764;
    classDef user fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843;

    class A thing;
    class B edge;
    class C access;
    class D core;
    class E cloud;
    class F app;
    class G user;
```

> *Reading guide:* This is the canonical **5-layer IoT reference architecture** mapped to the **Network and Communication** topic. The dotted bidirectional line between *Things* and *Application* indicates **actuation control** (e.g., turning off a motor from a mobile app) — a frequently tested KTU concept.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Protocol Stack Comparison (MQTT vs CoAP vs HTTP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MQTT["MQTT STACK"]
        m1[Application MQTT 3.1.1]
        m2[Transport TCP Port 8883 TLS]
        m3[Network IPv4 or IPv6]
    end

    subgraph COAP["CoAP STACK"]
        c1[Application CoAP RFC 7252]
        c2[Transport UDP Port 5683 DTLS]
        c3[Network 6LoWPAN IPv6]
    end

    subgraph HTTP["HTTP STACK"]
        h1[Application HTTP 1.1 or HTTP/2]
        h2[Transport TCP Port 443 TLS]
        h3[Network IPv4 or IPv6]
    end

    classDef mqtt fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e3a8a;
    classDef coap fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d;
    classDef http fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d;
    class m1,m2,m3 mqtt;
    class c1,c2,c3 coap;
    class h1,h2,h3 http;
```

> *Reading guide:* Notice **CoAP** rides on **UDP + 6LoWPAN** — that is *the* hallmark of constrained-node IoT. **MQTT** sits on TCP for reliability. **HTTP** is rarely used on sensor nodes directly but is the lingua franca of the cloud side.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between PAN, LAN, MAN, and WAN. Give one IoT example for each.**

**Model Answer (KTU marking scheme style — 3 points, 1 mark each):**

1. **PAN (Personal Area Network):** Covers an area $< 10$ m around a person. **IoT Example:** BLE-enabled fitness band transmitting heart-rate to a smartphone.
2. **LAN (Local Area Network):** Covers up to ~1 km, typically a building or campus. **IoT Example:** ZigBee-based home automation system.
3. **MAN (Metropolitan Area Network):** Spans a city (~5–50 km). **IoT Example:** City-wide smart-parking NB-IoT deployment in Kochi.
4. **WAN (Wide Area Network):** Country-/global-scale. **IoT Example:** Satellite-based asset tracking for inter-state logistics.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**Explain the role of the Network Layer in IoT with reference to IPv6 and 6LoWPAN.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Network Layer (OSI Layer 3)** is responsible for **logical addressing and packet routing** between source and destination. In IoT, classical IPv4 is unsuitable because:
- **Address exhaustion:** IPv4 offers only 2^{32} $\approx 4.3 \times 10^{9}$ addresses — insufficient for billions of sensors.
- **IPv6** solves this with **2^{128}** addresses (~3.4 $\times$ 10^{38}), enabling *every grain of sand* to have an IP.

However, IPv6's 40-byte header is too large for IEEE 802.15.4 frames whose MAC payload is only **~81 bytes** (127 minus 25 bytes of MAC overhead). **6LoWPAN** (RFC 4944) is an *adaptation layer* that **compresses** the IPv6 header down to as little as **2 bytes** using IPHC (IP Header Compression). It also performs **fragmentation/reassembly** to fit IPv6 packets into tiny 802.15.4 frames. **In one line:** *6LoWPAN is the glue that makes IPv6 work on constrained 802.15.4 IoT nodes.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, usually split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer either OR both choices.*

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) `[7 Marks]`
**With a neat diagram, explain the OSI 7-layer model. Map each layer to a representative IoT protocol.**

**Model Answer:**

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model** is a 7-layer abstraction created by ISO to standardize communication between heterogeneous systems. From top to bottom:

1. **Application Layer (L7):** Closest to the user. IoT protocols: **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP, WebSocket**. Function: request/response, publish/subscribe, resource observation.
2. **Presentation Layer (L6):** Data formatting, encryption. IoT: **TLS/DTLS, JSON, CBOR, Protobuf, EXI**.
3. **Session Layer (L5):** Dialog management. IoT: **CoAP confirmable/non-confirmable messages, MQTT session IDs**.
4. **Transport Layer (L4):** End-to-end channels. IoT: **TCP (HTTP/MQTT), UDP (CoAP)**.
5. **Network Layer (L3):** Logical addressing & routing. IoT: **IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks).**
6. **Data Link Layer (L2):** Framing & MAC. IoT: **IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, Wi-Fi MAC, LoRaWAN MAC**.
7. **Physical Layer (L1):** Bit transmission. IoT: **RF transceivers, IR LEDs, optical fibre, RS-485 copper.**

*(Examiner will allocate ~1 mark per layer for naming the protocol; the remaining mark is for stating the layer's function.)*

> *(Include the diagram from SECTION 4.1 in your answer sheet — 1 mark is reserved for the figure.)*

#### (b) `[7 Marks]`
**A LoRaWAN sensor operates at 868 MHz with +14 dBm TX power, 2 dBi TX antenna, 6 dBi RX antenna, 8 km distance, 1 dB TX cable loss and 1.5 dB RX cable loss. Compute the received power and comment on link closure.**

**Model Answer:** *(This is the exact numerical solved in SECTION 3.2.)*

- **FSPL** = $20\log_{10}(8) + 20\log_{10}(868) + 32.44 = 109.27$ dB
- **Received power** $P_R = 14 + 2 - 1 - 109.27 + 6 - 1.5 = -89.77$ dBm
- **Comment:** With LoRa sensitivity of $\sim -137$ dBm at SF12/125 kHz, the link has a **fade margin of ~47 dB**, so the link is *robustly closed*. The student should add: *"The dominant loss is FSPL; the system could tolerate 47 dB of additional obstruction/fading before failure."*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) `[7 Marks]`
**Compare and contrast Star, Mesh, and Tree topologies for IoT. Give one IoT protocol example for each and discuss fault tolerance.**

**Model Answer (use a tabular response for 5 marks + 2 marks for fault-tolerance discussion):**

| Parameter | Star | Mesh | Tree |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Topology** | All nodes to a central hub | Every node connects to multiple peers | Hierarchical, root-branches-leaves |
| **IoT Protocol Example** | ZigBee (with coordinator) / BLE star | ZigBee mesh / Thread / BLE mesh | RPL-over-6LoWPAN, BACnet |
| **Scalability** | Limited by hub | High | High |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (single hop) | High (multi-hop relay) | Moderate |
| **Latency** | Predictable, low | Variable | Predictable per branch |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Hub failure = total collapse | No SPOF | Root failure = partial collapse |

**Fault tolerance discussion (2 marks):** *Mesh* is most fault-tolerant because of multiple redundant paths; *star* is least; *tree* falls in between. In a hospital, mesh is preferred; in a small smart-home, star suffices.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]`
**Explain MQTT and CoAP. Compare them in terms of transport, message overhead, QoS, and typical IoT use-case.**

**Model Answer:**

- **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport):** A *publish/subscribe* protocol designed by IBM in 1999. Runs over **TCP (port 8883 with TLS)**. **Message overhead: 2-byte fixed header.** Offers **3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2)**. Best for **always-connected, reliable** scenarios such as industrial IoT dashboards.

- **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol):** A *request/response* protocol from IETF (RFC 7252), designed to mimic HTTP but for constrained nodes. Runs over **UDP (port 5683, with DTLS for security)**. **Message overhead: 4-byte base header + token (0–8 bytes).** Offers **2 message types (CON, NON)** corresponding to reliable vs best-effort. Best for **sleepy battery-powered nodes**.

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | MQTT | CoAP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Req/Res |
| Header | 2 bytes | 4 bytes |
| Security | TLS | DTLS |
| QoS | 0/1/2 | CON/NON + ACK/RST |
| Typical Use | Industrial, fleet tracking | Smart agriculture, smart metering |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks in "Networks and Communication":**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in FSPL / link-budget answers. *Always* state "dB" or "dBm".
> 2. **Confusing MQTT and CoAP** as "competing standards" — they are *complementary*; mention the TCP-vs-UDP difference.
> 3. **Stating "6LoWPAN = IPv6"** — wrong! 6LoWPAN is an *adaptation layer below IPv6* that *enables* IPv6 over 802.15.4.
> 4. **Drawing a topology without a central node label** — the diagram will be returned with 0 marks for the figure.
> 5. **Mixing up** $20 \log_{10}$ (voltage/power-field) vs $10 \log_{10}$ (power). For path loss, it is always $20 \log_{10}$ because FSPL is a *power ratio* expressed using field quantities.
> 6. **Forgetting the constant 32.44** in the FSPL formula when using km & MHz units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PBITT604 Module 1 / Networks and Communication**

- **Network classification** by span: PAN, BAN, LAN, CAN, MAN, NAN, WAN — know one IoT example for each.
- **Topologies:** Star (low power, SPOF), Mesh (self-healing, expensive), Tree (hierarchical), Bus (legacy industrial), Hybrid (real-world default).
- **OSI 7 layers** — memorise layer number, name, function, and *one IoT protocol per layer*.
- **TCP/IP 4 layers** — Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access; *CoAP and MQTT sit in the Application layer*.
- **IEEE 802.15.4** is the MAC/PHY basis for ZigBee, Thread, 6LoWPAN, WirelessHART — know its 2.4 GHz operation, 250 kbps data rate, 127-byte frame, 16-bit short address.
- **6LoWPAN** = adaptation layer that compresses IPv6 headers to fit 802.15.4 frames; *not* the same as IPv6 itself.
- **LoRaWAN** = long range, low data rate, sub-GHz ISM, licensed-free, perfect for metering/agriculture.
- **NB-IoT & LTE-M** = licensed cellular IoT, operator-managed, good mobility, higher cost.
- **BLE** = short range, ultra-low power, smartphone-friendly, ideal for wearables.
- **Wi-Fi** = high throughput but power-hungry; reserved for edge gateways and video nodes.
- **Shannon capacity** $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ — the absolute ceiling for any wireless link.
- **FSPL** $= 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ dB — use this exact form.
- **Link budget** = $P_T + G_T - L_T - \text{FSPL} + G_R - L_R$ → result in dBm; compare against receiver sensitivity.
- **MQTT = TCP, Pub/Sub, 2-byte header, QoS 0/1/2.**
- **CoAP = UDP, Req/Res, 4-byte header, CON/NON reliability.**
- **6LoWPAN address compression** can shrink an IPv6 header from **40 bytes** to as little as **2 bytes**.
- **Mesh** = high fault tolerance but high power; **Star** = low power but fragile — choose based on application criticality.
- **Always annotate diagrams** with node IDs and *protocol names* — KTU examiners mark diagrams strictly.
- **Hybrid topology** is the *de facto* topology in any real-world IoT deployment.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Output for PBITT604 / Module 1 / Networks and Communication.**
