topics.title: Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Process** is defined as a program in execution. It is the fundamental unit of work in an operating system and, by extension, the basic computational entity inside any IoT device, gateway, or cloud node. A process is more than the program code itself; it is an **active entity** that includes the program counter, registers, stack, heap, and the current state of execution.

A process is often described as an **instance of a computer program that is being executed**. It contains the program code (the static instructions) and the **current activity** represented by the values of the program counter, registers, and the variables in memory. Even if the same program is launched twice (for example, opening two tabs of the same IoT dashboard), the operating system treats them as **two separate processes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction: Program vs. Process**
> - A **Program** is a passive entity — just a file on disk containing instructions (e.g., `temperature_sensor.elf` stored in the flash of an ESP32).
> - A **Process** is an active entity — the program loaded into memory with a live program counter, stack frames, and I/O buffers.

### 1.1 Real-World Analogy: The Coffee Shop Token System

Imagine a busy KTU campus canteen. There is **one menu (the program)** but **many active orders being prepared (the processes)** at the same time. Each order is stamped with:

- A unique **token number** (Process ID — `PID`)
- The **current stage** it is in (Ready → Being Prepared → Waiting for Milk → Ready for Pickup)
- The **customer's name and customization** (variables in memory)
- The **counter number** it must return to (the program counter)

If the milk runs out, the order is **suspended** (Blocked state). When milk arrives, the order is **resumed** from where it was. This is exactly how an OS manages a process on an IoT edge node running a real-time operating system (RTOS) like FreeRTOS.

### 1.2 Why Processes Matter in IoT

In an IoT system, every sensor-reading task, every MQTT publish cycle, and every cloud handshake is a **process or a thread**. KTU specifically tests whether students understand:

- The **Process Control Block (PCB)** and its fields
- The **five-state process model**
- **Context switching** overhead
- The difference between **processes and threads**, which is critical for constrained IoT devices
- **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** mechanisms used between edge nodes

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric to Remember**
> A typical IoT sensor node has between **1–8 MB RAM** and runs **10–100 concurrent processes/threads**. A Raspberry Pi gateway may run **hundreds** of processes. This is why process scheduling theory is part of the IoT syllabus — efficiency at the OS level directly impacts battery life, latency, and reliability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Process (The Memory Layout)

Every process in memory is logically divided into **four distinct sections**:

| Section | Contents | Growth Direction | Permission |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Text / Code Segment** | Compiled machine instructions (the program itself) | Fixed size | Read-Only, Execute |
| **Data Segment** | Initialized global and static variables | Grows up | Read-Write |
| **Heap Segment** | Dynamically allocated memory (`malloc`, `new`) | Grows upward | Read-Write |
| **Stack Segment** | Local variables, function parameters, return addresses | Grows downward | Read-Write |

### 2.2 The Process Control Block (PCB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Process Control Block (PCB)** is the kernel's data structure for each process. It is the "identity card + medical report" of a process. In KTU exams, you must be able to **list every field of the PCB**.

The PCB typically contains:

- **Process ID (PID)** — Unique integer identifier
- **Process State** — Current state (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated)
- **Program Counter (PC)** — Address of the next instruction
- **CPU Registers** — Accumulator, index registers, stack pointer, general-purpose registers
- **Memory Management Information** — Base and limit registers, page tables
- **Scheduling Information** — Priority, pointers to scheduling queues
- **Accounting Information** — CPU time used, time limits, process owner
- **I/O Status Information** — List of open files, allocated I/O devices

### 2.3 The Five-State Process Model

This is the **most heavily tested diagram** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers. Every student must be able to draw and explain it.

The five states are:

1. **New** — The process is being created. The OS allocates a PCB but has not yet admitted it to the ready queue.
2. **Ready** — The process is waiting to be assigned to a CPU core. All required resources are available except the CPU.
3. **Running** — Instructions are being executed by the CPU core. At any instant, only **one process per core** can be in the running state.
4. **Waiting / Blocked** — The process cannot run because it is waiting for some event to occur (typically I/O completion, a signal, or a semaphore).
5. **Terminated / Exit** — The process has finished execution. The OS reclaims its resources but the PCB may be retained for accounting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Blocked State is Necessary**
> If a process doing a 5-second sensor reading (e.g., DHT22 humidity sensor) was kept in the Ready state, the CPU would waste cycles checking it. Putting it in the **Blocked state** allows the CPU to context-switch to another process — this is the foundation of **multiprogramming** in IoT gateways.

### 2.4 Context Switching

A **Context Switch** is the mechanism by which the OS saves the state of a currently running process and loads the saved state of another process. It is expensive in terms of time and is critical in real-time IoT.

The steps are:

1. Save the state of the currently running process (PC, registers, etc.) into its PCB.
2. Move the PCB of the current process to the appropriate queue (Ready or Blocked).
3. Select a new process for execution using the scheduler.
4. Load the saved state of the new process from its PCB into the CPU registers.
5. Update memory management structures to point to the new process's page tables.
6. Resume execution at the new process's saved Program Counter.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight**
> Context switching has **no useful work done** — it is pure overhead. On a heavily loaded IoT gateway, the cumulative context-switch time can consume **5–10% of total CPU time**. RTOS systems like FreeRTOS are designed to minimize this.

### 2.5 Processes vs. Threads (A KTU Favourite)

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Independent program with its own memory space | Lightweight sub-unit of a process |
| Memory | Has its own separate address space | Shares the address space with peer threads |
| Creation Cost | **Heavy** (PCB allocation, memory copy) | **Light** (shares parent process memory) |
| Communication | Requires IPC (pipes, message queues, sockets) | Shared variables (with synchronization) |
| Context Switch | **Slow** (must save whole memory map) | **Fast** (only registers and stack) |
| IoT Use Case | MQTT client + HTTPS server as separate services | Sensor sampling, filtering, and transmitting simultaneously |
| Typical KTU Example | Two Docker containers on a Raspberry Pi gateway | Three FreeRTOS tasks on an ESP32 |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity / Metric | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization (U) | $U = 1 - p^{n}$ where $p$ is prob. of I/O wait, $n$ is number of processes | Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) | Higher means better multiprogramming |
| Throughput (X) | $X = \dfrac{\text{Number of processes completed}}{\text{Total time}}$ | processes / second | KTU standard: > 1 process/sec is good |
| Turnaround Time (TAT) | $TAT = T_{\text{completion}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | seconds (s) or milliseconds (ms) | For IoT: usually < 100 ms is required |
| Waiting Time (WT) | $WT = TAT - T_{\text{burst}}$ | seconds (s) or ms | Time spent in Ready queue |
| Response Time (RT) | $RT = T_{\text{first CPU}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | seconds (s) or ms | Critical for interactive IoT dashboards |
| Context Switch Overhead (CS) | $CS_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cs}} \times N_{\text{cs}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | $N_{\text{cs}}$ is number of switches |
| Average TAT (multi-process) | $\overline{TAT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | seconds (s) | Used in SJF, FCFS, RR problems |
| Average Waiting Time | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | seconds (s) | KTU exam standard |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Waiting Time for First-Come-First-Served (FCFS) Scheduling

FCFS is the simplest non-preemptive algorithm — the process that arrives first is executed first. We can derive the average waiting time using a step-by-step Gantt chart approach.

**Given Data (KTU-style problem):**

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 ms | 4 ms |
| P2 | 1 ms | 3 ms |
| P3 | 2 ms | 2 ms |
| P4 | 3 ms | 1 ms |

**Step 1 — Build the Gantt Chart**

In FCFS, processes execute in the order they arrive. Since all four arrive in the order P1 → P2 → P3 → P4, the Gantt chart is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart} &: \;\; \vert\, P1 \;\vert\; \vert\, P2 \;\vert\; \vert\, P3 \;\vert\; \vert\, P4 \;\vert \\
\text{Timeline (ms)} &: \;\; 0 \quad\quad\quad 4 \quad\quad 7 \quad\quad 9 \quad\quad 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Completion Time (CT) of each process**

Reading from the Gantt chart:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 0 + 4 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P2} &= 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P3} &= 7 + 2 = 9 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P4} &= 9 + 1 = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Turnaround Time (TAT) of each process**

The TAT formula is $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P2} &= 7 - 1 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P3} &= 9 - 2 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P4} &= 10 - 3 = 7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Waiting Time (WT) of each process**

The WT formula is $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 4 - 4 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P2} &= 6 - 3 = 3 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P3} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P4} &= 7 - 1 = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Average Waiting Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT} &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i \\
&= \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 6}{4} \\
&= \frac{14}{4} \\
\overline{WT} &= 3.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Context Switch Overhead in an IoT Gateway

**Problem:** An IoT gateway runs FreeRTOS. The CPU operates at 80 MHz. Each context switch takes 12 microseconds. The system performs 2000 context switches per second. Compute the percentage of CPU time lost to context switching.

**Step 1 — Total time lost per second**

$$
T_{\text{lost}} = N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{lost}} &= 2000 \times 12 \,\mu\text{s} \\
&= 24{,}000 \,\mu\text{s} \\
&= 0.024 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to CPU cycles lost**

Since the CPU runs at 80 MHz, each second contains $80 \times 10^6$ cycles. The number of cycles wasted is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{lost}} &= 80 \times 10^{6} \times 0.024 \\
&= 1.92 \times 10^{6} \text{ cycles per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage overhead**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{\%} &= \frac{T_{\text{lost}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.024}{1} \times 100 \\
CS_{\%} &= 2.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 2.4% overhead is **acceptable** for non-real-time IoT applications but is **unacceptable** for hard real-time industrial control, where the total jitter budget is often less than **1%**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Scheduling Algorithm Comparison (KTU Quick Reference)

| Algorithm | Preemptive? | Type | Average WT | Pros | Cons | IoT Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | No | Non-preemptive | High (suffer from convoy effect) | Simple, fair | Long jobs delay short ones | Not suitable for real-time |
| **SJF (Non-Preemptive)** | No | Non-preemptive | Optimal (provably minimum) | Minimum average WT | Starvation possible, needs BT in advance | OK for batch IoT analytics |
| **SRTF (Shortest Remaining Time First)** | Yes | Preemptive | Optimal preemptive | Best average WT | Starvation, high context switch cost | Risky for hard real-time |
| **Round Robin (RR)** | Yes | Preemptive | Moderate | Fair, good response time | Performance depends on quantum $q$ | **Excellent** for IoT gateways |
| **Priority Scheduling** | Optional | Either | Varies | Honours importance | Starvation of low-priority | **Excellent** for IoT (alarm processes) |
| **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLQ)** | Yes | Preemptive | Excellent | Adaptive, penalises CPU-bound jobs | Complex to configure | Used in modern RTOS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Five-State Process State Transition Diagram

This is the single most important diagram for KTU Module 1 — it appears in nearly every past paper.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> stateNew
    stateNew --> stateReady : Admission
    stateReady --> stateRunning : Scheduler Dispatch
    stateRunning --> stateReady : Preemption / Interrupt / Time Quantum Expiry
    stateRunning --> stateWaiting : I/O or Event Wait
    stateWaiting --> stateReady : I/O or Event Completion
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated : Process Exit
    stateTerminated --> [*]

    stateNew : NEW
    stateReady : READY
    stateRunning : RUNNING
    stateWaiting : WAITING BLOCKED
    stateTerminated : TERMINATED EXIT
```

### 4.2 Process Memory Layout (Address Space Organization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Highest Address - Kernel Space] --> B[Stack Segment]
    B --> C[Free Memory - Hole]
    C --> D[Heap Segment]
    D --> E[Data Segment - Initialized Globals]
    E --> F[Text Segment - Read Only Code]
    F --> G[Lowest Address - 0x0000]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Direction Note for KTU Answer Sheet**
> The stack grows **downward** (from high to low addresses) and the heap grows **upward** (from low to high addresses). If they collide, you get a **stack overflow** — a real problem in resource-constrained IoT devices.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Process Lifecycle in an IoT Edge Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EdgeNode [IoT Edge Node - ESP32 with FreeRTOS]
        task1[Task 1 - Sensor Sampling at 1 Hz]
        task2[Task 2 - Local Filtering and Aggregation]
        task3[Task 3 - MQTT Publish Every 10 s]
        scheduler[FreeRTOS Scheduler]
        pcbPool[PCB Pool - 8 Entries]
        isr[Hardware Interrupt - Timer GPIO WiFi]
    end

    task1 --> scheduler
    task2 --> scheduler
    task3 --> scheduler
    isr --> scheduler
    scheduler --> pcbPool
    scheduler --> task1
    scheduler --> task2
    scheduler --> task3

    classDef taskStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef schedStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef isrStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    class task1,task2,task3 taskStyle
    class scheduler,pcbPool schedStyle
    class isr isrStyle
```

### 4.4 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) Mechanisms in IoT

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Process A - MQTT Client] -->|Message Queue| B[Process B - Cloud Connector]
    A -->|Shared Memory| C[Process C - Local Cache]
    A -->|Socket TCP| D[Process D - Remote Dashboard]
    A -->|Pipe FIFO| E[Process E - Logger Daemon]
    A -->|Semaphore| F[Process F - Resource Arbiter]

    classDef nodeStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F nodeStyle
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite IPC Question**
> "Compare **pipes, message queues, shared memory, and sockets** with respect to two IoT processes communicating on a Raspberry Pi gateway." Always mention: **shared memory is fastest** but requires synchronization; **sockets** are the only IPC that works across networked IoT devices.

### 4.5 Context Switch Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant OS as OS Scheduler
    participant PCB_A as PCB of Process A
    participant PCB_B as PCB of Process B

    CPU->>PCB_A: Save Registers and PC
    OS->>PCB_A: Update State to Ready or Blocked
    OS->>PCB_B: Load New PCB
    PCB_B-->>CPU: Restore Registers and PC
    CPU->>CPU: Resume Process B Execution
    Note over CPU,OS: Time wasted = T_context_switch
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Process. List the different states of a process with the help of a state transition diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A process is a program in execution. It is an active entity that includes the program counter, registers, stack, data section, and heap. A process is more than the program code — it is the dynamic execution context of that code.

> **States (1 mark):** The five states of a process are:
> 1. New — Process is being created.
> 2. Ready — Process is waiting in the ready queue to be assigned the CPU.
> 3. Running — Instructions are being executed by the CPU.
> 4. Waiting / Blocked — Process is waiting for an I/O event or signal.
> 5. Terminated — Process has finished execution and is being cleaned up.

> **State Diagram (1 mark):** The transitions are: New → Ready (admission), Ready → Running (dispatch), Running → Ready (preemption), Running → Waiting (I/O request), Waiting → Ready (I/O completion), Running → Terminated (exit).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a process and a thread. Why are threads preferred in IoT edge devices?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Separate address space | Shared address space within a process |
| Creation | Heavy (needs full PCB) | Light (just a TCB) |
| Communication | Needs IPC mechanisms | Direct shared memory access |

> **Why threads in IoT (1.5 marks):** IoT edge devices like ESP32 have very limited RAM (typically **320 KB**). Creating multiple heavy processes would quickly exhaust memory and cause **thrashing**. Threads share the address space and need only a small thread control block, making them **memory-efficient, faster to create, and quicker to context-switch** — all critical for battery-powered, real-time IoT nodes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Process Control Block (PCB)** in detail. List all fields and explain the role of each in process management. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Consider four IoT data-collection processes arriving at a Raspberry Pi gateway with the following arrival and burst times. Compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **FCFS** scheduling. Draw the Gantt chart. **(7 Marks)**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 5 |
| P2 | 2 | 4 |
| P3 | 4 | 2 |
| P4 | 6 | 3 |

---

**Model Solution for (a) — PCB Explanation (7 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Process Control Block is a data structure maintained by the operating system kernel for every process. It contains all the information required to manage, schedule, and resume the process.

> **Fields of the PCB (5 marks):**
> 1. **Process ID (PID):** A unique integer assigned by the OS to identify the process. Example: PID 1245 on a Linux-based IoT gateway.
> 2. **Process State:** Indicates the current state — New, Ready, Running, Waiting, or Terminated.
> 3. **Program Counter (PC):** Holds the address of the next instruction to execute. On a context switch, the PC is saved here.
> 4. **CPU Registers:** Includes the accumulator, base register, general-purpose registers, and stack pointer. Their values must be saved and restored across context switches.
> 5. **Memory Management Information:** Contains base and limit registers, page tables, or segment tables. On an IoT device with Memory Management Unit (MMU), this maps virtual to physical memory.
> 6. **Scheduling Information:** Includes process priority, pointers to the ready queue, and accounting of time waited.
> 7. **Accounting Information:** CPU time consumed, time limits, owner user ID.
> 8. **I/O Status Information:** List of open files, allocated I/O devices, pending I/O operations.

> **Role (1 mark):** The PCB is what allows the OS to **suspend a process and later resume it as if it had never been stopped**. This is the foundation of multiprogramming. On an RTOS like FreeRTOS, the equivalent structure is called the **Task Control Block (TCB)**, but it serves the same purpose.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — FCFS Scheduling (7 Marks):**

> **Step 1 — Gantt Chart (2 marks):**
> Since FCFS executes in arrival order (P1, P2, P3, P4), the Gantt chart is:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Timeline} &: \;\; 0 \quad\quad 5 \quad 9 \quad 11 \quad 14 \\
> \text{Process} &: \;\; \vert\, P1 \;\vert\; \vert\, P2 \;\vert\; \vert\, P3 \;\vert\; \vert\, P4 \;\vert
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2 — Completion Time, TAT, WT (4 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> CT_{P1} &= 0 + 5 = 5 \text{ ms}, & TAT_{P1} &= 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ ms}, & WT_{P1} &= 5 - 5 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
> CT_{P2} &= 5 + 4 = 9 \text{ ms}, & TAT_{P2} &= 9 - 2 = 7 \text{ ms}, & WT_{P2} &= 7 - 4 = 3 \text{ ms} \\
> CT_{P3} &= 9 + 2 = 11 \text{ ms}, & TAT_{P3} &= 11 - 4 = 7 \text{ ms}, & WT_{P3} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
> CT_{P4} &= 11 + 3 = 14 \text{ ms}, & TAT_{P4} &= 14 - 6 = 8 \text{ ms}, & WT_{P4} &= 8 - 3 = 5 \text{ ms}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 3 — Averages (1 mark):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \overline{TAT} &= \frac{5 + 7 + 7 + 8}{4} = \frac{27}{4} = 6.75 \text{ ms} \\
> \overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 5}{4} = \frac{13}{4} = 3.25 \text{ ms}
> \end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Explain the **five-state process model** with a neat state transition diagram. Discuss why the **Blocked state** is essential in IoT gateways that handle both network communication and sensor sampling. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Define **Context Switching**. An IoT edge device CPU operates at **120 MHz**. Each context switch consumes **18 microseconds**. If the system performs **2500 context switches per second**, calculate the **percentage overhead** and comment on whether it is acceptable for a hard real-time application. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Five-State Model (7 Marks):**

> **States and Diagram (4 marks):** [Refer to the state diagram in Section 4.1.] The five states are **New, Ready, Running, Waiting (Blocked), and Terminated**, with transitions driven by the OS scheduler, I/O events, and process exits.

> **Why Blocked state is essential in IoT gateways (3 marks):**
> An IoT gateway typically runs a **sensor sampling task** (reads a DHT22 sensor every 2 seconds) and a **network task** (publishes data to the MQTT broker). The sensor reading involves a hardware I/O wait of approximately 2 ms. If this task remained in the **Ready state** during the I/O wait, the scheduler would waste CPU cycles repeatedly checking if it is ready to run. By moving it to the **Blocked state**, the scheduler can immediately dispatch the network task or the MQTT keep-alive task, thereby **improving throughput and reducing response time** for time-critical cloud events. Without the Blocked state, the gateway would suffer from poor CPU utilization and missed deadlines in the MQTT heartbeat.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Context Switch Overhead (7 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Context switching is the procedure of saving the state of the currently running process (into its PCB) and loading the state of the next process to be executed. It is pure overhead because no useful user work is done during the switch.

> **Step 1 — Total time lost per second (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_{\text{lost}} &= N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{\text{cs}} \\
> &= 2500 \times 18 \,\mu\text{s} \\
> &= 45{,}000 \,\mu\text{s} = 0.045 \text{ s}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2 — Percentage overhead (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> CS_{\%} &= \frac{T_{\text{lost}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
> &= \frac{0.045}{1} \times 100 \\
> &= 4.5\%
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Comment on acceptability (2 marks):**
> A **4.5% overhead** is **marginal** — it is acceptable for **soft real-time** applications such as environmental monitoring or smart agriculture. However, for **hard real-time** applications like industrial control loops, automotive ECUs, or medical ventilators, the total jitter budget is often less than **1%**. In such cases, the overhead is **not acceptable** and the developer must use a **cooperative scheduling** RTOS, increase the time quantum, or merge tasks into fewer threads to reduce switch frequency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing TAT with WT:** Many students write $TAT = CT - BT$, which is **wrong**. The correct formula is $TAT = CT - AT$ and $WT = TAT - BT$. This single mistake can cost 3–4 marks in Part B.
> 2. **Forgetting the FCFS Gantt chart:** Even if your arithmetic is correct, you **must draw the Gantt chart** for full marks. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if it is missing.
> 3. **Not labelling state transitions:** When drawing the state diagram, every arrow **must be labelled** with the event causing the transition (e.g., "I/O completion", "preemption"). An unlabelled diagram gets only 50% credit.
> 4. **Wrong units:** Writing burst time in seconds when it is given in milliseconds (or vice versa) leads to a **full cascade of wrong answers**. Always carry the unit through every step.
> 5. **Mixing up PCB and TCB:** On FreeRTOS, the structure is a **Task Control Block (TCB)**, not a PCB. KTU accepts both terms but you must mention they are equivalent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Processes (KTU Module 1)**
>
> - A **process** is a program in execution; a **program** is a passive file on disk.
> - The **Process Control Block (PCB)** is the OS data structure that holds PID, state, PC, registers, memory info, scheduling info, accounting, and I/O status.
> - The **five states** of a process are: New, Ready, Running, Waiting (Blocked), and Terminated. You **must** be able to draw the labelled state diagram.
> - **Context switching** is the act of saving one process's state and loading another's. It is **pure overhead** with no useful work.
> - **Processes are heavy** (own memory space); **threads are light** (share memory of parent process). IoT devices prefer **threads** because of limited RAM.
> - **FCFS** is simple but suffers from the **convoy effect**.
> - **SJF / SRTF** gives the **minimum average waiting time** but causes **starvation**.
> - **Round Robin** uses a **time quantum $q$** and is the most common algorithm in IoT gateways running FreeRTOS or Linux.
> - **Priority scheduling** is essential for IoT alarm and emergency processes; risk of **starvation** is mitigated by **aging**.
> - **IPC mechanisms**: pipes, message queues, shared memory, sockets, and semaphores. **Shared memory is fastest**; **sockets work across networks**.
> - **TAT formula**: $TAT = CT - AT$
> - **WT formula**: $WT = TAT - BT$
> - **Context switch overhead formula**: $CS_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cs}} \times N_{\text{cs}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100$
> - Always draw a **Gantt chart** before computing scheduling metrics — this is non-negotiable for KTU full marks.
> - For hard real-time IoT, the total scheduling overhead (context switch + scheduler tick) must be **less than 1%** of CPU time.
> - On an RTOS, the PCB equivalent is called the **Task Control Block (TCB)**; remember the keyword *Task* for KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Management** in the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)** refers to the systematic, end-to-end governance of the data lifecycle — encompassing **acquisition, transmission, storage, organization, retrieval, processing, analytics, and secure archival** — generated by heterogeneous sensing devices, actuators, edge nodes, and gateway subsystems within an IoT ecosystem.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, IoT Data Management is the architectural discipline that ensures the **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value (the 5 V's of Big Data)** of sensor-generated data are efficiently transformed into actionable information and knowledge, while maintaining **scalability, latency guarantees, consistency, and security**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> Data Management deals with acquiring, storing, managing, and processing the massive volumes of heterogeneous data generated by IoT devices, using databases, cloud platforms, and analytics engines to convert raw data into meaningful insights.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you run a **smart health monitoring hospital**:

1. **Hundreds of patients** wear smart wristbands that continuously record heart rate, oxygen level, body temperature, and steps.
2. Every second, each wristband produces small numerical readings — this is raw **Data**.
3. These readings arrive at a central server, are stored in **databases**, cleaned, sorted, and grouped (e.g., all heart-rate readings of Patient #42 between 2 PM and 3 PM).
4. A doctor sees a pattern: *"Patient #42's heart rate spiked 40% during exercise"* — this is **Information**.
5. The system, after analyzing 10,000 such patients, concludes: *"Patients who walk less than 3,000 steps/day have a 28% higher cardiac risk"* — this is **Knowledge**.

**Data Management** is the invisible machinery that handles:
- **Capturing** the numbers from the wristbands (Acquisition)
- **Storing** them in warehouses (Storage)
- **Sorting & cleaning** them (Pre-processing)
- **Aggregating & analyzing** them (Analytics)
- **Delivering** insights to the doctor (Presentation)

Without proper Data Management, the hospital would drown in raw numbers and never extract lifesaving insights.

---

### 1.3 The DIKW Hierarchy — Foundation of IoT Data Management

IoT data transformation follows a strict hierarchy, a concept central to KTU Module 1:

| Level | Symbol | Description | Example in IoT |
|:------|:------:|:------------|:---------------|
| **D** | Data | Raw, unprocessed facts | `{"temp": 38.5, "id": "sensor_07"}` |
| **I** | Information | Processed, contextualized data | "Sensor 07 reports 38.5 °C" |
| **K** | Knowledge | Patterns, relationships, rules | "Temperature > 38 °C triggers alert" |
| **W** | Wisdom | Applied knowledge for decisions | Automated HVAC shutoff decision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Data** → Syntactic (raw symbols)
> - **Information** → Semantic (meaning attached)
> - **Knowledge** → Pragmatic (rules and patterns)
> - **Wisdom** → Action-oriented (decisions)

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in IoT Data Management

The following metrics are standardized for IoT data characterization:

- **Data Generation Rate (per device):** typically **1–100 KB/s** for low-end sensors; **MB/s** for cameras/LiDAR.
- **Latency tolerance:** **< 10 ms** (real-time control), **< 1 s** (interactive), **minutes–hours** (analytics).
- **Storage durability target:** **99.999999999% (eleven 9's)** for cloud-grade IoT data stores.
- **Typical compression ratio for time-series:** **3:1 to 10:1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These constants frequently appear in KTU numerical problems and design questions — memorize them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IoT Data Lifecycle — Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

The IoT data lifecycle consists of **six well-defined stages**. Each stage has distinct technologies, challenges, and KTU-exam-relevant characteristics.

#### **Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Sensing Layer)**

- **What:** Raw data capture from physical phenomena via sensors.
- **How:** Sensors (temperature, humidity, motion, chemical) convert physical signals to electrical signals, then to digital via **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)**.
- **Sampling frequency selection:** Governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**:
  
  $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

  where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency and $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the signal.
- **Why it matters:** Under-sampling causes **aliasing**; over-sampling wastes bandwidth and storage.

#### **Stage 2 — Data Pre-processing (Edge / Gateway Layer)**

- **What:** Cleaning, filtering, normalization, noise removal.
- **How:** Techniques include **Kalman filtering**, **moving averages**, **outlier detection (Z-score, IQR)**, and **data imputation**.
- **Why:** Reduces transmitted payload by up to **80%**, lowers bandwidth and energy costs.

#### **Stage 3 — Data Transmission (Network Layer)**

- **What:** Secure transfer from edge to cloud/data center.
- **How:** Protocols — **MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, HTTP/HTTPS, LwM2M**.
- **Why:** Choice of protocol impacts latency, throughput, and battery life.

#### **Stage 4 — Data Storage (Cloud / Data Center Layer)**

- **What:** Persistent storage of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.
- **How:** Selection among **SQL, NoSQL, Time-Series, In-Memory, and Object Storage** databases.
- **Why:** Different query patterns demand different storage engines.

#### **Stage 5 — Data Processing & Analytics (Compute Layer)**

- **What:** Transforming stored data into information and knowledge.
- **How:** **Batch processing** (Hadoop/MapReduce, Spark) vs **Stream processing** (Apache Flink, Kafka Streams).
- **Why:** Real-time needs demand stream engines; historical analysis uses batch.

#### **Stage 6 — Data Presentation & Action (Application Layer)**

- **What:** Dashboards, alerts, ML inference, automated actuation.
- **How:** Visualization tools (Grafana, Kibana), mobile apps, web portals, control systems.
- **Why:** Closes the loop from raw data to physical action.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Unit / Boundary Condition | Used For |
|:-:|:------------------|:----------------------|:--------------------------|:---------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ in Hz | Minimum sampling rate |
| 2 | Data Volume Estimate | $V = N \cdot D \cdot t$ | $V$ = bytes, $N$ = nodes, $D$ = bytes/sensor, $t$ = time | Total storage needed |
| 3 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{V_{original}}{V_{compressed}}$ | Dimensionless, $CR \geq 1$ | Storage efficiency |
| 4 | Throughput Requirement | $T = N \cdot D \cdot SF$ | $T$ in bps; $SF$ = safety factor (e.g., 1.5) | Network bandwidth sizing |
| 5 | Storage with Retention | $S = N \cdot D \cdot t_{retention} \cdot (1 + r)^{t}$ | $r$ = growth rate per year | Long-term capacity planning |
| 6 | Z-Score (Outlier) | $Z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\vert Z \vert > 3$ → outlier | Data cleaning |
| 7 | Latency-bound Storage Cost | $C_{total} = C_{hot} \cdot V_{hot} + C_{cold} \cdot V_{cold}$ | Tiered storage economics | Cloud cost optimization |
| 8 | Mean Squared Error (data quality) | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Units of $y^2$ | Sensor calibration |
| 9 | Information Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | Data compression potential |
| 10 | Aggregation Factor | $AF = \dfrac{N_{raw}}{N_{aggregated}}$ | $AF \geq 1$ | Edge computing efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> The equations involving vertical bars (e.g., the Z-score absolute value) have been written using the `\vert` notation in their original definitions; in the tables above they appear inside math delimiters to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data Management forms the backbone of:
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic, pollution, and energy data ingestion at petabyte scale.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance of turbines, motors, pipelines.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Continuous patient monitoring, anomaly detection.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil-moisture and weather data driving automated irrigation.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Multi-sensor fusion at GB/s data rates requiring sub-millisecond response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 ESE questions, students are frequently asked to **map a given IoT scenario to an appropriate data management technology** (e.g., "Which database for a smart-meter application producing 1 reading/sec from 10 million meters?"). Memorize the strengths of each database category.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Estimating Total Storage Requirement for an IoT Deployment

This is a **classic KTU numerical problem** under the topic of IoT Data Management.

**Problem Setup (typical KTU framing):**

A smart agriculture deployment has **5,000 soil-moisture sensors**. Each sensor generates a **64-bit (8-byte)** measurement every **30 seconds**. The data must be retained in cloud storage for **1 year**. Calculate:

(a) The total volume of raw data generated per day.
(b) The total storage needed for 1 year.
(c) If compression achieves a **3:1 ratio**, what is the actual storage required?

---

### **Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters**

We explicitly enumerate every variable from the problem statement:

$$N = 5{,}000 \text{ sensors}$$

$$D = 8 \text{ bytes/sample}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{30} \text{ samples/second} \quad \text{(since 1 sample every 30 s)}$$

$$t_{day} = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds}$$

$$t_{year} = 365 \times 86{,}400 = 31{,}536{,}000 \text{ seconds}$$

---

### **Step 2 — Derive the Core Formula**

The volume of data generated in any time interval is:

$$V = N \cdot D \cdot f \cdot t$$

This is a direct application of the **data volume equation** from Section 2.2.

- $N$ accounts for the number of parallel producers.
- $D$ accounts for the per-sample byte cost.
- $f \cdot t$ gives the total number of samples per sensor over the time window.

---

### **Step 3 — Solve Part (a): Daily Data Volume**

$$V_{day} = N \cdot D \cdot f \cdot t_{day}$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{day} = 5{,}000 \times 8 \times \frac{1}{30} \times 86{,}400$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$V_{day} = 5{,}000 \times 8 \times 2{,}880$$

$$V_{day} = 40{,}000 \times 2{,}880$$

$$V_{day} = 115{,}200{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

Convert to MB (divide by $10^6$):

$$V_{day} \approx 115.2 \text{ MB/day}$$

---

### **Step 4 — Solve Part (b): Annual Data Volume**

$$V_{year} = V_{day} \times 365$$

$$V_{year} = 115.2 \text{ MB} \times 365$$

$$V_{year} = 42{,}048 \text{ MB/year}$$

Convert to GB:

$$V_{year} \approx 42.05 \text{ GB/year}$$

---

### **Step 5 — Solve Part (c): Storage After 3:1 Compression**

Applying the compression ratio formula:

$$V_{compressed} = \frac{V_{original}}{CR} = \frac{42.05 \text{ GB}}{3}$$

$$V_{compressed} \approx 14.02 \text{ GB/year}$$

---

### **Step 6 — Practical Engineering Interpretation**

For cloud-grade deployment with **replication factor of 3** (for fault tolerance):

$$S_{actual} = V_{compressed} \times 3 \approx 42.05 \text{ GB/year}$$

This illustrates why **tiered storage** (hot SSD + cold archival) is critical in IoT — most of the data is accessed infrequently after 30 days.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Database Selection Matrix (Step-by-Step Decision Process)

This is a **highly-tested KTU conceptual problem**: *"Given an IoT scenario, justify the choice of database."*

The selection is performed via a **four-step analytical matrix**:

**Step 1 — Identify data characteristics**
- Is it structured or unstructured?
- Is it time-stamped (time-series)?
- What is the read/write ratio?

**Step 2 — Identify query patterns**
- Are queries simple key-value lookups?
- Are there complex joins/aggregations?
- Is geospatial querying required?

**Step 3 — Identify non-functional requirements**
- Latency target (ms? seconds? minutes?)
- Horizontal scalability needed?
- Consistency vs. availability trade-off (CAP theorem)?

**Step 4 — Map to appropriate database category**

| Data Profile | Recommended Database | Example System | Justification |
|:-------------|:---------------------|:---------------|:--------------|
| Time-series sensor readings | **Time-Series DB** | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB | Optimized for append-only, timestamp-indexed writes |
| Unstructured images/video | **Object Storage** | AWS S3, MinIO | Blob storage with metadata tags |
| Relational user/device records | **SQL DB** | PostgreSQL, MySQL | ACID transactions, complex joins |
| Flexible JSON payloads | **Document DB** | MongoDB, CouchDB | Schema-less, JSON-native |
| Graph relationships | **Graph DB** | Neo4j | Device–user–location–event relations |
| Real-time KV lookups | **In-memory KV** | Redis, Memcached | Sub-millisecond latency |
| Wide-column telemetry | **Column-family DB** | Cassandra, HBase | Massive write throughput |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, marks are awarded for **explicit justification** — not just naming a database. Always state *why* the database matches the workload.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT Data Management — End-to-End Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PERCEPTION["PERCEPTION LAYER (Sensors & Actuators)"]
        sensor1[Temperature Sensor]
        sensor2[Humidity Sensor]
        sensor3[Camera Stream]
        sensor4[Motion Detector]

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE / GATEWAY LAYER (Pre-processing)"]

        sqlDb[SQL Database]

    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTE LAYER (Processing and Analytics)"]
```

---

### 4.2 Data Transformation Flow — From Raw Sensor Reading to Actionable Wisdom

```mermaid

    A[Raw Sensor Data] -->|Acquisition| B[Data]
    B -->|Cleaning and Contextualization| C[Information]
    C -->|Pattern Mining| D[Knowledge]
    D -->|Decision Logic| E[Wisdom or Action]

    style A fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#ca8,color:#000

```

---

### 4.3 Database Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New IoT Data Workload] --> q1{Time-stamped and append-only?}
    q1 -- Yes --> ts[Time-Series DB: InfluxDB or TimescaleDB]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Strong relational schema and ACID needed?}
    q2 -- Yes --> sql[SQL DB: PostgreSQL or MySQL]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Unstructured or flexible schema?}
    q3 -- Yes --> nosql[Document DB: MongoDB]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Sub-millisecond KV lookups?}
    q4 -- Yes --> kv[In-Memory KV: Redis]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Complex device and relationship traversals?}
    q5 -- Yes --> graph[Graph DB: Neo4j]
    q5 -- No --> col[Wide-Column DB: Cassandra]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Data, Information, and Knowledge with a suitable IoT-based example. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | IoT Example |
|:-----|:-----------|:------------|
| **Data** | Raw, unprocessed facts without context | Sensor reads: `23.5, 24.1, 22.9` |
| **Information** | Data with meaning, context, and structure | "Room temperature varied between 22.9 °C and 24.1 °C between 2 PM and 3 PM" |
| **Knowledge** | Patterns and rules derived from information | "Temperature drops by ~1.2 °C when AC is switched ON" |

*(1 mark for each correct definition + example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain the 5 V's of Big Data in the context of IoT. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Volume** — Massive scale of data; e.g., a smart city generates **TBs/day**.
2. **Velocity** — Speed of data generation and processing; e.g., autonomous vehicles produce **GB/s**.
3. **Variety** — Heterogeneous formats (text, image, video, JSON).
4. **Veracity** — Trustworthiness and quality of sensor data.
5. **Value** — Useful insights extracted for decision-making.

*(0.5 marks per V + 0.5 for the IoT context.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3. (a) — Choice A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the IoT Data Management lifecycle with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IoT Data Management lifecycle comprises **six sequential stages**:

**1. Data Acquisition (Perception Layer):**
Sensors (temperature, humidity, motion, cameras) and actuators convert physical phenomena into digital signals using **ADCs**. Sampling follows the **Nyquist criterion** $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$.

**2. Pre-processing (Edge Layer):**
At edge gateways, data is **filtered, normalized, and compressed**. Techniques: Kalman filtering, Z-score outlier detection (Equation: $Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$), moving-average smoothing. This reduces network load by **60–80%**.

**3. Transmission (Network Layer):**
Protocols — **MQTT** (lightweight pub/sub), **CoAP** (constrained REST), **AMQP** (queuing), **HTTP/HTTPS** (web). Choice depends on bandwidth, energy, and latency.

**4. Storage (Persistence Layer):**
Selection among SQL, NoSQL, time-series, in-memory, and object stores — based on access pattern, query type, and retention policy.

**5. Processing & Analytics (Compute Layer):**
Two paradigms:
- **Batch processing** (Apache Spark, Hadoop) — historical, high-throughput.
- **Stream processing** (Apache Flink, Kafka Streams) — real-time, low-latency.

**6. Presentation & Action (Application Layer):**
Dashboards (Grafana, Kibana), alert systems, mobile apps, ML-based actuation.

> [!NOTE]
> *Marks distribution: 1 mark per stage definition + 1 mark for role + 1 mark overall integration.*

---

**(b) Compare SQL, NoSQL, and Time-Series databases for IoT workloads. Justify which would be best suited for a **smart-meter** application producing 1 reading per second from 10 million meters. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SQL (PostgreSQL) | NoSQL Document (MongoDB) | Time-Series (InfluxDB) |
|:----------|:-----------------|:-------------------------|:------------------------|
| Data Model | Rigid schema, tables | Flexible JSON | Optimized for time-stamped rows |
| Write Throughput | Moderate | High | Very High (millions/sec) |
| Query Language | SQL | MQL / Aggregation pipeline | InfluxQL / Flux |
| Time-Series Features | None native | Limited | First-class (downsampling, retention policies) |
| Scaling | Vertical (primary) | Horizontal (sharding) | Horizontal (sharding + compression) |
| Use Case | User accounts, billing | Mixed telemetry, metadata | Sensor streams, metrics |

**Justification for Smart-Meter Scenario:**

- **10 million meters × 1 reading/sec = 10 million inserts/sec** — extremely write-heavy.
- Data is **append-only** with a timestamp → classic time-series workload.
- Need **downsampling** (e.g., keep 1-min averages, archive hourly).
- **InfluxDB or TimescaleDB** is optimal due to:
  - High write throughput.
  - Built-in compression (TSM engine).
  - Retention policies (automatic data aging).
  - Continuous queries for aggregations.

SQL DBs would become a bottleneck; NoSQL document stores lack native time-series operators. Hence, **Time-Series DB is the correct KTU answer**.

*(4 marks comparison + 3 marks justification.)*

---

### ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q3. (b) — Choice B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Describe the challenges of Big Data management in IoT. Explain how cloud and edge computing address them. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Challenges:**

1. **Volume & Scalability** — Petabyte-scale data overwhelms traditional storage.
2. **Velocity** — Real-time ingestion from millions of devices.
3. **Variety** — Structured + unstructured data formats.
4. **Veracity** — Noisy, missing, or duplicated sensor readings.
5. **Network bandwidth** — Limited by last-mile connectivity.
6. **Security & privacy** — Sensitive data in transit and at rest.
7. **Power constraints** — Sensor nodes are battery-limited.
8. **Latency** — Cloud round-trip may exceed control-loop deadlines.

**Cloud Computing Solutions:**
- **Elastic storage** (S3, Azure Blob) handles volume.
- **Stream engines** (Kafka, Kinesis) address velocity.
- **Managed ML services** extract value from variety.
- **Replicated, encrypted storage** ensures security.

**Edge Computing Solutions:**
- **Local filtering** reduces transmitted volume → saves bandwidth.
- **On-device ML inference** reduces latency.
- **Local aggregation** lowers cloud storage cost.
- **Time-sensitive actuation** without cloud round-trip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answers, always conclude with a statement such as: *"A hybrid edge–cloud architecture combines the best of both: edge for latency-critical, bandwidth-limited tasks; cloud for heavy analytics and long-term storage."*

---

**(b) A smart-city traffic monitoring system deploys **2,000 cameras** each producing **200 KB/s** of image data. Estimate the daily data volume in GB and recommend a suitable storage architecture. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$$N = 2{,}000 \text{ cameras}, \quad D = 200 \text{ KB/s}, \quad t_{day} = 86{,}400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 1 — Total data rate:**
$$R = N \cdot D = 2{,}000 \times 200 \text{ KB/s} = 400{,}000 \text{ KB/s} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 2 — Daily volume:**
$$V_{day} = R \cdot t_{day} = 400 \text{ MB/s} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s}$$

$$V_{day} = 34{,}560{,}000 \text{ MB/day}$$

$$V_{day} \approx 34.56 \text{ TB/day}$$

**Step 3 — Annual volume:**
$$V_{year} \approx 34.56 \times 365 \approx 12{,}614 \text{ TB/year} \approx 12.6 \text{ PB/year}$$

**Step 4 — Recommended Storage Architecture:**

A **tiered, hybrid edge–cloud architecture**:

| Tier | Storage | Data Type | Justification |
|:-----|:--------|:----------|:--------------|
| **Edge** | Local SSD / NVMe | Last 24 hours of raw video | Low-latency access for incident response |
| **Hot Cloud** | Object Storage (S3 Standard) | Last 30 days | Frequent ML inference access |
| **Cold Cloud** | Object Storage (S3 Glacier) | 1+ year retention | Cheap archival for forensics |

**Additional Recommendations:**
- Use **H.265/AV1 compression** (CR ≈ 10:1) to reduce 34.56 TB → ~3.5 TB/day.
- Apply **metadata tagging** (timestamp, camera ID, location) for searchability.
- Use **distributed file systems** (HDFS) or **data lakes** (Delta Lake, Iceberg) for analytics.

*(3 marks formula + 2 marks substitution + 2 marks architecture reasoning.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse KB with KiB / MB with MiB.** Use SI units (1 MB = 10⁶ bytes) unless the question explicitly uses binary prefixes.
> 2. **Always state the assumption** for compression ratio — examiners deduct marks if not mentioned.
> 3. **Database selection questions require justification**, not just naming — "InfluxDB" alone is insufficient; you must explain *why* (write throughput, time-series features, retention).
> 4. **Do not write Python/C code** in data management theory answers — KTU expects formulas, diagrams, and prose only.
> 5. **Avoid using `|` inside markdown tables** — it breaks the table renderer. Use `\vert` for absolute values in math.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Data Management = full lifecycle** of IoT data — acquisition → preprocessing → transmission → storage → processing → presentation.
- **DIKW pyramid** is the foundation: Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom.
- **Big Data 5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — must be quoted in any "Big Data in IoT" answer.
- **Nyquist criterion**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — undersampling causes aliasing.
- **Storage volume equation**: $V = N \cdot D \cdot f \cdot t$ — memorize and apply to KTU numericals.
- **Database selection** depends on:
  - Data structure (time-series → TS-DB; relational → SQL; flexible → Document DB; relationships → Graph DB).
  - Access pattern (read-heavy vs write-heavy, latency requirements).
  - Scale (horizontal vs vertical).
- **Edge vs Cloud**:
  - **Edge** → low latency, bandwidth saving, local decisions.
  - **Cloud** → heavy analytics, long-term storage, ML training.
  - KTU prefers **hybrid edge–cloud** as the modern answer.
- **Compression** (3:1 to 10:1 typical) and **replication (factor 3)** multiply effective storage cost — always include in capacity planning.
- **CAP theorem** trade-offs apply to distributed IoT databases — choose **AP (availability + partition tolerance)** for most sensor workloads.
- **Tiered storage** (hot/warm/cold) is the standard cloud IoT pattern — used to optimize cost vs access latency.
- **Final mantra for KTU exams:** *Define → Diagram → Derive → Justify → Conclude.* Five-step structure guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Course Code:** PBITT604
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security
> - **Semester:** B.Tech S6 (2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Aligned)
> - **Target Outcome:** CO1 / CO2 – Apply IoT design principles and reason about foundational security primitives for constrained devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of IoT Security

## 1.1 What is "Security" in the IoT Context?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBITT604 – Internet of Things)** syllabus, the term *Security* refers to the collection of **cryptographic, architectural, and operational mechanisms** that protect an IoT system from intentional malicious interference. Formally, IoT security is the discipline of ensuring **confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, availability, and privacy** across heterogeneous, often resource-constrained, networked devices (sensors, actuators, gateways, and cloud backends).

The standard international reference used to formalize these goals is the **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)**, extended in IoT to include the **AAA model (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)** and **non-repudiation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *"IoT Security is the set of policies, protocols, and cryptographic mechanisms that ensure secure data exchange among connected things, the network, and the cloud, while preserving data CIA, device identity, and user privacy under resource-constrained environments."*

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of IoT Security

| Pillar | Formal Name | What it Protects |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | Confidentiality | Prevents eavesdropping of sensor data |
| **I** | Integrity | Detects tampering of messages in transit |
| **A** | Authenticity | Verifies the true identity of the device/user |
| **Av** | Availability | Defends against DoS / jamming / battery-drain attacks |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Smart Letterbox"

Imagine an ordinary letterbox on a wall. Now imagine that the letterbox is a **smart IoT sensor** that:
- Accepts letters (sensor data)
- Opens only for the postman (authentication)
- Seals letters inside an envelope (encryption → confidentiality)
- Has a wax seal (MAC/digital signature → integrity)
- Reports back to your phone via Wi-Fi (network layer)
- Has tiny battery (constrained device)

> **Intuitive Mapping:**
> - **Confidentiality** = the envelope hides the letter's content.
> - **Integrity** = the wax seal proves no one opened it.
> - **Authentication** = your house key proves you are the real owner.
> - **Availability** = a strong wall so thieves cannot throw it down (DoS).
> - **Privacy** = the postman does not read your bank statement before delivering it.

## 1.4 The IoT Security Attack Surface (High-Level)

IoT systems expose **three horizontal attack surfaces**:

1. **Perception / Device Layer** — physical tampering, side-channel, firmware extraction.
2. **Network / Transport Layer** — eavesdropping, MITM, replay, routing attacks.
3. **Application / Cloud Layer** — API abuse, data leakage, weak identity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Keywords to Memorize:** *Constrained device, CIA Triad, MITM (Man-in-the-Middle), Replay Attack, Side-Channel, Firmware Tampering, PKI (Public Key Infrastructure), Lightweight Cryptography.*

## 1.5 Formal Threat Model — STRIDE

Microsoft's **STRIDE** taxonomy (used in KTU board questions) categorises threats:

- **S** — Spoofing identity
- **T** — Tampering with data
- **R** — Repudiation
- **I** — Information disclosure
- **D** — Denial of service
- **E** — Elevation of privilege

Every IoT security countermeasure addresses one or more STRIDE categories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Defense — "Defense in Depth" Architecture

IoT security is **never** a single feature. KTU expects you to memorise the **four-layer IoT security stack**:

1. **Perceptual / Sensing Layer Security** – secure boot, hardware root-of-trust, physical tamper detection, lightweight cipher on the MCU.
2. **Network Layer Security** – DTLS, TLS 1.3, IPSec, 6LoWPAN security, IEEE 802.15.4 security suites.
3. **Transport / Middleware Security** – CoAPs (CoAP over DTLS), MQTT over TLS, AMQP over TLS.
4. **Application / Cloud Layer Security** – OAuth 2.0, JWT, API gateways, attribute-based access control (ABAC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU 14-mark answers:** "No single layer is sufficient — IoT security is built *defense in depth*."

## 2.2 Cryptographic Primitives Used in IoT

### 2.2.1 Symmetric Key Cryptography
Both parties share **one secret key** $K$.
- **Block ciphers:** AES-128, AES-256, PRESENT, CLEFIA (lightweight).
- **Stream ciphers:** ChaCha20, Trivium, Grain.
- **Modes of operation:** CBC, CTR, GCM (AEAD).

Encryption is a deterministic function:
$$C = E_K(P)$$
$$P = D_K(C) = E_K^{-1}(C)$$

### 2.2.2 Asymmetric Key Cryptography (Public-Key)
Each entity holds a key pair $(K_{\text{pub}}, K_{\text{priv}})$.
- **Algorithms suitable for IoT:** ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), RSA (legacy), NTRU (post-quantum).
- **Use cases:** Key exchange (ECDH), digital signatures (ECDSA, Ed25519).

Public-key encryption and digital signature:
$$C = E_{K_{\text{pub}}^{\text{rec}}}(P), \quad P = D_{K_{\text{priv}}^{\text{rec}}}(C)$$
$$\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{\text{priv}}^{\text{send}}}(M), \quad \text{Verify}_{K_{\text{pub}}^{\text{send}}}(\sigma) = \text{True/False}$$

### 2.2.3 Cryptographic Hash Functions
A hash is a one-way function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ with three security properties:
1. **Pre-image resistance** — given $h$, hard to find $m$ s.t. $H(m)=h$.
2. **Second pre-image resistance** — given $m_1$, hard to find $m_2 \neq m_1$ s.t. $H(m_1)=H(m_2)$.
3. **Collision resistance** — hard to find any $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1)=H(m_2)$.

Standard hash families:
- **MD5 (broken)** – 128-bit, collision-broken since 2004.
- **SHA-1 (deprecated)** – 160-bit, broken in 2017 (SHAttered).
- **SHA-2 family** – SHA-224/256/384/512.
- **SHA-3 / Keccak** – NIST 2015 winner; sponge construction; excellent for hardware.
- **Lightweight hashes** – PHOTON, SPONGENT.

### 2.2.4 Message Authentication Codes (MAC)
A MAC attaches a tag $\tau$ to a message $M$ using key $K$:
$$\tau = \text{MAC}_K(M)$$
Receiver recomputes and compares. Modern variant: **HMAC** (Hash-based MAC), still secure even if underlying hash has weak collision resistance.

### 2.2.5 Digital Signatures
Provide **integrity + authenticity + non-repudiation** without a shared secret.
- **ECDSA** – 256-bit curve provides ~128-bit security; signature size ~64 bytes.
- **Ed25519** – fast, compact, used in many IoT protocols.
- **RSA-2048** – 256-byte signature; widely used in PKI.

## 2.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)

PKI binds a public key to an identity through a **digital certificate** signed by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.

Certificate structure (X.509):
$$\text{Cert} = \text{Sign}_{K_{\text{priv}}^{\text{CA}}}(\text{Subject} \;\Vert\; K_{\text{pub}} \;\Vert\; \text{Meta})$$

PKI enables:
- Trust bootstrapping on first connection.
- Certificate revocation (CRL / OCSP).
- Mutual authentication between constrained devices.

## 2.4 Lightweight Cryptography (Mandatory IoT Topic)

Standard AES-128 needs ~3,000 GE (gate equivalents) and 1012 cycles/block, which is **too heavy** for a Class-0 IoT node (≤ 10 KB RAM, ≤ 100 KB ROM).

**NIST Lightweight Cryptography Standard (2023):** **Ascon** is the winner.
- AEAD cipher, hash family, and XOF.
- Suited to RFID, smart meters, medical implants.
- Tiny hardware footprint (~2,570 GE).

## 2.5 Common IoT Attack Families

| Attack | Target Layer | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | Network | TLS / DTLS / encryption |
| MITM | Network | Mutual authentication, certificate pinning |
| Replay | Network | Nonce + timestamp + session ID |
| DoS / DDoS | Network/Cloud | Rate limiting, IDS, fog offloading |
| Sybil | Application | Identity verification, trust scores |
| Firmware tampering | Device | Secure boot, signed firmware |
| Side-channel | Device | Constant-time code, masking |
| Physical attack | Device | Tamper-proof enclosure, TPM |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the single most-tested reference for KTU Module-1 Security numericals.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetric encryption | $C = E_K(P)$ | Shared key $K$ |
| 2 | Public-key encryption | $C = E_{K_{\text{pub}}}(P)$ | Receiver's public key |
| 3 | Digital signature | $\sigma = \text{Sig}_{K_{\text{priv}}}(H(M))$ | Sign on hash of $M$ |
| 4 | Hash output length | $n = 128/160/256/512$ bits | SHA-256 gives 256-bit digest |
| 5 | AES block | $b = 128$ bits | Key $K \in \{128,192,256\}$ bits |
| 6 | RSA security | Security $\approx 2^{k/2}$ for $k$-bit modulus | Use $\geq 2048$ bits |
| 7 | ECC security | 256-bit curve $\approx$ 128-bit AES | Much smaller key than RSA |
| 8 | HMAC | $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H((K \oplus opad) \,\Vert\, H((K \oplus ipad) \,\Vert\, M))$ | ipad/opad = 0x36 / 0x5C |
| 9 | Birthday bound | Collisions appear after $\approx 2^{n/2}$ hashes | For $n=128$, $2^{64}$ work |
| 10 | Shannon entropy | $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ bits | Measures key uncertainty |
| 11 | Ascon AEAD rate | $p$ bits/block processed | NIST 2023 winner |
| 12 | DTLS handshake | $\approx 1.5 \times$ RTT (vs TLS 1.2) | CoAPs uses DTLS 1.2/1.3 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Meters:** AES-128 CCM mode + asymmetric key pre-provisioning at manufacture.
- **Healthcare IoT (pacemakers, insulin pumps):** Ascon lightweight AEAD to protect 1–2 kbps data without draining battery.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** IEC 62443 standard requires zone-based defense in depth and signed firmware.
- **Connected Vehicles (V2X):** ECDSA signatures in CAM/DENM messages with rotating pseudonyms (non-repudiation + privacy).
- **Smart Home:** Matter protocol mandates DTLS 1.3 + X.509 device attestation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Avalanche Effect of a Hash Function

The **avalanche effect** states that flipping a single bit of the input changes, on average, **50 % of the output bits**. KTU frequently asks for a numerical proof.

Let input $M$ be a 256-bit message. Define
$$M' = M \oplus \Delta$$
where $\Delta$ is a single-bit flip at position $i$, and $H: \{0,1\}^{256} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$ is a 256-bit hash (e.g., SHA-256).

Let $d(H(M), H(M'))$ denote the **Hamming distance** between the two digests. The avalanche effect requires:
$$\mathbb{E}[d(H(M), H(M'))] = \frac{n}{2}$$
where $n = 256$.

For **SHA-256**, the empirical expectation measured over $10^6$ random messages is:
$$\mathbb{E}[d] \approx 127.999 \;\approx\; 128 = \frac{256}{2}$$

Therefore SHA-256 satisfies the strict avalanche criterion (SAC), making it suitable for IoT integrity checks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** "MD5 is 128-bit, so why is it unsafe?" → Answer: Although $n/2 = 64$ avalanche bits look fine, **collisions** can be generated in $\approx 2^{24}$ operations, far below the $2^{64}$ birthday bound. So MD5 is *cryptographically broken*, not avalanche-broken.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — RSA Encryption-Decryption Pair (Toy Example)

> KTU frequently includes an RSA numerical. We derive it fully so the model is correct.

**Step 1 — Choose two primes.**
$$p = 61, \qquad q = 53$$

**Step 2 — Compute modulus and totient.**
$$n = p \cdot q = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$

**Step 3 — Choose public exponent.**
$$e = 17 \quad \text{(commonly } 65537 \text{ in practice)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute private exponent.**
We need $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e.
$$17 d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Using the extended Euclidean algorithm:
$$\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$$
Apply Bezout's identity:
$$17 \cdot 2753 - 3120 \cdot 15 = 1$$

Hence:
$$d = 2753$$

**Step 5 — Verify.**
$$\left(17 \times 2753\right) \bmod 3120 = 46801 \bmod 3120 = 1 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Encrypt a plaintext block.**
Let $M = 65$. Ciphertext:
$$C = M^e \bmod n = 65^{17} \bmod 3233$$

Compute $65^{17}$ modulo 3233 using repeated squaring:

$$65^1 \bmod 3233 = 65$$
$$65^2 \bmod 3233 = 4225 \bmod 3233 = 992$$
$$65^4 \bmod 3233 = 992^2 \bmod 3233 = 984064 \bmod 3233 = 536$$
$$65^8 \bmod 3233 = 536^2 \bmod 3233 = 287296 \bmod 3233 = 2870$$
$$65^{16} \bmod 3233 = 2870^2 \bmod 3233 = 8236900 \bmod 3233 = 2790$$

Now combine for exponent $17 = 16 + 1$:
$$C = 65^{16} \cdot 65^1 \bmod 3233 = 2790 \times 65 \bmod 3233$$
$$C = 181350 \bmod 3233 = 2790 \times 65 = 181350$$
$$181350 \div 3233 \approx 56.09$$
$$181350 - 56 \times 3233 = 181350 - 181048 = 302$$

Hence:
$$C = 302$$

**Step 7 — Decrypt to recover plaintext.**
$$M = C^d \bmod n = 302^{2753} \bmod 3233$$

By Euler's theorem, since $\gcd(M,n)=1$ and $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, the inverse holds:
$$M \equiv C^d \equiv M^{e d} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \pmod{n} = 65 \;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real IoT caveat:** RSA-2048 produces 256-byte signatures that **bloat** every CoAP packet. Modern IoT deployments use **ECDSA-P256**, where a 64-byte signature achieves the same 128-bit security.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Shannon Entropy of an IoT Key

A smart sensor is provisioned with a 128-bit AES key. The key is chosen uniformly at random, so:
$$p(x_i) = \frac{1}{2^{128}} \quad \forall i \in \{1, \dots, 2^{128}\}$$

Shannon entropy:
$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{2^{128}} \frac{1}{2^{128}} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{2^{128}}\right)$$
$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{2^{128}} \frac{1}{2^{128}} \cdot (-128)$$
$$H(X) = 128 \;\text{bits}$$

So a uniformly random 128-bit key carries **128 bits of entropy** — equivalent to a perfectly unguessable secret. This is exactly the security level required by AES-128.

## 3.4 Worked Derivation #4 — HMAC Construction

HMAC is the standard MAC used in IoT (e.g., inside TLS 1.3 record layer). Given hash $H$, key $K$, and message $M$:

$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\!\left((K \oplus opad) \,\Vert\, H\!\left((K \oplus ipad) \,\Vert\, M\right)\right)$$

where:
$$ipad = 0x36 \;\text{repeated}\; b/8 \;\text{times},\quad opad = 0x5C \;\text{repeated}\; b/8 \;\text{times}$$
and $b$ is the hash internal block size (512 bits for SHA-256, 1024 for SHA-512).

**KTU-Memorable Point:** HMAC remains secure even if the underlying hash function $H$ loses collision resistance, as long as $H$ still resists pre-image attacks. This is why HMAC-SHA1 is still permitted in IoT, even though plain SHA-1 is deprecated.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping STRIDE → IoT Countermeasure

| STRIDE Threat | Real IoT Attack | Countermeasure | KTU-Friendly Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** Spoofing | Fake sensor injecting wrong data | Mutual TLS, ECDSA, device certificates | MQTT client certificate |
| **T** Tampering | Modifying firmware over the air | Signed firmware (Ed25519), secure boot | IoT device rejects unsigned image |
| **R** Repudiation | Device denies sending malicious command | Non-repudiation via digital signature | PLC command logged with ECDSA |
| **I** Information disclosure | Eavesdropping on 6LoWPAN | AES-CCM-128 link-layer encryption | IEEE 802.15.4 security suite |
| **D** Denial of service | Battery-drain attack on sensor | Rate limiting, authentication before processing | Gateway drops unauth packets |
| **E** Elevation of privilege | Smart bulb becomes LAN pivot | RBAC, scoped tokens (OAuth 2.0) | Matter device attestation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Security Architecture — Layered View

The figure below maps the **four-layer IoT security stack** with the active cryptographic primitive at each tier.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef layer fill:#f0f7ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef primitive fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#a64b00,color:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef attack fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#a60000,color:#000,stroke-width:1px

    A["Perception Layer\nSensors and Actuators"]:::layer
    B["Network Layer\n6LoWPAN, WiFi, BLE, LoRaWAN"]:::layer
    C["Transport Layer\nCoAP, MQTT, AMQP"]:::layer
    D["Application Layer\nCloud, Mobile, Web"]:::layer

    A1["Secure Boot\nTPM, Ascon AEAD\nSide-channel hardening"]:::primitive
    B1["IEEE 802.15.4 Security\nDTLS 1.3\nIPSec"]:::primitive
    C1["TLS 1.3 Record Layer\nHMAC SHA 256\nAES 256 GCM"]:::primitive
    D1["OAuth 2.0, JWT\nRBAC ABAC\nAudit logs"]:::primitive

    T1["Physical tampering\nSide channel\nFirmware theft"]:::attack
    T2["Eavesdropping MITM\nReplay, Sybil\nRouting attack"]:::attack
    T3["Session hijack\nDoS on broker"]:::attack
    T4["API abuse\nData exfiltration\nWeak credentials"]:::attack

    A --- A1
    B --- B1
    C --- C1
    D --- D1

    T1 --> A
    T2 --> B
    T3 --> C
    T4 --> D
```

## 4.2 PKI / Digital Certificate Workflow

The diagram shows how a **Certificate Authority (CA)** issues a certificate, and how an IoT device proves its identity during a **DTLS handshake**.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IoT as IoT Device
    participant CA as Certificate Authority
    participant GW as Gateway Server

    IoT->>CA: CSR = (Kpub_iot, identity)
    CA->>CA: Verify manufacturer identity
    CA->>IoT: Cert_iot = Sig_Kpriv_CA( Kpub_iot || identity )
    Note over IoT,CA: Certificate is provisioned at factory

    IoT->>GW: ClientHello
    GW->>IoT: ServerHello + Cert_gw
    IoT->>IoT: Verify Cert_gw against root CA
    IoT->>GW: Cert_iot + ECDHE key share
    GW->>IoT: Verify Cert_iot + accept
    Note over IoT,GW: Mutual authentication done. Session keys derived via HKDF
```

## 4.3 IoT Cryptographic Stack — Protocol-to-Primitive Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef proto fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef prim fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000,stroke-width:1px

    P1["CoAP"]:::proto
    P2["MQTT"]:::proto
    P3["AMQP"]:::proto
    P4["LwM2M"]:::proto
    P5["Matter"]:::proto

    Q1["DTLS 1.3\nECDHE + AES 128 GCM"]:::prim
    Q2["TLS 1.3\nECDSA + ChaCha20 Poly1305"]:::prim
    Q3["TLS 1.3 + SASL\nRSA OAEP"]:::prim
    Q4["DTLS + OSCORE\nAscon 128 AEAD"]:::prim
    Q5["Wi-SUN and Thread\nP 384 and Ed25519"]:::prim

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3
    P4 --> Q4
    P5 --> Q5
```

## 4.4 Fallback Block Diagram — Secure Boot Chain of Trust

When physical drawings are infeasible, the figure below captures the **chain-of-trust boot flow** for an IoT MCU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef hw fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fw fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef os fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef app fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000,stroke-width:1px

    R["Immutable ROM\nRoot Key burned in factory"]:::hw
    BL["Bootloader Stage 1\nVerifies Stage 2 signature"]:::fw
    BL2["Bootloader Stage 2\nVerifies OS image signature"]:::fw
    OS["Trusted OS / RTOS\nMeasured boot, TPM PCR extend"]:::os
    APP["Application Firmware\nSigned with Ed25519"]:::app

    R --> BL
    BL --> BL2
    BL2 --> OS
    OS --> APP
```
