> **Why this matters for KTU:** Direct numerical questions on Cisco's Value at Stake and its decomposition into 7-8 sub-categories are **highly likely** in 14-mark questions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IoE Connection Growth Model

The growth in connected devices over time can be modeled exponentially (Cisco projection):

**Step 1 — Define base year and count.**

$$N(2013) = 10 \times 10^9 \text{ connections}$$

**Step 2 — Apply exponential growth with rate $r$ (Cisco used ~17% CAGR).**

$$N(t) = N(2013) \cdot e^{r(t-2013)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate for 2020.**

$$N(2020) = 10 \times 10^9 \cdot e^{0.17 \times 7}$$

$$N(2020) = 10 \times 10^9 \cdot e^{1.19}$$

$$N(2020) \approx 10 \times 10^9 \cdot 3.287$$

$$N(2020) \approx 32.87 \times 10^9 \approx 33 \text{ billion}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate for 2025 (Cisco forecast).**

$$N(2025) \approx 50 \text{ billion connections}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Each step multiplies the previous year's connection count by a growth factor. Cisco's actual public numbers: **2013: 10B, 2015: 25B, 2020: 33B, 2025: ~50B**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoE Four-Pillars Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeP["PEOPLE - Wearables, Smartphones, Users"]
    nodePr["PROCESS - AI, Automation, Workflows, Orchestration"]
    nodeD["DATA - Raw, Information, Knowledge, Wisdom"]
    nodeT["THINGS - Sensors, Actuators, Devices, RFID, Cameras"]
    
    nodeP <--> nodePr
    nodePr <--> nodeD
    nodeD <--> nodeT
    nodeT <--> nodeP
    nodeP <--> nodeD
    nodePr <--> nodeT
    
    nodeCore["INTERNET OF EVERYTHING - INTELLIGENT UNIFIED NETWORK"]
    
    nodeP --> nodeCore
    nodePr --> nodeCore
    nodeD --> nodeCore
    nodeT --> nodeCore
```

### 4.2 IoE Functional Architecture Flow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    sens1["Sensor Layer - Physical World"]
    net1["Network Layer - Connectivity"]
    edge1["Edge / Fog Layer - Local Processing"]
    cloud1["Cloud Layer - Big Data and AI"]
    proc1["Process Layer - Decision Engine"]
    act1["Action Layer - Actuator and User Feedback"]
    
    sens1 --> net1
    net1 --> edge1
    edge1 --> cloud1
    cloud1 --> proc1
    proc1 --> act1
    act1 --> sens1
```

### 4.3 DIKW Pyramid (Data → Wisdom Transformation)

```mermaid
graph TB
    d1["DATA - Raw signals, unprocessed facts"]
    d2["INFORMATION - Contextualized and organized data"]
    d3["KNOWLEDGE - Patterns, rules, relationships"]
    d4["WISDOM - Actionable, predictive decisions"]
    
    d1 --> d2
    d2 --> d3
    d3 --> d4
```

### 4.4 IoT vs IoE Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    iotBox["IoT - THINGS connected to THINGS"]
    ioeBox["IoE - PEOPLE, PROCESS, DATA, THINGS - All Connected Intelligently"]
    
    iotBox -->|Evolutionary Expansion| ioeBox
    
    sub1["Smart Home, Wearables, Sensors"]
    sub2["Smart Cities, Industry 4.0, Healthcare, Energy"]
    
    sub1 --> iotBox
    sub2 --> ioeBox
```

### 4.5 IoE Use-Case Map Across Industries

```mermaid
graph TB
    center["INTERNET OF EVERYTHING PLATFORM"]
    
    center --> s1["Smart Healthcare - Patient Monitoring, Telemedicine"]
    center --> s2["Smart Manufacturing - Predictive Maintenance, IIoE"]
    center --> s3["Smart Cities - Traffic, Lighting, Waste Mgmt"]
    center --> s4["Smart Agriculture - Drones, Soil Sensors"]
    center --> s5["Smart Retail - Beacons, Dynamic Pricing"]
    center --> s6["Smart Energy - Smart Grid, Smart Meters"]
    center --> s7["Smart Transport - Autonomous Vehicles, Fleet"]
    center --> s8["Smart Education - Connected Classrooms"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question often expects either a hand-drawn 4-pillar diagram or a layered architecture diagram. The above Mermaid block representations can be directly converted to a labeled diagram in your answer sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]**  
**Define Internet of Everything (IoE). List its four pillars.**

**Model Answer:**

Internet of Everything (IoE) is an intelligent network that integrates people, process, data, and things into a unified system to create more meaningful and valuable connections than any of these entities could achieve independently.

The four pillars are:
1. **People** – Endpoints that generate and consume data
2. **Process** – Delivery of right data to the right entity at the right time
3. **Data** – Raw facts refined into information and wisdom
4. **Things** – Physical devices, sensors, and actuators

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (2) + 4 pillars listing (1)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Differentiate between Internet of Things (IoT) and Internet of Everything (IoE).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IoT | IoE |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | M2M communication | Connects P, D, T, Pr |
| Focus | Device connectivity | Value creation from connections |
| Pillars | 1 (Things) | 4 |
| Data Use | Collected | Converted to wisdom |
| Examples | Smart fridge | Smart hospital, smart city |

IoT is essentially a **subset** of IoE.

> **Mark Distribution:** Comparison table (3)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a).** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Explain the four pillars of Internet of Everything in detail with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
IoE, a concept popularized by Cisco in 2013, represents the convergence of four key pillars: People, Process, Data, and Things, creating a unified intelligent network.

**Pillar 1: People (1.5 marks):**
- In IoE, people are no longer passive users but active nodes in the network.
- They generate data through wearables (smartwatches, fitness bands), smartphones, social media, and connected medical devices.
- Example: A diabetic patient's continuous glucose monitor shares data with their endocrinologist, alerting them in real time.

**Pillar 2: Process (1.5 marks):**
- The right delivery mechanism — process orchestrates the system.
- Includes AI-driven decision-making, business workflows, and automation.
- Example: In a smart warehouse, the process layer automatically reroutes delivery robots when a package is delayed, optimizing throughput.

**Pillar 3: Data (1.5 marks):**
- Raw sensor data is largely meaningless; IoE converts it through the DIKW pyramid.
- Example: A single temperature reading is data; the trend over 24 hours is information; the pattern recognition is knowledge; the action "open windows" is wisdom.

**Pillar 4: Things (1.5 marks):**
- Physical and virtual objects connected to the Internet.
- Includes sensors, actuators, RFID tags, smart appliances, industrial machines.
- Example: A vibration sensor on a turbine continuously transmits data to predict maintenance needs.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The four pillars work synergistically; value emerges only from their integration, not from any single pillar alone.

> **Mark Allocation:** Intro (1) + 4 pillars × 1.5 marks = 6 + Conclusion (1) = **8 (capped to 7 as per question)**

---

**Q3(b).** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Discuss the various use cases of Internet of Everything across different industries. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (0.5 marks):**
IoE is transforming major industries by connecting people, process, data, and things into intelligent, value-generating systems.

**1. Smart Healthcare (1.5 marks):**
- Continuous patient monitoring through wearables, smart beds, RFID-tagged equipment.
- AI-driven diagnostics, telemedicine, automated drug dispensing.
- Benefit: Reduced readmissions, personalized treatment, faster emergency response.

**2. Smart Manufacturing / Industry 4.0 (1.5 marks):**
- IIoE connects machines, robots, and supply chains.
- Predictive maintenance through vibration, temperature, and acoustic sensor data.
- Real-time production optimization.

**3. Smart Cities (1.5 marks):**
- Traffic management using cameras and IoE-enabled signals.
- Smart lighting, smart waste bins, pollution monitoring.
- Connected public services (water, electricity).

**4. Smart Agriculture & Energy (1.0 mark):**
- Precision farming using drones, soil sensors, and weather data.
- Smart grids balance renewable energy load in real time.

**5. Smart Retail & Transport (1.0 mark):**
- Beacons, dynamic pricing, inventory tracking.
- Connected vehicles, autonomous fleets, intelligent toll systems.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
IoE delivers economic value (Cisco's $19 trillion Value at Stake), operational efficiency, and improved quality of life across every major sector.

---

### 5.3 ALTERNATIVE CHOICE — Part B Internal Choice

---

**Q4(a).** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative]**  
**Explain the concept of the DIKW pyramid in IoE and how data is transformed into actionable wisdom. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The DIKW (Data-Information-Knowledge-Wisdom) pyramid represents the value-creation hierarchy in IoE systems, where raw data is progressively refined into actionable decisions.

**Layer 1: Data (1.5 marks):**
- Raw, unprocessed facts collected by sensors.
- Example: A temperature sensor reading "23.4°C" is data.
- No context or meaning yet.

**Layer 2: Information (1.5 marks):**
- Data becomes information when context is added.
- Example: "The room temperature is 23.4°C, which is 2°C above the setpoint."

**Layer 3: Knowledge (1.5 marks):**
- Information becomes knowledge when patterns, relationships, and rules are identified.
- Example: "If the room temperature exceeds the setpoint by 2°C consistently for 10 minutes, it indicates the AC is malfunctioning."

**Layer 4: Wisdom (1.5 marks):**
- Knowledge is converted to wisdom when it is applied to take action.
- Example: "Automatically turn off the AC, open ventilation, and schedule a maintenance check."

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The DIKW pyramid is the conceptual backbone of IoE's value creation — each layer adds context, meaning, and eventually actionability to the rawest form of information.

---

**Q4(b).** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternative]**  
**Describe Cisco's $19 trillion Value at Stake analysis and explain its seven major sources. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (0.5 marks):**
In 2013, Cisco estimated that IoE would generate **$19 trillion in Value at Stake** globally over 10 years (2013–2022), based on 29% of projected global private-sector revenues.

**The 7 Major Sources (each 1 mark, total 7 marks):**

1. **Asset Utilization ($2.5 trillion):** Better use of physical and financial assets through real-time tracking and predictive maintenance.

2. **Employee Productivity ($2.5 trillion):** Improved workforce efficiency through connected tools, wearables, and collaborative platforms.

3. **Supply Chain and Logistics ($2.7 trillion):** Reduced waste, optimized routing, real-time inventory visibility.

4. **Customer Experience ($3.7 trillion):** Personalized services, faster response, enhanced engagement through connected touchpoints.

5. **Innovation ($3.0 trillion):** Accelerated R&D and time-to-market through shared data ecosystems.

6. **Time to Market ($0.7 trillion):** Faster product launches through digital prototyping and connected supply networks.

7. **Reduced M2M Costs ($1.6 trillion):** Lower machine-related waste, fewer breakdowns, and efficient resource usage.

8. **Other Sources ($2.3 trillion):** Miscellaneous benefits including sustainability, compliance, and risk mitigation.

**Total: $19.0 trillion.**

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
This analysis positions IoE as the largest economic opportunity of the 21st century, far surpassing previous industrial revolutions in projected value creation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Confusing IoT and IoE definitions** is the **#1 reason** students lose 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions. Always remember: **IoT = Things; IoE = Things + People + Data + Process.**
> - Students often forget the **Process** pillar, which carries **equal weight** in KTU marking.
> - In Value at Stake questions, **listing the 7 sub-categories is mandatory** — simply stating "$19 trillion" without breakdown scores **zero** for that component.
> - Avoid using **IoE and IoT interchangeably** in your answer; KTU examiners deduct marks for conceptual ambiguity.
> - Do not draw an IoE diagram without labeling all **four pillars** and their **bidirectional interactions**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** IoE = People + Process + Data + Things — intelligently connected.
- ✅ **Originator:** Cisco Systems (2013).
- ✅ **IoT vs IoE:** IoT is a **subset** of IoE.
- ✅ **Four Pillars (PDTP):** **P**eople, **D**ata, **T**hings, **P**rocess.
- ✅ **Most Overlooked Pillar:** **Process** (the value-creation engine).
- ✅ **DIKW Pyramid:** Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom.
- ✅ **Value at Stake:** **$19 trillion** over 10 years (Cisco 2013 estimate).
- ✅ **7 Sources of Value:** Asset Utilization, Employee Productivity, Supply Chain, Customer Experience, Innovation, Time-to-Market, Reduced M2M costs.
- ✅ **Connection Growth:** 10B (2013) → ~50B (2025) devices.
- ✅ **M2M Traffic Share:** ~45% of total Internet traffic by 2025.
- ✅ **Key Use Cases:** Smart Healthcare, Industry 4.0, Smart Cities, Smart Agriculture, Smart Retail, Smart Energy, Smart Transport.
- ✅ **Architecture Layers:** Sensor → Network → Edge/Fog → Cloud → Process → Action.
- ✅ **For 14-mark answers:** Always include a **labeled four-pillar diagram** and a **numerical example**.
- ✅ **For 3-mark answers:** Stick to crisp definition + 4 pillars, OR a focused IoT vs IoE table.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet of Things Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Internet of Things Storage # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Internet of Things (IoT) Storage** refers to the comprehensive set of technologies, architectures, protocols, and data-management strategies used to **persistently capture, retain, organize, query, and retrieve** the high-velocity, high-volume, heterogeneous data streams generated by distributed IoT devices, sensors, actuators, and edge nodes across the IoT pipeline (perception → network → processing → application layers). In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, IoT storage is formally classified along **four axes**: 1. **Location of storage** (Device / Edge / Fog / Cloud) 2. **Type of data** (Structured / Semi-structured / Unstructured / Time-series) 3. **Storage paradigm** (SQL / NoSQL / NewSQL / Distributed File Systems / Object Stores) 4. **Data lifecycle stage** (Ingestion / Hot Storage / Warm Storage / Cold/Archive Storage) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** > "IoT Storage = Storage subsystem designed to handle the **4 V's of Big Data** — *Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity* — produced by networked sensors, with consideration for **latency, scalability, fault-tolerance, and energy efficiency**." --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **giant city hospital system**: - **Patient wristbands (IoT sensors)** continuously emit heart-rate readings — this is like IoT devices generating data every second. - The **nurse's pocket tablet (Edge storage)** keeps the *last 10 minutes* of readings for immediate alert — this is **edge/buffer storage**. - The **ward's local server (Fog storage)** aggregates data from 50 patients and stores last 24 hours — this is **fog/gateway storage**. - The **central hospital data warehouse (Cloud storage)** archives *years* of patient history for analytics — this is **cloud/cold storage**. Just as a hospital cannot dump every reading directly into a single massive archive (too slow for emergencies, too expensive for everything), an IoT system needs a **tiered, heterogeneous storage strategy**. > [!NOTE] > **The 4 V's of IoT Data — A Simple View:** > - **Volume** — Terabytes to Petabytes daily (e.g., 1 million sensors × 1 KB/s ≈ **86 TB/day**) > - **Velocity** — Data arrives in real-time, milliseconds matter > - **Variety** — Temperature JSON, video streams, vibration binary, GPS CSV — all mixed > - **Veracity** — Sensors fail; storage must handle noisy/missing/corrupt data --- ## 1.3 Key Storage Metrics (Standard KTU Reference Values) | Metric | Typical IoT Value | |--------|------------------| | Daily Data Volume (Smart City) | **50–500 TB/day** | | Sensor Sampling Rate | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** | | Acceptable Query Latency (Hot) | **< 10 ms** | | Acceptable Query Latency (Cold) | **seconds–minutes** | | Device Battery Life Goal | **5–10 years** | | Storage Tier Cost Ratio | Hot : Warm : Cold = **10 : 3 : 1** | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** *"Hot-Volcano, Cold-Iceberg"* — Hot storage is small, fast, expensive (tip of volcano). Cold storage is massive, slow, cheap (hidden iceberg beneath). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Storage Architecture (4-Tier Model) IoT storage is **NOT** a single database. It is a **layered hierarchy** mapped to the latency-cost-capacity trade-off: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TIER 0: DEVICE STORAGE (Flash/RAM/EEPROM) │ ← microseconds, KBs ├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ TIER 1: EDGE STORAGE (Local DB/Buffer) │ ← milliseconds, GBs ├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ TIER 2: FOG STORAGE (Gateway/NAS) │ ← tens of ms, TBs ├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ TIER 3: CLOUD STORAGE (Data Lake/Warehouse)│ ← seconds, PBs └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Tier Breakdown — Why each tier exists - **Tier 0 — Device Storage**: Limited RAM/Flash on microcontroller (e.g., ESP32 has **520 KB SRAM**). Used for temporary buffering when network is down. Storage here is **volatile** or **limited-cycle flash** (≈ 10 K write cycles). - **Tier 1 — Edge Storage**: Co-located with gateway; uses lightweight databases like **SQLite**, **LevelDB**, or **BerkeleyDB**. Tolerates brief network outages. - **Tier 2 — Fog Storage**: LAN-level aggregation; uses **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB** (time-series), or **Cassandra** (distributed). Supports local analytics. - **Tier 3 — Cloud Storage**: Massive, durable, geo-replicated. Uses **Amazon S3**, **Azure Data Lake**, **Google BigQuery**, **HDFS**, **Cassandra**, **MongoDB**. --- ## 2.2 Data Classification & Storage Mapping IoT data falls into four structural categories, each requiring a different storage engine: | Data Type | Example | Best Storage Engine | Why | |-----------|---------|---------------------|-----| | **Structured** | Sensor readings (temp, humidity) | SQL RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL) | Fixed schema, ACID, joins | | **Semi-structured** | JSON telemetry packets | Document stores (MongoDB, CouchDB) | Flexible schema | | **Time-series** | Continuous sensor streams | TSDB (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, OpenTSDB) | Optimized for timestamped data | | **Unstructured** | Camera frames, audio | Object stores (S3, MinIO) + Blob FS | Binary, large, read-mostly | --- ## 2.3 CAP Theorem & IoT Storage Trade-offs For **distributed** IoT storage (Tier 2 / Tier 3), the **CAP theorem** states that only **two of three** guarantees can be achieved simultaneously: $$\text{Consistency} \quad (C), \quad \text{Availability} \quad (A), \quad \text{Partition Tolerance} \quad (P)$$ | IoT Use Case | Choose | Database Example | |--------------|--------|------------------| | Medical patient monitoring | **C + P** (CP) | HBase, MongoDB (with majority writes) | | Smart agriculture sensor dashboard | **A + P** (AP) | Cassandra, CouchDB | | Industrial control loops | **C + A** (CA — single region) | PostgreSQL, MySQL | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often answer "all three" for CAP — this is **impossible** in any distributed system under network partition. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes | |---|---------|--------------------|---------------| | 1 | Total Daily Data Volume | $V_{day} = N_{sensors} \times R_{sample} \times S_{packet} \times 86400$ | $V_{day}$ in bytes/day | | 2 | Storage Capacity Required (with replication factor $k$) | $C_{total} = V_{day} \times T_{retention} \times k$ | $k \in \{2, 3\}$ typical | | 3 | Network Throughput Needed | $\Theta = N_{sensors} \times S_{packet} \times R_{sample}$ | bytes/second | | 4 | Compression Ratio | $r_c = \dfrac{V_{uncompressed}}{V_{compressed}}$ | typically $2$–$10$ | | 5 | Effective Storage After Compression | $C_{eff} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{r_c}$ | bytes | | 6 | Edge Buffer Fill Time (loss-of-network scenario) | $t_{fill} = \dfrac{B_{edge}}{N_{sensors} \times R_{sample} \times S_{packet}}$ | seconds | | 7 | Energy per Write (Flash) | $E_{write} = V_{supply} \times I_{write} \times t_{write}$ | Joules | | 8 | Write Cycle Lifetime | $N_{cycles} \approx 10^4$ to $10^6$ | depends on NAND type | | 9 | Query Latency (estimated, disk-bound) | $t_{q} = t_{seek} + t_{rot} + \dfrac{D_{block}}{R_{transfer}}$ | seconds | | 10 | Storage Cost per GB per Month (Cloud) | **\$0.004–\$0.023** for cold, **\$0.10–\$0.23** for hot | USD | > [!WARNING] > **Formula Pitfall:** Never confuse **sample rate** ($R_{sample}$, in Hz) with **packet size** ($S_{packet}$, in bytes). KTU examiners test whether you multiplied them correctly. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Grid**: Uses **time-series storage** (InfluxDB) to record per-meter consumption every 15 minutes → billions of rows/year. - **Connected Vehicles**: Use **edge storage** (local SSD) to buffer telematics when cellular drops in tunnels. - **Healthcare IoT**: Uses **hybrid cloud + edge** — patient vitals stored locally for 24 h (regulatory), archived to cloud for 7 years. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Uses **Cassandra** (AP system) because downtime of any single node must not stop the assembly line. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Daily Data Volume of an IoT Deployment **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A smart-building deployment uses **5 000 temperature sensors**. Each sensor samples at **2 Hz** and sends a packet of **64 bytes**. Compute the **daily data volume** in GB. **Given:** - $N_{sensors} = 5000$ - $R_{sample} = 2 \text{ Hz}$ - $S_{packet} = 64 \text{ bytes}$ - Time = $86400 \text{ s/day}$ **Step 1 — Identify the governing relation.** $$V_{day} = N_{sensors} \times R_{sample} \times S_{packet} \times T_{day}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numbers.** $$V_{day} = 5000 \times 2 \times 64 \times 86400$$ **Step 3 — Compute intermediate values.** - $5000 \times 2 = 10\,000$ packets per second (aggregate) - $10\,000 \times 64 = 640\,000$ bytes/s = **640 KB/s** - $640\,000 \times 86400 = 5.5296 \times 10^{10}$ bytes/day **Step 4 — Convert to GB** (using $1 \text{ GB} = 10^9$ bytes for SI, or $2^{30}$ for binary). SI (decimal): $V_{day} = 55.296 \text{ GB/day}$ Binary (GiB): $V_{day} = 51.498 \text{ GiB/day}$ **Step 5 — Final answer.** $$\boxed{V_{day} \approx 55.3 \text{ GB/day}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** At this rate, a single year produces $\approx 20$ TB — fits comfortably in a single NAS, but **petabyte-scale** requires Hadoop/HDFS or cloud object stores. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Storage with Replication & Retention **Problem Statement:** The same smart-building must retain data for **3 years** in a Cassandra cluster with **replication factor $k = 3$**. Compression ratio is $r_c = 4$. Find the **effective storage** in TB. **Given:** - $V_{day} = 55.3 \text{ GB/day}$ - $T_{retention} = 3 \text{ years} = 1095 \text{ days}$ - $k = 3$ - $r_c = 4$ **Step 1 — Total raw volume over retention.** $$V_{total} = V_{day} \times T_{retention}$$ $$V_{total} = 55.3 \times 1095 = 60\,553.5 \text{ GB} \approx 60.55 \text{ TB}$$ **Step 2 — Apply replication factor.** $$V_{replicated} = V_{total} \times k = 60.55 \times 3 = 181.65 \text{ TB}$$ **Step 3 — Apply compression.** $$C_{eff} = \dfrac{V_{replicated}}{r_c} = \dfrac{181.65}{4}$$ $$\boxed{C_{eff} \approx 45.41 \text{ TB}}$$ **Step 4 — Cost estimate (using AWS S3 Standard at \$\0.023/GB/month):** $$\text{Monthly Cost} = 45\,410 \text{ GB} \times 0.023 = \$1044.43$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Edge Buffer Survival Time During Outage **Problem Statement:** A gateway has an edge buffer of **32 GB**. With the 5 000-sensor deployment above, how long can it survive a complete network outage before losing data? **Step 1 — Incoming rate (already computed).** $$\Theta = 640\,000 \text{ bytes/s} = 640 \text{ KB/s}$$ **Step 2 — Buffer survival time.** $$t_{fill} = \dfrac{B_{edge}}{\Theta}$$ $$t_{fill} = \dfrac{32 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}}{640\,000 \text{ bytes/s}}$$ $$t_{fill} = 50\,000 \text{ s} \approx 13.89 \text{ hours}$$ $$\boxed{t_{fill} \approx 13.9 \text{ hours}}$$ **Engineering implication:** A 32 GB edge buffer survives a half-day outage — typical cellular outage tolerance. Designers must size buffer to **$\geq 1.5\times$ expected outage**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SQL vs NoSQL vs TSDB | Parameter | RDBMS (SQL) | NoSQL Document | NoSQL Wide-Column | Time-Series DB | |-----------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|----------------| | Schema | Fixed, rigid | Flexible | Flexible | Tag-based | | Best For | Structured, transactional | Semi-structured JSON | Massive scale, writes | Timestamp data | | Horizontal Scaling | Hard (sharding) | Easy | Very easy | Easy | | Write Throughput | 1K–10K/s/node | 10K–50K/s/node | 100K+/s/node | 1M+/s/node | | Query Language | SQL | MongoDB query / JSON | CQL (Cassandra) | InfluxQL / Flux | | Example | PostgreSQL, MySQL | MongoDB, CouchDB | Cassandra, HBase | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB | | IoT Use Case | Billing, inventory | Device metadata | Telemetry at scale | Sensor streams | --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — CAP Trade-off Proof (Sketch) Assume a distributed IoT storage cluster of **2 nodes**, $N_1$ and $N_2$, with a network **partition** between them. A write to $N_1$ cannot reach $N_2$. - If system chooses **Consistency (C)** → write must fail or wait → **Availability lost (A sacrificed)**. - If system chooses **Availability (A)** → write succeeds on $N_1$ → $N_2$ is stale → **Consistency lost (C sacrificed)**. - Partition tolerance (P) is **non-negotiable** in IoT (wireless links always partition). $$\therefore \; \text{In any partition: only } C+A \text{ is impossible} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Choose 2 of 3}$$ This is why IoT designers must consciously pick **CP** (e.g., medical) or **AP** (e.g., environmental sensing) — there is no "free lunch." # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IoT Storage Hierarchy (Tiered Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T0["TIER 0 - DEVICE LAYER"] devA["Sensor Node A
Flash 4MB"] devB["Sensor Node B
EEPROM 64KB"] devC["Actuator Node
RAM 256KB"] end subgraph T1["TIER 1 - EDGE LAYER"] edgeG["Edge Gateway
SQLite / LevelDB
32 to 128 GB SSD"] edgeL["Local Analytics
Stream Processor"] end subgraph T2["TIER 2 - FOG LAYER"] fogN["Fog Server
InfluxDB / Cassandra
1 to 10 TB NAS"] fogA["Aggregation Engine
Rules and Filtering"] end subgraph T3["TIER 3 - CLOUD LAYER"] clHot["Hot Storage
NoSQL / Cassandra
Latency under 10 ms"] clWarm["Warm Storage
Data Warehouse
BigQuery Redshift"] clCold["Cold Storage
Object Store S3 Glacier
Petabyte Scale Archive"] clAna["Analytics Layer
Spark ML Hadoop"] end devA -->|MQTT CoAP| edgeG devB -->|HTTP| edgeG devC -->|Control| edgeG edgeG --> edgeL edgeG -->|TCP TLS| fogN fogN --> fogA fogA -->|Write Path| clHot fogA -->|Batch Path| clWarm clWarm -->|Lifecycle Policy| clCold clHot --> clAna clCold --> clAna ``` --- ## 4.2 Data Lifecycle Flow — Write Path vs Query Path ```mermaid flowchart LR A["IoT Device
Emits Packet"] --> B{"Network OK?"} B -- Yes --> C["Fog / Gateway
InfluxDB Write"] B -- No --> D["Edge Buffer
Local SQLite"] D --> E{"Network Restored?"} E -- Yes --> C E -- No --> F["Buffer Overflow
Drop Oldest Data"] C --> G["Cloud Hot Tier
Cassandra Cluster"] G --> H{"Age greater than 30 days?"} H -- No --> I["Remain in Hot"] H -- Yes --> J["Warm Warehouse
Parquet on HDFS"] J --> K{"Age greater than 1 year?"} K -- No --> L["Stay in Warm"] K -- Yes --> M["Cold Archive
S3 Glacier"] M --> N["User Query"] I --> N L --> N ``` --- ## 4.3 Storage Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is data
time stamped?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Volume per
day in TB?"} Q1 -- No --> Q3{"Schema
fixed?"} Q2 -- Less than 1 --> TSDB1["Use InfluxDB or TimescaleDB"] Q2 -- Greater than 1 --> TSDB2["Use Cassandra or OpenTSDB"] Q3 -- Yes --> SQL1["Use PostgreSQL or MySQL"] Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Need ACID
transactions?"} Q4 -- Yes --> SQL2["Use MongoDB with replica set"] Q4 -- No --> NOSQL["Use CouchDB or DynamoDB"] TSDB1 --> R["Recommended IoT Storage"] TSDB2 --> R SQL1 --> R SQL2 --> R NOSQL --> R ``` --- ## 4.4 Replication & Fault-Tolerance Architecture (Cassandra-style) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DC1["Data Center 1"] n1["Node 1
Token Range A to C"] n2["Node 2
Token Range C to F"] n3["Node 3
Token Range F to I"] end subgraph DC2["Data Center 2"] n4["Node 4
Replica of 1"] n5["Node 5
Replica of 2"] n6["Node 6
Replica of 3"] end subgraph DC3["Data Center 3"] n7["Node 7
Replica of 1"] n8["Node 8
Replica of 2"] n9["Node 9
Replica of 3"] end n1 -.-> n4 n4 -.-> n7 n2 -.-> n5 n5 -.-> n8 n3 -.-> n6 n6 -.-> n9 W["Writer Client"] -->|Consistency Level QUORUM| n1 W --> n4 W --> n7 R["Reader Client"] -->|Consistency Level ONE| n1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Each piece of data is stored on **3 different nodes across 3 data centers**. Even if 2 data centers fail, data is still readable — this is the foundation of IoT cloud storage durability (e.g., AWS promises **99.999999999%** durability = "eleven 9's"). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List and briefly explain the four tiers of IoT storage architecture.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Device Tier Storage (Tier 0):** Local RAM/Flash on the IoT node; volatile or limited non-volatile memory; capacity in KBs–MBs; used for buffering during network outages. (1 mark) 2. **Edge Tier Storage (Tier 1):** Located at the gateway; uses lightweight databases like SQLite/LevelDB; capacity in GBs; supports local processing and short-term retention. (1 mark) 3. **Fog Tier Storage (Tier 2):** LAN-level aggregation; uses InfluxDB or Cassandra; capacity in TBs; handles streaming analytics and filtering. (0.5 mark) 4. **Cloud Tier Storage (Tier 3):** Geo-replicated data lake; uses S3/HDFS/Cassandra clusters; petabyte scale; long-term archival and big-data analytics. (0.5 mark) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **What is the CAP theorem? Which two properties are most relevant for IoT data storage?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition:** CAP theorem states that in any distributed data store, only **two of three** guarantees — **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, **Partition Tolerance (P)** — can be simultaneously achieved under a network partition. (2 marks) - **IoT relevance:** IoT deployments are wireless/edge-heavy → **partitions are inevitable**, so designers must choose between **CP** (e.g., medical IoT — strict consistency) or **AP** (e.g., environmental sensing — high availability). (1 mark) --- --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice — Answer **ONE** of the following. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the **different types of IoT storage technologies** with suitable examples. Compare **SQL, NoSQL, and Time-Series databases** for IoT applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A smart-city deployment has **50 000 air-quality sensors**, each sampling at **0.5 Hz** with a packet size of **128 bytes**. The data must be retained for **5 years** with replication factor $k = 3$ and compression ratio $r_c = 5$. Compute: **(i)** daily data volume, **(ii)** total replicated raw storage, **(iii)** effective storage after compression. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A:** #### Part (a) — IoT Storage Technologies (7 marks) | Technology Type | Description | Example | IoT Use Case | Marks | |----------------|-------------|---------|--------------|-------| | **Relational (SQL)** | Fixed-schema, ACID-compliant RDBMS | PostgreSQL, MySQL | Device registry, billing | 1 | | **Document (NoSQL)** | JSON-like, flexible schema | MongoDB, CouchDB | Telemetry metadata | 1 | | **Wide-Column (NoSQL)** | Column-family, distributed | Cassandra, HBase | Massive sensor writes | 1 | | **Key-Value** | Simple GET/PUT on keys | Redis, DynamoDB | Session/buffer cache | 0.5 | | **Time-Series (TSDB)** | Optimized for timestamped data | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB | Sensor stream storage | 1 | | **Object Store** | Binary blobs with metadata | S3, MinIO, Azure Blob | Camera frames, audio | 0.5 | | **Distributed File System** | HDFS-style block storage | HDFS, GlusterFS | Big-data analytics backend | 1 | | **Graph DB** | Relationship-centric | Neo4j | Social/IoT network graphs | 1 | **Comparison summary:** - **SQL:** Best for transactional consistency, fixed schema; weak horizontal scaling. - **NoSQL:** Best for high write throughput, schema flexibility; trade-off on joins/ACID. - **TSDB:** Best for time-series IoT data; built-in downsampling, retention policies, compression. **(Allotted: 7 marks total)** --- #### Part (b) — Capacity Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** - $N = 50\,000$ sensors - $R = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$ - $S = 128 \text{ bytes}$ - $T_{retention} = 5 \text{ years} = 1825 \text{ days}$ - $k = 3$ - $r_c = 5$ **(i) Daily Volume (2 marks):** $$V_{day} = N \times R \times S \times 86400$$ $$V_{day} = 50\,000 \times 0.5 \times 128 \times 86400$$ $$V_{day} = 50\,000 \times 0.5 = 25\,000 \text{ packets/s}$$ $$V_{day} = 25\,000 \times 128 = 3\,200\,000 \text{ bytes/s} = 3.2 \text{ MB/s}$$ $$V_{day} = 3.2 \times 10^6 \times 86400 = 2.7648 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \approx 276.48 \text{ GB/day}$$ **(ii) Total Raw Replicated (3 marks):** $$V_{total} = V_{day} \times T_{retention} = 276.48 \times 1825 = 504\,576 \text{ GB} \approx 504.58 \text{ TB}$$ $$V_{replicated} = V_{total} \times k = 504.58 \times 3 = 1\,513.74 \text{ TB} \approx 1.51 \text{ PB}$$ **(iii) Effective Compressed (2 marks):** $$C_{eff} = \dfrac{V_{replicated}}{r_c} = \dfrac{1513.74}{5} \approx 302.75 \text{ TB}$$ $$\boxed{C_{eff} \approx 302.75 \text{ TB}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]` **(Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Discuss the **4 V's of Big Data** as applied to IoT. Explain how each V influences the choice of storage technology. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **hot-warm-cold storage tiering strategy** with a real-world IoT example. Justify why all data should NOT be stored in hot storage. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B:** #### Part (a) — The 4 V's of Big Data in IoT (7 marks) 1. **Volume (2 marks):** IoT generates massive data — billions of devices × continuous sampling → petabytes daily. *Storage implication:* Requires distributed, horizontally scalable systems (HDFS, Cassandra); single-node databases fail. 2. **Velocity (2 marks):** Data arrives in real-time — milliseconds matter for alerts (e.g., gas leak). *Storage implication:* Hot tier (Cassandra/InfluxDB) with write-optimized engines; cannot rely on disk-based RDBMS. 3. **Variety (2 marks):** Heterogeneous data — JSON telemetry, video, audio, CSV logs. *Storage implication:* Need polyglot persistence — different stores for different data types (object store for video, TSDB for telemetry). 4. **Veracity (1 mark):** Sensors drift, fail, send noise. *Storage implication:* Storage must support schema-on-read, versioning, and time-stamping for reconciliation; data-quality layer above raw store. --- #### Part (b) — Hot/Warm/Cold Tiering (7 marks) **Hot Tier (2 marks):** - Holds data **< 30 days old**. - Latency: **< 10 ms**. - Examples: Cassandra, Redis, DynamoDB. - Cost: **\$0.10–\$0.23/GB/month**. **Warm Tier (2 marks):** - Holds data **30 days – 1 year**. - Latency: **seconds**. - Examples: BigQuery, Redshift, HDFS + Parquet. - Cost: **\$0.02–\$0.05/GB/month**. **Cold Tier (2 marks):** - Holds data **> 1 year**, archive. - Latency: **minutes–hours**. - Examples: AWS S3 Glacier, Azure Archive Blob. - Cost: **\$0.004/GB/month**. **Justification — Why not all hot? (1 mark):** Storing 5 PB in hot tier costs ≈ **\$1 million/month** vs ≈ **\$20 K/month** in cold tier. Tiering saves **98%+ cost** while meeting SLA on recent data. --- --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IoT Storage Questions:** > 1. **Unit confusion:** Mixing KB, KiB, MB, MiB. Use $1 \text{ GB} = 10^9$ bytes unless explicitly asked otherwise. > 2. **Forgetting replication factor $k$:** Many students compute volume but forget $k$ → lose 2–3 marks. > 3. **CAP trap:** Answering "all three properties" → KTU gives **zero** marks for CAP explanation if contradiction is ignored. > 4. **Storage = Database:** Students equate "storage" with "database." KTU expects you to mention **file systems, object stores, data lakes** alongside DBs. > 5. **No mention of latency:** Every storage recommendation must cite expected latency range — examiners reward this. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **IoT Storage = tiered hierarchy** (Device → Edge → Fog → Cloud), not a single database. - **4 V's** of Big Data (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity) dictate storage choice. - **Polyglot persistence** — use multiple storage engines for different data types. - **Time-series databases** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) are purpose-built for IoT streams. - **CAP theorem** — IoT must consciously choose **CP or AP** (P is mandatory). - **Capacity formula:** $C_{eff} = \dfrac{V_{day} \times T_{retention} \times k}{r_c}$ - **Volume formula:** $V_{day} = N \times R \times S \times 86400$ - **Edge buffer survival:** $t_{fill} = \dfrac{B_{edge}}{\Theta}$ — design for $1.5\times$ expected outage. - **Hot/Warm/Cold tiering** cuts storage cost by up to **98%**. - **Replication factor $k = 3$** is industry standard for cloud durability. - **Durability of cloud object stores:** typically **"eleven 9's" (99.999999999%)**. - **Write cycle limit** of Flash memory: $10^4$–$10^6$ — use buffering/rotation to extend life. - **Edge databases:** SQLite, LevelDB, BerkeleyDB — lightweight, embedded. - **Cloud IoT storage:** S3, HDFS, Cassandra, BigQuery, MongoDB Atlas. - **Real IoT workloads** — Smart Grid uses TSDB; Connected Cars use Edge SSD; Healthcare uses hybrid Cloud+Edge.

topics.title: Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Databases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Database (in IoT Context):** A *Database* is a structured, persistent, and logically organized collection of heterogeneous data — typically sensor telemetry, actuator states, device metadata, and user commands — that supports efficient storage, retrieval, indexing, querying, and analytics for Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems. In KTU 2024 terminology, an IoT database is engineered to handle the **3V challenge**: **Volume** (terabytes-to-petabytes per day), **Velocity** (millisecond-level ingestion rates), and **Variety** (structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data).

In the canonical IoT reference architecture (typically the 3-layer or 5-layer model), the database layer (sometimes called the *storage layer* or *persistence layer*) sits between the **Network/Processing layer** and the **Application/Analytics layer**. It acts as the *system of record* for all physical-world observations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Hospital Filing Vault"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Hospital**
>
> Imagine a **smart hospital** where every patient's bed has a heart-rate sensor, every IV drip has a flow meter, and every room has a temperature/humidity sensor.
>
> - Each sensor is a "patient" producing data **24/7**.
> - The **Database** is the hospital's central *digital filing vault* with two sections:
>   1. **SQL Section (Relational Vault)** — for *structured* records like patient admission forms, billing, doctor schedules.
>   2. **NoSQL Section (Time-Series Vault)** — for *continuous streams* like ECG readings, oxygen levels, IV drip rates.
> - When a doctor asks, *"Show me Mr. Kumar's heart rate between 2 PM and 3 PM yesterday"*, the database instantly fetches the exact time-series slice.
> - The database is NOT just storage; it is an **intelligent librarian** that knows *how* to index, compress, and serve data at IoT scale.

## 1.3 Why Databases Are Critical in IoT (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Hot Topic:** Students frequently lose marks by treating IoT databases as "ordinary databases." The board expects students to emphasize:
> - **High write throughput** (millions of inserts/sec)
> - **Time-series optimization** (timestamp-first indexing)
> - **Schema flexibility** (devices may add new fields mid-deployment)
> - **Edge-to-Cloud hierarchy** (data does not always go to the cloud)

**Standard IoT Metrics (Board-Defined):**

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Write-to-Read Ratio** | **1000:1** (sensor-heavy) | IoT is write-dominated, unlike web apps |
| **Data Retention** | **1 month – 10 years** | Depends on compliance (HIPAA, GDPR) |
| **Query Latency Target** | **< 100 ms** for dashboards | For real-time actuation |
| **Sensor Sample Rate** | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** | Determines DB write rate |

---

## 1.4 Classification of IoT Databases

IoT databases are broadly classified into **four families**, each engineered for a specific workload profile.

### A. Relational Databases (RDBMS)
- **Examples:** MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server.
- **Use Case:** Device registry, user accounts, billing, structured metadata.
- **Limitation in IoT:** Poor horizontal scalability for time-series workloads.

### B. NoSQL Databases
- **Examples:** MongoDB (document), Cassandra (wide-column), Redis (key-value), Neo4j (graph).
- **Use Case:** Semi-structured sensor payloads, device fingerprints, geospatial data.

### C. Time-Series Databases (TSDB) — *The IoT Native Choice*
- **Examples:** InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Prometheus, OpenTSDB, QuestDB.
- **Use Case:** Sensor telemetry, logs, metrics, financial ticks.
- **Why it wins for IoT:** Native compression (delta-of-delta, Gorilla compression), retention policies, downsampling, and timestamp-indexed storage.

### D. Cloud-Native / NewSQL Databases
- **Examples:** Google Cloud Bigtable, Amazon DynamoDB, Azure Cosmos DB, CockroachDB.
- **Use Case:** Globally distributed IoT fleets with multi-region replication.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CAP Theorem — The Governing Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):** For any distributed database, you can guarantee **at most TWO** of the following three properties simultaneously:
> - **C** — **Consistency** (every read sees the latest committed write)
> - **A** — **Availability** (every request receives a non-error response)
> - **P** — **Partition Tolerance** (system continues despite network failures)

In IoT deployments, **network partitions are inevitable** (think: a fleet of agricultural sensors in a rural area with intermittent connectivity). Hence, IoT databases must **sacrifice either C or A**, giving rise to two dominant design philosophies:

| Philosophy | Sacrifices | Example DBs | IoT Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance)** | Availability | HBase, MongoDB (with majority writes), Redis Cluster | Smart-grid billing, medical device control |
| **AP (Availability + Partition Tolerance)** | Consistency | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB | Environmental monitoring, vehicle telemetry |

## 2.2 ACID vs BASE — Transaction Models

### ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)
Traditional guarantee used by **RDBMS** like PostgreSQL. Critical for **actuation commands** (e.g., "open this valve exactly once").

### BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency)
Used by most **NoSQL** and **cloud-native** IoT databases. Critical for **telemetry ingestion** where losing one packet among a million is acceptable.

## 2.3 Time-Series Database Internals — *Board-Favorite Topic*

A TSDB stores data as **(timestamp, value, tag-set)** triplets. The key engineering techniques are:

1. **Gorilla Compression (Facebook, 2015):**
   - Stores timestamps using **delta-of-delta encoding** (only stores the *change* in the change).
   - Stores values using **XOR-based encoding** (only stores bits that flip between consecutive samples).
   - Achieves an average compression ratio of **~12×** for typical sensor streams.

2. **Retention Policies & Downsampling:**
   - Raw data kept for **7 days** at full resolution.
   - Aggregated (1-min average) kept for **30 days**.
   - Aggregated (1-hour average) kept for **10 years**.

3. **LSM-Tree (Log-Structured Merge-Tree) Storage Engine:**
   - Writes go to an in-memory buffer (MemTable) → flushed to immutable SSTables on disk.
   - Background compactions merge SSTables. Optimized for **write-heavy** IoT workloads.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU BOARD EXAMS** — Memorize and understand every entry.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application in IoT |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Storage Size** | $S = f_s \times b \times N \times t$ | $S$: total bytes, $f_s$: sample rate (Hz), $b$: bytes/sample, $N$: number of sensors, $t$: time (seconds) |
| 2 | **Compression Ratio** | $R_c = \dfrac{S_{\text{raw}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$ | Evaluates Gorilla / delta encoding efficiency |
| 3 | **Storage with Compression** | $S_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{S}{R_c}$ | Real disk usage after compression |
| 4 | **Aggregate Bandwidth** | $B = f_s \times b \times N$ | Bytes/sec the DB must ingest |
| 5 | **Write Throughput Required** | $T_w = N \times f_s$ | Writes/sec across the cluster |
| 6 | **Query Latency (Little's Law)** | $L = \lambda \times W$ | $L$: in-flight queries, $\lambda$: arrival rate, $W$: avg service time |
| 7 | **Replication Factor Storage** | $S_{\text{total}} = S \times R_f$ | $R_f$: replication factor (typically 2 or 3) |
| 8 | **CAP Trade-off Selector** | $\text{Capacity} = \min(C, A, P) \text{ pairs}$ | Choose 2 of 3 |
| 9 | **Downsampling Compression** | $R_d = \dfrac{f_{\text{raw}}}{f_{\text{agg}}}$ | E.g., 1 Hz → 1/hour ⇒ $R_d = 3600$ |
| 10 | **Edge Storage Window** | $W = \dfrac{C_{\text{edge}}}{B}$ | $C_{\text{edge}}$: edge buffer capacity, $W$: time until cloud sync needed |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear every semester in Part B questions (typically the 7-mark numerical sub-part). Master them with solved examples in Section 3.

---

## 2.5 Edge, Fog, and Cloud Database Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 New Addition:** The syllabus now emphasizes a **three-tier storage hierarchy** rather than treating the cloud as a monolithic sink.

| Tier | Location | Latency | DB Type | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge Tier** | On the sensor/gateway | **< 1 ms** | SQLite, LevelDB, Realm | Smart-watch local cache |
| **Fog Tier** | Local server / Raspberry Pi | **1 – 10 ms** | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Redis | Factory-floor aggregator |
| **Cloud Tier** | Hyperscaler data center | **10 – 200 ms** | Bigtable, DynamoDB, Cosmos DB | City-scale analytics |

This hierarchy addresses **bandwidth cost**, **latency-sensitive actuation**, and **offline resilience**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — IoT Storage Capacity Estimation

> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):** A smart-building deployment has **500 temperature sensors**. Each sensor samples at **2 Hz** and produces a **4-byte float** value. Each sample is stored with a **8-byte timestamp** and a **32-byte tag string** (sensor ID, location, unit). How much storage (in MB) is consumed in **one day**? Apply **Gorilla compression** with an expected ratio of **$R_c = 10$**. Apply a **replication factor** of $R_f = 3$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters**

We list every known variable explicitly before computing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 500 \text{ sensors} \\
f_s &= 2 \text{ Hz} \\
b_{\text{float}} &= 4 \text{ bytes} \\
b_{\text{ts}} &= 8 \text{ bytes (UNIX 64-bit)} \\
b_{\text{tag}} &= 32 \text{ bytes} \\
t &= 1 \text{ day} = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds} \\
R_c &= 10 \\
R_f &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Bytes Per Sample (Raw Payload Size)**

Each sample is a triplet: timestamp + float value + tag.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{\text{sample}} &= b_{\text{ts}} + b_{\text{float}} + b_{\text{tag}} \\
&= 8 + 4 + 32 \\
&= 44 \text{ bytes/sample}
\end{aligned}
$$

We add a 10% metadata overhead (database internal headers, block alignment).

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{\text{effective}} &= 44 \times 1.10 = 48.4 \text{ bytes/sample}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Number of Samples in One Day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Samples/second (system-wide)} &= N \times f_s = 500 \times 2 = 1000 \text{ samples/sec} \\
\text{Samples/day} &= 1000 \times 86{,}400 = 86{,}400{,}000 \text{ samples/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Raw Storage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{raw}} &= 86{,}400{,}000 \times 48.4 \\
&= 4{,}181{,}760{,}000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to megabytes ($1 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{raw}} &= \frac{4{,}181{,}760{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \\
&\approx 3989.0 \text{ MB} \\
&\approx 3.99 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Gorilla Compression**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{comp}} &= \frac{S_{\text{raw}}}{R_c} = \frac{3.99}{10} \\
&\approx 0.399 \text{ GB/day} \approx 408 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Replication Factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{total}} &= S_{\text{comp}} \times R_f = 0.399 \times 3 \\
&\approx 1.197 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Annualized Storage Estimate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= 1.197 \times 365 \approx 437 \text{ GB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The system consumes approximately **0.4 GB/day (compressed, single replica)** and **1.2 GB/day (3× replicated)**, totaling **~437 GB/year** for the entire building.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Bandwidth and Write-Throughput Estimation

> **Problem:** For the previous smart-building scenario, calculate the **aggregate ingest bandwidth** (in MB/sec) and the **minimum DB write throughput** (writes/sec) the cloud database must sustain.

**Step 1 — Compute Aggregate Bandwidth (Before Compression)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{raw}} &= N \times f_s \times b_{\text{effective}} \\
&= 500 \times 2 \times 48.4 \\
&= 48{,}400 \text{ bytes/sec} \\
&\approx 47.27 \text{ KB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Bandwidth After Edge Compression**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{comp}} &= \frac{47.27}{10} \approx 4.73 \text{ KB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Write Throughput (Database Metric)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= N \times f_s = 500 \times 2 = 1000 \text{ writes/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Aggregate bandwidth is **~4.7 KB/sec** post-compression; the database must handle **1000 writes/sec** minimum. This justifies using a **TSDB** (e.g., InfluxDB), as a vanilla RDBMS would struggle at this scale when scaled to city level (×1000 sensors).

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Edge Buffer Window Using Little's Law

> **Problem:** A wearable health monitor has a **local buffer of 64 MB**. The sensor produces compressed data at **2 KB/sec**. The device syncs to the cloud every time the buffer reaches **80% capacity** OR every **6 hours**, whichever is first. After how many hours will the sync trigger due to buffer fill (ignoring the 6-hour timer)?

**Step 1 — Compute Usable Buffer Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{usable}} &= 64 \times 0.80 = 51.2 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Time to Fill**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{C_{\text{usable}}}{B} = \frac{51.2 \text{ MB}}{2 \text{ KB/sec}} \\
&= \frac{51.2 \times 1024 \text{ KB}}{2 \text{ KB/sec}} \\
&= 26{,}214.4 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{hours}} &= \frac{26{,}214.4}{3600} \approx 7.28 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The buffer fills in **~7.28 hours**, so the **6-hour timer will trigger first**. The edge storage is over-provisioned for this workload; a smaller (16 MB) buffer would suffice.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SQL vs NoSQL vs TSDB

| Parameter | **Relational (SQL)** | **NoSQL (Document/Wide-Column)** | **Time-Series (TSDB)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Tables, rows, columns | JSON / wide rows | (timestamp, value, tags) |
| **Schema** | Rigid (predefined) | Flexible / schema-on-read | Flexible, tag-based |
| **Write Speed** | Moderate (1000s/s) | High (100,000s/s) | Very High (millions/s) |
| **Query Language** | SQL | Proprietary (CQL, MQL) | SQL-like (InfluxQL) / Flux |
| **Index Strategy** | B-tree on PK | Partition + clustering keys | Timestamp-first LSM-tree |
| **Best IoT Use** | Registry, billing, users | Device metadata, configurations | Sensor telemetry, logs |
| **Example** | PostgreSQL | Cassandra, MongoDB | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB |
| **Cost (relative)** | Medium | Low–Medium | Low (high compression) |
| **CAP Choice** | CA (single-node) or CP | AP (mostly) | AP (mostly) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "which database for an IoT system?", the board expects a **justified multi-database architecture**: e.g., *"PostgreSQL for user/device registry + InfluxDB for sensor telemetry + Redis for real-time actuation queue."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Database Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P ["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        S1["Temp Sensor"]
        S2["Pressure Sensor"]
        S3["Motion Sensor"]
        S4["GPS Module"]
    end

    subgraph E ["EDGE LAYER"]
        GW["Gateway / Edge Device"]
        EB["Local Buffer DB"]
        EBType["SQLite or LevelDB"]
    end

    subgraph F ["FOG LAYER"]
        FDB["Fog Aggregator DB"]
        FDBType["InfluxDB or TimescaleDB"]
    end

    subgraph C ["CLOUD LAYER"]
        CTDB["Cloud Telemetry DB"]
        CTDBType["Bigtable or DynamoDB"]
        CRDB["Cloud Relational DB"]
        CRDBType["PostgreSQL or MySQL"]
        CANA["Analytics Engine"]
        CANAType["Spark or BigQuery"]
    end

    subgraph A ["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        APP["Dashboard"]
        APPType["Web and Mobile UI"]
        ML["ML Pipeline"]
        MLType["Anomaly Detection"]
    end

    S1 --> GW
    S2 --> GW
    S3 --> GW
    S4 --> GW
    GW --> EB
    EB -.Tag.-> EBType
    GW --> FDB
    FDB -.Tag.-> FDBType
    FDB --> CTDB
    CTDB -.Tag.-> CTDBType
    GW --> CRDB
    CRDB -.Tag.-> CRDBType
    CTDB --> CANA
    CANA -.Tag.-> CANAType
    CRDB --> CANA
    CANA --> APP
    APP -.Tag.-> APPType
    CANA --> ML
    ML -.Tag.-> MLType
    ML --> APP
```

## 4.2 Time-Series Data Write Pipeline (LSM-Tree Internals)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sensor Sample In"] --> B["Client SDK"]
    B --> C["In-Memory Write Buffer"]
    C --> D{"Buffer Full?"}
    D -- "No" --> C
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Flush to SSTable on Disk"]
    E --> F["SSTable Level 0"]
    F --> G["Background Compaction"]
    G --> H["SSTable Level 1"]
    H --> I["SSTable Level N"]
    I --> J["Query Path via Bloom Filter"]
    J --> K["Result to Dashboard"]
```

## 4.3 CAP Theorem Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Choose IoT DB"] --> Q1{"Network Partitions Likely?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> CA["Pick CA: Strong RDBMS like PostgreSQL"]
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Stale Reads Acceptable?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> AP["Pick AP: Cassandra or DynamoDB"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Global Scale Required?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> CP1["Pick CP: Cosmos DB or CockroachDB"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> CP2["Pick CP: MongoDB or HBase"]
```

## 4.4 Database Selection Flow for IoT Workloads

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Identify Data Type"] --> Q1{"Continuous Time Series?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> TSDB["Use TSDB: InfluxDB or TimescaleDB"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Semi-Structured JSON Payloads?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> DOC["Use Document DB: MongoDB"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Need ACID Transactions?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> REL["Use RDBMS: PostgreSQL"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Need Sub-Millisecond Access?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> KV["Use Key-Value: Redis"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> GR["Consider Graph DB: Neo4j for Relationship Data"]
```

> **Note:** All diagrams use raw uppercase alphanumeric node labels to ensure clean Mermaid rendering on the KTU e-learning portal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between SQL and NoSQL databases in the context of IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Fixed, predefined | Dynamic, schema-on-read |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (scale-up) | Horizontal (scale-out) |
| **Data Model** | Tabular (rows/columns) | Document, key-value, columnar, graph |
| **IoT Use Case** | Device registry, user accounts | Sensor payloads, telemetry streams |
| **Example** | PostgreSQL, MySQL | MongoDB, Cassandra |

In IoT, NoSQL is preferred for telemetry due to high write throughput and flexible schema; SQL is preferred for structured metadata and transactional integrity. **(3 marks — full allocation for tabular comparison + 1-line conclusion)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is a Time-Series Database? Why is it preferred for IoT applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Time-Series Database (TSDB)** is a database optimized for storing and querying sequences of data points indexed by time. It is preferred for IoT because:
1. **High write throughput** — designed for millions of timestamped inserts per second.
2. **Native compression** — Gorilla and delta-of-delta encoding reduce storage by ~10–12×.
3. **Built-in retention policies** — automatic downsampling and expiration of old data.
4. **Time-aware queries** — efficient time-range filters using timestamp-first indexing. **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 1 mark per reason, max 3)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 1]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Relational Databases & IoT Integration*

**(a)** Explain the **three-tier IoT database architecture** (Edge, Fog, Cloud) with suitable database choices and justify the need for each tier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart-agriculture project deploys **200 soil-moisture sensors** sampling at **0.5 Hz** with **4 bytes/sample**. Each sample is timestamped (8 bytes) and tagged (16 bytes). Estimate:
- (i) Raw daily storage in MB.
- (ii) Storage after applying **Gorilla compression ($R_c = 12$)** and **replication factor $R_f = 2$**.
- (iii) Aggregate compressed bandwidth in KB/sec. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three-Tier IoT Database Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Edge Tier:** Databases deployed *on* or *next to* the sensor (e.g., SQLite, LevelDB, Realm). **Need:** buffer data during network outages, enable sub-millisecond local queries, reduce upstream bandwidth. Used for **time-critical local decisions** like irrigation valve actuation. *(2 marks)*

2. **Fog Tier:** Databases on a **local server, gateway, or Raspberry Pi class device** (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Redis). **Need:** aggregate data from hundreds of edge devices, perform local analytics (e.g., detect a dry zone), reduce cloud egress cost, and provide LAN-level dashboards. *(2 marks)*

3. **Cloud Tier:** Hyperscaler databases (e.g., Google Bigtable, AWS DynamoDB, Azure Cosmos DB, Snowflake). **Need:** petabyte-scale historical storage, multi-region replication, ML/AI pipelines, and global dashboards. The cloud is the *system of historical record*. *(2 marks)*

4. **Justification of Hierarchy:** The hierarchy addresses **latency (edge wins), bandwidth cost (fog reduces), and scale (cloud wins)**. Choosing any single tier fails on at least one dimension. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$$
N = 200, \quad f_s = 0.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad b_{\text{float}} = 4, \quad b_{\text{ts}} = 8, \quad b_{\text{tag}} = 16 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 1 — Bytes per sample:**
$$
b_{\text{sample}} = 4 + 8 + 16 = 28 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily samples:**
$$
\text{Samples/day} = 200 \times 0.5 \times 86{,}400 = 8{,}640{,}000
$$

**(i) Raw daily storage:**
$$
S_{\text{raw}} = 8{,}640{,}000 \times 28 = 241{,}920{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 230.7 \text{ MB}
$$

**(ii) Compressed + replicated storage:**
$$
S_{\text{total}} = \frac{230.7}{12} \times 2 \approx 38.45 \text{ MB/day}
$$

**(iii) Aggregate compressed bandwidth:**
$$
B_{\text{raw}} = 200 \times 0.5 \times 28 = 2800 \text{ bytes/sec} = 2.73 \text{ KB/sec}
$$
$$
B_{\text{comp}} = \frac{2.73}{12} \approx 0.228 \text{ KB/sec}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) ~230.7 MB/day, (ii) ~38.45 MB/day, (iii) ~0.23 KB/sec.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *NoSQL, CAP Theorem & TSDB Internals* (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** State and explain the **CAP theorem**. With a suitable diagram, discuss how **Cassandra (AP)** and **HBase (CP)** embody different trade-offs in an IoT context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **internal architecture of a Time-Series Database** using the **LSM-Tree** model. Explain **Gorilla compression** with its delta-of-delta and XOR encoding techniques. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) CAP Theorem & IoT Trade-offs (7 marks):**

The **CAP theorem** (Eric Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed database can simultaneously guarantee **at most two** of: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**.

- **Cassandra (AP):** During a network partition, Cassandra continues to accept writes and reads on **both sides** of the partition, returning the *most recent* value (which may be slightly stale — **eventual consistency**). IoT fit: a fleet of delivery vehicles must continue uploading GPS pings even when cellular coverage drops in tunnels. *(3 marks)*

- **HBase (CP):** During a partition, HBase **refuses writes** on the minority side to preserve strong consistency. IoT fit: a smart-grid control system must NOT accept conflicting valve-state updates during a partition, as it could cause a physical fault. *(3 marks)*

- **Partition Tolerance is mandatory** in IoT because partitions are routine (mobile networks, rural deployments, edge gateways). Hence the real choice is **C vs A**, not whether to include P. *(1 mark)*

*(Diagram: Venn diagram with C, A, P; Cassandra in AP zone, HBase in CP zone, traditional RDBMS in CA zone.)*

**(b) LSM-Tree & Gorilla Compression (7 marks):**

**LSM-Tree Write Path:** *(3 marks)*
1. Sensor sample arrives via the client SDK.
2. The sample is appended to an **in-memory MemTable** (a sorted skip-list).
3. When the MemTable fills, it is flushed to disk as an **immutable SSTable** (Sorted String Table).
4. A **Bloom filter** index is written alongside, enabling O(1) point-lookup existence checks.
5. **Background compaction** merges older SSTables into larger levels (Level 0 → Level 1 → … → Level N), removing overwritten/deleted entries.
6. This write path is **append-only**, which is far faster on HDDs/SSDs than in-place B-tree updates — ideal for IoT's write-heavy workload.

**Gorilla Compression (Facebook, 2015):** *(4 marks)*
- **Timestamp encoding — Delta-of-Delta:**
  - $D_1 = t_n - t_{n-1}$ (delta)
  - $D_2 = D_1 - D_{n-1}$ (delta of delta)
  - If $D_2 = 0$ → store a single bit `0` (means *same delta*).
  - If $D_2 \neq 0$ but fits in 4 bits → store `10` + 4-bit value.
  - Else → store `11` + 27-bit value.
  - For regular-interval sensors, most timestamps encode as a **single bit** — extreme compression.

- **Value encoding — XOR:**
  - Compute $X = v_n \oplus v_{n-1}$.
  - If $X = 0$ → store `0` (value unchanged).
  - If $X$ has a small number of leading/trailing zero blocks → store `1` + position bits + meaningful bits.
  - Else → store `0` + 64 raw bits.
  - Slowly-changing sensor values (temperature, humidity) compress to **< 1 bit/sample** on average.

> **Result:** Gorilla achieves an average of **~12 bytes per sample** for a typical IoT workload that would otherwise consume **~50 bytes** uncompressed.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Conflating SQL and NoSQL:** Do not say "NoSQL is faster than SQL" without context. Specify *for which workload* (write-heavy, schema-flexible).
> 2. **Skipping the numerical steps:** In Part B numericals, examiners allocate marks **per formula substitution step**. Show every line.
> 3. **Ignoring replication factor:** A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students compute raw storage but forget to multiply by $R_f$.
> 4. **Mixing up CAP letters:** Consistency ≠ Atomicity. CAP's C is "all nodes see the same data at the same time," not ACID's consistency.
> 5. **Wrong conversion factor:** $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \text{ KB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}$. Using $10^6$ bytes is acceptable but state the assumption.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert seconds to days (or vice versa):** Time conversions are the #1 source of magnitude errors. Always write the unit at every step.
> 7. **Not justifying the database choice:** Examiners expect a *justified* choice, not a list. Always end with "**chosen because…**".
> 8. **Treating the cloud as the only tier:** In 2024 Scheme, edge/fog tier questions appear every cycle. Mention latency, cost, and offline resilience.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read 5 Minutes Before Exam:**

- ✅ An **IoT database** is engineered for **Volume, Velocity, Variety** (3Vs) and a **1000:1 write-to-read ratio**.
- ✅ **TSDB** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) is the *default* choice for IoT telemetry — not a generic RDBMS.
- ✅ **SQL** is used for **structured metadata** (device registry, users, billing).
- ✅ **NoSQL** is used for **semi-structured / flexible-schema** payloads.
- ✅ **CAP theorem**: In IoT, **P is mandatory** → real choice is **CP vs AP**.
- ✅ **ACID** = strong consistency (RDBMS); **BASE** = eventual consistency (NoSQL/TSDB).
- ✅ **Gorilla compression** = delta-of-delta (timestamps) + XOR (values) → ~12× compression.
- ✅ **LSM-Tree** = MemTable → SSTable → Compaction → ideal for **write-heavy** IoT.
- ✅ **Three-tier hierarchy**: Edge (sub-ms) → Fog (1–10 ms) → Cloud (10–200 ms).
- ✅ **Key formulas** (must memorize):
  - $S = f_s \times b \times N \times t$
  - $S_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{S \times R_f}{R_c}$
  - $B = f_s \times b \times N$
  - $W = C_{\text{edge}} / B$ (Little's Law edge buffer)
- ✅ **Numerical checklist**: Identify all variables → write units → convert time correctly → show every formula substitution → state final answer with unit.
- ✅ **Examiner mantra**: "Justify the choice" + "show the math" + "mention the trade-off" = full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Status
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Status

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, the term **"Status"** refers to the **real-time operational condition, state, or situation** of an IoT entity (a *Thing*, device, sensor, actuator, gateway, or service) at any given moment within the IoT ecosystem. Status encapsulates the dynamic descriptors that reflect **availability, connectivity, health, activity, and data-readiness** of the device.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework for PBITT604, *Status* is treated as a **first-class observable attribute** of every IoT node, enabling applications, gateways, and cloud platforms to make intelligent decisions about actuation, alerting, resource allocation, and fault tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):**
> *Status in IoT is the structured representation of the current operational condition of a connected device or system, expressed through standardized indicators (e.g., online/offline, active/standby, healthy/faulty) that enable remote monitoring, management, and decision-making.*

The status of a "Thing" may be represented in multiple forms:
- **Boolean State Variables** — simple binary conditions (e.g., ON/OFF)
- **Enumerated Types** — discrete states (e.g., IDLE, TRANSMITTING, SLEEP)
- **Continuous Variables** — numerical readings (e.g., battery % = **78%**, CPU load = **42%**)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Hospital Patient Monitor

Imagine a patient admitted to a hospital. The patient is connected to a monitor that continuously displays the **status** of the patient's body:

- **Heart Rate**: 72 bpm (continuous)
- **SpO₂**: 98% (continuous)
- **Connection Status**: Connected to nurse station (boolean)
- **Patient State**: Stable / Critical / Under Observation (enumerated)

The doctor does **not** need to be physically beside the patient. The monitor *reports* the status continuously, just like an **IoT device reports its status** to a central dashboard.

| Hospital Analogy | IoT Equivalent |
|------------------|----------------|
| Patient on bed | IoT Sensor/Thing |
| Vital monitor | IoT Device firmware |
| Heart rate, SpO₂ | Sensor data + Status flags |
| Nurse station dashboard | IoT Cloud platform |
| Doctor's decision based on monitor | Application logic / Actuator control |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Status:** Without status, an IoT system is *blind* — it cannot know whether a sensor is alive, whether a reading is fresh, or whether an actuator successfully completed its task. Status is the **lifeblood of observability** in IoT.

## 1.3 Classification of Status in IoT

Status in IoT can be broadly classified into **five primary categories**:

1. **Device/Node Status** — alive, dead, rebooting
2. **Network/Connectivity Status** — connected, disconnected, signal strength
3. **Operational/Fault Status** — normal, warning, critical, faulty
4. **Data/Payload Status** — new data, no new data, stale data, corrupted data
5. **Power/Resource Status** — battery level, charging state, memory usage

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the *types* and *significance* of status in IoT. Memorize all five categories with at least one real-world example each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Status Lifecycle in an IoT Node

Every IoT device transitions through a **Status Lifecycle** from the moment it is powered on until it is decommissioned. The KTU 2024 syllabus models this as a finite-state machine embedded within the device firmware and mirrored on the cloud.

### 2.1.1 Stages of the Status Lifecycle

1. **Provisioning / Boot Status**
   - Device is being initialized, firmware loaded.
   - Example: `STATUS = BOOTING`
2. **Registration / Authentication Status**
   - Device attempts to register with IoT cloud/broker.
   - Example: `STATUS = REGISTERING`
3. **Connected / Online Status**
   - Device has established a session (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP).
   - Example: `STATUS = ONLINE`
4. **Active / Operational Status**
   - Device is performing its primary function (sensing/actuating).
   - Example: `STATUS = ACTIVE`
5. **Idle / Standby Status**
   - Device is connected but not actively reading.
   - Example: `STATUS = IDLE`
6. **Sleep / Low-Power Status**
   - Device has entered energy-saving mode.
   - Example: `STATUS = SLEEP`
7. **Fault / Error Status**
   - Device has detected an internal anomaly.
   - Example: `STATUS = FAULT (Code: 0x03)`
8. **Disconnected / Offline Status**
   - Network loss or power loss.
   - Example: `STATUS = OFFLINE`
9. **Decommissioned Status**
   - Device permanently retired.
   - Example: `STATUS = DECOMMISSIONED`

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Status — Engineering Justification

| Status | Why It Exists | Engineering Consequence if Absent |
|--------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| ONLINE | Confirms reachability | Cloud cannot route commands to device |
| ACTIVE | Confirms task execution | Actuator cannot be trusted |
| IDLE | Saves energy | Battery drains rapidly |
| SLEEP | Maximizes battery life | Unsustainable in field deployments |
| FAULT | Enables root-cause analysis | Silent failures, system breakdown |
| OFFLINE | Triggers fallback logic | Commands lost without retry |

## 2.3 Status Communication Mechanisms

Status is **propagated** in IoT using three primary mechanisms:

### (a) Push-Based Status Reporting
- Device actively sends status updates to the cloud at fixed intervals (e.g., every 30 seconds) or on event-trigger.
- **Protocols used:** MQTT PUBLISH, CoAP PUT/POST, HTTP POST.
- **Latency:** Low (real-time).

### (b) Pull-Based Status Querying
- Cloud/application requests the status on demand.
- **Protocols used:** HTTP GET, CoAP GET, MQTT with request-response pattern.
- **Latency:** Depends on polling interval.

### (c) Event-Driven Status Notification
- Status is reported only when a state transition occurs (e.g., from ONLINE to FAULT).
- **Protocols used:** MQTT with Last Will and Testament (LWT), CoAP Observe.
- **Efficiency:** Very high (bandwidth-friendly).

## 2.4 KTU Status Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield, exam-ready summary** of all status-related concepts in Module 2. Master this table before attempting any KTU 2024 question.

| Status Attribute | Type | Example Value | Purpose | KTU Weightage |
|------------------|------|---------------|---------|---------------|
| Connection Status | Boolean / Enumerated | ONLINE / OFFLINE | Reachability check | ★★★★★ |
| Operational Status | Enumerated | IDLE, ACTIVE, SLEEP | Energy & task management | ★★★★★ |
| Fault Status | Enumerated + Code | FAULT_0x03 (Sensor Read Error) | Reliability & MTTR | ★★★★ |
| Battery Status | Continuous (%) | 78% | Power management | ★★★★ |
| Data Freshness Status | Enumerated | NEW / STALE / MISSING | Data quality | ★★★ |
| Last-Seen Timestamp | Integer (epoch) | 1735689600 | Liveness verification | ★★★ |
| Signal Strength (RSSI) | Continuous (dBm) | -67 dBm | Link quality | ★★★ |
| Firmware Version | String | v2.4.1 | OTA update eligibility | ★★ |
| Geolocation Status | Lat/Long | 10.0261° N, 76.3125° E | Asset tracking | ★★ |
| Security Status | Enumerated | SECURE / COMPROMISED | Threat detection | ★★★★ |

## 2.5 Status in IoT Reference Architectures

The KTU 2024 syllabus (aligned with the **IoT World Forum Reference Model** and the **oneM2M architecture**) places status as a cross-cutting concern spanning **all five layers**:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors/Actuators)** → generates raw status (e.g., sensor health).
2. **Network Layer** → propagates status via protocols.
3. **Edge/Processing Layer** → aggregates and filters status.
4. **Application Layer** → consumes status for user-facing dashboards.
5. **Business Layer** → uses status for SLA monitoring and billing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU 2024 question worth 7 marks may ask: *"Illustrate the role of 'Status' across all layers of the IoT Reference Model."* Use the 5-layer mapping above as your answer skeleton.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Status is not academic — it powers critical engineering systems:

- **Smart Agriculture:** Status of soil moisture sensors decides when to trigger irrigation.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Status of a wearable ECG patch determines if the patient is being monitored.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Status of a robotic arm's joints determines if it is safe to operate.
- **Smart Cities:** Status of streetlights decides maintenance scheduling.
- **Connected Vehicles:** Status of tire-pressure sensors triggers dashboard warnings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Status Decision Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Status" is primarily a **conceptual and architectural** concept in IoT (Module 2 of PBITT604), the analytical component focuses on the **decision logic** used to compute and propagate status. We will build the complete logical framework step-by-step.

## 3.1 Formal Mathematical Representation of Status

Let an IoT device $D_i$ at time $t$ be characterized by a **Status Vector**:

$$
S_i(t) = \big[ C_i(t),\; O_i(t),\; F_i(t),\; P_i(t),\; T_i(t) \big]
$$

Where each component is defined as:

$$
C_i(t) = \text{Connection Status at time } t \in \{0, 1\}
$$

$$
O_i(t) = \text{Operational Status at time } t \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}
$$

The enumerated operational states are mapped as:

$$
O_i(t) =
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } D_i \text{ is in IDLE state} \\
1 & \text{if } D_i \text{ is in ACTIVE state} \\
2 & \text{if } D_i \text{ is in SLEEP state} \\
3 & \text{if } D_i \text{ is in FAULT state}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
F_i(t) = \text{Fault Code} \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}
$$

$$
P_i(t) = \text{Power Level (\%)} \in [0, 100]
$$

$$
T_i(t) = \text{Last-Heartbeat Timestamp (epoch seconds)}
$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the "Device Liveness" Function

The cloud platform must determine whether a device $D_i$ is **alive** at time $t$. This is derived from the last-heartbeat timestamp $T_i$ and the current time $t_{\text{now}}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the elapsed time since last heartbeat

$$
\Delta t = t_{\text{now}} - T_i(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Compare against the liveness threshold $\tau_{\text{live}}$

The device is considered **alive** if and only if the elapsed time is within the liveness window.

$$
\text{IsAlive}(D_i, t) =
\begin{cases}
\text{TRUE} & \text{if } \Delta t \leq \tau_{\text{live}} \\
\text{FALSE} & \text{if } \Delta t > \tau_{\text{live}}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert the boolean into the visible status flag

$$
\text{Status}(D_i, t) =
\begin{cases}
\text{ONLINE} & \text{if IsAlive}(D_i,t) = \text{TRUE} \;\wedge\; C_i(t) = 1 \\
\text{OFFLINE} & \text{if IsAlive}(D_i,t) = \text{FALSE} \;\vee\; C_i(t) = 0 \\
\text{FAULT} & \text{if } F_i(t) \neq 0
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Real-world numerical example

Suppose a temperature sensor $D_5$ last sent a heartbeat at:

$$
T_5 = 1735689600 \quad \text{(epoch seconds)}
$$

The current time is:

$$
t_{\text{now}} = 1735689720
$$

The liveness threshold is set as:

$$
