| **Device** | Hosts sensors/actuators, often resource-constrained |
| **Resource** | Software/hardware component that produces/consumes data |

### Operational Flow (Textual Walkthrough)

1. The **Device** senses the environment and produces **Resources** (sensor readings).
2. These resources feed into the **Service** layer, which standardizes them as services.
3. **Virtual Entities** are created and continuously synchronized with the device data.
4. **Applications** query VEs through Services to deliver value to the **User**.

---

## 2.3 The 5 Views of the IoT-A Reference Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-frequency KTU question** — "List the 5 views of the IoT Reference Architecture and explain any two."

| View | Concern | Stakeholder |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **Functional View (FV)** | What functions exist and how they interact | System Architect |
| **Information View (IV)** | Information flow, data models, semantics | Data Architect |
| **Deployment View (DV)** | How components map to physical/edge/cloud infrastructure | Infrastructure Engineer |
| **Operational View (OV)** | Tools, processes for operation & management | DevOps / SysAdmin |
| **Business View (BV)** | Business goals, KPIs, regulatory concerns | Business Stakeholder |

Each view addresses a specific stakeholder's concerns — making the IoT-A RA a **truly stakeholder-driven** model.

---

## 2.4 The Sub-Models — Detailed Decomposition

### A. Domain Model
- Represents **physical entities** (PE) and their **virtual counterparts** (VE).
- Each VE has:
  - A **unique ID**
  - **Attributes** (e.g., temperature, location)
  - **Methods** (e.g., query, update)
- Forms the foundation of the digital twin concept in IoT.

### B. Information Model
- Defines **how VEs and resources are described** in digital form.
- Uses **linked data / semantic web** principles (RDF, JSON-LD, etc.).
- Supports **augmentation** — additional metadata about an entity.
- Key relationship: **VE ↔ Service** (through Resources)

### C. Communication Model
- Provides an **end-to-end communication perspective**.
- Built on a layered protocol stack (similar to OSI):
  - **Application Layer** (e.g., CoAP, MQTT, HTTP)
  - **Transport Layer** (UDP / TCP)
  - **Network Layer** (IPv6 / 6LoWPAN)
  - **Link / Physical Layer** (IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, LTE-M)
- Identifies **Interactions** between system participants: *Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe, Streaming, Push, Pull*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Question:** "What are the interaction patterns supported by the IoT-A communication model?"
> **Answer:** Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe (Pub/Sub), Event-driven Push, Streaming, and Pull-based.

### D. Functional Model
- Decomposes the system into **Functional Groups (FGs)**.
- The most cited version contains **7 Functional Groups**:

| Functional Group | Purpose |
|------------------|---------|
| **Device** | Hosts sensors, actuators, VEs |
| **Communication** | Handles routing and protocol handling |
| **IoT Service** | Provides abstract interfaces to VEs and resources |
| **Virtual Entity** | Manages digital twins |
| **IoT Process Management** | Orchestrates workflows |
| **Service Organization** | Service discovery, registry |
| **Security, Trust & Privacy (STP)** | Identity, encryption, access control |

### E. Security, Trust & Privacy (STP) Model
- Cross-cutting — applies to **all other sub-models**.
- Covers: **Authentication, Authorization, Integrity, Confidentiality, Non-repudiation, Privacy, Trust management**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> IoT-A is primarily architectural, so the "formulas" below are conceptual formulas — KTU tests understanding, not calculation.

| Concept | Expression / Definition | Meaning |
|---------|------------------------|---------|
| Reference Model | $RM = \{DM,\ IM,\ CM,\ FM,\ STP\}$ | Set of sub-models |
| Virtual Entity Sync | $VE_{state}(t+1) = f(VE_{state}(t),\ Sensor(t))$ | Digital twin update rule |
| Trust Score | $T = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot c_i}{n}$ | Weighted credibility (concept) |
| Service Layer | $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$ | Set of services exposed |
| Information Flow | $I: A \rightarrow B$ where $A, B \in \{VE,\ Service,\ App\}$ | Data direction |
| Protocol Stack Depth | $P = \{App,\ Trans,\ Net,\ Link,\ Phys\}$ | Layered communication |
| Functional Groups | $FG = \{D,\ C,\ S,\ VE,\ PM,\ SO,\ ST\}$ | Functional decomposition |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of IoT-A |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Smart Healthcare** | VEs for patients, IoT services for vitals, secure remote monitoring |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Sensor VEs for machines, predictive maintenance services |
| **Smart Cities** | City-wide information model, unified communication across vendors |
| **Precision Agriculture** | Soil/weather VEs, edge-to-cloud information flow |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Package VEs with location & temperature tracking |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical & Conceptual Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> Since the IoT-A Reference Model is **architectural rather than mathematical**, the "derivations" below are **conceptual step-by-step derivations** that lead from the high-level problem to the formal solution (Reference Model). This matches the KTU 2024 scheme's focus on *descriptive analytics* and *structured reasoning* in Module 2.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From "IoT Problem" to "IoT-A Reference Model"

### Step 1: Identify the Problem Space
Modern IoT systems suffer from fragmentation:
- Different vendors use **different terminologies** for the same concept.
- A "smart bulb" in one system may not interact with a "smart switch" in another.
- Let this fragmented set be represented as $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n\}$.

$$
S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n\}
$$

Where each $S_i$ represents a proprietary IoT system with vocabulary $V_i$ and architecture $A_i$.

### Step 2: Recognize the Need for Abstraction
Since direct integration is infeasible, a **higher-level abstraction** is needed that subsumes all $S_i$:
The IoT-A project defines a **Reference Model (RM)** that contains the **supersets of concepts** common to all $S_i$:

$$
RM \supseteq \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} V_i \quad \text{and} \quad RM \supseteq \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

Here, $\bigcap V_i$ is the **intersection of vocabularies** — the universal concepts that every IoT system shares.

### Step 3: Define the Sub-Models
The RM is decomposed into 5 sub-models based on orthogonal concerns:
- Domain (DM) — entities
- Information (IM) — data
- Communication (CM) — connectivity
- Functional (FM) — capabilities
- Security (STP) — protection

Therefore:

$$
RM = DM \cup IM \cup CM \cup FM \cup STP
$$

### Step 4: Build the Reference Architecture (RA)
The RM is realized as a Reference Architecture (RA) with 5 stakeholder views:

$$
RA = \{FV,\ IV,\ DV,\ OV,\ BV\}
$$

Each view instantiates the relevant sub-model.

### Step 5: Conclude the Architecture
The final architecture of any IoT system $A_i$ can be mapped to the RA:

$$
\forall S_i : \exists \text{mapping} \quad \phi_i : S_i \rightarrow RA
$$

Meaning: every real IoT system can be **mapped to the common reference** — guaranteeing **interoperability by alignment**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Virtual Entity (VE) State Synchronization

The VE mirrors a Physical Entity (PE). At every time-step, the VE must reflect the PE's actual state.

### Step 1: Define the PE State Vector
A PE has a state vector at time $t$:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{PE}(t) = [x_1(t),\ x_2(t),\ \dots,\ x_m(t)]^\top
$$

Where each $x_i(t)$ is an attribute (e.g., temperature, location).

### Step 2: The Sensor Reading
A sensor on the device provides noisy samples of the PE state:

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = H \cdot \mathbf{x}_{PE}(t) + \mathbf{n}(t)
$$

Where $H$ is the observation matrix and $\mathbf{n}(t)$ is measurement noise.

### Step 3: Virtual Entity Update Rule
The VE updates its state using the sensor reading:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{VE}(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_{VE}(t) + K \cdot (\mathbf{y}(t) - H \cdot \mathbf{x}_{VE}(t))
$$

This is essentially a **Kalman-style update**, where $K$ is the correction gain. In practice, simpler **last-write-wins** or **smoothing** strategies are used.

### Step 4: Convergence Condition
The VE remains in sync with the PE when the error is bounded:

$$
E(t) = \vert \mathbf{x}_{VE}(t) - \mathbf{x}_{PE}(t) \vert \le \epsilon
$$

Where $\epsilon$ is a small acceptable error threshold.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping KTU Sub-Questions to Sub-Models

| Typical KTU Sub-Question | Sub-Model Required | Key Concept Tested |
|--------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| "What is a Virtual Entity?" | Domain Model | Digital twin concept |
| "Explain service exposure in IoT." | Functional / Service Model | Service orientation |
| "What protocols are used in IoT-A?" | Communication Model | Protocol stack |
| "How is data represented in IoT-A?" | Information Model | Semantic description |
| "What are the views of IoT-A?" | Reference Architecture | FV, IV, DV, OV, BV |
| "How is trust managed in IoT?" | STP Model | Authentication, privacy |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Decomposition — Functional View (FV) in IoT-A

The FV is described using a **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** approach.

### Step 1: Identify Functional Groups (FGs)
A canonical IoT-A system has the following 7 FGs:
- Device (D)
- Communication (C)
- IoT Service (S)
- Virtual Entity (VE)
- Process Management (PM)
- Service Organization (SO)
- Security, Trust & Privacy (ST)

### Step 2: Identify Functional Components (FCs) inside each FG
For example, the **Device FG** contains:
- *Sensing FC* — produces data
- *Actuation FC* — performs actions
- *Device Configuration FC* — manages device settings

### Step 3: Define FC Interactions
FCs communicate via **Interaction Patterns** defined in the Communication Model:
- *Request-Response* (e.g., "GET temperature")
- *Publish-Subscribe* (e.g., MQTT topic subscription)
- *Push* (e.g., alarm notification)

### Step 4: Form the Functional Decomposition Diagram
The result is a layered functional block diagram that maps to a real implementation (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub).

---

## 3.5 Communication Model — Interaction Pattern Mapping

| Pattern | Latency Profile | Use Case in IoT-A |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Request-Response** | Low–Medium | Querying a single VE attribute |
| **Publish-Subscribe** | Medium | Sensor data streams |
| **Event-Stream (Push)** | Very Low | Alarms, anomalies |
| **Pull** | High | Bulk data retrieval |
| **Streaming** | Continuous | Audio/video IoT |

---

## 3.6 Why IoT-A Is **Stakeholder-Driven** (Derivation)

### Step 1: Identify Stakeholders
- Business owner, system architect, developer, network engineer, end-user, security officer.

### Step 2: Map Each Stakeholder to a Concern
- Business owner → BV
- Architect → FV
- Data engineer → IV
- Infra engineer → DV
- Ops engineer → OV

### Step 3: Conclude
Since concerns differ, the architecture is **viewed** differently by each stakeholder:

$$
\forall \text{ stakeholder } k \in K : \exists \text{ view } V_k \text{ s.t. } V_k \text{ satisfies concern of } k
$$

This justifies the **5-view structure** of IoT-A.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, I have carefully named all Mermaid nodes using alphanumeric prefixes and avoided all reserved keywords inside quoted labels.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level IoT-A Reference Model Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Physical World]
    nodeB[Domain Model]
    nodeC[Information Model]
    nodeD[Communication Model]
    nodeE[Functional Model]
    nodeF[Security Trust Privacy Model]
    nodeG[IoT Reference Architecture]
    nodeH[Functional View FV]
    nodeI[Information View IV]
    nodeJ[Deployment View DV]
    nodeK[Operational View OV]
    nodeL[Business View BV]
    nodeM[Concrete IoT System]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -.crosscutting.-> nodeC
    nodeF -.crosscutting.-> nodeD
    nodeF -.crosscutting.-> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — IoT-A Functional View (7 Functional Groups)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[User]
    node2[Application]
    node3[IoT Service FG]
    node4[Process Management FG]
    node5[Virtual Entity FG]
    node6[Service Organization FG]
    node7[Communication FG]
    node8[Device FG]
    node9[Security Trust Privacy FG]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node5
    node5 --> node8
    node8 --> node7
    node3 --> node6
    node9 -.secures.-> node3
    node9 -.secures.-> node7
    node9 -.secures.-> node8
    node4 --> node3
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Communication Model Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Application Layer: CoAP MQTT HTTP]
    nodeB[Transport Layer: TCP UDP]
    nodeC[Network Layer: IPv6 6LoWPAN RPL]
    nodeD[Link Layer: IEEE 802.15.4 BLE]
    nodeE[Physical Layer: Radio Frequencies]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Virtual Entity (Digital Twin) Synchronization Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Physical Entity PE]
    node2[Sensor]
    node3[Device Resource]
    node4[IoT Service]
    node5[Virtual Entity VE]
    node6[Application]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 -.bi directional sync.-> node3
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Mapping Real IoT Systems to IoT-A Reference Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Real IoT System: AWS IoT Core]
    nodeB[FV: Device Gateway Registry]
    nodeC[IV: Shadow Service JSON]
    nodeD[DV: Cloud Edge Devices]
    nodeE[OV: CloudWatch Monitoring]
    nodeF[BV: Pay per device pricing]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Security Trust Privacy Cross-Cutting Concern

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA[Authentication: Identity Verification]
    nodeB[Authorization: Access Control]
    nodeC[Integrity: Data Not Tampered]
    nodeD[Confidentiality: Encrypted Data]
    nodeE[Non Repudiation: Audit Trail]
    nodeF[Privacy: Data Minimization]
    nodeG[Trust: Reputation Score]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**Define the IoT-A Reference Model. How is it different from the IoT Reference Architecture?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU 2024 Scheme, CO2, Bloom: Remember/Understand):**
>
> The **IoT-A Reference Model (RM)** is a technology-independent, abstract description of an IoT system that defines its **key concepts, components, and relationships** using a common vocabulary. It is built from 5 sub-models: Domain, Information, Communication, Functional, and Security/Trust/Privacy.
>
> The **IoT Reference Architecture (RA)**, on the other hand, is the **realization** of the RM into a set of **stakeholder-specific views** — Functional, Information, Deployment, Operational, and Business Views.
>
> In short, the RM answers **"WHAT"** an IoT system is, while the RA answers **"HOW"** the RM is implemented.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List the five views of the IoT-A Reference Architecture. Write a one-line description for each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> 1. **Functional View (FV)** — Describes the functions of an IoT system and their interactions.
> 2. **Information View (IV)** — Describes information flow, data models, and semantics.
> 3. **Deployment View (DV)** — Maps functions to physical/edge/cloud infrastructure.
> 4. **Operational View (OV)** — Describes tools and processes for system operation.
> 5. **Business View (BV)** — Captures business goals, KPIs, and regulatory concerns.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Part B questions carry 14 marks**, usually with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Two alternative choices are provided.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Pattern — 2024 Scheme]

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain the **5 sub-models of the IoT-A Reference Model** in detail, with examples. *(7 marks)*
  *(b)* Describe the **IoT-A Conceptual Model** with a neat block diagram. Identify the role of **Virtual Entities** and **Services**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) The 5 Sub-Models of the IoT-A Reference Model — 7 Marks**

The IoT-A Reference Model is structured into 5 orthogonal sub-models:

**1. Domain Model — 1.5 Marks**
- Represents **physical and virtual entities**.
- A **Physical Entity (PE)** is a real-world object (e.g., a car).
- A **Virtual Entity (VE)** is its digital representation, holding attributes (location, speed) and methods.
- Foundation of the **digital twin** concept.

**2. Information Model — 1.5 Marks**
- Describes **how entities and resources are represented digitally**.
- Uses **semantic web standards** (RDF, JSON-LD).
- Supports **information augmentation** — adding metadata.
- Example: A temperature VE holds not just the current value, but also the unit, timestamp, sensor ID, and accuracy.

**3. Communication Model — 1.5 Marks**
- Defines the **end-to-end protocol stack** for IoT.
- Identifies **interaction patterns**:
  - Request-Response
  - Publish-Subscribe
  - Push (Event)
  - Pull
  - Streaming
- Stack: App → Transport → Network → Link → Physical.

**4. Functional Model — 1.5 Marks**
- Decomposes IoT system into **7 Functional Groups (FGs)**:
  Device, Communication, IoT Service, Virtual Entity, Process Management, Service Organization, Security/Trust/Privacy.
- Each FG contains multiple **Functional Components (FCs)**.

**5. Security, Trust & Privacy (STP) Model — 1 Mark**
- **Cross-cutting** — applies to every other sub-model.
- Handles authentication, authorization, integrity, confidentiality, privacy, and trust.

---

**(b) The IoT-A Conceptual Model — 7 Marks**

The **Conceptual Model (CM)** formalizes the relationships between the major components identified in the Reference Model.

**Core Components (1.5 Marks):**
- **User** — Human or software agent using IoT services.
- **Application** — Business logic (e.g., dashboard, analytics).
- **Service** — A function exposed through a well-defined interface.
- **Virtual Entity (VE)** — Digital twin of a real-world object.
- **Resource** — A software/hardware element producing/consuming data.
- **Device** — Hosts sensors/actuators and resources.

**Interactions (2 Marks):**
- A Device hosts Resources.
- Resources feed Services.
- Services provide data to VEs.
- Applications query VEs through Services.
- Users interact with Applications.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**
```
User → Application → Service → VE ← Resource ← Device
                       ↑                      ↑
                       └──── Service Org ─────┘
                  (Secured by STP layer)
```

**Role of Virtual Entities (0.75 Mark):**
- Decouple the physical world from applications.
- Enable **digital twin** behavior — applications work on VEs without directly accessing hardware.

**Role of Services (0.75 Mark):**
- Provide **standardized, reusable** interfaces to VE data and resources.
- Enable **interoperability** across heterogeneous devices.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** *(a)* What are the **5 views of the IoT-A Reference Architecture**? Explain the **Functional View and Information View** in detail. *(7 marks)*
  *(b)* Discuss the **Communication Model** of the IoT-A Reference Model with its **interaction patterns and protocol stack**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) The 5 Views — 7 Marks**

**1. Functional View (FV) — 3.5 Marks**
- **Concern:** What functions the IoT system performs and how they interact.
- **Structure:** Service-Oriented, organized into **7 Functional Groups** (Device, Communication, IoT Service, VE, Process Management, Service Organization, STP).
- **Example:** In a smart building, the FV identifies that the Lighting Service and the HVAC Service both consume Motion VE data.

**2. Information View (IV) — 3.5 Marks**
- **Concern:** How data is modeled, exchanged, and semantically enriched.
- **Key concepts:**
  - **Entities and Attributes** — VEs and their properties.
  - **Linked Data** — Semantic relationships between VEs.
  - **Information Augmentation** — Adding extra metadata (e.g., weather context to a temperature reading).
- **Example:** A temperature VE may have an augmented property `unit: "Celsius"`, `accuracy: "±0.5°C"`.

**Brief mention of other views (formative knowledge):**
- **Deployment View (DV)** — Maps FGs to physical/edge/cloud.
- **Operational View (OV)** — Tooling and process management.
- **Business View (BV)** — Business goals and regulation.

---

**(b) Communication Model — 7 Marks**

**End-to-End Communication Perspective (2 Marks):**
The Communication Model views IoT communication as a **chain of layered protocols** spanning the device to the cloud.

**Protocol Stack (2 Marks):**
| Layer | Protocols |
|-------|-----------|
| Application | CoAP, MQTT, HTTP |
| Transport | TCP, UDP |
| Network | IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL |
| Link | IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, LoRa |
| Physical | Radio Frequencies (sub-GHz, 2.4 GHz) |

**Interaction Patterns (3 Marks):**
1. **Request-Response** — Client sends request, server replies (e.g., GET sensor value).
2. **Publish-Subscribe** — Publisher emits to a topic, subscribers receive (e.g., MQTT broker).
3. **Event/Push** — Server pushes notifications (e.g., alarm triggered).
4. **Pull** — Bulk data retrieval by the client.
5. **Streaming** — Continuous data flow (e.g., video IoT).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Reference Model with Reference Architecture** — RM = concepts/structure; RA = realization/views. Marks are deducted for mixing them.
> 2. **Listing views without explaining them** — Always describe at least 2 views in detail for 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the 5th sub-model (STP)** — Many students list only 4; STP must always be included.
> 4. **Not mentioning Virtual Entities** — A Virtual Entity is *the* core concept of IoT-A; missing it costs marks.
> 5. **Missing cross-cutting nature of STP** — STP is NOT a standalone model with its own components only — it applies to all other sub-models.
> 6. **Mixing up IoT-A with OSI** — IoT-A is an *IoT architecture* model, not a network stack.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Pointers — KTU 2024 Module 2, IoT-A Reference Model:**

- ⭐ **IoT-A** = European FP7 project; produced the **first common architectural approach** for IoT.
- ⭐ **Reference Model (RM)** = **5 sub-models**: Domain, Information, Communication, Functional, Security/Trust/Privacy.
- ⭐ **Reference Architecture (RA)** = **5 views**: Functional, Information, Deployment, Operational, Business.
- ⭐ **Virtual Entity (VE)** = Digital twin of a physical entity; foundation of the conceptual model.
- ⭐ **Resource** = Sensor/actuator data exposed by a device; feeds services.
- ⭐ **Service** = Standardized, reusable interface to VE/RES data.
- ⭐ **Functional View (FV)** = Organized into **7 Functional Groups (FGs)**.
- ⭐ **Communication Model** = Protocol stack: App → Transport → Network → Link → Physical.
- ⭐ **Interaction Patterns** = Request-Response, Pub/Sub, Push, Pull, Streaming.
- ⭐ **STP Model** = **Cross-cutting** — applies to ALL other sub-models.
- ⭐ **Information View** = Uses **semantic web** principles; supports **augmentation**.
- ⭐ **Business View** = Captures business goals, KPIs, regulations.
- ⭐ **Key exam distinction:** RM = WHAT, RA = HOW.
- ⭐ **Common exam mantra:** "VE decouples applications from physical devices."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Software Platforms: Node-RED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Software Platforms: Node-RED

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Node-RED

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Node-RED** is an **open-source, flow-based development tool** originally developed by **IBM's Emerging Technology Services** in **2013** and now maintained by the **OpenJS Foundation**. It is a **visual programming environment** built on top of **Node.js** that allows developers, system integrators, and IoT enthusiasts to wire together hardware devices, Application Programming Interfaces (APIs), and online services by chaining together functional blocks called **"nodes"** using a browser-based flow editor.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBITT604 – Internet of Things)**, Node-RED is positioned as a **lightweight IoT middleware / application-layer platform** that sits between the **perception layer (sensors/actuators)** and the **application layer (dashboards, cloud services)**, enabling rapid prototyping of IoT pipelines without writing extensive boilerplate code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Remember Verbatim):**
> *"Node-RED is a flow-based, visual programming tool for wiring together hardware devices, APIs, and online services, particularly suited for IoT applications. It uses a browser-based editor and runs on Node.js."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are building a **water supply system** in your house. You don't pour cement, solder copper, or weld steel pipes yourself. Instead, you go to a hardware store, pick up **pre-made pipe segments** (elbows, T-junctions, valves, taps), and simply **click-snap them together** to route water from the overhead tank to your kitchen sink.

Node-RED works **exactly the same way**, but for **data instead of water**:

- The **pre-made pipe segments** → these are called **"nodes"** (e.g., an MQTT-in node, a function node, a dashboard gauge node).
- The **water** flowing through the pipes → this is the **"message"** (`msg` object, most commonly `msg.payload`).
- The **snap-connectors** joining the pipes → these are the **"wires"** in the flow editor.
- The **plumber** designing the layout → that's **you**, dragging and dropping nodes onto a **canvas** in a web browser.
- The **water tank supplying everything** → the **Node.js runtime** executing the flows in the background.

You never "weld" code from scratch for routing; you just visually assemble logic, and Node-RED compiles this visual graph into an actual running data pipeline under the hood.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for KTU exams:** Examiners love questions framed around "Explain Node-RED using a real-world analogy." The pipe-and-valve metaphor scores full marks because it captures **flow-based programming**, **node modularity**, and **event-driven execution** in one go.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology to Memorize

| Term | Definition | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Flow** | A tab representing a collection of wired nodes | Logical grouping of an IoT pipeline |
| **Node** | A runtime process performing a single function | Atomic unit of work in Node-RED |
| **Wire** | A connection carrying `msg` objects between nodes | Defines the data path |
| **msg** | JavaScript object passed between nodes (default key: `msg.payload`) | The "blood" of every flow |
| **Context** | Persistent storage (flow / global / node scope) | Holds state across messages |
| **Palette** | The left sidebar listing all installed nodes | Node library browser |
| **Subflow** | A reusable module created from a group of nodes | Encapsulation & reusability |
| **Deploy** | The act of pushing the flow graph to the runtime | Activates changes |
| **Runtime** | The Node.js engine executing flows in the background | The "engine" under the hood |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Message Object Structure (Board Exam Favorite):**
> ```json
> {
>   "payload": "<actual data>",
>   "topic": "sensor/temperature",
>   "_msgid": "unique-id"
> }
> ```
> The `msg.payload` property is the **de facto convention** for the data being transmitted between nodes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown: The "Why" and "How"

Node-RED's architecture is **deliberately thin and modular**, designed around three concentric layers. Understanding this layering is critical for 14-mark KTU questions.

#### Layer 1 — The Node.js Runtime (The Engine)
- Node-RED is built atop **Node.js**, a JavaScript runtime built on Google's **V8 engine**.
- This means it is **inherently event-driven** and **non-blocking**, perfectly suited for asynchronous IoT data streams.
- The runtime continuously listens on **port 1880** (HTTP) for the editor UI and for incoming connections.
- It is launched via the command: `node-red` or `node red.js`.

#### Layer 2 — The Flows Engine (The Brain)
- Maintains the **flow registry**, executes nodes in topological order, and routes messages.
- Responsible for **dependency resolution**: a downstream node does not fire until its upstream predecessor completes (by default, asynchronous behavior is configurable).
- Implements **context stores** for state retention (default: in-memory; alternatives: file-based, Redis).

#### Layer 3 — The Browser-Based Flow Editor (The Cockpit)
- A **web-based drag-and-drop UI** served at `http://localhost:1880`.
- Communicates with the runtime over **WebSockets** for live updates and **REST APIs** for deployment.
- Provides palette management, configuration panels, debug sidebar, and a JSON flow export.

### 2.2 The Five Core Node Categories (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** KTU examiners expect you to **classify nodes into categories** and give **one example per category**. Memorize this table — it appears in 80% of Part A questions.

| Category | Purpose | Standard Examples | Typical Use in IoT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Nodes** | Receive external data | `mqtt in`, `http in`, `tcp in`, `inject` | Reading sensor values, REST endpoints |
| **Output Nodes** | Send data outward | `mqtt out`, `http response`, `tcp out`, `debug` | Publishing to broker, replying to clients |
| **Function Nodes** | Custom logic (JS code) | `function`, `change`, `switch`, `template` | Parsing JSON, decision branching |
| **Social / Cloud Nodes** | Integrate with web services | `twitter`, `email`, `aws`, `azure iot hub` | Cloud upload, notifications |
| **Storage / Analytics Nodes** | Persistence & analysis | `file`, `csv`, `mongodb` | Logging sensor data |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Node-RED is a software platform, the "formulas" are mostly **architectural constants, message-passing rules, and deployment metrics**. Use this table as a last-minute revision anchor.

| Concept | Rule / Value | Unit / Format | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default HTTP Port | **1880** | TCP Port | Editor UI + API endpoint |
| MQTT Default Broker Port | **1883** | TCP Port | Used by `mqtt in/out` nodes |
| Default Message Property | `msg.payload` | String / Buffer / Object | The convention for data carriage |
| Deployment Modes | **Full / Modified Flows / Modified Nodes** | 3 modes | Controls scope of redeploy |
| Context Scopes | `flow`, `global`, `node` | 3 scopes | State retention hierarchy |
| Default Log Levels | `fatal / error / warn / info / debug / trace` | 6 levels | Configurable in `settings.js` |
| Install Command | `npm install -g node-red` | CLI | Global installation |
| Start Command | `node-red` | CLI | Launches the runtime |
| Project File Format | `.json` | JSON | Exports/imports complete flows |
| Flow Concurrency | **1 message per node at a time** (by default) | Behavior | Per-node queueing model |
| `msg.parts` Structure | `{id, index, count}` | Object | Used for stream splitting/rejoining |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Formatting Note:** The table above avoids the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside cell content to prevent markdown table breaking. Use `\vert` or `\mid` whenever a math delimiter is needed inside a markdown cell.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** A `mqtt in` node reads soil-moisture data from an ESP32; a `switch` node decides if irrigation is needed; an `http request` node triggers a relay via a REST API.
- **Industrial Monitoring:** A `modbus` node reads PLC registers; a `function` node computes moving averages; a `dashboard` node plots live gauges for operators.
- **Smart Home Hubs:** Node-RED on a Raspberry Pi acts as the central broker, integrating Zigbee, Z-Wave, and MQTT devices into a unified Home Assistant dashboard.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Streams BLE heart-rate data through `tcp in` → parses with `json` → pushes alerts via `twilio` SMS node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Node-RED is preferred in KTU practicals:** It offers **lower time-to-prototype** compared to writing raw Python/C++ MQTT clients, making it ideal for the **6th-semester mini-project** and the **IoT laboratory component** of PBITT604.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The Message Lifecycle

For a 14-mark question, examiners often ask you to **"Trace the path of a message from sensor to dashboard."** Below is the **exhaustive analytical matrix** of how a typical IoT message propagates through a Node-RED flow.

Let us define the system parameters clearly:

$$\text{Sensor Reading} = S(t), \quad \text{Sampling Interval} = \Delta t, \quad \text{Broker Topic} = T$$

**Step 1 — Physical Layer (Sensor)**
The physical sensor (e.g., DHT22) produces an analog/digital reading $S(t)$ at every $\Delta t$ interval. An MCU (e.g., NodeMCU) reads this and publishes it as a JSON string over MQTT.

$$\text{Payload}_{\text{raw}} = \{\text{temp} : S(t), \text{hum} : H(t)\}$$

**Step 2 — Broker Layer (MQTT)**
The MQTT broker (e.g., Mosquitto) receives the publish and forwards it to all subscribers on topic $T$ — which includes our `mqtt in` node.

**Step 3 — Input Node (`mqtt in`)**
The `mqtt in` node constructs the standard `msg` object:

$$\text{msg} = \{\text{payload}: \text{Payload}_{\text{raw}}, \text{topic}: T, \text{qos}: 1, \text{retain}: \text{false}\}$$

**Step 4 — Parsing Layer (`json` node)**
A `json` node parses the stringified `msg.payload` into a real JavaScript object so that downstream nodes can access `msg.payload.temp` and `msg.payload.hum` as numeric values.

$$\text{msg.payload} \xRightarrow{\text{parse}} \{\text{temp}: 28.4, \text{hum}: 65.2\}$$

**Step 5 — Transformation Layer (`function` node)**
A `function` node applies custom logic. For example, converting temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Fahrenheit}} &= T_{\text{Celsius}} \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 \\
&= 28.4 \times 1.8 + 32 \\
&= 83.12 \,^{\circ}\text{F}
\end{aligned}$$

The function returns a modified `msg`:
```javascript
msg.payload.tempF = msg.payload.temp * 9/5 + 32;
return msg;
```

**Step 6 — Decision Layer (`switch` node)**
A `switch` node checks a condition and routes the message to one of multiple output wires:

$$\text{If } T_{\text{Celsius}} > 30 \rightarrow \text{Output 1 (Alert path)} \quad \text{else} \rightarrow \text{Output 2 (Normal path)}$$

**Step 7 — Output Layer (`mqtt out` or `ui_gauge`)**
The message reaches the visualization or actuation node. A `ui_gauge` dashboard node renders a live dial; a `mqtt out` node re-publishes a control command to an actuator.

**Step 8 — Storage Layer (`influxdb` / `csv` node)**
In parallel, a tap from the wire feeds a database node for historical analytics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board-Exam Insight:** The `msg` object is **passed by reference in JavaScript semantics** within a single flow run, but each node receives an **immutable copy** (best practice). If a node does not return the `msg`, the flow **terminates immediately**. This is a classic 3-mark viva question.

### 3.2 Context Store Equations (State Retention)

Node-RED's three context scopes follow a **hierarchical visibility rule**:

$$\text{Visibility}(\text{node}) \subset \text{Visibility}(\text{flow}) \subset \text{Visibility}(\text{global})$$

A node can read/write its own context and any higher scope:

| Scope | Lifetime | API |
|---|---|---|
| `node` | Single node instance | `node.context().get("key")` |
| `flow` | Entire flow tab | `flow.get("key")` |
| `global` | All flows in the instance | `global.get("key")` |

Example: A counter shared across all flows:

```javascript
// In a function node
var count = global.get("counter") || 0;
count += 1;
global.set("counter", count);
msg.payload = count;
return msg;
```

### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Installation Walkthrough (For Practical Exams)

**Step A — Verify Node.js installation:**

```bash
node --version    # Expected: v18.x or higher
npm --version     # Expected: 9.x or higher
```

**Step B — Install Node-RED globally:**

```bash
npm install -g --unsafe-perm node-red
```

**Step C — Launch the runtime:**

```bash
node-red
```

Expected console output:

$$\text{Server now running at } \texttt{http://127.0.0.1:1880/}$$

**Step D — Open the editor:** Navigate to `http://localhost:1880` in any modern browser.

**Step E — Install additional nodes via Palette Manager or CLI:**

```bash
cd ~/.node-red
npm install node-red-dashboard
```

**Step F — Restart Node-RED** to register the new nodes, then refresh the browser.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Node-RED Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph BROWSER["Browser-Based Editor :1880"]
        UI["Flow Canvas Drag and Drop"]
        PAL["Palette Manager Node Library"]
        CFG["Configuration Panels"]
        DBG["Debug Sidebar Live Logs"]
    end

    subgraph RUNTIME["Node.js Runtime :1880"]
        CORE["Flows Engine Topological Executor"]
        CTX["Context Store flow global node"]
        AUTH["Admin API Authentication Layer"]
        CFGJS["settings.js Configuration"]
    end

    subgraph FLOWS["User-Defined Flows"]
        FIN["Input Nodes mqtt in http in inject"]
        FLOGIC["Function Nodes change switch template function"]
        FOUT["Output Nodes mqtt out http response debug ui gauge"]
    end

    EXT["External World Sensors Brokers Cloud APIs"]
    DASH["Dashboard Web UI :1880/ui"]

    UI -->|WebSocket Live Sync| CORE
    PAL -->|npm Registry| CORE
    CFG -->|POST /flows| CORE
    DBG <-->|Log Stream| CORE

    CORE --> CTX
    CORE --> AUTH
    CORE --> CFGJS
    CORE --> FLOWS

    FIN -->|msg object| FLOGIC
    FLOGIC -->|msg object| FOUT
    FLOWS -->|Wire Data| EXT
    FOUT -->|HTTP WebSocket| DASH
    EXT -->|MQTT HTTP TCP| FIN
```

### 4.2 A Canonical IoT Flow: Sensor → Dashboard (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SENSOR_LAYER["Perception Layer"]
        SENS["DHT22 Sensor"]
        MCU["ESP32 Microcontroller"]
    end

    subgraph BROKER["Network Layer"]
        MOSQ["Mosquitto MQTT Broker :1883"]
    end

    subgraph NR_INSTANCE["Node-RED Application Layer :1880"]
        IN_NODE["mqtt in Node Topic sensor dht"]
        PARSE["json Node Parse String"]
        FUNC["function Node Compute Heat Index"]
        SWITCH["switch Node Threshold 30 C"]
        GAUGE["ui gauge Node Dashboard"]
        CHART["ui chart Node Time Series"]
        ALERT["email Node Alert Path"]
    end

    subgraph VIS_LAYER["Visualization Layer"]
        DASH_WEB["Web Dashboard :1880 ui"]
    end

    SENS -->|Digital Signal| MCU
    MCU -->|Publish JSON| MOSQ
    MOSQ -->|Subscribe| IN_NODE
    IN_NODE -->|msg payload string| PARSE
    PARSE -->|msg payload object| FUNC
    FUNC -->|msg enriched| SWITCH
    SWITCH -->|Below Threshold| GAUGE
    SWITCH -->|Below Threshold| CHART
    SWITCH -->|Above Threshold| ALERT
    GAUGE -->|WebSocket Push| DASH_WEB
    CHART -->|WebSocket Push| DASH_WEB
    ALERT -->|SMTP| EMAIL_USER["Operator Email"]
```

### 4.3 Node Execution Lifecycle (State Machine)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Node Instantiated by Runtime"] --> B["input Listener Registered"]
    B --> C["Waiting for msg on input wire"]
    C -->|msg Arrives| D["Node Handler Function Invoked"]
    D --> E{"Returns msg"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Message Routed to Next Node via Wire"]
    E -->|No Return| G["Flow Terminated Silent Drop"]
    D -->|Error Thrown| H["Catch Node Triggered or Error Logged"]
    F --> C
    H --> C
    G --> C
```

### 4.4 Context Scope Hierarchy (Conceptual Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    G["Global Context Visible to ALL Flows"]
    F1["Flow A Context Visible only to Flow A"]
    F2["Flow B Context Visible only to Flow B"]
    N1A["Node 1 in Flow A"]
    N2A["Node 2 in Flow A"]
    N1B["Node 1 in Flow B"]

    G --> F1
    G --> F2
    F1 --> N1A
    F1 --> N2A
    F2 --> N1B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to *"draw the Node-RED architecture"*, you may either (a) reproduce the four diagrams above in your answer sheet by hand, or (b) describe the layered architecture in text. Always **label the three layers** (Browser Editor, Node.js Runtime, User Flows) and **cite port 1880 explicitly** — this is a recurring valuation marker.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Node-RED. List any four categories of nodes available in Node-RED with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Node-RED is an **open-source, flow-based, visual programming tool** developed by IBM in 2013, built on Node.js, used for wiring together hardware devices, APIs, and online services — particularly in IoT applications. It uses a browser-based editor accessible at the default port **1880**.

**Four Node Categories with Examples (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

| Category | Example Node |
|---|---|
| Input Nodes | `mqtt in` (subscribes to MQTT topic) |
| Output Nodes | `mqtt out` (publishes to MQTT topic) |
| Function Nodes | `function` (executes custom JavaScript) |
| Social/Storage Nodes | `email` (sends alert) or `file` (logs to disk) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the category names without **examples**, losing 2 of 3 marks. Always pair category → example → 1-line use case.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Solved Paper]**
**Explain the role of the `msg` object in Node-RED. What is the default property used for data carriage?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `msg` object is a **JavaScript object** that flows between nodes through the wires of a flow. It is the **fundamental data carrier** in Node-RED's event-driven architecture. Each node receives a `msg`, processes it (optionally modifying properties), and either returns a new/modified `msg` to pass downstream or returns `null` to terminate the flow.

**Default Property (1 Mark):** `msg.payload` is the standard, convention-driven property used for the actual data being transmitted. Other optional properties include `msg.topic`, `msg._msgid`, `msg.parts`, and `msg.headers`.

**Example (1 Mark):**
```json
{ "payload": 28.4, "topic": "sensor/temp", "_msgid": "abc123" }
```

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 2 Part B questions offer internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of Node-RED**. Label all three layers and the default port. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Node-RED's architecture consists of **three interconnected layers** working in tandem:

1. **Browser-Based Flow Editor (Presentation Layer):** A web UI served at `http://localhost:1880`. It provides the drag-and-drop canvas, palette manager, configuration panels, and the debug sidebar. It communicates with the runtime over **WebSockets** (for live updates) and **HTTP REST APIs** (for deployment).

2. **Node.js Runtime (Middleware / Engine Layer):** The core of Node-RED. It hosts the **flows engine** (which executes nodes in topological order), the **context store** (for state retention), the **admin API** (for authentication & flow management), and the **`settings.js`** configuration file. Default port: **1880**.

3. **User-Defined Flows (Application Layer):** Collections of nodes wired together on tabs. They include **input nodes** (e.g., `mqtt in`, `http in`), **function nodes** (e.g., `change`, `switch`, `function`), and **output nodes** (e.g., `mqtt out`, `ui gauge`, `debug`).

**Block Diagram (Hand-draw in exam, 2 Marks):**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Browser Editor (Port 1880)                    │
│  [Canvas] [Palette] [Config] [Debug]           │
└────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                     │ WebSocket + REST
┌────────────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
│  Node.js Runtime (Port 1880)                   │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Flows Engine │ Context Store │ Admin API │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                     │ msg objects
┌────────────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
│  User Flows (Input → Function → Output)        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

**(b)** Design a Node-RED flow that **reads temperature data from an MQTT topic**, converts it from Celsius to Fahrenheit, and **displays it on a dashboard gauge**. List the nodes used and explain each step. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — `mqtt in` node (Input):** Configure with broker `localhost:1883`, topic `sensor/temp`, QoS `1`. The payload is a JSON string: `{"temp": 28.4}`.

**Step 2 — `json` node (Parser):** Converts the stringified `msg.payload` into a real object so `msg.payload.temp` becomes accessible as a number.

**Step 3 — `function` node (Transform):** Applies the Celsius-to-Fahrenheit conversion:

$$T_F = T_C \times \frac{9}{5} + 32$$

```javascript
var celsius = msg.payload.temp;
msg.payload.tempF = celsius * 9/5 + 32;
return msg;
```

**Step 4 — `change` node (Optional Routing):** Sets `msg.payload` to `msg.payload.tempF` if the dashboard expects a single scalar.

**Step 5 — `ui gauge` node (Visualization):** Part of the `node-red-dashboard` package. Configure with label "Temperature (°F)", min `0`, max `120`, and a red color band for > 90 °F. Accessible at `http://localhost:1880/ui`.

**Step 6 — Deploy** with the "Deploy" button (full or modified flow) and verify in the dashboard.

**Flow Diagram (2 Marks — drawn in exam):**
```
[mqtt in: sensor/temp] → [json] → [function: C→F] → [ui gauge]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Part A & B):** Examiners **deduct 1 mark** if students forget to mention **port 1880** and **MQTT port 1883**. Also, **never write raw Python/C++** when asked for a Node-RED solution — the question tests **flow-based design**, not code alternatives.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Context Storage in Node-RED**. Compare the three context scopes with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Context storage in Node-RED is a **persistent key-value store** used to retain state across messages. Node-RED provides **three hierarchical scopes**, each differing in lifetime and visibility:

| Scope | Lifetime | Visibility | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **`node`** | Lifetime of a single node instance | Only that node | Storing a node's last received timestamp |
| **`flow`** | Lifetime of a flow tab | All nodes within the same flow | Sharing a moving-average buffer between two function nodes |
| **`global`** | Lifetime of the Node-RED instance | All flows in the instance | A global device-count shared across 5 flows |

**Syntax (2 Marks):**
```javascript
// Read
var val = flow.get("sum") || 0;
var gval = global.get("threshold");
// Write
flow.set("sum", val + msg.payload);
global.set("threshold", 30);
```

**Hierarchical Rule (1 Mark):** $\text{node} \subset \text{flow} \subset \text{global}$. A node can access its own context and any higher-level scope, but **not vice versa** (a global function cannot read a `flow` scope of Flow A).

**Backends:** Default = in-memory; alternatives = file-based (`localfilesystem`), Redis, or custom plugins installed via npm.

---

**(b)** What is a **Subflow** in Node-RED? Explain with an example how it promotes **modularity and reusability** in large IoT applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

A **Subflow** is a **user-defined composite node** that encapsulates a group of existing nodes into a single reusable black-box with **configurable input, output, and properties**. It is Node-RED's native mechanism for **abstraction and modularization**.

**Creation Steps (3 Marks):**
1. Select a group of nodes on the canvas.
2. Right-click → **"Selection → Subflow from selection"**.
3. Define the subflow's input ports, output ports, and environment variables in the subflow properties dialog.
4. The subflow now appears as a **single draggable node** in the palette.

**Example:** Suppose three different IoT flows (Home, Office, Greenhouse) all need to **parse MQTT temperature, convert to Fahrenheit, and log to InfluxDB**. Without a subflow, you would duplicate 5 nodes × 3 times = 15 nodes. With a subflow called `ProcessTemp`, you define it once and **instantiate it 3 times**, passing different MQTT topics via environment properties.

**Benefits (2 Marks):**
- **DRY Principle (Don't Repeat Yourself):** Single source of truth for shared logic.
- **Maintainability:** Fix a bug once, all instances update.
- **Encapsulation:** Internal complexity is hidden from the calling flow.
- **Reusability:** Subflows can be **exported as JSON** and shared across Node-RED installations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Subflow** with **Function Node**. Remember: a **Function Node** contains **only JavaScript code**, while a **Subflow** contains **a chain of nodes including a function node**. This distinction is a favorite 2-mark sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Board Revision — Pin This to Your Wall:**

- ✅ **Node-RED = Visual, flow-based, Node.js, IBM 2013, OpenJS Foundation, port 1880.**
- ✅ **Three layers:** Browser Editor, Node.js Runtime, User Flows.
- ✅ **Message object:** `msg` → default key is `msg.payload`.
- ✅ **Five node categories:** Input, Output, Function, Social, Storage — know one example each.
- ✅ **Three context scopes:** `node` ⊂ `flow` ⊂ `global` (hierarchical visibility).
- ✅ **Subflow = encapsulation of multiple nodes** for reusability (NOT the same as a function node).
- ✅ **Deployment modes:** Full, Modified Flows, Modified Nodes.
- ✅ **MQTT default port:** 1883 (distinct from editor port 1880).
- ✅ **Install:** `npm install -g node-red` → **Run:** `node-red` → **Editor:** `http://localhost:1880`.
- ✅ **Dashboard package:** `node-red-dashboard` exposes UI at `http://localhost:1880/ui`.
- ✅ **Flow export format:** `.json` — supports import/export and version control.
- ✅ **Valuation mantra:** Always cite the **port (1880)**, name **3 layers**, list **node categories with examples**, and use a **flow diagram** wherever possible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ThingSpeak
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ThingSpeak

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**ThingSpeak** is an open-source Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform hosted in the cloud that allows **aggregation**, **visualization**, and **real-time analysis** of live data streams originating from sensors, actuators, and embedded devices. Developed and maintained by **MathWorks** (the makers of MATLAB), ThingSpeak provides a RESTful API, an MQTT broker, and native MATLAB analytics to process incoming telemetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> "ThingSpeak is an IoT platform-as-a-service (PaaS) that enables devices to publish sensor data to a cloud-based *Channel*, where it can be stored, time-stamped, plotted, and analyzed using MATLAB."

### Core Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Channel** | A virtual data store where a device posts data. Each channel supports up to **8 fields**. |
| **Field** | A named data variable (e.g., `field1 = temperature`). Max **8 fields per channel**. |
| **Write API Key** | A secret token a device uses to *upload* data to a channel. |
| **Read API Key** | A token used by a client to *fetch* data from a channel. |
| **Feed** | The chronological list of all entries uploaded to a channel. |
| **TalkBack / React** | Server-side command queue and threshold-based alerting system. |
| **TimeStamp** | ISO 8601 date-time automatically attached to every entry. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Think of ThingSpeak as a Facebook Page for Sensors"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine every IoT sensor is a person who wants to post status updates (temperature, humidity, GPS location). ThingSpeak acts like a **public journal + newsroom**:
> - Each **Channel** is a personal diary page dedicated to one sensor/project.
> - Each **Field** is a different column in that diary (e.g., Column-1 = Temperature, Column-2 = Humidity).
> - The **Write API Key** is like the *pen* the sensor uses to write into the diary.
> - The **Read API Key** is the *magnifying glass* given to anyone you allow to read the diary.
> - **MATLAB Analysis** is the *editor* who studies the diary and produces trend reports and alarms.

## 1.3 Why ThingSpeak Exists — The Problem It Solves

In a bare-bones IoT project, the device → internet → user chain is incomplete because:

1. The device has **no permanent place to dump data**.
2. The user has **no dashboard** to view that data.
3. The system has **no intelligence** to react to data automatically.

ThingSpeak closes this gap by offering **storage + visualization + analysis** as a single managed cloud service, eliminating the need to host private servers or write plotting code from scratch.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Limits (Remember for MCQs)

> [!NOTE]
> **ThingSpeak Hard Limits (Free Tier):**
> - Max update interval: **15 seconds** per message.
> - Max message size: **255 characters**.
> - Max message rate: **≈ 8,200 messages per day per channel**.
> - Max channels per account: **4 (free)**, **unlimited (paid)**.
> - Data retention: up to **3 years** depending on licence.
> - Field count per channel: **8 fields** plus status / location / metadata.

## 1.5 Real-World Applications

- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture and temperature field monitoring.
- **Weather Stations:** Multi-field weather logging with public dashboards.
- **Smart Energy:** Solar inverter and grid-monitoring analytics.
- **Health Wearables:** Patient heart-rate and SpO₂ tracking.
- **Industrial IoT:** Predictive maintenance using MATLAB machine-learning scripts on the cloud.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 ThingSpeak Architecture — Layered View

The ThingSpeak ecosystem is divided into **four operational layers**:

### Layer 1 — Device Layer (Edge / Perception)
- Microcontrollers (ESP8266, ESP32, Arduino) and SBCs (Raspberry Pi).
- Sensors and actuators that collect and forward telemetry.
- Communication protocols used: **HTTP/HTTPS (REST)**, **MQTT**, and **WebSockets**.

### Layer 2 — Ingestion / Gateway Layer
- ThingSpeak's **MQTT broker** (`mqtt://mqtt.thingspeak.com:1883`) and **HTTPS REST API endpoints**.
- Validates the **Write API Key** before accepting a payload.
- Stamps the entry with server-side UTC time and a sequential **entry_id**.

### Layer 3 — Storage Layer
- Time-series database keyed by `(channel_id, entry_id)`.
- Field data stored as a JSON-serializable object of name–value pairs.
- **Retention windows:** 1 day (free), 30 days, 1 year, 3 years (paid tiers).

### Layer 4 — Application / Presentation Layer
- **Web Dashboard:** Auto-generated line / scatter plots for each field.
- **MATLAB Analysis & Visualizations:** TimeControl, React, and MATLAB functions executed in the cloud.
- **Plugins & Add-ons:** ThingSpeak Apps, MathWorks File Exchange widgets, custom MATLAB Visualizations.
- **Mobile Web View:** The dashboard is mobile-responsive by default.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Data Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" and "How" of Every Step:**

1. **Sensor sampling** — `Why?` Continuous phenomena (temperature, motion) need periodic digitisation.
2. **Local buffering** — `How?` Microcontroller holds last N readings in RAM/EEPROM to survive network drops.
3. **Encoding** — `How?` Pack field values into a URL or JSON body, e.g., `field1=27.3&field2=58`.
4. **Authentication** — `How?` Append `api_key=XXXXXXXX` parameter; the server checks against its store.
5. **Transport** — `How?` Send over `POST https://api.thingspeak.com/update` or MQTT `publish` to channels/<id>/publish.
6. **Server validation** — `How?` Server checks rate limit (1 msg per 15 s), field count, and data length.
7. **Persistence** — `How?` Insert into time-series store with auto `entry_id` and `created_at`.
8. **Triggers** — `How?` ThingReact / TalkBack / TimeControl run on the new entry (e.g., if temp > 50 → email alert).
9. **Visualisation** — `How?` Dashboard refreshes; MATLAB Visualizations regenerate on the next fetch.

## 2.3 REST API Endpoints — The Must-Know Table

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — REST API**

| Operation | HTTP Method | Endpoint | Required Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write a single entry | `POST` | `/update` | `api_key`, `field1`…`field8` |
| Write bulk (CSV) | `POST` | `/update.csv` | `api_key`, `body=<csv>` |
| Read latest entry | `GET` | `/channels/<id>/feeds/last` | optional `api_key` |
| Read feed (window) | `GET` | `/channels/<id>/feeds` | `api_key`, `results`, `start`, `end` |
| Read single field | `GET` | `/channels/<id>/fields/<n>` | `results`, `days` |
| Channel metadata | `GET` | `/channels/<id>` | `api_key` |
| Create channel | `POST` | `/channels.json` | user API key + JSON body |
| Status update | `POST` | `/channels/<id>/status` | `api_key`, `status` |

## 2.4 MQTT Topics — Must-Know for Board Exams

| Direction | Topic | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Publish (device → cloud)** | `channels/<channel_id>/publish` | Upload field data |
| **Publish (private)** | `channels/<channel_id>/publish/<write_api_key>` | Secure upload |
| **Subscribe (device ← cloud)** | `channels/<channel_id>/subscribe` | Listen to TalkBack commands |
| **Subscribe (private)** | `channels/<channel_id>/subscribe/<api_key>` | Secure subscribe |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Using the *Read* API key for publishing — ThingSpeak silently rejects the request. **Always use the Write API Key to publish.**

## 2.5 MATLAB Integration — The Analytical Power

ThingSpeak's **killer feature** is its first-class MATLAB integration.

- **MATLAB Visualizations:** Custom `.m` scripts executed on the server to render specialized plots (histograms, gauges, heatmaps).
- **MATLAB Analysis:** TimeControl-scheduled `.m` scripts that perform data cleansing, regression, anomaly detection, or machine-learning inference.
- **ThingSpeak Apps:** No-code triggers and visualizations that you chain together to build reactive IoT pipelines.
- **Example MATLAB Visualisation Snippet (Reference for understanding — not for hand-running):**
  - `thingSpeakRead(channelID, 'NumDays', 1)` reads 24 h of data.
  - `plot(time, data)` renders a trend graph that ThingSpeak embeds in the dashboard.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU vivas / placements:**
> - Demonstrates understanding of *cloud PaaS* layers.
> - Shows familiarity with **time-series databases** — a key industry concept.
> - Bridges embedded systems with **data analytics** — the cornerstone of modern Industry 4.0 careers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Model of an IoT Data Packet (Hand-Derivable in Exams)

When a device writes to ThingSpeak, the **effective data payload** is bounded by the platform's transport rules. We can derive the **maximum sustainable throughput** for a single channel.

### 3.1.1 Variables and Definitions

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T$ | Minimum update interval enforced by ThingSpeak | **15 s** |
| $N$ | Number of fields in a single update payload | dimensionless |
| $L_{f}$ | Length of one field value as ASCII string | bytes |
| $L_{h}$ | Length of HTTP header overhead (URL, key, slashes) | bytes |
| $L_{msg}$ | Total message length on the wire | bytes |
| $R$ | Maximum sustainable messages per day | msg/day |
| $B$ | Daily bandwidth requirement | bytes/day |

### 3.1.2 Derivation — Maximum Messages per Day

> [!NOTE]
> **Why?** The KTU board frequently asks "How many updates per day can a free ThingSpeak channel sustain?" This derivation gives the answer in closed form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Updates per minute} &= \frac{60}{T} \\
&= \frac{60}{15} \\
&= 4 \text{ updates / minute.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Updates per hour} &= \frac{60}{T} \times 60 \\
&= 4 \times 60 \\
&= 240 \text{ updates / hour.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Updates per day}\ (R) &= \frac{60}{T} \times 60 \times 24 \\
&= 4 \times 60 \times 24 \\
&= 5760 \text{ updates / day.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion (memorise):** With the 15-second hard limit, the theoretical max is **5,760 messages/day per channel** on the free tier. (ThingSpeak's official docs cite ≈8,200 for an averaging window, but the strict 15-s limit gives 5,760.)

### 3.1.3 Derivation — Daily Bandwidth Requirement

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{msg} &= L_{h} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{f,i} \\
\text{For a single-field temperature reading,} \ L_{msg} &\approx L_{h} + L_{f} \\
L_{msg} &\approx 200 \text{ B} + 6 \text{ B} \\
&= 206 \text{ B per message.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= R \times L_{msg} \\
&= 5760 \times 206 \text{ B} \\
&= 1{,}186{,}560 \text{ B/day} \\
&= \frac{1{,}186{,}560}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ MB/day} \\
&\approx 1.13 \text{ MB/day.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A single ThingSpeak channel needs only ≈1 MB/day of upstream bandwidth — a fraction of a 4G connection. This is why ThingSpeak is friendly to NB-IoT and LoRa gateways.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Predicting Bandwidth for a 4-Sensor Weather Station

**Problem Statement:** A weather station uploads 4 fields (temperature, humidity, pressure, wind-speed) every 30 seconds. Estimate daily message count and bandwidth.

### Step 1 — Effective Update Interval
$$
T_{eff} = 30 \text{ s} \quad \text{(well above the 15-s limit, so valid).}
$$

### Step 2 — Messages per Day

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{86400}{T_{eff}} \\
&= \frac{86400}{30} \\
&= 2880 \text{ messages / day.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Estimated Payload Size
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{msg} &= 200 \text{ B (header)} + (6 + 4 + 7 + 5) \text{ B (fields)} \\
&= 222 \text{ B.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Daily Bandwidth

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= R \times L_{msg} \\
&= 2880 \times 222 \\
&= 639{,}360 \text{ B/day} \\
&\approx 0.61 \text{ MB/day.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** **2,880 messages/day, ≈0.61 MB/day** — comfortably within free-tier limits.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ThingSpeak vs. Alternatives

| Feature | ThingSpeak | Blynk | AWS IoT Core | Firebase RTDB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloud Provider** | MathWorks | Blynk Inc. | Amazon | Google |
| **Built-in Plotting** | ✅ Yes (auto) | ✅ Yes (widgets) | ❌ Manual via QuickSight | ❌ Manual |
| **Native MATLAB** | ✅ First-class | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **MQTT Support** | ✅ Native broker | ✅ Native | ✅ Native | ❌ No |
| **Free-Tier Updates/Day** | 5,760 | Unlimited (limited energy) | 1 M (1 yr) | Unlimited (throttled) |
| **Max Fields/Channel** | 8 | 40 (widgets) | n/a | n/a |
| **Built-in Analytics** | ✅ MATLAB scripts | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Lambda / SageMaker | ⚠️ Limited |
| **Best for** | Education + research | Hobby prototyping | Enterprise IoT | Mobile/web backends |

## 3.4 End-to-End Worked Scenario — Temperature Alert Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the kind of explanation KTU examiners expect for a 14-mark question.**

**Scenario:** An ESP32 reads a DS18B20 temperature sensor and uploads to ThingSpeak every 30 s. If temperature > 50 °C, a ThingReact should send an email.

### Step 1 — Channel Setup (User Action)
- Create channel `weatherStation01` with `field1 = temperature`.
- Copy the **Write API Key** (e.g., `K7Q4P9X2V5BC0LM3`).

### Step 2 — ESP32 Code Logic (Logic Outline)
1. Connect to Wi-Fi.
2. Initialise DS18B20 on pin GPIO4.
3. Loop:
   - Read temperature: `float t = sensors.getTempC();`
   - Build URL: `String url = "/update?api_key=K7Q4P9X2V5BC0LM3&field1=" + String(t);`
   - `http.begin(client, "http://api.thingspeak.com" + url);`
   - `http.POST("");`
   - `delay(30000);`

### Step 3 — ThingReact Configuration (Cloud Action)
- Condition: `field1 > 50`
- Action: **Send Email** to `alerts@firm.com`
- Cooldown: 300 s to avoid alert flooding.

### Step 4 — Verification
- Open dashboard; observe live temperature plot.
- Heat the sensor; expect an alert email within the next 30 s.
- Inspect the **Activity Log** in the channel for the alert event.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this architecture wins:** The device stays simple (single HTTP POST). All intelligence — alerting, trending, regression — is delegated to ThingSpeak's cloud, exemplifying the **Edge-vs-Cloud compute split** in modern IoT design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ThingSpeak System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef device fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A66,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#B7E0FF,stroke:#247,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#D2F2C2,stroke:#383,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#E2C2FF,stroke:#535,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ui fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B45,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    subgraph EDGE["LAYER 1: EDGE / DEVICES"]
        ESP["ESP32 / Arduino / Raspberry Pi"]:::device
        SENSOR["Sensor Array: Temp Humidity Pressure"]:::device
    end

    subgraph PROTO["LAYER 2: PROTOCOL TRANSPORT"]
        HTTP["HTTPS REST API"]:::net
        MQTT["MQTT Broker mqtt dot thingspeak dot com"]:::net
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["LAYER 3: THINGSPEAK CLOUD"]
        IN["Ingestion Service Auth and Rate Limit"]:::cloud
        DB["Time Series Database Channel Feed"]:::cloud
        TRIG["Trigger Engine TimeControl and React"]:::cloud
    end

    subgraph ANALYTICS["LAYER 4: ANALYTICS and VISUALIZATION"]
        MATLAB["MATLAB Analysis Scripts"]:::logic
        VIZ["Auto Plots and Visualizations"]:::logic
        REACT["ThingReact Alert Engine"]:::logic
    end

    subgraph USER["LAYER 5: USER"]
        DASH["Web Dashboard Mobile Browser"]:::ui
        ALERT["Email / SMS / Webhook Alert"]:::ui
    end

    SENSOR --> ESP
    ESP --> HTTP
    ESP --> MQTT
    HTTP --> IN
    MQTT --> IN
    IN --> DB
    DB --> TRIG
    TRIG --> MATLAB
    TRIG --> REACT
    DB --> VIZ
    MATLAB --> VIZ
    REACT --> ALERT
    VIZ --> DASH
    ALERT --> USER
```

## 4.2 Channel–Field–Entry Data Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef ch fill:#FFF4B0,stroke:#A80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fd fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#07A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en fill:#C2F0C2,stroke:#2A2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    CH["Channel ID: 1234567"]:::ch
    F1["field1 Temperature float"]:::fd
    F2["field2 Humidity float"]:::fd
    F3["field3 Pressure float"]:::fd
    FN["fieldN Custom Data"]:::fd

    E1["Entry 1 t1 25.4 60.1 1012"]:::en
    E2["Entry 2 t2 25.5 60.0 1012"]:::en
    E3["Entry N tN ... ... ..."]:::en

    CH --> F1
    CH --> F2
    CH --> F3
    CH --> FN
    F1 --> E1
    F1 --> E2
    F1 --> E3
```

## 4.3 Workflow — From Sensor Sensed Value to Email Alert

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DEV as ESP32 Sensor Node
    participant API as ThingSpeak REST or MQTT
    participant DB as Channel Time Series DB
    participant REACT as ThingReact Engine
    participant MAIL as Email Service

    DEV->>API: POST update with api_key and field1 equal to temperature
    API->>API: Validate rate limit and api_key
    API->>DB: Insert entry with auto timestamp and entry_id
    DB-->>REACT: New entry event notification
    REACT->>REACT: Evaluate rule field1 greater than 50
    alt Condition Met
        REACT->>MAIL: Trigger alert with channel metadata
        MAIL-->>REACT: 250 OK delivery
    else Condition Not Met
        REACT-->>DB: Idle and wait for next event
    end
    DB-->>DEV: Return 200 OK to publisher
```

## 4.4 Comparison — REST vs MQTT Communication Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef dev fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A66,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proto fill:#B7E0FF,stroke:#247,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef srv fill:#D2F2C2,stroke:#383,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    subgraph REST["REST FLOW"]
        D1["ESP32"]:::dev
        H1["HTTPS POST /update"]:::proto
        S1["ThingSpeak Ingest"]:::srv
        D1 --> H1 --> S1
    end

    subgraph MQ["MQTT FLOW"]
        D2["ESP32"]:::dev
        T1["Topic channels slash 1234567 slash publish"]:::proto
        B1["ThingSpeak MQTT Broker"]:::srv
        D2 --> T1 --> B1
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
