# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q1, 3 Marks]
**Define ThingSpeak. Mention any two key features of it.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** ThingSpeak is a cloud-based open-source IoT analytics platform, developed by **MathWorks**, that allows sensors and embedded devices to **upload**, **store**, **visualize**, and **analyze** data streams in real time.

**Two Key Features:**
1. **Native MATLAB Integration** — supports MATLAB visualizations and analytics executed directly on the cloud.
2. **Multi-Protocol Support** — accepts data over **REST (HTTP/HTTPS)** and **MQTT**, providing flexibility for diverse device types.
3. *(Bonus)* Time-series database with auto-generated dashboards per channel.
4. *(Bonus)* Server-side triggers via **ThingReact**, **TalkBack**, and **TimeControl**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 3 Marks]
**What is a ThingSpeak Channel? How many fields can a channel support?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Channel** is the fundamental data container in ThingSpeak — a virtual data store where a single IoT device or project uploads its telemetry. Each channel has a unique **Channel ID** and a **Write API Key** for authentication. A channel can contain up to **8 numeric data fields** plus descriptive metadata such as latitude, longitude, elevation, and a status string. Each uploaded reading becomes a new **Entry** in the channel's feed, automatically tagged with a server-side timestamp and sequential `entry_id`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule (ESE):** Module-2 Part-B questions must offer an internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each worth 14 marks (split into two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Architecture & Workflow

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **layered architecture of ThingSpeak** with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each layer in the IoT data pipeline.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram and a real-world use case (e.g., smart agriculture), describe how a sensor node uploads data to ThingSpeak and how a cloud-side alert is triggered when a threshold is exceeded.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**Layered Architecture of ThingSpeak (4 layers):**

1. **Device / Perception Layer:**
   - Microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino), SBCs (Raspberry Pi), sensors, and actuators.
   - *Role:* Generate the raw data and perform local conditioning.

2. **Network / Ingestion Layer:**
   - Protocols: **HTTP/HTTPS REST** and **MQTT** broker.
   - *Role:* Securely transport data to the cloud, perform authentication via the **Write API Key**, and enforce rate limits (15 s minimum interval).

3. **Cloud / Storage & Processing Layer:**
   - **Time-series database** stores entries as `(entry_id, channel_id, field, value, timestamp)`.
   - **Trigger Engine** (TimeControl, ThingReact, TalkBack) executes server-side logic.
   - **MATLAB Analytics** — runs `.m` scripts on stored data for cleaning, regression, or ML.

4. **Application / Presentation Layer:**
   - Web-based dashboard with auto-plots per field, MATLAB Visualizations, mobile-responsive UI.
   - Alert channels: email, SMS, or webhook.

**Diagram (already drawn in Section 4.1) — should be re-drawn in the exam script.**

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Hint (KTU Standard):**
> - Diagram with proper labels: **3 marks**.
> - Description of each layer with one example: **4 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**Use Case:** Smart Agriculture — soil-moisture and ambient temperature monitoring with auto irrigation alert.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Soil-Moisture Sensor] → [ESP32] → [Wi-Fi Router] → [Internet]
                ↘                      ↘
           [Temp Sensor]        [ThingSpeak REST API]
                                       ↓
                          [Channel: farmField01]
                          (field1 = moisture, field2 = temperature)
                                       ↓
                          [ThingReact Rule: if field1 < 30 → email]
                                       ↓
                          [Farmer Receives Email → Turns On Pump]
```

**Workflow Steps:**
1. ESP32 reads soil-moisture and temperature every 60 s.
2. ESP32 builds an HTTP POST: `POST /update?api_key=...&field1=28.4&field2=32.1`.
3. ThingSpeak validates the key, tags the entry with a timestamp, and persists it.
4. The **ThingReact** engine checks the rule `field1 < 30` and triggers an email when crossed.
5. The farmer receives the alert and remotely switches on the irrigation pump.

**Exam Tip:** Always draw the diagram with **arrow labels** showing protocol (e.g., "HTTPS"), and mention **at least one security mechanism** (API key authentication) to score full marks.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **REST and MQTT** as data-ingestion methods in ThingSpeak. Discuss the **write API key vs read API key** model.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe **ThingSpeak's MATLAB integration**. Explain MATLAB Visualizations and MATLAB Analysis with a suitable example of predictive maintenance.

---

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 Marks]

**REST vs MQTT Comparison Table:**

| Feature | REST (HTTP/HTTPS) | MQTT |
|---|---|---|
| Transport model | Request–response | Publish–subscribe |
| Direction | Unidirectional (push) | Bi-directional |
| Overhead | Higher (headers, handshakes) | Minimal (fixed 2-byte header) |
| Latency | Higher | Low (suitable for real-time) |
| Power | More battery drain | Battery-friendly (low bandwidth) |
| Reliability | Built on TCP | QoS levels 0/1/2 on TCP |
| Best for | Sparse, polled data | High-frequency, time-critical data |

**API Key Model:**
- **Write API Key:** Long random string, used by the *device* to push data. Should be kept secret on the firmware.
- **Read API Key:** Optional, used by *clients* to fetch data. Can be public for open dashboards.
- A **Channel ID** is publicly visible; the security boundary is the **API key**.
- MQTT topic embedding: `channels/<id>/publish/<write_api_key>` provides end-to-end authentication without exposing the key in the URL.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Treating the *Read* API key as the publisher key — ThingSpeak silently rejects the request. This is a frequent 1-mark loss.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 Marks]

**MATLAB Integration — Two Building Blocks:**

1. **MATLAB Visualizations:**
   - Custom `.m` scripts that pull channel data and render specialised plots (3-D surface, gauges, histograms).
   - Output image is automatically embedded in the channel dashboard.
   - Useful for **tailored graphical representation** beyond default line plots.

2. **MATLAB Analysis:**
   - Server-side scripts scheduled by **TimeControl** (e.g., every 1 min, 5 min, hourly).
   - Use functions like `thingSpeakRead(channelID, 'NumDays', 7)` to fetch past data.
   - Apply any MATLAB toolbox: statistics, signal processing, or machine learning.

**Example — Predictive Maintenance:**
- A vibration sensor feeds `field1` (RMS amplitude) and `field2` (peak frequency) to a channel every 10 s.
- A TimeControl triggers a MATLAB Analysis script every 5 minutes that:
  1. Reads the last 30 minutes of data.
  2. Computes the moving RMS.
  3. Compares it to a learned baseline.
  4. If deviation > 3σ, posts a "Maintenance Required" message to `field3` and triggers a ThingReact email.

**Exam Tip:** Always pair a *Visualization* with a corresponding *Analysis* script in your answer to score the full 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on ThingSpeak questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Channel vs Field** — losing 1 mark for saying "a channel is a single sensor" (it's a data container that holds up to 8 fields).
> 2. **Mixing up Write/Read API keys** — losing 1 mark for the same example described in 5.2 (a).
> 3. **Forgetting the 15-s rate-limit** — examiners explicitly test this; mention it.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram** — in 14-mark questions, a diagram is worth 3–4 marks; never skip it.
> 5. **Ignoring MATLAB integration** — ThingSpeak's differentiator from Blynk/Firebase is MATLAB; failing to mention it loses easy marks.
> 6. **Treating ThingSpeak as a hardware device** — it is a *cloud platform*, not a board or chip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> - ☐ **ThingSpeak** is a **cloud-based IoT PaaS by MathWorks**.
> - ☐ A **Channel** holds up to **8 fields**; each reading is an **Entry**.
> - ☐ Authentication uses **Write API Key** (publish) and **Read API Key** (fetch).
> - ☐ Two supported transports: **HTTPS REST** and **MQTT** broker at `mqtt.thingspeak.com:1883`.
> - ☐ **Free-tier rate limit:** minimum **15 seconds** between successive updates.
> - ☐ Server-side triggers: **TimeControl**, **ThingReact**, **TalkBack**.
> - ☐ Killer feature: **native MATLAB Visualizations and Analysis** scripts.
> - ☐ Standard endpoint: `POST https://api.thingspeak.com/update`.
> - ☐ Standard MQTT publish topic: `channels/<channel_id>/publish`.
> - ☐ Each entry auto-tagged with **server-side UTC timestamp** and **entry_id**.
> - ☐ Free-tier channel limit: **4 per account**; up to 8 fields each.
> - ☐ Max sustainable updates/day (strict 15-s rule): **5,760** messages/day.
> - ☐ Always draw the **layered architecture diagram** for any 14-mark answer.
> - ☐ Distinguish ThingSpeak from **Blynk, AWS IoT Core, Firebase** in comparison questions.
> - ☐ Real-world exam-favourite use case: **smart agriculture with auto-alert**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blynk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blynk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Blynk** is a full-stack **Internet of Things (IoT) platform** that provides a digital dashboard infrastructure for the rapid prototyping, deployment, and remote control of hardware devices (such as Arduino, ESP8266, ESP32, Raspberry Pi, and NodeMCU) over the Internet. It consists of three tightly integrated components: **(i) the Blynk Mobile/Web Application** (frontend interface), **(ii) the Blynk Cloud Server** (backend message broker), and **(iii) the Blynk Libraries** (firmware-side client).

Blynk was originally introduced in **2015** by Blynk Inc. and is widely adopted in academic IoT curricula because it abstracts away low-level socket programming, HTTP/MQTT handshaking, and JSON parsing, allowing students to focus on sensor-actuator logic. The platform employs a publish–subscribe model where hardware pushes data to **virtual pins** and the mobile app reads/writes them in real time with a typical latency of **less than 100 ms** on healthy networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Remote Control for Things" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy:** Think of Blynk as a *universal TV remote control for any electronic gadget you build*. Just as a TV remote has labeled buttons (Volume, Channel, Power) that send invisible infrared signals to the television, Blynk gives your smartphone **labeled buttons, gauges, sliders, and graphs** that send invisible cloud signals to your Arduino or ESP module. The TV (your hardware) does not care how the remote looks; it only cares about the *signal* it receives.

In this analogy:

- The **smartphone screen with buttons** = Blynk App (drag-and-drop dashboard).
- The **invisible infrared signal in the air** = Internet packets relayed by the Blynk Cloud.
- The **TV's infrared receiver** = The Blynk Library running on your microcontroller.
- The **button labels (Volume, Channel)** = *Widgets* (Button, Slider, Gauge) bound to *Virtual Pins (V0, V1, V2…)*.
- The **TV's channel number** = The data value (integer, float, string) carried by the virtual pin.

## 1.3 Why Blynk Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly positions Blynk under **Module 2 — IoT Communication Models and Protocols**, alongside MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP. Examiner questions typically test the student's ability to:

1. Sketch the **three-tier Blynk architecture**.
2. Explain the role of **Virtual Pins, Auth Tokens, and Datastreams**.
3. Write a minimal **Blynk sketch for NodeMCU/ESP8266**.
4. Differentiate between **Blynk Legacy (1.0)** and **Blynk IoT (2.0)**.

## 1.4 Core Terminology — The Blynk Lexicon

| Term | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Auth Token** | A unique **256-bit alphanumeric credential** generated per project that authorises a device to communicate with a specific Blynk dashboard. |
| **Virtual Pin (V0–V255)** | Software-defined bidirectional channels used to exchange data between hardware and widgets. |
| **Widget** | A graphical UI element (Button, Slider, Gauge, Graph, LCD, Map) on the Blynk dashboard. |
| **Datastream** | Blynk 2.0 equivalent of a virtual pin — a typed channel (Integer / Float / String) with metadata. |
| **Template** | Blynk 2.0's pre-defined device blueprint that holds pin configurations and metadata. |
| **Energy Balance** | The **2,000 (free) or 100,000 (PRO)** point limit that determines how many widgets a user can place. |
| **Blynk.Edgent** | The dynamic provisioning firmware that auto-claims Wi-Fi credentials over BLE/Wi-Fi. |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The "B" in Blynk is capitalised, the rest is lower-case. In answer sheets, writing "BLYNK" or "blynk" is acceptable but stylistically **"Blynk"** is the KTU textbook convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Blynk Architecture

Blynk operates on a **client–server–client** topology. Every data transaction passes through the Blynk Cloud, which is the authoritative message broker. Unlike peer-to-peer MQTT setups, Blynk does *not* allow direct device-to-device communication — all traffic is server-mediated.

### Tier 1 — The Hardware (Device Tier)

The device tier is your physical microcontroller. It runs the **Blynk C++/Arduino library** (or MicroPython equivalent), connects to your local Wi-Fi router using SSID and password, and opens a persistent **TLS-encrypted TCP socket** (default port **8443**) to the Blynk Cloud. The library handles reconnection, heartbeat, and command dispatch transparently.

### Tier 2 — The Blynk Cloud (Server Tier)

The cloud is a globally distributed infrastructure hosted on **AWS (Amazon Web Services)**. It performs four critical functions:

1. **Authentication:** Validates the Auth Token of every incoming connection.
2. **Routing:** Forwards widget write events to the correct device, and device-sent data to the correct widget.
3. **Storage:** Persists the last-known value of every pin for late-joiner clients.
4. **Notification Dispatch:** Triggers Email / Push / SMS / Webhook actions.

### Tier 3 — The Blynk App / Web Console (Client Tier)

This is the user-facing dashboard. The mobile app (iOS / Android) and the Blynk.Console (web) provide a **WYSIWYG drag-and-drop canvas** where users place widgets onto a grid (the "Project"). Each widget is bound to a specific Datastream / Virtual Pin.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Data Flow

Let us trace what happens when a user taps a "Button" widget on the Blynk app to switch ON an LED connected to the NodeMCU:

> **Step 1 — User Interaction:** The user taps the Button widget on the smartphone. The Blynk app immediately optimistically updates the UI (visual feedback within **<50 ms**).

> **Step 2 — Cloud Forwarding:** The app sends a JSON-wrapped message `{"cmd":"vw","pin":"V1","value":"1"}` over TLS to the Blynk Cloud at `blynk-cloud.com:8443`.

> **Step 3 — Cloud Authentication:** The cloud validates the Auth Token, locates the device associated with this project, and pushes the message to that device's open socket.

> **Step 4 — Library Reception:** The Blynk library on the NodeMCU receives the byte stream, parses it, and invokes the user-defined callback `BLYNK_WRITE(V1)`.

> **Step 5 — Hardware Action:** Inside the callback, the firmware executes `digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH)`, physically energising the LED.

> **Step 6 — Acknowledgement (optional):** If the firmware calls `Blynk.virtualWrite(V1, 1)`, the new state is sent back to the cloud, updating the widget's stored value (useful for "sync" after reconnection).

The **reverse direction** (device → app) follows the same path in reverse: sensor data → `Blynk.virtualWrite(V0, value)` → cloud → widget updates its visual representation.

## 2.3 Communication Protocol Support

Blynk is protocol-agnostic at the application layer but uses a specific transport beneath:

| Transport | Default Port | Encryption | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP/TLS** | **8443** | TLS 1.2 | Primary secure channel (default). |
| **TCP (plain)** | 80 / 8080 | None | Legacy / debug only. |
| **MQTT** | 8883 | TLS | Blynk 2.0 supports native MQTT bridging. |
| **HTTP REST API** | 443 | HTTPS | Server-to-server webhook integration. |
| **Bluetooth / BLE** | N/A | N/A | Blynk.Edgent provisioning only. |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Which protocol does Blynk use?", the safest answer is **"Blynk uses its own proprietary application protocol running over TCP with TLS encryption on port 8443, but it can also integrate with MQTT and HTTP for interoperability."** This shows layered understanding.

## 2.4 Blynk 2.0 (Blynk IoT) vs. Blynk Legacy (1.0)

The KTU 2024 syllabus may reference the newer Blynk IoT platform. Key upgrades:

| Feature | Blynk Legacy (1.0) | Blynk IoT (2.0) |
|---|---|---|
| **Dashboard limit** | One project per device | Unlimited templates, multi-device |
| **Web dashboard** | ❌ | ✅ (Blynk.Console) |
| **Provisioning** | Manual Auth Token | QR code / BLE via Edgent |
| **Energy model** | 2,000 free points | 100,000 free / PRO tiers |
| **Automation** | Manual only | Built-in Automations (if-this-then-that) |
| **Datastreams** | Virtual Pins (untyped) | Typed (Int / Float / String / Enum) |
| **Organisation** | Single user | Workspaces, roles, organisations |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The "formulas" in Blynk are conceptual — they describe throughput, energy cost, and timing. Memorise the numerical constants below; they appear in 2-mark questions.

| Symbol / Concept | Expression | Value / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Free Energy** (Legacy) | $E_{free}$ | $\mathbf{2{,}000}$ points |
| **Button widget cost** | $c_{button}$ | $\mathbf{200}$ energy points |
| **Slider widget cost** | $c_{slider}$ | $\mathbf{300}$ energy points |
| **Gauge widget cost** | $c_{gauge}$ | $\mathbf{400}$ energy points |
| **Graph widget cost** | $c_{graph}$ | $\mathbf{600}$ energy points |
| **LCD widget cost** | $c_{lcd}$ | $\mathbf{400}$ energy points |
| **Max widgets per project** | $N_{max}$ | $\mathbf{12}$ (free) / unlimited (PRO) |
| **Cloud latency** | $\tau_{cloud}$ | $\le \mathbf{100~ms}$ over 4G |
| **Auth Token length** | $L_{token}$ | $\mathbf{32}$ hex characters ($\equiv 128$ bit) in 2.0 |
| **Default secure port** | $P_{tls}$ | $\mathbf{8443}$ |
| **Heartbeat interval** | $T_{hb}$ | $\mathbf{10~s}$ (device → cloud) |
| **Max virtual pins** | $V_{max}$ | $\mathbf{256}$ (V0–V255) in Legacy |

> **Engineering Utility:** Blynk is the de-facto platform for **academic IoT prototypes, smart-agriculture demos, home-automation projects, and rapid industry POCs** because it reduces a typical 2-week HTTP+JSON project to a 2-hour assembly task. It is also used in **Industry 4.0** for low-volume SCADA-style dashboards where the cost of custom Android development is prohibitive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Energy Budget Calculation (Legacy Blynk)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Problem (KTU-style 3-mark):** A student designs a Blynk dashboard using **3 Buttons, 2 Sliders, 2 Gauges, 1 Graph, and 1 LCD**. Determine whether the project fits within the free energy limit. If not, calculate the additional PRO energy required.

### Step 1 — Identify widget energy costs

From the reference sheet:

$$
c_{button} = 200, \quad c_{slider} = 300, \quad c_{gauge} = 400, \quad c_{graph} = 600, \quad c_{lcd} = 400
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the total energy required

$$
E_{required} = n_b \cdot c_{button} + n_s \cdot c_{slider} + n_g \cdot c_{gauge} + c_{graph} + c_{lcd}
$$

Substitute the values $n_b = 3$, $n_s = 2$, $n_g = 2$:

$$
E_{required} = (3)(200) + (2)(300) + (2)(400) + 600 + 400
$$

$$
E_{required} = 600 + 600 + 800 + 600 + 400
$$

$$
E_{required} = 3{,}000 \text{ energy points}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with the free allowance

$$
E_{free} = 2{,}000 \text{ points}
$$

$$
\Delta E = E_{required} - E_{free} = 3{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 1{,}000 \text{ points}
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

> **Result:** The project **exceeds** the free limit by **1,000 energy points**. The student must either reduce the widget count (e.g., remove the Graph widget costing 600) or purchase a **PRO plan** to unlock additional energy.

$$
\boxed{\Delta E = +1{,}000 \text{ points over the free quota}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cloud Latency Budget

> **Problem:** A temperature sensor reports data every **T = 2 s** to Blynk. The round-trip time (RTT) to the cloud is **R = 85 ms**, and the widget rendering takes **$\tau_r = 15$ ms**. What is the end-to-end latency perceived by the user, and is the sampling rate sustainable?

### Step 1 — Express total latency

$$
L_{total} = R + \tau_r
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
L_{total} = 85 + 15 = 100~\text{ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with sampling period

$$
L_{total} \ll T = 2{,}000~\text{ms}
$$

$$
\frac{L_{total}}{T} = \frac{100}{2{,}000} = 0.05 \;(\text{5%})
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

> The system uses only **5% of the available time budget** for round-trip communication, leaving **95%** for sensor acquisition, ADC conversion, and sleep cycles. The sampling rate is therefore highly sustainable.

$$
\boxed{L_{total} = 100~\text{ms} \quad;\quad \text{Sustainable at } T = 2~\text{s}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Blynk vs. Alternatives

| Parameter | **Blynk** | **Firebase** | **ThingSpeak** | **AWS IoT Core** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary use** | Hobbyist / Academic dashboards | Realtime database | Data logging & analytics | Enterprise fleet management |
| **Mobile app builder** | ✅ Drag & drop | ❌ DIY | ❌ DIY | ❌ DIY |
| **Free tier** | 2,000 energy points | 1 GB storage | 4 channels / 8,200 msg/day | 10 devices / 1 yr |
| **Protocol** | Proprietary TCP/TLS | HTTPS / WebSocket | HTTP / MQTT | MQTT / HTTPS / LoRaWAN |
| **Latency** | **<100 ms** | ~150 ms | ~1 s | <50 ms |
| **Learning curve** | Low | Medium | Low | High |
| **Code size on MCU** | ~50 KB | ~70 KB | ~30 KB | >200 KB |
| **Privacy** | Cloud-mediated | Cloud-mediated | Cloud-mediated | User-controlled |

## 3.4 Derivation — Why the Auth Token is 32 Hex Characters

The Auth Token is generated by hashing a project UUID with a 128-bit random nonce and base-16 encoding the result. Each hex character encodes 4 bits, so:

$$
L_{token} = \frac{N_{bits}}{4} = \frac{128}{4} = 32 \text{ characters}
$$

Equivalently, the entropy in bits is:

$$
H = L_{token} \times \log_2(16) = 32 \times 4 = 128~\text{bits}
$$

This yields a keyspace of $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ unique tokens — collision probability is negligible for any practical IoT deployment.

$$
\boxed{H = 128~\text{bits} \;;\; 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \text{ unique tokens}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Tier Blynk Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph clientTier[TIER 3: Blynk App and Web Console]
        appUser[User Smartphone or Browser]
        widgetA[Button Widget V0]
        widgetB[Gauge Widget V1]
        widgetC[Graph Widget V2]
    end

    subgraph cloudTier[TIER 2: Blynk Cloud Server AWS]
        auth[Auth Token Validator]
        router[Message Router and Pin Registry]
        storage[In Memory Pin Storage]
        notify[Notification Service Email Push]
    end

    subgraph deviceTier[TIER 1: Hardware NodeMCU ESP8266]
        wifi[Wi Fi Module]
        lib[Blynk C plus plus Library]
        mcu[Microcontroller]
        sensorA[Temp Sensor DHT11]
        sensorB[LED Actuator]
    end

    appUser --> widgetA
    appUser --> widgetB
    appUser --> widgetC
    widgetA -->|TLS 8443| auth
    widgetB -->|TLS 8443| auth
    widgetC -->|TLS 8443| auth
    auth --> router
    router --> storage
    router --> notify
    router -->|Downstream| lib
    lib -->|Sensor data| router
    lib --> mcu
    mcu --> sensorA
    mcu --> sensorB
    mcu --> wifi
    wifi -->|TLS 8443| auth
```

> The diagram illustrates the **closed-loop bidirectional data path**. All arrows labelled **TLS 8443** traverse the public Internet, while internal arrows within each tier are local (Bluetooth, GPIO, or memory bus).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Data Lifecycle of a Virtual Pin Write

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User taps Button on App] --> B[App sends JSON over TLS 8443]
    B --> C[Cloud validates Auth Token]
    C -->|Valid| D[Cloud looks up device by Token]
    C -->|Invalid| Z[Reject Connection and Log]
    D --> E[Cloud pushes message to device socket]
    E --> F[Blynk library invokes BLYNK_WRITE V0]
    F --> G[Callback executes digitalWrite LED HIGH]
    G --> H[Optional Blynk virtualWrite V0 1]
    H --> I[Cloud updates stored pin value]
    I --> J[App widget reflects new state]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Blynk Project Creation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p1[Step 1: Install Blynk App from Play Store] --> p2[Step 2: Create New Project and Select Hardware NodeMCU]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Auth Token is emailed automatically]
    p3 --> p4[Step 4: Add Widgets Button Slider Gauge]
    p4 --> p5[Step 5: Bind each Widget to a Virtual Pin V0 to V255]
    p5 --> p6[Step 6: Flash Blynk sketch to ESP8266 with SSID Pass Token]
    p6 --> p7[Step 7: Device comes online and status turns Green]
    p7 --> p8[Step 8: Test bidirectional control]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — End-to-End Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant User
    participant App as Blynk App
    participant Cloud as Blynk Cloud
    participant Lib as Blynk Library on MCU
    participant HW as Sensor or Actuator

    User->>App: Tap Button Widget
    App->>Cloud: WRITE V0 = 1 over TLS
    Cloud->>Lib: Forward command via persistent socket
    Lib->>HW: digitalWrite LED_PIN HIGH
    HW-->>Lib: State confirmed
    Lib-->>Cloud: virtualWrite V0 1 acknowledgement
    Cloud-->>App: Update widget display
    App-->>User: Visual feedback within 100 ms
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading aid:** The numbered arrows (1–8) correspond to the eight discrete events in a single user interaction. In a KTU 14-mark question, examiners award 1 mark for correctly identifying **each event** in the data flow.
