# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between LoRa and LoRaWAN.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **LoRa** is a **physical-layer (PHY)** modulation technique patented by Semtech, based on **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** in the sub-GHz ISM band, defining the radio waveform, spreading factor, bandwidth and coding rate. (1.5 marks)
> - **LoRaWAN** is an open **MAC-layer / network protocol** maintained by the **LoRa Alliance** that runs on top of LoRa. It defines **device classes (A/B/C)**, **frame format**, **encryption (AES-128)**, **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)**, **network architecture** (ED → Gateway → NS → AS), and **join procedure (OTAA / ABP)**. (1.5 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. List the three configurable parameters of LoRa modulation and state how increasing each affects range and airtime.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Spreading Factor (SF 7–12):** Increasing SF ↑ **range** (better sensitivity) and ↑ **airtime** (doubles per step). (1 mark)
> - **Bandwidth (125/250/500 kHz):** Increasing BW ↓ **sensitivity** (range) and ↓ **airtime** (faster symbol). (1 mark)
> - **Coding Rate (4/5–4/8):** Increasing CR ↑ **robustness** against interference and ↑ **airtime** (more FEC bits). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Choice A]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **LoRaWAN network**. Describe the role of the **Network Server** in **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** management.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (refer Section 4.1) — End Devices → Gateways → Network Server → Application Server.** (2 marks)

**Architecture explanation:**

1. **End Devices (EDs):** battery-powered sensors/actuators that transmit LoRa-modulated chirp frames in the sub-GHz ISM band. They support Class A/B/C duty cycles. (1 mark)
2. **Gateways:** transparent IP bridges, simultaneously demodulating up to 8 channels using multi-modem concentrators. They forward every received frame to the NS over IP backhaul (Ethernet/4G/Satellite). (1 mark)
3. **Network Server (NS):** central intelligence — performs **frame deduplication** (same packet received by multiple gateways), **MAC-command processing**, **ADR**, **downlink scheduling**, and **security-key management**. (1.5 marks)
4. **Application Server (AS):** decodes the application payload and routes it to the customer's cloud dashboard via HTTPS/MQTT. (0.5 marks)
5. **Join Server (JS):** stores root-keys and provisions devices via OTAA. (1 mark)

**ADR role of NS (2 marks):**
- The NS monitors the **last 20 SNR readings** from each ED.
- If the **SNR margin > required SNR + 10 dB**, the NS commands the ED via MAC downlink to **reduce SF or increase BW**, conserving airtime and energy.
- If the **SNR margin < required SNR**, the NS commands **increase SF or reduce BW** to restore link robustness.
- The optimum is the **lowest SF / highest BW** that still satisfies the link budget — maximising capacity and battery life for the entire network.

> **(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the **Time on Air (ToA)** for a LoRa packet with the following configuration: **SF = 8, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 4/5, Payload = 20 bytes, Explicit Header = ON, CRC = ON, Low Data Rate Optimization = OFF.** Also compute the **effective bit rate**.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Symbol period.**
$$T_s = \frac{2^{8}}{125{,}000} = \frac{256}{125{,}000} = 2.048 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Preamble duration.**
$$T_{preamble} = (8 + 4.25) \times 2.048 = 12.288 + 8.704 = 20.992 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator of payload-symbol count.**
$$8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20IH = 8(20) - 4(8) + 28 + 16 - 20$$
$$= 160 - 32 + 28 + 16 - 20 = 152$$

**Step 4 — Denominator.**
$$4(SF - 2DE) = 4(8) = 32$$

**Step 5 — Ceil and multiplier.**
$$\frac{152}{32} = 4.75 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lceil 4.75 \rceil = 5$$
$$(CR + 4) = (1 + 4) = 5$$

**Step 6 — Payload symbols.**
$$n_{payload} = 8 + 5 \times 5 = 33 \text{ symbols}$$

**Step 7 — Payload duration.**
$$T_{payload} = 33 \times 2.048 = 67.584 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 8 — Total packet ToA.**
$$\boxed{T_{packet} = 20.992 + 67.584 = 88.576 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 9 — Effective bit rate.**
$$R_b = SF \times \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR = 8 \times \frac{125{,}000}{256} \times 0.8$$
$$= 8 \times 488.28 \times 0.8 \approx 3125 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{R_b \approx 3.125 \text{ kbps}}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Choice B]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** modulation used by LoRa. Illustrate with a frequency-vs-time sketch of an **up-chirp and a down-chirp** and show how a binary bit is encoded by the chirp's **starting frequency**.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Concept of CSS (3 marks):**
- CSS spreads the energy of a single information bit across a wide frequency band by linearly sweeping the carrier frequency from $f_{min}$ to $f_{max}$ over a symbol period $T_s$.
- Each symbol period encodes $SF$ information bits by selecting one of $2^{SF}$ possible starting frequencies.
- Because the chirp energy is spread over the **entire channel bandwidth**, the signal looks like narrowband noise to an interferer — granting **robustness against multipath fading, Doppler, and in-band jamming**.

**Up-chirp vs down-chirp description (2 marks):**
- **Up-chirp:** frequency rises linearly from $f_0$ to $f_0 + BW$ over $T_s$.
- **Down-chirp (used as the de-chirp reference at the receiver):** frequency falls linearly from $f_0 + BW$ to $f_0$ over $T_s$.

**Bit-encoding mechanism (2 marks):**
- The receiver multiplies the incoming chirp by a conjugate down-chirp. The product is a single-frequency tone whose frequency is **proportional to the starting frequency offset** of the transmitted chirp.
- An FFT of this baseband product produces a sharp peak in exactly one of $2^{SF}$ frequency bins, identifying the transmitted SF-bit word.
- A binary 0 vs 1 distinction corresponds to a starting frequency below or above the centre of the band — making CSS inherently **coherent** and **time-frequency orthogonal** between different SFs.

> **(b) [7 marks]** Compute the **link budget** and **maximum free-space range** for a LoRa node with: TX power = **17 dBm**, TX gain = **2 dBi**, RX gain = **2 dBi**, operating frequency = **868 MHz**, required receiver sensitivity = **−137 dBm**. Use FSPL formula. Comment on the result.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Link Budget.**
$$LB = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - S + G_{RX}$$
$$= 17 + 2 - (-137) + 2$$
$$= 17 + 2 + 137 + 2$$
$$\boxed{LB = 158 \text{ dB}}$$

**Step 2 — Free-space path-loss equation.**
$$FSPL(d) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$$
where $d$ is in km and $f$ in MHz.

**Step 3 — Equate FSPL to LB and solve for $d$:**
$$158 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(868) + 32.45$$
$$20\log_{10}(d) = 158 - 20\log_{10}(868) - 32.45$$
$$20\log_{10}(868) = 20 \times 2.9385 = 58.77$$
$$20\log_{10}(d) = 158 - 58.77 - 32.45 = 66.78$$
$$\log_{10}(d) = 3.339 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d = 10^{3.339} \approx 2{,}183 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Real-world comment (2 marks):**
- This **2,183 km** is a **theoretical free-space** value; actual terrestrial range in suburban deployments is typically **5–10 km** because of:
  - **Multi-path fading** and shadowing (clutter loss).
  - **Building/foliage attenuation** (an additional 15–25 dB over FSPL).
  - **Antenna height and ground-Plane** losses.
  - **Duty-cycle regulations** (e.g., 1 % in EU868 band) limiting throughput.
- Conclusion: LoRa is engineered for **kilometer-scale**, not hundred-kilometer-scale, terrestrial IoT links.

$$\boxed{d_{\max,\text{FSPL}} \approx 2{,}183 \text{ km (theoretical free-space)};\ \text{practical suburban range} \approx 5\text{–}10 \text{ km}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on LoRa questions:**
> 1. **Confusing LoRa with LoRaWAN** — they are *not* synonyms. 1 mark lost per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4.25 chirps** in the preamble formula — silently dropping it loses **2 marks** in any ToA numerical.
> 3. **Wrong $CR$ value** in bit-rate formula — the question gives "CR = 4/5"; the student must plug $CR = 0.8$ (or 4/5), not just the *parameter* value 1.
> 4. **Sensitivity sign error** — sensitivity is **negative** dBm; forgetting the negative sign flips the link budget by 274 dB and tanks the answer.
> 5. **Mixing up ToA with throughput** — ToA is in **milliseconds**; effective throughput is **bits per second** — they are *not* the same and must both be computed.
> 6. **Forgetting DE = 1** when $T_s \ge 16$ ms (i.e., SF 11 @ 125 kHz or SF 12 @ 125 kHz) — yields an incorrect $n_{payload}$ and loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Quoting FSPL range as practical range** — examiners specifically look for the "**real-world ~5–10 km**" qualifier. Missing it loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-remember bullets before you enter the exam hall:**
> - **LoRa** = PHY, **LoRaWAN** = MAC/Network. **Not the same thing.**
> - Modulation = **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** in **sub-GHz ISM** (433/868/915 MHz).
> - Three tunables: **SF (7–12)**, **BW (125/250/500 kHz)**, **CR (4/5–4/8)**.
> - Bit rate: $R_b = SF \times \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}} \times CR$.
> - Symbol period: $T_s = \dfrac{2^{SF}}{BW}$; doubles per SF step.
> - Sensitivity: $S = -174 + 10\log_{10}(BW) + NF - \lvert SNR_{req} \rvert$.
> - Preamble: $T_{preamble} = (8 + 4.25) \times T_s$ (the **4.25 is non-negotiable**).
> - Payload symbols: $n_{payload} = 8 + \lceil \cdot \rceil \times (CR+4)$.
> - ToA: $T_{packet} = T_{preamble} + n_{payload} \cdot T_s$.
> - Link budget: $LB = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S$.
> - FSPL: $20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ (d in km, f in MHz).
> - LoRaWAN device classes: **A** (lowest power, mandatory), **B** (synchronized ping slots), **C** (always listening).
> - LoRaWAN architecture: **ED → Gateway → Network Server → Application Server**, with optional **Join Server**.
> - **ADR** = Network Server adjusts SF/BW per node to minimize airtime while keeping link margin.
> - **Security** = **AES-128** at two layers (network + application keys).
> - Range: **2–8 km urban, >15 km rural LOS**; data rate: **0.25–50 kbps**.
> - Practical battery life: **8–10 years** on 2 × AA (duty-cycled).
> - Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) is the network server's tool to balance **range, capacity, and battery**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zigbee
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Zigbee

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Zigbee** is a specification for a suite of high-level communication protocols used to create **Personal Area Networks (PANs)** built from small, low-power digital radios. It is based on the **IEEE 802.15.4 standard** for Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs). Zigbee operates in the **2.4 GHz, 915 MHz, and 868 MHz ISM (Industrial, Scientific, and Medical) radio bands** and is specifically engineered for applications requiring **low data rates (20/40/250 kbps)**, **long battery life (months to years)**, and **secure mesh networking** — making it a cornerstone protocol in the Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Zigbee is grouped under the "Short-Range IoT Communication Protocols" along with Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and RFID. Examiners expect students to clearly differentiate Zigbee from BLE and Wi-Fi based on **range, data rate, power consumption, and topology**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Neighbourhood Watch Network"

Imagine a gated community where every household (a **sensor node**) is fitted with a small walkie-talkie. Instead of every house shouting directly to the main security office (the **base station**), houses can pass messages to their immediate neighbours, who in turn relay it further. The walkie-talkies consume so little battery that they last for years, and even if one house is sleeping (powered off), the message finds an alternative path. This is **exactly how Zigbee works** — a **self-healing mesh network** where battery-powered nodes collaborate to forward data toward a coordinator. Unlike a loud megaphone (Wi-Fi) or a one-to-one phone call (Bluetooth), Zigbee is a calm, persistent, neighbour-to-neighbour whisper network designed for *efficiency over speed*.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Zigbee Specification |
|---|---|
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.15.4 (PHY/MAC) + Zigbee Alliance (NWK/APL) |
| **Frequency Bands** | **2.4 GHz (Global)**, 915 MHz (USA), 868 MHz (Europe) |
| **Data Rate** | **250 kbps @ 2.4 GHz**, 40 kbps @ 915 MHz, 20 kbps @ 868 MHz |
| **Range** | **10 – 100 metres** (line-of-sight) |
| **Network Nodes** | Up to **65,536 devices** per network |
| **Encryption** | **AES-128 bit** block cipher |
| **Topology** | Star, Mesh (P2P), Tree, Cluster-Tree |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:** Zigbee is **not** a single hardware chip — it is a **protocol stack** that sits *on top of* the IEEE 802.15.4 radio standard. The lower two layers (PHY and MAC) come from IEEE, while the upper layers (NWK, APS, ZDO, Application) are defined by the **Zigbee Alliance** (now Connectivity Standards Alliance).

### 1.4 Why Zigbee Matters in IoT

Zigbee is the silent backbone of **smart homes, industrial automation, smart metering, and healthcare monitoring**. Because it supports **hundreds of low-power nodes** on a single mesh coordinator, it enables massive Machine-Type Communication (mMTC) scenarios central to the vision of Industry 4.0 and smart cities.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Zigbee Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

Zigbee's architecture follows the **OSI 7-layer model** in a condensed form. The KTU syllabus specifically requires understanding these layers:

#### A. IEEE 802.15.4 Defined Layers (Lower Two)

1. **Physical Layer (PHY)**
   - Responsible for **radio activation/deactivation, channel selection, energy detection (ED), link quality indication (LQI), and Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)**.
   - Uses **O-QPSK (Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying) modulation** in the 2.4 GHz band.
   - Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) is applied.

2. **Medium Access Control Layer (MAC)**
   - Manages **beacon management, channel access (CSMA/CA), GTS (Guaranteed Time Slot) allocation, frame validation, and acknowledgements**.
   - Uses **16-bit short addresses** and **64-bit extended addresses**.

#### B. Zigbee Alliance Defined Layers (Upper Four)

3. **Network Layer (NWK)**
   - Handles **mesh routing, network formation, address assignment, device discovery, and neighbour tables**.
   - Implements two routing protocols: **Tree Routing (AODV-like)** and **Mesh Routing (AODVjr)**.

4. **Application Support Sub-Layer (APS)**
   - Manages **end-to-end binding between devices, message reliability counters, and group addressing**.

5. **Zigbee Device Objects (ZDO)**
   - Defines the **role of a node** (Coordinator, Router, End Device) and provides device/service discovery.

6. **Application Framework**
   - Hosts the **application objects (endpoints 1-240)** which contain the user-defined clusters and attributes.

### 2.2 Zigbee Device Types — The Three Pillars

| Device Type | Symbol | Function | Can Sleep? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zigbee Coordinator (ZC)** | 🔴 | One per network; forms the PAN, stores security keys, bridges to other networks | ❌ No (always ON) |
| **Zigbee Router (ZR)** | 🔵 | Relays frames between nodes, supports child devices, participates in mesh routing | ❌ No (mains powered) |
| **Zigbee End Device (ZED)** | ⚪ | Senses/actuates, talks only to parent, lowest power | ✅ **Yes** (battery powered) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Footprint (KTU Favourite):** Coordinator requires the **largest memory** (~32 KB RAM), Router is **medium** (~16 KB RAM), and End Device is the **lightest** (~4-8 KB RAM). This is why ZEDs are ideal for coin-cell powered sensors.

### 2.3 Network Topologies Supported

```
A. STAR Topology
   [ZR/C]  <----- ZED
   [ZC]    <----- ZED
           <----- ZED

B. MESH Topology (Peer-to-Peer)
   [ZR] --- [ZR] --- [ZR]
    |        |        |
   [ZED]   [ZR]    [ZED]
    |        |        |
   [ZR] --- [ZC] --- [ZR]

C. TREE / Cluster-Tree Topology
              [ZC]
             /    \
          [ZR]    [ZR]
          /  \      \
       [ZED][ZR]  [ZED]
              \
             [ZED]
```

- **Star:** All devices communicate only with the coordinator.
- **Mesh:** Any device can route through any other device — provides **self-healing** and **redundancy**.
- **Tree:** Hierarchical parent-child routing using **distributed address assignment**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum nodes per Zigbee PAN** | $N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Defines the address space ceiling for entire PAN |
| **Symbol Rate (2.4 GHz)** | $R_s = 62.5 \text{ ksymbols/s}$ | Each symbol carries 4 bits via O-QPSK |
| **Bit Rate (2.4 GHz)** | $R_b = R_s \times 4 = 250 \text{ kbps}$ | Theoretical throughput ceiling |
| **Chip Rate (DSSS)** | $R_c = 2 \text{ Mchips/s}$ | Spreading sequence speed for DSSS |
| **Processing Gain** | $G_p = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b} = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000} = 8$ (i.e. **9 dB**) | Resistance to interference |
| **Time Slot Duration** | $T_{slot} = 60 \text{ symbols} \times 16 \mu s = 960 \mu s$ | Superframe slot timing |
| **Beacon Interval** | $BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO}$ | Determines duty cycle |
| **Superframe Duration** | $SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO}$ | Active period length |
| **Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{SD}{BI} = 2^{SO - BO}$ | Battery life tuning |
| **AES Block Size** | 128 bits | Security strength metric |
| **Maximum Hop Count** | 30 (default in Zigbee PRO) | Limits routing loops |
| **Maximum Route Discovery Time** | 10 seconds | Network convergence metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Used:** $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 15.36 \text{ ms}$ (960 symbols × 16 μs); $BO$ = Beacon Order (0–14); $SO$ = Superframe Order (0–BO).

### 2.5 Analytical Breakdown — Why Each Layer Exists

- **PHY (Why?):** Converts digital bits to RF waves. The choice of 2.4 GHz provides **global unlicensed access** at the cost of higher path loss.
- **MAC (How?):** Uses **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) — not CSMA/CD — because radios cannot detect collisions while transmitting (half-duplex).
- **NWK (Why?):** Enables **multi-hop mesh routing** so a packet can travel beyond the radio's single-hop range, allowing coverage of large industrial floors.
- **APS (How?):** Provides **application-level addressing (endpoints + clusters)** so a single device can host multiple logical functions (e.g., a smart bulb with brightness + colour cluster).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing Zigbee Network Capacity

**Problem:** A Zigbee network uses a hierarchical tree topology. Given the maximum number of children ($C_m$), maximum depth ($L_m$), and maximum routers ($R_m$), determine the total number of addressable end devices.

**Given:** $C_m = 7$, $R_m = 5$, $L_m = 4$, $n = 4$ (number of bits for addressing).

**Step 1: Define the address space allocation.**
Zigbee's distributed address assignment reserves a portion of the 16-bit address space (65536) for routing. The depth of the tree is bounded by the parameter $L_m$.

**Step 2: Compute the skip address offset ($C_{skip}$).**

The address skipping function depends on the node's depth $d$:

$$
C_{skip}(d) = 
\begin{cases} 
1 + C_m \cdot (L_m - d - 1), & \text{if } R_m = 1 \\
\dfrac{1 + C_m - R_m - C_m \cdot R_m^{L_m - d - 1}}{1 - R_m}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate for $R_m > 1$.**

$$
C_{skip}(d) = \dfrac{1 + C_m - R_m - C_m \cdot R_m^{L_m - d - 1}}{1 - R_m}
$$

**Step 4: Plug in values for the root coordinator ($d = 0$):**

$$
C_{skip}(0) = \dfrac{1 + 7 - 5 - 7 \cdot 5^{4 - 0 - 1}}{1 - 5}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the exponent term.**

$$
5^{3} = 125
$$

**Step 6: Substitute and compute the numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1 + 7 - 5 - (7 \times 125) = 8 - 5 - 875 = -872
$$

**Step 7: Compute the denominator.**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1 - 5 = -4
$$

**Step 8: Final division.**

$$
C_{skip}(0) = \dfrac{-872}{-4} = 218
$$

**Interpretation:** The coordinator can allocate **218 unique address blocks** to its child routers. This defines the deterministic scalability of the tree.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Duty Cycle and Battery Life Estimation

**Problem:** A Zigbee end device uses Beacon-Enabled mode with $BO = 6$, $SO = 2$. Compute the duty cycle.

**Step 1: Recall the base superframe duration.**

$$
SD_{base} = 15.36 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Beacon Interval (BI).**

$$
BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO} = 15.36 \times 2^{6} = 15.36 \times 64 = 983.04 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Superframe Duration (SD).**

$$
SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO} = 15.36 \times 2^{2} = 15.36 \times 4 = 61.44 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the duty cycle.**

$$
D = \dfrac{SD}{BI} = 2^{SO - BO} = 2^{2 - 6} = 2^{-4} = 0.0625
$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage.**

$$
D_{\%} = 0.0625 \times 100 = 6.25\%
$$

**Step 6: Estimate battery life.**
Assuming an average current of $20 \text{ mA}$ when active and $5 \mu A$ when sleeping, and a 2400 mAh CR2032 coin cell:

$$
I_{avg} = (D \times 20) + ((1 - D) \times 0.005) = (0.0625 \times 20) + (0.9375 \times 0.005) = 1.25 + 0.0047 = 1.2547 \text{ mA}
$$

$$
\text{Battery Life} = \dfrac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{1.2547 \text{ mA}} = 1913 \text{ hours} \approx 79.7 \text{ days}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When $SO = BO$, the node stays **100% awake** (no power saving). When $SO = 0$, the node is **maximally asleep**. The ratio $2^{SO-BO}$ is the most-tested formula in KTU Zigbee numericals.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Competing Protocols

| Parameter | **Zigbee** | **Bluetooth LE (BLE)** | **Wi-Fi (802.11n)** | **LoRaWAN** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IEEE Standard** | 802.15.4 | 802.15.1 | 802.11 | (Proprietary) |
| **Frequency** | 2.4 GHz / 868/915 MHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 433/868/915 MHz |
| **Data Rate** | **250 kbps** | 2 Mbps | 150+ Mbps | 0.3 – 50 kbps |
| **Range** | 10–100 m | 10–30 m | 50–200 m | **2 – 15 km** |
| **Topology** | **Mesh** (Star/Tree) | Piconet (Star) | Star (AP-centric) | Star-of-Stars |
| **Battery Life** | **Months – Years** | Months | Hours – Days | **Years** |
| **Max Nodes** | **65,536** | 8 (active) | ~32 (per AP) | Millions |
| **Power** | Very Low | Low | High | Very Low |
| **Best Use** | Smart home, sensor mesh | Wearables, beacons | Video, web | Long-range agriculture |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Air Time / Energy Cost of a Single Frame

**Problem:** Compute the transmission time of a Zigbee ACK frame (length 5 bytes) at 2.4 GHz.

**Step 1: Compute the preamble + SFD overhead (fixed).**

$$
T_{overhead} = 6 \text{ bytes} = 48 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the actual frame air time (data portion).**

$$
L_{data} = 5 \text{ bytes} = 40 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Total bits to transmit.**

$$
L_{total} = 48 + 40 = 88 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the time at 250 kbps.**

$$
T_{frame} = \dfrac{88 \text{ bits}}{250{,}000 \text{ bits/s}} = 352 \mu s
$$

**Step 5: Add the turnaround time ($192 \mu s$) per IEEE 802.15.4.**

$$
T_{total} = 352 + 192 = 544 \mu s
$$

This **sub-millisecond air time** is why Zigbee achieves extremely low average power consumption.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Zigbee Protocol Stack — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["Application Layer (User Defined)"]
        EP1["Endpoint 1\nLight Cluster"]
        EP2["Endpoint 2\nTemp Cluster"]
    end

    subgraph ZDO["ZDO Layer"]
        ZDO1["Device Discovery\nBind Mgmt\nSecurity Mgr"]
    end

    subgraph APS["APS Sub-Layer"]
        APS1["Binding Tables\nGroup Addressing\nAPS Frames"]
    end

    subgraph NWK["NWK Layer (Zigbee Alliance)"]
        NWK1["Mesh Routing\nTree Routing\nAddr Assignment"]
    end

    subgraph MAC["MAC Layer (IEEE 802.15.4)"]
        MAC1["CSMA-CA\nBeacon Mgmt\nGTS Allocation"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["PHY Layer (IEEE 802.15.4)"]
        PHY1["2.4 GHz O-QPSK\nDSSS Modulation\nCCA / ED"]
    end

    APP --> ZDO
    APP --> APS
    ZDO --> APS
    APS --> NWK
    NWK --> MAC
    MAC --> PHY
    PHY -. RF Waves .-> PHY
```

### 4.2 Zigbee Mesh Network Topology — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ZC["ZC - Coordinator\nPAN ID 0x1234\nBridge to Internet"]
    ZR1["ZR - Router\nMains Powered"]
    ZR2["ZR - Router\nMains Powered"]
    ZR3["ZR - Router\nMains Powered"]
    ZED1["ZED - Temp Sensor\nBattery Powered"]
    ZED2["ZED - Motion Sensor\nBattery Powered"]
    ZED3["ZED - Smart Lock\nBattery Powered"]
    ZED4["ZED - Light Switch\nBattery Powered"]
    ZED5["ZED - Gas Sensor\nBattery Powered"]
    CLOUD["Cloud Server\nvia Wi-Fi Gateway"]

    ZR1 --- ZC
    ZR2 --- ZC
    ZR3 --- ZC
    ZR1 --- ZR2
    ZR2 --- ZR3
    ZR1 --- ZR3
    ZED1 --> ZR1
    ZED2 --> ZR1
    ZED3 --> ZR2
    ZED4 --> ZR3
    ZED5 --> ZR3
    ZC --> CLOUD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dotted lines represent **logical parent-child associations**, while the solid lines represent **wireless radio links**. Notice how each ZED has only *one* parent (its router), but the routers are interconnected — this is the hallmark of a **self-healing mesh**. If the link between ZR1 and ZC fails, ZR1 can reroute through ZR2.

### 4.3 Zigbee Frame Format (IEEE 802.15.4 MAC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Preamble\n4 bytes"] --> B["SFD\n1 byte"]
    B --> C["Frame Control\n2 bytes"]
    C --> D["Sequence Number\n1 byte"]
    D --> E["Address Fields\n0-20 bytes"]
    E --> F["Aux Security\n0/5/6 bytes"]
    F --> G["Payload\n0-127 bytes"]
    G --> H["FCS\n2 bytes"]

    style A fill:#f9e79f
    style B fill:#f9e79f
    style H fill:#fadbd8
    style G fill:#d5f5e3
```

### 4.4 Network Formation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Step 1: Energy Detect Scan\nSearch for clean RF channel"]
    S2["Step 2: Active Scan\nListen for existing PANs"]
    S3["Step 3: Channel Selection\nPick least noisy channel"]
    S4["Step 4: PAN ID Assignment\nChoose 16-bit PAN ID"]
    S5["Step 5: Become Coordinator\nZC starts network"]
    S6["Step 6: Permit Joining\nZR/ZED request to join"]
    S7["Step 7: Address Assignment\nDistributed addresses given"]
    S8["Step 8: Network Operational\nData flows begin"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List any three differences between Zigbee and Bluetooth.*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Zigbee | Bluetooth |
|---|---|---|
| 1. | Based on **IEEE 802.15.4** | Based on **IEEE 802.15.1** |
| 2. | Supports **mesh, star, tree** topologies (up to **65,536 nodes**) | Supports only **piconet/star** (max **8 active** nodes) |
| 3. | Data rate **250 kbps**; range up to **100 m** | Data rate up to **2 Mbps**; range ~**10 m** |
| 4. | Designed for **low-duty-cycle sensor** applications | Designed for **cable-replacement / streaming** |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*What is the role of a Zigbee Coordinator? Can a network have more than one Coordinator?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Zigbee Coordinator (ZC)** is responsible for:
1. **Network formation** — selecting the channel and PAN ID.
2. **Storing security keys** (Trust Center function).
3. **Bridge/router** to other networks (e.g., Wi-Fi gateway to the cloud).
4. **Address allocation** for child devices.

**No**, a Zigbee network can have **only one Coordinator** per PAN. It is the root of the network tree and must always remain powered on.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Zigbee protocol stack** with a neat diagram. Compare the responsibilities of the **IEEE 802.15.4 layers** with those of the **Zigbee Alliance layers**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **three network topologies** supported by Zigbee. Why is **mesh topology** preferred for industrial IoT deployments? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Zigbee Protocol Stack Explanation:**

The Zigbee stack is a **4-block, 7-layer hybrid** architecture. The **bottom two layers** (PHY and MAC) are standardized by **IEEE 802.15.4**, while the **top four layers** (NWK, APS, ZDO, AF) are owned by the **Zigbee Alliance (now Connectivity Standards Alliance)**.

1. **Physical Layer (PHY) — IEEE Defined:**
   - Operates in the **2.4 GHz** (global), **915 MHz** (USA), and **868 MHz** (Europe) ISM bands.
   - Uses **O-QPSK modulation** with **DSSS** at **2 Mchips/s**.
   - Provides 16 channels in 2.4 GHz, each **5 MHz wide** with **2 MHz spacing**.
   - Performs **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)**, **Energy Detection (ED)**, and **Link Quality Indication (LQI)**.

2. **MAC Layer — IEEE Defined:**
   - Controls access using **CSMA/CA** (slotted in beacon mode, unslotted otherwise).
   - Supports **beacon management** for synchronized operation.
   - Allocates **Guaranteed Time Slots (GTS)** for time-critical data.
   - Uses **16-bit short** or **64-bit extended** addresses.

3. **Network Layer (NWK) — Zigbee Alliance:**
   - Performs **mesh routing** (AODVjr) and **tree routing** (hierarchical).
   - Manages **neighbour tables**, **routing tables**, and **route discovery**.
   - Limits route discovery to **30 hops** and **10 seconds**.

4. **Application Support Sub-Layer (APS):**
   - Manages **binding tables** that map clusters across devices.
   - Handles **group addressing** and **fragmentation**.

5. **Zigbee Device Objects (ZDO):**
   - Defines device roles: **Coordinator, Router, End Device**.
   - Handles **device/service discovery** and **security management**.

6. **Application Framework (AF):**
   - Hosts the user-defined **application objects** at endpoints **1–240**.

**Comparison Summary (Marks Table):**

| Layer | Defining Body | Core Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| PHY | IEEE | Radio modulation, channel sensing |
| MAC | IEEE | Channel access, addressing |
| NWK | Zigbee | Routing, network formation |
| APS / ZDO / AF | Zigbee | Application binding, device roles |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Three Zigbee Topologies:**

**1. Star Topology:**
- All ZEDs communicate *only* with the **central coordinator**.
- **Pros:** Simple, low-latency.
- **Cons:** Coordinator is a single point of failure; range limited to one hop.

**2. Tree (Cluster-Tree) Topology:**
- Hierarchical parent-child structure rooted at the coordinator.
- Routing follows the **tree path** using **distributed address assignment** ($C_{skip}$).
- **Pros:** Predictable addresses, low memory overhead.
- **Cons:** Failure of a parent isolates its subtree (no alternative paths).

**3. Mesh (Peer-to-Peer) Topology:**
- Every router can talk to *any other* router within radio range.
- **Pros:** **Self-healing**, **redundant paths**, extended range via **multi-hop**, robust to node/link failures.
- **Cons:** Higher memory, more complex routing, route discovery overhead.

**Why Mesh is preferred for Industrial IoT:**

1. **Resilience** — Industrial environments suffer from RF interference, metal obstructions, and node failures. Mesh's **self-healing** rerouting keeps the network alive.
2. **Extended Coverage** — Multi-hopping covers large factory floors, warehouses, and outdoor plants without extra infrastructure.
3. **Scalability** — Up to **65,536 nodes** can join a single mesh PAN, ideal for dense sensor deployments.
4. **No Single Point of Failure** — Unlike star topology, the failure of one router does not partition the network.

---

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Zigbee device types** (Coordinator, Router, End Device) with their characteristics, memory requirements, and use cases. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC frame format**. Compute the **transmission time of a 25-byte Zigbee data frame** at 2.4 GHz. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Zigbee Device Types — Detailed Comparison:**

| Property | Coordinator (ZC) | Router (ZR) | End Device (ZED) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PAN Formation** | ✅ Yes (one per network) | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Routing** | Yes | Yes | ❌ No (parent only) |
| **Child Devices** | Yes | Yes | ❌ No |
| **Can Sleep?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** |
| **Power Source** | Mains | Mains | **Battery** |
| **Memory Required** | **~32 KB RAM** | ~16 KB RAM | **~4–8 KB RAM** |
| **Typical Role** | Bridge to cloud, trust center | Network backbone, message relay | Sensor / actuator |
| **Use Case** | Smart-home gateway, industrial gateway | Mains-powered smart switches, repeaters | Battery sensors (temp, motion, contact) |

**Coordinator (ZC):**
- Only **one per network**.
- Assigns the **16-bit PAN ID** and selects the **operating channel**.
- Acts as the **Trust Center** storing the network key for **AES-128 encryption**.
- Bridges Zigbee to other IP networks.

**Router (ZR):**
- Always awake; **mains-powered** typically.
- Maintains **neighbour and routing tables**.
- Forwards frames in **multi-hop** mesh.
- Can also serve as a **parent** to multiple ZEDs.

**End Device (ZED):**
- Talks **only to its parent** (one hop away).
- **Sleeps** between transmissions → minimal energy use.
- Cannot route → saves memory and CPU.
- **Ideal for coin-cell sensors** that must last **2–5 years**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Frame Format:**

A MAC frame consists of:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Frame Control | 2 | Identifies frame type, addressing mode, security |
| Sequence Number | 1 | Matches frame with its ACK |
| Destination PAN ID | 0/2 | PAN of receiver (omitted if intra-PAN) |
| Destination Address | 0/2/8 | Short or extended |
| Source PAN ID | 0/2 | PAN of sender (omitted if intra-PAN) |
| Source Address | 0/2/8 | Short or extended |
| Auxiliary Security | 0/5/6/10 | Security header (if enabled) |
| **Payload** | **0–127** | Upper-layer data (NWK + APS + AF) |
| FCS | 2 | Frame Check Sequence (CRC-16) |

**Diagram:**

```
[Frame Control | Seq Num | Dest PAN ID | Dest Addr | Src PAN ID | Src Addr | Aux Sec | Payload | FCS]
   2 bytes      1 B       0/2 B        0/2/8 B    0/2 B       0/2/8 B    0/5/6    0–127 B    2 B
```

**Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** Data payload = 25 bytes. Assume 16-bit short addressing, no security, intra-PAN (no dest/source PAN ID), preamble = 4 B, SFD = 1 B.

**Step 1: Compute the overhead (MHR + MFR).**

$$
\text{Frame Control} + \text{Seq} + \text{Dest Addr} + \text{Src Addr} + \text{FCS} = 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 9 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total MAC frame.**

$$
L_{MAC} = 9 + 25 = 34 \text{ bytes} = 272 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Add PHY overhead (preamble + SFD).**

$$
L_{PHY} = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ bytes} = 40 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Total bits on air.**

$$
L_{total} = 272 + 40 = 312 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5: Compute transmission time at 250 kbps.**

$$
T_{tx} = \dfrac{312 \text{ bits}}{250{,}000 \text{ bits/s}} = 1.248 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6: Add MAC turnaround and ACK (if applicable).**

$$
T_{total} = 1.248 + 0.192 \text{ (turnaround)} + 0.352 \text{ (ACK)} = 1.792 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 25-byte data frame takes approximately **1.25 ms** of pure air time — confirming Zigbee's suitability for low-duty-cycle IoT sensing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Beacon Interval" with "Superframe Duration."** Many students swap $BI$ and $SD$ in duty-cycle problems.
> 2. **Zigbee ≠ IEEE 802.15.4.** Always clarify which layer you are referring to. PHY/MAC = IEEE; NWK/APS/ZDO/AF = Zigbee Alliance.
> 3. **The Coordinator ≠ Router.** ZC forms the network; ZR joins an already-formed network and extends it.
> 4. **Maximum nodes = 65,536**, *not* 65,000. Memorize the exact figure.
> 5. **AES-128** means 128-bit key length, not 128-byte. Common slip.
> 6. **DSSS** is used, **not** FHSS. A classic trick question.
> 7. In the **MAC frame**, payload can be **0–127 bytes**, not 0–255. Students often write the wrong upper bound.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Zigbee** is built on **IEEE 802.15.4** (PHY + MAC) and the **Zigbee Alliance** (NWK + APS + ZDO + AF).
- ✅ Operates at **2.4 GHz (global)**, **915 MHz (US)**, and **868 MHz (EU)** with **250 / 40 / 20 kbps** data rates.
- ✅ Supports **3 topologies**: **Star, Tree, Mesh**; Mesh is preferred for **self-healing, scalable IoT**.
- ✅ **3 device types**: **ZC (Coordinator)**, **ZR (Router)**, **ZED (End Device)** — only ZED can sleep.
- ✅ Maximum **65,536** devices per PAN; **16-bit short / 64-bit extended** addressing.
- ✅ Security via **AES-128 bit encryption** with the Coordinator as **Trust Center**.
- ✅ Modulation = **O-QPSK**, Spreading = **DSSS @ 2 Mchips/s**, Processing Gain = **9 dB**.
- ✅ MAC access = **CSMA/CA** (slotted/un-slotted), GTS for time-critical traffic.
- ✅ Duty cycle formula = $D = 2^{SO - BO}$; Beacon Interval = $15.36 \times 2^{BO}$ ms.
- ✅ Routing: **AODVjr (mesh)** + **Tree routing**; max **30 hops**, **10 s** discovery timeout.
- ✅ Frame: 2 B Control + 1 B Seq + Addr fields + **0–127 B payload** + 2 B FCS, plus **preamble + SFD**.
- ✅ **Not** Zigbee-WiFi-Bluetooth confusion: Zigbee = **low power + mesh + long battery**, BLE = **short range + star**, Wi-Fi = **high power + high throughput**.
- ✅ **Applications:** Smart home (Philips Hue, Amazon Echo Plus), industrial automation, smart metering, healthcare monitoring, asset tracking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth/Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bluetooth/Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bluetooth** is a short-range, low-power, low-cost **wireless personal area network (WPAN)** technology standardized under **IEEE 802.15.1**, operating in the globally license-free **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio band**. It uses **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** with a symbol rate of **1 Msps** in Classic Bluetooth and **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** modulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** — formally designated **Bluetooth 4.0+ LE** and later rebranded **Bluetooth Smart** — is a distinct, lean protocol stack engineered from the ground up for **ultra-low power consumption**, targeting coin-cell battery IoT devices. BLE is *not* an upgrade of Classic Bluetooth; the two share the brand name but use **incompatible physical layers and link layers**. BLE supports an air-interface bit rate of **1 Mbps (LE 1M)** or **2 Mbps (LE 2M, from Bluetooth 5.0)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polite Postman vs. The Whispering Mailbox"

Imagine the Classic Bluetooth protocol as a **loud postman** who walks into your house, sits at your dining table, sets up an elaborate two-way intercom (LMP, L2CAP, RFCOMM, OBEX), and exchanges large parcels of audio data continuously. He is fast, but he is **always in the room**, draining your phone's battery.

Now imagine **BLE as a whispering mailbox**. The IoT sensor (a *peripheral*, e.g., a heart-rate chest strap) only **shouts a small advertisement** every 100 ms to 1 second on three dedicated "whispering frequencies". A smartphone (*central*) catches the shout, opens a **short, polite dialogue** lasting a few milliseconds to exchange a handful of bytes, then both devices go back to **deep sleep**. The mailbox wakes up only when it has something genuinely new to say.

This is the philosophical core of BLE: **asymmetric, bursty, ultra-low-duty-cycle communication**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner to remember:** *Classic Bluetooth = always-connected streaming pipe*; *BLE = occasional burst-talking polite mailbox*.

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Classic Bluetooth (BR/EDR) | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Band | **2.400 – 2.4835 GHz** | **2.400 – 2.4835 GHz** |
| RF Channels | **79** channels × 1 MHz | **40** channels × 2 MHz |
| Modulation | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | **GFSK** only |
| Symbol Rate | 1 Msps (BR), 2/3 Msps (EDR) | **1 Msps** (LE 1M) / 2 Msps (LE 2M) |
| Peak Current | ~30 mA | **< 15 mA** (typical ~6 mA) |
| Sleep Current | ~50 µA (Sniff mode) | **~1 µA** |
| Range | 10 – 100 m | **50 – 200+ m** (LE Coded PHY, BT 5.0) |
| Connection Setup | ~100 ms – 2 s | **< 3 ms** |
| Max Data Throughput | 2.1 Mbps | **~2 Mbps (LE 2M PHY)** |
| Profile Stack Size | ~250 KB | **~50 KB** (suitable for MCUs) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Examiners frequently test the **fundamental architectural difference** between Classic BT and BLE. Memorize that BLE uses **40 channels of 2 MHz spacing** while Classic uses **79 channels of 1 MHz spacing**, and that BLE was **invented by Nokia (Wibree, 2006)** before being merged into Bluetooth 4.0.

---

## 1.4 Evolution Timeline (KTU Hot Topic)

```
Bluetooth 1.0 (1999)  →  Basic Rate (BR), 1 Mbps, audio focus
Bluetooth 2.0 + EDR   →  Enhanced Data Rate, 2–3 Mbps
Bluetooth 3.0 + HS    →  AMP (Wi-Fi co-existence), up to 24 Mbps
Bluetooth 4.0 (2010)  →  BLE INTRODUCED ★ (Wibree absorbed)
Bluetooth 4.1 / 4.2   →  IoT improvements, IPv6/6LoWPAN, LE Privacy
Bluetooth 5.0 (2016)  →  LE 2M PHY, LE Coded PHY (500/125 kbps), 4× range, 8× adv
Bluetooth 5.1         →  Direction Finding (AoA / AoD)
Bluetooth 5.2         →  LE Audio, LE Power Control, Isochronous Channels
Bluetooth 5.3 / 5.4   →  Periodic Adv, Encrypted Adv, PAwR, ESL
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BLE Protocol Stack Architecture — The "Why" Behind Each Layer

BLE is architected as a **lean vertical stack** with two mandatory host-side sub-systems: **GAP** (Generic Access Profile) and **GATT** (Generic Attribute Profile). The protocol stack is deliberately split into a **Controller** (often a separate BLE SoC radio chip) and a **Host** (running on the application MCU), joined by the **HCI (Host Controller Interface)**.

### 2.1.1 Controller Sub-Layers

1. **Physical Layer (PHY)**
   - **Why:** Defines how 0s and 1s are converted into radio waves.
   - **How:** Uses **Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK)** with a bandwidth-bit-period product $BT = 0.5$ and a modulation index $h \in [0.45,\ 0.55]$. A logical '1' is transmitted as a **positive frequency deviation** of at least **185 kHz**, and a logical '0' as a **negative deviation** of equal magnitude.

2. **Link Layer (LL)**
   - **Why:** Manages radio state, channel selection, advertising, scanning, and connection events.
   - **How:** Implements a finite state machine with five states: *Standby, Advertising, Scanning, Initiating, Connection*. Uses **Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH)** that hops on a per-event basis according to the formula $f_n = 2402 + 2k_n$ MHz where $k_n$ is a pseudo-random channel index.

3. **Direct Test Mode (DTM) & HCI (Host Controller Interface)**
   - **Why:** Provides standardized test hooks and a transport (UART/SPI/USB) between host and controller.

### 2.1.2 Host Sub-Layers

4. **L2CAP (Logical Link Control & Adaptation Protocol)**
   - **Why:** Multiplexes higher-layer protocols (ATT, SMP) and performs segmentation/reassembly.
   - **How:** In BLE, L2CAP operates in a simplified **LE-only mode**, supporting fixed channel ID 0x0005 for ATT and 0x0006 for SMP. Maximum L2CAP SDU in BLE is **251 bytes** (vs. 65535 in Classic BT).

5. **ATT (Attribute Protocol)**
   - **Why:** The "language" of BLE data — defines how attributes are discovered, read, written, and notified.
   - **How:** Operates on a strict **client-server model** where the server exposes a database of *attributes*. Each attribute = **{Handle (2B), Type UUID (2B or 16B), Value (≤512B), Permissions}**.

6. **GATT (Generic Attribute Profile)**
   - **Why:** Groups attributes into logical *Services* and *Characteristics* — the real API surface for IoT apps.
   - **How:** Hierarchy: **Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor → Value**. A characteristic exposes operations: *Read, Write, Write Without Response, Notify, Indicate*.

7. **GAP (Generic Access Profile)**
   - **Why:** Defines device **roles** (*Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central*) and discovery/connection procedures.
   - **How:** Manages advertising data format (max 31 bytes payload), scan response, and bonding.

8. **SMP (Security Manager Protocol)**
   - **Why:** Handles pairing, encryption, and identity resolution (LE Privacy).
   - **How:** Uses **AES-CCM-128** encryption. Four pairing methods: *Just Works, Numeric Comparison, Passkey, Out-of-Band (OOB)*.

---

## 2.2 BLE Channel Classification — The 2.4 GHz Band Math

The 2.4 GHz ISM band (2400 – 2483.5 MHz) is partitioned by BLE into **40 RF channels**, each **2 MHz wide**, centered at:

$$
f_k \;=\; 2402 \;+\; 2k \quad \text{MHz}, \qquad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 39\}
$$

These 40 channels are split into two functional groups:

- **Advertising Channels: k = 37, 38, 39** → $f = 2402,\ 2426,\ 2480$ MHz.
  - **Why these three?** They lie at the **band edges**, deliberately avoiding the **noisy Wi-Fi mid-band** (channels 1–13 of Wi-Fi occupy ~2412–2472 MHz). This guarantees that advertisements survive in dense 2.4 GHz interference.
- **Data Channels: k = 0, 1, 2, …, 36** → 37 channels used during an active connection for **adaptive frequency hopping**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** To find the center frequency of any BLE channel instantly → *Add double the channel index to 2402 MHz.* Example: Channel 17 → $2402 + 2(17) = 2436$ MHz.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Wearables** (heart-rate straps, CGM sensors, smart inhalers): BLE's < 1 µA sleep current enables **3–12 month coin-cell operation**.
- **Beacon-Based Retail / Indoor Positioning**: iBeacon (Apple) and Eddystone (Google) exploit the **3 advertising channels** to broadcast a 31-byte payload hundreds of times per second for sub-meter proximity detection.
- **Smart Home & Matter Ecosystem**: BLE is the **commissioning channel** of choice for Matter-over-Thread/Wi-Fi devices, thanks to its **< 3 ms pairing latency**.
- **Automotive TPMS & Keyless Entry**: BLE 5.x's **LE Coded PHY** (S=2 or S=8) trades raw throughput for **4× range and 12 dB link-budget improvement**, ideal for garage environments.
- **Industrial Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**: BLE Mesh (Bluetooth 5.0+) enables **flooded, managed, or friend-node** topologies for warehouse lighting and HVAC control.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the **analytical machinery** that examiners use to set 14-mark Part B questions. Each derivation is exhaustive, every algebraic transition is justified.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — BLE Channel Center Frequency Set

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A KTU examiner asks: *"Given the BLE PHY occupies the 2.4 GHz ISM band, derive the center frequency and number of channels, identifying the advertising channels."*

**Step 1 — Identify the band edges.**
The license-free ISM band allocated globally for short-range devices has:
- Lower edge: $f_L = 2400$ MHz
- Upper edge: $f_U = 2483.5$ MHz

**Step 2 — Define the channelization rule.**
BLE defines a regular grid of channels, each of bandwidth $B_{ch} = 2$ MHz, with **guard-band offsets** to protect the band edges. The center of channel $k$ is:

$$
f_k \;=\; 2402 + 2k \quad \text{MHz}, \qquad k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 39\}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the channel count.**
The highest channel index is $k = 39$, so:

$$
\text{Total channels} \;=\; 39 - 0 + 1 \;=\; 40
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the upper guard band.**
The center of channel 39 is:

$$
f_{39} = 2402 + 2(39) = 2402 + 78 = 2480 \text{ MHz}
$$

Each channel occupies $f_k \pm 1$ MHz, so channel 39 occupies **[2479, 2481] MHz**, leaving a **2.5 MHz guard band** up to the regulatory upper limit of 2483.5 MHz. This satisfies FCC/ETSI spectral masks.

**Step 5 — Classify advertising channels.**
The Bluetooth SIG designates three channels for advertising (used by *advertisers* and *scanners*), chosen to **escape Wi-Fi interference** in the 2412–2472 MHz region:

$$
f_{37} = 2402 + 2(37) = 2476 \text{ MHz}
$$
$$
f_{38} = 2402 + 2(38) = 2478 \text{ MHz}
$$

Wait — recheck: channel 37 lies *below* channel 38 by exactly 2 MHz. The classical assignment places the **three advertisement channels at indices {37, 38, 39}**, but the SIG chose channel indices that map to frequencies **2402, 2426, and 2480 MHz** to put one channel below, one within, and one above the dense Wi-Fi band. The formula is therefore:

$$
k_{adv} \in \{0,\ 12,\ 39\}
$$

**Step 6 — Re-express advertising frequencies using the same formula.**

$$
f_{0} = 2402 + 2(0) = 2402 \text{ MHz}
$$
$$
f_{12} = 2402 + 2(12) = 2426 \text{ MHz}
$$
$$
f_{39} = 2402 + 2(39) = 2480 \text{ MHz}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Hint:** Many students wrongly memorize "channels 37, 38, 39 are for advertising." This is the **data-channel index range** for the *highest* 3 RF channels. The advertising channels are RF indices **0, 12, 39**, which yield the canonical frequencies **2402 / 2426 / 2480 MHz**. Memorize the *frequencies*, not the indices!

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Link Budget & Range Estimation

**Problem Setup:**
A KTU IoT question asks: *"Estimate the maximum outdoor range of a BLE transmitter with $P_t = 0$ dBm, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and receiver sensitivity $P_{rx,min} = -94$ dBm. Use the free-space path loss (FSPL) model with $f = 2.45$ GHz."*

**Step 1 — Recall the Friis free-space transmission equation.**

$$
P_r(d) \;=\; P_t \,+\, G_t \,+\, G_r \;-\; L_{path}(d)
$$

where $L_{path}$ is the path loss in dB.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum tolerable path loss.**
Set $P_r = P_{rx,min}$:

$$
L_{max} \;=\; P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,min}
$$

$$
L_{max} \;=\; 0 \;+\; 2 \;+\; 2 \;-\; (-94)
$$

$$
L_{max} \;=\; 98 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the FSPL in dB form.**

$$
L_{FSPL}(d)\;[\text{dB}]\;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)
$$

Substitute $f = 2.45 \times 10^9$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$
L_{FSPL}(d)\;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi \cdot d \cdot 2.45 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the wavelength constant.**

$$
\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{c}{f} \;=\; \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 0.1224 \text{ m}
$$

So:

$$
L_{FSPL}(d) \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi \cdot 0.1224 \cdot d}{1}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Equate $L_{FSPL}$ to $L_{max}$ and solve for $d$.**

$$
20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi \cdot 0.1224 \cdot d}{1}\right) \;=\; 98
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(1.538 \cdot d\right) \;=\; \dfrac{98}{20} \;=\; 4.9
$$

$$
1.538 \cdot d \;=\; 10^{4.9} \;=\; 79432.8
$$

$$
d_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{79432.8}{1.538} \;\approx\; 51{,}640 \text{ m} \;\approx\; 51.6 \text{ km}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply a real-world safety margin.**
This theoretical FSPL assumes perfect line-of-sight and no atmospheric/fading losses. Applying a **fade margin of 20 dB** (typical for outdoor IoT):

$$
L_{FSPL,\,real} \;=\; 98 - 20 \;=\; 78 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(1.538 \cdot d) \;=\; 78 \;\Longrightarrow\; \log_{10}(1.538\,d) \;=\; 3.9
$$

$$
1.538\, d \;=\; 7943.3 \;\Longrightarrow\; d_{real} \;\approx\; 5164 \text{ m} \;\approx\; \textbf{5.16 km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A well-designed BLE 5.x link with LE Coded PHY (additional +12 dB link budget) can reach **15+ km in open-air line-of-sight** conditions — a remarkable figure for a coin-cell-powered radio!

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Throughput in a Connection Event

**Problem Setup:**
Given $T_{CI} = 30$ ms, $L_s = 0$, packet length $L_{app} = 20$ bytes per connection event, and LE 1M PHY, compute the application throughput.

**Step 1 — Compute bits per connection event.**

$$
N_{bits} \;=\; L_{app} \times 8 \;=\; 20 \times 8 \;=\; 160 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective throughput.**

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{N_{bits}}{T_{CI}} \;=\; \dfrac{160 \text{ bits}}{30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}
$$

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{160}{0.03} \;\approx\; 5333.3 \text{ bps} \;\approx\; 5.33 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Add slave-latency generalization.**
With slave latency $L_s > 0$, the peripheral *skips* $L_s$ connection events between two active events. The effective average throughput is:

$$
\eta_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{bits}}{T_{CI} \cdot (L_s + 1)}
$$

For $L_s = 9$, $T_{CI} = 100$ ms, $L_{app} = 20$ B:

$$
\eta_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{20 \times 8}{0.1 \times 10} \;=\; \dfrac{160}{1.0} \;=\; 160 \text{ bps}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Slave latency is the **single most powerful lever** for reducing average power consumption in a BLE peripheral — the radio effectively sleeps through $L_s$ intervals.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Classic BT vs. BLE

| Feature | Classic Bluetooth | BLE | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Channelization | 79 × 1 MHz | 40 × 2 MHz | BLE has fewer, wider, less interference-prone channels |
| Modulation | GFSK / DQPSK / DPSK | GFSK only | BLE HW is **simpler → cheaper SoC** |
| Hop Rate | 1600 hops/sec | **Per-event hop** | BLE saves the energy of frequent re-tuning |
| Peak Power | ~30 mA | < 15 mA | **2–3× lower** |
| Sleep Power | ~50 µA (Sniff) | ~1 µA | **50× lower** — enables coin-cell IoT |
| Connection Time | 100 ms – 2 s | **3 ms** | BLE enables latency-critical apps |
| Stack Size | ~250 KB | ~50 KB | BLE fits on Cortex-M0 (8 KB RAM) |
| Data Throughput | 2.1 Mbps | 2 Mbps (LE 2M) | Roughly comparable in raw burst speed |
| Topology | Piconet + Scatternet | Point-to-point + **Mesh** | BLE Mesh supports thousands of nodes |

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    standby --> advertising : LL SET ADV ENABLE
    standby --> scanning : LL SET SCAN ENABLE
    standby --> initiating : LL CREATE CONNECTION
    standby --> initiating : SCAN REQ FROM CENTRAL

    advertising --> connection : CONNECTION REQ FROM CENTRAL

    connection --> connection : CONNECTION EVENT REPEAT
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Read Before You Write:**
> 1. **Advertising channel indices are {0, 12, 39} → frequencies {2402, 2426, 2480} MHz**, NOT {37, 38, 39}. Mixing these up is the single most common 3-mark deduction.
> 2. **BLE is NOT backward-compatible with Classic Bluetooth at the radio level.** If you write "BLE works with Classic BT headsets", you lose marks.
> 3. **Throughput calculations must divide by $(L_s + 1)$**, not just $T_{CI}$, when slave latency is non-zero.
> 4. **Modulation index for BLE GFSK is 0.45 – 0.55 (nominal 0.5)** — do not confuse with Bluetooth Classic's 0.32 nominal value.
> 5. Always convert $T_{CI}$ from **ms to seconds** before dividing bits by it, or your throughput unit will be wrong.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Classic Bluetooth and Bluetooth Low Energy in terms of modulation, channel count, bandwidth per channel, and target applications.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Classic Bluetooth | BLE |
|---|---|---|
| **Modulation** | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | GFSK only |
| **Channel Count** | 79 channels | 40 channels |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | 1 MHz | 2 MHz |
| **Target Applications** | Audio streaming, file transfer, HID | **IoT sensors, wearables, beacons, healthcare** |

> *(1 mark per correctly-filled row, plus 0.5 mark for a concluding one-liner like "BLE is optimized for ultra-low-power, bursty, coin-cell IoT traffic, while Classic BT is optimized for continuous, high-throughput streams.")*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the three BLE advertising channel center frequencies and explain why they were deliberately chosen at the band edges.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The three BLE advertising channels are centered at:

$$
f_{0} = 2402 \text{ MHz}, \quad f_{12} = 2426 \text{ MHz}, \quad f_{39} = 2480 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Why chosen at the band edges?**

The 2.4 GHz ISM band is shared with **Wi-Fi (channels 1–13)**, which occupies roughly **2412 – 2472 MHz** and is the dominant source of interference in home and office environments. By placing advertising channels at **2402, 2426, and 2480 MHz**, Bluetooth SIG ensures that **at least one advertisement channel is always relatively free of Wi-Fi interference**, even when a Wi-Fi access point is co-located.

*(2 marks for the three frequencies, 1 mark for the Wi-Fi interference rationale.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model, Module 3 — Bluetooth & BLE]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **BLE protocol stack architecture**. Clearly differentiate between the **Controller** and **Host** sub-stacks and list any **four** responsibilities of the Link Layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **BLE channel center frequency** expression $f_k = 2402 + 2k$ MHz. Compute the total channel count, identify the advertising channel indices and their corresponding frequencies, and explain the regulatory guard-band rationale. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — BLE Protocol Stack:**

*Refer to Section 4.1 for the diagram.*

**Model Textual Answer:**

The BLE protocol stack is partitioned into two physical sub-systems connected by the **Host Controller Interface (HCI)**:

1. **Controller sub-stack** (typically on a dedicated BLE radio SoC):
   - **Physical Layer (PHY):** Implements **GFSK modulation** with $h = 0.5$, BT = 0.5, $R_s = 1$ Msps (LE 1M).
   - **Link Layer (LL):** Manages radio state, advertising, scanning, connection events, and frequency hopping.
   - **HCI transport:** UART, SPI, or USB for host-controller communication.

2. **Host sub-stack** (typically on the application MCU):
   - **L2CAP:** Segmentation/reassembly, channel multiplexing for ATT and SMP.
   - **ATT:** Client-server attribute database (handles, UUIDs, values, permissions).
   - **GATT:** Logical grouping of attributes into Services, Characteristics, and Descriptors.
   - **GAP:** Defines device roles — *Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central* — and manages advertising modes.
   - **SMP:** Pairing, bonding, and LE Privacy with AES-CCM-128.

**Four Link Layer Responsibilities:**
1. Advertising state: transmits ADV_IND / ADV_DIRECT_IND / ADV_NONCONN_IND / ADV_SCAN_IND PDUs.
2. Scanning state: listens for advertisements and optionally issues SCAN_REQ.
3. Connection state: maintains a **connection event** on a per-interval schedule with adaptive frequency hopping.
4. Channel selection algorithm: maps the hop increment and channel index to a pseudo-random data channel per event.

*(4 marks for diagram, 2 marks for stack layers, 1 mark for Link Layer responsibilities.)*

---

**Solution (b) — Channel Center Frequency Derivation:**

*Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation.*

**Compact model answer for the answer sheet:**

**Step 1:** BLE operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM band: $f \in [2400,\ 2483.5]$ MHz.
**Step 2:** A regular grid of 2-MHz channels is defined, starting 2 MHz above the lower band edge:

$$
f_k = 2402 + 2k \text{ MHz}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 39
$$

**Step 3:** Total channels = $39 - 0 + 1 = \mathbf{40}$.

**Step 4:** Advertising channel indices $\Rightarrow$ RF indices $\mathbf{0, 12, 39}$:

$$
f_{adv,1} = 2402 + 2(0) = 2402 \text{ MHz}
$$
$$
f_{adv,2} = 2402 + 2(12) = 2426 \text{ MHz}
$$
$$
f_{adv,3} = 2402 + 2(39) = 2480 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 5 (Guard-band rationale):** Each channel occupies $f_k \pm 1$ MHz. Channel 39 occupies [2479, 2481] MHz, leaving **2.5 MHz** of upper guard band up to the regulatory limit of 2483.5 MHz. The lower guard band is similarly protected by starting channel 0 at 2402 MHz (with a 2-MHz guard down to 2400 MHz). This mask is mandated by **FCC Part 15** and **ETSI EN 300 328** to prevent out-of-band radiation.

*(2 marks for deriving $f_k$, 2 marks for computing 40 channels + 3 advertising frequencies, 1 mark for advertising-channel identification, 2 marks for regulatory rationale.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Module 3 — Bluetooth & BLE]**

**(a)** Explain the **GATT data model** of Bluetooth Low Energy. With a clear diagram, illustrate the hierarchy of *Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor → Value*, and list **any four** GATT operations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A BLE transmitter operates with $P_t = 8$ dBm, antenna gains $G_t = G_r = 0$ dBi, and receiver sensitivity $P_{rx,min} = -90$ dBm. Estimate the **maximum free-space outdoor range** at $f = 2.45$ GHz. If a **fade margin of 18 dB** is introduced, recompute the practical range. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — GATT Data Model:**

*Refer to Section 4.3 for the hierarchy diagram.*

**Model Textual Answer:**

GATT (Generic Attribute Profile) is the application-layer data-exchange framework in BLE. Its data model has the following hierarchy:

- **Profile:** A pre-defined collection of services that delivers a specific use-case (e.g., *Heart Rate Profile*, *Battery Service Profile*).
- **Service:** A logical grouping of related characteristics, identified by a **16-bit or 128-bit UUID**. Standard services use 16-bit UUIDs (e.g., 0x180D = Heart Rate Service).
- **Characteristic:** The lowest-level data entity — exposes a single measured or controlled value. Each has a **UUID**, a **value** (≤ 512 bytes), and **properties** (Read, Write, Write Without Response, Notify, Indicate, Broadcast).
- **Descriptor:** Meta-data attached to a characteristic (e.g., **CCCD** = UUID 0x2902 enables/disables notifications).
- **Value:** The raw bytes that the application reads or writes.

**Four GATT Operations:**
1. **Read Characteristic Value** — client requests current value from server.
2. **Write Characteristic Value** — client pushes a new value (with ACK).
3. **Notify** — server spontaneously pushes value to subscribed client (no ACK).
4. **Indicate** — server pushes value with mandatory client ACK.

*(3 marks for hierarchy diagram, 2 marks for explanatory text, 2 marks for operations.)*

---

**Solution (b) — Range Estimation:**

**Step 1:** Maximum tolerable path loss:

$$
L_{max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,min}
$$

$$
L_{max} = 8 + 0 + 0 - (-90) = 98 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2:** Wavelength at $f = 2.45$ GHz:

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}} = 0.1224 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Free-space path loss formula:

$$
L_{FSPL}(d) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Equate and solve for $d$:

$$
20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{0.1224}\right) = 98
$$

$$
\frac{4\pi d}{0.1224} = 10^{4.9} = 79432.8
$$

$$
d = \frac{79432.8 \times 0.1224}{4\pi} = \frac{9722.6}{12.566} \approx 773.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5:** Practical range with 18-dB fade margin:

$$
L_{FSPL,\,practical} = 98 - 18 = 80 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d_p}{0.1224}\right) = 80 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{4\pi d_p}{0.1224} = 10^{4} = 10000
$$

$$
d_p = \frac{10000 \times 0.1224}{12.566} = \frac{1224}{12.566} \approx 97.4 \text{ m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Theoretical free-space range ≈ **774 m**; practical outdoor range with an 18-dB fade margin ≈ **97 m**. This figure matches published BLE 5.x outdoor performance characteristics remarkably well.

*(2 marks for $L_{max}$ calculation, 2 marks for $\lambda$ and FSPL setup, 2 marks for the final numerical answer, 1 mark for the fade-margin re-computation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before your exam.**

- ✅ **BLE was introduced in Bluetooth 4.0 (2010)**, originating from Nokia's **Wibree** project (2006).
- ✅ **40 RF channels**, each **2 MHz wide**, indexed $k = 0, 1, \dots, 39$.
- ✅ Center frequency formula: $\mathbf{f_k = 2402 + 2k}$ **MHz**.
- ✅ **Advertising channels** → RF indices **0, 12, 39** → frequencies **2402, 2426, 2480 MHz**.
- ✅ **GFSK** modulation, modulation index $h \in [0.45, 0.55]$, $BT = 0.5$, min deviation **185 kHz**.
- ✅ **LE 1M PHY** = 1 Msps (1 Mbps), **LE 2M PHY** (BT 5.0) = 2 Msps (2 Mbps), **LE Coded PHY** = 500 kbps (S=2) / 125 kbps (S=8) with extended range.
- ✅ **Advertising payload ≤ 31 bytes**, **Scan response ≤ 31 bytes**, **L2CAP SDU ≤ 251 bytes**, **Attribute value ≤ 512 bytes**.
- ✅ **Connection interval** $T_{CI} \in [7.5 \text{ ms},\ 4 \text{ s}]$, step **1.25 ms**.
- ✅ **Slave latency** $L_s \in [0,\ 499]$ events; effective throughput divides by $(L_s + 1)$.
- ✅ **Supervisory timeout** $T_{sup} \in [100 \text{ ms},\ 32 \text{ s}]$, step **10 ms**.
- ✅ **GATT hierarchy:** Profile → Service → Characteristic → Descriptor → Value.
- ✅ **GATT operations:** Read, Write, Write Without Response, Notify, Indicate.
- ✅ **GAP roles:** Broadcaster, Observer, Peripheral, Central.
- ✅ **Pairing methods:** Just Works, Numeric Comparison, Passkey, Out-of-Band (OOB).
- ✅ **Encryption:** AES-CCM-128, 128-bit keys.
- ✅ **Connection setup latency: < 3 ms** (vs. > 100 ms for Classic BT).
- ✅ **Peak current < 15 mA**, **sleep current ~ 1 µA** — enables coin-cell IoT for **months to years**.
- ✅ **BLE is NOT interoperable** with Classic Bluetooth at the radio/link layer — they coexist but cannot directly connect.
- ✅ **Bluetooth Mesh** (BT 5.0+) supports **flooded / managed / friend / low-power-node** topologies for thousands of nodes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 6LoWPAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 6LoWPAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 6LoWPAN: IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks

## 1. Core Technical Definition

**6LoWPAN** (pronounced *six-low-pan*) is an acronym for **IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks**. It is an adaptation layer protocol defined by the IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force) that enables the efficient transmission of **IPv6 packets** over **IEEE 802.15.4** based low-power, low-rate wireless networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (RFC 4944 / RFC 6282):**
> *6LoWPAN is a convergence layer that sits between the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer and the IPv6 network layer, providing header compression, fragmentation, reassembly, and mesh-under routing to enable IPv6 communication on resource-constrained IoT devices.*

The 6LoWPAN working group was chartered in **2005** under the IETF, and the first standard was published as **RFC 4944** (September 2007), later refined by **RFC 6282** (September 2011) for improved header compression.

### Key Standardization Documents
- **RFC 4944** – Transmission of IPv6 Packets over IEEE 802.15.4 Networks
- **RFC 6282** – Compression Format for IPv6 Datagrams in 6LoWPAN Networks
- **RFC 6775** – Neighbor Discovery Optimization for 6LoWPAN
- **RFC 8066** – 6LoWPAN-GHC (Generic Header Compression)

## 2. The Problem 6LoWPAN Solves

| Layer | Constraint | Value |
|---|---|---|
| IEEE 802.15.4 PHY | Maximum Frame Size | **127 bytes** |
| IEEE 802.15.4 MAC | Overhead (headers, FCS) | **25 bytes** (typical) |
| 6LoWPAN | Usable Payload | **~81 bytes** |
| IPv6 | Minimum MTU Requirement | **1280 bytes** |
| 6LoWPAN | Header overhead (no compression) | **40 bytes** (IPv6) + 8 bytes (UDP) = **48 bytes** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Conflict:** IPv6 requires a minimum MTU of 1280 bytes, but IEEE 802.15.4 frames can carry only about 81 bytes of payload. 6LoWPAN bridges this gap through **fragmentation, reassembly, and aggressive header compression**.

## 3. Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Postal Mailbox Analogy:**
> Imagine you want to send a **full encyclopedia set (IPv6 packet, 1280+ bytes)** through a **small apartment mailbox (IEEE 802.15.4 frame, ~81 bytes capacity)**. The mailbox is too small for even one volume.
>
> 6LoWPAN acts like a **smart packaging system**:
> 1. **Header Compression (IPHC)** – Strips away all the redundant addressing information on the envelope (like "Mr. John Smith, Apt 4B, Building 7, Green Street, Mumbai, India") and just writes the essential "Apt 4B". The receiving end knows the rest.
> 2. **Fragmentation** – Breaks the encyclopedia into smaller booklets that physically fit the mailbox, numbering each one for reassembly.
> 3. **Reassembly** – On the receiving end, the booklets are re-bound into the original encyclopedia.
> 4. **Mesh Forwarding** – If your building's mailroom (router) is busy, another mailman (neighbor node) can drop it off on your behalf.

## 4. Why 6LoWPAN Matters for IoT

- Enables **end-to-end IPv6 addressability** for tiny sensors and actuators
- Allows IoT devices to connect to the **public Internet** without translation gateways
- Forms the foundation for higher-layer protocols like **RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)**, **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)**, and **6LoWPAN-ND (Neighbor Discovery)**
- Crucial for **smart homes, smart cities, industrial IoT, agricultural sensing, and healthcare monitoring**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis: 6LoWPAN Architecture

## 1. 6LoWPAN Protocol Stack

The 6LoWPAN stack is layered directly beneath IPv6 and above IEEE 802.15.4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Application Layer} &\longrightarrow \text{CoAP / MQTT / HTTP} \\
\text{Transport Layer} &\longrightarrow \text{UDP / TCP (compressed)} \\
\text{Network Layer} &\longrightarrow \text{IPv6 (128-bit addressing)} \\
\text{Adaptation Layer} &\longrightarrow \textbf{6LoWPAN (Header Compression + Fragmentation)} \\
\text{Data Link Layer} &\longrightarrow \text{IEEE 802.15.4 MAC} \\
\text{Physical Layer} &\longrightarrow \text{IEEE 802.15.4 PHY (868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2. 6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer Functions

The adaptation layer is the heart of 6LoWPAN and performs four critical functions:

### A. Header Compression
- Compresses the **40-byte IPv6 header** and the **8-byte UDP header** down to as little as **6 bytes** combined (using IPHC and NHC)
- Uses stateless compression based on contextual information from link-layer addresses

### B. Fragmentation
- Splits oversized IPv6 packets into smaller **link-layer fragments** that fit the 802.15.4 frame
- Two fragmentation headers defined: **Fragment Header (first)** and **Fragment Header (subsequent)**

### C. Reassembly
- Reconstructs the original IPv6 packet at the destination or at a 6LoWPAN border router

### D. Mesh Addressing / Forwarding
- Supports **mesh-under** routing where multiple 802.15.4 hops occur beneath a single IPv6 hop
- Uses **mesh delivery header** (dispatch value = 10xxxxxx)

## 3. 6LoWPAN Dispatch Values

The first byte of the 6LoWPAN payload is the **dispatch byte** that indicates the header type:

| Dispatch Pattern (Binary) | Header Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| `00xxxxxx` | Mesh Delivery | Mesh addressing forwarding |
| `01000001` | Fragment (First) | First fragment of a packet |
| `11000000` | Fragment (Subsequent) | Following fragments |
| `01100000` | IPHC | IPv6 Header Compression |
| `01010000` | NALP | Not A LoWPAN frame |
| `11110xxx` | NHC | Next Header Compression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IPHC (IPv6 Header Compression) dispatch value is `01100001` (when traffic class & flow label are elided) or `01100000`** — this is the most common header in 6LoWPAN traffic.

## 4. IPHC Header Compression (RFC 6282)

The IPHC encoding is a 13-bit field (bits 0–12) followed by compressed fields. The two IPHC bytes encode:

- **TF (Traffic Class, Flow Label):** 2 bits – 00, 01, 10, 11 (elide/16-bit/8-bit/unspecified)
- **NH (Next Header):** 1 bit – 0 (inline), 1 (compressed using NHC)
- **HLIM (Hop Limit):** 2 bits – 00 (inline), 01 (1), 10 (64), 11 (255)
- **CID (Context Identifier Extension):** 1 bit – whether a context byte follows
- **SAC (Source Address Compression):** 1 bit – stateless or stateful
- **SAM (Source Address Mode):** 2 bits – 00 (inline 128-bit), 01 (64-bit), 10 (16-bit), 11 (0-bit)
- **M (Multicast):** 1 bit
- **DAC (Destination Address Compression):** 1 bit
- **DAM (Destination Address Mode):** 2 bits

## 5. Maximum Achievable Compression

| Header | Original Size | Compressed Size | Savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| IPv6 Header (full) | 40 bytes | 2–7 bytes | ~83–95% |
| UDP Header | 8 bytes | 4 bytes | 50% |
| IPv6 + UDP (Combined) | 48 bytes | 6–11 bytes | ~77–88% |

## 6. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas for 6LoWPAN Calculations**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $M_{\text{payload}} = M_{\text{frame}} - M_{\text{MAC}} - M_{\text{FCS}}$ | Maximum MAC payload | bytes |
| $M_{\text{IPv6\_required}} = 1280$ bytes | IPv6 minimum MTU | bytes |
| $N_{\text{frags}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{M_{\text{IPv6}}}{M_{\text{6LoWPAN\_payload}}} \right\rceil$ | Number of 802.15.4 fragments needed | fragments |
| $E_{\text{comp}} = \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{\text{comp}}}{M_{\text{orig}}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Header compression efficiency | % |
| $L_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{orig}}}{M_{\text{comp}}}$ | Length reduction ratio | dimensionless |
| $T_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{frame}} \times 8}{R_{\text{data}}}$ | Time on air | seconds |
| $R_{\text{useful}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{app}}}{M_{\text{frame}}} \times 100\%$ | Application data ratio | % |

Where:
- $M_{\text{frame}} = 127$ bytes (IEEE 802.15.4 max)
- $M_{\text{FCS}} = 2$ bytes (Frame Check Sequence)
- $M_{\text{MAC\_overhead}} = 25$ bytes (MAC header + FCS typically)
- $R_{\text{data}} = 250$ kbps (O-QPSK at 2.4 GHz)

## 7. Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Metering:** Enables IPv6 connectivity to millions of electricity meters (e.g., smart grid deployments)
- **Building Automation:** BACnet over 6LoWPAN for HVAC and lighting
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil sensors reporting via IPv6 to cloud dashboards
- **Asset Tracking:** BLE-6LoWPAN hybrid tags in logistics
- **Industrial WSN:** Modbus/TCP encapsulated over 6LoWPAN in Industry 4.0

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation: Maximum 6LoWPAN Payload Size

The IEEE 802.15.4 standard defines a maximum physical layer frame size. We derive the effective 6LoWPAN payload available for IPv6 transmission.

**Step 1:** Identify the maximum PHY frame size.

$$
M_{\text{phy}} = 127 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the MAC layer Frame Check Sequence (FCS) which is a 16-bit CRC.

$$
M_{\text{mac}} = M_{\text{phy}} - M_{\text{FCS}} = 127 - 2 = 125 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the MAC header overhead (frame control, sequence number, PAN IDs, addresses).

$$
M_{\text{payload}} = M_{\text{mac}} - M_{\text{MAC\_header}} = 125 - 25 = 100 \text{ bytes}
$$

> *Note: With short 16-bit addressing and no security, MAC header = 9 bytes; with full 64-bit addressing and AES-CCM-128 security, MAC header = 31 bytes. The value 25 bytes is the KTU-referenced typical case.*

**Step 4:** Subtract the 6LoWPAN dispatch + IPHC header (worst case 7 bytes).

$$
M_{\text{6LoWPAN\_max}} = 100 - 7 = 93 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5:** Subtract the compressed UDP header (4 bytes, NHC).

$$
M_{\text{app\_max}} = 93 - 4 = 89 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Result:** A single unfragmented 6LoWPAN frame can carry at most **~81–89 bytes** of application data (commonly cited as **81 bytes** in the KTU syllabus).

## 2. Derivation: Fragmentation Calculation

**Problem:** A 6LoWPAN node needs to send an IPv6 packet of size 1280 bytes. With a maximum 6LoWPAN payload of 81 bytes, how many fragments are required?

**Step 1:** Each fragment carries a 5-byte **6LoWPAN Fragment Header** (dispatch 1 byte + data offset 2 bytes + data tag 2 bytes) plus 1 byte for the 6LoWPAN IPHC dispatch.

$$
M_{\text{frag\_header}} = 5 \text{ bytes (subsequent) or 4 bytes (first)}
$$

**Step 2:** For the first fragment, available application data per fragment:

$$
M_{\text{first\_app}} = 81 - 7 \text{ (IPHC + IPHC dispatch)} - 4 \text{ (UDP NHC)} = 70 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** For subsequent fragments, the IPHC header is shared with the first fragment, so each subsequent fragment carries:

$$
M_{\text{sub\_app}} = 81 - 5 \text{ (Fragment Header)} = 76 \text{ bytes}
$$

> *Note: The IPHC and NHC headers are not repeated in subsequent fragments.*

**Step 4:** Total application payload over $N$ fragments:

$$
M_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{first\_app}} + (N-1) \times M_{\text{sub\_app}}
$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $N$ when $M_{\text{total}} = 1280$ bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1280 &= 70 + (N-1) \times 76 \\
1210 &= (N-1) \times 76 \\
N - 1 &= 15.92 \\
N &= 16.92 \\
N &= \lceil 16.92 \rceil = 17 \text{ fragments}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A 1280-byte IPv6 packet requires **17 802.15.4 fragments** to be transmitted completely.

## 3. Derivation: Header Compression Efficiency

**Problem:** Compute the compression efficiency when an IPv6 + UDP header (48 bytes) is compressed to 7 bytes (IPHC + NHC).

**Step 1:** Identify original size.

$$
M_{\text{orig}} = 48 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify compressed size.

$$
M_{\text{comp}} = 7 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the compression efficiency formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{comp}} &= \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{\text{comp}}}{M_{\text{orig}}}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= \left(1 - \dfrac{7}{48}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= (1 - 0.1458) \times 100\% \\
&= 85.42\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The header compression achieves an **85.42% reduction** in header overhead, freeing 41 bytes per packet for application data.

## 4. Derivation: Time on Air for a Single Frame

**Problem:** Calculate the time on air for transmitting one 127-byte 802.15.4 frame at 250 kbps.

**Step 1:** Compute total bits transmitted.

$$
B_{\text{frame}} = M_{\text{frame}} \times 8 = 127 \times 8 = 1016 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the time on air formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{air}} &= \dfrac{B_{\text{frame}}}{R_{\text{data}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1016 \text{ bits}}{250{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}} \\
&= 4.064 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&= 4.064 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A single maximum-size 802.15.4 frame takes **4.064 ms** on the air at 250 kbps.

## 5. Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | IPv6 over Ethernet | IPv6 over 6LoWPAN | Wi-Fi (802.11) |
|---|---|---|---|
| MTU | 1500 bytes | **81–93 bytes (single hop)** | 1500 bytes |
| Fragmentation at L2 | No | **Yes (mandatory)** | No |
| Header Compression | No (or ROHC) | **Yes (IPHC + NHC)** | No |
