### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

MQTT is an **application-layer** messaging protocol built on top of TCP/IP that decouples the **publisher** (message producer) from the **subscriber** (message consumer) through a central mediating server known as the **MQTT Broker**. Communication is asynchronous, event-driven, and supports bi-directional messaging with minimal header overhead (as low as **2 bytes** for the fixed header).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key MQTT Design Principles (KTU Board Focus):**
> 1. **Lightweight footprint** — Suitable for microcontrollers with limited RAM/ROM.
> 2. **Publish/Subscribe pattern** — Decouples producers from consumers.
> 3. **Three Quality of Service (QoS) levels** — Guarantees message delivery semantics.
> 4. **Topic-based filtering** — Hierarchical subject addressing using `/` separator.
> 5. **Asynchronous communication** — No tight coupling between clients.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Newspaper Delivery System"

Imagine a **community newspaper system**:

| Component | Real-World Analogue | MQTT Equivalent |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Newspaper Printing Press | Reporter / Sensor | **Publisher** |
| Central Newsstand | Distribution Hub | **MQTT Broker** |
| Newspaper Sections (Sports, Politics) | Headlines | **Topics** |
| Subscribed Reader | Customer | **Subscriber** |
| Home Delivery Van | Topic Subscription | **Push Notification** |

In this analogy:
- A **publisher** (sensor) prints a news article under a specific **topic** (e.g., *home/livingroom/temperature*).
- The **broker** (central newsstand) receives all articles and routes them to every **subscriber** who has registered interest in that topic.
- The reader (subscriber) does **not** need to know which printing press sent the news — only which topic to follow.
- This **decoupling** is the fundamental elegance of MQTT: publishers and subscribers never communicate directly.

### 1.3 Why MQTT is the Backbone of IoT

Traditional **HTTP Request/Response** requires the client to constantly poll the server ("Are there any updates?"). This wastes bandwidth and battery. MQTT reverses this — the broker **pushes** messages to subscribers the moment they arrive, conserving both **bandwidth** and **energy**.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics for KTU:**
> - Default non-encrypted port: **1883**
> - Default TLS-encrypted port: **8883**
> - Minimum header size: **2 bytes**
> - Standardized by: **OASIS (2014)**, **ISO/IEC 20922 (2016)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Architecture Components

The MQTT ecosystem consists of **three logical entities**:

1. **MQTT Client** — Any device or application that runs the MQTT library and connects to the broker. A client can simultaneously be a **Publisher**, a **Subscriber**, or both.
2. **MQTT Broker (Server)** — The heart of the system. It receives all messages from publishers, filters them by topic, and dispatches them to subscribed clients. Examples: *Mosquitto*, *HiveMQ*, *EMQX*.
3. **MQTT Connection** — A persistent TCP/IP connection between client and broker, identified by a unique **Client ID**.

### 2.2 The Publish/Subscribe Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Client connects** to the broker via a `CONNECT` packet carrying Client ID, credentials, Keep Alive timer, and clean session flag.
2. **Broker acknowledges** with a `CONNACK` packet indicating session status.
3. **Subscriber registers interest** by sending a `SUBSCRIBE` packet for one or more topics with a chosen QoS level.
4. **Broker confirms** with a `SUBACK` packet returning the granted QoS for each topic.
5. **Publisher sends** a `PUBLISH` packet to the broker containing the topic name and payload.
6. **Broker routes** the message to **every subscriber** whose topic filter matches the published topic.
7. **Acknowledgement** is exchanged depending on the QoS level (see Section 3).
8. **Heartbeat** is maintained using `PINGREQ` / `PINGRESP` packets at intervals defined by the Keep Alive timer.
9. **Clean disconnect** is performed using the `DISCONNECT` packet.

### 2.3 Topic Hierarchy & Wildcards

Topics are **UTF-8 strings** organized hierarchically using the forward slash `/` as a level separator. This resembles a file-system path.

**Example Topic Tree:**
- `home/livingroom/temperature`
- `home/livingroom/humidity`
- `home/bedroom/temperature`

**Wildcards (used in SUBSCRIBE only):**

| Wildcard | Meaning | Example Filter | Matches |
|----------|---------|---------------|---------|
| `+` | Single-level match | `home/+/temperature` | `home/livingroom/temperature`, `home/bedroom/temperature` |
| `#` | Multi-level match (must be last) | `home/#` | All topics beginning with `home/` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** The `#` wildcard MUST be the last character in the topic filter and MUST be preceded by `/`. Writing `#` alone or `home#` is invalid.

### 2.4 Quality of Service (QoS) Levels

MQTT defines **three QoS levels** that govern the reliability of message delivery between publisher ↔ broker ↔ subscriber:

| QoS Level | Name | Guarantee | Mechanism | Packets Exchanged |
|-----------|------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget | No acknowledgement | 1 (`PUBLISH`) |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Possible duplicates | `PUBACK` handshake | 2 (`PUBLISH`, `PUBACK`) |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Single delivery | 4-step handshake | 4 (`PUBLISH`, `PUBREC`, `PUBREL`, `PUBCOMP`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The QoS for a `PUBLISH` message from publisher → broker and from broker → subscriber are **independent**. The broker can downgrade or upgrade QoS based on subscriber capabilities.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Remarks |
|---------|----------------|-------|---------|
| Fixed Header Minimum Size | $1 + 1 = 2$ | Bytes | Control byte + 0 remaining length |
| Fixed Header Maximum Size | $1 + 4 = 5$ | Bytes | Variable-length remaining length encoding |
| Topic Length Field | $2$ | Bytes | MSB + LSB of UTF-8 string length |
| QoS 0 Round Trips | $1$ | Trip | One-way transmission |
| QoS 1 Round Trips | $2$ | Trips | PUBLISH plus PUBACK |
| QoS 2 Round Trips | $3$ | Trips | PUBLISH, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP |
| Keep Alive Timer Range | $0 \le T_{keep} \le 65535$ | Seconds | $T_{keep} = 0$ disables keep alive |
| Keep Alive Multiplier | $1.5 \times T_{keep}$ | Seconds | Broker timeout threshold |
| Packet Identifier Range | $0x0001$ to $0xFFFF$ | Hex | $0x0000$ reserved/invalid |
| Max Topic Length | $65535$ | Bytes | Per MQTT specification |
| Max Payload Size | $\approx 256 \text{ MB}$ | Megabytes | Constrained by remaining length field |
| Default Port (Plain) | $1883$ | TCP Port | Non-encrypted |
| Default Port (TLS) | $8883$ | TCP Port | Encrypted with TLS |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

MQTT has become the **de-facto protocol** for:
- **Smart Home Automation** (e.g., AWS IoT, Google Cloud IoT Core).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** for SCADA systems and predictive maintenance.
- **Connected Vehicles** for telemetry, fleet management, and OTA updates.
- **Healthcare IoT** for remote patient monitoring with low-power wearables.
- **Smart Agriculture** for soil moisture, weather, and irrigation control.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain** for asset tracking across global supply chains.

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Efficiency Insight:** Studies show MQTT consumes up to **10× less power** than HTTP polling for equivalent messaging workloads, making it ideal for battery-operated IoT nodes running on **LoRaWAN**, **NB-IoT**, or **Zigbee** backhauls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of MQTT Control Packet Structure

Every MQTT control packet consists of **three components**:

$$
\text{MQTT Packet} = \underbrace{F}_{\text{Fixed Header}} \;+\; \underbrace{V}_{\text{Variable Header}} \;+\; \underbrace{P}_{\text{Payload}}
$$

#### Step 1: Fixed Header Construction

The Fixed Header is **always present** and occupies exactly **2 to 5 bytes**.

**Byte 1 — Control Packet Type + Flags:**

$$
\text{Byte}_1 = \underbrace{TTTT}_{\text{4 bits (Type)}} \;+\; \underbrace{FFFF}_{\text{4 bits (Flags)}}
$$

The first 4 bits denote the **Control Packet Type**. The upper 4 bits denote packet-specific flags (e.g., DUP, QoS, RETAIN for PUBLISH).

**Bytes 2 to 5 — Remaining Length:**

The Remaining Length is a **Variable Byte Integer** representing the number of bytes remaining in the packet (Variable Header + Payload), excluding the encoding itself.

$$
\text{Remaining Length} = \vert V \vert + \vert P \vert
$$

**Encoding rule:** Each byte uses the lower 7 bits for data and the MSB as a continuation bit.

$$
\text{Byte}_i = \begin{cases} (X_{7i} \mod 128) + 128 & \text{if more bytes follow} \\ X_{7i} \mod 128 & \text{if last byte} \end{cases}
$$

where $X$ is the Remaining Length value and $i$ is the byte index starting at 0.

**Conversion Logic:** When reading, you continue accumulating 7-bit chunks until you encounter a byte with MSB = 0.

#### Step 2: Variable Header Composition

The Variable Header content depends on the **Control Packet Type**:

| Packet Type | Variable Header Contents |
|-------------|-------------------------|
| CONNECT | Protocol Name, Protocol Level, Connect Flags, Keep Alive |
| CONNACK | Session Present Flag, Return Code |
| PUBLISH | Topic Name, Packet Identifier (only if QoS > 0) |
| PUBACK | Packet Identifier |
| SUBSCRIBE | Packet Identifier, Property Length (v5) |

#### Step 3: Payload Composition

The Payload is **optional** and its content depends entirely on the packet type:
- **CONNECT** → Client ID, Will Topic, Will Message, Username, Password
- **PUBLISH** → Application-defined message bytes (binary or text)
- **SUBSCRIBE** → List of Topic Filters and QoS bytes

---

### 3.2 Derivation of QoS 2 Four-Step Handshake

The QoS 2 handshake guarantees **exactly-once delivery** through a four-packet exchange. Let us derive the state transitions:

**State Variables:**
- Let $M$ denote the message to be delivered.
- Let $PID$ denote the unique 16-bit Packet Identifier assigned to $M$.

**Step-by-Step Transition:**

**Step 1 — Publisher sends `PUBLISH` with QoS=2 and DUP=0:**

$$
\text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH}(PID, M, QoS=2)} \text{Broker}
$$

The publisher stores $M$ with state $S = \text{await PUBREC}$.

**Step 2 — Broker acknowledges receipt with `PUBREC`:**

$$
\text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBREC}(PID)} \text{Publisher}
$$

The broker stores $M$ with state $S = \text{await PUBREL}$.

**Step 3 — Publisher releases the message with `PUBREL`:**

$$
\text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBREL}(PID)} \text{Broker}
$$

The publisher state transitions to $S = \text{await PUBCOMP}$.

**Step 4 — Broker confirms release with `PUBCOMP`:**

$$
\text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBCOMP}(PID)} \text{Publisher}
$$

The publisher discards $M$. Delivery is complete and **deduplication is guaranteed**.

$$
\boxed{\text{QoS 2} \Rightarrow \text{Exactly once delivery via 4-packet handshake}}
$$

**Packet Identifier Reuse Rule:** The same $PID$ cannot be reused until the handshake completes. This prevents stale acknowledgements from corrupting new messages.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of QoS Levels

| Parameter | QoS 0 | QoS 1 | QoS 2 |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
| **Delivery Guarantee** | At most once | At least once | Exactly once |
| **Packet Count** | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| **Round Trips** | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| **Duplicate Risk** | None | Possible | Eliminated |
| **Bandwidth Overhead** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Latency** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Storage Required** | None | Sender stores msg | Both sides store msg |
| **Use Case** | Sensor telemetry | Reliable commands | Billing, billing-critical |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Trivial | Low | Moderate (state machine) |

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Keep Alive Timeout

The MQTT specification mandates that the broker disconnects any client that does not send a control packet within **1.5 times the Keep Alive interval**.

$$
T_{\text{timeout}} = 1.5 \times T_{\text{keep alive}}
$$

**Example Calculation:** If a client specifies $T_{\text{keep alive}} = 60$ seconds, the broker will terminate the connection after:

$$
T_{\text{timeout}} = 1.5 \times 60 = 90 \text{ seconds of inactivity}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The client must send either a `PINGREQ` packet or any other control packet before $T_{\text{timeout}}$ elapses to remain connected. The broker responds with `PINGRESP`.

---

### 3.5 MQTT Control Packet Type Reference Table

| Packet Type | Hex Value (Lo) | Direction | Description |
|-------------|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| Reserved | 0x0 | — | Forbidden |
| CONNECT | 0x1 | Client → Broker | Request connection |
| CONNACK | 0x2 | Broker → Client | Connection acknowledgement |
| PUBLISH | 0x3 | Bidirectional | Publish message |
| PUBACK | 0x4 | Bidirectional | QoS 1 acknowledgement |
| PUBREC | 0x5 | Bidirectional | QoS 2 received (step 1) |
| PUBREL | 0x6 | Bidirectional | QoS 2 release (step 2) |
| PUBCOMP | 0x7 | Bidirectional | QoS 2 complete (step 3) |
| SUBSCRIBE | 0x8 | Client → Broker | Subscribe to topic |
| SUBACK | 0x9 | Broker → Client | Subscription acknowledgement |
| UNSUBSCRIBE | 0xA | Client → Broker | Unsubscribe from topic |
| UNSUBACK | 0xB | Broker → Client | Unsubscribe acknowledgement |
| PINGREQ | 0xC | Client → Broker | Ping request |
| PINGRESP | 0xD | Broker → Client | Ping response |
| DISCONNECT | 0xE | Client → Broker | Clean disconnect |
| Reserved | 0xF | — | Forbidden |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    pub1["PUBLISHER A SENSOR NODE"]
    pub2["PUBLISHER B GATEWAY DEVICE"]
    pub3["PUBLISHER C SMART METER"]
    sub1["SUBSCRIBER MOBILE APPLICATION"]
    sub2["SUBSCRIBER ANALYTICS DASHBOARD"]
    sub3["SUBSCRIBER ACTUATOR MODULE"]
    broker["MQTT BROKER MOSQUITTO HIVE EMQX"]

    pub1 -->|PUBLISH TOPIC HOME TEMP| broker
    pub2 -->|PUBLISH TOPIC HOME HUMIDITY| broker
    pub3 -->|PUBLISH TOPIC FACTORY POWER| broker
    broker -->|PUSH TEMP UPDATES| sub1
    broker -->|PUSH HUMIDITY UPDATES| sub2
    broker -->|PUSH POWER COMMANDS| sub3
```

> **Interpretation:** Note the **complete decoupling**. The publishers and subscribers have no direct knowledge of each other; the broker handles all routing based on topic filters.

---

### 4.2 MQTT Topic Hierarchy with Wildcards

```mermaid
graph TB
    root["ROOT HOME"]
    lr["LIVING ROOM"]
    br["BEDROOM"]
    kt["KITCHEN"]
    lrT["TEMPERATURE"]
    lrL["LIGHT"]
    brT["TEMPERATURE"]
    brH["HUMIDITY"]
    ktT["TEMPERATURE"]

    root --> lr
    root --> br
    root --> kt
    lr --> lrT
    lr --> lrL
    br --> brT
    br --> brH
    kt --> ktT
```

**Wildcard Examples Applied to This Tree:**
- `home/+/temperature` matches `home/livingroom/temperature`, `home/bedroom/temperature`, `home/kitchen/temperature`.
- `home/#` matches **every topic** under `home/`.

---

### 4.3 QoS Level Flow Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    q0Start["PUBLISHER"] --> q0Pub["SEND PUBLISH ONLY"]
    q0Pub --> q0Done["DELIVERY COMPLETE NO ACK"]

    q1Start["PUBLISHER"] --> q1Pub["SEND PUBLISH"]
    q1Pub --> q1Ack["RECEIVE PUBACK"]
    q1Ack --> q1Done["DELIVERY ACKNOWLEDGED MAY DUPLICATE"]

    q2Start["PUBLISHER"] --> q2Pub["SEND PUBLISH DUP ZERO"]
    q2Pub --> q2Rec["RECEIVE PUBREC"]
    q2Rec --> q2Rel["SEND PUBREL"]
    q2Rel --> q2Comp["RECEIVE PUBCOMP"]
    q2Comp --> q2Done["EXACTLY ONCE DELIVERY GUARANTEED"]
```

---

### 4.4 CONNECT / CONNACK Handshake Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    clientA["MQTT CLIENT PUBLISHER SUBSCRIBER"]
    brokerA["MQTT BROKER SERVER"]

    clientA -->|STEP 1 CONNECT CLIENT ID KEEP ALIVE| brokerA
    brokerA -->|STEP 2 CONNACK RETURN CODE SESSION FLAG| clientA
    clientA -->|STEP 3 SUBSCRIBE OR PUBLISH| brokerA
    brokerA -->|STEP 4 PUSH MESSAGE OR SUBACK| clientA
    clientA -->|STEP 5 PINGREQ HEARTBEAT| brokerA
    brokerA -->|STEP 6 PINGRESP HEARTBEAT ACK| clientA
    clientA -->|STEP 7 DISCONNECT CLEAN SHUTDOWN| brokerA
```

---

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MQTT Broker

```mermaid
graph TB
    inT["INPUT TCP SOCKET LISTENER PORT 1883"]
    auth["AUTHENTICATION MODULE USERNAME PASSWORD TLS"]
    parse["PACKET PARSER FIXED VARIABLE HEADER"]
    topicDB["TOPIC TREE SUBSCRIPTION DATABASE"]
    sessionDB["SESSION STORE CLIENT ID TO STATE MAP"]
    qosM["QOS ENGINE STATE MACHINE QOS 0 1 2"]
    dispatch["MESSAGE DISPATCHER ROUTER"]
    outS["OUTPUT TCP SOCKET WRITER TO CLIENTS"]
    retainDB["RETAINED MESSAGE STORE"]

    inT --> auth
    auth --> parse
    parse --> qosM
    qosM --> sessionDB
    qosM --> topicDB
    topicDB --> dispatch
    dispatch --> retainDB
    dispatch --> outS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define MQTT. List any four salient features of MQTT that make it suitable for IoT applications.**

**Model Answer:**

MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) is an OASIS-standardized, lightweight **publish/subscribe** messaging protocol designed for constrained devices operating over unreliable, low-bandwidth networks. It operates at the application layer over TCP/IP.

**Four Salient Features:**

1. **Lightweight Footprint:** The minimum packet header is only 2 bytes, making it ideal for resource-constrained devices.
2. **Publish/Subscribe Model:** Decouples message producers from consumers, allowing scalable fan-out architecture.
3. **Three QoS Levels:** Provides flexible delivery guarantees — at most once, at least once, exactly once.
4. **Asynchronous Communication:** Clients do not need to poll; the broker pushes messages when they arrive.
5. **Topic-Based Filtering:** Hierarchical topic structure with wildcards (`+`, `#`) enables flexible subscription.
6. **Persistent Sessions & Last Will:** Supports offline message buffering and disconnect notifications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT with their packet exchange sequences.**

**Model Answer:**

| QoS Level | Name | Packet Sequence | Guarantee |
|-----------|------|-----------------|-----------|
| QoS 0 | At most once | `PUBLISH` | Fire-and-forget, no acknowledgement |
| QoS 1 | At least once | `PUBLISH` → `PUBACK` | Acknowledged delivery; duplicates possible |
| QoS 2 | Exactly once | `PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP` | Four-step handshake ensures single delivery |

QoS 0 has the lowest overhead but no delivery guarantee. QoS 1 ensures delivery at the cost of possible duplicates. QoS 2 uses a four-packet handshake to guarantee exactly-once delivery through a state machine, eliminating duplicates at the cost of higher latency and bandwidth.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

## ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the MQTT architecture with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the broker and the publish/subscribe mechanism. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**MQTT Architecture:**

The MQTT architecture follows the **publish/subscribe** paradigm, mediated by a central **broker**. It consists of three main entities: **Publishers**, **Subscribers**, and the **Broker**.

**Role of the Broker:**
- The broker is the central server responsible for receiving all messages, filtering them by topic, and dispatching them to interested subscribers.
- It maintains a registry of all active subscriptions indexed by topic.
- It handles authentication, authorization, and session management.
- It implements the QoS handshake state machines.
- It stores **retained messages** and **Last Will and Testament (LWT)** notifications.
- Examples: Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX.

**Publish/Subscribe Mechanism — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Connection Establishment:** The client sends a `CONNECT` packet with its Client ID, Keep Alive timer, and credentials. The broker replies with `CONNACK`.
2. **Subscription:** A subscriber sends `SUBSCRIBE` with one or more topic filters and a requested QoS. The broker responds with `SUBACK`.
3. **Publishing:** A publisher sends a `PUBLISH` packet containing the topic name, payload, and QoS level.
4. **Routing:** The broker matches the published topic against all subscribed topic filters using wildcard matching.
5. **Delivery:** The broker pushes the message to each matching subscriber, potentially with an independent QoS level.
6. **Heartbeat:** Clients send `PINGREQ` at intervals equal to Keep Alive; broker replies `PINGRESP`.
7. **Disconnection:** Clients send `DISCONNECT` for clean shutdown.

**Architectural Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    pa["PUBLISHER SENSOR"]
    pb["PUBLISHER ACTUATOR"]
    sa["SUBSCRIBER MOBILE APP"]
    sb["SUBSCRIBER DATABASE"]
    br["MQTT BROKER MOSQUITTO"]

    pa -->|PUBLISH TOPIC SENSOR DATA| br
    pb -->|PUBLISH TOPIC CONTROL CMD| br
    br -->|PUSH TO MOBILE| sa
    br -->|PUSH TO DATABASE| sb
```

---

### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the MQTT control packet format with a neat sketch. List any six control packet types with their functions. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**MQTT Control Packet Format:**

Every MQTT packet consists of three parts: **Fixed Header**, **Variable Header**, and **Payload**.

**Structure Diagram:**

$$
\boxed{
\text{Fixed Header (2-5 B)} \;||\; \text{Variable Header (0-N B)} \;||\; \text{Payload (0-N B)}
}
$$

**Fixed Header (2 to 5 bytes):**

- **Byte 1:** Contains the Control Packet Type (upper 4 bits) and packet-specific flags (lower 4 bits). For PUBLISH, these flags are DUP, QoS (2 bits), and RETAIN.
- **Bytes 2–5:** Contains the **Remaining Length**, a Variable Byte Integer encoding the size of (Variable Header + Payload).

**Variable Header:** Packet-type-specific fields such as Packet Identifier, Topic Name, or Keep Alive timer.

**Payload:** Optional application data (e.g., message body for PUBLISH, topic filters for SUBSCRIBE).

**Six Major Control Packet Types:**

| Type | Value (Hex) | Function |
|------|-------------|----------|
| CONNECT | 0x1 | Initiates client-to-broker connection with credentials. |
| CONNACK | 0x2 | Broker acknowledges connection with return code. |
| PUBLISH | 0x3 | Carries the application message from publisher to broker or vice versa. |
| SUBSCRIBE | 0x8 | Client requests to receive messages on one or more topics. |
| SUBACK | 0x9 | Broker acknowledges subscription with granted QoS. |
| DISCONNECT | 0xE | Client cleanly closes the connection. |

---

## ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Quality of Service levels in MQTT with message flow diagrams. Compare the overhead and reliability of each. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**QoS Level 0 — At Most Once:**

- Single `PUBLISH` packet is sent with no acknowledgement.
- Fire-and-forget semantics; the message may be lost.
- Lowest latency and bandwidth overhead.
- **Use Case:** Frequent sensor telemetry where occasional loss is acceptable (e.g., temperature monitoring).

**Flow:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    p0["PUBLISHER"] -->|PUBLISH| b0["BROKER"]
```

**QoS Level 1 — At Least Once:**

- `PUBLISH` is sent; the receiver replies with `PUBACK`.
- Guarantees delivery but duplicates may occur if the `PUBACK` is lost and the publisher retries.
- Requires the sender to store the message until acknowledgement.
- **Use Case:** Reliable command delivery where duplicates can be tolerated.

**Flow:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1["PUBLISHER"] -->|PUBLISH| b1["BROKER"]
    b1 -->|PUBACK| p1
```

**QoS Level 2 — Exactly Once:**

- Four-step handshake: `PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP`.
- Uses a state machine on both sender and receiver to guarantee **single, unique delivery**.
- Highest overhead and latency but eliminates all duplicates.
- **Use Case:** Billing systems, financial transactions, or critical actuator commands.

**Flow:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    p2["PUBLISHER"] -->|PUBLISH| b2["BROKER"]
    b2 -->|PUBREC| p2
    p2 -->|PUBREL| b2
    b2 -->|PUBCOMP| p2
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | QoS 0 | QoS 1 | QoS 2 |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
| Packets | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Round Trips | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| Duplicate Risk | No | Yes | No |
| Storage Cost | None | Sender only | Both sides |
| Latency | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

---

### (b) [7 Marks] Compare MQTT and HTTP. State the situations where each is preferable. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MQTT | HTTP |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (Broker-mediated) | Request/Response (Client-Server) |
| **Direction** | Bi-directional, push-based | Uni-directional, pull-based |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes minimum | Hundreds of bytes (headers + cookies) |
| **Transport** | TCP (typically), port 1883/8883 | TCP, port 80/443 |
| **Communication** | Asynchronous, event-driven | Synchronous, polling |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (suitable for battery devices) | High (constant polling drains battery) |
| **Message Size** | Small payloads (kilobytes) | Larger payloads possible |
| **Security** | TLS, username/password, ACL | TLS, OAuth, JWT, cookies |
| **State Management** | Persistent sessions, QoS, retained messages | Stateless by design |
| **Best For** | Real-time telemetry, constrained devices | Web browsers, REST APIs, large file transfers |

**When to Prefer MQTT:**
- Real-time IoT sensor data (temperature, humidity, location).
- Battery-powered devices that cannot poll frequently.
- One-to-many broadcast scenarios (firmware updates, alerts).
- Mobile applications with intermittent connectivity.

**When to Prefer HTTP:**
- Web-based user interfaces and REST APIs.
- File uploads/downloads.
- Scenarios requiring caching, cookies, or standard browser tooling.
- Server-to-server microservices communication.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing QoS levels with TCP reliability** — TCP guarantees transport-level delivery; MQTT QoS guarantees **application-level** delivery semantics.
> 2. **Forgetting that QoS is per-hop** — Publisher → broker and broker → subscriber are independent QoS negotiations.
> 3. **Incorrect wildcard placement** — `#` must be the last character and must follow `/`. Writing `home#` or `home/#/temp` is invalid.
> 4. **Forgetting the Variable Byte Integer encoding** — Remaining Length uses a variable-length scheme, not a fixed 4-byte integer.
> 5. **Misstating the keep alive formula** — The timeout is $1.5 \times T_{\text{keep alive}}$, not $2 \times T_{\text{keep alive}}$.
> 6. **Confusing PUBLISH with SUBACK packet IDs** — Packet IDs are mandatory for QoS > 0 PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, UNSUBSCRIBE.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **MQTT** = Message Queuing Telemetry Transport, ISO/IEC 20922, OASIS standard.
- ✅ **Default ports:** **1883** (plain), **8883** (TLS).
- ✅ **Minimum header size:** **2 bytes**.
- ✅ **Three components:** Publisher, Subscriber, Broker.
- ✅ **Pattern:** Publish/Subscribe (asynchronous, decoupled).
- ✅ **Three QoS levels:**
  - QoS 0 → At most once → 1 packet (`PUBLISH`).
  - QoS 1 → At least once → 2 packets (`PUBLISH`, `PUBACK`).
  - QoS 2 → Exactly once → 4 packets (`PUBLISH`, `PUBREC`, `PUBREL`, `PUBCOMP`).
- ✅ **Topic wildcards:** `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level, must be last).
- ✅ **Keep Alive timeout formula:** $T_{\text{timeout}} = 1.5 \times T_{\text{keep alive}}$.
- ✅ **Packet Identifier range:** $0x0001$ to $0xFFFF$ (avoid $0x0000$).
- ✅ **CONNECT** packet initiates session; **CONNACK** acknowledges it.
- ✅ **PINGREQ / PINGRESP** maintain the heartbeat.
- ✅ **DISCONNECT** ensures clean shutdown.
- ✅ **Last Will and Testament (LWT):** Message broadcast by broker if a client disconnects ungracefully.
- ✅ **Retained messages:** Last published message stored by broker and immediately delivered to new subscribers.
- ✅ **Persistent sessions:** Broker stores subscription info and queued messages for offline clients.
- ✅ **MQTT vs HTTP:** MQTT is push-based and lightweight; HTTP is pull-based and verbose.
- ✅ **Use MQTT** for real-time, constrained, battery-powered IoT devices.
- ✅ **Packet IDs are reused** only after handshake completes — critical for QoS 1 and QoS 2.
- ✅ **Brokers handle QoS downgrade** if subscriber requests lower QoS than publisher.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HTTP/HTTPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HTTP/HTTPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of HTTP

**HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)** is an *application-layer* request-response protocol used in the **TCP/IP** communication model. It is the foundational data communication standard for the **World Wide Web (WWW)**, originally defined in **RFC 2616 (HTTP/1.1)** and currently standardized under **RFC 9110 (HTTP Semantics)** and **RFC 9112 (HTTP/1.1)**.

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, HTTP serves as one of the primary **synchronous communication protocols** that enables IoT devices (clients) to exchange data with cloud servers, web services, and APIs (Application Programming Interfaces).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** HTTP operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of the **OSI model** and relies on **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** at the Transport Layer, typically using **Port 80** for unencrypted communication.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of HTTPS

**HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure)** is the encrypted extension of HTTP. It layers HTTP on top of **TLS (Transport Layer Security)**, the successor of the deprecated **SSL (Secure Sockets Layer)** protocol. HTTPS operates on **Port 443** by default and guarantees three security properties: **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, and **Authentication**.

The current TLS standards are **TLS 1.2 (RFC 5246)** and **TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446)**. **TLS 1.3** is the modern recommended version, reducing the handshake to just **1 round-trip (0-RTT in resumption mode)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**

Think of HTTP as **sending a postcard through a public postal service**:
- ✉️ Anyone handling the postcard (postal workers, sorting centers) can **read the message** because it is in plain text.
- ✉️ There is **no proof** that the postcard actually came from the person whose name is signed at the bottom.
- ✉️ If someone intercepts it, they can even **tamper with the message** before it reaches its destination.

Now think of **HTTPS as sending a sealed, tamper-proof letter via a certified courier**:
- 🔒 The letter is inside a **locked, opaque box** — nobody can read it.
- 🔐 The box has a **unique wax seal** — if the seal is broken, the receiver knows it was tampered with.
- 🪪 The courier verifies the **identity** of the sender using a government-issued certificate.

In IoT scenarios (smart sensors sending temperature data to a cloud dashboard), **HTTP** is acceptable for non-sensitive telemetry over a private LAN, but **HTTPS is mandatory** for any data traversing the public internet, especially credentials, firmware updates, or personal health data.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Default HTTP Port: 80** (unencrypted)
- **Default HTTPS Port: 443** (encrypted via TLS)
- **HTTP Versions: HTTP/0.9, HTTP/1.0, HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3**
- **HTTP/2** uses binary framing and **multiplexing** (RFC 7540)
- **HTTP/3** runs over **QUIC** (Quick UDP Internet Connections) — RFC 9114
- **TLS Latest Stable Version: TLS 1.3 (2018)**
- **Stateless Protocol:** Each HTTP request is independent; no session memory is retained by the server (state is managed via **Cookies**, **JWT**, or **URL parameters**).
- **Resource Identification:** Uses **URIs (Uniform Resource Identifiers)** — most commonly **URLs (Uniform Resource Locators)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HTTP Communication Architecture

HTTP follows a strict **Client-Server Model**:

1. **Client:** Initiates the connection. In IoT, this is typically a sensor node, microcontroller (e.g., **ESP32**, **Raspberry Pi**), or a gateway.
2. **Server:** Listens on a specific port and responds. In IoT, this could be a **RESTful API**, an **MQTT broker's HTTP bridge**, or a cloud platform like **AWS IoT**, **Azure IoT Hub**, or **ThingsBoard**.

### 2.2 HTTP Request-Response Cycle (The "Why" and "How")

**Why does HTTP work this way?** Because the web was originally designed as a *document retrieval* system, where users *ask* for resources and servers *deliver* them.

**How a transaction occurs:**

1. **TCP Connection Establishment:** A **3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)** is performed to open a reliable connection on Port 80.
2. **Client sends an HTTP Request Message** containing:
   - A **Request Line**: Method, URI, and HTTP version.
   - **Request Headers**: Metadata (Host, User-Agent, Accept, Content-Type, Authorization).
   - An empty line (CRLF).
   - An optional **Message Body** (for methods like POST, PUT).
3. **Server processes the request** and sends back an **HTTP Response Message** containing:
   - A **Status Line**: HTTP version, Status Code, Reason Phrase.
   - **Response Headers**: Metadata (Content-Type, Content-Length, Set-Cookie, Date).
   - An empty line.
   - The **Message Body** (HTML, JSON, XML, plain text, or binary).
4. **Connection Management:**
   - **HTTP/1.0:** New TCP connection per request (inefficient).
   - **HTTP/1.1:** Introduced **persistent connections** (`Connection: keep-alive`).
   - **HTTP/2:** Multiplexes many streams over a **single TCP connection**.

### 2.3 HTTP Methods (Verbs) — High-Yield for KTU

| Method | Purpose | Idempotent? | Safe? | Body Allowed? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GET** | Retrieve a resource | Yes | Yes | No |
| **POST** | Submit data / create resource | No | No | Yes |
| **PUT** | Replace/Update a resource entirely | Yes | No | Yes |
| **PATCH** | Partial modification of a resource | No | No | Yes |
| **DELETE** | Remove a resource | Yes | No | Rarely |
| **HEAD** | Same as GET but returns headers only | Yes | Yes | No |
| **OPTIONS** | Describes communication options | Yes | Yes | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** "Idempotent" means executing the same request multiple times produces the **same server-side state**. This is a common **2-mark short-answer question**.

### 2.4 HTTP Status Codes (The 5 Classes)

| Class | Range | Meaning | Common Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1xx** | 100–199 | **Informational** — request received, continuing process | 100 Continue |
| **2xx** | 200–299 | **Success** — request successfully received/understood/accepted | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx** | 300–399 | **Redirection** — further action needed to complete request | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx** | 400–499 | **Client Error** — request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, **404 Not Found** |
| **5xx** | 500–599 | **Server Error** — server failed to fulfill a valid request | 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable |

### 2.5 HTTPS Deep Dive — TLS Handshake & Encryption

**Why HTTPS?** Three security goals are addressed:
- **Confidentiality** via symmetric encryption (e.g., **AES-256-GCM**).
- **Integrity** via Message Authentication Codes (e.g., **HMAC-SHA256**) and AEAD ciphers.
- **Authentication** via **X.509 Digital Certificates** issued by trusted **Certificate Authorities (CAs)** like Let's Encrypt, DigiCert, or GlobalSign.

**Asymmetric vs. Symmetric Cryptography in TLS:**
- **Asymmetric (Public-Key)** algorithms like **RSA, ECDHE, X25519** are used *only during the handshake* to securely exchange a shared secret.
- **Symmetric** algorithms like **AES** are used *for the bulk data transfer* (much faster).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table consolidates every numerically verifiable fact for exam answers.

| Parameter | HTTP | HTTPS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default Port** | **80** | **443** |
| **Transport Protocol** | TCP | TCP (over TLS) |
| **Encryption** | None | TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3 |
| **URL Prefix** | `http://` | `https://` |
| **Certificate Required** | No | Yes (X.509) |
| **Handshake Round-Trips (TLS 1.2)** | 0 (plain TCP only) | 2 RTT |
| **Handshake Round-Trips (TLS 1.3)** | 0 | **1 RTT** |
| **Performance** | Faster (no crypto overhead) | Slower (~5–10% overhead) |
| **Use in IoT** | Local private networks | Public internet, PII data |
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 7 (Application) | Layer 7 (Application) + Layer 5/6 (Session/Presentation via TLS) |
| **Underlying UDP Support** | No (HTTP/3 via QUIC only) | Yes (HTTP/3 + QUIC) |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering

- **RESTful APIs:** Almost all public IoT cloud platforms (AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT, ThingSpeak, Ubidots) expose data via **HTTP/REST endpoints**.
- **Webhooks:** IoT gateways push events to a server using HTTP **POST** requests.
- **OTA (Over-The-Air) Firmware Updates:** Devices download new firmware from an HTTPS server, verifying integrity using **SHA-256 hashes** signed by the manufacturer.
- **Constrained Devices:** Pure HTTP is too heavy for Class 0/1 IoT devices (e.g., 8-bit MCUs) — for these, **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** over UDP is preferred. However, **HTTP is widely used on Wi-Fi/ESP32-class devices.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Breakdown of an HTTP Request

A complete HTTP/1.1 request from an IoT sensor (e.g., a temperature node) submitting data to a cloud endpoint looks like this:

```
POST /api/v1/sensors/temperature/data HTTP/1.1
Host: api.iotcloud.example.com
User-Agent: ESP32-IoT-Node/1.4.2
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 58
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
Connection: keep-alive
Accept: application/json

{"sensor_id":"T-014","value":27.4,"unit":"C","timestamp":"2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"}
```

**Component-by-Component Logical Analysis:**

| Line / Field | Value | Meaning / Engineering Logic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Request Line | `POST /api/v1/... HTTP/1.1` | Method is POST (creating new data record), URI path identifies the resource, version is HTTP/1.1 |
| `Host` header | `api.iotcloud.example.com` | Mandatory in HTTP/1.1; allows virtual hosting on a single server IP |
| `User-Agent` | `ESP32-IoT-Node/1.4.2` | Identifies the client device and firmware version — useful for server-side analytics |
| `Content-Type` | `application/json` | Tells the server the body uses **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)** |
| `Content-Length` | `58` | Number of bytes in the body — required for the server to know when the body ends |
| `Authorization` | `Bearer eyJ...` | **JWT (JSON Web Token)** for OAuth 2.0 authentication |
| `Connection` | `keep-alive` | Reuses the TCP connection for subsequent requests (HTTP/1.1 default behavior) |
| `Accept` | `application/json` | Client prefers JSON in the response |
| Blank Line | (CRLF) | Separator between headers and body |
| Body | `{"sensor_id":...}` | The actual telemetry payload |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: HTTP Transaction Over TCP

We now derive the complete communication sequence, capturing every packet exchange.

**Step 1 — TCP Handshake (Connection Setup):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client} &\rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{SYN, seq} = x \\
\text{Server} &\rightarrow \text{Client} : \text{SYN, ACK, seq} = y, \text{ack} = x+1 \\
\text{Client} &\rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{ACK, seq} = x+1, \text{ack} = y+1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Interpretation:** This is the classic **3-way handshake** that synchronizes sequence numbers (`seq`) and acknowledgment numbers (`ack`) before any application data is sent. It establishes a **full-duplex** reliable channel.

**Step 2 — HTTP Request Transmission:**

The client transmits the entire request (headers + body) as a stream of TCP segments. The server's TCP stack reassembles them in order using sequence numbers and acknowledges receipt.

**Step 3 — Server Processing:**

The web server (e.g., **Nginx**, **Apache**, or an IoT cloud platform) parses the request, routes it to the correct handler (e.g., a Flask/Django/Node.js route), and may persist data to a database (e.g., **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**, **MongoDB**).

**Step 4 — HTTP Response Transmission:**

The server replies with a status line, headers, and body. For example:

```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Date: Wed, 15 Jan 2025 10:30:01 GMT
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 45
Location: /api/v1/sensors/temperature/data/9921

{"status":"success","record_id":9921,"stored":true}
```

**Step 5 — Connection Teardown (or Reuse):**

With HTTP/1.1 persistent connections, the TCP connection stays open. After a configurable **timeout (commonly 60–120 seconds)**, a 4-way termination occurs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Client} &\rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{FIN} \\
\text{Server} &\rightarrow \text{Client} : \text{ACK} \\
\text{Server} &\rightarrow \text{Client} : \text{FIN} \\
\text{Client} &\rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{ACK}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: TLS 1.2 Handshake (HTTPS)

For HTTPS, an additional cryptographic handshake occurs *after* the TCP handshake.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{ClientHello} \quad (\text{supported ciphers, random } R_C) \\
&\text{Step 2: Server} \rightarrow \text{Client} : \text{ServerHello} + \text{Certificate} + \text{ServerHelloDone} \\
&\text{Step 3: Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} : \text{Key Exchange} + \text{ChangeCipherSpec} + \text{Finished} \\
&\text{Step 4: Server} \rightarrow \text{Client} : \text{ChangeCipherSpec} + \text{Finished} \\
&\text{Step 5: Both sides derive a shared symmetric key from the exchanged pre-master secret.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Interpretation of Each Step:**

1. **ClientHello:** The client advertises the highest TLS version it supports, a list of cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`), and a random nonce `R_C`.
2. **ServerHello + Certificate:** The server selects a cipher suite, sends its random nonce `R_S`, and transmits its **X.509 certificate** containing its **public key**. The client validates the certificate against a trusted CA chain.
3. **Key Exchange:** The client encrypts a **pre-master secret** using the server's public key (RSA) or performs a **Diffie-Hellman key exchange** (DHE/ECDHE) for **forward secrecy**, then sends it.
4. **ChangeCipherSpec + Finished:** Both parties switch to the negotiated symmetric encryption and send a MAC of all previous handshake messages to detect tampering.
5. **Symmetric Key Derivation:** Using a **Key Derivation Function (HKDF)** with `R_C`, `R_S`, and the pre-master secret, both sides independently compute identical **session keys** for bulk encryption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Security Property — Forward Secrecy (PFS):** With **ECDHE**, even if the server's long-term private key is later compromised, past recorded sessions *cannot* be decrypted because a fresh ephemeral key is used per session.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: IoT Use-Case Decision

| IoT Scenario | Recommended Protocol | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Battery-powered soil sensor over LoRaWAN | **CoAP** (not HTTP) | UDP-based, lightweight, fits RFC 7252 |
| Wi-Fi smart bulb firmware update | **HTTPS** | Authenticity, integrity of binary firmware critical |
| Indoor air quality monitor on private LAN | **HTTP** | Low risk; no public exposure |
| Wearable health device sending vitals to cloud | **HTTPS with TLS 1.3** | HIPAA/GDPR compliance, sensitive PII |
| Industrial PLC control over cellular | **HTTPS with mutual TLS (mTLS)** | Both client and server authenticate each other |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HTTP Request-Response Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Device Client ESP32] -->|TCP SYN| B[Cloud Server Nginx]
    B -->|TCP SYN ACK| A
    A -->|TCP ACK| B
    A -->|HTTP Request POST data| B
    B -->|Process and Store| C[(Database InfluxDB)]
    C -->|Acknowledge Write| B
    B -->|HTTP Response 201 Created| A
    A -->|TCP FIN| B
    B -->|TCP FIN ACK| A
```

### 4.2 HTTPS TLS 1.2 Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as IoT Client
    participant S as Cloud Server
    participant CA as Certificate Authority

    C->>S: TCP SYN
    S-->>C: TCP SYN ACK
    C->>S: TCP ACK
    C->>S: ClientHello ciphers random_RC
    S->>CA: Verify Certificate Chain
    CA-->>S: Valid Certificate
    S-->>C: ServerHello Certificate ServerHelloDone
    C->>C: Validate X.509 Certificate
    C->>S: KeyExchange ChangeCipherSpec Finished
    S->>S: Derive Session Keys
    S-->>C: ChangeCipherSpec Finished
    Note over C,S: Encrypted HTTPS Communication Begins
    C->>S: Encrypted HTTP GET request
    S-->>C: Encrypted HTTP 200 OK response
```

### 4.3 IoT Cloud Communication Block Diagram (HTTP vs HTTPS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FieldLayer[Field Layer IoT Devices]
        D1[Smart Sensor ESP32]
        D2[Actuator Node]
        D3[Gateway Raspberry Pi]
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer[Network Layer]
        R1[WiFi Router]
        R2[Cellular Modem]
    end

    subgraph CloudLayer[Cloud Layer]
        API[REST API Endpoint]
        DB[(Time Series Database)]
        UI[Web Dashboard]
    end

    D1 -->|HTTP HTTPS| R1
    D2 -->|HTTPS only| R2
    D3 -->|HTTPS TLS 1.3| R1
    R1 -->|TLS Encrypted| API
    R2 -->|TLS Encrypted| API
    API --> DB
    DB --> UI
```

### 4.4 HTTP Message Structure Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Msg[HTTP Message] --> Req{Request or Response}
    Req -->|Request| RL[Start Line: Method URI Version]
    Req -->|Response| SL[Status Line: Version Code Phrase]
    RL --> H1[Headers: Host User Agent Content Type]
    SL --> H1
    H1 --> BL[Empty Line CRLF]
    BL --> BD[Optional Message Body JSON XML Binary]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS with respect to **port numbers, security mechanisms, and OSI layer operation**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | HTTP | HTTPS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default Port** | 80 | 443 |
| **Security Mechanism** | No encryption; data sent in plaintext | Encrypted using **TLS/SSL**; provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication via X.509 certificates |
| **OSI Layer** | Operates at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** only | Operates at **Layer 7 (Application)** with TLS providing session-layer encryption between Layer 4 (Transport) and Layer 7 |

In IoT, HTTPS is preferred for any data traversing public networks, while HTTP may be used in isolated private LANs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any **six HTTP methods** and state the purpose of each in one line.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **GET** – Retrieves a representation of a resource. Safe and idempotent.
2. **POST** – Submits data to the server, typically creating a new resource. Not idempotent.
3. **PUT** – Replaces an existing resource or creates one at a specific URI. Idempotent.
4. **DELETE** – Removes a specified resource. Idempotent.
5. **PATCH** – Applies a partial modification to a resource. Not idempotent.
6. **HEAD** – Retrieves the response headers only, without the body. Safe and idempotent.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of HTTP with a neat block diagram. Describe the structure of an HTTP request message and an HTTP response message with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

HTTP follows a **client-server request-response architecture**. The client (e.g., a browser or IoT device) opens a **TCP connection on Port 80** to the server, sends a request, and receives a response. The architecture consists of:

1. **Client Layer** – Issues HTTP requests (e.g., ESP32 sensor).
2. **Network Layer** – TCP/IP stack for reliable delivery.
3. **Server Layer** – Web server (Nginx/Apache) processes the request.
4. **Application Logic** – Server-side code handles the resource.

**Structure of an HTTP Request Message:**

```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Connection: keep-alive

(empty line, no body for GET)
```

- **Request Line** = Method + URI + HTTP version.
- **Headers** = Metadata about the request.
- **Blank Line** = CRLF separator.
- **Body** = Optional payload (used in POST, PUT).

**Structure of an HTTP Response Message:**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Wed, 15 Jan 2025 10:00:00 GMT
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: 1024

<html><body>Hello IoT</body></html>
```

- **Status Line** = Version + Status Code + Reason Phrase.
- **Headers** = Metadata about the response.
- **Body** = The requested resource.

---

**(b)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the **TLS 1.2 handshake process** used in HTTPS. Mention the role of the **X.509 certificate** and **forward secrecy**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The TLS 1.2 handshake establishes a secure channel through 4 main steps after the TCP handshake:

1. **ClientHello** → Client sends supported TLS versions, cipher suites, and a random nonce `R_C`.
2. **ServerHello + Certificate** → Server selects a cipher, sends its `R_S`, and presents its **X.509 certificate** containing its public key and the issuing CA's digital signature.
3. **Client Key Exchange** → Client verifies the certificate chain against a trusted root CA. It then generates a **pre-master secret** and encrypts it with the server's public key (or uses **ECDHE** for forward secrecy), then sends a **ChangeCipherSpec** and **Finished** message.
4. **Server Completion** → Server decrypts the pre-master secret, both sides run an **HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function)** to derive symmetric session keys, and the server sends its own **ChangeCipherSpec** and **Finished**.

**Role of X.509 Certificate:**
- A digital certificate binds a **public key** to an identity (domain name).
- It is signed by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)** such as Let's Encrypt.
- Enables **authentication** — the client knows it is talking to the genuine server.

**Forward Secrecy (PFS):**
- Achieved via **ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (DHE or ECDHE)** key exchanges.
- A **new, temporary key pair** is generated for every session.
- Even if the server's long-term private key is compromised later, past session traffic **cannot** be decrypted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **HTTP and HTTPS** in detail. Discuss why HTTPS is recommended for IoT devices communicating over the public internet. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | HTTP | HTTPS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Port** | 80 | 443 |
| **Encryption** | None | TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3 |
| **Authentication** | None | X.509 certificates via CAs |
| **Integrity** | Not guaranteed | HMAC + AEAD ciphers |
| **URL Prefix** | `http://` | `https://` |
| **Performance** | Faster | ~5–10% overhead due to crypto |
| **Search Engine Ranking** | Penalized by Google | Boosted |
| **Use Case** | Local LAN, public APIs | Internet, PII, financial, firmware |

**Why HTTPS for IoT:**

1. **Data Confidentiality:** Sensor data (health vitals, location, industrial metrics) may be intercepted in transit; TLS encrypts it.
2. **Authentication:** Prevents **man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks** where a rogue gateway impersonates the cloud server.
3. **Data Integrity:** Ensures firmware updates are not tampered with during download (a malicious actor could otherwise inject a backdoored binary).
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS mandate encryption of personal and financial data in transit.
5. **Mutual TLS (mTLS):** For high-security industrial IoT, even the **device** must present a client certificate to the server.

---

**(b)** Describe the various **HTTP status code classes** (1xx to 5xx) with two examples each. Explain the difference between **idempotent** and **safe** HTTP methods. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**HTTP Status Code Classes:**

| Class | Range | Meaning | Example Codes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Provisional response; client should continue | **100 Continue**, 101 Switching Protocols |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request received and processed successfully | **200 OK**, **201 Created**, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Client must take additional action | **301 Moved Permanently**, **304 Not Modified** |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Bad request from client side | **400 Bad Request**, **404 Not Found**, 401 Unauthorized |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfill a valid request | **500 Internal Server Error**, **503 Service Unavailable** |

**Idempotent vs. Safe Methods:**

- **Safe Method:** A method is *safe* if it is intended only for **read-only** operations and **does not modify** server state. Examples: `GET`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`. Calling them 100 times produces the same observable effect as calling once.
- **Idempotent Method:** A method is *idempotent* if **repeated identical requests** have the **same effect** as a single request, even though the server state may change. Examples: `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`.
- **Key Distinction:** `DELETE` is idempotent (deleting an already-deleted resource still yields "404" or "204" consistently), but it is **not safe** because it changes server state. `POST` is neither safe nor idempotent (each call may create a new resource).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Port Numbers:** Many students write "HTTPS uses Port 80" — this is a guaranteed **1-mark loss**. Memorize **80 (HTTP)** and **443 (HTTPS)**.
> 2. **Ignoring the TLS Layer:** When asked about HTTPS, students often describe HTTP and stop. Always explicitly mention **TLS/SSL** and the **certificate-based authentication**.
> 3. **Status Code Classes:** Listing codes without mentioning the **range** (e.g., "404") loses a mark. Always state the **class and range** (e.g., "4xx — Client Error, 400–499").
> 4. **Idempotency vs. Safety:** Examiners frequently ask the difference. Conflating them costs **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** A 7-mark sub-question on HTTP architecture or TLS handshake *requires* a neat diagram. Missing it = **at least 2 marks deducted**.
> 6. **HTTP/2 & HTTP/3 Ignorance:** If the question says "latest trends" or "modern versions", mention **HTTP/2 multiplexing** and **HTTP/3 over QUIC**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ⚡ **HTTP = Port 80, Plaintext, Application Layer (L7), No encryption.**
- ⚡ **HTTPS = Port 443, Encrypted via TLS 1.2/1.3, Adds Authentication & Integrity.**
- ⚡ **HTTP is stateless** — state is managed via Cookies, JWT, or URL params.
- ⚡ **HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections** (keep-alive); **HTTP/2** added **multiplexing**; **HTTP/3** uses **QUIC over UDP**.
- ⚡ **Key Methods:** GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove), HEAD (headers only).
- ⚡ **Idempotent Methods:** GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE.
- ⚡ **Safe Methods:** GET, HEAD, OPTIONS.
- ⚡ **Status Code Ranges:** 1xx Info, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirect, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error.
- ⚡ **TLS 1.2 Handshake = 2 RTT; TLS 1.3 Handshake = 1 RTT (faster).**
- ⚡ **X.509 certificates** issued by **CAs** (Let's Encrypt, DigiCert) enable server authentication.
- ⚡ **Forward Secrecy (PFS)** is achieved using **ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (DHE/ECDHE)**.
- ⚡ **In IoT:** Use HTTPS for public internet, firmware OTA, and PII; HTTP is acceptable only in isolated private LANs.
- ⚡ **Alternative for constrained IoT devices:** **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** over UDP, which mimics HTTP semantics with far less overhead.
- ⚡ **Symmetric encryption (AES-256-GCM)** is used for bulk data; **asymmetric (RSA/ECDHE)** only for the handshake.
- ⚡ **Mutual TLS (mTLS)** is used in industrial IoT where the *device* must also authenticate to the *server*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: XMPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** XMPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol)** is an open, XML-based, real-time communication protocol standardized by the **IETF** through a series of RFCs (RFC 6120, RFC 6121, RFC 6122). Originally conceived as **Jabber** in 1999 by Jeremie Miller, XMPP was formalized by the **IETF XMPP Working Group** in 2004. It is purpose-built for **instant messaging (IM)**, **presence notification**, **contact list maintenance**, and **real-time, asynchronous message exchange** between networked entities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** XMPP is classified as one of the **Application Layer Protocols for IoT**, specifically designed for **human-to-human (H2H)** and increasingly **machine-to-machine (M2M)** communication where low-bandwidth, secure, and presence-aware messaging is required.

### Formal Specification Triad

XMPP's foundation is built upon three core RFC documents that every KTU student must memorize:

| RFC Number | Standard Title | Function |
|------------|----------------|----------|
| **RFC 6120** | XMPP: Core | Defines the core protocol, XML streaming, TLS, SASL |
| **RFC 6121** | XMPP: Instant Messaging and Presence | Defines IM semantics and presence |
| **RFC 6122** | XMPP: Address Format | Defines the Jabber ID (JID) addressing scheme |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "WhatsApp of the Internet"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of XMPP as the **postal system for instant messages**. Just as the postal service uses a standardized envelope format (with sender address, receiver address, and content) delivered through a decentralized network of post offices, XMPP uses **standardized XML "envelopes" (called stanzas)** routed through a federated network of XMPP servers. Anyone can run their own "post office" (XMPP server), and they all agree to deliver messages to each other.

### Why This Analogy Works
- **Decentralized Servers** ↔ Multiple Post Offices: No single company owns the network
- **Jabber ID (JID)** ↔ Postal Address: Unique identifier like `user@domain/resource`
- **XML Stanzas** ↔ Standardized Envelopes: Same format regardless of which server handles it
- **Federation** ↔ Universal Postal Union: Servers cooperate across organizational boundaries

## 1.3 Core Characteristics (KTU Board Definition)

XMPP exhibits the following distinguishing technical properties:

- **Decentralized Architecture:** No central authority; anyone can run a server
- **Open Standards:** XML-based, royalty-free, IETF-ratified
- **Extensibility:** New features added via XEPs (XMPP Extension Protocols) without breaking the core
- **Security-First:** Native **TLS** (Transport Layer Security) and **SASL** (Simple Authentication and Security Layer) for encryption and authentication
- **Presence-Aware:** Built-in support for online/offline/away status tracking
- **Asynchronous:** Messages are stored and forwarded when recipients come online

> [!TIP]
> **Key Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"DOSE-PA"** — **D**ecentralized, **O**pen, **S**ecure, **E**xtensible, **P**resence-aware, **A**synchronous.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|--------|---------------|---------|
| **Default Port (Client-to-Server)** | **5222** | Unencrypted XMPP communication |
| **Default Port (Client-to-Server + TLS)** | **5223** | Legacy SSL port (deprecated) |
| **Default Port (Server-to-Server)** | **5269** | Federation between XMPP servers |
| **Default Port (BOSH)** | **5280 / 80** | Bidirectional-streams Over Synchronous HTTP |
| **Default Port (WebSocket)** | **5280 / 443** | Browser-based XMPP connections |
| **XML Namespace (Core)** | **`jabber:client`** | Default client namespace |
| **XML Namespace (Server)** | **`jabber:server`** | Server-to-server namespace |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 XMPP Architecture — Layered Breakdown

XMPP follows a **Client-Server architecture with Server-to-Server (S2S) federation**. Let us deconstruct each component:

### 2.1.1 The Three Architectural Tiers

**Tier 1: XMPP Client**
- Software library or application initiating/receiving XMPP stanzas
- Examples: **Pidgin**, **Conversations**, **Gajim**, **Adium**
- Maintains a **persistent TCP connection** to a server on port 5222
- Responsible for parsing and generating XML stanzas

**Tier 2: XMPP Server**
- Central node that handles session management, message routing, presence, and storage
- Examples: **ejabberd** (Erlang), **Openfire** (Java), **Prosody** (Lua), **Tigase** (Java)
- Stores **user accounts, contact lists (rosters), offline messages**

**Tier 3: Server-to-Server (S2S) Link**
- Direct TCP connection between two XMPP servers on port **5269**
- Enables **federation**: a user on Server A can message a user on Server B
- Uses **server dialback** (RFC 6120 § 10.3) for anti-spoofing

### 2.1.2 The Operational Flow (Why and How)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How a message travels from Alice to Bob across different servers:**

**Step 1 — Authentication:** Alice's client opens a TCP connection to `alice.tld` (her server) and authenticates via **SASL** (typically `PLAIN`, `DIGEST-MD5`, or `SCRAM-SHA-1`).

**Step 2 — Presence Publication:** Alice's client publishes her presence status (`available`, `away`, `dnd`) to her server, which fans it out to all subscribed contacts.

**Step 3 — Message Origination:** Alice composes a `<message/>` stanza addressed to Bob's JID (`bob@bobsserver.org`).

**Step 4 — Server Lookup:** Alice's server performs **DNS SRV lookup** for `_xmpp-server._tcp.bobsserver.org` to find Bob's server.

**Step 5 — S2S Handshake:** Alice's server establishes a TLS-encrypted S2S connection to Bob's server on port 5269 and performs **server dialback** for domain verification.

**Step 6 — Delivery:** The stanza is relayed over the S2S link. Bob's server checks Bob's presence and either delivers it immediately (online) or stores it in Bob's **offline message queue** (offline).

**Step 7 — Acknowledgment:** Bob's server returns an XMPP error or success acknowledgment to Alice's server.

## 2.2 The Jabber ID (JID) — The Addressing System

Every XMPP entity is identified by a **Jabber ID (JID)**, structurally similar to an email address:

$$\text{JID} = \text{node} \, \text{@} \, \text{domain} \, \text{/} \, \text{resource}$$

### Component Breakdown

| Component | Purpose | Example | Mandatory? |
|-----------|---------|---------|------------|
| **Node** | Username / local identifier | `alice` | Optional (for rooms, channels) |
| **Domain** | Server hostname (routable) | `chat.example.com` | **Yes** |
| **Resource** | Specific client/session identifier | `laptop`, `mobile` | Optional |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** The JID `alice@chat.example.com/laptop` identifies the user `alice` on server `chat.example.com` using her laptop client. If Alice logs in from her phone, the resource changes to `mobile`, allowing both sessions to receive messages simultaneously.

### Bare JID vs. Full JID
- **Bare JID:** `alice@chat.example.com` (used for persistent identity, contact lists)
- **Full JID:** `alice@chat.example.com/laptop` (used for routing to a specific session)

## 2.3 XMPP Stanzas — The Three Building Blocks

All XMPP communication occurs through three fundamental XML stanza types. **This is the highest-weight topic for KTU board exams.**

### Stanza Type 1: `<message/>`
Used for one-to-one or one-to-many **push** of data (chat, alerts, notifications).

### Stanza Type 2: `<presence/>`
Used to broadcast **online status** information to subscribed entities.

### Stanza Type 3: `<iq/>` (Info/Query)
Used for **request-response** interactions (similar to HTTP GET/POST). Always paired with a response.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Element / Concept | Technical Definition | Default Values / Units |
|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **JID Structure** | $\text{node}@\text{domain}/\text{resource}$ | node: optional, domain: mandatory |
| **C2S Port** | Client-to-Server TCP port | **5222** |
| **S2S Port** | Server-to-Server TCP port | **5269** |
| **BOSH Port** | HTTP fallback port | **5280 / 80** |
| **WebSocket Port** | Browser-based XMPP | **5280 / 443** |
| **Core Namespace** | XML default namespace | `jabber:client` |
| **Stanza Types** | Three top-level elements | `<message>`, `<presence>`, `<iq>` |
| **IQ Types** | Get, Set, Result, Error | 4 child element semantics |
| **Message Types** | chat, error, groupchat, headline, normal | 5 types per RFC 6121 |
| **Presence Types** | available, unavailable, subscribe, etc. | 7+ types per RFC 6121 |
| **Authentication** | SASL mechanism | PLAIN, DIGEST-MD5, SCRAM-SHA-1 |
| **Encryption (C2S)** | TLS mandatory | Port 5222 + STARTTLS |
| **Encryption (S2S)** | TLS + Server Dialback | Port 5269 + STARTTLS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse BOSH port (5280) with WebSocket port. **BOSH uses long-polling HTTP**, while **WebSocket uses full-duplex TCP upgrade**. Both run on the same port but are different transports.

## 2.5 XMPP in IoT — The Engineering Utility

XMPP is particularly well-suited for **high-latency, low-power IoT scenarios**:

- **Smart Home Hubs:** Devices can publish presence (online/offline) to a central hub
- **Industrial M2M:** Machine status updates routed via federated gateways
- **Healthcare Telemetry:** Secure, encrypted patient monitoring over standard internet
- **Asset Tracking:** GPS-equipped devices publish location as presence updates
- **Smart Agriculture:** Sensor nodes send `<message/>` stanzas with soil/weather data to a central broker

> [!TIP]
> **Why XMPP over MQTT in some IoT scenarios?** XMPP's native **federation** allows IoT devices on different organizational networks to discover and message each other without a central broker — critical for cross-domain IoT deployments. MQTT requires a central broker, creating a single point of failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Stanza Type Comparison

The following matrix is critical for KTU Part A and Part B questions. Every student must be able to construct valid XML for all three stanza types.

### 3.1.1 Message Stanza — Detailed Schema

**Definition:** A `message` stanza is a "push" mechanism with no inherent response requirement.

**Schema Specification:**

$$\text{Message} = \langle \text{message} \rangle \{ \text{Attributes} \} \{ \text{Child Elements} \} \langle /\text{message} \rangle$$

**Allowed Attributes:**
- `to` — Recipient JID (Full or Bare)
- `from` — Sender JID (Full or Bare)
- `type` — One of: `chat`, `error`, `groupchat`, `headline`, `normal`
- `id` — Optional stanza identifier
- `xml:lang` — Language tag

**Child Elements (Subordinate Payloads):**
- `<body/>` — Human-readable text content
- `<subject/>` — Optional subject line
- `<thread/>` — Conversation thread identifier
- `<error/>` — Error condition element

### 3.1.2 Presence Stanza — Detailed Schema

**Definition:** A `presence` stanza is broadcast to all entities that have subscribed to the sender's presence.

**Schema Specification:**

$$\text{Presence} = \langle \text{presence} \rangle \{ \text{Attributes} \} \{ \text{Child Elements} \} \langle /\text{presence} \rangle$$

**Allowed `type` Values (per RFC 6121 § 4.7):**

| Presence Type | Meaning | Show Value |
|---------------|---------|------------|
| **available** (omitted) | Online | `chat` |
| **unavailable** | Offline | N/A |
| **subscribe** | Request to subscribe | N/A |
| **subscribed** | Grant subscription | N/A |
| **unsubscribe** | Cancel subscription | N/A |
| **unsubscribed** | Deny subscription | N/A |
| **error** | Stanza error | N/A |

**Child Elements:**
- `<show/>` — Free-form status: `away`, `chat`, `dnd`, `xa`
- `<status/>` — Human-readable status message
- `<priority/>` — Integer (-128 to 127) for routing priority

### 3.1.3 IQ (Info/Query) Stanza — Detailed Schema

**Definition:** An `iq` stanza is a **request-response** transaction. Each IQ request MUST receive exactly one response (result or error).

**Schema Specification:**

$$\text{IQ} = \langle \text{iq} \rangle \{ \text{Attributes} \} \{ \text{Child Elements} \} \langle /\text{iq} \rangle$$

**Mandatory `type` Attribute Values:**

| IQ Type | Direction | Purpose |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **get** | Client → Server | Request information |
| **set** | Client → Server | Send/Update information |
| **result** | Server → Client | Successful response to get/set |
| **error** | Server → Client | Error response to get/set |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Examples — Stanza Construction

### Example 1: Constructing a Chat Message

> **Question:** Write the XMPP stanza for Alice (`alice@chat.example.com/laptop`) sending "Hello Bob!" to Bob (`bob@chat.example.com/mobile`).

**Step 1 — Identify stanza type:** This is a real-time chat → `<message type="chat">`

**Step 2 — Construct attributes:**
- `to = "bob@chat.example.com/mobile"`
- `from = "alice@chat.example.com/laptop"`
- `xml:lang = "en"`

**Step 3 — Add body element:** `<body>Hello Bob!</body>`

**Final Stanza:**

```xml
<message
    to='bob@chat.example.com/mobile'
    from='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'
    type='chat'
    xml:lang='en'>
  <body>Hello Bob!</body>
</message>
```

### Example 2: Constructing a Presence Broadcast

> **Question:** Write the XMPP stanza for Alice announcing she is online and "Available for chat" with priority 10.

**Step 1 — Stanza type:** `<presence>` with no `type` attribute (default = available)

**Step 2 — Add child elements:**
- `<show>chat</show>` — Indicates "free for chat"
- `<status>Available for chat</status>` — Human-readable message
- `<priority>10</priority>` — Routing priority

**Final Stanza:**

```xml
<presence from='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'>
  <show>chat</show>
  <status>Available for chat</status>
  <priority>10</priority>
</presence>
```

### Example 3: Constructing an IQ Roster Request

> **Question:** Write the XMPP stanza for Alice requesting her contact list (roster) from the server.

**Step 1 — Stanza type:** `<iq type="get">`

**Step 2 — Child element:** `<query xmlns="jabber:iq:roster"/>` (namespaced child)

**Final Stanza:**

```xml
<iq from='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'
    type='get'
    id='roster1'>
  <query xmlns='jabber:iq:roster'/>
</iq>
```

**Server's Response (IQ Result):**

```xml
<iq to='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'
    type='result'
    id='roster1'>
  <query xmlns='jabber:iq:roster'>
    <item jid='bob@chat.example.com' name='Bob'/>
    <item jid='carol@chat.example.com' name='Carol'/>
  </query>
</iq>
```

## 3.3 XMPP Session Lifecycle — Full Derivation

The complete XMPP session follows a strict sequence. We derive this step-by-step:

**Phase 1 — Stream Negotiation (C2S):**

$$\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{TLS Upgrade}} \text{Server (Port 5222)}$$

**Step 1.1:** Client opens TCP connection to server on port **5222**.

**Step 1.2:** Server sends `<stream:stream>` opening tag with version, namespace, and supported features.

**Step 1.3:** Client sends its own `<stream:stream>` opening tag.

**Step 1.4:** Server advertises `<starttls/>` feature; client sends `<starttls/>` request.

**Step 1.5:** TLS handshake completes; old stream is closed and a new encrypted stream is opened.

**Phase 2 — Authentication (SASL):**

**Step 2.1:** Server advertises supported SASL mechanisms (e.g., `SCRAM-SHA-1`, `PLAIN`).

**Step 2.2:** Client selects a mechanism and exchanges credentials.

**Step 2.3:** Server authenticates the client and binds a resource.

**Phase 3 — Resource Binding (RFC 6120 § 7):**

**Step 3.1:** Client sends `<iq type="set"><bind/></iq>` to request a resource.

**Step 3.2:** Server responds with `<iq type="result">` containing the assigned JID.

**Phase 4 — Session Establishment:**

**Step 4.1:** Client sends `<iq type="set"><session/></iq>` to start the IM session.

**Step 4.2:** Server acknowledges with `<iq type="result"/>`.

**Phase 5 — Active Communication:**

**Step 5.1:** Client sends `<presence/>` to announce online status.

**Step 5.2:** Server broadcasts presence to all roster contacts.

**Step 5.3:** Client and server exchange `<message/>` and `<iq/>` stanzas.

**Phase 6 — Stream Closure:**

**Step 6.1:** Either party sends `</stream:stream>` to gracefully close.

**Phase 6.2:** Underlying TCP connection is terminated.

## 3.4 XMPP Namespace Inheritance — The Mathematical Model

XMPP uses XML namespace inheritance to avoid repetitive prefixing. The model is:

$$\text{Default Namespace} = \begin{cases} \text{jabber:client} & \text{for C2S streams} \\ \text{jabber:server} & \text{for S2S streams} \end{cases}$$

**Why this matters for IoT:** By inheriting the default namespace, every child element (e.g., `<body/>`, `<query/>`) automatically belongs to the XMPP namespace, reducing XML payload size — critical for bandwidth-constrained IoT devices.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 XMPP Network Architecture — Federated Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ClientA["Client A Domain (chat.example.com)"]
        direction TB
        clientA1["Alice's Laptop Client\nresource=laptop"]
        clientA2["Alice's Mobile Client\nresource=mobile"]
        clientA3["Bob's Web Client\nresource=web"]
    end

    subgraph ServerA["Server A (chat.example.com)"]
        direction TB
        srvA1["ejabberd / Openfire\nMain Server Engine"]
        srvA2["Offline Message Store\nPersistent Storage"]
        srvA3["Roster Database\nContact Lists"]
        srvA4["Presence Manager\nOnline Status Tracker"]
    end

    subgraph Network["Public Internet (Federation)"]
        direction TB
        net1["S2S TLS Link\nPort 5269"]
        net2["DNS SRV Lookup\n_xmpp-server._tcp"]
    end

    subgraph ServerB["Server B (bobsserver.org)"]
        direction TB
        srvB1["ejabberd / Prosody\nMain Server Engine"]
        srvB2["Offline Message Store\nPersistent Storage"]
        srvB3["Roster Database\nContact Lists"]
    end

    subgraph ClientB["Client B Domain (bobsserver.org)"]
        direction TB
        clientB1["Bob's Desktop Client\nresource=desktop"]
        clientB2["Bob's Tablet Client\nresource=tablet"]
    end

    clientA1 -->|C2S Port 5222| srvA1
    clientA2 -->|C2S Port 5222| srvA1
    clientA3 -->|WebSocket 5280| srvA1
    srvA1 <--> srvA2
    srvA1 <--> srvA3
    srvA1 <--> srvA4
    srvA1 -->|net2| net1
    net1 -->|net2| srvB1
    srvB1 <--> srvB2
    srvB1 <--> srvB3
    srvB1 -->|C2S Port 5222| clientB1
    srvB1 -->|C2S Port 5222| clientB2
```

## 4.2 XMPP Stanza Type Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["XMPP Stanza Required"] --> question{"What is the\ncommunication pattern?"}
    question -->|One-way push\nNo response needed| msg["MESSAGE STANZA\ntype=message\nAttributes: to, from, type\nChildren: body, subject, thread"]
    question -->|Broadcast online status\nto subscribers| pres["PRESENCE STANZA\ntype=presence\nAttributes: to, from, type\nChildren: show, status, priority"]
    question -->|Request-response\nGet/Set operation| iq["IQ STANZA\ntype=iq\nAttributes: to, from, type, id\nChildren: query, bind, roster, etc."]
    msg --> msgcheck{"Message subtype?"}
    msgcheck -->|chat| msgchat["Real-time chat message"]
    msgcheck -->|groupchat| msggrp["Multi-user chat room"]
    msgcheck -->|headline| msghead["Alert / notification"]
    msgcheck -->|normal| msgnorm["Standard IM message"]
    msgcheck -->|error| msgerr["Error reply"]
    pres --> prescheck{"Presence subtype?"}
    prescheck -->|available| presavail["Online announcement"]
    prescheck -->|unavailable| presunavail["Offline notification"]
    prescheck -->|subscribe| pressub["Subscription request"]
    iq --> iqcheck{"IQ operation type?"}
    iqcheck -->|get| iqget["Retrieve data"]
    iqcheck -->|set| iqset["Update data"]
    iqcheck -->|result| iqres["Successful response"]
    iqcheck -->|error| iqerr["Error response"]
```

## 4.3 XMPP Session Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    start([Client Initiates\nTCP Connection]) --> tcp["Step 1: TCP Handshake\nPort 5222"]
    tcp --> stream1["Step 2: Open Stream\nstream:stream tags exchanged"]
    stream1 --> tls["Step 3: STARTTLS\nTLS Upgrade on Port 5222"]
    tls --> sasl["Step 4: SASL Auth\nSCRAM-SHA-1 or PLAIN"]
    sasl --> bind["Step 5: Resource Binding\nIQ set bind"]
    bind --> session["Step 6: Session Establish\nIQ set session"]
    session --> presence["Step 7: Send Presence\nOnline Announcement"]
    presence --> active(["Step 8: ACTIVE COMMUNICATION\nmessage, presence, iq stanzas"])
    active --> close1["Step 9a: Stream Closure\n</stream:stream>"]
    active --> error1["Step 9b: Error Occurs\nError Stanza Generated"]
    close1 --> end1([TCP Connection\nTerminated])
    error1 --> end1
```
