# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define XMPP. List its key features.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

**XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol)** is an open, XML-based application layer communication protocol standardized by the IETF (RFC 6120, 6121, 6122) for real-time messaging, presence information, and contact list management.

**Key Features:**
1. **Decentralized architecture** with server-to-server federation
2. **Open standard** based on XML and free from royalties
3. **Secure** through native TLS and SASL mechanisms
4. **Extensible** via XEPs (XMPP Extension Protocols)
5. **Presence-aware** with built-in online status tracking
6. **Asynchronous** messaging with offline message storage

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Full 3 marks require: (1) Correct full-form expansion (1 mark), (2) Standardizing body mention (1 mark), (3) At least 4 key features listed (1 mark).

---

### Question A2: What is a JID in XMPP? Explain with an example.
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Jabber ID (JID)** is the unique identifier for any entity in the XMPP network. Its structure is:

$$\text{JID} = \text{node} \, \text{@} \, \text{domain} \, \text{/} \, \text{resource}$$

**Example:** `alice@chat.example.com/laptop`

- **Node:** `alice` (the username)
- **Domain:** `chat.example.com` (the XMPP server)
- **Resource:** `laptop` (specific client session)

**Bare JID:** `alice@chat.example.com` (used in contact lists)
**Full JID:** `alice@chat.example.com/laptop` (used for routing to a specific session)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Full 3 marks require: (1) Full-form of JID (1 mark), (2) Structure formula with all three components (1 mark), (3) Example with explanation of bare vs. full JID (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE 1]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Part (a): Explain the architecture of XMPP with a neat block diagram. Discuss Client-to-Server and Server-to-Server communication with appropriate port numbers. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**XMPP Architecture:**

XMPP follows a **Client-Server architecture with Server-to-Server (S2S) federation**. It consists of three main tiers:

**Tier 1: XMPP Client**
- The end-user application that initiates/receives XMPP stanzas
- Maintains a persistent TCP connection to the server
- Examples: Pidgin, Conversations, Gajim

**Tier 2: XMPP Server**
- Central node managing sessions, routing, presence, and offline storage
- Examples: ejabberd, Openfire, Prosody, Tigase

**Tier 3: Server-to-Server (S2S) Link**
- Direct connection between two XMPP servers for federation
- Uses DNS SRV records for server discovery

**Client-to-Server (C2S) Communication:**
- Default Port: **5222** (unencrypted, upgraded to TLS via STARTTLS)
- The client opens a TCP connection, negotiates the stream, performs SASL authentication, binds a resource, and starts the session
- All communication is XML-based over a persistent connection

**Server-to-Server (S2S) Communication:**
- Default Port: **5269** (encrypted via TLS)
- Enables **federation** — users on different XMPP servers can communicate
- Uses **Server Dialback** (RFC 6120) for domain verification and anti-spoofing
- DNS SRV record lookup: `_xmpp-server._tcp.targetdomain.com`

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):**

```
Client A → [Server A:5222] → [S2S TLS:5269] → [Server B:5222] → Client B
```

**Conclusion:** XMPP's federated architecture eliminates single points of failure and enables cross-organizational communication without a central authority.

---

**Part (b): List and explain the three types of XMPP stanzas with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

XMPP defines three top-level stanza types, all of which are child elements of the root `<stream:stream>` element.

**Stanza 1: `<message/>` Stanza**
- **Purpose:** One-way push of data (chat, alerts, notifications)
- **Subtypes:** `chat`, `error`, `groupchat`, `headline`, `normal`
- **Example:**

```xml
<message from='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'
         to='bob@chat.example.com/mobile'
         type='chat'>
  <body>Hello Bob!</body>
</message>
```

**Stanza 2: `<presence/>` Stanza**
- **Purpose:** Broadcast online status to subscribed entities
- **Subtypes:** `available` (default), `unavailable`, `subscribe`, `subscribed`, `unsubscribe`, `unsubscribed`
- **Example:**

```xml
<presence from='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'>
  <show>chat</show>
  <status>Available for chat</status>
  <priority>10</priority>
</presence>
```

**Stanza 3: `<iq/>` Stanza (Info/Query)**
- **Purpose:** Request-response transaction (similar to HTTP GET/POST)
- **Types:** `get`, `set`, `result`, `error`
- **MUST** receive exactly one response
- **Example — Client Request:**

```xml
<iq from='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'
    type='get'
    id='roster1'>
  <query xmlns='jabber:iq:roster'/>
</iq>
```

**Example — Server Response:**

```xml
<iq to='alice@chat.example.com/laptop'
    type='result'
    id='roster1'>
  <query xmlns='jabber:iq:roster'>
    <item jid='bob@chat.example.com' name='Bob'/>
  </query>
</iq>
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | `<message/>` | `<presence/>` | `<iq/>` |
|---------|--------------|---------------|---------|
| Pattern | Push | Broadcast | Request-Response |
| Response Required | No | No | **Yes (exactly one)** |
| Primary Use | Chat | Status | Data operations |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **DO NOT** confuse IQ stanzas with HTTP — they are NOT stateless. An IQ transaction is a stateful request-response pair identified by the `id` attribute.
> 2. **Memorize the exact `type` values** for each stanza — examiners frequently test `unsubscribe` vs `unsubscribed` and `subscribe` vs `subscribed`.
> 3. **Always include the `id` attribute** in IQ stanzas in your exam answers — it is mandatory for correlation.
> 4. Students lose 1-2 marks by writing stanzas **without the proper namespace declaration** (`xmlns='jabber:client'` or specific XEP namespace like `jabber:iq:roster`).
> 5. **Do not forget** that `<message/>` with `type="chat"` implies real-time delivery, while `type="normal"` implies store-and-forward.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE 2]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Part (a): What is a JID in XMPP? Explain its structure and differentiate between Bare JID and Full JID. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A **Jabber ID (JID)** is the unique identifier for any XMPP entity (user, server, room, component) within the XMPP network, as defined in **RFC 6122**.

**Structure:**

$$\text{JID} = \langle \text{node} \rangle \, \text{@} \, \langle \text{domain} \rangle \, \text{/} \, \langle \text{resource} \rangle$$

| Component | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Node** | Local username (optional for rooms/channels) | `alice` |
| **Domain** | Server hostname (mandatory, routable via DNS) | `chat.example.com` |
| **Resource** | Specific session/client identifier (optional) | `laptop` |

**Worked Example:**
- Full JID: `alice@chat.example.com/laptop`
- Bare JID: `alice@chat.example.com`

**Differentiation — Bare JID vs. Full JID:**

| Aspect | Bare JID | Full JID |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Format** | `node@domain` | `node@domain/resource` |
| **Resource** | Absent | Present |
| **Uniqueness** | Identifies the account | Identifies a specific session |
| **Use Case** | Contact lists (rosters), subscription management | Message routing, presence delivery |
| **Example** | `bob@chat.example.com` | `bob@chat.example.com/mobile` |
| **Persistence** | Permanent for the account | Temporary for each session |

**Practical Implication:** When a user adds a contact, they use the **Bare JID**. When a server routes a message, it uses the **Full JID** to deliver to the correct active session. If the user is online on two devices, the message is delivered to the one with the **highest positive priority** (per RFC 6121 § 8).

---

**Part (b): Explain the XMPP session lifecycle. Describe the STARTTLS and SASL mechanisms used during session establishment. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The XMPP session lifecycle consists of **six distinct phases** as defined in RFC 6120.

**Phase 1: TCP Connection Establishment**
- The client opens a TCP connection to the server on **port 5222**
- Three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) completes

**Phase 2: Stream Negotiation**
- Both parties exchange `<stream:stream>` opening tags
- Attributes: `from`, `to`, `id`, `version='1.0'`, `xmlns='jabber:client'`
- Server advertises supported features: `<starttls/>`, `<mechanisms/>`, `<bind/>`

**Phase 3: STARTTLS (TLS Upgrade)**
- Client sends `<starttls xmlns='urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:xmpp-tls'/>` request
- Server responds with `<proceed/>`
- TLS handshake occurs on the existing TCP connection (port **5222**)
- All subsequent communication is encrypted using TLS 1.2 or 1.3
- After TLS upgrade, a new stream is opened (old stream is discarded)

**Phase 4: SASL Authentication**
- Server advertises supported SASL mechanisms: `SCRAM-SHA-1`, `SCRAM-SHA-256`, `PLAIN`, `DIGEST-MD5`
- **PLAIN:** Username and password sent in plain text (only safe over TLS)
- **SCRAM-SHA-1:** Challenge-response with salted hash; credentials never sent over the wire
- Client selects mechanism, exchanges challenges, server verifies credentials
- On success, server responds with `<success/>` element

**Phase 5: Resource Binding**
- Client sends `<iq type='set' id='bind1'><bind xmlns='urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:xmpp-bind'><resource>laptop</resource></bind></iq>`
- Server responds with the assigned full JID

**Phase 6: Session Establishment and Active Communication**
- Client sends `<iq type='set'><session xmlns='urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:xmpp-session'/></iq>`
- Server acknowledges with `<iq type='result'/>`
- Client sends initial `<presence/>` stanza to announce online status
- **Active phase:** Exchange of `<message/>`, `<presence/>`, `<iq/>` stanzas

**Stream Closure:**
- Either party sends `</stream:stream>` to gracefully close
- TCP connection is terminated

**Security Summary Table:**

| Mechanism | Purpose | Port | RFC Reference |
|-----------|---------|------|---------------|
| **STARTTLS** | Encryption of XML stream | 5222 | RFC 6120 § 5 |
| **SASL (SCRAM-SHA-1)** | Authentication | 5222 | RFC 5802 + RFC 6120 § 6 |
| **Server Dialback** | S2S domain verification | 5269 | RFC 6120 § 10.3 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse port 5222 with port 5269.** Port 5222 is C2S (Client-to-Server), and port 5269 is S2S (Server-to-Server).
> 2. **STARTTLS is NOT a separate port** — it is an in-band upgrade on port 5222. Students often write "TLS uses port 5223" which is the deprecated SSL port.
> 3. **SASL authentication requires TLS first** if using PLAIN mechanism; otherwise, credentials would be transmitted in cleartext.
> 4. **Resource binding is mandatory** — without it, the server cannot route messages to the correct client session.
> 5. Students commonly **omit the `xmlns` namespace declaration** in IQ child elements, losing 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — XMPP**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **XMPP** = Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol (RFC 6120, 6121, 6122)
- [ ] Originally called **Jabber**, created by Jeremie Miller in 1999
- [ ] XML-based, open standard, IETF-ratified
- [ ] Uses **Client-Server** architecture with **S2S federation**

### Critical Port Numbers (Memorize!)
- [ ] **5222** = C2S (Client-to-Server) with STARTTLS
- [ ] **5269** = S2S (Server-to-Server) with TLS
- [ ] **5280 / 80** = BOSH (HTTP fallback)
- [ ] **5280 / 443** = WebSocket (browser-based)

### JID Structure
- [ ] JID = `node@domain/resource`
- [ ] **Domain** is the only mandatory component
- [ ] **Bare JID** = `node@domain` (for rosters)
- [ ] **Full JID** = `node@domain/resource` (for routing)

### Three Stanza Types
- [ ] **`<message/>`** = Push, no response required
- [ ] **`<presence/>`** = Broadcast online status
- [ ] **`<iq/>`** = Request-Response, MUST get exactly one response

### Subtype Values to Memorize
- [ ] **Message types:** chat, error, groupchat, headline, normal
- [ ] **Presence types:** available, unavailable, subscribe, subscribed, unsubscribe, unsubscribed
- [ ] **IQ types:** get, set, result, error

### Security Mechanisms
- [ ] **STARTTLS** for encryption (in-band upgrade on port 5222)
- [ ] **SASL** for authentication (SCRAM-SHA-1 preferred)
- [ ] **Server Dialback** for S2S domain verification (port 5269)

### XMPP for IoT — Key Use Cases
- [ ] Smart home hubs with presence-aware device tracking
- [ ] Industrial M2M with federated gateways
- [ ] Healthcare telemetry with end-to-end encryption
- [ ] Smart agriculture with sensor pub/sub via **XEP-0060**
- [ ] Cross-domain IoT messaging without central broker

### Common KTU Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] ❌ Confusing port 5222 (C2S) with port 5269 (S2S)
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting `xmlns` namespace in IQ child elements
- [ ] ❌ Treating IQ stanzas as stateless (they are stateful request-response)
- [ ] ❌ Writing PLAIN SASL without mentioning TLS prerequisite
- [ ] ❌ Confusing BOSH (HTTP long-polling) with WebSocket
- [ ] ❌ Omitting the `id` attribute in IQ stanzas in diagrams

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** For stanza types, remember **"MPI"** = **M**essage-**P**ush-**I**nfoQuery. Or visualize: **Message = Mail, Presence = Phone Status, IQ = HTTP Request.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AMQP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** AMQP ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Advanced Message Queuing Protocol (AMQP)** is an open-standard **application layer** messaging protocol designed for **message-oriented middleware (MOM)**, enabling reliable, secure, and interoperable communication between distributed applications, IoT brokers, and cloud services. It was originally developed by **JPMorgan Chase** in 2003 and standardized through **OASIS** (Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards). The current stable specification is **AMQP 1.0 (ISO/IEC 19464)**, which is wire-protocol compatible and supports peer-to-peer messaging with brokered and brokerless topologies. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Postal System > [!NOTE] > **Think of AMQP as a highly automated, programmable postal system for software.** Imagine you want to send a letter to a friend, but instead of writing their home address, you write a **category** (e.g., "Bills", "Greetings", "Urgent"). The postal system reads the category and decides: - Which delivery truck (queue) to put the letter in - Whether to copy the letter to multiple recipients (topic-based routing) - Whether to drop the letter if no one is listening (discard policy) In this analogy: - **Sender (Publisher)** → IoT sensor sending data - **Letter (Message)** → Data payload - **Post Office (Broker/RabbitMQ)** → AMQP broker - **Sorting Machine (Exchange)** → Routes the message - **Mailbox (Queue)** → Where messages wait for consumers - **Recipient (Consumer)** → Application processing the data ### 1.3 Core Salient Features - **Platform-Neutral & Wire-Level Standardized:** Works across any programming language and OS. - **Reliability Guarantees:** Supports **at-most-once**, **at-least-once**, and **exactly-once** delivery semantics. - **Asynchronous Messaging:** Decouples producers from consumers (no need to be online simultaneously). - **Message Acknowledgement:** Confirms receipt to avoid data loss. - **Transaction Support:** Atomic multi-message operations across queues. - **Security:** Supports **TLS/SSL** encryption and **SASL** authentication. > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Fact:** In the KTU 2024 syllabus, AMQP is listed alongside **MQTT** and **CoAP** as a key IoT application/protocol. AMQP is the "heavy-duty" choice — used when **enterprise-grade reliability** and **message queuing** are required, unlike MQTT which is optimized for lightweight sensor telemetry. --- ### 1.4 Where AMQP Sits in the IoT Stack | Layer | Protocol(s) | | :--- | :--- | | Application Layer | **AMQP**, MQTT, CoAP, HTTP | | Transport Layer | TCP (AMQP uses **TCP port 5672** by default; TLS uses **5671**) | | Network Layer | IP | | Physical / Link | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, LoRa, Cellular | --- ### 1.5 AMQP vs MQTT — The Common Confusion | Property | AMQP | MQTT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Primary Use | Enterprise messaging, IoT cloud backends | Lightweight IoT telemetry | | Transport | TCP | TCP (also WebSockets) | | Message Pattern | **Pub/Sub + Queues + Routing** | Pub/Sub with Topics | | Header Size | Larger (~8 bytes minimum) | Tiny (2 bytes fixed) | | Reliability | **Transactions, Acknowledgements** | QoS 0, 1, 2 | | Standard Body | OASIS, ISO/IEC 19464 | OASIS | | Broker Examples | RabbitMQ, Apache Qpid, ActiveMQ | Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 AMQP Architecture — The Big Picture AMQP's architecture is built around **three primary actors** that interact through the broker: 1. **Producer (Publisher):** Application that creates and sends messages. 2. **Consumer (Subscriber):** Application that receives and processes messages. 3. **Broker (Message Broker):** The middleware (e.g., RabbitMQ) that handles exchange, routing, queuing, and delivery. > [!NOTE] > In **AMQP 1.0**, the protocol supports **direct peer-to-peer** communication without a broker, but in **AMQP 0-9-1** (most widely deployed), the broker is mandatory. --- ### 2.2 AMQP Messaging Model — Step-by-Step Breakdown The messaging model consists of **three core building blocks**: #### 2.2.1 Exchanges The **exchange** is the routing engine. Producers **never send directly to a queue**; they send to an exchange. **Four Standard Exchange Types:** | Exchange Type | Routing Logic | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Direct** | Message routed by exact `routing_key` match | Task queues, RPC | | **Fanout** | Ignores routing key; broadcasts to all bound queues | Broadcast notifications | | **Topic** | Pattern matching with wildcards `*` (one word) and `#` (many) | Pub/Sub with categories | | **Headers** | Routes based on message header attributes | Complex conditional routing | > [!TIP] > The **default exchange** (nameless, `""`) is a pre-declared direct exchange that routes any message to the queue whose name equals the routing key. #### 2.2.2 Queues Queues are **buffers** that store messages until consumed. - Messages in a queue are ordered (FIFO by default). - Queues have properties: `durable`, `exclusive`, `auto-delete`, `arguments`. - Messages can be persistent (stored on disk) or transient. #### 2.2.3 Bindings A **binding** is the **rule** linking an exchange to a queue. For direct/topic exchanges, this rule includes a `routing_key`. For fanout exchanges, the routing key is ignored. --- ### 2.3 AMQP Message Lifecycle 1. **Producer → Exchange:** Producer publishes a message to an exchange with a routing key. 2. **Exchange → Queue:** The exchange evaluates the message against all bindings and forwards to matching queues. 3. **Queue → Consumer:** Consumer subscribes to the queue; broker delivers message. 4. **Acknowledgement:** Consumer sends `basic.ack` on successful processing or `basic.nack` on failure. 5. **Removal/Dead-Letter:** On acknowledgement, message is removed. On failure, it may be re-queued or sent to a **Dead Letter Exchange (DLX)**. --- ### 2.4 AMQP Frame Format (High-Yield for KTU) Communication occurs in **frames**. The smallest unit is the frame. AMQP frames fall into these categories: | Frame Type | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | | **Method Frame** | Carries RPC-like operations (e.g., `basic.publish`, `queue.declare`) | | **Content Header Frame** | Carries message properties and size | | **Content Body Frame** | Carries the actual message payload (can span multiple frames) | | **Heartbeat Frame** | Keeps the connection alive (zero-payload, periodic) | A complete AMQP message = **Method Frame + Content Header Frame + Content Body Frame(s)** --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula / Symbol Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > AMQP is conceptual, not formula-heavy, but the following symbols and codes are **high-yield** for KTU exams. | Symbol / Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | `routing_key` | String used by exchange for routing (max 255 bytes) | | `exchange_name` | Identifier of the exchange | | `queue_name` | Identifier of the queue | | `binding_key` | Pattern defined on the binding | | `delivery_tag` | Monotonic counter identifying delivery on a channel | | `QoS prefetch_count` | Max unacknowledged messages per consumer | | `content_type` | MIME type of payload (e.g., `application/json`) | | `message_id` | Unique ID for a message (UUID recommended) | | `correlation_id` | Links request/response in RPC | | `reply_to` | Queue name to reply to | | `expiration` | Message TTL in milliseconds | | `priority` | Integer 0–9 for queue prioritization | | `persistent` | Boolean; if true, message is written to disk | > [!NOTE] > **Heartbeat Timeout Equation:** > > $$\text{Missed Heartbeats Allowed} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Negotiated Timeout (seconds)}}{2} \right\rfloor$$ > > If a peer fails to send a heartbeat within **2 × heartbeat-interval**, the broker assumes the connection is dead and closes it. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking & Financial Services:** JPMorgan's original use case — transactional integrity. - **IoT Cloud Backends:** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub use AMQP for device-to-cloud telemetry. - **Microservices:** Service-to-service asynchronous communication (replaces REST in high-throughput systems). - **Industrial Automation:** SCADA systems use AMQP for reliable machine-to-machine (M2M) messaging. - **Log Aggregation:** Centralized logging systems (e.g., Logstash) use AMQP to ship logs. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 AMQP 0-9-1 Frame Structure — Exhaustive Derivation > [!NOTE] > The frame is the **fundamental wire unit** in AMQP. Understanding its binary layout is essential for KTU Part B questions on "Explain AMQP frame format." #### 3.1.1 The Generic Frame Layout Every AMQP frame follows this binary structure on the wire: $$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{type}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{channel}} + \underbrace{4}_{\text{size}} + \underbrace{\text{size}-7}_{\text{payload}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{end-byte}}$$ Where: - **type (1 byte):** A single ASCII octet identifying the frame kind. - **channel (2 bytes):** A 16-bit unsigned integer for the channel ID (used for multiplexing over a single TCP connection). - **size (4 bytes):** A 32-bit unsigned integer indicating the **total payload size in bytes** (excluding the 7-byte header and 1-byte frame-end). - **payload (variable):** The frame-specific data. - **end-byte (1 byte):** Always `0xCE` (the constant `FRAME_END`). #### 3.1.2 The Size Field — Boundary Condition $$\text{size}_{\min} = 0 \quad ; \quad \text{size}_{\max} = 2^{32} - 1 \text{ bytes}$$ The size field **does not** include: - The 1-byte type field - The 2-byte channel field - The 4-byte size field itself - The 1-byte `FRAME_END` (0xCE) terminator So a frame's **total wire bytes** are: $$\text{WireBytes} = 1 + 2 + 4 + \text{size} + 1 = \text{size} + 8$$ #### 3.1.3 Frame Type Octets (KTU Memorization Table) | Type Octet (hex) | ASCII | Frame Kind | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `0x01` | `A` | Method Frame | | `0x02` | `B` | Content Header Frame | | `0x03` | `C` | Content Body Frame | | `0x04` | `D` | Heartbeat Frame | | `0x05` | `E` | SASL Mechanism | | `0x06` | `F` | SASL Authentication | | `0x07` | `G` | SASL Challenge/Response | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frame-end marker (FRAME_END)** = `0xCE`. This appears at the end of every frame and is **mandatory**. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Content Header Frame Derivation The **Content Header Frame** carries message metadata. Its layout: $$\text{Header} = \underbrace{2}_{\text{class-id}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{weight}} + \underbrace{8}_{\text{body-size}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{property-flags}} + \underbrace{\text{Properties}}_{\text{variable}}$$ Where: - **class-id (2 bytes):** Identifies the message class (e.g., `60` for `basic`). - **weight (2 bytes):** Always `0` (reserved). - **body-size (8 bytes):** The total size of the content body in bytes. - **property-flags (2 bytes):** A bitmask indicating which properties are present. - **Properties (variable):** The actual property list (content-type, message-id, etc.). #### Property Flag Bitmask Each bit in the 16-bit property-flags indicates presence: $$\text{flags} = \sum_{i=0}^{15} b_i \cdot 2^i$$ Where $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$. A `1` means the corresponding property is present in the variable payload. Common bits (KTU key): | Bit | Property | | :--- | :--- | | 15 | `content-type` | | 14 | `content-encoding` | | 13 | `headers` | | 12 | `delivery-mode` (persistent) | | 11 | `priority` | | 10 | `correlation-id` | | 9 | `reply-to` | | 8 | `expiration` | | 7 | `message-id` | | 6 | `timestamp` | | 5 | `type` | | 4 | `user-id` | | 3 | `app-id` | | 2 | `cluster-id` | --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Message Delivery Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU-style):** A producer sends 5 messages, each 2048 bytes, to a queue. AMQP frames impose overhead: 7-byte header + 1-byte end per frame. The Content Header adds 16 bytes. Calculate total bytes on the wire. **Step 1 — Per Message Overhead (Frame Level)** Each message produces: - 1 × Method Frame: overhead = 8 bytes (header+end) - 1 × Content Header Frame: overhead = 8 bytes + 16 bytes (payload) = 24 bytes - 1 × Content Body Frame: overhead = 8 bytes + 2048 bytes (payload) = 2056 bytes **Step 2 — Per Message Total** $$ \text{Bytes}_{\text{per msg}} = 8 + 24 + 2056 = 2088 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3 — Total for 5 Messages** $$ \text{Bytes}_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 2088 = 10440 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to KB** $$ \text{Bytes}_{\text{total}} = 10440 \div 1024 \approx 10.195 \text{ KB} $$ --- ### 3.4 Method Frame — Class & Method ID Derivation A **Method Frame** carries an RPC request. Its layout: $$\text{Method} = \underbrace{2}_{\text{class-id}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{method-id}} + \underbrace{\text{Arguments}}_{\text{variable}}$$ Example: `basic.publish` has: - **class-id** = `60` (basic class) - **method-id** = `40` (`basic.publish` method) > [!TIP] > **Naming Convention:** `basic.publish` is the dot-notation form. On the wire, it is encoded as `60, 40`. The arguments follow AMQP **type encoding**: - Long strings: 4-byte length + UTF-8 bytes. - Short strings: 1-byte length + ASCII bytes. - Integers: Network byte order (big-endian). ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 AMQP High-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Producer / IoT Sensor] -->|Publish Message| EX[Exchange] EX -->|Routing Key Match 1| Q1[Queue 1] EX -->|Routing Key Match 2| Q2[Queue 2] EX -->|Fanout Broadcast| Q3[Queue 3] Q1 -->|Push / Pull| C1[Consumer 1] Q2 -->|Push / Pull| C2[Consumer 2] Q3 -->|Push / Pull| C3[Consumer 3] C1 -->|basic.ack| Q1 C2 -->|basic.nack| Q2 Q2 -.->|Requeue or DLX| DLX[Dead Letter Exchange] ``` --- ### 4.2 Exchange-to-Queue Routing Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Message Arrives at Exchange] --> CheckType{Exchange Type?} CheckType -->|Direct| DirectMatch[Match routing_key exactly
with binding key] CheckType -->|Fanout| FanoutAll[Ignore routing_key
Send to ALL bound queues] CheckType -->|Topic| TopicMatch[Pattern match with * and #
wildcards] CheckType -->|Headers| HeaderMatch[Match message header
attributes] DirectMatch --> Route[Route to matching queue] FanoutAll --> Route TopicMatch --> Route HeaderMatch --> Route Route --> Deliver[Broker delivers to Consumer] ``` --- ### 4.3 AMQP Frame Transmission Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Producer participant Broker participant Consumer Producer->>Broker: 1. Connection.Start (Method Frame) Broker-->>Producer: 2. Connection.StartOk Producer->>Broker: 3. Connection.Tune + Open Producer->>Broker: 4. Channel.Open Producer->>Broker: 5. Method Frame: basic.publish Producer->>Broker: 6. Content Header Frame Producer->>Broker: 7. Content Body Frame (Payload) Broker->>Consumer: 8. basic.deliver (Method) Broker->>Consumer: 9. Content Header + Body Consumer->>Broker: 10. basic.ack Note over Producer,Broker: Periodic Heartbeat frames (0x04) ``` --- ### 4.4 AMQP Connection / Channel / Queue Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD TCP[TCP Connection - Port 5672] --> CH1[Channel 1] TCP --> CH2[Channel 2] TCP --> CH3[Channel 3] CH1 --> EX1[Exchange] EX1 --> Q1[Queue A] EX1 --> Q2[Queue B] CH2 --> EX2[Direct Exchange] EX2 --> Q3[Queue C] CH3 --> EX3[Topic Exchange] EX3 --> Q4[Queue D] EX3 --> Q5[Queue E] ``` > [!NOTE] > A **single TCP connection** can multiplex **multiple channels** — this is AMQP's efficiency trick. Each channel is an independent logical stream, avoiding the overhead of opening new TCP connections per logical flow. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define AMQP. List any four salient features of AMQP.** **Model Answer:** > **Advanced Message Queuing Protocol (AMQP)** is an open-standard, **application-layer** messaging protocol used for message-oriented middleware. It provides reliable, asynchronous, and interoperable communication between distributed systems and IoT applications. It was standardized by **OASIS** and is currently ratified as **ISO/IEC 19464**. **Salient Features (any four):** 1. **Reliable delivery** with three acknowledgement modes: at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once. 2. **Asynchronous messaging** — producers and consumers are decoupled in time. 3. **Topic-based and queue-based routing** through exchanges (direct, fanout, topic, headers). 4. **Transaction support** — atomic multi-message operations. 5. **Security** via TLS/SSL and SASL authentication. 6. **Platform and language independent** — wire-level standardized. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between the four AMQP exchange types: Direct, Fanout, Topic, and Headers.** **Model Answer:** | Exchange Type | Routing Logic | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Direct** | Routes based on exact match between message's routing key and binding key | Task distribution, RPC | | **Fanout** | Ignores routing key; copies message to all bound queues | Broadcast notifications | | **Topic** | Uses pattern matching with wildcards `*` (one word) and `#` (multiple words) | Categorized pub/sub | | **Headers** | Routes based on `x-match` and message header attributes | Complex conditional routing | --- --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a) Explain the AMQP messaging architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of exchanges, queues, and bindings.** (7 Marks) **(b) Describe the AMQP frame format. Explain Method Frame, Content Header Frame, and Content Body Frame with their structure.** (7 Marks) --- ### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) AMQP Messaging Architecture (7 Marks)** The AMQP messaging architecture consists of **three logical layers**: 1. **Producer / Publisher Layer:** The application that creates messages and publishes them to the broker. In IoT, this could be a sensor publishing temperature readings. 2. **Broker Layer:** The middleware (e.g., RabbitMQ, Apache Qpid) that handles message storage and routing. It contains: - **Exchanges:** The routing decision-makers. They receive messages from producers and decide which queues to forward them to. - **Queues:** Buffers that store messages until consumers are ready to process them. Queues follow FIFO order by default and can be `durable`, `exclusive`, or `auto-delete`. - **Bindings:** The rules linking exchanges to queues. For direct and topic exchanges, a binding includes a `routing_key` pattern. 3. **Consumer / Subscriber Layer:** The application that subscribes to one or more queues and processes incoming messages. **Working:** - Producer sends message to an exchange (not directly to a queue). - Exchange evaluates the message against bindings. - Based on exchange type (direct/fanout/topic/headers), message is routed to one or more queues. - Consumer receives the message, processes it, and sends acknowledgement (`basic.ack`). - If processing fails, consumer sends `basic.nack`, and the broker may requeue the message or send it to a **Dead Letter Exchange (DLX)**. [Refer to Diagram in Section 4.1 for visual representation.] **(b) AMQP Frame Format (7 Marks)** Communication in AMQP happens in **frames** — discrete units transmitted over TCP. The generic frame structure is: $$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{1B}_{\text{type}} + \underbrace{2B}_{\text{channel}} + \underbrace{4B}_{\text{size}} + \underbrace{\text{size}-7B}_{\text{payload}} + \underbrace{1B}_{\text{frame-end}}$$ **Total wire size** = `size + 8` bytes. **Three Major Frame Types:** 1. **Method Frame (Type `0x01`, ASCII 'A'):** - Carries RPC-like operations. - Structure: `class-id (2 bytes) + method-id (2 bytes) + arguments (variable)`. - Example: `basic.publish` is encoded as class 60, method 40. 2. **Content Header Frame (Type `0x02`, ASCII 'B'):** - Carries message metadata. - Structure: `class-id (2B) + weight (2B) + body-size (8B) + property-flags (2B) + properties (variable)`. - Property flags are a 16-bit bitmask indicating which optional fields are present. 3. **Content Body Frame (Type `0x03`, ASCII 'C'):** - Carries the actual message payload. - A large payload may span multiple content body frames. 4. **Heartbeat Frame (Type `0x04`):** - Zero-payload frame sent at negotiated intervals to keep the connection alive. The **frame-end byte** is always `0xCE` and marks the boundary of each frame. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a) Compare AMQP with MQTT. Discuss the relative advantages and disadvantages of each in IoT contexts.** (7 Marks) **(b) Explain the concept of Dead Letter Exchange (DLX) and message acknowledgement mechanisms in AMQP.** (7 Marks) --- ### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) AMQP vs MQTT Comparison (7 Marks)** | Parameter | AMQP | MQTT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Origin** | JPMorgan Chase, 2003 | IBM, 1999 | | **Standardization** | OASIS, ISO/IEC 19464 | OASIS | | **Transport** | TCP (port 5672) | TCP (port 1883) / WebSockets | | **Message Pattern** | Pub/Sub + Queues + Routing (1:1, 1:N, N:1) | Pure Pub/Sub (Topics) | | **Routing** | Via Exchanges (Direct, Fanout, Topic, Headers) | Via Topic hierarchy with `/` and `+`/`#` wildcards | | **Reliability** | At-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once + Transactions | QoS 0, 1, 2 | | **Payload Size** | Larger headers (~8 bytes min) | Very small (2-byte fixed header) | | **Use Case** | Enterprise middleware, financial systems, cloud backends | Lightweight IoT telemetry, mobile | | **Broker Examples** | RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ, Qpid | Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX | **Advantages of AMQP:** richer routing, transactional support, better for high-throughput enterprise systems. **Advantages of MQTT:** lower bandwidth, better for constrained devices, simpler topic model. **IoT Recommendation:** Use **MQTT** for constrained sensor-to-broker links; use **AMQP** for broker-to-cloud or device-to-server enterprise integration. **(b) Dead Letter Exchange & Acknowledgement (7 Marks)** **Message Acknowledgements:** When a message is delivered to a consumer, the broker marks it as **"unacknowledged"**. The consumer must respond with one of: - **`basic.ack`** → Message successfully processed; broker removes it from queue. - **`basic.nack`** → Message failed; broker may requeue or discard. - **`basic.reject`** → Single message reject; equivalent to nack with `requeue=false`. If the consumer disconnects without acknowledging, the broker **automatically requeues** the message for redelivery (at-least-once guarantee). **Dead Letter Exchange (DLX):** A DLX is a **special exchange** where messages are sent when: 1. The message is rejected with `requeue=false`. 2. The message's **TTL (Time-To-Live)** expires. 3. The queue exceeds its **maximum length**. **Mechanism:** $$\text{Message} \xrightarrow{\text{failure/TTL/overflow}} \text{DLX} \rightarrow \text{DLQ (Dead Letter Queue)}$$ DLQs enable operators to **inspect failed messages**, debug, and re-publish them after fixing the underlying issue — a critical feature for **reliable IoT data pipelines**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > > 1. **Frame-end byte `0xCE` is mandatory** — many students forget to mention it and lose 1 mark. > 2. **Do NOT confuse AMQP exchanges with MQTT topics** — exchanges are routing engines inside the broker; topics in MQTT are part of the topic hierarchy. > 3. **Always specify class-id and method-id** when describing a Method Frame — examiners explicitly look for these numbers. > 4. **Mention the port number** (`5672` for plain, `5671` for TLS) in AMQP questions — it is a high-yield "giveaway" mark. > 5. **Dead Letter Exchange (DLX)** is a common 3-mark sub-question — students often confuse it with a normal queue and lose marks. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - ✅ **AMQP** = Application-layer messaging protocol standardized by **OASIS / ISO/IEC 19464**. - ✅ Default port: **5672 (TCP)**, **5671 (TLS)**. - ✅ AMQP 0-9-1 uses **brokered architecture**; AMQP 1.0 supports **peer-to-peer** too. - ✅ Core components: **Exchanges**, **Queues**, **Bindings**. - ✅ Four exchange types: **Direct, Fanout, Topic, Headers**. - ✅ Wildcards in Topic exchanges: **`*` (one word)**, **`#` (zero or more words)**. - ✅ Three delivery semantics: **at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once**. - ✅ Frame structure: `type (1B) + channel (2B) + size (4B) + payload + end (1B = 0xCE)`. - ✅ Frame types: **Method (0x01)**, **Content Header (0x02)**, **Content Body (0x03)**, **Heartbeat (0x04)**. - ✅ **basic.publish** is encoded as class 60, method 40. - ✅ **Heartbeat timeout** = negotiation interval; connection drops after **2× interval** of silence. - ✅ **Acknowledgements:** `basic.ack`, `basic.nack`, `basic.reject`. - ✅ **Dead Letter Exchange (DLX)** receives messages on rejection, TTL expiry, or queue overflow. - ✅ AMQP supports **transactions** for atomic multi-message operations. - ✅ **Security:** TLS for transport encryption, SASL for authentication. - ✅ AMQP is **heavier than MQTT** — use it for **enterprise/IoT-cloud backends**, not for constrained sensors. - ✅ Common brokers: **RabbitMQ**, **Apache Qpid**, **ActiveMQ**. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Memory Trick — "E Q B C H F":** > **E**xchange, **Q**ueue, **B**inding, **C**lass-id, **H**eader, **F**rame-end (0xCE) — these six are the most-tested AMQP concepts in KTU exams.

topics.title: DDS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DDS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Distribution Service (DDS)** is a **vendor-independent**, **open-standard** middleware protocol and API standard for real-time, **data-centric publish-subscribe (DCPS)** communication, originally developed by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It is formally standardized as **OMG Document `formal/2014-09-01`** and is defined in the specifications:

- **DDS Specification v1.4** (core specification)
- **Real-Time Publish-Subscribe (RTPS) Wire Protocol DDS Interoperability Wire Protocol Specification v2.3**

DDS operates at the **Application Layer (OSI Layer 7)** and is designed to provide **deterministic, low-latency, high-reliability, peer-to-peer data exchange** between distributed nodes without requiring a centralized message broker. It uses a **decentralized architecture** based on a **Global Data Space (GDS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "DDS is an Object Management Group (OMG) machine-to-machine (M2M) standard that enables scalable, real-time, dependable, high-performance, and interoperable data exchange using a publish-subscribe pattern."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — The Smart Notice Board System in a University Campus:**

Imagine a **modern university notice board** with the following features:

1. **Each Notice Board Topic = A DDS Topic** — e.g., "Exam Timetable", "Canteen Menu", "Placement Updates". Any notice pinned belongs to one of these categories.

2. **Students & Staff = Publishers and Subscribers** — The "Examination Cell" (a Publisher) puts up exam timetables. The students (Subscribers) only look at that particular board because they are *interested* in exams.

3. **The Board Knows Who Wants What = QoS Policies** — A student may say: *"Only notify me if exam date is **urgent** (DEADLINE) and I must not miss it (**RELIABILITY = RELIABLE**)."* This is a **Quality of Service (QoS)** rule.

4. **No Central Postman** — There is no single "postman" running around the campus delivering notices. The board itself broadcasts to anyone listening. Likewise, DDS has **no central broker**; nodes discover each other automatically.

5. **Strict Access Control = Domain** — The "Mechanical Department Board" is not visible to the "CS Department Board". This separation in DDS is called a **Domain** (`Domain ID`).

This decentralized, smart-notice-board approach is exactly how DDS works for IoT devices, robots, vehicles, and industrial systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Snapshot for KTU Exam:**
> - **DCPS** = Data-Centric Publish-Subscribe
> - **RTPS** = Real-Time Publish-Subscribe (the wire protocol)
> - **QoS** = Quality of Service (rules governing delivery)
> - **GDS** = Global Data Space (the shared universe of topics)
> - **DDSI-RTPS** = The implementation of the RTPS protocol for interoperable discovery and data delivery.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Standard Body** | **Object Management Group (OMG)** |
| **Port Number** | **7400** (default RTPS discovery), **7401** (user-traffic) |
| **Transport** | **UDP (unreliable) and TCP (reliable)**, multicast & unicast |
| **Encoding** | **CDR (Common Data Representation)** — both CDR Encapsulation and Little-Endian / Big-Endian |
| **Discovery Protocol** | **Simple Endpoint Discovery (SEDP)**, **Participant Discovery Protocol (PDP)** |
| **Architecture Style** | **Decentralized, broker-less, peer-to-peer** |
| **Latency Target** | **Microseconds to low milliseconds** (real-time) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DDS Conceptual Model — DCPS

The **Data-Centric Publish-Subscribe (DCPS)** layer is the heart of DDS. Instead of nodes sending messages *to each other* (as in client-server), the system maintains a **Global Data Space** in which *data is the interface*. The data has an **identity (Topic name + key)** and is delivered based on **QoS contracts**.

### 2.1.1 Core DCPS Entities

The five principal DCPS entities are:

1. **DomainParticipant** — The entry point; represents an application's membership in a Domain. Every DDS application creates at least one `DomainParticipant`.
2. **Topic** — A named data-flow identifier (e.g., `Temperature`, `GPS_Position`). Topics bind Publishers and Subscribers *logically*, not by address.
3. **Publisher** — A container for `DataWriter` objects; responsible for *publishing* data.
4. **Subscriber** — A container for `DataReader` objects; responsible for *receiving* data.
5. **DataWriter / DataReader** — The actual endpoints that push/pull typed samples to/from the Global Data Space.

### 2.1.2 Operational Flow (Why & How)

**Why this layered design?** Because in mission-critical IoT (e.g., autonomous vehicles, medical robotics, smart grids), you cannot afford a single point of failure (a broker), and you need **fine-grained per-topic control** over delivery guarantees.

**How it works (step by step):**

- **Step 1 — Domain Creation:** An application instantiates a `DomainParticipant` with a numeric `Domain ID` (0 to 230). Only participants in the *same* Domain can communicate.
- **Step 2 — Topic Definition:** A `Topic` (with a name, type, and optional key) is defined inside the participant. The type is registered using **IDL (Interface Definition Language)**.
- **Step 3 — Publisher & DataWriter Setup:** A `Publisher` is created. Inside it, a `DataWriter<T>` is created and bound to the Topic. The Writer is configured with **QoS policies**.
- **Step 4 — Subscriber & DataReader Setup:** A `Subscriber` is created. Inside it, a `DataReader<T>` is created on the same Topic, also with its own QoS contract.
- **Step 5 — Automatic Discovery:** Using the **RTPS protocol**, endpoints announce themselves via **PDP (Participant Discovery Protocol)** and **SEDP (Simple Endpoint Discovery Protocol)**. Matching is automatic.
- **Step 6 — Data Exchange:** The Writer calls `write(sample)`. The DDS middleware guarantees delivery according to the negotiated QoS (e.g., `RELIABLE`, `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`, `KEEP_LAST_10`).
- **Step 7 — Data Arrival:** The Reader receives the data in its **Listener** callback or polls via `take()`.

## 2.2 The RTPS Wire Protocol

**RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe)** is the **interoperability wire protocol** that defines how DDS nodes talk to each other over UDP/IP.

### 2.2.1 RTPS Message Structure

An RTPS message has:

$$\text{RTPS Message} = \{\text{RTPS Header}\} \cup \{\text{Submessage}_1, \text{Submessage}_2, \ldots, \text{Submessage}_n\}$$

Where each `Submessage` can be one of:

| Submessage ID (Hex) | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `0x01` | **DATA** | Contains the actual user data payload |
| `0x02` | **GAP** | Indicates samples *not* available to a reader |
| `0x03` | **HEARTBEAT** | Writer announces available samples |
| `0x04` | **ACKNACK** | Reader acknowledges received samples / requests retransmit |
| `0x06` | **INFO_DST** | Destination Participant GUID |
| `0x0C` | **INFO_TS** | Timestamp (used for `DESTINATION_ORDER` & `LATENCY_BUDGET`) |
| `0x0D` | **DATA_FRAG** | Fragmented large samples |
| `0x0E` | **NACK_FRAG** | Negative acknowledgement of fragments |

### 2.2.2 Discovery Mechanism (PDP & SEDP)

**Why two discovery protocols?** PDP discovers *participants*; SEDP discovers the *endpoints (writers/readers)* bound to topics. This separation makes the system **scalable**.

The simple flow is:

- **P1:** Participant A sends `SPDP (Simple PDP)` via well-known multicast address `239.255.0.1:7400` (IPv4) every `leaseDuration` interval.
- **P2:** Participant B receives the announcement → adds A to its *discovered participants list*.
- **P3:** A's writers emit `SEDP` announcements describing Topic, type, and QoS.
- **P4:** B's readers match → reader sends `ACKNACK`; writer delivers `DATA`.

## 2.3 Quality of Service (QoS) Policies

QoS is the **defining feature** of DDS. There are **22 standard QoS policies** in the DDS spec. The most high-yield for KTU are:

| # | QoS Policy | Allowed Values | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RELIABILITY** | `BEST_EFFORT` / `RELIABLE` | Determines retransmission on loss |
| 2 | **DURABILITY** | `VOLATILE` / `TRANSIENT_LOCAL` / `TRANSIENT` / `PERSISTENT` | How long data persists for late-joiners |
| 3 | **HISTORY** | `KEEP_LAST_n` / `KEEP_ALL` | How many samples are stored |
| 4 | **DEADLINE** | Duration `D` (e.g., 50 ms) | Expected max time between samples |
| 5 | **LATENCY_BUDGET** | Duration `L` | Hint on how fast delivery should be |
| 6 | **LIVELINESS** | `AUTOMATIC` / `MANUAL_BY_PARTICIPANT` | Asserts writer is alive |
| 7 | **OWNERSHIP** | `SHARED` / `EXCLUSIVE` | Multiple writers on same instance |
| 8 | **DESTINATION_ORDER** | `BY_RECEPTION_TIMESTAMP` / `BY_SOURCE_TIMESTAMP` | Order at receiver |
| 9 | **TRANSPORT_PRIORITY** | Integer 0–N | OS-level priority hint |
| 10 | **RESOURCE_LIMITS** | Integers | Max samples/instance memory cap |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** If Writer offers `RELIABLE` and Reader requests `BEST_EFFORT`, the negotiation is **successful** (Reader gets the more reliable version). If Writer offers `BEST_EFFORT` and Reader requests `RELIABLE`, the request is **incompatible** and the reader remains *unmatched* with the writer.

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Reference Sheet

Since DDS is a **standards-based protocol** (not a math-heavy formula), the "formula sheet" below maps the **decision rules** and **latency/throughput relationships** tested in KTU exams.

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput (T) | $$T = \frac{N_{\text{samples}} \times S_{\text{bytes}}}{t_{\text{interval}}}$$ | bytes/s | Estimating required bandwidth |
| Bandwidth per topic | $$BW = S \times f_s$$ (sample size × sample rate) | bits/s | Capacity planning |
| End-to-end Latency (L_e2e) | $$L_{e2e} = L_{\text{write}} + L_{\text{serial}} + L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{deserialize}} + L_{\text{rx}}$$ | ms | Real-time guarantee |
| Deadline violation | If $\Delta t > D_{\text{Deadline}}$ → `offered_incompatible_deadline` event fires | — | QoS alarm |
| KEEP_LAST depth | Memory per Writer = $D \times S_{\text{avg}}$ | bytes | RESOURCE_LIMITS tuning |
| Retransmit rate | $$R = \lambda \times (1 - P_{rx})$$ | msgs/s | RELIABLE + UDP tuning |
| Heartbeat period | Default $= 100 \text{ ms}$ | ms | RTPS tuning |
| NACK suppression | Window $\tau_{nack}$ typically $= 50\text{–}200$ ms | ms | RTPS tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state that DDS is a **brokerless, peer-to-peer, databus-style** architecture. Students often confuse it with **MQTT** (which is broker-based) — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in MCQs.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | DDS Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | ROS 2 uses DDS as default middleware; sensor fusion (LIDAR, camera, radar) |
| **Aerospace & Defense** | NASA uses DDS in the Mars Rover (Curiosity, Perseverance) |
| **Smart Grid** | IEC 61850 over DDS for substation automation |
| **Medical Devices** | Real-time patient monitoring with strict reliability |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | OPC UA over DDS, predictive maintenance |
| **Robotics** | Inter-component communication in robotic arms and swarms |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Although DDS is a **protocol standard** rather than a calculus problem, KTU frequently asks *analytical/derivation-style* questions on **latency budgeting**, **QoS negotiation**, **bandwidth estimation**, and **RTPS discovery** sequences. The following worked derivations cover every high-yield question type.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — End-to-End Latency in a DDS System

**Problem:** A temperature sensor publishes 1 sample of 256 bytes every 10 ms over a DDS Reliable channel. The Writer serialization takes 0.05 ms, the Reader deserialization takes 0.04 ms, the network (LAN) has a one-way delay of 0.5 ms, and the kernel queue adds 0.2 ms. Compute the **total end-to-end latency** and verify if it meets a **5 ms deadline QoS**.

**Step 1 — Identify each component of latency.**

From the formula in §2.4:

$$L_{e2e} = L_{\text{write}} + L_{\text{serial}} + L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{deserialize}} + L_{\text{rx}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e} &= 0.05 \text{ ms (write)} + 0.05 \text{ ms (serial)} \\
&\quad + 0.5 \text{ ms (network)} + 0.04 \text{ ms (deserialize)} \\
&\quad + 0.2 \text{ ms (RX queue)} \\
&= 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.5 + 0.04 + 0.2 \\
L_{e2e} &= 0.84 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare against deadline.**

$$L_{e2e} = 0.84 \text{ ms} \;<\; D_{\text{Deadline}} = 5 \text{ ms} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Deadline MET} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** Since 0.84 ms ≪ 5 ms, the system meets the strictest real-time deadline. Even under 3× jitter, the new latency would be 2.52 ms, still safely under 5 ms.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Bandwidth Estimation for an IIoT DDS Topic

**Problem:** A factory has 200 vibration sensors. Each sensor publishes 1 sample of 512 bytes every 50 ms. Compute the **required bandwidth** if all sensors publish in a single DDS domain.

**Step 1 — Sample rate per sensor.**

$$f_s = \frac{1}{50 \text{ ms}} = \frac{1}{0.05 \text{ s}} = 20 \text{ samples/s}$$

**Step 2 — Per-sensor bandwidth.**

$$BW_{\text{per}} = S \times f_s = 512 \text{ bytes} \times 20 \text{ samples/s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{per}} &= 10{,}240 \text{ bytes/s} \\
&= 10{,}240 \times 8 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= 81{,}920 \text{ bits/s} \approx 80 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total bandwidth for 200 sensors.**

$$\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{total}} &= 200 \times 81{,}920 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= 16{,}384{,}000 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= 16.384 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Add DDS overhead (≈ 12% for RTPS header + UDP + IP).**

$$\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{with\_overhead}} &= 16.384 \times 1.12 \\
&\approx 18.35 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** A standard **100BASE-T** Ethernet (100 Mbps) link supports this traffic with 80%+ headroom. For wireless, an **802.11n** link (>150 Mbps) is sufficient. If reduced to 50 ms sampling becomes too aggressive, we can increase the interval to 100 ms to halve the bandwidth.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — QoS Compatibility Matrix (RELIABILITY)

**Problem:** Verify the compatibility outcome for the following Writer-Reader RELIABILITY requests:

| Case | Writer | Reader | Compatible? | Final State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | RELIABLE | RELIABLE | ? | ? |
| B | BEST_EFFORT | RELIABLE | ? | ? |
| C | RELIABLE | BEST_EFFORT | ? | ? |
| D | BEST_EFFORT | BEST_EFFORT | ? | ? |

**Step 1 — Apply the DDS OMG rule.**  
The Reader's request must be **no stronger** than what the Writer offers. Equivalently:

$$\text{Compatible} \iff \text{Reader}_{\text{req}} \le \text{Writer}_{\text{off}}$$

Where the order is: `BEST_EFFORT < RELIABLE`.

**Step 2 — Evaluate each case.**

- **Case A:** Reader (`RELIABLE`) ≤ Writer (`RELIABLE`) → ✅ **Compatible**, Reader gets `RELIABLE`.
- **Case B:** Reader (`RELIABLE`) > Writer (`BEST_EFFORT`) → ❌ **Incompatible**, no data flow.
- **Case C:** Reader (`BEST_EFFORT`) ≤ Writer (`RELIABLE`) → ✅ **Compatible**, Reader receives in `BEST_EFFORT` mode (the lesser of the two).
- **Case D:** Reader (`BEST_EFFORT`) ≤ Writer (`BEST_EFFORT`) → ✅ **Compatible**, both `BEST_EFFORT`.

**Step 3 — Final compatibility table.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Writer} \backslash \text{Reader} &= \begin{bmatrix} \text{BE} & \text{REL} \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{Writer BE} &= \begin{bmatrix} \text{C} & \text{INC} \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{Writer REL} &= \begin{bmatrix} \text{C} & \text{C} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Where `C` = Compatible, `INC` = Incompatible.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — KEEP_LAST History Memory Calculation

**Problem:** A DDS DataWriter is configured with `HISTORY = KEEP_LAST_50` and publishes samples of 256 bytes each. Compute the **per-Writer memory allocation** for this history buffer, including a 64-byte DDS sample header per sample.

**Step 1 — Effective sample size with header.**

$$S_{\text{eff}} = S_{\text{user}} + S_{\text{header}} = 256 + 64 = 320 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the history formula.**

$$M_{\text{Writer}} = D \times S_{\text{eff}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{Writer}} &= 50 \times 320 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 15.625 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verify against RESOURCE_LIMITS.**  
DDS requires `RESOURCE_LIMITS.max_samples` ≥ `DEPTH`. If `RESOURCE_LIMITS.max_samples_per_instance = 50`, the configuration is valid.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DDS vs MQTT vs AMQP vs CoAP

This **comparison matrix** is a KTU favourite for 14-mark questions.

| Feature | **DDS** | **MQTT** | **AMQP** | **CoAP** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | **Brokerless P2P** | **Broker-based (P2P via broker)** | Broker-based | Request-Response |
| Standard Body | OMG | OASIS | OASIS | IETF (RFC 7252) |
| Transport | UDP / TCP | TCP | TCP | UDP |
| Pattern | Publish-Subscribe | Publish-Subscribe | Pub/Sub + Queues | REST-like |
| QoS Levels | **22 policies** | 3 (0, 1, 2) | Limited | Confirmable / Non-confirmable |
| Real-Time | **Microsecond latency** | ~10–100 ms | ~10–100 ms | ~10–50 ms |
| Discovery | **Automatic (RTPS)** | Manual (broker) | Manual | mDNS / DNS-SD |
| Suitability | Robotics, autonomous systems, defense, IIoT | Mobile, smart home, telemetry | Enterprise messaging | Constrained sensor nodes |
| Failure Point | **None (decentralized)** | Broker SPOF | Broker SPOF | Server SPOF |
| Data Model | Typed (IDL) | Byte-payload | Byte-payload | REST resources |
| Domain Isolation | **Yes (Domain ID)** | Topic-tree | Exchanges | URI-path |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DDS Architectural Block Diagram (Functional Flow)

The figure below maps the **functional architecture** of a DDS system — the high-level data flow from a publisher app to a subscriber app, including the RTPS discovery exchange.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP1["Application Node 1 (Publisher App)"]
        P1["DomainParticipant (Domain 7)"]
        PUB1["Publisher"]
        WR1["DataWriter for Topic Temperature"]
        APP1LOGIC["App Logic: sensor.read()"]
    end

    subgraph DDSBUS["DDS Global Data Space (Brokerless)"]
        TOPIC1["Topic: Temperature (key = sensor_id)"]
        QOSREG["QoS Negotiation Registry"]
        DISCOVERY["RTPS Discovery (PDP + SEDP) over Multicast 239.255.0.1:7400"]
    end

    subgraph APP2["Application Node 2 (Subscriber App)"]
        P2["DomainParticipant (Domain 7)"]
        SUB2["Subscriber"]
        RD2["DataReader for Topic Temperature"]
        APP2LOGIC["App Logic: actuator.write()"]
    end

    APP1LOGIC --> WR1
    WR1 --> PUB1
    PUB1 --> P1
    P1 --> TOPIC1
    P1 <--> DISCOVERY
    P2 <--> DISCOVERY
    TOPIC1 --> RD2
    RD2 --> SUB2
    SUB2 --> P2
    RD2 --> APP2LOGIC
    QOSREG -.negotiates.-> WR1
    QOSREG -.negotiates.-> RD2
```

## 4.2 RTPS Discovery Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Participant A
    participant MC as Multicast Group 239.255.0.1:7400
    participant B as Participant B
    participant UA as Unicast UDP Channel

    Note over A,B: Phase 1 - Participant Discovery (PDP / SPDP)
    A->>MC: SPDP Announce (GUID_A, leaseDuration=10s)
    MC->>B: Forward SPDP Announce
    B->>A: SPDP Announce (GUID_B, leaseDuration=10s)

    Note over A,B: Phase 2 - Endpoint Discovery (SEDP)
    A->>MC: SEDP Publication Data (Topic=Temperature, Type=Temp_t)
    B->>MC: SEDP Subscription Data (Topic=Temperature, Type=Temp_t)
    A->>B: SEDP Reader info via unicast

    Note over A,B: Phase 3 - Matching
    A->>A: Match: Writer(Temperature) <-> Reader(Temperature)

    Note over A,B: Phase 4 - Data Exchange
    A->>B: HEARTBEAT (samples available)
    B->>A: ACKNACK (request samples)
    A->>B: DATA (sample 1, sample 2, sample 3, ...)
    B->>A: ACKNACK (received OK)
```

## 4.3 DDS QoS Negotiation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[App requests DataWriter Q1] --> B[Writer offered Q1]
    C[App requests DataReader Q2] --> D[Reader requested Q2]
    B --> E{QoS Compatible?}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Reader matched with Writer]
    E -- No --> G[Reader remains unmatched]
    F --> H[Data exchange begins]
    G --> I[Listener called: requested_incompatible_qos]
```

## 4.4 DDS Layered Reference Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Application Layer: User App (IDL-generated types)"]
    L2["DCPS Layer: DomainParticipant / Topic / Publisher / Subscriber / DataWriter / DataReader"]
    L3["QoS Layer: 22 Policies (RELIABILITY, DURABILITY, HISTORY, DEADLINE, etc.)"]
    L4["RTPS Layer: Discovery (PDP / SEDP) + Data Submessages (DATA, HEARTBEAT, ACKNACK)"]
    L5["Transport Layer: UDP (unreliable / multicast) and TCP (reliable)"]
    L6["Network Layer: IPv4 / IPv6"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6
```
