# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse DDS with MQTT** — DDS is *brokerless*; MQTT is *broker-based*. This distinction alone can decide 2 marks.
> 2. **Always mention the standard body** (OMG) and the **specification** (`DDS v1.4`, `RTPS v2.3`) when defining DDS. Examiners award 1 mark for the standard name.
> 3. **Don't list QoS policies without their effect** — `RELIABLE` ≠ "data is reliable" alone; you must state *"guarantees delivery with retransmission"*.
> 4. **Forget to mention "Data-Centric" (DCPS)** and you will lose 1 mark. The "DC" in DCPS is mandatory terminology.
> 5. In **comparison questions**, use a **table** with parameters (Architecture, Transport, QoS, Latency). A bullet list is partial credit; a table is full credit.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define DDS. List any four QoS policies used in DDS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**DDS (Data Distribution Service)** is an OMG-standardized, brokerless, **machine-to-machine (M2M) middleware** that provides **data-centric publish-subscribe (DCPS)** communication for real-time distributed systems using the **RTPS wire protocol**. *(1 Mark)*

**Four QoS Policies:** *(2 Marks — 0.5 each)*

1. **RELIABILITY** — Controls whether the system retransmits lost samples (`BEST_EFFORT` vs `RELIABLE`).
2. **DURABILITY** — Determines persistence for late-joining subscribers (`VOLATILE`, `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`, `TRANSIENT`, `PERSISTENT`).
3. **DEADLINE** — Sets the maximum expected time between successive samples.
4. **HISTORY** — Controls how many samples are retained per instance (`KEEP_LAST_n` or `KEEP_ALL`).

*(Any 4 of the 22 standard policies accepted.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between **DDS** and **MQTT** in IoT communication. (Write any 3 points.)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per point):**

| Parameter | **DDS** | **MQTT** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Architecture | **Brokerless, peer-to-peer (decentralized)** — no central server | **Broker-based (centralized)** — requires a broker (e.g., Mosquitto) |
| 2. Standard Body | **OMG** | **OASIS / IBM** |
| 3. QoS & Real-Time | **22 QoS policies**, microsecond latency, deterministic, typed (IDL) | **3 QoS levels** (0, 1, 2), best for low-bandwidth telemetry, byte-payload |

*(Any 3 valid points accepted; a comparative table is preferred for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **DDS architecture** in detail. Describe the **DCPS model** and list its main entities with their roles. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
DDS (Data Distribution Service) is an **OMG-standardized middleware** that provides **data-centric publish-subscribe (DCPS)** communication for real-time IoT and M2M systems. It is **brokerless, peer-to-peer, and deterministic**, communicating over the **RTPS wire protocol** on UDP/TCP.

**DCPS Model (3 Marks):**
The DCPS layer abstracts a *Global Data Space* shared among all participants. Publishers write data into this space; Subscribers read from it. **Matching** between a Writer and a Reader is done *implicitly* (by Topic + Type + QoS) rather than by an address — this is called **decoupling in space, time, and flow.**

**Main Entities & Roles (3 Marks):**

1. **DomainParticipant** *(1 mark)* — The application's *entry point* into a Domain. Each Participant has a unique `Domain ID` (0–230) and a `GUID` (Globally Unique Identifier). Participants from different Domains cannot see each other.

2. **Topic** *(1 mark)* — A logical channel with a **name** and **data type** (registered via **IDL**). Example: `Topic("Temperature", Temp_t)`. Topics are the *contracts* between publishers and subscribers.

3. **Publisher / DataWriter** *(0.5 mark)* — A `Publisher` is a factory for `DataWriter` objects. Each `DataWriter` is bound to a single Topic and is responsible for *pushing* typed samples into the Global Data Space.

4. **Subscriber / DataReader** *(0.5 mark)* — A `Subscriber` is a factory for `DataReader` objects. Each `DataReader` is bound to a Topic and exposes data to the application via a **Listener** callback or `take()` polling.

**Conclusion (0 Mark, optional):**
DDS achieves **low-latency, high-reliability, scalable** communication suitable for autonomous vehicles, robotics, and industrial automation.

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **RTPS discovery mechanism** in DDS. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
RTPS (Real-Time Publish-Subscribe) is the **interoperability wire protocol** specified by OMG. It defines how DDS endpoints discover each other and exchange data over **UDP/IP** with optional **TCP** support. The default well-known port is **7400**.

**Two-Phase Discovery (4 Marks):**

**Phase 1 — Participant Discovery (SPDP / PDP)** *(2 Marks)*

- Every `DomainParticipant` periodically announces itself via **SPDP (Simple Participant Discovery Protocol)** messages.
- Announcements are sent to a well-known **multicast address** — IPv4: `239.255.0.1:7400`, IPv6: `FF02::FFFF:239.255.0.1:7400`.
- The announcement contains the **GUID**, **Domain ID**, **Vendor ID**, and **lease duration**.
- Announcements are sent at a randomized interval (default every ~3 seconds) and repeated within the lease duration for redundancy.

**Phase 2 — Endpoint Discovery (SEDP)** *(2 Marks)*

- Once participants know each other, they exchange **SEDP (Simple Endpoint Discovery Protocol)** messages describing their **DataWriters and DataReaders**, along with the **Topic names, types, and QoS** offered/requested.
- A Reader automatically matches with a Writer if:
  1. They are in the **same Domain**,
  2. They have the **same Topic name and compatible type**,
  3. Their **QoS policies are compatible** (negotiated by the system).

**Diagram (1.5 Marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Participant A
    participant MC as Multicast 239.255.0.1:7400
    participant B as Participant B
    A->>MC: SPDP Announce
    MC->>B: Forward
    B->>MC: SPDP Announce
    MC->>A: Forward
    A->>B: SEDP Publication (Writers)
    B->>A: SEDP Subscription (Readers)
    Note over A,B: Auto-match if Topic, Type, QoS compatible
    A->>B: DATA submessages begin
```

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
RTPS's **automatic, decentralized discovery** is what makes DDS a true *plug-and-play* middleware — new participants can join a domain at runtime with zero configuration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Similar Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain **Quality of Service (QoS)** in DDS. Discuss any **six important QoS policies** in detail. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
QoS is a **declarative, machine-readable contract** between a `DataWriter` and `DataReader`. Each side specifies what it can *offer* (Writer) or *request* (Reader); the DDS runtime **negotiates** a compatible contract automatically. The DDS specification defines **22 standard QoS policies**.

**Six Important QoS Policies (6 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **RELIABILITY** *(1 Mark)* — Specifies the *delivery guarantee*:
   - `BEST_EFFORT`: No retransmission; lowest latency.
   - `RELIABLE`: Lost samples are retransmitted via `HEARTBEAT`/`ACKNACK`.

2. **DURABILITY** *(1 Mark)* — Controls persistence for **late-joining subscribers**:
   - `VOLATILE`: Late joiners get *nothing*.
   - `TRANSIENT_LOCAL`: Late joiners get the cached samples.
   - `PERSISTENT`: Samples survive even system restart (via persistence service).

3. **HISTORY** *(1 Mark)* — Controls buffer depth:
   - `KEEP_LAST_n`: Store only the *n* most recent samples.
   - `KEEP_ALL`: Store every sample until consumed.

4. **DEADLINE** *(1 Mark)* — Sets the **maximum expected time** between successive samples. The system fires an `on_offered_deadline_missed` event if a Writer fails to publish in time.

5. **LIVELINESS** *(1 Mark)* — Asserts that a Writer is *still alive*:
   - `AUTOMATIC` (lease-based), `MANUAL_BY_PARTICIPANT`, or `MANUAL_BY_TOPIC`.

6. **LIFESPAN** *(1 Mark)* — Specifies the **expiration time** for each sample. After the duration, the sample is removed from caches and Readers' queues, preventing stale data use.

**Conclusion (0 Mark):**
QoS is the **key differentiator** that makes DDS suitable for **mission-critical** systems where MQTT's 3 QoS levels are insufficient.

---

**Q. (b)** Compare **DDS and MQTT** in detail. State **two IoT application scenarios** where each is the most appropriate choice and justify. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | **DDS** | **MQTT** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Standard Body | OMG | OASIS / IBM |
| 2. Architecture | **Brokerless, peer-to-peer** | **Broker-based, client-broker** |
| 3. Transport | UDP, TCP, multicast | TCP (mainly) |
| 4. Pattern | **Data-centric Pub/Sub (DCPS)** | Topic-based Pub/Sub |
| 5. QoS Support | **22 detailed QoS policies** | **3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2)** |
| 6. Data Model | **Typed (IDL / CDR)** | Opaque byte-payload |
| 7. Discovery | **Automatic (RTPS / SPDP / SEDP)** | Manual (broker known a priori) |
| 8. Real-Time | **Microsecond latency** | ~10–100 ms typical |
| 9. Reliability | End-to-end with retransmit | Broker-mediated |
| 10. Single Point of Failure | **None (decentralized)** | Broker is SPOF |
| 11. Resource Footprint | **Medium to High** | **Very Low** |
| 12. Suitable Domains | Robotics, autonomous vehicles, defense, IIoT | Smart home, mobile, telemetry |

**Scenario 1: DDS for Autonomous Vehicles (1.5 Marks)** *(any 1.5-mark scenario)*

**Application:** A self-driving car uses **LIDAR (40 Hz), Camera (30 Hz), Radar (20 Hz), GPS (10 Hz)** for sensor fusion. The obstacle-detection module must receive every sample within **1 ms** for safety.

**Why DDS is appropriate:**
- **Microsecond latency** meets the 1 ms deadline.
- **RELIABLE + DEADLINE QoS** guarantees no missed samples and alarms on late data.
- **Brokerless architecture** means no SPOF — critical for life-safety.
- **22 QoS policies** allow fine-grained control (e.g., `TRANSPORT_PRIORITY` for safety messages).
- ROS 2 and **AUTOSAR Adaptive** both use DDS.

**Scenario 2: MQTT for Smart Home Telemetry (1.5 Marks)** *(any 1.5-mark scenario)*

**Application:** A temperature sensor in a home sends readings to a cloud dashboard every 5 minutes.

**Why MQTT is appropriate:**
- **Low bandwidth and small payload** (a few bytes) is ideal for MQTT.
- **Constrained devices** (8-bit MCUs) can run MQTT with minimal footprint.
- **Broker-based model** suits cloud integration (e.g., AWS IoT Core, HiveMQ).
- **Latency of ~50–100 ms** is acceptable; hard real-time is not required.
- Topic structure (`home/livingroom/temp`) is intuitive and easy to subscribe to.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
DDS and MQTT are **complementary, not competing**. DDS is the choice for **deterministic, real-time, mission-critical** systems. MQTT is the choice for **lightweight, best-effort, cloud-centric** IoT telemetry. Modern stacks sometimes combine both: **MQTT at the edge → DDS inside the cloud fabric**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Facts
- [ ] DDS = **Data Distribution Service**, standardized by **OMG**.
- [ ] Core spec: **`DDS v1.4`** + **`RTPS v2.3`**.
- [ ] DDS is **brokerless, peer-to-peer, decentralized** — *NOT broker-based*.
- [ ] **DCPS** = Data-Centric Publish-Subscribe (the conceptual layer).
- [ ] **RTPS** = Real-Time Publish-Subscribe (the wire protocol over UDP/TCP).
- [ ] Default discovery port: **UDP 7400** (multicast `239.255.0.1`).
- [ ] 22 standard **QoS policies** are the defining feature.

### The Five DCPS Entities
- [ ] `DomainParticipant` → application entry point (owns a `Domain ID`).
- [ ] `Topic` → typed, named data channel (defined in IDL).
- [ ] `Publisher` → factory for `DataWriter` objects.
- [ ] `Subscriber` → factory for `DataReader` objects.
- [ ] `DataWriter` / `DataReader` → endpoints that push/pull typed samples.

### QoS Must-Knows
- [ ] `RELIABLE` → retransmit lost samples via HEARTBEAT / ACKNACK.
- [ ] `BEST_EFFORT` → no retransmit, lowest latency.
- [ ] `DURABILITY` → controls what late-joiners get.
- [ ] `HISTORY` → `KEEP_LAST_n` vs `KEEP_ALL`.
- [ ] `DEADLINE` → max time between samples; alarm on miss.
- [ ] `LATENCY_BUDGET` → delay hint for the middleware.
- [ ] `LIVELINESS` → writer-aliveness assertion mechanism.
- [ ] `LIFESPAN` → expiration time for each sample.
- [ ] **Negotiation rule:** Reader's request ≤ Writer's offer.

### RTPS Must-Knows
- [ ] Two-phase discovery: **SPDP (Participant) + SEDP (Endpoint)**.
- [ ] Key submessages: **DATA, HEARTBEAT, ACKNACK, GAP, INFO_TS, DATA_FRAG, NACK_FRAG**.
- [ ] Default discovery IP: `239.255.0.1` (IPv4 multicast).
- [ ] **HEARTBEAT** = Writer → Readers (announces available samples).
- [ ] **ACKNACK** = Reader → Writer (acknowledges / requests samples).

### DDS vs MQTT
- [ ] DDS = **brokerless + typed + 22 QoS** + real-time.
- [ ] MQTT = **broker + 3 QoS** + lightweight + cloud telemetry.
- [ ] DDS in **ROS 2, AUTOSAR, OPC UA over DDS, NASA Mars Rover**.
- [ ] MQTT in **smart home, mobile apps, constrained sensor networks**.

### Key Mnemonics
- [ ] "**D**DS = **D**ecentralized, **D**eterministic, **D**ata-centric" (the 3 D's).
- [ ] "**R**TPS = **R**eal-Time **P**ubli**S**ub" — note the order.
- [ ] "**DCPS** has **D**omain, **C**onfig, **P**ublisher, **S**ubscriber" components.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Day Tip:** When you see a question that says *"Compare DDS and MQTT"* or *"Discuss RTPS"*, **always draw a diagram AND a table**. Diagrams and tables in KTU scripts earn **1–2 extra marks** over plain prose. Use the **sequence diagram** in §4.2 for any RTPS question, and the **table in §3.5** for any comparison question. Time spent drawing = marks saved in valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MQTT Publisher-Subscriber Model using MQTT Broker (Implementation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MQTT Publisher-Subscriber Model using MQTT Broker (Implementation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — MQTT Publisher–Subscriber Model using MQTT Broker (Implementation)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is an OASIS-standardized, lightweight **publish/subscribe (pub/sub) messaging protocol** designed for constrained devices and low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks. It operates over TCP/IP (typically port **1883** for unencrypted, **8883** for TLS) and follows the client–broker architecture defined in **ISO/IEC 20922:2016**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Publisher–Subscriber Model:** A messaging architectural pattern in which senders (publishers) and receivers (subscribers) are **decoupled** in time, space, and synchronization. Communication is mediated by a central **MQTT Broker** that filters and routes messages based on logical string identifiers called **Topics**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Newspaper Stand"

Imagine a **Newspaper Publishing Company**:
- A **Reporter** writes an article and hands it to the **Print Press (Broker)**. The reporter does **not** know who will read it.
- Readers **subscribe** to specific sections (e.g., *Sports*, *Politics*, *Tech*). The press **delivers** only matching sections to interested readers.
- A reader can subscribe *before* the article is published (queued) or *after* (gets future issues only).
- The reporter and reader **never meet** — the broker is the only intermediary.

| Real-World Element | MQTT Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Reporter (writer) | **Publisher** (e.g., temperature sensor) |
| Print Press | **MQTT Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) |
| Newspaper Section name | **Topic** (e.g., `home/livingroom/temp`) |
| Subscribed Reader | **Subscriber** (e.g., mobile dashboard) |
| Delivery preference | **QoS Level (0, 1, 2)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** MQTT is one of the most popular application-layer protocols in IoT because it consumes as little as **2 bytes** of overhead per message, making it ideal for **resource-constrained** sensor nodes, **LPWAN** networks (LoRa, NB-IoT), and **edge gateways**.

### 1.3 Why Pub/Sub over Request–Response?

In traditional **HTTP (request/response)**, a sensor must continuously poll a server, wasting bandwidth and battery. MQTT's **event-driven pub/sub** model pushes data only when an event of interest occurs — a **>90% reduction** in network traffic for typical telemetry workloads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MQTT specification defines **three roles**:
> 1. **Publisher** — client that sends messages.
> 2. **Subscriber** — client that receives messages of interest.
> 3. **Broker** — central server that receives all messages, filters them by topic, and distributes to subscribers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table

### 2.1 Anatomy of an MQTT Message

An MQTT control packet consists of:
- **Fixed Header** (2 bytes minimum) — packet type, flags, remaining length.
- **Variable Header** — packet identifier, topic length, etc.
- **Payload** — the actual application data (up to **256 MB** in MQTT v5.0).

The packet types are defined by a **4-bit control field** with 14 possible values: `CONNECT`, `CONNACK`, `PUBLISH`, `SUBSCRIBE`, `SUBACK`, `UNSUBSCRIBE`, `UNSUBACK`, `PINGREQ`, `PINGRESP`, `DISCONNECT`, `AUTH`, `PUBACK`, `PUBREC`, `PUBREL`, `PUBCOMP`.

### 2.2 The Three Quality of Service (QoS) Levels

This is the **most frequently asked KTU question** on MQTT.

| QoS Level | Name | Guarantee | Handshake | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget; no ACK | `PUBLISH` only | Sensor data that is time-sensitive but loss-tolerant (e.g., periodic temperature) |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Guaranteed delivery; duplicates possible | `PUBLISH` → `PUBACK` | Critical alerts where duplicate is acceptable |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | Guaranteed single delivery | `PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP` (4-step) | Billing, medical, or control commands where duplicates are harmful |

### 2.3 Topic Hierarchy & Wildcards

Topics are **UTF-8 strings** separated by `/`, e.g., `home/floor1/room3/temperature`. Two wildcard types exist in subscriptions:

- **Single-level wildcard `+`**: matches exactly one segment.
  - Example: `home/+/room3/temperature` matches `home/floor1/room3/temperature`.
- **Multi-level wildcard `#`**: matches all remaining segments; **must be the last character**.
  - Example: `home/floor1/#` matches everything under floor1.

> [!WARNING]
> The `$` prefix is reserved by the broker for internal statistics. Subscribing to `$SYS/#` is allowed but should be used cautiously in production.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Concept | Specification | Default Value / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| MQTT Standard | ISO/IEC 20922:2016 | OASIS Standard v3.1.1 / v5.0 |
| Default TCP Port (unencrypted) | **1883** | IANA registered |
| Default TCP Port (TLS) | **8883** | IANA registered |
| Default WebSocket Port | **8000** / 8080 | Broker-specific |
| Keep-Alive Timer | 0–65535 seconds | **60 s** |
| Maximum QoS | 0, 1, 2 | — |
| Max Payload (v3.1.1) | 256 MB | Client/Broker limit |
| Topic Wildcards | `+` and `#` | — |
| Retained Messages | 1 flag bit | Last known good value |
| Clean Session | 0/1 flag | `1` (no persistence) |
| Last Will & Testament (LWT) | 3 fields | QoS, retain, topic, payload |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** MQTT is the messaging backbone of **AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT, Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, Facebook Messenger (historically), and Tesla vehicle telemetry**. Mastering its QoS and topic model is essential for **smart agriculture, industrial SCADA, fleet management, and home automation** projects.

### 2.5 Last Will and Testament (LWT) — A Unique MQTT Feature

If a client disconnects **abnormally** (network failure, battery death), the broker automatically publishes a pre-defined "will" message on behalf of the dead client. This is critical for **device liveness monitoring** in IoT deployments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation: Building a Pub/Sub System

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **hands-on implementation** using open-source brokers. The de-facto academic choice is **Eclipse Mosquitto** (C-based, ~300 KB footprint). The reference Python client is `paho-mqtt` (Eclipse Foundation).

### 3.1 Installation and Setup

**Step 1 — Install the Mosquitto Broker (Linux/Raspberry Pi):**

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install mosquitto mosquitto-clients -y
sudo systemctl enable mosquitto
sudo systemctl start mosquitto
```

**Step 2 — Install Python Client Library:**

```bash
pip install paho-mqtt
```

**Step 3 — Verify Broker Connectivity:**

```bash
mosquitto -v          # starts broker in verbose mode on port 1883
```

### 3.2 Publisher Implementation (Step-by-Step)

**File: `publisher.py`**

```python
import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt
import time, random, json

BROKER  = "localhost"
PORT    = 1883
TOPIC   = "iot/sensor/temperature"
CLIENT  = "sensor_node_01"

# 1. Create client instance with a unique ID
client = mqtt.Client(client_id=CLIENT, clean_session=True)

# 2. (Optional) Attach Last Will & Testament
client.will_set(topic="iot/sensor/status",
                payload="offline",
                qos=1,
                retain=True)

# 3. Connect to broker
client.connect(host=BROKER, port=PORT, keepalive=60)

# 4. Announce online status
client.publish(topic="iot/sensor/status", payload="online", qos=1, retain=True)

# 5. Publish loop
try:
    while True:
        temperature = round(random.uniform(20.0, 35.0), 2)
        payload = json.dumps({
            "node_id"   : CLIENT,
            "value"     : temperature,
            "unit"      : "Celsius",
            "timestamp" : time.time()
        })
        result = client.publish(topic=TOPIC, payload=payload, qos=1)
        status = result.rc
        if status == 0:
            print(f"✅ Sent -> {TOPIC} : {payload}")
        else:
            print(f"❌ Failed with code {status}")
        time.sleep(2)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    client.publish("iot/sensor/status", "offline", qos=1, retain=True)
    client.disconnect()
```

**Logical Breakdown of Every Line:**

- **`mqtt.Client(...)`** → Initializes a client object. The `client_id` must be unique per broker connection.
- **`client.will_set(...)`** → Registers a "death certificate." If the broker does not receive a `DISCONNECT` packet within the keep-alive window, it auto-publishes this LWT.
- **`client.connect(...)`** → Opens a TCP socket and sends a `CONNECT` control packet; awaits `CONNACK`.
- **`client.publish(...)`** → Returns an `MQTTMessageInfo` object. `result.rc == 0` denotes `MQTT_ERR_SUCCESS`.
- **`client.disconnect()`** → Sends a graceful `DISCONNECT` packet, preventing LWT from firing.

### 3.3 Subscriber Implementation (Step-by-Step)

**File: `subscriber.py`**

```python
import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt

BROKER = "localhost"
PORT   = 1883
TOPIC  = "iot/sensor/#"   # subscribe to all sub-topics

# Callbacks
def on_connect(client, userdata, flags, rc, properties=None):
    if rc == 0:
        print(f"Connected to broker, subscribing to {TOPIC}")
        client.subscribe(topic=TOPIC, qos=1)
    else:
        print(f"Connection failed with code {rc}")

def on_message(client, userdata, msg):
    print(f"📩 Topic: {msg.topic}  |  QoS: {msg.qos}  |  Payload: {msg.payload.decode()}")

def on_disconnect(client, userdata, rc):
    print("Disconnected from broker")

# Client setup
client = mqtt.Client(client_id="dashboard_subscriber", clean_session=True)
client.on_connect = on_connect
client.on_message = on_message
client.on_disconnect = on_disconnect

client.connect(BROKER, PORT, 60)
client.loop_forever()      # blocking call; auto-reconnects on failure
```

**Logical Breakdown of Every Line:**

- **`on_connect`** fires after receiving the `CONNACK` from the broker. We subscribe here (not before connect) to ensure the subscription request is acknowledged.
- **`on_message`** fires for every `PUBLISH` packet whose topic matches a subscription.
- **`client.loop_forever()`** starts a background network thread that handles I/O, reconnection, and message dispatching.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pub/Sub vs. Traditional Models

| Parameter | MQTT (Pub/Sub) | HTTP (Req/Resp) | CoAP (Req/Resp) | AMQP (Pub/Sub) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Client–Server | Client–Server | Pub/Sub |
| Header Size | **2 bytes** | ~200–2000 bytes | 4 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Transport | TCP | TCP | UDP (usually) | TCP |
| Power Efficiency | High | Low | High | Medium |
| QoS Support | 0, 1, 2 | None | Confirmable/Non | 0, 1, 2 |
| Best For | Constrained IoT | Web APIs | Simple sensors | Enterprise messaging |
| KTU Relevance | **★★★★★** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |

### 3.5 Verification Procedure

1. Open **Terminal 1** → start subscriber: `python3 subscriber.py`
2. Open **Terminal 2** → start publisher: `python3 publisher.py`
3. Observe JSON payloads arriving every 2 seconds in Terminal 1.
4. **Test LWT:** Kill the publisher with `kill -9`; within 60 s the subscriber should receive `payload=offline` on `iot/sensor/status`.
5. **Test wildcard:** Change subscriber topic to `iot/#` — it should still receive all messages.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Pub/Sub Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Sensor Node A\nPublisher"]
    P2["Mobile App\nPublisher"]
    P3["Gateway\nPublisher"]

    S2["Actuator\nSubscriber"]
    S3["Logging Service\nSubscriber"]

    T2["Topic\nhome/control/light"]
    T3["Topic\nhome/status/all"]

    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T2
    P3 --> T3

    T2 --> B

    style B fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    style S1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 QoS Handshake Sequences

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pub as Publisher
    participant Bro as Broker
    participant Sub as Subscriber

    Note over Pub,Sub: QoS 0: At most once
    Pub->>Bro: PUBLISH
    Bro->>Sub: PUBLISH

    Note over Pub,Sub: QoS 1: At least once
    Pub->>Bro: PUBLISH
    Bro-->>Pub: PUBACK
    Bro->>Sub: PUBLISH
    Sub-->>Bro: PUBACK

    Note over Pub,Sub: QoS 2: Exactly once
    Pub->>Bro: PUBLISH
    Bro-->>Pub: PUBREC
    Pub->>Bro: PUBREL
    Bro-->>Pub: PUBCOMP
    Bro->>Sub: PUBLISH
    Sub-->>Bro: PUBREC
    Bro->>Sub: PUBREL
    Sub-->>Bro: PUBCOMP
```

### 4.3 Topic Tree Visualization


### 4.4 Complete Implementation Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid

    B --> C

    E --> F
    E --> F2
    F --> G
    G --> H
    F2 --> G
    G --> I

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing QoS 1 and QoS 2 handshakes, (2) forgetting that the `#` wildcard must be the **last character** of a topic, (3) using `client.loop_forever()` inside a `try/except` without `loop_stop()` on disconnect, and (4) omitting the `clean_session` flag in exam code.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT and state the number of control packets exchanged for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| QoS | Name | Handshake Sequence | Packets Exchanged |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | At most once | `PUBLISH` | 1 |
| 1 | At least once | `PUBLISH` → `PUBACK` | 2 |
| 2 | Exactly once | `PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP` | 4 |

**Exam Tip:** Writing the four packet names in correct order for QoS 2 fetches full 3 marks.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of an MQTT Broker? List two popular open-source MQTT brokers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **MQTT Broker** is the central server in the pub/sub model. Its roles are:
1. **Receiving** all messages from publishers.
2. **Filtering** them by topic subscriptions.
3. **Forwarding** matching messages to subscribers.
4. Managing **client sessions, retained messages, and LWT** messages.

Two popular open-source brokers: **Eclipse Mosquitto** (C, lightweight) and **HiveMQ** (Java, enterprise-grade). *(Other acceptable answers: EMQX, RabbitMQ-MQTT plugin, VerneMQ.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Implementation-focused*

**Q3(a). [7 marks] Write a Python program to publish a JSON payload `{"temp": <random>, "id": "node1"}` every 3 seconds to topic `lab/sensor/data` using QoS 1, with proper LWT configuration.**

**Model Solution:**

```python
import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt
import time, random, json

client = mqtt.Client("node1_publisher", clean_session=True)

# LWT configuration
client.will_set("lab/sensor/status",
                payload=json.dumps({"id":"node1","status":"dead"}),
                qos=1, retain=True)

client.connect("localhost", 1883, 60)

# Announce alive
client.publish("lab/sensor/status",
               json.dumps({"id":"node1","status":"alive"}),
               qos=1, retain=True)

try:
    while True:
        payload = json.dumps({"temp": round(random.uniform(25,40),2),
                              "id": "node1"})
        client.publish("lab/sensor/data", payload, qos=1)
        print("Published:", payload)
        time.sleep(3)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    client.disconnect()
```

**Mark Split (7):**
- LWT configuration — **2 marks**
- CONNECT and online announcement — **1 mark**
- Publish loop with JSON + QoS 1 — **3 marks**
- Graceful disconnect — **1 mark**

---

**Q3(b). [7 marks] Write a Python subscriber that connects to the broker, subscribes to `lab/sensor/#`, and prints topic + payload for every incoming message. Explain the purpose of `on_connect` and `on_message` callbacks.**

**Model Solution:**

```python
import paho.mqtt.client as mqtt

def on_connect(client, userdata, flags, rc, properties=None):
    if rc == 0:
        client.subscribe("lab/sensor/#", qos=1)
    else:
        print("Connect failed:", rc)

def on_message(client, userdata, msg):
    print(f"Topic: {msg.topic} | Payload: {msg.payload.decode()}")

client = mqtt.Client("dashboard_sub", clean_session=True)
client.on_connect = on_connect
client.on_message = on_message
client.connect("localhost", 1883, 60)
client.loop_forever()
```

**Purpose of Callbacks (3 marks of the 7):**
- **`on_connect`**: Triggered after the broker sends `CONNACK`. Subscription is registered here to guarantee the broker is ready to accept it; also lets the program check `rc` (return code) for authentication/network errors.
- **`on_message`**: Triggered for every incoming `PUBLISH` packet whose topic matches a subscription; it is the function that implements the application logic (display, store, forward, actuate).

**Mark Split (7):**
- Correct `on_connect` with subscription — **2 marks**
- Correct `on_message` payload decoding — **2 marks**
- `loop_forever()` and connect — **2 marks**
- Explanation of callbacks — **1 mark**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Theory + Comparative Analysis*

**Q4(a). [7 marks] With a neat diagram, explain the MQTT Publish/Subscribe model. Differentiate between QoS 0, QoS 1, and QoS 2 with packet exchange diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

*(Diagram — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the architecture and Section 4.2 for QoS handshakes.)*

In the **Publish/Subscribe model**, publishers and subscribers are **fully decoupled**. Publishers send messages tagged with a **topic** to the **broker**; the broker filters these by topic and forwards to all matching subscribers. Publishers do not know — and need not know — the identity, number, or location of subscribers.

| QoS | Guarantee | Handshake | Overhead | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | At most once | 1 packet | Lowest | Real-time telemetry, loss-tolerant |
| 1 | At least once | 2 packets | Medium | Alarms, notifications |
| 2 | Exactly once | 4 packets | Highest | Billing, control commands |

**Mark Split (7):**
- Architecture diagram — **2 marks**
- Decoupling explanation — **2 marks**
- QoS comparison table — **2 marks**
- Examples for each QoS — **1 mark**

---

**Q4(b). [7 marks] Explain MQTT Topics, Wildcards, Retained Messages, and Last Will & Testament. Why is MQTT preferred over HTTP for IoT applications?**

**Model Solution:**

- **Topics** are hierarchical UTF-8 strings separated by `/`, e.g., `home/floor1/room2/temp`. They provide logical addressing without IP/port knowledge.
- **Wildcards** (subscribe-side only):
  - `+` → matches one segment.
  - `#` → matches all remaining segments; must be last.
- **Retained Messages**: A publisher can set the **retain flag** so the broker stores the last message of a topic. Any new subscriber immediately receives this "last known good" value upon connection — eliminating the need for an initial-value request.
- **Last Will & Testament (LWT)**: A message pre-registered with the broker. If the client disconnects ungracefully, the broker publishes it on the client's behalf — useful for **device liveness** and fault detection.

**Why MQTT > HTTP for IoT (any 4):**
1. **Tiny header** (~2 B vs. ~200–2000 B for HTTP).
2. **Persistent TCP connection** — no per-message handshake overhead.
3. **Push-based** — no polling, saving battery and bandwidth.
4. **Native QoS** — guarantees message delivery semantics.
5. **Asynchronous pub/sub** — natural fit for many-to-many IoT topologies.
6. **Designed for unstable networks** — session resumption and LWT support.

**Mark Split (7):**
- Topics + Wildcards — **2 marks**
- Retained Messages — **1 mark**
- LWT — **1 mark**
- MQTT vs. HTTP advantages (any 4) — **3 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic:**
> - Always draw the **broker in the center** of pub/sub diagrams. (–1 mark otherwise)
> - When asked "Why pub/sub?", mention **decoupling** explicitly. (–1 mark otherwise)
> - For QoS, writing the **4 packet names** for QoS 2 in correct order is mandatory for full marks.
> - Code answers must include `clean_session`, `keepalive`, and `disconnect()` for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **MQTT** = lightweight pub/sub messaging protocol on TCP (port 1883 / 8883 TLS).
- [x] **Three roles**: Publisher, Subscriber, **Broker** (central mediator).
- [x] **Decoupling** is the core design advantage — time, space, and synchronization independence.
- [x] **Topics** = UTF-8 hierarchical strings; subscribe-side wildcards `+` (single) and `#` (multi-level, must be last).
- [x] **QoS 0** = fire-and-forget (1 packet). **QoS 1** = at-least-once (PUBLISH + PUBACK). **QoS 2** = exactly-once (4-step handshake: PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP).
- [x] **Retained Messages** = broker stores last value of a topic; new subscribers get it instantly.
- [x] **LWT (Last Will & Testament)** = auto-published on abnormal disconnect; used for device liveness.
- [x] **Keep-Alive Timer** (default 60 s) detects dead clients.
- [x] **Clean Session** flag: `True` = no persistence; `False` = broker stores missed messages for offline clients.
- [x] **Mosquitto** + **paho-mqtt (Python)** is the standard KTU lab combination.
- [x] Implementation flow: install broker → install client lib → instantiate Client → set callbacks → connect → subscribe/publish → `loop_forever()`.
- [x] **MQTT vs HTTP**: smaller headers, persistent connection, push-based, native QoS, designed for constrained devices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HTTP - WebSocket Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** HTTP - WebSocket Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 HTTP — The Hypertext Transfer Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)** is an *application-layer* request–response protocol used for transmitting hypermedia documents (HTML, JSON, XML, images) between a **client** (typically a browser or IoT device) and a **server** over **TCP/IP** on **port 80** (or **port 443** for HTTPS). It is the foundational data-communication standard of the World Wide Web and the most widely adopted application protocol in IoT data exchange today.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu"

Imagine you walk into a restaurant:

1. The **waiter (HTTP Client)** walks up to your table.
2. You (the **user/IoT sensor**) hand over a **written order (HTTP Request)** to the waiter.
3. The waiter takes it to the **kitchen (Server)**.
4. The kitchen prepares the dish and the waiter brings back the **prepared food (HTTP Response)**.
5. After the meal, the waiter walks away. The next time you want something, you must **call the waiter again** — i.e., a **new request** must be sent.

This walk-back-and-forth nature is the classic **request/response model** of HTTP. Every time the client wants data, it must initiate a brand-new connection (or re-use one with restrictions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Features of HTTP:**
> - **Stateless:** Each request is independent; the server does not remember prior requests.
> - **Text-based:** Requests and responses are human-readable ASCII/UTF-8 text.
> - **Connection-oriented (historically):** Built on top of **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)**, guaranteeing ordered, reliable delivery.
> - **Default Port:** **80** for HTTP, **443** for HTTPS (TLS-encrypted).

---

## 1.2 WebSocket — The Full-Duplex Persistent Channel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **WebSocket** is a **full-duplex**, **persistent**, **bi-directional** communication protocol standardized by the **IETF as RFC 6455**. Unlike HTTP, it establishes a **single long-lived TCP connection** (typically on **port 80 or 443**) that allows both client and server to send data to each other **independently and simultaneously**, without the overhead of re-establishing connections for every exchange. WebSocket is critical for **real-time IoT applications** such as live sensor dashboards, smart-home controls, and industrial SCADA.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Open Phone Line"

Compare HTTP's restaurant waiter to a **mobile phone** placed on the table:

- The phone is **always connected** (one persistent line).
- Either party (you OR the kitchen) can **speak at any moment** — no need to "re-order" the connection.
- The line stays open until one party **hangs up**.

This **"open phone line"** is essentially what a WebSocket provides: an unbroken channel of communication. That's why it is called **"full-duplex"** — both sides can transmit at the same time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Features of WebSocket:**
> - **Full-Duplex:** Both client and server can send data at any time.
> - **Persistent Connection:** Single TCP socket reused for the entire session.
> - **Low Latency:** No HTTP headers re-sent per message; suitable for **sub-100 ms** real-time IoT telemetry.
> - **Message-oriented:** Communication happens in discrete **frames**, not streams of text.
> - **Default Port:** **80** (ws://) or **443** (wss://) — same as HTTP for firewall traversal.

---

## 1.3 Why KTU 2024 Asks You to Study Both Together

In IoT systems, you must choose the right tool for the right job:

| Scenario | Better Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Periodic sensor upload every 5 min | **HTTP** | Simple, stateless, low-frequency |
| Live control of a robotic arm | **WebSocket** | Real-time bi-directional control |
| Smart home dashboard refresh | **WebSocket** | Instant updates from sensors |
| Firmware OTA update | **HTTP** | Large file, no real-time need |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024:** Always remember that **WebSocket begins as an HTTP request** (the famous **"Upgrade" handshake**) and then *upgrades* the connection. This duality is the most frequently asked concept in board examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an HTTP Transaction

### 2.1.1 The HTTP Request Structure

An HTTP request is composed of four parts:

1. **Request Line** — contains the *method*, *URI*, and *HTTP version*.
2. **Headers** — metadata about the request (key-value pairs).
3. **Blank Line** — separator (CRLF).
4. **Body** (optional) — payload such as JSON, form data, or file contents.

**Standard Request Example:**

```
GET /api/sensor/temperature HTTP/1.1
Host: iot.kerala.edu
User-Agent: ESP32/1.0
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 9 Standard HTTP Methods (Verbs) — KTU High-Yield:**
>
> | Method | Purpose | Idempotent? | Body? |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **GET** | Retrieve a resource | Yes | No |
> | **POST** | Create / submit new data | No | Yes |
> | **PUT** | Replace existing resource | Yes | Yes |
> | **PATCH** | Partial update | No | Yes |
> | **DELETE** | Remove resource | Yes | Optional |
> | **HEAD** | Same as GET but no body | Yes | No |
> | **OPTIONS** | Discover allowed methods | Yes | No |
> | **CONNECT** | Establish a tunnel (proxy) | No | No |
> | **TRACE** | Echo the received request | Yes | No |

### 2.1.2 The HTTP Response Structure

A response is composed of:

1. **Status Line** — HTTP version + status code + reason phrase.
2. **Headers** — metadata of the response.
3. **Blank Line** — separator.
4. **Body** — the actual content (HTML, JSON, image, etc.).

**Standard Response Example:**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 87
Date: Mon, 14 Oct 2024 10:30:00 GMT

{"sensor": "DHT22", "value": 27.4, "unit": "Celsius", "status": "online"}
```

### 2.1.3 HTTP Status Code Classes — KTU Must-Know

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Status Code Classes (RFC 7231):**
>
> | Class | Range | Meaning | Example |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **1xx** | 100–199 | Informational | 100 Continue |
> | **2xx** | 200–299 | Success | 200 OK, 201 Created |
> | **3xx** | 300–399 | Redirection | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
> | **4xx** | 400–499 | Client Error | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found |
> | **5xx** | 500–599 | Server Error | 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable |

### 2.1.4 HTTP Connection Evolution — Persistent & Pipelined

| HTTP Version | Connection Behavior | Throughput Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/1.0** | One connection per request (short-lived) | **High latency** — TCP handshake per request |
| **HTTP/1.1** | Persistent + Pipelined (`keep-alive`) | **Reduced latency** — reuse TCP connection |
| **HTTP/2** | Multiplexed, binary, header compression (HPACK) | **High throughput** — many streams over one TCP |
| **HTTP/3** | Runs over **QUIC** (UDP-based) | **Lowest latency** — no head-of-line blocking |

---

## 2.2 The WebSocket Protocol — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 The WebSocket Handshake (RFC 6455)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Point:** WebSocket **always** begins as an HTTP request with special headers. The client requests an *upgrade* of the connection.

**Step 1 — Client sends an HTTP GET with `Upgrade` header:**

```
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Host: iot.kerala.edu
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
Origin: http://kerala.edu
```

**Step 2 — Server responds with 101 Switching Protocols:**

```
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Accept: s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo=
```

**Step 3 — TCP socket is now re-purposed** as a WebSocket connection. HTTP ends here; from now on, **binary frames** are exchanged.

### 2.2.2 Why the Sec-WebSocket-Key / Accept Exchange?

The server concatenates the client's `Sec-WebSocket-Key` with the **magic GUID** `258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11`, computes the **SHA-1 hash**, then **Base-64 encodes** the result and returns it as `Sec-WebSocket-Accept`. This proves the server actually supports WebSocket (and is not a confused proxy).

$$H = \text{SHA1}(\text{ClientKey} \oplus \text{MAGIC\_GUID})$$

$$Accept = \text{Base64}(H)$$

Where the magic GUID is the fixed string:

$$\text{MAGIC\_GUID} = \text{258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11}$$

### 2.2.3 WebSocket Frame Format

Each message is broken into one or more **frames**. A frame has:

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **FIN** | 1 bit | 1 = final fragment, 0 = more fragments follow |
| **RSV1/2/3** | 3 bits | Reserved for extensions (e.g., per-message deflate) |
| **Opcode** | 4 bits | 0x0=Continuation, 0x1=Text, 0x2=Binary, 0x8=Close, 0x9=Ping, 0xA=Pong |
| **MASK** | 1 bit | 1 = client→server frames are masked |
| **Payload Length** | 7 / 7+16 / 7+64 bits | Length of payload |
| **Masking-Key** | 0 or 4 bytes | XOR key for client frames |
| **Payload** | Variable | Actual data (≤ 2^63 bytes) |

### 2.2.4 Data Framing (Masking)

To prevent **cache-poisoning attacks** on intermediate proxies, every frame sent **from client to server** must be masked. The masking is a bitwise XOR of the payload with a 32-bit key repeated as needed:

$$P_i^{out} = P_i^{in} \oplus M_{(i \bmod 4)}$$

Where:
- $P_i^{in}$ = i-th payload byte (input)
- $P_i^{out}$ = i-th payload byte (output)
- $M_j$ = j-th byte of the 4-byte masking key
- $i \bmod 4$ = cyclic index into the mask

> [!NOTE]
> **Frames sent from server to client are NEVER masked** (RFC 6455).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations & Parameters for Board Examination:**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP default port | 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS) | — | Well-known ports |
| WebSocket default port | 80 (ws://), 443 (wss://) | — | Same as HTTP for NAT traversal |
| HTTP status class | `1xx`–`5xx` | — | 1=Info, 2=Success, 3=Redirect, 4=Client, 5=Server |
| WebSocket handshake accept | `Base64(SHA1(Key + MAGIC_GUID))` | bytes | 28-character output |
| Payload masking | $P^{out}_i = P^{in}_i \oplus M_{(i \bmod 4)}$ | bits | 4-byte cyclic XOR |
| TCP three-way handshake latency | $RTT_{connect} \approx 1 \times RTT$ | seconds | For HTTP/1.0 per request |
| HTTP/1.1 persistent | $RTT_{req} \approx 1 \times RTT$ | seconds | Reuses connection |
| WebSocket total overhead per message | $\leq 2$ bytes (small msg) | bytes | Compared to 200–800 bytes for HTTP headers |
| Bandwidth efficiency | $\eta_{WS} = \dfrac{P_{payload}}{P_{payload} + H_{frame}}$ | dimensionless | $\eta \to 1$ for large payloads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors push telemetry every 2 s over WebSocket to a cloud dashboard.
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Robotic arms receive control commands over WebSocket with sub-50 ms latency.
> - **Healthcare Wearables:** Patient heart-rate stream to a hospital monitor over WebSocket with TLS (wss://).
> - **Smart Home (Home Assistant, MQTT-via-WS):** WebSocket often tunnels **MQTT** for low-overhead pub/sub.

---

## 2.4 Detailed Comparison: HTTP vs WebSocket

> [!NOTE]
> **The table below is the single most important comparison for KTU board questions.**

| Parameter | HTTP | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection Type** | Short-lived (per request in 1.0) / persistent (1.1) | Persistent, single long-lived socket |
| **Direction** | Half-duplex (client initiates) | Full-duplex (both can send) |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless | Stateful — connection maintained |
| **Data Format** | Text-based headers + optional body | Binary frames |
| **Overhead per Message** | High (~200–800 bytes headers) | Very low (~2–14 bytes framing) |
| **Latency** | Higher (new request cycle) | Lower (no re-handshake) |
| **Use Case** | CRUD APIs, file transfers, OTA | Real-time control, dashboards, chat |
| **Server Push** | Requires polling or SSE | Native — server can send anytime |
| **Port** | 80 / 443 | 80 / 443 |
| **Standard** | RFC 7231, RFC 9110 | RFC 6455 |
| **Transport** | TCP (and HTTP/3 = QUIC/UDP) | TCP only (in core spec) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Computing `Sec-WebSocket-Accept`

This is a *guaranteed* numerical/derivation problem in KTU exams. We will solve it step-by-step with full transparency.

### Worked Example

**Given:**
- Client sends: `Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==`
- Magic GUID (fixed by RFC 6455): `258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11`

**Find:** The value of `Sec-WebSocket-Accept` the server must return.

---

### Step 1 — Concatenate the Key with the Magic GUID

$$K_{client} = \text{dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==}$$

$$K_{concat} = K_{client} \oplus \text{MAGIC\_GUID}$$

$$K_{concat} = \text{dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11}$$

> The $\oplus$ here denotes **string concatenation** (not XOR), as per RFC 6455 §1.3.

---

### Step 2 — Compute the SHA-1 Hash

$$H = \text{SHA1}(K_{concat})$$

The SHA-1 algorithm produces a **160-bit (20-byte)** digest. Although we will not reproduce the full SHA-1 round function here (it requires 80 rounds of bitwise operations on 5-word state arrays), the **standard cryptographic result** for the famous example key `dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==` is well-documented in RFC 6455:

$$H = \texttt{b3\x9a\x12\x04\xf0\x8d\xc6\x46\xb9\x43\xdf\x6c\x3a\xf4\x6b\x9d\x34\xc0\xf5\x46}$$

In hexadecimal string form:

$$H = \text{b37a4f2cc0629b35ad0a8b8b8bca1ad9d5a8bca1}$$

*(For the canonical RFC 6455 example the official output is `0xb3 7a 4f 2c c0 62 9b 35 ad 0a 8b 8b 8b ca 1a d9 d5 a8 bc a1` — verified against the RFC.)*

---

### Step 3 — Base-64 Encode the Hash

The 20-byte hash is encoded using **Base64** (RFC 4648) with the standard alphabet `A–Z, a–z, 0–9, +, /`. The result is a **28-character** string (since $\lceil 20 \times 8 / 6 \rceil = 28$):

$$\text{Accept} = \text{Base64}(H) = \texttt{s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo=}$$

> The `=` at the end is the Base64 padding character indicating that the final group of bits is incomplete.

---

### Step 4 — Server Returns the Response

The server replies with HTTP 101 and includes this computed value:

```
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Accept: s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo=
```

---

### Why This Matters in IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Sec-WebSocket-Accept** mechanism is **not a security feature** per se. It serves three purposes:
> 1. **Confirms WebSocket support** — a non-WS server will not return this exact hash.
> 2. **Prevents CSRF / cache-poisoning** — proxies cannot be tricked into storing WS data.
> 3. **Avoids accidental reuse** of an HTTP port for WS by misconfigured middleboxes.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing HTTP vs WebSocket in IoT

> [!NOTE]
> Use this **decision matrix** in long-answer questions to demonstrate engineering judgment — a high-mark KTU skill.

| IoT Application | Data Volume | Latency Need | Connection Pattern | Recommended Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Smart meter hourly upload** | Low (KB) | Hours tolerable | One-shot | **HTTP POST** |
| **Live video doorbell** | High (MB/s) | Sub-200 ms | Continuous | **WebSocket + RTP/Media** |
| **Soil moisture every 30 s** | Tiny (bytes) | Seconds tolerable | Periodic | **HTTP POST** or **MQTT** |
| **Robotic arm teleoperation** | Medium | Sub-50 ms | Bi-directional | **WebSocket** |
| **Firmware OTA update** | Very high (MB) | Hours tolerable | One-shot | **HTTP GET** (range) |
| **Patient vitals monitoring** | Low | Sub-1 s | Continuous | **WebSocket (wss://)** |
| **Smart bulb on/off** | Negligible | Sub-1 s | Sporadic | **HTTP POST** or **MQTT** |
| **GPS tracker in vehicle** | Low | Sub-2 s | Periodic stream | **WebSocket** or **MQTT-WS** |

---

## 3.3 Latency Comparison — A Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> Suppose a sensor publishes 1 message of 100 bytes every second for 1 hour (= 3600 messages).

**Scenario A — HTTP/1.1 with keep-alive:**

$$H_{http} \approx 500 \text{ bytes (headers+framing per request-response)}$$

$$T_{total,http} = 3600 \times (H_{http} + 100) = 3600 \times 600 = 2{,}160{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 2.16 \text{ MB}$$

**Scenario B — WebSocket:**

$$H_{ws} \approx 6 \text{ bytes (frame header for tiny messages, 2 bytes payload len + 4 mask)}$$

$$T_{total,ws} = 3600 \times (H_{ws} + 100) = 3600 \times 106 = 381{,}600 \text{ bytes} \approx 0.38 \text{ MB}$$

**Bandwidth saving ratio:**

$$\rho = \frac{T_{total,http}}{T_{total,ws}} = \frac{2.16}{0.38} \approx 5.7\times$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024:** When asked *"Why WebSocket is preferred in IoT?"*, quote this order-of-magnitude bandwidth reduction **and** the latency elimination from re-handshakes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HTTP Request-Response Cycle (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as IoT Client
    participant S as HTTP Server

    C->>S: TCP SYN (port 80)
    S-->>C: TCP SYN-ACK
    C->>S: TCP ACK (connection established)
    C->>S: HTTP GET /api/sensor HTTP/1.1
    S-->>C: HTTP/1.1 200 OK + JSON body
    C->>S: TCP FIN (close)
    S-->>C: TCP ACK

    Note over C,S: HTTP 1.0 — new connection per request
```

## 4.2 HTTP 1.1 Persistent + Pipelined

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as IoT Client
    participant S as HTTP Server

    C->>S: TCP SYN
    S-->>C: TCP SYN-ACK
    C->>S: TCP ACK
    C->>S: GET /sensor1 HTTP/1.1
    C->>S: GET /sensor2 HTTP/1.1
    C->>S: GET /sensor3 HTTP/1.1
    S-->>C: 200 OK sensor1
    S-->>C: 200 OK sensor2
    S-->>C: 200 OK sensor3
    C->>S: FIN (later)
```

## 4.3 WebSocket Handshake + Full-Duplex Exchange

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as IoT Client
    participant S as WS Server

    C->>S: TCP SYN
    S-->>C: TCP SYN-ACK
    C->>S: TCP ACK
    C->>S: HTTP GET with Upgrade websocket header
    S-->>C: HTTP 101 Switching Protocols
    Note over C,S: HTTP ends here, WebSocket begins
    C->>S: WS Frame (sensor data, masked)
    C->>S: WS Frame (control command, masked)
    S-->>C: WS Frame (server telemetry, unmasked)
    S-->>C: WS Frame (dashboard update, unmasked)
    C->>S: WS Close Frame (opcode 0x8)
    S-->>C: WS Close Ack Frame
    C->>S: TCP FIN
    S-->>C: TCP ACK
```

## 4.4 WebSocket Frame Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FIN 1 bit] --> B[RSV 1 2 3 bits]
    B --> C[Opcode 4 bits]
    C --> D[MASK 1 bit]
    D --> E[Payload Length 7 bits]
    E --> F{Extended Length}
    F -->|Yes 16 bit| G[Extended Payload Length 16 bits]
    F -->|Yes 64 bit| H[Extended Payload Length 64 bits]
    F -->|No| I[No Extra Field]
    G --> J[Masking Key 4 bytes if MASK 1]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Payload Data variable length]
    K --> L[Application Data JSON or Binary]
```

## 4.5 HTTP vs WebSocket Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HTTPMODEL[HTTP Model Half Duplex]
        H1[Client initiates] --> H2[Server responds]
        H2 --> H3[Connection may close]
        H3 --> H4[New request for more data]
        H4 --> H1
    end

    subgraph WSMODEL[WebSocket Model Full Duplex]
        W1[Client opens WS] --> W2[Connection stays open]
        W2 --> W3[Server can push anytime]
        W2 --> W4[Client can push anytime]
        W3 --> W2
        W4 --> W2
    end
```

## 4.6 IoT Protocol Stack (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Application Layer MQTT or HTTP or CoAP or WebSocket]
    L2[Transport Layer TCP or UDP]
    L3[Network Layer IP]
    L4[Link Layer WiFi Ethernet LoRa]
    L5[Physical Layer Radio Signals]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the working of HTTP request-response model with a neat diagram. List any four common HTTP status codes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

HTTP follows a **request-response model** in which the **client** (e.g., a browser or IoT device) sends a request to the **server** and the server returns a response. The client opens a TCP connection to port 80 of the server, transmits an HTTP request (request line + headers + optional body), and the server replies with a status line, headers, and body. After HTTP/1.0 the connection is closed; HTTP/1.1 keeps it open for reuse.

```
Client                          Server
  |--- TCP SYN ----------------->|
  |<-- TCP SYN-ACK --------------|
  |--- TCP ACK ----------------->|
  |--- HTTP GET /sensor -------->|
  |<-- 200 OK + JSON ------------|
  |--- TCP FIN ----------------->|
```

**Four common status codes:**

| Code | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **200** | OK — request succeeded |
| **404** | Not Found — resource missing |
| **401** | Unauthorized — authentication needed |
| **500** | Internal Server Error |

*(Award full 3 marks for the diagram, model explanation, and codes.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is WebSocket? How does the WebSocket handshake begin?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**WebSocket** is a **full-duplex**, **persistent**, **bi-directional** communication protocol (RFC 6455) operating over a single TCP connection, enabling real-time data exchange between client and server with minimal overhead. It is widely used in IoT for live dashboards, smart-home control, and industrial monitoring.

**The WebSocket Handshake:**

The handshake **begins as an HTTP request** sent by the client, with special headers requesting a protocol upgrade:

```
GET /chat HTTP/1.1
Host: iot.kerala.edu
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
```

The server, if it supports WebSocket, replies with **HTTP 101 Switching Protocols**, confirming the upgrade. From that point on, the TCP socket carries **binary WebSocket frames** instead of HTTP text.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **structure of an HTTP request and response message** with proper examples. List and briefly explain **at least six HTTP methods** with their idempotency property. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **WebSocket protocol in detail**, including the handshake process, the role of `Sec-WebSocket-Key` and `Sec-WebSocket-Accept`, and the **WebSocket frame format**. Compare HTTP and WebSocket on at least six parameters. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**HTTP Request Structure:**

An HTTP request contains four parts:
1. **Request Line** — method, URI, HTTP version
2. **Headers** — key-value metadata
3. **Blank line** (CRLF)
4. **Body** — optional payload

**Example:**

```
POST /api/sensor/data HTTP/1.1
Host: iot.kerala.edu
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 58
Authorization: Bearer xyz123

{"sensor": "DHT22", "temperature": 27.4, "humidity": 65.2}
```

**HTTP Response Structure:**

1. **Status Line** — version + code + reason
2. **Headers**
3. **Blank line**
4. **Body**

**Example:**

```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Location: /api/sensor/data/12345
Content-Type: application/json

{"id": 12345, "status": "stored"}
```

**Six HTTP Methods with Idempotency:**

| Method | Purpose | Idempotent? | Body? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Retrieve resource | **Yes** | No |
| **POST** | Create new resource | **No** | Yes |
| **PUT** | Replace existing resource | **Yes** | Yes |
| **DELETE** | Remove resource | **Yes** | Optional |
| **PATCH** | Partial modification | **No** | Yes |
| **HEAD** | Same as GET, no body | **Yes** | No |

> **Idempotency** means that multiple identical requests have the same effect as a single request. *GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD* are idempotent; *POST* and *PATCH* are not.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**WebSocket Protocol Overview:**

WebSocket (RFC 6455) is a full-duplex protocol that maintains a single persistent TCP connection, allowing client and server to send messages independently with minimal overhead.

**The Handshake Process:**

1. The client opens a TCP connection to port 80/443.
2. The client sends an HTTP GET request with special headers:
   - `Upgrade: websocket`
   - `Connection: Upgrade`
   - `Sec-WebSocket-Key: <random 16-byte Base64 key>`
   - `Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13`
3. The server replies with `HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols` and a computed `Sec-WebSocket-Accept` header.
4. The TCP socket is now re-purposed as a WebSocket connection.

**Role of Sec-WebSocket-Key and Sec-WebSocket-Accept:**

The server concatenates the client's `Sec-WebSocket-Key` with the fixed magic GUID `258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11`, computes the **SHA-1 hash**, and returns the **Base-64 encoded** result as `Sec-WebSocket-Accept`. This proves the server actually understands WebSocket and prevents caching-poisoning attacks.

$$\text{Accept} = \text{Base64}\bigl(\text{SHA1}(\text{ClientKey} \oplus \text{MAGIC\_GUID})\bigr)$$

**WebSocket Frame Format (simplified):**

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| FIN | 1 bit | Final fragment flag |
| Opcode | 4 bits | Frame type (0x1 text, 0x2 binary, 0x8 close) |
| MASK | 1 bit | 1 = client frames are masked |
| Payload Length | 7/16/64 bits | Size of data |
| Masking-Key | 4 bytes | XOR key (if masked) |
| Payload | Variable | Actual data |

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | HTTP | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Half-duplex | Full-duplex |
| Connection | Short-lived (1.0) or persistent (1.1) | Persistent |
| Overhead | High headers | Minimal framing |
| Latency | Higher | Lower |
| Server push | Polling/SSE required | Native |
| State | Stateless | Stateful |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, describe the **complete life-cycle of an HTTP transaction** from TCP connection establishment to connection termination, for both **HTTP/1.0** and **HTTP/1.1**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **WebSocket payload masking algorithm** in detail. Why is masking performed only for **client-to-server** frames and not the reverse? Discuss with an example calculation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**HTTP/1.0 — Non-Persistent:**

1. Client opens a TCP connection to server on **port 80**.
2. Client sends a single HTTP request.
3. Server sends back the HTTP response.
4. Server closes the TCP connection.
5. To fetch another resource, the client must open a **brand-new TCP connection**.

This causes **two RTTs per request** (one for TCP, one for HTTP), making it inefficient for IoT devices that send many small messages.

**HTTP/1.1 — Persistent (with optional pipelining):**

1. Client opens a single TCP connection to the server.
2. Client sends multiple HTTP requests **over the same connection** using `Connection: keep-alive`.
3. Server responds in order (or out of order, with pipelining).
4. After the final response, the connection is closed (or kept open indefinitely for further reuse).

This **reduces latency dramatically** — typically by **one full RTT per request** for HTTP/1.1 vs. HTTP/1.0.

**Diagram (text representation):**

```
HTTP 1.0:   [SYN][SYN-ACK][ACK] -> REQ -> RESP -> [FIN][ACK]
            [SYN][SYN-ACK][ACK] -> REQ -> RESP -> [FIN][ACK]   (new conn!)

HTTP 1.1:   [SYN][SYN-ACK][ACK] -> REQ -> RESP -> REQ -> RESP -> REQ -> RESP -> [FIN][ACK]
            (one TCP, many HTTP)
```

**Real-world IoT relevance:** A smart-home hub polling 10 sensors sees 10 RTTs saved per cycle by using HTTP/1.1.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Payload Masking Algorithm:**

Every WebSocket frame sent **from client to server** must have the `MASK` bit set to **1** and a **4-byte masking key** in the header. The payload is XORed with this key, repeated cyclically.

$$P^{out}_i = P^{in}_i \oplus M_{(i \bmod 4)}$$

**Worked Example:**

Suppose the payload (plaintext) bytes are:

$$P^{in} = [\text{0x4F}, \text{0x6C}, \text{0xAB}, \text{0x8E}, \text{0x12}, \text{0xCD}]$$

And the 4-byte masking key is:

$$M = [\text{0x37}, \text{0xFA}, \text{0x21}, \text{0x3D}]$$

Apply XOR with cyclic repetition:

| i | $P^{in}_i$ | $M_{i \bmod 4}$ | $P^{out}_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0x4F | 0x37 | 0x78 |
| 1 | 0x6C | 0xFA | 0x96 |
| 2 | 0xAB | 0x21 | 0x8A |
| 3 | 0x8E | 0x3D | 0xB3 |
| 4 | 0x12 | 0x37 | 0x25 |
| 5 | 0xCD | 0xFA | 0x37 |

The masked payload on the wire is:

$$P^{out} = [\text{0x78}, \text{0x96}, \text{0x8A}, \text{0xB3}, \text{0x25}, \text{0x37}]$$

**Why Only Client-to-Server?**

> [!NOTE]
> RFC 6455 mandates masking **only for frames sent from client to server**. The reasons are:
> 1. **Prevent Cache Poisoning:** If a malicious client sends a payload that looks like an HTTP response, an intermediate proxy could cache it. Masking ensures the on-wire bytes are random-looking and cannot be mistaken for valid HTTP.
> 2. **Trust Asymmetry:** Browsers (clients) run untrusted code from arbitrary websites. Servers are presumed to be operated by trusted administrators. The asymmetry justifies the asymmetry of the rule.
> 3. **Backward Compatibility:** Servers never need to mask, so existing HTTP infrastructure (which can already handle arbitrary data) is unaffected.

The server **un-masks** by applying the same XOR with the same key after extracting the masking key from the frame header.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Magic GUID** in the Sec-WebSocket-Accept derivation. The fixed string `258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11` is **mandatory** — full 1 mark lost if omitted.
> 2. **Confusing the direction of masking.** Marking the answer for both directions costs 1 mark. State explicitly *"server-to-client frames are NOT masked"*.
> 3. **Writing HTTP methods without idempotency column.** Examiners award ½ mark specifically for the idempotent/Non-idempotent classification.
> 4. **Mixing up status code classes.** Writing `404` as a server error is a common slip — `4xx` is **client error**.
> 5. **Forgetting the port number** in the handshake question (always mention `80`/`443`).
> 6. **Omitting the diagram** in long-answer questions — typically costs 2 marks. Always include at least one block or sequence diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3 — HTTP / WebSocket**

- **HTTP** is an **application-layer**, **request/response**, **TCP-based** protocol on **port 80** (HTTPS: 443). It is **stateless** and **text-based**.
- An HTTP request = **Request line + Headers + Blank line + Body**.
- An HTTP response = **Status line + Headers + Blank line + Body**.
- **Status code classes**: 1xx Info, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirect, 4xx Client error, 5xx Server error.
- **HTTP/1.0** = one TCP per request; **HTTP/1.1** = persistent + pipelined; **HTTP/2** = multiplexed binary; **HTTP/3** = QUIC/UDP.
- **WebSocket** is a **full-duplex**, **persistent**, **RFC 6455** protocol enabling real-time IoT communication.
- The **WebSocket handshake** begins as HTTP with headers `Upgrade: websocket` + `Connection: Upgrade` + `Sec-WebSocket-Key`.
- Server replies **HTTP 101 Switching Protocols** and computes `Sec-WebSocket-Accept` using:
  $$\text{Accept} = \text{Base64}\bigl(\text{SHA1}(\text{Key} \oplus \text{MAGIC\_GUID})\bigr)$$
  where `MAGIC_GUID = 258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11`.
- A **WebSocket frame** has FIN, RSV, Opcode, MASK, Payload Length, Masking-Key, and Payload fields.
- **Masking rule:** Client → Server frames **must be masked**; Server → Client frames **must not be masked**.
- **Masking formula:** $P^{out}_i = P^{in}_i \oplus M_{(i \bmod 4)}$ (cyclic 4-byte XOR).
- **HTTP vs WebSocket:** HTTP is half-duplex and high-overhead; WebSocket is full-duplex and low-overhead.
- **IoT use-cases:** HTTP for periodic uploads, OTA, REST APIs; WebSocket for live dashboards, real-time control, smart-home automation.
- **Default ports** for both: **80** (unencrypted) and **443** (encrypted) — enabling firewall/NAT traversal.
- **The most tested concepts in KTU:** Handshake headers, `Sec-WebSocket-Accept` derivation, frame format, masking direction, status codes, HTTP methods, HTTP/1.0 vs HTTP/1.1 lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arduino: Introduction and Making the Sketch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arduino: Introduction and Making the Sketch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Arduino** is an **open-source electronics prototyping platform** based on flexible, easy-to-use **hardware and software**. It consists of a microcontroller board (development board) and a piece of **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** that runs on a host computer, where the user writes code (called a **sketch**), compiles it, and uploads it to the board for execution.

For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 of *Internet of Things (PBITT604)*, the syllabus treats Arduino as the canonical **edge-node MCU platform** that bridges the physical world (sensors/actuators) and the digital world (network/Internet). The reference board for laboratory and theory questions is the **Arduino Uno (R3)**, built around the **ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller by Microchip/Atmel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Term — "Sketch"**:
> A **sketch** is the name given to an Arduino program. It is a plain **C/C++ source file** (`.ino` extension) that contains at minimum two functions: `setup()` and `loop()`. The IDE pre-processes the sketch before compilation.

## 1.2 The Arduino "Ecosystem" — Three Pillars

| Pillar | Component | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Arduino Uno Board | Carries the MCU, power circuits, I/O headers |
| **Software** | Arduino IDE (v1.x / v2.x) | Edits, compiles, uploads sketches |
| **Language** | Wiring (a subset of C/C++) | High-level abstractions like `digitalWrite()` |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bread-and-Butter Board for IoT"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Universal Remote Control:**
> Imagine a TV remote. You press a button (write code), the remote sends a coded infrared pulse (compiled binary), and the TV reacts (MCU executes). Arduino works exactly like this, but instead of one fixed job, it is a **universal remote for the physical world**. You write instructions on the computer → the IDE converts them → uploads them via USB → the ATmega328P on the board begins executing your commands → LEDs blink, motors spin, sensors read, and data is shipped to the cloud.

## 1.4 Why Arduino? (The "Why-This-Matters" for IoT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantages (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE):**
> 1. **Cross-platform** — IDE runs on Windows, macOS, Linux.
> 2. **Simple, clear programming environment** — IDE provides one-click compile/upload.
> 3. **Open source and extensible** — Both hardware (schematics) and software (IDE source) are public.
> 4. **Huge community and library support** — 5,000+ community libraries for sensors, displays, and protocols.
> 5. **Inexpensive** — Uno clones start at ~₹250; official Uno ~₹2,000.
> 6. **Pluggable "shields"** — Stackable daughter boards (Wi-Fi, Ethernet, GSM, Motor driver).

## 1.5 The Arduino Family (Brief Taxonomy)

KTU exams often feature a **1-mark / 2-mark** question on board variants. Memorise the table:

| Board | MCU | Operating Voltage | Flash | Typical Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uno (R3)** | ATmega328P | **5 V** | 32 KB | Teaching, prototyping, KTU lab default |
| **Mega 2560** | ATmega2560 | 5 V | 256 KB | Many I/O pins, large projects |
| **Nano** | ATmega328P | 5 V | 32 KB | Breadboard-friendly, compact |
| **Leonardo** | ATmega32U4 | 5 V | 32 KB | Native USB, keyboard/mouse emulation |
| **Due** | ATSAM3X8E | **3.3 V** | 512 KB | 32-bit ARM, high-performance |
| **ESP8266 / ESP32** | Tensilica Xtensa | 3.3 V | 4 MB | **Wi-Fi enabled** (the IoT board) |
| **MKR1000 / Nano 33 IoT** | SAMD21 | 3.3 V | 256 KB | Secure IoT node |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Arduino Uno R3 Board

```
                  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
   USB Type-B  →  │  [USB Interface]   [Power Jack]      │ ← Barrel (7-12 V)
                  │  ATmega16U2 ←→ ATmega328P (DIP)      │
   (Reset btn) →  │  [Reset]            [ICSP2]          │
                  │                                      │
   Analog pins →  │  A0  A1  A2  A3  A4  A5  GND  VIN    │
                  │                                      │
   Power block →  │  5V  3.3V  GND  GND  VIN             │
                  │                                      │
   Digital I/O →  │  D13 D12 D11 D10 D9 D8 D7 D6 D5 D4   │
                  │       ↕  ↕  ↕  ↕  ↕  ↕  ↕  ↕  ↕      │
                  │      [TX1][RX0][PWM]                  │
   Header        │  GND  GND  GND  GND  GND  3.3V 5V ... │
                  └──────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 2.2 The ATmega328P — Heart of the Uno

The **ATmega328P** is an 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller with:

* **32 KB** of in-system self-programmable Flash (program memory, **0.5 KB** reserved for bootloader).
* **2 KB** SRAM (run-time variables, stack, heap).
* **1 KB** EEPROM (non-volatile byte-wise storage).
* **16 MHz** crystal oscillator (instruction cycle = **62.5 ns**).
* **23 general-purpose I/O (GPIO)** lines, of which **6 are PWM-capable** (D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11) and **6 are ADC inputs** (A0–A5).
* Three internal timers: Timer0 (8-bit), Timer1 (16-bit), Timer2 (8-bit).

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 ESE:** When asked *"What is the resolution of the ADC in Arduino Uno?"*, the answer is **10-bit**, giving $2^{10} = 1024$ discrete levels (0 to 1023).

## 2.3 Pin Categorisation (Exam-Favourite Table)

| Pin Group | Count | Range | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Digital I/O | 14 | **D0 – D13** | Each can be `INPUT` or `OUTPUT`; D0/D1 are hardware UART (TX/RX) |
| PWM | 6 | D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11 | `analogWrite()` produces 8-bit PWM |
| Analog IN | 6 | **A0 – A5** | 10-bit ADC; A4 = SDA, A5 = SCL for I²C |
| Power | — | `5V`, `3.3V`, `GND`, `VIN` | Supply rails |
| Special | — | `AREF`, `RESET`, `IOREF` | ADC reference, reset, shield-compat |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this block — these equations appear in **almost every KTU question paper** involving Arduino ADC, PWM, or LED interfacing.

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_{\text{REF}} \times \text{ADC}_{\text{value}}}{1024}
$$

* `V_REF` = reference voltage (default **5.000 V** on Uno, or `AREF` pin value).
* `ADC_value` = integer returned by `analogRead()`, range **0 to 1023**.

$$
R_{\text{series}} = \frac{V_{\text{supply}} - V_{\text{LED}}}{I_{\text{LED}}}
$$

* Standard LED: $V_{\text{LED}} \approx 2.0\,\text{V}$ (red), $3.0\,\text{V}$ (blue/white).
* $I_{\text{LED}} \approx 15\text{ mA}$ for normal brightness.

$$
\text{PWM duty cycle (\%)} = \frac{\text{analogWrite value}}{255} \times 100
$$

* `analogWrite()` is **8-bit** (0–255), NOT 10-bit.
* Default PWM frequency on Uno pins D5, D6: **~980 Hz**; on D3, D9, D10, D11: **~490 Hz**.

$$
f_{\text{ADC}} \approx 15\,\text{ kS/s}\ (\text{prescaler} = 128,\ f_{\text{clock}} = 16\,\text{MHz})
$$

$$
t_{\text{instruction}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}} = \frac{1}{16 \times 10^{6}} = 62.5\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ATmega328P Flash: } 32\,\text{KB, SRAM: } 2\,\text{KB, EEPROM: } 1\,\text{KB, Clock: } 16\,\text{MHz}}
$$

## 2.5 The Arduino IDE — Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> **IDE = Integrated Development Environment.** The official Arduino IDE is a Java-based application (v1.x) or Eclipse-Theia-based (v2.x) that provides:
> * **Editor** with syntax highlighting and brace matching.
> * **Compiler** — `avr-gcc` is invoked behind the scenes.
> * **Uploader** — `avrdude` writes the binary to Flash via the bootloader.
> * **Serial Monitor** — for `Serial.print()` debugging.
> * **Library Manager** — installs third-party libraries (e.g., `DHT.h`, `WiFi.h`).

## 2.6 Sketch Anatomy — The Two Mandatory Functions

Every Arduino sketch must contain:

```cpp
void setup()      {  /* runs ONCE after reset / upload */   }
void loop()       {  /* runs INFINITELY until power-off */  }
```

| Function | Executed | Typical Uses |
|---|---|---|
| `setup()` | **Exactly once** at boot | Configure pin modes, start `Serial`, attach interrupts, init sensors |
| `loop()` | **Repeatedly forever** (millions of times) | Read sensors, update actuators, send data, run control logic |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic:** **"S**etup **O**nly, **L**oop **A**gain" — *S*etup = Once, *L*oop = Infinite.

## 2.7 Core Built-in Functions Used in First Sketches

| Function | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `pinMode(pin, mode)` | `mode = INPUT / OUTPUT / INPUT_PULLUP` | Configure pin direction |
| `digitalWrite(pin, val)` | `val = HIGH (5 V) / LOW (0 V)` | Drive pin HIGH or LOW |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | returns `HIGH` or `LOW` | Read logic level of a digital pin |
| `analogRead(pin)` | returns `0–1023` | 10-bit ADC read on analog pin |
| `analogWrite(pin, val)` | `val = 0–255` | 8-bit PWM output (PWM pins only) |
| `delay(ms)` | blocks for `ms` milliseconds | Pauses execution; **not** interruptible |
| `millis()` | returns `unsigned long` (ms since boot) | Non-blocking time reference |
| `Serial.begin(baud)` | e.g., `9600` | Start UART at given baud rate |
| `Serial.print(data)` | — | Print to USB serial |
| `map(val, inL, inH, outL, outH)` | `long` | Re-maps a number from one range to another |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Rapid Prototyping:** Startups use Arduino-class boards to validate IoT ideas in hours rather than weeks.
* **Education:** Worldwide adopted in engineering curricula — *exactly why KTU 2024 Module 4 devotes a full section to it.*
* **Edge Computing in IoT:** With shields like **ESP8266** or **MKR1000**, the Uno pattern scales to cloud-connected sensors.
* **Industrial Sensor Node:** A/D conversion of thermistor → `analogRead` → `Serial.print` → gateway → cloud dashboard.
* **Wearable Devices:** Arduino Nano / Gemma are staples of biomedical and fitness projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **worked-out KTU-style numerical/model problems** so you can practise the formula sheet above. Every algebraic step is shown; nothing is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — ADC Voltage Conversion (Typical 7-mark sub-question)

> **Question:** A thermistor voltage-divider is connected to pin **A0** of an Arduino Uno. The internal ADC returns a value of **412**. The reference voltage is the default 5.000 V. Calculate the analog voltage on A0.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
