```
        DELAY 1 ms                       // Settle time
        
        FOR j = 0 TO COLS-1:
            IF READ(col_pins[j]) == LOW THEN
                DELAY DEBOUNCE_MS        // Anti-bounce
                IF READ(col_pins[j]) == LOW THEN
                    pressed_key = key_map[i][j]
                    WAIT UNTIL key released  // Blocking wait
                    RETURN pressed_key
                END IF
            END IF
        END FOR
        
        SET row_pins[i] = HIGH          // Deactivate row i
    END FOR
END PROCEDURE
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Matrix Structure (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        R1P["ROW PIN R1"]
        R2P["ROW PIN R2"]

```

---

## 4.2 Row-Column Scanning Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START"] --> B["INITIALIZE ROWS AS OUTPUT AND COLS AS INPUT PULLUP"]
    B --> C["SET ROW 1 LOW OTHERS HIGH"]
    C --> D["READ ALL 4 COLUMNS"]
    D --> E{"ANY COLUMN READS LOW"}
    E -- NO --> F["SET ROW 1 HIGH"]
    F --> G["SET ROW 2 LOW OTHERS HIGH"]
    G --> H["READ ALL 4 COLUMNS"]
    H --> I{"ANY COLUMN READS LOW"}
    I -- NO --> J["SET ROW 2 HIGH"]
    J --> K["SET ROW 3 LOW OTHERS HIGH"]
    K --> L["READ ALL 4 COLUMNS"]
    L --> M{"ANY COLUMN READS LOW"}
    M -- NO --> N["SET ROW 3 HIGH"]
    N --> O["SET ROW 4 LOW OTHERS HIGH"]
    O --> P["READ ALL 4 COLUMNS"]
    P --> Q{"ANY COLUMN READS LOW"}
    Q -- NO --> R["SET ROW 4 HIGH"]
    R --> S["REPEAT CYCLE FROM ROW 1"]
    E -- YES --> T["WAIT DEBOUNCE 20 MS"]
    T --> U["CONFIRM COLUMN STILL LOW"]
    U -- YES --> V["MAP ROW AND COL TO KEY CHARACTER"]
    V --> W["WAIT FOR KEY RELEASE"]
    W --> X["RETURN KEY TO CALLING PROGRAM"]
    I -- YES --> T
    M -- YES --> T
    Q -- YES --> T
    X --> S
```

---

## 4.3 Typical Interfacing Block Diagram (Arduino/ESP32)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KEYPAD_BLOCK["4x4 MEMBRANE KEYPAD"]
        KEYPADHW["8 PIN CONNECTOR R1 R2 R3 R4 C1 C2 C3 C4"]
    end

    subgraph MCU_BLOCK["ARDUINO UNO OR ESP32"]
        GPIO["GPIO PINS 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9"]
        LOGIC["FIRMWARE SCANNING ALGORITHM"]
        SERIAL["SERIAL MONITOR OR LCD DISPLAY"]
    end

    subgraph VCC_BLOCK["POWER AND REFERENCE"]
        V5V["5V OR 3.3V SUPPLY"]
        GND["COMMON GROUND"]
    end

    KEYPADHW -->|8 SIGNAL WIRES| GPIO
    GPIO -->|DIGITAL READ WRITE| LOGIC
    LOGIC -->|DECODED CHARACTER| SERIAL
    V5V -->|VCC| KEYPADHW
    GND -->|REFERENCE| KEYPADHW
    GND -->|REFERENCE| MCU_BLOCK
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Wiring Note:** All 8 keypad pins share a **common ground** reference with the microcontroller. The columns MUST be configured with **pull-up resistors** (internal `INPUT_PULLUP` is preferred to save external components). Without pull-ups, the columns will exhibit **floating input** and produce **ghost key presses**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Q: What is a matrix keypad? Explain the concept of key debouncing with respect to keypad interfacing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **matrix keypad** is an arrangement of push-button switches organized in a grid of rows and columns, where each key is located at the intersection of one row and one column conductor. This arrangement reduces the number of microcontroller I/O pins required to interface multiple keys.

**Key Debouncing (3 marks breakdown):**
- **Problem:** Mechanical push-buttons exhibit "contact bounce" — when pressed, the metal contacts physically vibrate and make/break connection several times over **5–25 ms**, producing multiple false signals.
- **Solution:** Software debouncing is implemented by introducing a **delay (typically 20 ms)** after the first detection of a key press, then **re-reading** the column to confirm the key is still pressed. This eliminates spurious multiple detections of a single press.
- **Alternative:** Hardware debounce using an **RC low-pass filter** (resistor + capacitor) can also be used.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: List any three advantages of using a matrix keypad over individual push-buttons in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pin Optimization:** A 4×4 matrix keypad requires only **8 GPIO pins** instead of **16**, saving **50%** of the microcontroller's I/O resources — critical for IoT boards like NodeMCU with limited pins. *(1 mark)*

2. **Compact Wiring:** A single 8-pin ribbon cable replaces 16 individual wires, reducing PCB complexity, cost, and assembly time. *(1 mark)*

3. **Scalability:** Adding more keys requires only one additional row or column pin, not a complete rewiring — making it easy to extend from 3×4 to 4×4 to 4×5 layouts. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU Pattern: Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice. Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a **4×4 matrix keypad with an Arduino Uno**. Mention the pin connections and explain the row-column scanning algorithm with a suitable lookup table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram & Connections (3 marks):**

| Keypad Pin | Arduino Pin | Configuration |
|------------|-------------|---------------|
| R1 | D9 | OUTPUT |
| R2 | D8 | OUTPUT |
| R3 | D7 | OUTPUT |
| R4 | D6 | OUTPUT |
| C1 | D5 | INPUT_PULLUP |
| C2 | D4 | INPUT_PULLUP |
| C3 | D3 | INPUT_PULLUP |
| C4 | D2 | INPUT_PULLUP |

Common **GND** is shared between keypad and Arduino.

**Scanning Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. Set all row pins `HIGH`.
2. For each row `i` from 0 to 3:
   - Set `row[i] = LOW`.
   - Read all 4 column pins.
   - If any column reads `LOW`, the pressed key is at `(row=i, col=j)`.
   - Wait 20 ms, re-verify (debounce).
   - Map `(i, j)` to a character using the lookup table.
   - Set `row[i] = HIGH` and continue.
3. Repeat continuously.

**Lookup Table (1 mark):**

$$
\text{key}[i][j] = \begin{bmatrix}
'1' & '2' & '3' & 'A' \\
'4' & '5' & '6' & 'B' \\
'7' & '8' & '9' & 'C' \\
'*' & '0' & '#' & 'D'
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b)** Explain the concept of **key debouncing** in detail. Discuss both **software** and **hardware** debouncing techniques with suitable diagrams. Why is debouncing critical in IoT applications such as a password-based door lock? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Key debouncing is the technique of eliminating the spurious multiple signals generated due to mechanical bouncing of switch contacts during make or break. The bouncing typically lasts **5–25 ms** and produces several false transitions that the microcontroller may interpret as multiple key presses.

**Software Debouncing (2.5 marks):**
- After detecting a key press (column goes LOW), introduce a **delay of 5–25 ms**.
- Re-read the column to confirm it is still LOW.
- If confirmed, register a single valid press.
- Then **wait for key release** (column returns to HIGH) before accepting the next press.

**Hardware Debouncing (1.5 marks):**
- **RC Filter:** A resistor (10 kΩ) in series with the switch and a capacitor (0.1 µF) to ground form a low-pass filter that smooths the bouncing waveform.
- **Schmitt Trigger:** An inverter with hysteresis (e.g., 74HC14) provides clean digital transitions.

**Critical Importance in IoT Door Locks (1 mark):**
Without debouncing, a single button press could register as 3–5 presses, corrupting the entered PIN and causing **false rejection of legitimate users** or **unauthorized access** — a serious security flaw in IoT deployments.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Compare **membrane keypads** and **mechanical keypads** used in IoT applications. Explain any two **types of matrix keypads** with their construction and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Membrane Keypad | Mechanical Keypad |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Construction | Flat polyester layers with printed traces | Discrete tactile switches on PCB |
| Tactile Feedback | Low (sometimes none) | High (audible click) |
| Durability | ~1 million presses | ~5–10 million presses |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Sealing | Naturally sealed (waterproof possible) | Requires external sealing |
| Typical Use | Microwave ovens, industrial panels | Keyboards, premium IoT devices |

**Type 1: 3×4 Numeric Keypad (2 marks):**
- Contains **12 keys**: 0–9, *, #.
- Uses **7 GPIO pins** (3 rows + 4 columns).
- Common in **telephony, ATM PIN pads, security alarm panels**.

**Type 2: 4×4 Alpha-Numeric Keypad (2 marks):**
- Contains **16 keys**: 0–9, A–D, *, #.
- Uses **8 GPIO pins** (4 rows + 4 columns).
- Used in **password-based door locks, elevator panels, menu-driven HMI systems**.

**(b)** Design an **IoT-based password-protected door lock** system using a 4×4 keypad, Arduino/ESP32, a servo motor (for lock actuation), and a buzzer (for alerts). Draw the system block diagram, list the components, and write the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**System Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER["USER"] -->|KEYS PIN| KEYPAD["4x4 KEYPAD"]
    KEYPAD -->|DIGITAL SIGNALS| MCU["ARDUINO UNO OR ESP32"]
    MCU -->|PWM CONTROL SIGNAL| SERVO["SERVO MOTOR SG90 LOCK ACTUATOR"]
    MCU -->|DIGITAL HIGH LOW| BUZZER["PIEZO BUZZER FOR ALERT"]
    MCU -->|UART OR WIFI| IOT["IOT CLOUD BLYNK OR THINGSPEAK"]
    IOT -->|MOBILE APP| USER
```

**Component List (1.5 marks):**

| # | Component | Quantity | Purpose |
|---|-----------|----------|---------|
| 1 | Arduino Uno / ESP32 | 1 | Main controller |
| 2 | 4×4 Membrane Keypad | 1 | User PIN entry |
| 3 | SG90 Servo Motor | 1 | Physical lock actuator |
| 4 | Piezo Buzzer | 1 | Audible feedback |
| 5 | LED (Green + Red) | 2 | Visual access status |
| 6 | Wi-Fi Module (if Arduino) | 1 | IoT cloud logging |

**Algorithm (3.5 marks):**

```
1. INITIALIZE:
   - Keypad rows as OUTPUT, columns as INPUT_PULLUP
   - Servo motor on PWM pin (locked = 0°)
   - Buzzer and LEDs as OUTPUT
   - Set MASTER_PASSWORD = "1234" in EEPROM

2. PROCEDURE loop():
   a. DISPLAY "ENTER PIN" on LCD/Serial
   b. READ 4 keypresses using scan_keypad()
   c. IF entered_PIN == MASTER_PASSWORD THEN
         ROTATE servo to 180° (UNLOCK)
         GREEN_LED ON, BUZZER single beep
         LOG event to IoT cloud with timestamp
         WAIT 5 seconds
         ROTATE servo to 0° (RE-LOCK)
      ELSE
         RED_LED ON, BUZZER triple beep
         INCREMENT failed_attempts
         IF failed_attempts >= 3 THEN
            DISABLE keypad for 60 seconds
            SEND intrusion alert to mobile app
         END IF
      END IF
3. END PROCEDURE
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention **debouncing** in your algorithm — a common 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation if omitted.
> - **Do NOT** draw the keypad as individual buttons in a circuit diagram — the matrix structure with 4 row wires and 4 column wires is the **expected diagram**. Drawing 16 separate switches will lose **2–3 marks**.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the direction of pull-ups: columns are **INPUT_PULLUP**, rows are **OUTPUT**. Reversed roles are a frequent mistake.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention the **common ground** connection between keypad and microcontroller.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **4×4 matrix keypad** has **16 keys** but uses only **8 GPIO pins** (4 rows + 4 columns).
- [x] **Row pins** are configured as **OUTPUT**; **Column pins** are configured as **INPUT_PULLUP**.
- [x] **Scanning** = activate one row at a time (`LOW`) and read all 4 columns to detect the key.
- [x] **Pull-up resistors** are essential to prevent floating input on column lines.
- [x] **Debounce delay** of **5–25 ms** (typically 20 ms) is mandatory to filter mechanical switch bounce.
- [x] The **2D lookup table** `key[i][j]` converts hardware coordinates into user-readable characters.
- [x] **Pin savings** for a 4×4 keypad: $\eta = 50\%$ — a critical advantage for ESP8266/NodeMCU.
- [x] **Polling cycle time** for 4×4 keypad with 20 ms debounce ≈ **84 ms** (≈ 11.9 Hz scan rate).
- [x] **Common IoT applications:** smart door locks, alarm panels, vending machines, elevator controls, menu-driven HMI.
- [x] **Hardware debouncing** (RC filter / Schmitt trigger) is an alternative to software debouncing.
- [x] **Common KTU mistake:** Forgetting to wait for **key release** after detection — leads to repeated character registration.
- [x] **Reserved keywords** like `*` and `#` are often used as `ENTER` and `CLEAR` in password-based systems.
- [x] **Interrupt-based** keypad reading is more efficient than polling for battery-powered IoT nodes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading Analog Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reading Analog Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Reading Analog Values** in the context of IoT refers to the process of acquiring, sampling, and converting continuous-time, continuous-amplitude physical signals (such as temperature, light intensity, pressure, or humidity) into discrete digital values that can be processed by a microcontroller (MCU) or System on Chip (SoC). This conversion is performed by a hardware peripheral known as the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus for PBITT604, "Reading Analog Values" is framed as the foundational interface between the *physical world* (analog sensors) and the *cyber world* (digital processing), specifically targeting platforms such as **Arduino UNO**, **NodeMCU (ESP8266)**, and **ESP32**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (KTU Expected Terminology):**
> An **Analog Signal** is a continuous signal with infinite resolution in both time and amplitude. A **Digital Signal** is discrete in both time and amplitude, represented using binary (base-2) logic levels (`0` and `1`). The bridge between them is the **ADC**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a glass of water and you want to tell a friend (who is blind) exactly how much water is in the glass, but you can only speak in whole numbers from 0 to 10.

- The **actual water level** is the *analog signal* (it can be 3.7, 4.215, or any value in between).
- The act of looking at the glass and rounding to a whole number (0, 1, 2, ..., 10) is the **ADC process**.
- The **whole number you report** is the *digital value*.
- The **size of the glass** (how much water fits inside) is the **Reference Voltage ($V_{ref}$)**.
- The **number of distinct whole numbers you can say (0–10 = 11 levels)** is the **Resolution ($2^n$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this important?**
> Almost every real-world sensor (LM35 temperature, LDR light, soil moisture, potentiometer) outputs a continuously varying analog voltage. Microcontrollers CANNOT process these directly. Without ADC, the IoT node is deaf to the physical world.

## 1.3 Core Parameters of an Analog Read

| Parameter | Standard Value (Arduino UNO) | Standard Value (ESP32) |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolution ($n$)** | **10 bits** | **12 bits** (default) |
| **Reference Voltage** | **5.0 V** (default) | **3.3 V** (default) |
| **Output Range** | `0` to `1023` | `0` to `4095` |
| **Step Size / Resolution** | $\approx$ **4.88 mV** | $\approx$ **0.81 mV** |
| **ADC Pins** | `A0` to `A5` | `GPIO32` to `GPIO39` |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory of ADC

The Analog-to-Digital Conversion process is not instantaneous. It is a sequential pipeline of operations. Understanding this pipeline is critical for KTU exam derivations and numerical problems.

### Step 1: Signal Acquisition (Sampling)
The continuous-time analog signal $V_{in}(t)$ is measured at discrete time intervals $t = 0, T_s, 2T_s, \dots$ The interval $T_s$ is the **Sampling Period**, and the rate $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the **Sampling Frequency** measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **Samples per Second (S/s)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:**
> To faithfully reconstruct an analog signal from its samples, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be **at least twice** the highest frequency component $f_{max}$ present in the original signal:
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
> This minimum rate $2 \cdot f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

### Step 2: Quantization
Each sampled voltage level is "rounded off" to the nearest representable digital step. The number of discrete steps is governed by the **bit-depth (n)** of the ADC:
$$\text{Total Discrete Levels} = 2^n$$

### Step 3: Encoding
The quantized level is then converted into a binary number (e.g., `10100110` for an 8-bit ADC).

## 2.2 Core ADC Formulas (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the most frequently tested content in KTU ESE for this module. Memorize it.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$ | Digital output from analog input | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | Reconstructing voltage from digital reading | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Step size (Resolution) per LSB | Volts per step (V/step) |
| 4 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem | Hertz (Hz) |
| 5 | $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio | decibels (dB) |

> Where:
> - $D$ = Digital output value (integer)
> - $V_{in}$ = Input analog voltage (between 0 V and $V_{ref}$)
> - $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage of the ADC
> - $n$ = Number of bits (resolution) of the ADC
> - $\Delta V$ = Voltage step per least significant bit (**LSB**)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Reading soil moisture and ambient temperature to trigger irrigation systems.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Reading heart-rate (PPG) and SpO2 (blood oxygen) signals.
- **Industrial Automation:** Reading pressure, flow, and gas sensor voltages in SCADA systems.
- **Smart Energy:** Reading current/voltage from CT/PT sensors for smart meters.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Reading gas concentrations (MQ-135), PM2.5, and humidity (DHT22).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most students confuse the **step size formula**. The denominator for digital output calculation is $2^n - 1$ (because levels go from 0 to $2^n - 1$), but the denominator for step size is $2^n$ (because $2^n$ intervals are created by $2^n + 1$ levels). This is a classic 1–2 mark trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Step Size and Digital Output Relationship

Let us formally derive the step size of an ADC. The input voltage range is $[0, V_{ref}]$, which must be partitioned into a finite number of equal sub-ranges. The total number of such sub-ranges is $2^n$, since an $n$-bit converter can represent $2^n$ distinct values.

By definition, step size $\Delta V$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{\text{Total Voltage Span}}{\text{Number of Intervals}} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{ref} - 0}{2^n} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the minimum change in $V_{in}$ that the ADC can detect. Any change smaller than $\Delta V$ will not be reflected in the output (this is called **Quantization Error**).

Now, to convert a given analog voltage $V_{in}$ into a digital count $D$:

The digital output represents *how many* steps of size $\Delta V$ fit into $V_{in}$. The number of steps $D$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \\[6pt]
D &= \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref} / 2^n} \\[6pt]
D &= \frac{V_{in} \times 2^n}{V_{ref}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an 8-bit ADC, the maximum output is $2^8 - 1 = 255$. Thus the practical, normalized formula is:

$$
D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** An Arduino UNO (10-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 5$ V) is connected to an LM35 temperature sensor that outputs **250 mV** (which represents 25 °C). Calculate:
> 1. The step size ($\Delta V$) of the ADC.
> 2. The digital output value returned by `analogRead()`.
> 3. The reconstructed digital value if the ADC were 8-bit instead of 10-bit.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Step Size Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= \frac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{10}} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= \frac{5}{1024} \text{ V} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= 4.8828125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &\approx 4.88 \text{ mV per step}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Digital Output for 10-bit ADC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{10} &= \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{10} - 1) \\[6pt]
D_{10} &= \frac{0.250}{5.0} \times 1023 \\[6pt]
D_{10} &= 0.05 \times 1023 \\[6pt]
D_{10} &= 51.15
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the ADC can only output integer values, it **floors** to:
$$D_{10} = 51$$

**Part (c) — Digital Output for 8-bit ADC (Hypothetical):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{8} &= \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{8} - 1) \\[6pt]
D_{8} &= 0.05 \times 255 \\[6pt]
D_{8} &= 12.75 \rightarrow \lfloor 12.75 \rfloor = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The 10-bit ADC gives 51 levels of granularity, while the 8-bit gives only 12. Higher bit-depth = **better resolution = finer temperature measurement**. This is why the ESP32 (12-bit) is preferred for precision sensing.

## 3.3 Reconstruction Error Analysis

The **Quantization Error** $e_q$ is the maximum deviation between the true analog value and the digitally represented value. It is bounded by half of one LSB:

$$
-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \Delta V \leq e_q \leq +\frac{1}{2} \cdot \Delta V
$$

For our Arduino example:
$$e_q \leq \pm 2.44 \text{ mV} \quad \text{(equivalently } \pm 0.244 \text{ °C for LM35)}$$

## 3.4 Analog vs Digital Read — Comparison Matrix

| Property | `analogRead()` | `digitalRead()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Pin Type** | ADC-enabled pin (e.g., `A0`) | Any GPIO pin |
| **Signal Type** | Continuous (0 V to $V_{ref}$) | Binary (`HIGH` / `LOW`) |
| **Return Value** | Integer (0 to $2^n - 1$) | Boolean (`0` or `1`) |
| **Speed** | Slower (≈ 100 µs/sample) | Fast (≈ 50 ns/sample) |
| **Use Case** | Sensors (LM35, LDR, potentiometer) | Buttons, IR obstacle sensors, relays |
| **Internal Circuit** | Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC | Schmitt Trigger comparator |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Analog Reading Pipeline (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Phenomenon e.g. Temperature, Light, Pressure] --> B[Analog Sensor e.g. LM35, LDR, Potentiometer]
    B --> C[Analog Voltage V_in in Volts]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning Optional: Op-Amp / Voltage Divider / RC Filter]
    D --> E[ADC Peripheral inside MCU e.g. Arduino A0 / ESP32 GPIO34]
    E --> F[Sampling at f_s Hz Nyquist Compliance Check]
    F --> G[Quantization into 2^n discrete steps]
    G --> H[Binary Encoding to n-bit value]
    H --> I[Integer Digital Value D e.g. 0 to 1023]
    I --> J[MCU Processing: Mapping, Scaling, Thresholding]
    J --> K[Communication: UART / I2C / SPI / Wi-Fi]
    K --> L[Cloud / Dashboard / Actuator Response]
```

## 4.2 Internal ADC Architecture (Successive Approximation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[V_in Analog Input] --> Mux
    Ref[V_ref Reference Voltage] --> Mux
    Mux{Analog Multiplexer selects channel} --> Cmp[Comparator]
    SAR[SAR Logic with n-bit Register] --> DAC[Internal DAC]
    DAC --> Cmp
    Cmp --> SAR
    SAR --> Out[n-bit Digital Output D]
```

## 4.3 Typical Potentiometer Interfacing Schematic (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[+5V Arduino V_ref] --> P1[Potentiometer Terminal 1]
    P1 --> W[Wiper Terminal - Variable Tap]
    W --> A0[Arduino A0 ADC Pin]
    P2[Potentiometer Terminal 2] --> GND[Ground 0V]
    A0 --> AR[analogRead returns 0 to 1023]
    AR --> SC[Scale Map: voltage = D times 4.88 mV]
    SC --> APP[Application: LED brightness / Servo angle]
```

## 4.4 Voltage Divider with LDR — Light Sensor Reading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V5[+5V Supply] --> R[LDR Light Dependent Resistor]
    R --> Node[Junction Node]
    Node --> G[GND through fixed 10k ohm Resistor]
    Node --> ADC[Arduino A1]
    ADC --> Logic[Logic: Bright Light = Low Resistance = High Voltage = High D]
    Logic --> Act[Trigger: e.g., Turn ON Streetlight at D greater than 600]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, PBITT604]`
**Define the term "Resolution" of an ADC. An 8-bit ADC has a reference voltage of 5 V. Calculate its resolution in volts per step.**

**Model Answer:**
The **resolution** of an ADC is the smallest change in the input analog voltage that causes a change in the digital output. It is also called the **step size** or **LSB (Least Significant Bit) voltage**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= \frac{5}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= 0.01953 \text{ V} \approx 19.53 \text{ mV per step}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PBITT604]`
**State the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. Why is it important when reading analog values in IoT?**

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a continuous-time analog signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ must be sampled at a rate $f_s$ that is at least **twice** the highest frequency component to allow perfect reconstruction:
$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Importance in IoT:** If an analog sensor signal (e.g., vibration or audio) is sampled below the Nyquist rate, **aliasing** occurs, where high-frequency components falsely appear as low-frequency distortions. This leads to incorrect IoT decisions, e.g., false detection of machine failure or misclassification of audio events.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, PBITT604]`**

**(a)** Explain the working of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) using the Successive Approximation Register (SAR) technique. List its advantages and limitations.
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 3.3 V is used with an ESP32 to read a voltage from a soil moisture sensor.
   - (i) Calculate the digital output when the sensor outputs **1.65 V**. **(3 Marks)**
   - (ii) If the ADC returns the value **3000**, what is the corresponding input voltage? **(3 Marks)**
   - (iii) Calculate the step size in mV. **(1 Mark)**

#### Model Solution (a):
**SAR ADC Working:**

1. The analog input voltage $V_{in}$ is held constant during conversion (a **sample-and-hold** stage).
2. The **SAR register** starts by setting the MSB (Most Significant Bit) to `1` and all other bits to `0`, producing a trial digital code of `1000...0` (mid-scale).
3. This trial code is fed into an **internal DAC**, which produces an analog voltage $V_{DAC}$.
4. A **comparator** checks: Is $V_{DAC} > V_{in}$ or $V_{DAC} \leq V_{in}$?
5. If $V_{DAC} > V_{in}$, the MSB is reset to `0`. Otherwise, it is kept as `1`.
6. The next bit is then tested in the same fashion, and so on, until all $n$ bits are decided.
7. After $n$ cycles, the SAR holds the final digital value.

**Advantages:**
- Fast conversion (fixed $n$ cycles)
- Low power consumption
- Good trade-off between speed and resolution
- Compact hardware footprint (suited for MCUs)

**Limitations:**
- Requires a high-precision internal DAC
- Speed drops as $n$ increases
- Susceptible to noise on $V_{ref}$

#### Model Solution (b):
**(i) Digital Output at $V_{in} = 1.65$ V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^{12} - 1) \\[6pt]
D &= \frac{1.65}{3.3} \times 4095 \\[6pt]
D &= 0.5 \times 4095 \\[6pt]
D &= 2047.5 \rightarrow \lfloor 2047.5 \rfloor = 2047
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Voltage for $D = 3000$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \frac{D \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} \\[6pt]
V_{in} &= \frac{3000 \times 3.3}{4095} \\[6pt]
V_{in} &= 2.417 \text{ V (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Step Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{4096} \\[6pt]
\Delta V &= 8.056 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, PBITT604]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the process of reading an analog value in an Arduino-based IoT system. Discuss the role of `analogRead()` and the function `analogReference()`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An LM35 temperature sensor is connected to the Arduino UNO. The sensor outputs 10 mV per °C.
   - (i) At a room temperature of 27 °C, what voltage does the sensor output? **(1 Mark)**
   - (ii) What digital value will the Arduino's 10-bit ADC return? **(3 Marks)**
   - (iii) Write the Arduino sketch (code) to display this temperature on the Serial Monitor every 1 second. **(3 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):
**Block Diagram of Analog Reading in Arduino:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sensor LM35 / LDR / MQ-135] --> V[Analog Voltage 0 to 5V]
    V --> P[Arduino Analog Pin A0]
    P --> ADC[10-bit SAR ADC inside ATmega328P]
    ADC --> D[Digital Value 0 to 1023]
    D --> MCU[Sketch Processing in loop]
    MCU --> SER[Serial.println to PC]
```

**Role of `analogRead(pin)`:** Reads the analog value from the specified pin (e.g., `A0`) and returns an integer between 0 and 1023. It internally triggers the ADC with a default reference of 5 V (or 3.3 V on some boards) and a prescaler of 128, giving a sampling rate of approximately 9.6 kHz.

**Role of `analogReference(type)`:** Configures the reference voltage used by the ADC. The available types are:
- `DEFAULT` — 5 V on UNO (built-in reference)
- `INTERNAL` — 1.1 V (onboard bandgap reference, high precision for low-voltage signals)
- `EXTERNAL` — AREF pin voltage (must be between 0 V and 5 V)

Using a smaller $V_{ref}$ **increases the resolution** of the ADC for small signals (e.g., thermocouple outputs in the millivolt range).

#### Model Solution (b):
**(i) Sensor Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{out} = 27 \text{ °C} \times 10 \text{ mV/°C} = 270 \text{ mV} = 0.270 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Digital Value Returned:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{0.270}{5.0} \times 1023 \\[6pt]
D &= 0.054 \times 1023 \\[6pt]
D &= 55.242 \rightarrow \lfloor 55.242 \rfloor = 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Arduino Sketch:**

```cpp
// LM35 Temperature Reading - Arduino UNO
const int LM35_PIN = A0;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  analogReference(DEFAULT); // 5V reference
}

void loop() {
  int rawValue = analogRead(LM35_PIN);
  float voltage = rawValue * (5.0 / 1023.0);    // Reconstruct voltage
  float temperatureC = voltage * 100.0;         // 10 mV per °C => 1V = 100°C

  Serial.print("Raw ADC: ");
  Serial.print(rawValue);
  Serial.print("  |  Temperature: ");
  Serial.print(temperatureC);
  Serial.println(" °C");

  delay(1000); // 1 second
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using `1024` instead of `1023` in voltage reconstruction** — This causes a 0.1% systematic error. Always use $2^n - 1$ for digital-to-analog conversion, since `analogRead` returns 1023, not 1024.
> 2. **Confusing sensor scaling factor** — LM35 outputs 10 mV/°C. Multiplying voltage by 1000/10 = 100 gives °C, NOT 1000.
> 3. **Forgetting `float` casting** — Using `int temperature = voltage * 100;` will truncate decimals and show wrong values like `26` instead of `26.84`.
> 4. **Exceeding ADC range** — Supplying > 5 V to an Arduino analog pin will permanently damage the MCU. Always use a voltage divider for sensors above 5 V.
> 5. **Floating analog pins** — Unconnected analog pins pick up EMI noise and give random readings. Always tie unused analog pins to GND via a 100 kΩ resistor or call `analogRead()` and ignore.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ADC = Analog-to-Digital Converter.** It is the hardware that bridges analog sensors and digital MCUs.
- [x] **Arduino UNO ADC:** 10-bit, $V_{ref} = 5$ V default, range 0–1023, step size ≈ **4.88 mV**.
- [x] **ESP32 ADC:** 12-bit, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V default, range 0–4095, step size ≈ **0.81 mV**.
- [x] **Core formula (forward):** $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$.
- [x] **Core formula (reverse):** $V_{in} = \dfrac{D \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$.
- [x] **Step size:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ — memorize the denominator as $2^n$, not $2^n - 1$.
- [x] **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- [x] **Resolution vs Precision:** Resolution is theoretical (bit count). Precision is real-world (noise, $V_{ref}$ stability).
- [x] **SAR ADC:** Most common in MCUs. Uses comparator + internal DAC + successive approximation logic.
- [x] **Quantization Error:** Bounded by $\pm \frac{1}{2} \cdot \Delta V$.
- [x] **`analogRead()` returns integer**, not voltage. Conversion must be done in code.
- [x] **Sampling rate** on Arduino UNO ≈ 9.6 kHz (suitable for slow sensors like temperature, light, gas).
- [x] **For high-precision work**: use `analogReference(INTERNAL)` to reduce $V_{ref}$ and improve effective resolution.
- [x] **ESP32 ADC quirk:** GPIO34, GPIO35, GPIO36, GPIO39 are input-only — do not use them as output pins.
- [x] **Common analog sensors:** LM35 (10 mV/°C), TMP36 (10 mV/°C with 500 mV offset), LDR (resistance changes with light), MQ-2/MQ-135 (gas), potentiometer (voltage divider), soil moisture (resistive output), piezoelectric (vibration).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Getting Input from Sensors - Detecting Movement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Getting Input from Sensors - Detecting Movement # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Movement Detection Sensors** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) are specialized **transducers** that convert physical motion, displacement, velocity, or proximity changes within their Field of View (FoV) into corresponding electrical signals interpretable by microcontrollers, microprocessors, or edge-computing devices. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, these sensors form the foundational layer of the **IoT Perception Layer (Layer 1)** of the standard 5-layer IoT architecture, enabling the system to "sense" the physical world. They operate on diverse physical principles including **electromagnetic radiation sensing, acoustic wave reflection, mechanical inertia, and the Doppler Effect**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize This):** > *"A movement sensor is an electronic device that detects physical motion in its environment and generates a corresponding electrical output. In IoT, these sensors act as the primary data acquisition units, capturing kinematic parameters of objects or living beings and converting them into digital signals for network transmission and cloud analytics."* ## 1.2 Classification of Movement Sensors The four primary classes of movement sensors relevant to KTU Module 4 are: | Sensor Class | Physical Principle | Typical IoT Application | |--------------|-------------------|------------------------| | **PIR (Passive Infrared)** | Pyroelectric effect detecting IR radiation from warm bodies | Smart lighting, security alarms | | **Ultrasonic Sensor** | Time-of-Flight (ToF) of acoustic waves | Distance measurement, parking sensors | | **Accelerometer / IMU** | Mechanical inertia (mass displacement against proof mass) | Wearables, vehicle tracking, fall detection | | **Microwave / Doppler Radar** | Doppler frequency shift of reflected microwave signals | Automatic doors, traffic monitoring | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically focuses on *passive* detection mechanisms (like PIR) and *active* ranging mechanisms (like Ultrasonic HC-SR04), often combined with microcontroller boards (Arduino/NodeMCU) for edge-level decision making. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Eyes and Ears" of IoT Imagine a security guard standing at the entrance of a building at midnight. - **PIR Sensor** → Like the guard's *thermal vision goggles* — he doesn't shine any light, but he feels the "warmth" of a person walking past. - **Ultrasonic Sensor** → Like a *blind person tapping a cane* — he sends out a sound ("tap!") and listens for the echo to know *how far* something is. - **Accelerometer** → Like a *fitness band on your wrist* — it can feel the *swing* of your hand and count your steps. - **Doppler/Microwave** → Like a *police radar gun* — it sends out radio waves and measures how the frequency changes when they bounce off a moving car. > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Mnemonic — "PATD"** > - **P**assive IR → senses *warmth* > - **A**ccelerometer → senses *swing/tilt* > - **T**ime-of-Flight (Ultrasonic) → senses *distance via echo* > - **D**oppler (Microwave) → senses *speed via frequency shift* ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Emphasis) The following physical constants and standard metrics are **mandatory** for KTU numerical problems: - **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$ - **Speed of electromagnetic waves (light/radio) in vacuum/air:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ - **Standard operating frequency of HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor:** $f = 40 \text{ kHz}$ - **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **PIR detection wavelength range:** typically **8 µm to 14 µm** (human body IR peak ≈ 9.4 µm) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Working Principles ### 2.1.1 PIR (Passive Infrared) Sensor — HC-SR501 **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Infrared Emission Source:** All objects with temperature above absolute zero (0 K) emit infrared radiation as a function of their thermal energy. The human body, being at ~310 K, emits IR strongly in the **8–14 µm band** (per Stefan-Boltzmann law). 2. **Fresnel Lens Collection:** A multi-segmented **Fresnel lens** on the front of the PIR module collects IR from multiple zones and focuses it onto the pyroelectric sensor. 3. **Pyroelectric Crystal Reaction:** The internal sensor consists of **two slots** made of crystalline material. When a warm body (like a human) crosses from one zone to another, the *differential* IR incidence between the two slots changes, generating a tiny voltage (typically a few millivolts). 4. **BISS0001 ASIC Amplification:** A low-noise amplifier ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) amplifies this weak signal into a clean **3.3 V/5 V digital HIGH pulse**. 5. **Trigger Output:** The OUT pin of the sensor goes HIGH for a configurable duration (Tx time, adjustable via potentiometer) — typically **3 seconds to 5 minutes**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Passive"?** The sensor does *not* emit any energy (no IR LED, no laser). It purely *receives* ambient radiation. This is why battery-powered PIR nodes can last **2–3 years** on a single coin cell — critical for IoT deployments. ### 2.1.2 Ultrasonic Sensor — HC-SR04 **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Trigger Pulse:** The microcontroller sends a **10 µs HIGH pulse** to the TRIG pin of the HC-SR04. 2. **Burst Transmission:** The sensor emits **8 cycles of 40 kHz ultrasonic burst** from its transmitter. 3. **Wave Propagation:** The acoustic wave travels through air at $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$. 4. **Reflection and Reception:** The wave reflects off the target object and is captured by the receiver transducer. 5. **Echo Pulse Generation:** The ECHO pin goes HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time ($t_{echo}$). 6. **Distance Computation:** The microcontroller measures $t_{echo}$ and computes distance using the formula in Section 2.2. ### 2.1.3 Accelerometer (MEMS-based, e.g., ADXL345) **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Proof Mass Displacement:** A microscopic silicon proof mass is suspended by compliant springs inside the MEMS die. 2. **Capacitance Change:** Acceleration causes the mass to displace, changing the capacitance between interdigitated fingers: $\Delta C = f(\text{acceleration})$. 3. **Differential Measurement:** A differential capacitor arrangement cancels common-mode noise. 4. **Voltage Output / I²C Output:** The signal is digitized (typically 10–13 bit resolution) and output via I²C/SPI. ### 2.1.4 Doppler / Microwave Sensor **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. **Continuous Wave (CW) Transmission:** A Gunn diode emits microwave radiation (typically **X-band: 10.525 GHz**). 2. **Reflection from Moving Target:** A moving object reflects the wave with a frequency shift. 3. **Mixer Detection:** The reflected wave is mixed with the transmitted wave in a Schottky diode mixer. 4. **IF Beat Frequency Output:** The Intermediate Frequency (IF) is the Doppler shift: $f_d = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Note:** Every numerical problem on movement detection in the ESE (End Semester Examination) will require one of the equations below. Memorize the table. | # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $d = \frac{v \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$ | Ultrasonic distance (Time-of-Flight) | $d$ = distance (m); $v$ = speed of sound (m/s); $t_{echo}$ = round-trip time (s) | | 2 | $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound as a function of temperature | $T$ = temperature in °C; result in m/s | | 3 | $f_d = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift (microwave/sonar) | $f_d$ = Doppler shift (Hz); $v$ = target velocity (m/s); $f_0$ = transmitted frequency (Hz); $\theta$ = angle between motion and beam axis; $c$ = speed of light (m/s) | | 4 | $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{F}{m}$ | Acceleration (Newton's 2nd Law) | $a$ (m/s²); $F$ (N); $m$ (kg) | | 5 | $P = \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (PIR IR emission) | $P$ = radiated power (W); $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴; $A$ = surface area; $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | | 6 | $I = \frac{P}{4\pi r^2}$ | Inverse square law of IR intensity | $I$ = intensity at distance $r$; $P$ = source power | | 7 | $v_{max} = 2 \pi f A$ | Maximum velocity in SHM (vibration sensing) | $f$ = frequency (Hz); $A$ = amplitude (m) | | 8 | $s = \frac{1}{2} a t^2$ | Kinematic equation (motion detection) | $s$ = displacement (m); $t$ = time (s) | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** In equation 1 (ultrasonic distance), students often **forget the division by 2**. The echo time is for the *round trip* (transmit + receive), so the *one-way* distance is half the product $v \cdot t_{echo}$. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Sensor | Industry Deployment | Engineering Value | |--------|--------------------|--------------------| | **PIR** | Smart homes, museum galleries | Energy savings of **30–60%** on lighting | | **Ultrasonic** | Autonomous robots, drones, ADAS | Contactless distance measurement up to **4 m** with ±3 mm accuracy | | **Accelerometer** | Smartphones (screen rotation), pacemakers, gaming | ~10,000+ units manufactured per second globally | | **Doppler Radar** | Speed guns, weather radar, ballistic missiles | Detects velocity of objects at ranges of **km** | ## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Sensor Selection — KTU Perspective In IoT, the choice of movement sensor depends on the **Design Constraint Triangle**: 1. **Power Budget** → PIR wins (passive, no transmission) 2. **Range Requirement** → Doppler/Microwave wins (long range) 3. **Resolution** → Accelerometer wins (sub-mm scale) 4. **Cost** → PIR + Ultrasonic combo is cheapest (<$2 total) # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement (HC-SR04) **Problem:** An HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor is triggered by a microcontroller. The measured echo pulse width is **588 µs**. The ambient temperature is **25°C**. Calculate: - (a) The speed of sound at this temperature - (b) The one-way distance to the target ### Step 1: Identify Given Data Given: - $t_{echo} = 588 \, \mu s = 588 \times 10^{-6} \, s$ - $T = 25°C$ - To find: $v_{sound}$ and $d$ ### Step 2: Apply the Temperature-Dependent Velocity Formula The speed of sound in dry air varies linearly with temperature: $$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s)}$$ Substituting $T = 25$: $$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 25$$ $$v_{sound} = 331 + 15$$ $$v_{sound} = 346 \text{ m/s}$$ **Explanation:** The base speed of sound at 0°C is 331 m/s. For every 1°C rise, the speed increases by 0.6 m/s because hotter air molecules vibrate faster and transmit the wave more efficiently. ### Step 3: Apply the Time-of-Flight Equation The total distance covered by the ultrasonic wave is: $$d_{round-trip} = v_{sound} \times t_{echo}$$ $$d_{round-trip} = 346 \times 588 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ $$d_{round-trip} = 0.2034 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 4: Divide by 2 for One-Way Distance Since the wave travels to the object and *back*: $$d = \frac{d_{round-trip}}{2} = \frac{v_{sound} \times t_{echo}}{2}$$ $$d = \frac{346 \times 588 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ $$d = 0.1017 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 10.17 \text{ cm}}$$ **Conclusion:** The target object is approximately **10.17 cm** away from the sensor. This matches real-world HC-SR04 precision. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Doppler Shift for Speed Detection **Problem:** A Doppler radar operating at $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz (X-band) is mounted on a roadside pole. It observes a car moving *directly toward* the radar at a constant speed of **72 km/h**. Calculate the Doppler frequency shift detected. ### Step 1: Unit Conversion Convert car velocity to m/s: $$v = 72 \text{ km/h} = 72 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 20 \text{ m/s}$$ ### Step 2: Identify Angle θ Since the car is moving *directly toward* the radar, the direction of motion is along the line of sight: $$\theta = 0° \implies \cos\theta = \cos(0°) = 1$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Doppler Formula $$f_d = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$$ $$f_d = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 10.525 \times 10^9 \times 1}{3 \times 10^8}$$ $$f_d = \frac{4.21 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^8}$$ $$f_d = 1403.33 \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_d \approx 1.403 \text{ kHz}}$$ **Conclusion:** The radar receiver will detect a beat frequency of about **1.4 kHz** — well within the audible range (20 Hz – 20 kHz), which is why police radar "speed guns" can sometimes be *heard* as a faint whistle. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison Table (for KTU 14-Mark Answers) | Parameter | PIR | Ultrasonic | Accelerometer | Doppler/Microwave | |-----------|-----|------------|---------------|-------------------| | **Energy Emission** | None (Passive) | Active (sound) | None | Active (microwave) | | **Range** | 3 – 7 m (typical) | 0.02 – 4 m | N/A (contact/wearable) | 10 – 150 m | | **Power Consumption** | ~65 µA idle | ~15 mA active | ~40 µA active | ~50 mA active | | **Output Type** | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | Digital pulse width | Digital (I²C/SPI) | Analog / Digital | | **Accuracy** | Coarse (motion/no-motion) | ±3 mm | ±0.003 g | ±1 km/h | | **Penetration of Obstacles** | No (line of sight) | No (acoustic shadowing) | N/A | Yes (through plastic/wood) | | **Sensing Dimension** | 1D (binary) | 1D (distance) | 3D (x, y, z) | 1D (radial velocity) | | **IoT Use Case** | Intrusion detection | Smart parking | Fitness tracker | Traffic enforcement | --- ## 3.4 KTU-Style Sub-Question Walkthrough: Motion Detection Threshold **Problem:** A PIR-based IoT security system is configured to trigger an alarm when an object moves at a velocity exceeding 0.5 m/s within a 5 m radius. The Fresnel lens has 12 zones, each 0.5 m wide. Calculate: - (a) The minimum displacement to cross one zone - (b) The minimum time the object must stay in the FoV to trigger ### Part (a): Minimum Displacement Crossing a single Fresnel zone boundary is the minimum detectable event: $$s_{min} = \text{zone width} = 0.5 \text{ m}$$ ### Part (b): Minimum Time Using $s = v \cdot t$: $$t_{min} = \frac{s_{min}}{v_{threshold}} = \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 1 \text{ s}$$ **Interpretation:** The object must traverse at least one zone (0.5 m) within 1 second for the alarm to trigger. This filters out slow-moving shadows or small animals. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Practice Set (Self-Evaluation) > [!TIP] > Solve these *before* checking solutions. They mirror the KTU 2024 ESE pattern. 1. **Q1:** An ultrasonic sensor measures $t_{echo} = 4.7$ ms at 30°C. Find the distance. *(Ans: ≈ 0.81 m)* 2. **Q2:** A Doppler radar at 24 GHz detects $f_d = 2$ kHz. Find the speed of the approaching object. *(Ans: ≈ 12.5 m/s)* 3. **Q3:** An accelerometer reads 2.5 g. Convert to m/s². *(Ans: 24.5 m/s²)* 4. **Q4:** A PIR triggers on $s = 1$ m displacement at $v = 0.4$ m/s. Will it trigger? *(Ans: No, 2.5 s > threshold)* # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IoT Movement Detection System — High-Level Block Diagram This Mermaid block diagram represents the complete signal chain of an IoT movement-sensing node (e.g., a smart security camera using a PIR + Ultrasonic combination). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Target Object
Human Vehicle Animal] --> B1[PIR Sensor
HC-SR501] A --> B2[Ultrasonic Sensor
HC-SR04] A --> B3[Microwave Doppler
HB100 Module] B1 --> C1[Analog Front End
Amplifier Filter] B2 --> C2[Time of Flight
Measurement Unit] B3 --> C3[Frequency Mixer
Doppler Detector] C1 --> D[Microcontroller
Arduino ESP32 NodeMCU] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Signal Processing
Thresholding Noise Filtering] E --> F[Wireless Module
WiFi BLE LoRa] F --> G[Cloud Server
AWS IoT Azure] G --> H[Mobile App
Push Notification Alert] G --> I[Actuator
Buzzer Door Lock] D --> J[Local Decision
Edge Computing] J --> I ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Internal Architecture of a PIR Sensor Module ```mermaid flowchart TD K[Ambient IR Radiation
from Environment] --> L1[Fresnel Lens
Segment 1] K --> L2[Fresnel Lens
Segment 2] L1 --> M1[Pyroelectric Slot 1
Pyro1] L2 --> M2[Pyroelectric Slot 2
Pyro2] M1 --> N[Differential Amplifier
BISS0001 ASIC] M2 --> N N --> O[Comparator
Threshold Detect] O --> P[Timer Circuit
Tx 3s to 300s] P --> Q[Output Pin
3.3V or 5V HIGH] R[Potentiometer 1
Sensitivity] --> O S[Potentiometer 2
Time Delay] --> P Q --> T[Microcontroller
Interrupt Pin] ``` ## 4.3 Ultrasonic HC-SR04 Timing Diagram (Logical Sequence) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MCU as Microcontroller participant TRIG as TRIG Pin participant US as Ultrasonic Burst participant ECHO as ECHO Pin Note over MCU,ECHO: t = 0 us MCU->>TRIG: Send 10 us HIGH pulse TRIG->>US: Trigger 8 cycles of 40 kHz Note over US: Acoustic wave travels to target US->>ECHO: ECHO pin goes HIGH Note over ECHO: Wait for return signal... Note over ECHO: Wave reflects and returns ECHO->>MCU: ECHO pin goes LOW Note over MCU: Measure t_echo pulse width MCU->>MCU: Compute d = v x t_echo / 2 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Sensor Selection in IoT Design ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: IoT Motion Detection Design] --> Q1{What is the
detection range?} Q1 -->|Less than 7 m| Q2{Need to measure
distance precisely?} Q1 -->|More than 7 m| M[Doppler Microwave Sensor] Q2 -->|Yes| U[Ultrasonic HC-SR04] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need to detect
body heat only?} Q3 -->|Yes| P[PIR HC-SR501] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need to track
tilt or vibration?} Q4 -->|Yes| A[Accelerometer ADXL345] Q4 -->|No| C[Combine Multiple
Sensor Fusion] P --> Out[End] U --> Out M --> Out A --> Out C --> Out ``` ## 4.5 Edge-Computing Loop for Smart Motion Detection ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Sensor Read] --> S2[Data Filter] S2 --> S3{Threshold
Exceeded?} S3 -->|No| S1 S3 -->|Yes| S4[Capture Snapshot] S4 --> S5[Timestamp Log] S5 --> S6[Encrypt Payload] S6 --> S7[MQTT Publish] S7 --> S8[Cloud Ingest] S8 --> S9[Dashboard Update] S9 --> S10[Mobile Push] S10 --> S1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note for KTU Board Exam:** When drawing these in the answer sheet, examiners award **2 marks** for clearly labeled blocks and **1 mark** for showing data flow direction with arrows. Always use rectangular boxes and directional arrows. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Define a PIR sensor. Explain why it is called "passive" and list two IoT applications.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Passive Infrared (PIR) sensor** is an electronic sensor that detects infrared radiation emitted by objects in its field of view, particularly the **8–14 µm wavelength band** corresponding to human body heat (~310 K). It is called **"passive"** because it does **not emit any energy** of its own — it merely *receives* ambient IR radiation from the environment and converts it to a voltage via the pyroelectric effect. **Two IoT Applications:** 1. **Smart Home Lighting** — Automatically switches on lights when a person enters a room. 2. **Intrusion Detection Systems** — Triggers alarms and sends MQTT notifications to homeowner when motion is detected after midnight. > **[Marking Scheme: 1 mark definition + 1 mark "passive" justification + 1 mark applications]** --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Differentiate between active and passive sensors with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Active Sensors | Passive Sensors | |-----------|----------------|-----------------| | Energy Emission | Emit energy (signal) into environment | Do not emit any energy | | Power Consumption | Higher | Lower | | Examples | Ultrasonic HC-SR04, Doppler Radar | PIR HC-SR501, Thermopile, Accelerometer | | Working Principle | Measure reflected/return signal | Measure ambient/natural radiation | **Examples:** - **Active:** (i) HC-SR04 Ultrasonic (ii) HB100 Microwave Doppler - **Passive:** (i) HC-SR501 PIR (ii) ADXL345 Accelerometer --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Sub-Part (a) + (b) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | Module 4 | 14 Marks]** **Question A:** **(a)** Explain the working principle of an **HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor** with a neat block diagram. Derive the formula for distance measurement. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An IoT-enabled **smart parking system** uses an HC-SR04 mounted above each parking slot. The measured echo time is **t = 2.94 ms** at a temperature of **30°C**. Calculate the distance from the sensor to the car roof. Discuss the suitability of this sensor for outdoor parking lots. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Working Principle (4 Marks):** The HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle. It consists of: - A **piezoelectric transmitter** (40 kHz) - A **piezoelectric receiver** - **Control circuitry** for signal conditioning **Step-by-step operation:** 1. The microcontroller sends a **10 µs trigger pulse** to the TRIG pin. 2. The sensor internally generates **8 cycles of 40 kHz burst** from the transmitter. 3. The acoustic wave propagates through air, strikes the target, and reflects. 4. The reflected wave is captured by the receiver. 5. The ECHO pin goes HIGH for a duration equal to the **round-trip travel time** ($t_{echo}$). **Formula Derivation (3 Marks):** Let: - $v$ = speed of sound in air - $t$ = round-trip time (echo duration) - $d$ = one-way distance to target The wave travels distance $2d$ in time $t$: $$2d = v \cdot t$$ Therefore: $$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$ **Block Diagram (Already covered in Section 4 — TRIG → Burst Gen → TX → Air → Target → RX → ECHO → MCU).** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Numerical Solution (5 Marks):** Given: - $t_{echo} = 2.94$ ms $= 2.94 \times 10^{-3}$ s - $T = 30°C$ **Step 1:** Compute speed of sound at 30°C: $$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Apply distance formula: $$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{echo}}{2}$$ $$d = \frac{349 \times 2.94 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$ $$d = \frac{1.02606}{2} = 0.513 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 51.3 \text{ cm}}$$ **Suitability Discussion (2 Marks):** - ✅ **Advantages for outdoor parking:** Non-contact measurement, works in darkness, immune to color/reflectivity, low cost (~$2 per unit), wide community support on Arduino/ESP32 platforms. - ❌ **Limitations outdoors:** (1) Soft surfaces (car fabric, grass) absorb ultrasound and may give false readings; (2) Cross-talk between adjacent sensors requires time-division triggering; (3) Heavy rain or dust can attenuate the 40 kHz signal; (4) Maximum range of 4 m may be insufficient for tall vehicles. **Hence, suitable for indoor/covered parking and short-range outdoor deployment, but not for open large outdoor lots.** > **Total = 5 + 2 = 7 marks** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model | Module 4 | 14 Marks]** **Question B:** **(a)** With a block diagram, explain the **Doppler Effect** as used in **microwave motion sensors** for IoT-based automatic doors. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Doppler radar operates at **24 GHz** and detects a Doppler shift of **1500 Hz** from an approaching pedestrian. The pedestrian is moving along the line of sight. Calculate the speed of the pedestrian in km/h. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Conceptual Explanation (4 Marks):** The **Doppler Effect** is the change in observed frequency of a wave when the source and observer are in relative motion. In a microwave motion sensor (e.g., HB100 module): 1. A **Gunn diode** transmits Continuous Wave (CW) microwave radiation at frequency $f_0$. 2. The wave strikes a moving object (e.g., a person walking toward the door). 3. The reflected wave returns with a **shifted frequency** $f_r = f_0 + f_d$. 4. The shifted wave is mixed with the original transmitted signal in a **Schottky diode mixer**. 5. The mixer output is the **beat frequency (IF)**, which equals the Doppler shift $f_d$. 6. When $f_d$ exceeds a threshold (typically 50–100 Hz), the door control circuit activates the motor to open the door. **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** ``` [Oscillator] → [Gunn Diode TX] → [Antenna] → [Moving Target] ↑ | | ↓ [Schottky Mixer] ← [Antenna RX] ← [Reflected Wave] ↓ [IF Amplifier + Comparator] → [Microcontroller] → [Door Motor Driver] → [Door Motor] ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** - $f_0 = 24 \text{ GHz} = 24 \times 10^9$ Hz - $f_d = 1500$ Hz - $\theta = 0° \implies \cos\theta = 1$ - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - To find: $v$ in km/h **Doppler formula:** $$f_d = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c}$$ **Solving for v:** $$v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$ $$v = \frac{1500 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 24 \times 10^9 \times 1}$$ $$v = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{11}}{4.8 \times 10^{10}}$$ $$v = 9.375 \text{ m/s}$$ **Convert to km/h:** $$v = 9.375 \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 9.375 \times 3.6$$ $$\boxed{v = 33.75 \text{ km/h}}$$ > **Total = 7 marks** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU 2024 ESE):** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF formula** — Results in doubled distance. Deduct **1.5 marks**. > 2. **Using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for sound** — The speed of sound in air is **343 m/s**, not the speed of light. Wrong substitution deducts **2 marks**. > 3. **Not specifying temperature** — Speed of sound depends on temperature. If the question gives $T$, use the temperature-dependent formula; if $T$ is not given, default to **$v = 343$ m/s** and explicitly state the assumption. Missing this loses **0.5 marks**. > 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing km/h with m/s, or mm with m, without conversion. Deduct **0.5 to 1 mark**. > 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU examiners strictly enforce SI unit notation. Always write "$d = 51.3 \text{ cm}$" or "$v = 33.75 \text{ km/h}$". > 6. **Block diagram without arrows** — Marks reserved for direction of signal flow are forfeited. Always use directional arrows (`→`). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **This is your last-15-minutes revision sheet. Read 3 times before the exam.** - 🔹 **PIR Sensor** = Passive = No emission = Senses 8–14 µm IR from human body = Used for **motion presence** detection. - 🔹 **Ultrasonic HC-SR04** = Active = 40 kHz burst = 10 µs trigger pulse = Distance formula: $d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$. - 🔹 **Speed of sound** = $v = 331 + 0.6T$ m/s (linear in temperature). Default 343 m/s at 20°C. - 🔹 **Accelerometer** = MEMS proof mass + capacitive sensing = Used for **tilt, vibration, fall detection** (e.g., ADXL345). - 🔹 **Doppler Radar** = Microwave reflection = Frequency shift = $f_d = \frac{2vf_0\cos\theta}{c}$ = Used for **speed guns, automatic doors**. - 🔹 **KTU Favourite Numerical:** Ultrasonic distance given $t$ and $T$ → Apply $v = 331 + 0.6T$, then $d = \frac{vt}{2}$. - 🔹 **KTU Favourite Theory:** Difference between active and passive sensors; *why* PIR is called passive. - 🔹 **IoT Layer:** Movement sensors = **Perception Layer (Layer 1)** of the 5-layer IoT architecture. - 🔹 **Energy Efficiency:** PIR is **lowest power** (µA range) → Ideal for battery-powered IoT nodes. - 🔹 **Range Hierarchy (low → high):** Accelerometer < Ultrasonic (4 m) < PIR (7 m) < Doppler Radar (150 m+). - 🔹 **Output Type:** PIR = Digital HIGH/LOW | Ultrasonic = Pulse-width modulated | Accelerometer = I²C/SPI | Doppler = Beat frequency. - 🔹 **Critical Pitfall:** Always divide echo time by **2** for ultrasonic; never use $c$ (speed of light) for acoustic calculations. - 🔹 **Block Diagram Rule:** Always include **TRIG → TX → Air → Target → RX → ECHO → MCU** for ultrasonic, with arrows showing signal direction. - 🔹 **Exam Mnemonic — "TRUST DAD":** - **T**emperature affects $v_{sound}$ - **R**ound-trip requires division by 2 - **U**ltrasonic uses **40 kHz** acoustic (not EM) - **S**peed of sound ≈ **343 m/s** at 20°C - **T**rigger pulse = **10 µs** HIGH - **D**oppler formula has factor of **2** (Tx + Rx paths) - **A**ccelerometer output is **3-axis** (x, y, z) - **D**efault units: meters, seconds, m/s² --- **End of Module 4 Notes — Getting Input from Sensors: Detecting Movement** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | OBE-Compliant*

topics.title: Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Light" in the IoT Context?

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, *Light* is treated as a **physical environmental parameter (a measurand)** that is sensed, quantified, and communicated over a network. From an engineering perspective, light is a form of **electromagnetic radiation** in the visible spectrum (wavelengths roughly between **380 nm and 750 nm**) that the human eye can detect. In IoT systems, light is typically categorized into two functional roles:

1. **Light as a Stimulus (Input Phenomenon)** → Measured using light sensors such as LDRs, photodiodes, phototransistors, and ambient light sensors (ALS like BH1750, TEMT6000).
2. **Light as an Actuator Output** → Generated by LEDs, OLEDs, smart bulbs (Philips Hue), and used in Li-Fi communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Light in IoT refers to the measurable radiant energy in the visible/IR/UV spectrum that interacts with electronic transducers (sensors) to produce a quantifiable electrical signal for networked decision-making.*

## 1.2 Standard Physical Units (SI Metrics)

| Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Intensity | Candela | **cd** | Base SI unit of light intensity |
| Luminous Flux | Lumen | **lm** | Total visible light emitted by a source |
| Illuminance | Lux | **lx** | Luminous flux per unit area (1 lx = 1 lm/m²) |
| Wavelength | Nanometer | **nm** | Spatial period of the EM wave |
| Frequency | Hertz | **Hz** | Temporal frequency of oscillation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Light as a Mailbox Sensor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine a mailbox with a tiny window. When daylight (sunlight) falls on the window, a photo-sensor inside "notices" it the way your eyes do, and an IoT chip connected to it sends a message to your phone: *"The mailbox is in sunlight."* At night, no light falls, and the chip sends *"Mailbox is in the dark."* This is exactly how a streetlight automation system works — it senses the **absence/presence of ambient light** and switches ON/OFF automatically. The mailbox is your environment; the window is the photodiode; the phone is the cloud.

## 1.4 Classification of Light in IoT Applications

- **Visible Light Sensing (380–750 nm)** → Smart lighting, display backlight control
- **Infrared Sensing (750 nm – 1 mm)** → IR motion detectors, night vision
- **Ultraviolet Sensing (10–380 nm)** → UV index monitoring, sterilization verification
- **Light as Data Carrier (VLC/Li-Fi)** → High-speed indoor wireless communication

## 1.5 Light Sensors — The Five Primary Categories

| Sensor Type | Operating Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** | Photoconductivity — resistance changes with light | Automatic streetlights |
| **Photodiode** | Photovoltaic effect — generates current/voltage | Optical communication, Li-Fi receivers |
| **Phototransistor** | Light amplifies base current | Object detection, encoders |
| **Ambient Light Sensor (ALS)** | Integrated photodiode + ADC + I²C | Smartphones, laptops |
| **CCD/CMOS Image Sensor** | Array of photodiodes with on-chip processing | Cameras, machine vision |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory of Light Sensing

### 2.1.1 Photoconductive Effect (LDR)
An LDR (also called **photoresistor**) is a passive component whose resistance **decreases** as incident light intensity **increases**. It is fabricated from a high-resistance semiconductor such as **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)**.

**Why it works:** When photons strike the CdS lattice, they transfer energy to valence electrons, freeing them into the conduction band. This increases the number of free charge carriers, hence **decreasing resistance**.

### 2.1.2 Photovoltaic Effect (Photodiode)
A photodiode is a **reverse-biased PN-junction**. Incident photons generate electron-hole pairs in the depletion region. The electric field in the junction separates these carriers, producing a photocurrent proportional to light intensity.

**Why it matters:** Photodiodes are **faster**, **more linear**, and have a **much wider spectral response** than LDRs, making them the preferred choice for precision IoT light meters.

### 2.1.3 Phototransistor
A phototransistor is essentially a BJT whose base current is supplied by a built-in photodiode. Light → photocurrent → amplified collector current. Gain can be **100×–1000×** higher than a bare photodiode, but at the cost of slower response.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | $E$ = Illuminance (lx), $\Phi$ = Luminous flux (lm), $A$ = Area (m²) | Converting lumens to lux for sensor calibration |
| 2 | $E \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$ | Inverse Square Law of Light | Calculating light intensity at distance $d$ from a point source |
| 3 | $E_d = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$ | $E_d$ = Illuminance at distance $d$, $I$ = Luminous intensity (cd) | Streetlight pole spacing design |
| 4 | $R_{LDR} \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$ | Resistance inversely proportional to light $L$ (lux) | Designing voltage divider circuits |
| 5 | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R + R_{LDR}}$ | Voltage divider output for LDR | Threshold detection in embedded systems |
| 6 | $I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi_{ph}$ | Photocurrent; $q$ = electron charge, $\eta$ = quantum efficiency, $\Phi_{ph}$ = photon flux | Photodiode response modeling |
| 7 | $E = h \nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy; $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**, $c$ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s | Selecting sensor for specific wavelength |
| 8 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | RC cutoff frequency of light-sensing filter | Bandwidth-limited light measurement |
| 9 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage of photodiode (solar cell eqn) | Energy harvesting IoT nodes |
| 10 | $\text{Capacity}_{LiFi} = A \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_i)$ | Li-Fi channel capacity (Hartley/Shannon) | VLC system design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Formula #2 (Inverse Square Law) and Formula #4 (LDR Resistance-Light Relation) appear most frequently in KTU Numerical/Analytical questions. Master the units: **1 lx = 1 lm/m²** and **1 cd = 1 lm/sr** (steradian).

## 2.3 Real-World IoT Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation) sensors measure light in the 400–700 nm band to optimize greenhouse yields.
- **Smart Cities:** Streetlight automation using ALS saves up to **70% energy** compared to always-on systems.
- **Healthcare:** Wearable UV index sensors alert users to overexposure; blue-light monitoring protects retinal health.
- **Industrial IoT:** Machine vision with CMOS sensors enables quality control on assembly lines.
- **Li-Fi (IEEE 802.15.7):** Uses modulated LED light for wireless data transmission at speeds up to **224 Gbps** in lab demos.
- **Solar-Powered IoT:** Photovoltaic energy harvesting powers remote sensor nodes in agricultural fields.

## 2.4 Spectral Response — Why It Matters

> [!NOTE]
> A sensor's **spectral response curve** defines its sensitivity vs. wavelength. An LDR (CdS) peaks around **540–600 nm** (green-yellow), whereas a silicon photodiode peaks at **~900 nm** (near-IR). Choosing the wrong sensor for a wavelength range gives systematically wrong lux readings — a common KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: The Inverse Square Law of Light

**Statement:** The illuminance produced by a point light source on a surface is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance** from the source to the surface.

### Derivation Steps:

**Step 1 — Define the Geometry.**
Consider a point isotropic source of luminous intensity $I$ (in candelas) placed at the center of a sphere of radius $d$. By definition, the source emits a total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions.

**Step 2 — Total Flux on the Sphere.**
The total solid angle of a sphere is $4\pi$ steradians. Therefore:

$$\Phi = 4\pi \cdot I$$

**Step 3 — Flux per Unit Area at Radius d.**
The surface area of the sphere at radius $d$ is:

$$A = 4\pi d^2$$

The flux per unit area (which by definition is the **illuminance** $E$) is:

$$E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**
Substituting the expressions for $\Phi$ and $A$:

$$E = \dfrac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}}$$

**Interpretation:** If the distance is doubled, illuminance drops to **one-fourth**. If the distance is tripled, illuminance drops to **one-ninth**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Voltage Divider Output for an LDR Circuit

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a voltage divider circuit where one resistor is an LDR.

### Derivation Steps:

**Step 1 — Circuit Setup.**
We have $V_{cc}$ applied across a series combination of a fixed resistor $R$ and an LDR of resistance $R_{LDR}$. The output is taken across the LDR (or alternatively across $R$).

**Step 2 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule.**

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{LDR}}{R + R_{LDR}}$$

**Step 3 — Express $R_{LDR}$ in terms of light intensity.**
The LDR resistance relation is:

$$R_{LDR} = \dfrac{k}{L}$$

where $k$ is a device-specific constant (in lux·ohms) and $L$ is the illuminance in lux.

**Step 4 — Substitute $R_{LDR}$.**

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{k}{L}}{R + \dfrac{k}{L}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the fraction** (multiply numerator and denominator by $L$):

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{k}{RL + k}$$

**Step 6 — Final Form.**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{cc} \cdot k}{R \cdot L + k}}$$

**Interpretation:** As $L \to 0$ (complete darkness), $V_{out} \to V_{cc}$ (maximum voltage). As $L \to \infty$ (very bright), $V_{out} \to 0$. This monotonic relationship is what allows an ADC on a microcontroller to read and threshold the light level.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Photon Energy and Wavelength Relationship

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between the energy of a single photon and its wavelength.

### Derivation Steps:

**Step 1 — Start with the Planck–Einstein Equation.**

$$E = h \nu$$

where $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** and $\nu$ is the frequency in Hz.

**Step 2 — Express Frequency in terms of Wavelength.**
The wave equation gives:

$$\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$$

where $c$ = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s** and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

**Step 3 — Substitute $\nu$ into the Planck equation.**

$$E = h \cdot \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical constants.**

$$E = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$E = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{\lambda} \; \text{Joules}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to electron-volts** (since 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J):

$$E(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})} \; \text{eV}$$

**Step 7 — Box the final form.**

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda} \; \text{eV} \quad \text{with } \lambda \text{ in nm}}$$

**Interpretation:** A 620 nm (red) photon has energy ≈ 2.0 eV. A 450 nm (blue) photon has energy ≈ 2.76 eV. This is why **blue light is more energetic** and can damage silicon photodiodes more aggressively than red light at the same intensity.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A streetlight has a luminous intensity of **800 cd**. Calculate the illuminance on the road at a horizontal distance of **5 m** directly beneath it, and again at **10 m**.

**Step 1 — Apply the Inverse Square Law** (Formula #3):

$$E_5 = \dfrac{I}{d^2} = \dfrac{800}{(5)^2} = \dfrac{800}{25} = 32 \; \text{lx}$$

**Step 2 — Repeat for 10 m:**

$$E_{10} = \dfrac{800}{(10)^2} = \dfrac{800}{100} = 8 \; \text{lx}$$

**Step 3 — Verification of the "Quarter Rule":**

$$\dfrac{E_5}{E_{10}} = \dfrac{32}{8} = 4 = \left(\dfrac{10}{5}\right)^2 \; \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Answer Format:** Always state the formula first, then substitute, then box the final answer with units. This is the exact pattern that fetches full marks in KTU ESE numericals.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
