# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
**Define the term "Illuminance" and state its SI unit. How is it mathematically related to luminous flux?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Illuminance** is defined as the **luminous flux incident per unit area** on a surface.

**SI Unit:** **Lux (lx)**, where $1 \; \text{lx} = 1 \; \text{lm/m}^2$.

**Mathematical Relation:**

$$E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$$

where $E$ = illuminance (lx), $\Phi$ = luminous flux (lm), and $A$ = area (m²). **(1 mark each for definition, unit, and formula = 3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Expected Pattern]**
**Compare LDR and photodiode as light sensors in IoT applications (any 3 points).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | LDR | Photodiode |
|---|---|---|
| Response time | Slow (tens of ms) | Fast (nanoseconds) |
| Linearity | Non-linear | Highly linear |
| Spectral range | Narrow (visible) | Wide (UV to IR) |
| Active/Passive | Passive | Active (needs bias) |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate |

**(1 mark per valid comparison point × 3 = 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[Choice 1]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Actual Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an IoT-based **automatic streetlight control system** using an LDR. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **inverse square law of light** and use it to find the illuminance at a point **8 m** away from a point source of intensity **1200 cd**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The IoT-based automatic streetlight system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Light Sensing Block (LDR + Voltage Divider):** Detects ambient light. Resistance drops as light increases.
2. **Signal Conditioning Block (Comparator/Op-Amp):** Converts the analog voltage to a clean digital threshold.
3. **Microcontroller Block (Arduino/ESP32):** Reads the digitized light value via ADC and applies control logic.
4. **Actuation Block (Relay + Lamp):** Switches the mains-powered streetlight ON or OFF.
5. **Communication Block (Wi-Fi Module):** Reports status and lux values to the cloud.
6. **Power Supply Block (SMPS + Battery Backup):** Provides regulated DC to the entire system.

**Working (2 marks):**
During daytime, the LDR resistance is low (≈ few hundred ohms), so the voltage at the ADC pin exceeds the threshold. The microcontroller interprets this as "daylight" and keeps the relay OFF, switching OFF the lamp. At night, LDR resistance rises (≈ MΩ range), the ADC voltage drops below the threshold, and the microcontroller energizes the relay to turn ON the streetlight. The status is published to an MQTT broker every 60 seconds.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Saves up to **70% energy** versus always-on systems.
- Reduces manual intervention and maintenance costs.
- Provides real-time fault monitoring and dimming control.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}}$$

**Numerical Substitution (3 marks):**

Given $I = 1200 \; \text{cd}$ and $d = 8 \; \text{m}$:

$$E = \dfrac{1200}{(8)^2} = \dfrac{1200}{64}$$

$$\boxed{E = 18.75 \; \text{lx}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[Choice 2 — Internal Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a photodiode** and derive an expression for its open-circuit voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the working of a **Li-Fi (Light Fidelity) communication system**. Mention at least **two advantages** over traditional Wi-Fi. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (3 marks):**
A photodiode operates on the **photovoltaic effect** (or **photoconductive mode** under reverse bias). When photons of energy $E = h\nu$ greater than the bandgap energy strike the depletion region of a reverse-biased PN-junction, they generate **electron-hole pairs**. The built-in electric field sweeps the electrons toward the N-region and holes toward the P-region, producing a **photocurrent** $I_{ph}$ proportional to the incident optical power.

**Derivation of $V_{oc}$ (4 marks):**
The diode current equation including photocurrent is:

$$I = I_0 \left(e^{qV/kT} - 1\right) - I_{ph}$$

At open circuit, $I = 0$, so:

$$I_{ph} = I_0 \left(e^{qV_{oc}/kT} - 1\right)$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$:

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)}$$

where $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**, $T$ = absolute temperature, $q$ = electron charge.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Li-Fi block diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.

**Working (3 marks):**
The transmitting microcontroller sends serial data to an **LED driver circuit**, which modulates the intensity of a high-power LED at extremely high frequencies (in the MHz range), beyond the perception of the human eye. The modulated light propagates through free space and is received by a **PIN photodiode** at the receiver end. The photodiode converts the light intensity variations back into a varying electrical signal, which is then **amplified and demodulated** to recover the original data stream.

**Advantages over Wi-Fi (1 mark):**
1. **Security:** Light cannot penetrate walls, so Li-Fi is inherently confined to a room — eliminating external eavesdropping.
2. **Speed:** Li-Fi offers bandwidth **1000× greater** than Wi-Fi because the visible spectrum is unregulated and vast.
3. **No EMI:** Safe for use in hospitals, aircraft, and petrochemical plants where RF interference is dangerous.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert cm to m, or nm to m, in light-based numericals. Always use SI units in the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing Luminous Flux (lm) with Luminous Intensity (cd):** Flux is total light emitted; intensity is flux per solid angle. Marks lost for swapping these.
> 3. **Inversely Proportional Mistakes:** Many students write $E \propto d^2$ instead of $E \propto 1/d^2$. This reverses the answer entirely.
> 4. **Skipping the Formula Statement:** KTU examiners award **1 full mark** just for writing the correct formula. Even if the arithmetic is wrong, you get partial credit.
> 5. **No Units in Final Answer:** A numerical like "32" without "lx" will lose 0.5 to 1 mark. Always annotate units.
> 6. **Confusing Photodiode with Phototransistor:** Photodiode has no internal gain; phototransistor has gain ≈ 100–1000.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Topic: Light (IoT)**
> - [x] **Light** is an EM wave in the **380–750 nm** visible band; in IoT it is a *measurand* and an *output*.
> - [x] Key SI units: **lux (lx)** for illuminance, **lumen (lm)** for flux, **candela (cd)** for intensity.
> - [x] **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I/d^2$ — memorize and apply rigorously.
> - [x] **LDR:** Passive, slow, non-linear, cheap; based on photoconductivity (CdS).
> - [x] **Photodiode:** Active, fast, linear; based on photovoltaic effect; reverse-biased PN-junction.
> - [x] **Phototransistor:** Photodiode + BJT amplification; gain 100–1000×, slower response.
> - [x] **Ambient Light Sensor (ALS):** Integrated photodiode + ADC + I²C (e.g., BH1750, TEMT6000).
> - [x] **Voltage Divider Output:** $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot R_{LDR}/(R + R_{LDR})$ — central to threshold detection.
> - [x] **Photon Energy:** $E(\text{eV}) = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$ — use this to select sensor wavelength.
> - [x] **Li-Fi:** VLC using modulated LEDs; IEEE 802.15.7; faster, more secure than Wi-Fi.
> - [x] **Smart Streetlight** is the canonical KTU Module 4 application example.
> - [x] **Photodiode $V_{oc}$:** $V_{oc} = (kT/q) \ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$ — derive in any 7-mark analytical question.
> - [x] Always state **units, formula, substitution, and boxed answer** in numerical problems.
> - [x] **Planck's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s and **speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s are mandatory constants.
> - [x] Be ready to draw a **block diagram** in every 7-mark question — KTU examiners allocate **2–3 marks** for clean diagrams alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, **Motion** refers to the physical displacement, velocity, acceleration, or angular movement of an object or living entity that is detected, quantified, and digitized by an IoT sensor subsystem for the purpose of environmental perception, context-aware computing, and automated actuation.

A **Motion Sensor** (or **Motion Detector**) is an electronic device that converts physical kinematic parameters into processable electrical signals. In KTU's 2024 scheme treatment of IoT, motion forms one of the most critical "sense-act" primitives, forming the basis of *smart surveillance*, *human activity recognition*, *occupancy analytics*, *gesture-controlled IoT*, and *predictive maintenance* in Industry 4.0.

The principal categories of motion sensing examined under Module 4 are:

1. **Passive Infrared (PIR) Sensors** — Detect infrared radiation emitted by warm bodies.
2. **Ultrasonic Sensors** — Emit and receive high-frequency sound waves (typically **40 kHz**).
3. **Microwave / Doppler Radar Sensors** — Use the Doppler effect at microwave frequencies (typically **2.4 GHz / 5.8 GHz**).
4. **Accelerometers (MEMS-based)** — Measure proper acceleration along 1, 2, or 3 axes.
5. **Gyroscopes (MEMS-based)** — Measure angular velocity (in degrees/second or rad/s).
6. **Image / Vision-based Motion Sensors** — Use computer vision algorithms on sequential frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Sensing Layer):** Motion sensing is part of the broader **Physical / Sensor Layer** of the IoT architecture. It bridges the physical world to the *Network* and *Application* layers via embedded microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino UNO, ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing in a dark, silent room. Suddenly, you *feel* someone walk behind you — even though you did not see or hear them. How?

- Your skin detected a **slight change in heat (infrared radiation)** from a nearby body.
- Your inner-ear vestibular system sensed a **subtle vibration** through the floor.
- The motion of air molecules against your skin told your brain that **something was moving**.

A **motion sensor in IoT is essentially a digital imitation of this biological perception**:

- A **PIR sensor** mimics your skin's heat detection — it "sees" warm bodies moving in its field of view.
- An **ultrasonic sensor** mimics a bat's echolocation — it "hears" the echo of its own sound pulses bouncing off moving objects.
- An **accelerometer** mimics your inner ear — it measures how a device itself is moving or tilting.
- A **gyroscope** mimics the rotational balance in your eyes — it tracks orientation and spin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Every motion sensor answers one (or more) of three physical questions:
> 1. **Is there motion?** (Presence / Boolean detection) → PIR, Radar
> 2. **How far is the object?** (Ranging) → Ultrasonic, LiDAR
> 3. **How is the object accelerating or rotating?** (Inertial Measurement) → Accelerometer, Gyroscope

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Speed of sound in air (20 °C)** | **343 m/s** |
| **Speed of light in vacuum (c)** | **≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| **Standard PIR spectral window** | **8 µm – 14 µm** (long-wave IR) |
| **Standard ultrasonic frequency** | **40 kHz** |
| **Standard microwave Doppler frequency** | **2.4 GHz** (ISM band) |
| **Standard acceleration due to gravity (g)** | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| **Standard sample rate for wearables** | **50 Hz – 200 Hz** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — By Sensor Type

### 2.1.1 Passive Infrared (PIR) Sensor
- **How it works (The 'Why' and 'How'):**
  1. A **Fresnel lens** segments the field-of-view into multiple zones.
  2. A **pyroelectric crystal** behind the lens generates a small voltage when the IR radiation incident on it changes.
  3. A stationary human emits a constant IR flux → **no voltage change** → no detection.
  4. A moving human causes IR flux to **fluctuate** between adjacent zones → **voltage pulse** → digital HIGH output.

- **KTU High-Yield Facts:**
  - PIR is **passive** (no transmitter) — it does not emit energy.
  - Range: typically **3 m – 7 m**, FOV ≈ **100° – 120°**.
  - **Low power** (suitable for battery IoT nodes).
  - **Cannot** measure distance or velocity — only binary motion.

### 2.1.2 Ultrasonic Sensor (e.g., HC-SR04)
- **How it works:**
  1. The transmitter emits an **8-cycle 40 kHz burst** when triggered.
  2. The wave travels through air, hits an object, and **reflects back**.
  3. The receiver detects the echo.
  4. The microcontroller measures the round-trip time **Δt**.
  5. Distance is computed using the speed of sound.

- **KTU High-Yield Facts:**
  - Effective range: **2 cm – 400 cm**.
  - Accuracy: ±**3 mm**.
  - Blind zone: **< 2 cm** (transducer recovery time).
  - **Cannot** detect soft/sound-absorbent surfaces (foam, fabric).

### 2.1.3 Accelerometer (MEMS)
- **How it works:**
  1. A microscopic **proof mass** is suspended by compliant flexures.
  2. Acceleration causes the mass to displace; displacement is measured capacitively (most common) or piezoresistively.
  3. Output is proportional to **specific force** (acceleration minus gravity).

- **KTU High-Yield Facts:**
  - Axes: **1-axis, 2-axis, or 3-axis** (tri-axial is IoT standard).
  - Range: ±**2g**, ±**4g**, ±**8g**, ±**16g** (selectable).
  - Used for **fall detection, activity recognition, vibration monitoring**.

### 2.1.4 Gyroscope (MEMS)
- **How it works:**
  1. A vibrating proof mass experiences the **Coriolis force** when rotated.
  2. Coriolis force is proportional to angular velocity.
  3. Capacitive sensing converts this into a voltage.

- **KTU High-Yield Facts:**
  - Output: **angular velocity** (°/s or rad/s).
  - Combined with an accelerometer → forms an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values — never the raw `|` pipe symbol** (to preserve markdown table integrity).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variable Description | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Distance via Ultrasonic | $d \;=\; \dfrac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $d$ = distance; $\Delta t$ = round-trip echo time; factor **2** for round trip | metres (m) | HC-SR04 ranging |
| 2 | Speed of sound (temperature-corrected) | $v_{\text{sound}} \;=\; 331 \;+\; 0.6 \cdot T$ | $T$ = air temperature in °C | m/s | Compensation in ultrasonic IoT |
| 3 | Doppler Shift (radar / microwave) | $\Delta f \;=\; \dfrac{2 \cdot v_r \cdot f_0}{c}$ | $v_r$ = radial velocity of object; $f_0$ = transmitted freq.; $c$ = speed of light | Hz | Microwave motion detection |
| 4 | Specific Force (Accelerometer) | $a_{\text{specific}} \;=\; a_{\text{kinematic}} \;-\; g$ | $a$ = acceleration; $g$ = gravity vector | m/s² | Tilt & free-fall detection |
| 5 | Angular Velocity | $\omega \;=\; \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | $\theta$ = angular displacement; $t$ = time | rad/s | Gyroscope output |
| 6 | Orientation (Pitch / Roll) | $\theta \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{a_y}{\sqrt{a_x^{\,2} + a_z^{\,2}}}\right)$ | $a_x, a_y, a_z$ = tri-axial accelerometer readings | degrees / rad | IMU tilt estimation |
| 7 | Wavelength of IR peak (human body) | $\lambda_{\text{peak}} \;=\; \dfrac{b}{T}$ | $b$ = Wien's constant $\approx \text{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}$ m·K; $T$ = body temp in K | m | PIR window design |
| 8 | Coriolis force (MEMS gyro) | $\vec{F}_c \;=\; 2m \vec{v} \times \vec{\omega}$ | $m$ = proof mass; $v$ = velocity; $\omega$ = angular velocity | N | MEMS gyroscope principle |
| 9 | Power emitted by blackbody (Stefan–Boltzmann) | $P \;=\; \sigma T^{4}$ | $\sigma = \text{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴; $T$ = temp in K | W/m² | Why PIR detects warm bodies |
| 10 | Rotational Kinematics | $\theta \;=\; \omega_0 t \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$ | $\omega_0$ = initial angular velocity; $\alpha$ = angular acceleration | rad | Gyro integration |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | IoT Motion Application |
|---|---|
| **Smart Home** | Auto-lighting via PIR; HVAC zoning via occupancy sensors |
| **Healthcare (Wearables)** | Fall detection in elderly via accelerometer |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Predictive maintenance via vibration analysis on motors |
| **Smart Cities** | Traffic flow measurement via Doppler radar |
| **Automotive** | Airbag trigger via high-g accelerometer |
| **Agriculture** | Drone-based crop motion / livestock tracking |
| **Sports** | IMU-based swing analysis, gait analysis |
| **Retail Analytics** | People-counting in malls using overhead PIR arrays |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Distance Measurement

**Problem Statement:** An HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor is triggered at $t = 0$. The echo is received at $t = \Delta t = 5.82$ ms. The ambient air temperature is $T = 25$ °C. Compute the distance to the object.

### Step 1 — Temperature-Corrected Speed of Sound

We use the linearised temperature compensation formula:

$$v_{\text{sound}} \;=\; 331 \;+\; 0.6 \cdot T$$

Substituting $T = 25$ °C:

$$v_{\text{sound}} \;=\; 331 \;+\; 0.6 \cdot 25$$

$$v_{\text{sound}} \;=\; 331 \;+\; 15$$

$$v_{\text{sound}} \;=\; 346 \;\text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Round-Trip Distance

The wave travels to the object **and back**, so the *total* path length is $v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \Delta t$. The **one-way** distance is half of this:

$$d \;=\; \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substituting the Numerical Values

We first convert $\Delta t$ into seconds:

$$\Delta t \;=\; 5.82 \;\text{ms} \;=\; 5.82 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s}$$

Now substitute:

$$d \;=\; \frac{346 \times 5.82 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$346 \times 5.82 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 2.01372 \;\text{m}$$

Divide by 2:

$$d \;=\; \frac{2.01372}{2} \;=\; 1.00686 \;\text{m}$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer with Units

$$\boxed{\,d \;\approx\; 1.007 \;\text{m}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Doppler Shift for a Microwave Motion Sensor

**Problem Statement:** A microwave motion sensor emits at $f_0 = 2.4$ GHz. An intruder walks **directly toward** the sensor at $v_r = 1.5$ m/s. Compute the Doppler frequency shift. (Take $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.)

### Step 1 — State the Doppler Formula for a Reflected Signal

For a radar-style reflection off a moving target, the round-trip shift is doubled:

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot v_r \cdot f_0}{c}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 2.4 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify Step-by-Step

First, multiply numerator:

$$2 \times 1.5 \;=\; 3$$

$$3 \times 2.4 \times 10^{9} \;=\; 7.2 \times 10^{9}$$

Now divide:

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{7.2 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{7.2}{3} \times 10^{9-8}$$

$$\Delta f \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^{1}$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer

$$\boxed{\,\Delta f \;=\; 24 \;\text{Hz}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **24 Hz** shift falls within the audible band of the human ear (20 Hz – 20 kHz), which is why some older microwave sensors produced a faint "click" or "buzz" on detection — a phenomenon called *microphony*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pitch Angle from a Tri-Axial Accelerometer

**Problem Statement:** A stationary IoT wearable reports the following accelerometer readings (in units of g): $a_x = 0.10$, $a_y = 0.34$, $a_z = 0.94$. Compute the pitch angle $\theta$ of the device.

### Step 1 — Pitch Angle Formula

For a stationary device, the gravity vector alone is sensed. Pitch is computed as:

$$\theta \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{\sqrt{a_x^{\,2} + a_z^{\,2}}}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Denominator

$$a_x^{\,2} \;=\; (0.10)^2 \;=\; 0.0100$$

$$a_z^{\,2} \;=\; (0.94)^2 \;=\; 0.8836$$

$$a_x^{\,2} + a_z^{\,2} \;=\; 0.0100 + 0.8836 \;=\; 0.8936$$

$$\sqrt{0.8936} \;=\; 0.9453$$

### Step 3 — Form the Argument of arctan

$$\frac{a_y}{\sqrt{a_x^{\,2} + a_z^{\,2}}} \;=\; \frac{0.34}{0.9453}$$

$$= \; 0.3596$$

### Step 4 — Apply arctan

$$\theta \;=\; \arctan(0.3596)$$

$$\theta \;\approx\; 19.78^{\circ}$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer

$$\boxed{\,\theta \;\approx\; 19.8^{\circ}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Verification of Accelerometer Free-Fall Detection

**Problem Statement:** An accelerometer is in true free-fall. Show that the magnitude of the measured specific force is zero.

### Step 1 — Specific Force Definition

$$\vec{a}_{\text{specific}} \;=\; \vec{a}_{\text{kinematic}} \;-\; \vec{g}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Free-Fall Kinematics

In true free-fall, $\vec{a}_{\text{kinematic}} = \vec{g}$ (the body accelerates downward at $g$). Substituting:

$$\vec{a}_{\text{specific}} \;=\; \vec{g} \;-\; \vec{g} \;=\; \vec{0}$$

### Step 3 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{\,\vert \vec{a}_{\text{specific}} \vert \;=\; 0\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Application:** This is the principle used by **fall detection algorithms** in smartwatches — when all three accelerometer axes sum to a magnitude near 0 g, the device is in free-fall. A *subsequent high-g spike* confirms impact.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Motion Sensors in IoT

| Property | PIR | Ultrasonic | Accelerometer | Gyroscope | Microwave |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output Type** | Binary | Distance (m) | Acceleration (m/s² or g) | Angular velocity (°/s) | Binary / Velocity |
| **Active / Passive** | Passive | Active | Passive | Passive | Active |
| **Range** | 3–7 m | 0.02–4 m | ±2 g to ±16 g | ±250 °/s typical | 5–15 m |
| **Power** | **Very Low** | Medium | Low | Low | Medium–High |
| **Works in Dark?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (own motion) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Through-wall?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | N/A | N/A | ✅ Yes |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹40–₹80 | ₹80–₹150 | ₹60–₹200 (MEMS IC) | ₹80–₹250 | ₹300–₹800 |
| **Best IoT Use** | Occupancy / Lighting | Proximity / Level | Activity / Fall | Orientation | Intrusion / Speed |
| **Susceptibility to Noise** | Sunlight, heaters | Soft surfaces, temperature | Vibration | Drift over time | EMI, false triggers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Motion-Sensing Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Motion Source] --> B[Motion Sensor Module]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Microcontroller MCU]
    E --> F[Wireless Transceiver WiFi BLE LoRa]
    F --> G[IoT Cloud Server MQTT Broker]
    G --> H[Application Layer Dashboard Alerts]
    H --> I[Actuator Response Light Lock Motor]

    subgraph SensorBank ["Sensor Variants"]
        B1[PIR HC-SR501]
        B2[Ultrasonic HC-SR04]
        B3[Accelerometer ADXL345]
        B4[Gyroscope MPU6050]
        B5[Microwave RCWL-0516]
    end

    B1 -. implements .-> B
    B2 -. implements .-> B
    B3 -. implements .-> B
    B4 -. implements .-> B
    B5 -. implements .-> B
```

## 4.2 Ultrasonic Sensor Ranging — Sequence Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant TRIG as Trigger Pin
    participant US as Ultrasonic Sensor
    participant OBJ as Object Target
    participant ECHO as Echo Pin

    MCU->>TRIG: Send 10 µs HIGH pulse
    TRIG->>US: Activate transmitter
    US->>OBJ: Emit 40 kHz burst 8 cycles
    OBJ-->>US: Reflected echo returns
    US->>ECHO: Set ECHO pin HIGH for duration Delta t
    MCU->>ECHO: Measure pulse width using timer
    MCU->>MCU: Compute distance d = v sound × Delta t / 2
    MCU->>MCU: Publish to IoT broker via Wi Fi
```

## 4.3 IMU Sensor Fusion — Accelerometer + Gyroscope (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tri axial Accelerometer] --> C[Complementary Filter / Kalman Filter]
    B[Tri axial Gyroscope] --> C
    C --> D[Pitch Angle theta]
    C --> E[Roll Angle phi]
    C --> F[Yaw Angle psi]
    D --> G[Application Layer Gesture Detection Activity Recognition]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 PIR Sensor Detection Mechanism — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IR Radiation from Human Body] --> B[Fresnel Lens Segments FOV into Zones]
    B --> C[Pyroelectric Crystal]
    C --> D{IR Flux Change Greater Than Threshold}
    D -- Yes --> E[Voltage Pulse Generated]
    D -- No --> F[No Detection Output LOW]
    E --> G[Amplifier Comparator Stage]
    G --> H[Digital Output HIGH to MCU]
    H --> I[IoT Event Published Motion Detected]
    F --> J[Continue Polling]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between active and passive motion sensors. Give one example of each used in IoT applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Emission | Emits its own signal (sound, microwave, IR) | Does not emit; only receives energy |
| Power Consumption | Higher | Lower |
| Range / Direction Info | Can provide distance / velocity | Mostly binary output |
| Example | HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor | HC-SR501 PIR Sensor |

*(Marking Scheme: Definition – 1 mark, Comparison – 1 mark, Example – 1 mark)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the working principle of a PIR sensor in a smart lighting IoT system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Passive Infrared (PIR) sensor** detects motion by sensing changes in the infrared radiation emitted by a warm body (such as a human at ~310 K).

1. A **Fresnel lens** focuses and segments the incoming IR from the field of view into multiple zones. *(1 mark)*
2. When a person moves across zones, the incident IR flux on the **pyroelectric crystal** changes rapidly, producing a small voltage pulse. *(1 mark)*
3. The pulse is amplified and sent as a digital HIGH signal to the microcontroller, which switches ON the smart light via a relay / MOSFET. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Module Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from this module.* Each question carries **14 marks** with internal choice between **(a)** and **(b)** sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **ultrasonic motion sensor** in an IoT-based smart parking system. Derive the formula used for distance computation. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Sequence Diagram above — 2 marks)*

**Working Principle:**

1. The microcontroller (e.g., Arduino / ESP32) sends a **10 µs HIGH pulse** to the *Trig* pin of the HC-SR04. *(1 mark)*
2. The sensor emits **eight 40 kHz ultrasonic bursts** into the air.
3. The wave reflects off the obstacle (e.g., a parked car) and returns to the receiver.
4. The *Echo* pin is held HIGH for a duration $\Delta t$ proportional to the round-trip time.
5. The MCU measures $\Delta t$ using a hardware timer / `pulseIn()` function.

**Derivation of Distance Formula:** *(3 marks — see Section 3.1 of these notes)*

$$d \;=\; \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

For temperature $T$ in °C: $v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6T$

**IoT Integration:** The computed distance is uploaded to an MQTT broker over Wi-Fi, displayed on a dashboard, and used to indicate slot availability (e.g., $d < 30$ cm → OCCUPIED, $d > 30$ cm → FREE). *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** Describe the **working of a MEMS accelerometer**. How is it used for **fall detection** in IoT wearables? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**MEMS Accelerometer — Working:** *(3 marks)*

1. A microscopic **proof mass** is suspended by compliant mechanical flexures (springs) etched from silicon.
2. When the device accelerates, the proof mass displaces relative to the frame by **Newton's Second Law** ($F = ma$).
3. The displacement changes the **capacitance** between interdigitated comb fingers.
4. A dedicated ASIC converts this capacitance change into a voltage proportional to specific force.

**Fall Detection Algorithm in IoT Wearables:** *(4 marks)*

A fall consists of **four phases**:

| Phase | Sensor Signature |
|---|---|
| 1. **Free-fall** | All axes sum to magnitude $\approx 0\,g$ (typically < 0.4 g for ≥ 200 ms) |
| 2. **Impact** | Sudden spike of $\geq 2.5\,g$ to $4\,g$ |
| 3. **Post-fall stillness** | Magnitude returns near 1 g but with **very low variance** for several seconds |
| 4. **Alarm trigger** | If phases 1–3 occur in a temporal window of ≤ 2 seconds, an SMS / MQTT alert is sent via cellular / Wi-Fi to a caretaker's phone. |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **Doppler effect** as applied to **microwave motion sensors**. A 2.4 GHz microwave sensor detects an object approaching at 2 m/s. Compute the Doppler shift. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Doppler Effect Concept:** *(3 marks)*

When a source emits a wave and the target moves, the reflected wave's frequency is shifted by an amount proportional to the radial velocity. For a radar / microwave reflection:

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot v_r \cdot f_0}{c}$$

The factor **2** appears because the wave undergoes a frequency shift **twice** — once when received by the moving target, and once again when re-emitted.

**Numerical Computation:** *(3 marks)*

Given:
- $f_0 = 2.4 \times 10^{9}$ Hz
- $v_r = 2$ m/s
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{2 \times 2 \times 2.4 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{9.6 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\Delta f \;=\; 32 \;\text{Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta f = 32$ Hz *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** Compare **PIR, Ultrasonic, Accelerometer, and Microwave motion sensors** in terms of range, power, output, and best IoT use case. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:** *(Refer to Section 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix for full table)*

| Sensor | Range | Power | Output | Best IoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PIR | 3–7 m | Very Low | Binary | Smart lighting |
| Ultrasonic | 0.02–4 m | Medium | Distance | Smart parking, level sensing |
| Accelerometer | ±2g to ±16g | Low | Acceleration | Wearables, fall detection |
| Microwave | 5–15 m | Medium-High | Binary / Velocity | Intrusion detection |

*(Marking Scheme: Tabular comparison – 5 marks, Conclusion – 2 marks)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Motion Sensor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic distance formula — students often write $d = v \cdot t$ instead of $d = v \cdot t / 2$. *(−2 marks typical)*
> 2. **Using $v_{\text{sound}} = 343$ m/s without temperature correction** — full credit only if $T$ is given and the formula $v = 331 + 0.6T$ is shown. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Confusing PIR with IR transmitter** — PIR is *passive* (does not emit IR).
> 4. **Writing "PIR detects motion"** instead of *"PIR detects change in IR flux"* — examiner expects the precise wording.
> 5. **In Doppler problems**, missing that the shift is **doubled** for reflected radar systems.
> 6. **No diagram = −2 marks** on any 7-mark question that explicitly asks for one.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Motion sensing is a **physical-layer (perception-layer)** function in IoT.
- ✅ **PIR** → Passive, binary, low-power, detects warm-body movement via IR flux change.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic** → Active, distance output, formula: $d = (v_{\text{sound}} \cdot \Delta t)/2$; HC-SR04 uses **40 kHz**; $v_{\text{sound}} = 331 + 0.6T$.
- ✅ **Accelerometer** → MEMS, measures **specific force**; in free-fall, output = **0 g** (used for fall detection).
- ✅ **Gyroscope** → Measures **angular velocity** via Coriolis force; combines with accelerometer → **IMU**.
- ✅ **Microwave / Doppler Radar** → Active, detects motion via Doppler shift: $\Delta f = 2 v_r f_0 / c$; can sense through thin walls.
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann law** $P = \sigma T^4$ explains why a human body (310 K) emits strong IR detectable by PIR.
- ✅ **Wien's displacement law** $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = b/T$ — human body peak ≈ **9.4 µm**, inside the 8–14 µm atmospheric IR window.
- ✅ **Smart parking, fall detection, smart lighting, intrusion alarms** — the four classic KTU viva applications.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers (m, Hz, m/s², g).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **"Distance"** refers to the quantitative measurement of spatial separation between two physical entities (typically a sensor node and a target object, or between two communicating IoT devices). It forms the foundational parameter for **localization, ranging, proximity sensing, navigation, and context-aware computing** in IoT ecosystems.

Mathematically, distance is denoted as a **scalar quantity** $d$ representing the length of the shortest path (Euclidean straight-line) between two points in a defined coordinate space.

$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$$

For 2D IoT deployments (which is the most common planar WSN scenario), this reduces to:

$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Distance estimation in IoT is the process of determining the spatial separation between IoT nodes using signal propagation characteristics, time measurements, or wave-based sensing techniques. It is the **pre-requisite for localization** in any Smart City, Smart Agriculture, or Industrial IoT (IIoT) deployment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Echo in a Mountain Valley"

Imagine you shout across a deep mountain valley. The time it takes for your voice to travel to the opposite mountain wall and return as an echo tells you **how far away** the wall is.

- If the echo returns in **2 seconds**, the wall is approximately **340 meters** away (since sound travels at ~340 m/s, and the sound traveled to the wall and back).
- This is precisely how an **ultrasonic distance sensor (HC-SR04)** works in an IoT parking system — it sends a high-frequency sound pulse and waits for the echo to calculate the distance of an approaching vehicle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In IoT, distance is rarely "measured" directly with a ruler. Instead, it is **inferred** from physical phenomena such as signal strength, time-of-flight, or angle of arrival.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and metrics are **mandatory** for any KTU examination on this topic:

| Symbol | Constant / Metric | Value | Unit |
|--------|-------------------|-------|------|
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| $v_s$ | Speed of sound in air (at 20°C) | **343** | m/s |
| $f$ | Carrier frequency (typical IoT) | 2.4 to 5 | GHz |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength | $\lambda = c/f$ | meters |
| $P_t$ | Transmitted power | — | dBm / mW |
| $P_r$ | Received power | — | dBm / mW |
| $d$ | Distance (the unknown) | — | meters |

## 1.4 Categories of Distance Estimation in IoT

Distance estimation in IoT is broadly classified into **three families**:

1. **Wave-based Sensing (Active Proximity Sensors)**
   - Ultrasonic sensors
   - Infrared (IR) sensors
   - LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)

2. **RF-based Ranging (Wireless Signal Methods)**
   - Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)
   - Time of Arrival (ToA)
   - Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA)
   - Angle of Arrival (AoA)

3. **Hybrid / Inertial Methods**
   - Accelerometer + Time integration
   - Computer vision with stereo cameras

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Wave-Based Distance Sensing

Wave-based distance sensors operate on the principle of **time-of-flight (ToF)**. A transmitter emits a wave packet, and the receiver measures how long it takes for the wave to either:
- **Bounce back** from a target (monostatic mode, e.g., ultrasonic), or
- **Arrive** at a remote node (bistatic mode, e.g., RF ToA).

### Step-by-Step Operating Principle (Ultrasonic HC-SR04)

1. The IoT microcontroller (e.g., ESP32, Arduino) sends a **10 µs trigger pulse** to the sensor.
2. The sensor emits **8 cycles of 40 kHz ultrasonic burst**.
3. The wave travels through air at velocity $v_s \approx 343$ m/s.
4. The wave strikes an object and **reflects back** (echo).
5. The sensor sets the **Echo pin HIGH** for a duration equal to the round-trip time $t$.
6. The microcontroller reads $t$ and computes distance.

The total distance traveled by the wave is $v_s \times t$, but since this is a **round trip**, the actual one-way distance to the object is:

$$d = \frac{v_s \times t}{2}$$

Where:
- $d$ = distance to object (meters)
- $v_s$ = speed of sound in air (m/s)
- $t$ = round-trip echo time (seconds)

## 2.2 Operational Logic — RF-Based Ranging

### A. RSSI Method (Received Signal Strength Indicator)

Every wireless IoT device (Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, LoRa) reports the strength of the received signal. As the distance $d$ increases, the signal strength decreases following the **Log-Distance Path Loss Model**:

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$

Where:
- $PL(d)$ = Path loss at distance $d$ (in dB)
- $PL(d_0)$ = Path loss at the reference distance $d_0$ (typically $d_0 = 1$ m)
- $n$ = **Path Loss Exponent** (depends on environment)
- $X_\sigma$ = Shadow fading component (Gaussian, zero mean)

Rearranging for distance $d$:

$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL(d) - PL(d_0)}{10n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Loss Exponent ($n$) values (must memorize for KTU):**
> - Free Space: $n = 2$
> - Urban Cellular: $n = 2.7$ to $3.5$
> - Shadowed Urban: $n = 3$ to $5$
> - Obstructed Indoor: $n = 4$ to $6$

### B. ToA (Time of Arrival)

$$d = c \times (t_r - t_t)$$

Where $c$ is the speed of light, $t_t$ is the transmit timestamp, and $t_r$ is the receive timestamp. **Requires precise clock synchronization** between transmitter and receiver.

### C. TDoA (Time Difference of Arrival)

Uses the difference in arrival times at **multiple synchronized receivers** to estimate the transmitter's location. No synchronization is needed between transmitter and receiver, but the receivers must be precisely synchronized.

### D. AoA (Angle of Arrival)

Uses antenna arrays to estimate the angle from which the signal arrived. The distance is then computed using geometric triangulation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Method | Core Formula | Variables | Typical Error |
|---|--------|--------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Ultrasonic ToF | $d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$ | $v_s$ = 343 m/s, $t$ = echo time | ±1 cm |
| 2 | RSSI Path Loss | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL(d) - PL(d_0)}{10n}}$ | $n$ = path loss exponent | 2–5 m |
| 3 | ToA | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | <1 m |
| 4 | TDoA | $\Delta d = c \cdot \Delta t_{ij}$ | $\Delta t_{ij}$ = TDoA at receivers $i, j$ | 1–3 m |
| 5 | AoA | $\theta = \arctan(\Delta y / \Delta x)$ | Angle from antenna array | 1–2 m |
| 6 | Friis Transmission | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains | Used to derive RSSI |
| 7 | 2D Distance | $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$ | Coordinates | Geometric |
| 8 | Trilateration | $d_i^2 = (x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2$ | $i = 1, 2, 3$ | Localization |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Distance Method Used | Purpose |
|--------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Smart Parking (IoT) | Ultrasonic | Detect vehicle slot occupancy |
| Asset Tracking (BLE Beacons) | RSSI | Indoor asset positioning |
| Autonomous Vehicles | LiDAR + ToF | Obstacle avoidance, mapping |
| Smart Agriculture | LoRa RSSI | Soil sensor localization in field |
| Industrial IoT (IIoT) | UWB ToA | High-precision robot navigation |
| Healthcare Wearables | IR Proximity | Fall detection, gesture sensing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: RSSI to Distance Conversion

We will now rigorously derive the distance formula starting from the **Friis Transmission Equation** — the foundation of all RF-based ranging in IoT.

### Given:

The Friis Transmission Equation in free space:

$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $P_r$ = received power (W)
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (W)
- $G_t$ = transmit antenna gain (linear)
- $G_r$ = receive antenna gain (linear)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $d$ = distance between antennas (m)

### Step 1: Express in Decibel (dB) Scale

Converting to dB (since IoT radios report RSSI in dBm):

$$P_r^{dBm} = P_t^{dBm} + G_t^{dB} + G_r^{dB} - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Explanation:** The term $20 \log_{10}(4\pi d / \lambda)$ is the free-space path loss $PL_{FS}$.

### Step 2: Define Free-Space Path Loss

$$PL_{FS}(d) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$

**Explanation:** Substituting $\lambda = c/f$ into the path loss expression gives a frequency-dependent loss.

### Step 3: Generalize to Log-Distance Model

In real IoT environments, the path is not free space. The general log-distance model extends the above with the path loss exponent $n$:

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

**Explanation:** When $d = d_0$, the path loss is $PL(d_0)$, and the loss grows logarithmically with distance at rate $10n$ dB per decade.

### Step 4: Solve for Distance $d$

Rearranging the log-distance equation:

$$PL(d) - PL(d_0) = 10n \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

$$\frac{PL(d) - PL(d_0)}{10n} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

$$\frac{d}{d_0} = 10^{\frac{PL(d) - PL(d_0)}{10n}}$$

$$\boxed{d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL(d) - PL(d_0)}{10n}}}$$

### Step 5: Connect to RSSI

Since $PL(d) = P_t - P_r$ (in dBm), we can also write:

$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{(P_t - P_r) - PL(d_0)}{10n}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** An IoT BLE beacon transmits at $P_t = 0$ dBm. A smartphone receives it at $P_r = -65$ dBm. The reference path loss at $d_0 = 1$ m is $PL(d_0) = 40$ dB. The environment path loss exponent is $n = 3$. Calculate the distance to the beacon.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute total path loss at the unknown distance.

$$PL(d) = P_t - P_r = 0 - (-65) = 65 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the log-distance formula.

$$d = 1 \cdot 10^{\frac{65 - 40}{10 \times 3}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponent.

$$\frac{25}{30} = 0.8333$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the power of 10.

$$d = 10^{0.8333}$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$d \approx 6.81 \text{ meters}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A path loss of 65 dB in an urban-like environment ($n=3$) corresponds to a "few meters" — the answer 6.81 m is dimensionally and physically consistent. ✓

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Equation

### Given:

The HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor returns a HIGH pulse of width $t$ on its Echo pin. The wave travels at speed of sound $v_s = 343$ m/s at 20°C.

### Step 1: Total Distance Traveled by Wave

The wave travels from the transmitter to the object AND back. Total distance:

$$d_{total} = v_s \cdot t$$

### Step 2: Account for Round Trip

Since the object is only at the **midpoint** of the round trip:

$$d = \frac{d_{total}}{2} = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$

### Step 3: Temperature Correction (Advanced)

Speed of sound varies with air temperature $T$ (in °C):

$$v_s = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T \text{ m/s}$$

For $T = 25°C$:

$$v_s = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 25 = 331.3 + 15.15 = 346.45 \text{ m/s}$$

### Worked Example: Ultrasonic

> **Problem:** An HC-SR04 sensor returns an echo pulse width of $t = 5.85$ ms. Air temperature is $T = 20°C$. Calculate the object's distance.

**Step 1:** Compute speed of sound.

$$v_s = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 20 = 343.42 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply distance formula.

$$d = \frac{343.42 \times 5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$d = \frac{2.009}{2} = 1.004 \text{ m}$$

> The object is **1 meter** away. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Ultrasonic | IR | RSSI (BLE/Wi-Fi) | LiDAR | UWB |
|-----------|-----------|----|--------------------|-------|-----|
| Range | 0.02–4 m | 0.1–1.5 m | 1–50 m | 0.1–100 m | 1–100 m |
| Accuracy | ±1 cm | ±5 cm | 2–5 m | ±2 cm | <10 cm |
| Cost | Very Low | Very Low | Low (inbuilt) | High | High |
| Affected by | Soft surfaces, temp | Sunlight, color | Multipath, obstacles | Rain, dust | Metal reflection |
| Use Case | Parking, bin level | Line follower | Indoor tracking | Autonomous cars | IIoT, AR/VR |
| Speed of wave | Sound (343 m/s) | Light | RF | Light | RF pulse |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define distance estimation in IoT. List any two methods used for measuring distance in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer:**

In IoT, **distance estimation** refers to the process of quantitatively determining the spatial separation between two nodes (or between a node and an object) using signal propagation, time-of-flight, or wave reflection principles. It is a fundamental primitive for **localization, tracking, and context-aware applications** in IoT.

**Two methods:**
1. **Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Sensing** — Uses high-frequency sound waves (40 kHz) and measures echo time. Formula: $d = (v_s \cdot t) / 2$.
2. **RSSI-based RF Ranging** — Uses received signal strength attenuation following the log-distance path loss model. Formula: $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(PL(d) - PL(d_0))/(10n)}$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the path loss exponent $n$ in RSSI-based distance estimation. What is its typical range in indoor environments?**

**Model Answer:**

The **path loss exponent $n$** quantifies the rate at which the RF signal strength decays with distance in a given environment. It accounts for physical phenomena like **reflection, diffraction, scattering, and multipath fading**.

- In **free space**: $n = 2$ (theoretical minimum).
- In **indoor obstructed environments** (typical KTU exam answer): $n$ ranges from **4 to 6**.
- In **urban outdoor**: $n$ ranges from **2.7 to 3.5**.

A higher $n$ means faster signal decay with distance, which improves ranging accuracy at short ranges but degrades performance over longer distances.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **ultrasonic distance measurement system** used in an IoT-based smart parking application. Derive the distance formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT BLE beacon transmits at $P_t = +4$ dBm. A receiver reports $P_r = -72$ dBm. The reference path loss at $d_0 = 1$ m is $PL(d_0) = 38$ dB, and the path loss exponent is $n = 2.8$. Calculate the distance between the beacon and the receiver. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Ultrasonic Distance Measurement:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
ESP32 Microcontroller  -->  HC-SR04 Sensor  -->  Air Medium  -->  Vehicle
        ^                      |   (echo)
        |______________________|
        (Echo pin pulse width t)
```

**Working Principle (Step-by-step):**

1. The ESP32 sends a **10 µs HIGH trigger pulse** to the HC-SR04.
2. The sensor internally transmits **8 pulses of 40 kHz ultrasonic sound**.
3. The sound wave propagates through air at $v_s \approx 343$ m/s.
4. Upon striking the vehicle, the wave **reflects** back as an echo.
5. The sensor raises its **Echo pin HIGH** for a duration $t$ equal to the round-trip travel time.
6. The ESP32 measures $t$ using a hardware timer or `pulseIn()`.
7. Distance is computed as $d = (v_s \cdot t)/2$.
8. The value is published via **MQTT** to a cloud dashboard for parking slot monitoring.

**Derivation:**

Let $t$ be the round-trip time and $v_s$ be the speed of sound. The total distance traveled by the wave is $v_s \cdot t$. Since this is a **round trip** (forward + return):

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$

If temperature $T$ (in °C) is considered:

$$v_s = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T$$

$$\boxed{d = \frac{(331.3 + 0.606T) \cdot t}{2}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute total path loss.

$$PL(d) = P_t - P_r = 4 - (-72) = 76 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the log-distance formula.

$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\frac{PL(d) - PL(d_0)}{10n}}$$

$$d = 1 \cdot 10^{\frac{76 - 38}{10 \times 2.8}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.

$$76 - 38 = 38$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator.

$$10 \times 2.8 = 28$$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$\frac{38}{28} = 1.3571$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate.

$$d = 10^{1.3571} \approx 22.76 \text{ meters}$$

**Final Answer:** $d \approx 22.76$ m

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **ToA, TDoA, AoA, and RSSI** methods of distance estimation in IoT. Mention the synchronization requirement for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT sensor measures distance using ultrasonic. The echo time is $t = 8.75$ ms at $T = 30°C$. Calculate the distance and comment on the effect of temperature. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Method | Measured Quantity | Distance Formula | Synchronization Required |
|--------|------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **ToA** | Absolute arrival time | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Between Tx and Rx (strict) |
| **TDoA** | Difference in arrival times at multiple Rx | $\Delta d = c \cdot \Delta t_{ij}$ | Between Receivers only |
| **AoA** | Angle of incoming signal | $\theta = \arctan(\Delta y/\Delta x)$ | Not required; uses antenna array |
| **RSSI** | Received signal strength | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(PL(d) - PL(d_0))/(10n)}$ | Not required |

**Discussions:**
- **ToA** offers high accuracy but demands expensive atomic clocks for nanosecond-level synchronization.
- **TDoA** is more practical — only the base stations need synchronization (e.g., GPS-disciplined oscillators).
- **AoA** requires antenna arrays and is sensitive to multipath.
- **RSSI** is the simplest and cheapest, but accuracy is poor in dynamic environments.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute speed of sound at $T = 30°C$.

$$v_s = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 30 = 331.3 + 18.18 = 349.48 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the distance formula.

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2} = \frac{349.48 \times 8.75 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.

$$349.48 \times 8.75 \times 10^{-3} = 3.058 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by 2.

$$d = \frac{3.058}{2} = 1.529 \text{ m}$$

**Comment on Temperature Effect:**

If we had naively used $v_s = 343$ m/s (assumed at 20°C):

$$d_{naive} = \frac{343 \times 8.75 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.501 \text{ m}$$

**Error introduced:** $1.529 - 1.501 = 0.028$ m (i.e., **2.8 cm error**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to divide by 2 in the ultrasonic formula — this yields a **2× error** and immediate loss of 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In RSSI problems, students confuse the sign of $P_r$. If $P_r$ is in dBm, remember $P_r$ is negative, so $P_t - P_r$ becomes a positive large number.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Path loss exponent $n$ is often written as just "$n$" without units (it is dimensionless). Do not write "m" or "dB" with $n$.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Speed of sound must be temperature-corrected in precise applications. Using 343 m/s blindly at 30°C introduces ~2% error.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In trilateration, students often confuse $d_i^2$ with $d_i$ while solving the linear system. Always square the distance first, then linearize the equations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before KTU Exam):**

- ✅ **Ultrasonic formula:** $d = (v_s \cdot t) / 2$ — always divide by 2 for round trip.
- ✅ **Speed of sound:** $v_s = 331.3 + 0.606T$ (m/s) where $T$ is in °C.
- ✅ **RSSI formula:** $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(PL(d) - PL(d_0))/(10n)}$.
- ✅ **Path loss exponent $n$:** Free space = 2, Indoor = 4–6, Urban = 2.7–3.5.
- ✅ **ToA formula:** $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **ToA requires Tx-Rx clock synchronization**; **TDoA requires only Rx-Rx synchronization**.
- ✅ **HC-SR04** uses 40 kHz ultrasonic waves, range ~2 cm to 4 m, accuracy ±1 cm.
- ✅ **Trilateration** uses **3 anchor nodes** with known positions to compute the unknown node's coordinates.
- ✅ **Euclidean 2D distance:** $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2}$.
- ✅ **RSSI is the cheapest but least accurate** method; UWB is the most accurate but expensive.
- ✅ **Multipath fading, NLOS (Non-Line-of-Sight), and shadowing** are the main sources of error in RF-based distance estimation.
- ✅ **RSSI is reported in dBm** (decibel-milliwatt); remember $0$ dBm = $1$ mW.
- ✅ **LiDAR** uses light waves; offers high accuracy but is expensive and affected by weather.
- ✅ Always state the **units** of the final answer — marks are deducted for "22.76" without "meters".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) and embedded sensing, **Vibration** is defined as the periodic or oscillatory motion of a body, structure, or mechanical component about a reference equilibrium position, characterized by quantifiable parameters of **displacement**, **velocity**, and **acceleration** as functions of time. From an IoT perspective, vibration is not merely a mechanical phenomenon but a critical *physical observable* that is transduced into electrical signals by micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS) and piezoelectric transducers, digitized, and transmitted over a network for remote monitoring, fault diagnosis, and predictive analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4 — Sensors & Actuators for IoT):**
> Vibration is classified as a *kinematic / mechanical stimulus* that, when coupled with a suitable transducer, becomes a first-class IoT data modality. It is the foundational signal for Industrial IoT (IIoT), Structural Health Monitoring (SHM), and smart transportation systems.

The two fundamental descriptors of any vibration signal in IoT are:
- **Amplitude** — the magnitude of oscillation (measured in $m$, $m/s$, or $g$).
- **Frequency** — the rate at which oscillations repeat (measured in **Hz** or **rad/s**).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **tuning fork** you strike gently. The prongs swing back and forth, creating a "hum" you can hear. That microscopic, periodic wobble is vibration. Now imagine a massive industrial motor in a factory. When its bearings begin to wear, the motor starts to "wobble" imperceptibly — a vibration that grows in intensity long before the machine fails. An IoT vibration sensor is essentially a **tiny digital ear**, glued to the motor, that listens to this wobble 24/7, converts the mechanical jiggle into electrical data, and posts it to the cloud so an engineer (or an AI) can predict the breakdown *weeks in advance*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in IoT:** Vibration is the single most informative indicator of *machine health*. Industry data shows **70% of rotating machinery failures** are preceded by abnormal vibration signatures, making vibration sensing the cornerstone of *predictive maintenance* — a flagship IIoT use case.

### 1.3 Vibration as a Vector Quantity in IoT

Modern 3-axis MEMS accelerometers (e.g., ADXL345, MPU6050, LIS3DHTR) capture vibration along three orthogonal Cartesian axes ($X$, $Y$, $Z$), making the resultant magnitude a vector quantity:

$$\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$$

This is essential in IoT nodes because the *direction* of vibration often reveals the root cause (e.g., imbalance vs. misalignment in rotating shafts).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Model of Vibration (Simple Harmonic Motion — SHM)

Any periodic vibration can be decomposed — via the **Fourier theorem** — into a sum of sinusoidal components. The elementary building block is **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)**, governed by a second-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE):

$$m\frac{d^2x(t)}{dt^2} + c\frac{dx(t)}{dt} + kx(t) = F(t)$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of the vibrating element (kg)
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = stiffness of the spring-like element (N/m)
- $F(t)$ = external excitation force (N)
- $x(t)$ = displacement response (m)

The homogeneous (free-vibration) solution yields the **natural (undamped) angular frequency**:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

The corresponding **natural frequency in Hz**:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

### 2.2 Degree-of-Freedom (DOF) Classification in IoT Sensors

| Class | Description | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1-DOF** | Single axis, no coupling | Piezoelectric pencil-probe accelerometer on a bearing housing |
| **3-DOF (3-axis)** | Three orthogonal MEMS proof masses | ADXL345 in a wearable fall-detection wristband |
| **6-DOF (IMU)** | 3-axis accel + 3-axis gyro | MPU6050 in a drone for flight stabilization |

### 2.3 Types of Vibration Relevant to IoT

1. **Free Vibration** — Occurs after a single impulse, decaying due to damping. Used in *resonance-based mass sensing* (e.g., MEMS chemical sensors).
2. **Forced Vibration** — Continuous excitation by an external periodic force. Dominant in *rotating machinery monitoring*.
3. **Damped Vibration** — Energy dissipates via friction, fluid drag, or material hysteresis. Real-world systems are always damped.
4. **Random Vibration** — Stochastic, broadband (e.g., road roughness sensed by a vehicle's IMU). Analyzed via *Power Spectral Density (PSD)*.

### 2.4 Vibration Sensor Taxonomy

| Sensor Type | Operating Principle | Output | Typical IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric** | Stress-induced charge in quartz/ceramic | Charge / Voltage (µA range) | High-frequency bearing monitoring (>1 kHz) |
| **MEMS Capacitive** | Proof-mass displaces between fixed plates → ΔC | Analog voltage / I²C / SPI | Wearables, structural health |
| **Piezoresistive** | Strain changes semiconductor resistance | Resistance change | High-g shock, crash sensors |
| **Eddy-Current (Proximity)** | Induced eddy currents in target | Displacement | Turbine blade tip-timing |
| **Laser Doppler Vibrometer** | Doppler shift of reflected laser | Optical (interferometric) | Non-contact precision metrology |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Displacement in SHM | m |
| 2 | $v(t) = A\omega\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | Velocity in SHM | m/s |
| 3 | $a(t) = -A\omega^2\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Acceleration in SHM | m/s² |
| 4 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular–frequency conversion | rad/s |
| 5 | $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $f_d = f_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Damped natural frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $x_{\text{rms}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS amplitude (sine) | m |
| 8 | $g = 9.80665$ | Standard gravity (exact) | m/s² |
| 9 | $\text{Crest Factor} = \frac{x_{\text{peak}}}{x_{\text{rms}}}$ | Impulsiveness indicator | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{PSD} = \lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}E\!\left[\vert X(f)\vert^2\right]$ | Power Spectral Density | g²/Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** The relation $a = -\omega^2 x$ is the single most tested identity. If a problem gives displacement amplitude, you can immediately obtain acceleration amplitude by multiplying with $\omega^2$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Continuous monitoring of pumps, motors, gearboxes in refineries, reducing unplanned downtime by up to **70%**.
- **Smart Cities:** Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) of bridges and high-rises using distributed MEMS nodes.
- **Automotive IoT:** Tire-pressure and wheel-imbalance sensing via in-tire MEMS accelerometers.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Tremor quantification in Parkinson's disease; gait analysis.
- **Agriculture IoT:** Vibration-based seed-flow and grain-loss sensors on harvesters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equation of Motion for a Spring–Mass–Damper System (Free Vibration)

**Step 1 — System Description.**
Consider a mass $m$ attached to a linear spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper $c$, free to move on a frictionless surface.

**Step 2 — Force Balance (Newton's Second Law).**
By D'Alembert's principle, the inertial force $m\ddot{x}$ must balance the restoring spring force $-kx$ and the damping force $-c\dot{x}$:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) = -kx(t) - c\dot{x}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrangement into Standard Form.**
Moving all terms to the LHS:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Assume Exponential Solution.**
Trial solution $x(t) = X_0 e^{st}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= sX_0 e^{st} \\
\ddot{x}(t) &= s^2 X_0 e^{st}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the ODE.**

$$(ms^2 + cs + k)X_0 e^{st} = 0$$

Since $X_0 e^{st} \neq 0$ for a non-trivial motion, the **characteristic equation** is:

$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve the Quadratic.**

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

**Step 7 — Define Damping Ratio $\zeta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\zeta &= \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} \\
c_{\text{crit}} &= 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Sub-divide Critical Damping Constant.**

The roots can then be compactly expressed as:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 9 — Damped Natural Frequency.**

For the underdamped case ($0 < \zeta < 1$), the imaginary part of the root defines the **damped natural angular frequency**:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

**Step 10 — Final Time-Domain Solution (Underdamped).**

$$x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin\!\bigl(\omega_d t + \phi\bigr)$$

This is a **decaying sinusoid** — the canonical signature of free damped vibration.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Value of a Sinusoidal Vibration Signal

**Step 1 — Definition of RMS.**
The Root Mean Square value of a periodic signal $x(t)$ over period $T$ is:

$$x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t)\,dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the SHM Waveform.**
Let $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$:

$$x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T A^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\,dt}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Power-Reduction Identity.**

$$\sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^T A^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\,dt &= \frac{A^2}{2}\int_0^T \bigl[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\bigr]\,dt \\
&= \frac{A^2}{2}\Bigl[t - \tfrac{1}{2\omega}\sin(2\omega t)\Bigr]_0^T \\
&= \frac{A^2}{2}\cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

(The $\sin(2\omega T)$ term vanishes because $\omega T = 2\pi$.)

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{A^2}{2}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071\,A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Industrial vibration standards (ISO 10816, ISO 20816) prescribe RMS velocity (mm/s) as the primary metric for assessing machine health, because RMS is *independent of phase* and best correlates with vibration energy / damage potential.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration Amplitudes

**Step 1 — Starting Point: SHM Displacement.**

$$x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to Obtain Velocity.**

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = A\omega\cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

Hence the velocity amplitude is $V = A\omega$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate Again to Obtain Acceleration.**

$$a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = -A\omega^2\sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Hence the acceleration amplitude is $A_{\text{accel}} = A\omega^2$.

**Step 4 — Unified Phase-Space Relations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \omega X \\
A_{\text{accel}} &= \omega^2 X = \omega V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**
Given: $X = 0.1$ mm, $f = 50$ Hz, find the acceleration amplitude in $g$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159 \text{ rad/s} \\
A_{\text{accel}} &= \omega^2 X = (314.159)^2 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 98696 \times 10^{-4} = 9.8696 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
A_{\text{accel}}\,(g) &= \frac{9.8696}{9.80665} = 1.0064\,g
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus a **mere 0.1 mm** oscillation at 50 Hz corresponds to **~1 g** of acceleration — illustrating why MEMS accelerometers must have wide dynamic range.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Vibration Metric

| Metric | Formula | Best Used For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak | $x_{\text{peak}} = \max\vert x(t)\vert$ | Shock, transient events | Sensitive to outliers |
| Peak-to-Peak | $x_{p-p} = 2A$ | Sinusoidal check | Insensitive to broadband |
| RMS | $x_{\text{rms}} = A/\sqrt{2}$ | Steady-state machine health (ISO 10816) | Hides impulsive faults |
| Crest Factor | $\text{CF} = x_{\text{peak}}/x_{\text{rms}}$ | Bearing / gear-tooth defects | Requires calibrated sensor |
| Kurtosis | $K = E[(x-\mu)^4]/\sigma^4$ | Early bearing wear (impulsiveness) | Needs long record |
| PSD | $S_{xx}(f) = \vert X(f)\vert^2 / \Delta f$ | Frequency-domain diagnostics | Computationally heavier (FFT) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IoT Vibration Sensing Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Vibration Source] --> B[Transducer - MEMS or Piezoelectric]
    B --> C[Charge / Voltage Amplifier]
    C --> D[Analog Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[12 to 16 bit ADC]
    E --> F[MCU - ARM Cortex or ESP32]
    F --> G[FFT and Feature Extraction Engine]
    G --> H[Wireless Module - WiFi, BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT]
    H --> I[Cloud Gateway and MQTT Broker]
    I --> J[Time-Series Database - InfluxDB]
    J --> K[Anomaly Detection and ML Model]
    K --> L[Dashboard and Alert Engine]

    subgraph nodeA["Analog Front End AFE"]
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph nodeB["Digital Signal Processing"]
        F
        G
    end

    subgraph nodeC["Network and Cloud Tier"]
        H
        I
        J
        K
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **transducer stage** converts physical oscillation into an electrical signal.
- The **AFE** (Analog Front End) conditions, filters, and digitizes the signal.
- The **DSP stage** performs on-node FFT and extracts features like RMS, kurtosis, dominant frequency.
- The **cloud tier** stores time-series data, runs predictive ML, and dispatches alerts.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Vibration Feature Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Raw[Raw Acceleration Samples a_t] --> Window[Hanning Window - 1024 samples]
    Window --> FFT[FFT - Real Valued]
    FFT --> PSDCalc[Power Spectral Density - Welch Method]
    PSDCalc --> PeakDet[Peak Picking - Dominant Frequency f_d]
    PSDCalc --> RMSCalc[RMS in ISO Bands 10 to 1000 Hz]
    PeakDet --> Feat[Feature Vector V = f_d, RMS, Crest, Kurtosis]
    RMSCalc --> Feat
    Feat --> ML[Trained Classifier - SVM or Random Forest]
    ML --> Decision{Fault Type}
    Decision -->|Normal| OK[Green Status]
    Decision -->|Imbalance| W1[Imbalance Alert]
    Decision -->|Bearing Defect| W2[Outer Race Defect Alert]
    Decision -->|Looseness| W3[Mechanical Looseness Alert]
```

---

### 4.3 Sensor Output Characteristics (Qualitative)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph A[Accelerometer Type]
        A1[MEMS Capacitive - ADXL345]
        A2[Piezoelectric - PCB 352C33]
        A3[Piezoresistive - Endevco 7264]
    end
    A1 --> B1[Bandwidth - DC to 1.6 kHz]
    A2 --> B2[Bandwidth - 1 Hz to 20 kHz]
    A3 --> B3[Bandwidth - DC to 50 kHz]
    B1 --> C1[Use - Tilt, Motion, Wearables]
    B2 --> C2[Use - Industrial Bearing Health]
    B3 --> C3[Use - Crash and High-g Shock]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 7-mark question asks: *"Compare MEMS capacitive and piezoelectric accelerometers for IoT vibration monitoring."* The above diagram gives you the structural skeleton to write a comparative answer covering bandwidth, power consumption, interface (analog vs. I²C/SPI), and cost.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define vibration in the context of IoT. List any two vibration sensor types used in IoT applications.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Vibration, in IoT, is the *periodic oscillatory motion* of a body or structure about an equilibrium position, transduced into electrical signals for remote monitoring and analytics. It is characterized by amplitude, frequency, and phase.

Two commonly used vibration sensor types in IoT:
1. **MEMS capacitive accelerometer** (e.g., ADXL345) — used in wearables and structural monitoring.
2. **Piezoelectric accelerometer** (e.g., PCB 352C33) — used in high-frequency industrial machine-health monitoring.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1.5 M) + 2 sensor types (1.5 M).

---

**Q2. What is the significance of RMS value in vibration measurement? State the RMS value of a pure sinusoidal signal of peak amplitude $A$.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
The RMS (Root Mean Square) value represents the *effective* or *energy-equivalent DC* value of a time-varying vibration signal. It is the metric prescribed by international standards (ISO 10816 / ISO 20816) for assessing the severity of machine vibration because it correlates directly with vibration energy and damage potential.

For a pure sine wave of peak amplitude $A$:

$$x_{\text{rms}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071\,A$$

**Mark Distribution:** Significance (1.5 M) + Formula (1.5 M).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**Q.A) (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an IoT-based vibration monitoring system. List the role of each block. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

An IoT-based vibration monitoring system converts mechanical oscillation into actionable data through four functional tiers:

1. **Sensing Tier (Transducer):** A MEMS or piezoelectric accelerometer converts vibration into an analog voltage proportional to acceleration. Example: ADXL345 (±16 g range, I²C interface).

2. **Signal Conditioning Tier (AFE):** Comprises a charge amplifier (for piezo), an anti-aliasing low-pass filter (cut-off at $f_s/2$, where $f_s$ is the sampling rate), and a 12–16 bit ADC for digitization.

3. **Processing Tier (Edge / MCU):** A microcontroller (e.g., ESP32) performs on-node DSP — FFT computation, RMS extraction, dominant frequency detection, and feature vector generation.

4. **Communication Tier:** Wireless transmission via Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRaWAN, or NB-IoT to a cloud gateway, using lightweight protocols such as MQTT or CoAP.

5. **Cloud / Analytics Tier:** Data is stored in a time-series database (InfluxDB), analyzed by ML models (SVM, Random Forest, LSTM) for fault classification, and visualized via dashboards (Grafana) with real-time alerts.

**Mark Split:** Block diagram (2 M) + 5 blocks explained (1 M each = 5 M).

---

**Q.A) (b)** A machine vibrates sinusoidally with a displacement amplitude of **0.2 mm** at a frequency of **60 Hz**. Calculate:
   (i) the peak velocity, (ii) the peak acceleration in m/s², and (iii) the peak acceleration in $g$. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $X = 0.2$ mm $= 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $f = 60$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Angular frequency.**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 60 = 376.991 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(i) Peak Velocity.**

$$V = \omega X = 376.991 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0754 \text{ m/s} = 75.4 \text{ mm/s}$$

**(ii) Peak Acceleration (m/s²).**

$$A_{\text{accel}} = \omega^2 X = (376.991)^2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 142122 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} = 28.42 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(iii) Peak Acceleration in $g$.**

$$A_{\text{accel}}(g) = \frac{28.42}{9.80665} \approx 2.90\,g$$

**Mark Split:** ω calculation (1 M) + (i) 2 M + (ii) 2 M + (iii) 2 M.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**Q.B) (a)** Differentiate between **MEMS capacitive** and **piezoelectric** accelerometers. State one IoT application of each. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | MEMS Capacitive | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Proof-mass displacement changes capacitance between fixed plates | Stress on piezoelectric crystal generates charge |
| **Frequency Response** | DC to ~1.6 kHz (limited by mass & air damping) | ~1 Hz to >20 kHz (excellent high-freq) |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (mW range, suitable for battery IoT) | Moderate (needs charge amplifier) |
| **Output Interface** | Digital (I²C / SPI) — easy to MCU | Analog (charge / voltage) |
| **Sensitivity** | Lower (~mg level) | Very high (µg level) |
| **Cost** | Low (batch-fabricated) | Higher (precision assembly) |
| **Temperature Drift** | Moderate | Low |
| **IoT Application** | Wearable fall detection, structural tilt | Industrial bearing / gearbox monitoring |

**Mark Split:** Tabular comparison (5 M) + one application each (2 M).

---

**Q.B) (b)** Derive the relationship between displacement amplitude, velocity amplitude, and acceleration amplitude for a simple harmonic vibration. Hence, for a vibration of displacement amplitude **0.5 mm** and frequency **25 Hz**, compute the RMS acceleration in $g$. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**
Starting from $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(t) &= \frac{dx}{dt} = A\omega\cos(\omega t + \phi) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V = A\omega \\
a(t) &= \frac{dv}{dt} = -A\omega^2\sin(\omega t + \phi) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_{\text{accel}} = A\omega^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Unified relations:

$$V = \omega X \quad \text{and} \quad A_{\text{accel}} = \omega^2 X = \omega V$$

**Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $X = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $f = 25$ Hz.

$$\omega = 2\pi \times 25 = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Peak acceleration:**

$$A_{\text{peak}} = \omega^2 X = (157.08)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 24674 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 12.337 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**RMS acceleration:**

$$A_{\text{rms}} = \frac{A_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{12.337}{1.4142} = 8.724 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**In $g$:**

$$A_{\text{rms}}(g) = \frac{8.724}{9.80665} \approx 0.890\,g$$

**Mark Split:** Derivation (3 M) + numerical computation steps (4 M).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm → m** before substitution in SI equations — *−1 to −2 marks*.
> 2. **Mixing peak and RMS** in the final answer without clarification — *−1 mark*.
> 3. **Not stating units** in the final answer — *−0.5 mark per missing unit*.
> 4. **Writing $g = 9.8$ m/s²** (approximate) is acceptable; writing $g = 10$ m/s² is *loosely* acceptable only if the question permits; for full credit use **9.80665 m/s²** unless told otherwise.
> 5. **Omitting the direction-of-vibration mention** for 3-axis sensors — *−1 mark* on architecture-type questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Vibration in IoT** = periodic mechanical oscillation transduced into electrical signals for remote monitoring.
- ✅ **Key parameters:** amplitude ($X$, $V$, or $A_{\text{accel}}$), frequency ($f$ in Hz), phase, and damping ratio $\zeta$.
- ✅ **Master identity:** $a = -\omega^2 x$ — instantly convert between displacement, velocity, and acceleration.
- ✅ **Angular frequency conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ (rad/s ↔ Hz).
- ✅ **Natural frequency of a 1-DOF system:** $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$.
- ✅ **Damped natural frequency:** $f_d = f_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$.
- ✅ **RMS of a pure sine:** $x_{\text{rms}} = A/\sqrt{2}$.
- ✅ **3-axis acceleration magnitude:** $\vert \vec{a}\vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$.
- ✅ **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.80665$ m/s² (use this for full marks in KTU 2024).
- ✅ **MEMS vs. Piezo:** MEMS → low-power, DC-capable, wearables; Piezo → high-freq, high-sensitivity, industrial.
- ✅ **IoT vibration pipeline:** Sensor → AFE (amp + LPF + ADC) → MCU/FFT → Wireless (MQTT) → Cloud DB → ML → Dashboard.
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** **ISO 10816 / ISO 20816** prescribe vibration severity in RMS velocity (mm/s).
- ✅ **Crest Factor & Kurtosis** are *impulsiveness* metrics critical for early-stage bearing-defect detection.
- ✅ **Always carry units** through every line of a numerical solution — a frequent mark-loser.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sound and Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sound and Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Temperature Sensors (Thermal Transducers)

### Formal Definition
A **temperature sensor** is a thermo-resistive, thermo-electric, or semiconductor-based transducer that converts a thermal stimulus (heat energy) into a quantifiable electrical signal — voltage, current, or resistance — proportional to the absolute or relative temperature of the measurement medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
