> In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – IoT (PBITT604)**, Module 4 specifically focuses on sensors used as **edge devices** that interface with microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP8266, NodeMCU, Raspberry Pi) through **ADC channels** or digital protocols (**I²C, 1-Wire, SPI**).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a balloon filled with air. When you place it near a stove, the air molecules inside move faster, the balloon expands, and its surface pushes harder against your hand. A **temperature sensor works exactly like your hand** — it "feels" the invisible molecular motion (kinetic energy) and translates that "push" into a measurable electrical quantity. Different sensors (thermistor, RTD, thermocouple) just use different materials to feel this push more accurately.

### Major Categories of Temperature Sensors

| Category | Operating Principle | Typical Examples |
|----------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Thermistors** (NTC/PTC) | Resistance changes with temperature | NTC 10k, MF52 |
| **RTD** (Resistance Temperature Detector) | Metal resistance varies linearly with T | PT100, PT1000 |
| **Thermocouples** | Seebeck Effect (junction EMF) | Type K, Type J |
| **Semiconductor IC Sensors** | Bandgap voltage reference | **LM35, TMP36, DS18B20** |
| **Infrared / Non-contact** | Black-body radiation | MLX90614 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Constants & Standards:**
> - Reference temperature **T₀ = 0 °C = 273.15 K**
> - Standard platinum RTD resistance **R₀ = 100 Ω** at 0 °C
> - LM35 scale factor **10 mV / °C**
> - Seebeck coefficient for Type K ≈ **41 μV / °C**

---

## 1.2 Sound Sensors (Acoustic Transducers)

### Formal Definition
A **sound sensor** (or acoustic sensor) is an electro-acoustic transducer that converts variations in **acoustic pressure waves** propagating through an elastic medium (usually air) into an equivalent **analog voltage signal** or **digital data stream** representing amplitude, frequency, or spectral content.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture dropping a stone into a still pond. The ripples that travel outward are exactly what **sound waves** are — but instead of water moving, it's the air molecules bumping into each other. A **microphone** is like a tiny, ultra-sensitive floating cork on that pond: it bobs up and down with every ripple and, by doing so, converts that mechanical motion into an electrical wave your microcontroller can "read."

### Key Acoustic Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Reference Constants (KTU Board Standard):**
> - Threshold of human hearing: **p₀ = 20 μPa** (at 1 kHz)
> - Reference intensity: **I₀ = 10⁻¹² W / m²**
> - Speed of sound in dry air at 20 °C: **c ≈ 343 m / s**

### Types of Microphones / Sound Sensors

| Type | Working Principle | IoT Examples |
|------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Electret Condenser** | Capacitance change via diaphragm | **KY-038, MAX4466, MAX9814** |
| **MEMS Microphone** | Piezo-resistive / capacitive on silicon | **SPH0645, INMP441 (I²S)** |
| **Dynamic Microphone** | Electromagnetic induction | Standard stage mics |
| **Piezoelectric** | Piezo crystal voltage generation | Contact / vibration mics |
| **Sound Level Meter Module** | Pre-amplified dB output | **SEN0231, Grove Sound Sensor** |

### Why "Sound + Temperature" Are Paired in IoT
In **acoustic-based IoT applications** (e.g., environmental noise monitoring, smart city pollution mapping, industrial machine fault diagnosis, and acoustic localization), the **speed of sound is temperature-dependent**. Accurate distance ranging (sonar) therefore requires **simultaneous temperature compensation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Temperature Sensor Working Principles

### (a) Thermistor (NTC — Negative Temperature Coefficient)
The resistance of an NTC thermistor decreases *exponentially* as temperature rises, following an Arrhenius-type law.

### (b) RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)
A pure metal (typically platinum) whose resistance varies **almost linearly** with temperature, governed by the Callendar–Van Dusen equation.

### (c) Thermocouple — Seebeck Effect
When two dissimilar metals are joined, the temperature difference between the *hot junction* and *cold junction* produces a small DC voltage.

### (d) Semiconductor IC Sensor (LM35)
The LM35 uses the **bandgap reference principle** of silicon. Two transistors operating at different current densities yield a ΔV_BE that is **linear in absolute temperature** (PTAT). On-chip amplifier produces **10 mV per °C** at the output pin.

---

## 2.2 Sound Sensor Working Principle

A condenser microphone consists of a **fixed back-plate** and a **flexible diaphragm**, separated by a small air gap (~20 μm), forming a capacitor. Sound pressure deflects the diaphragm, changing the capacitance and thereby the voltage across it (charge remains constant on a pre-charged capacitor).

$$V = \frac{Q}{C}$$

For a pre-amplified module like the **KY-038**, the analog output is a rectified/envelope-detected waveform that the microcontroller's ADC reads as an instantaneous loudness index.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **direct KTU 2024 board-exam favourites**. Memorize the symbols and units.

### Temperature Sensor Formulas

| # | Formula (Symbolic) | Description / Application |
|---|--------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = 10 \cdot T \;\; \text{[mV]}$ | LM35 linear output (T in °C) |
| 2 | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\,B\!\left(\frac{1}{T}-\frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | NTC thermistor (β-model) |
| 3 | $\frac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C\!\left(\ln R\right)^3$ | **Steinhart–Hart** high-accuracy |
| 4 | $R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T\right)$ | Linear RTD approximation (T in °C) |
| 5 | $V \approx S \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermocouple (Seebeck) |
| 6 | $T_K = T_C + 273.15$ | Celsius → Kelvin conversion |
| 7 | $T_F = T_C \cdot \tfrac{9}{5} + 32$ | Celsius → Fahrenheit |

### Sound Sensor Formulas

| # | Formula (Symbolic) | Description |
|---|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \; \text{dB}$ | **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** |
| 2 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \; \text{dB}$ | **Sound Intensity Level (SIL)** |
| 3 | $c = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T \; \text{m/s}$ | Speed of sound in air (T in °C) |
| 4 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | Speed of sound (ideal gas, T in K) |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength relation |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho c}$ | Acoustic intensity |
| 7 | $L_{total} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Decibel addition rule |

### Boundary Conditions & Reference Values

| Quantity | Reference Value | Used In |
|----------|----------------|---------|
| Reference pressure **p₀** | **20 μPa** | SPL formula |
| Reference intensity **I₀** | **10⁻¹² W/m²** | SIL formula |
| Standard temperature **T₀** | **273.15 K** | Gas laws, thermistor |
| PT100 resistance **R₀** | **100 Ω** at 0 °C | RTD equation |
| LM35 scale factor | **10 mV/°C** | LM35 output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in real-world engineering:**
> - **Acoustic ranging** (ultrasonic HC-SR04, sonar drones, parking sensors): wrong temperature → wrong distance. A 10 °C error shifts the speed of sound by ≈ 6 m/s, which at 5 m range produces **~9 cm error** — unacceptable in robotics.
> - **Cold-chain IoT logistics** (vaccine transport, dairy supply): LM35 + ESP8266 form the cheapest FDA-compliant monitoring node.
> - **Industrial predictive maintenance**: sound sensors detect bearing failures at frequencies 2–10 kHz *before* vibration rises, saving millions in downtime.

---

## 2.4 Operational Logic — Stepwise (for Board Answer)

1. **Physical Stimulus** (heat wave / pressure wave) impinges on the transducer.
2. **Energy Conversion** (thermal → electrical / acoustic → electrical) occurs via the chosen principle (Seebeck, bandgap, piezoelectric, capacitive).
3. **Signal Conditioning** (amplification, linearization via Steinhart-Hart, cold-junction compensation for thermocouples).
4. **ADC Sampling** by the microcontroller (e.g., ESP32 has 12-bit SAR ADC, range 0–3.3 V).
5. **Digital Processing & Transmission** over MQTT / CoAP to the cloud.
6. **Actuation / Alert** triggers if threshold breached.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — LM35 Output Voltage from ADC Reading

A student frequently faces: *"ESP32 ADC reads 1.23 V from an LM35. What is the temperature in °C and in K?"*

**Given:**
- ADC reference voltage $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V
- ADC resolution = 12 bits → $N_{max} = 4095$
- Raw ADC count = $N = 1527$

### Step 1 — Reconstruct the analog input voltage

The ADC maps a 0–V_ref range linearly into 0–N_max integer counts. Therefore, the analog voltage corresponding to one count is:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{N_{max}} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \; \text{V per count}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 8.058 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{V} = 0.8058 \; \text{mV / count}$$

The analog input voltage is therefore the count multiplied by the LSB weight:

$$V_{in} = N \cdot V_{LSB} = 1527 \times 0.8058 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V}$$

Evaluating the product:

$$V_{in} = 1.2305 \; \text{V} \approx 1.23 \; \text{V}$$

### Step 2 — Apply LM35 transfer function

The LM35 datasheet specifies a linear scale factor of **10 mV / °C**. Thus, the temperature in Celsius is:

$$T_C = \frac{V_{in}}{10 \; \text{mV/°C}} = \frac{1230.5 \; \text{mV}}{10 \; \text{mV/°C}}$$

Evaluating:

$$T_C = 123.05 \; °\text{C}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Kelvin (KTU favourite follow-up)

Using the standard thermodynamic relation $T_K = T_C + 273.15$:

$$T_K = 123.05 + 273.15 = 396.20 \; \text{K}$$

### Step 4 — Verify against datasheet operating range

The LM35's specified operating range is **−55 °C to +150 °C**. Our computed 123.05 °C is *within* the rated band, so the reading is valid. If it had been 200 °C, the device would have saturated at V_out ≈ 1.5 V.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Sound Pressure Level (SPL) from Pressure Amplitude

**Given:** RMS acoustic pressure $p_{rms} = 0.02$ Pa (a busy street at 5 m).

**Goal:** Compute SPL in decibels.

### Step 1 — Recall the definition of SPL

The Sound Pressure Level is defined as twenty times the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of measured RMS pressure to the reference pressure $p_0$:

$$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \; \text{dB}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the reference pressure

By international standard (IEC 61672), the reference is $p_0 = 20 \; \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa. Inserting the values:

$$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.02}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the ratio inside the logarithm

$$\frac{0.02}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.02}{0.00002} = 1000$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm

$$\log_{10}(1000) = 3$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by 20

$$L_p = 20 \times 3 = 60 \; \text{dB}$$

This corresponds to a typical "normal conversation at 1 m" — physically reasonable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Speed-of-Sound Temperature Correction in Ultrasonic Ranging

**Given:** HC-SR04 reports an echo time of $t = 5.86$ ms. Ambient temperature $T = 40$ °C.

**Goal:** Correct the measured distance for temperature drift.

### Step 1 — Speed of sound at the operating temperature

The empirical formula valid for dry air between −20 °C and +40 °C is:

$$c = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T \; \text{m/s}$$

Substituting $T = 40$:

$$c = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 40 = 331.3 + 24.24 = 355.54 \; \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Round-trip distance

The ultrasonic pulse travels to the target and back, so the one-way distance is:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$d = \frac{355.54 \times 5.86 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Numerator evaluation

$$355.54 \times 5.86 \times 10^{-3} = 2.0834 \; \text{m}$$

### Step 4 — Divide by 2

$$d = \frac{2.0834}{2} = 1.0417 \; \text{m}$$

### Step 5 — Compare with naive calculation using c = 343 m/s

Without correction:

$$d_{naive} = \frac{343 \times 5.86 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.005 \; \text{m}$$

### Step 6 — Quantify the error

$$\Delta d = 1.0417 - 1.005 = 0.0367 \; \text{m} \approx 3.7 \; \text{cm}$$

This is a **3.7 % over-estimation** — enough to crash a wall-following robot.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Steinhart–Hart Coefficients for NTC Thermistor

**Given:** A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor has resistance:
- $R_1 = 27{,}000$ Ω at $T_1 = 0$ °C = 273.15 K
- $R_2 = 10{,}000$ Ω at $T_2 = 25$ °C = 298.15 K
- $R_3 = 4{,}170$ Ω at $T_3 = 50$ °C = 323.15 K

**Goal:** Find coefficients **A, B, C** in:

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln R + C (\ln R)^3$$

### Step 1 — Logarithmic resistance values

$\ln R_1 = \ln(27000) = 10.203$

$\ln R_2 = \ln(10000) = 9.210$

$\ln R_3 = \ln(4170) = 8.336$

### Step 2 — Inverse temperatures

$\frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{273.15} = 3.661 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹

$\frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹

$\frac{1}{T_3} = \frac{1}{323.15} = 3.095 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹

### Step 3 — Set up the 3×3 linear system

$$
\begin{aligned}
3.661 \times 10^{-3} &= A + B(10.203) + C(10.203)^3 \\
3.354 \times 10^{-3} &= A + B(9.210)  + C(9.210)^3 \\
3.095 \times 10^{-3} &= A + B(8.336)  + C(8.336)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve numerically (Gaussian elimination, board accepts standard form)

Using matrix inversion yields typical values for a 10 kΩ NTC with β ≈ 3950:

$$A \approx 1.13 \times 10^{-3}, \quad B \approx 2.38 \times 10^{-4}, \quad C \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-7}$$

These constants can then be stored in the firmware and called at runtime to convert raw ADC → thermistor resistance → temperature in Kelvin.

> [!TIP]
> KTU board usually accepts the **β-model** form $R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ as an alternative — simpler, 1-coefficient, ~1 °C accuracy over a 50 °C span.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Temperature Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Heat Source] --> B[Temperature Sensor e.g. LM35 or PT100]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Linearizer]
    C --> D[Microcontroller ADC Channel]
    D --> E[Edge Processing MCU like ESP32]
    E --> F[Wireless Transceiver WiFi or LoRa]
    F --> G[Cloud MQTT Broker]
    G --> H[Dashboard Visualization]
    H --> I[Threshold Based Actuator Trigger]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sound Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Acoustic Pressure Wave] --> M1[Microphone Capsule Electret or MEMS]
    M1 --> P2[Pre Amplifier Stage MAX4466]
    P2 --> P3[Envelope Detector or True RMS Chip]
    P3 --> P4[Anti Aliasing Filter LPF at 8 kHz]
    P4 --> A1[MCU ADC Sampling]
    A1 --> S1[FFT Spectral Analysis]
    S1 --> D1[Feature Extraction SPL and Peak Frequency]
    D1 --> N1[Edge AI Inference Anomaly Detection]
    N1 --> T1[MQTT Publish to Cloud]
```

## 4.3 Combined IoT Acoustic-Thermal Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sensorLayer [Sensor Subsystem]
        d1[DS18B20 Temperature 1 Wire]
        d2[MEMS Microphone INMP441 I2S]
    end
    subgraph mcuLayer [Edge MCU Subsystem]
        c1[ESP32 Dual Core Processor]
        c2[Firmware Task Scheduler]
    end
    subgraph networkLayer [Communication Subsystem]
        n1[WiFi 802.11 b g n]
        n2[MQTT TLS 1.2 Publisher]
    end
    subgraph cloudLayer [Cloud Subsystem]
        k1[AWS IoT Core or HiveMQ Broker]
        k2[Time Series Database InfluxDB]
        k3[Grafana Dashboard]
    end
    d1 --> c1
    d2 --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> n1
    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> k1
    k1 --> k2
    k2 --> k3
```

## 4.4 Hardware Interfacing Schematic (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[3.3 V Rail] --> LM35V[LM35 Pin 1 Vcc]
    LM35V --> LM35C[LM35 Pin 2 Vout]
    LM35C --> ESPADC[ESP32 GPIO34 ADC1 CH6]
    ESPADC --> ESP[ESP32 Microcontroller]
    GND[Ground 0 V] --> LM35G[LM35 Pin 3 GND]
    VCC --> MICV[MEMS Mic VDD]
    MICV --> MIC[MEMS Microphone]
    MIC --> MICSD[SD Pin to ESP32 GPIO]
    MIC --> MICWS[WS Pin to ESP32 GPIO]
    MIC --> MICSCK[SCK Pin to ESP32 GPIO]
    ESP --> WIFI[WiFi Transmission]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between NTC thermistor and PTC thermistor with one IoT application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* exponentially as temperature rises. Mathematical relation: $R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$. Used in **inrush current limiters** and **3-D printer hot-end monitoring**.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* with temperature, often showing a sharp rise near the Curie point. Used in **self-resetting fuses** and **over-temperature protection circuits**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL). What is the reference pressure used and why?**

**Model Answer:**
The Sound Pressure Level is the logarithmic measure of RMS acoustic pressure relative to a reference:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \; \text{dB}$$

The reference pressure **p₀ = 20 μPa** is chosen because it approximates the *threshold of human hearing* at 1 kHz — the faintest sound a healthy young ear can detect. Using a logarithmic scale compresses the enormous hearing range (20 μPa to 200 Pa, a 10-million-fold ratio) into a manageable 0–140 dB scale.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

### ▶ Question A (Choice 1) — Total 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of an NTC thermistor with the β-equation. A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor has β = 3950 and R₀ = 10 kΩ at 25 °C. Calculate its resistance at 80 °C.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
An NTC thermistor is a semiconductor ceramic (typically a sintered mixture of metal oxides such as Mn, Co, Ni). As temperature rises, the number of charge carriers available for conduction increases due to thermal excitation across the band gap, causing resistance to drop. The conduction mechanism follows an Arrhenius-type thermal activation law.

**β-equation (2 marks):**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\,B\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where B (β) is the material constant in Kelvin, T and T₀ are absolute temperatures in K.

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
Given:
- R₀ = 10,000 Ω at T₀ = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 K
- T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15 K
- B = 3950 K

Compute exponent:

$$B\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)$$

$$= 3950 \cdot (2.832 \times 10^{-3} - 3.354 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= 3950 \cdot (-5.22 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.062$$

Therefore:

$$R_T = 10000 \cdot e^{-2.062} = 10000 \times 0.1272 \approx 1272 \; \Omega$$

**Answer:** R_T ≈ **1.27 kΩ** at 80 °C.

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain how an LM35 temperature sensor is interfaced with an ESP32 microcontroller. State any two precautions to be taken while interfacing.

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Heat Source] → [LM35] → [Output Pin to ESP32 GPIO34 ADC] → [ESP32 MCU] → [WiFi] → [Cloud]
```

- The LM35 output (10 mV/°C) is fed directly to the ESP32's ADC pin (e.g., GPIO34, ADC1_CH6).
- ESP32's 12-bit SAR ADC converts analog voltage into a digital count.
- The firmware computes $T_C = V_{in}/10 \text{ mV}$ and transmits over MQTT.
- The 3.3 V ESP32 ADC accepts 0–3.3 V → LM35 directly supports 4–30 V supply, but its output never exceeds ~1.5 V in normal use (at 150 °C), so no level shifter is needed.

**Two Precautions (4 marks — 2 each):**

1. **Decoupling Capacitor:** Place a 0.1 μF ceramic capacitor close to the LM35 supply pins to suppress switching noise from the ESP32 WiFi module, which otherwise couples into the analog output.
2. **Avoid Self-Heating:** The LM35 draws ~60 μA. At 5 V supply this dissipates ~0.3 mW, raising the die temperature by < 0.1 °C — generally negligible. However, if used in still air without thermal coupling to the medium, readings drift. Always mount with thermal paste or in airflow.
3. (Bonus) **Use ADC1 not ADC2** on ESP32: ADC2 is unusable when WiFi is active due to internal multiplexer conflict.

---

### ▶ Question B (Choice 2) — Total 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] With necessary equations, explain the working of a condenser microphone as a sound sensor. The sound pressure level of a machine is 87 dB. Find the RMS sound pressure and intensity.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A condenser (capacitor) microphone has two plates — a *fixed back-plate* and a *flexible diaphragm* — separated by ~20 μm air gap forming a capacitor of value C = ε₀A/d. A permanently charged electret layer provides the bias. Incident sound waves deflect the diaphragm, varying d and hence C. Since charge Q is fixed, V = Q/C varies inversely with capacitance, producing an output voltage proportional to sound pressure.

**Equation (2 marks):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{Q \cdot d}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

For the analog output of a sound-level meter:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given L_p = 87 dB, p₀ = 20 μPa:

$$87 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$\frac{87}{20} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$4.35 = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$\frac{p_{rms}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{4.35} = 22387$$

$$p_{rms} = 22387 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 0.448 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Intensity calculation:**

$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

Since acoustic intensity and pressure are related, and for plane/free progressive waves $L_p = L_I$ in dB:

$$I = I_0 \times 10^{L_p/10} = 10^{-12} \times 10^{8.7} = 10^{-3.3} = 5.01 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Answer:** p_rms ≈ **0.448 Pa**, I ≈ **5.01 × 10⁻⁴ W/m²**.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe how the speed of sound depends on temperature. Why is temperature compensation critical in ultrasonic IoT distance measurement?

**Model Solution:**

**Temperature Dependence (4 marks):**

The speed of sound in an ideal gas is derived from Newton's law applied to adiabatic compressions:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}$$

where γ = C_p/C_v (ratio of specific heats, 1.4 for air), R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = absolute temperature in K, M = molar mass of the gas (0.029 kg/mol for air). Since $c \propto \sqrt{T}$, an empirical linear approximation valid for the range −20 °C to +40 °C is:

$$c \approx 331.3 + 0.606 \, T \; \text{m/s}$$

with T in °C. This yields:
- c ≈ 331.3 m/s at 0 °C
- c ≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C (standard reference)
- c ≈ 355.5 m/s at 40 °C

**Critical Need for Compensation (3 marks):**

In ultrasonic ranging (HC-SR04, industrial level sensors, autonomous vehicles), distance is computed from:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

A 20 °C temperature swing causes a ≈ 6 m/s change in c. At a 4 m range with t ≈ 23 ms, this translates to an uncorrected error of **~7 cm** — enough to crash a drone or misread a tank level. Therefore every professional ultrasonic IoT node co-locates a temperature sensor (DS18B20 / LM35) and applies the compensation formula in firmware before publishing the distance reading. Without it, the IoT system violates the **accuracy** requirement of any ISO 9001 process.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in 'Sound and Temperature' questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in LM35:** Writing "V_out = 10 × T" without specifying T in °C and V_out in mV → -1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting absolute temperature** in the thermistor β-equation (using 25 instead of 298.15) → -2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong SPL formula** (using 10·log instead of 20·log for pressure) → -2 marks.
> 4. **No mention of reference pressure p₀ = 20 μPa** in SPL derivations → -1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the temperature compensation argument** in ultrasonic distance problems → -2 marks.
> 6. **Using ADC2 of ESP32 with WiFi active** in interfacing diagrams → -1 mark (ADC2 conflict).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **LM35** → 10 mV/°C linear, range −55 °C to +150 °C, output ≤ 1.5 V in normal operation.
- **NTC** → R decreases with T; uses Arrhenius β-model.
- **RTD (PT100)** → R increases linearly with T; R₀ = 100 Ω at 0 °C.
- **Thermocouple** → Seebeck effect produces ΔV; needs cold-junction compensation.
- **Steinhart–Hart equation** → 3-coefficient, high-accuracy NTC model.
- **Speed of sound in air** → $c = 331.3 + 0.606\,T$ m/s; varies ≈ 0.6 m/s per °C.
- **SPL formula** → $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ dB; $p_0 = 20$ μPa.
- **SIL formula** → $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ dB; $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **Condenser microphone** → V = Q/C; diaphragm displacement → capacitance change → voltage output.
- **MEMS microphones** → compact, I²S digital output (INMP441), immune to analog noise.
- **Temperature compensation is mandatory** in all acoustic ranging IoT applications.
- **ESP32 interfacing**: use ADC1 (GPIO32–39), avoid ADC2 when WiFi is active.
- **DS18B20**: 1-Wire digital temperature sensor, ±0.5 °C accuracy, parasite-power capable.
- **Remember to convert °C → K** (add 273.15) before plugging into Arrhenius-type formulas.
- **Reference values are standardized by IEC 61672** for sound and **ITS-90** for temperature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading RFID Tags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reading RFID Tags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📡 Reading RFID Tags — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading an RFID Tag** is the orchestrated process by which an **RFID Reader (Interrogator)** electromagnetically energises, interrogates, and decodes the unique identification (UID), Electronic Product Code (EPC), or sensor data stored in the memory of an **RFID Tag (Transponder)**. The process relies on **RF coupling** (inductive or radiative) and **backscatter modulation** to establish a wireless data link compliant with standards such as **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO/IEC 18000-63)**, **ISO 14443 (NFC)**, and **ISO 15693 (Vicinity)**.

**Core operational parameters in KTU syllabus terminology:**

- **Operating Frequency Bands (regulated by ITU-R & national authorities):**
  - **LF (Low Frequency): 125 – 134 kHz**
  - **HF (High Frequency): 13.56 MHz**
  - **UHF (Ultra High Frequency): 860 – 960 MHz** (India: **865 – 867 MHz** as per WPC/ETSI EN 302 208)
  - **Microwave: 2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz**
- **Coupling Mechanism:** *Inductive (Near Field)* for LF/HF, *Radiative/Backscatter (Far Field)* for UHF/Microwave.
- **Read Range Envelope:** **10 cm – 15 m** (passive tags) and **up to 100 m** (active tags).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🛒

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Whistling Postman" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a sleepy village where every house has a unique postman who is **deaf-mute until the central bell rings**. The moment the bell (the *Reader's RF signal*) rings with sufficient loudness, the postman wakes up, gets his strength from the bell's sound energy (the *passive tag harvests RF power*), writes his house number on a piece of paper (the *tag's memory*), folds it into a paper plane, and throws it back at the bell's source (*backscatter modulation*). The bell tower then reads the paper plane (*demodulation*). This is exactly how an RFID reader "reads" a tag — the tag has no internal battery; it is entirely powered by the reader's interrogating wave.

---

## 1.3 Tag Taxonomy (Critical for KTU Viva)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tag Classification by Power Source:**
>
> | Tag Type | Power Source | Read Range | Cost (INR approx.) | Typical Use |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Passive** | Harvested RF Energy | **≤ 10 m** | **₹ 5 – 40** | Barcode replacement, retail |
> | **Semi-Passive (SPS)** | On-board battery (chip only) | **≤ 50 m** | **₹ 150 – 600** | Cold-chain, sensor tags |
> | **Active** | On-board battery (TX + chip) | **≤ 100 m** | **₹ 500 – 5000** | Container tracking, tolling |

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of a Successful "Read"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A tag is considered **"read"** only when three conditions are satisfied:
> 1. **Energy Threshold:** $P_{received} \geq P_{threshold,tag}$ (tag chip powers up).
> 2. **Modulation Compliance:** The tag's backscattered signal is at a recognised modulation depth (ASK/PSK) and bit-rate.
> 3. **Collision-Free Slot:** The tag occupies an unambiguous timeslot in the reader's anti-collision inventory cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Stage Read Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> A successful RFID read progresses through **six deterministic stages**. Each stage has a measurable physical quantity and a failure mode.

**Stage 1 — Continuous Wave (CW) Emission**
The reader broadcasts an unmodulated carrier $f_c$ through its antenna at EIRP $P_{EIRP}$ (typically regulated at **+33 dBm (2 W)** in India for UHF).

**Stage 2 — Energy Harvesting at the Tag**
A tiny portion of the incident field is intercepted by the tag's antenna (effective aperture $A_e$). The tag's rectifier (a Schottky-diode bridge) converts the AC RF into DC to charge a capacitor; voltage must reach $V_{dd} \geq 1.0$ V (typical).

**Stage 3 — Command Decoding (Reader → Tag)**
The reader momentarily amplitude-shifts the CW to encode a **Select / Query / Ack / Req_RN** command (PIE / Miller coding in EPC Gen2).

**Stage 4 — Backscatter Modulation (Tag → Reader)**
The tag's IC toggles its input impedance between two states ($Z_1$ and $Z_2$), creating a differential radar cross-section $\Delta\sigma$. This modulates the amplitude/phase of the reflected wave.

**Stage 5 — Demodulation at the Reader**
The reader's circulator separates the transmitted and received signals. The weak backscatter is amplified and decoded.

**Stage 6 — Inventory Update (Anti-Collision Resolution)**
If multiple tags respond, the reader runs the **Q-Algorithm** (slotted ALOHA variant) to singulate them one-by-one.

---

## 2.2 The Two Coupling Regimes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Near-Field (Inductive) Coupling** — used in LF and HF (13.56 MHz) systems.
> Dominates when the inter-tag distance $d \ll \lambda / (2\pi)$ (i.e., $d < 0.16\lambda$).
> Power transfer follows the **transformer analogy**: reader antenna = primary coil, tag antenna = secondary coil.
> Typical range: **0 – 50 cm**.
>
> **Far-Field (Radiative / Backscatter) Coupling** — used in UHF and microwave systems.
> Dominates when $d > \lambda / (2\pi)$. Power transfer follows **Friis free-space propagation** plus **Radar Cross-Section (RCS)** principles.
> Typical range: **1 m – 15 m** (passive), **> 100 m** (active).

---

## 2.3 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **examiner's cheat-sheet** for RFID read-range derivations. Memorise the variables, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Variable Glossary | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Free-Space Wavelength** | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c = 3\times10^8$ m/s, $f$ in Hz | Maps frequency band to physical antenna size |
| 2 | **Friis Path Loss (Linear)** | $PL = \left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right)^2$ | $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength | Calculates isotropic loss in dB |
| 3 | **Friis Path Loss (dB form)** | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | Quickest hand-calculation tool |
| 4 | **Received Power at Tag** | $P_{r,tag} = P_t G_t G_r \frac{\lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$ | $P_t$ = TX power, $G_t,G_r$ = gains | Energy harvesting threshold check |
| 5 | **Radar Range Equation (RFID)** | $R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \sigma}{P_{th}}}$ | $\sigma$ = tag RCS, $P_{th}$ = chip sensitivity | Maximum read distance estimation |
| 6 | **Tag RCS Variation** | $\sigma = \frac{\lambda^2 R_a^2}{4\pi \cdot \vert Z_{ant} + Z_{chip} \vert^2} \cdot \vert \Gamma_1 - \Gamma_2 \vert^2$ | $\Gamma$ = reflection coefficients | Backscatter strength design |
| 7 | **Inductive Coupling Voltage** | $V_2 = j\omega M I_1$ | $M$ = mutual inductance, $I_1$ = primary current | HF/NFC tag wake-up voltage |
| 8 | **Mutual Inductance** | $M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | $k$ = coupling coefficient (0 to 1) | Coil alignment optimisation |
| 9 | **Slotted ALOHA Throughput** | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $G$ = offered load, $S$ = throughput | Anti-collision capacity (max = **0.368** at $G=1$) |
| 10 | **EPC Gen2 Q-Algorithm** | $Q_{fp} = \text{round}(Q_{fp} \times C)$ | $C \in (0.1, 0.5)$ adjustment constant | Dynamic slot adaptation |
| 11 | **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP)** | $EIRP_{dBm} = P_t + G_t - L_c$ | $L_c$ = cable loss | Regulatory compliance (India = **36 dBm**) |
| 12 | **Read Range (Practical)** | $R = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\sqrt{\frac{P_{EIRP} \cdot G_{tag} \cdot \tau}{P_{th}}}$ | $\tau$ = power transmission coefficient | Final system-level range |

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a CW is sent before the command?** The tag needs an unmodulated carrier to keep its energy reservoir (capacitor) charged while it is idle — modulation would cause power droops and reset the chip.
- **Why backscatter and not active transmission in passive tags?** Active transmission would require the tag to generate its own carrier, demanding a battery. By *modulating the existing wave*, the passive tag needs only micro-watts of harvested energy.
- **Why modulate input impedance?** Changing the impedance mismatches the antenna, which in turn changes the **radar cross-section (RCS)**, which changes the **amplitude of the reflected wave** — a beautiful trick of physics that needs zero extra power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Field Application:** This passive backscatter principle is the *direct technological ancestor* of **Ambient IoT / Passive IoT (3GPP Release 19)**, **Wi-Fi backscatter (USC 2016)**, and **LoRa backscatter (2017)** — all active areas of KTU final-year project research.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RFID Read-Range Equation (EPC Gen2 UHF)

> [!NOTE]
> The **canonical read-range equation** for a passive UHF tag is derived by chaining the Friis transmission equation (forward link) with the radar equation (reverse link). The limit is set by whichever side — the tag's energy harvesting or the reader's receive sensitivity — fails first.

### Step 1: Power Density at Distance *d*

The reader transmits an EIRP $P_t G_t$. At a distance $d$ in free space, the power density illuminating the tag is:

$$S_{tag} = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

*Explanation:* The transmitted power spreads over the surface of an expanding sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. $G_t$ concentrates energy in the reader's antenna boresight direction.

### Step 2: Power Captured by the Tag Antenna

The tag antenna has an effective aperture $A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 G_{tag}}{4\pi}$. The power available at the tag's antenna terminals is:

$$P_{available} = S_{tag} \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_{tag} \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$$

*Explanation:* $A_e$ is the "collecting area" of the tag antenna. Larger apertures (higher $G_{tag}$) capture more energy.

### Step 3: Power Delivered to the Chip (Impedance Matching)

Due to the conjugate-mismatch between tag antenna and chip, only a fraction $\tau$ (the *power transmission coefficient*) is delivered to the IC. With $0 \le \tau \le 1$:

$$P_{chip} = \tau \cdot P_{available} = \frac{\tau \cdot P_t G_t G_{tag} \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$$

*Explanation:* Real antennas are not perfectly matched; reflection losses reduce the energy actually reaching the rectifier.

### Step 4: Energy-Harvesting Threshold Constraint

For the chip to wake up, $P_{chip} \ge P_{th}$ (chip sensitivity, typically **−15 dBm to −22 dBm**). Equating and solving for the maximum range $R_{max}$:

$$\frac{\tau \cdot P_t G_t G_{tag} \lambda^2}{(4\pi R_{max})^2} = P_{th}$$

$$(4\pi R_{max})^2 = \frac{\tau \cdot P_t G_t G_{tag} \lambda^2}{P_{th}}$$

$$4\pi R_{max} = \lambda \sqrt{\frac{\tau \cdot P_t G_t G_{tag}}{P_{th}}}$$

$$\boxed{R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_{EIRP} \cdot G_{tag} \cdot \tau}{P_{th}}}}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **forward-link-limited read range**. The reader's signal strength must be strong enough to wake the tag.

### Step 5: Reverse-Link Limit (Radar Equation)

The tag backscatters with effective RCS $\sigma$. The power returning to the reader is:

$$P_{reader,rx} = \frac{P_{tag,back} G_{reader} \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^3 d^4 \sigma^{-1}}$$

Setting $P_{reader,rx} = P_{reader,sensitivity}$ and solving for $d$:

$$R_{reverse} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt[4]{\frac{P_{EIRP} \cdot G_{tag}^2 \cdot \tau^2 \cdot \sigma}{P_{reader,sens}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Asymmetry:** The forward link obeys a $\frac{1}{d^2}$ law, but the reverse (backscatter) link obeys a $\frac{1}{d^4}$ law. **Therefore, for distant passive tags, the read range is always limited by the reverse link (the reader's ability to "hear" the weak echo), not by the tag's wake-up energy.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Operating Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> A frequently asked KTU 14-mark question is: *"Compare LF, HF, and UHF RFID for a warehouse inventory project."* Use this analytical matrix to construct your answer.

| Parameter | LF (134 kHz) | HF (13.56 MHz) | UHF (865–867 MHz India) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Coupling Type** | Inductive | Inductive | Backscatter (Far-field) |
| **Read Range (Passive)** | **≤ 10 cm** | **≤ 1 m** | **1 – 15 m** |
| **Antenna Size** | Very large (coil) | Small coil | Compact patch/dipole |
| **Anti-Collision** | Poor (often 1 tag only) | Good (ISO 15693) | Excellent (EPC Gen2, 1000+ tags) |
| **Material Penetration** | Excellent (water, meat) | Good | Poor around metal & liquid |
| **Data Rate** | Low (1–4 kbps) | Medium (26 kbps) | High (40–640 kbps) |
| **Standards** | ISO 11784/85 | ISO 14443, 15693 | EPC Gen2 / ISO 18000-63 |
| **Cost per Tag (INR)** | ₹ 30 – 80 | ₹ 15 – 40 | ₹ 5 – 25 |
| **Best Use-Case** | Animal tagging | Smart cards, payments | Supply chain, retail |
| **Regulatory Limit in India (EIRP)** | Not specified | Not specified | **+33 dBm (2 W)** |

---

## 3.3 Anti-Collision Mathematics (Slotted ALOHA)

For a reader trying to read $n$ tags in $N$ slots (where $N = 2^Q$, with $Q \in [0,15]$), the probability that a given tag chooses a unique slot is:

$$P_{success} = N \cdot \left(\frac{1}{N}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{n-1}$$

The expected number of successful reads in one cycle is:

$$S = n \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{n-1}$$

**Throughput efficiency** $\eta$ is:

$$\eta = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{n}{N} \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^{n-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the continuous (un-slotted) ALOHA limit, the maximum throughput is **18.4%**. In **slotted ALOHA**, the maximum doubles to **36.8%**, reached when the offered load $G = 1$. The EPC Gen2 Q-algorithm continuously tunes $N$ to keep $G$ near this optimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — RFID Reading Process

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **end-to-end physics and logic** of reading a single passive UHF tag. It is the most frequently drawn diagram in KTU Module-4 exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reader Microcontroller: Sends Query Command] --> B[RF Front-End: Generates CW at 866 MHz]
    B --> C[Reader Antenna: Radiates EIRP P_t G_t]
    C --> D[Free-Space Channel: Path Loss 1 over d squared]
    D --> E[Tag Antenna: Captures Power via Aperture A_e]
    E --> F[Charge Pump Rectifier: AC to DC Conversion]
    F --> G{Energy Check: P_chip greater than P_threshold?}
    G -- No --> H[Tag Remains Silent / No Read]
    G -- Yes --> I[Tag Microcontroller Wakes Up]
    I --> J[Tag Reads Memory: EPC plus TID plus User]
    J --> K[Tag Toggles Load Impedance Z1 to Z2]
    K --> L[Backscatter Modulation: Modifies RCS sigma]
    L --> M[Reflected Wave Returns to Reader Antenna]
    M --> N[Reader Circulator: Separates TX and RX]
    N --> O[Demodulator: Recovers Baseband]
    O --> P{Anti-Collision Q-Algorithm: Slot Unique?}
    P -- No --> Q[Reader Issues Req_RN to Resolve]
    P -- Yes --> R[Data Sent to Host Application]
    R --> S[Application Layer: Inventory Update in ERP]
```

---

## 4.2 Anti-Collision State Machine (Q-Algorithm)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Query: Reader sends Query with Q value
    Query --> Slot_0: Tag picks slot via RN16
    Slot_0 --> Ack: If slot equals 0
    Slot_0 --> Slot_N: If slot greater than 0
    Slot_N --> Ack: Wait until slot equals 0
    Ack --> Singulated: Reader sends ACK with handle
    Singulated --> Read: Tag transmits EPC plus PC
    Read --> Done: Inventory cycle complete
    Done --> Idle: Reader starts new round
    Query --> Adjust_Q: If no response observed
    Adjust_Q --> Query: Q = Q plus 1 or Q = Q minus 1
```

---

## 4.3 Reader-Antenna Field Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NF[Near-Field Zone: d less than lambda over 2pi]
        NF1[Inductive Coupling]
        NF2[Coil Antenna]
        NF3[Range: 0 to 50 cm]
    end
    subgraph FF[Far-Field Zone: d greater than lambda over 2pi]
        FF1[Radiative Backscatter]
        FF2[Dipole or Patch Antenna]
        FF3[Range: 1 to 15 m passive]
    end
    Reader[RFID Reader] --> NF
    Reader --> FF
    TagA[LF plus HF Tag] -. Inductive .-> NF
    TagB[UHF Tag] -. Backscatter .-> FF
```

---

## 4.4 Coupling & Read-Range Trade-off Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Operating Frequency] --> Q1{Required Read Range?}
    Q1 -- Less than 50 cm --> LF_HF[Select LF or HF: Inductive Coupling]
    Q1 -- Greater than 1 m --> UHF[Select UHF: Backscatter Coupling]
    LF_HF --> LF_Antenna[Design Loop Coil Antenna]
    UHF --> UHF_Antenna[Design Dipole or Patch Antenna]
    LF_Antenna --> Calc1[Apply Transformer Model: V2 = j omega M I1]
    UHF_Antenna --> Calc2[Apply Friis plus Radar Equation]
    Calc1 --> R1[Compute R_max]
    Calc2 --> R2[Compute R_max from forward and reverse link]
    R1 --> Decision1[Tag is Readable if R_max greater than d]
    R2 --> Decision2[Tag is Readable if R_max greater than d]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎓 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** Define *backscatter modulation* in RFID and state why it is preferred over active transmission in passive tags. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Backscatter modulation is the technique in which a passive RFID tag alters the **radar cross-section (RCS)** of its antenna by switching its input impedance between two states ($Z_1$ and $Z_2$), thereby modulating the amplitude (or phase) of the incident reader's continuous wave. It is preferred over active transmission because a passive tag lacks an internal carrier generator; the **reader supplies the carrier**, and the tag only needs to **toggle a transistor switch**, consuming micro-watts of harvested energy. This enables ultra-low-cost, battery-free operation suitable for **barcode replacement** in supply-chain applications.

---

> **Question 2.** List the three operational frequency bands of RFID and give one example application for each. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **LF (125–134 kHz):** Animal identification and pet tagging.
> 2. **HF (13.56 MHz):** Contactless smart cards (e.g., Metro travel cards, NCMC).
> 3. **UHF (860–960 MHz):** Retail inventory management (e.g., apparel tagging).
> **Microwave (2.45 GHz):** Vehicle tolling systems (e.g., FASTag).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **Part (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an RFID reader. Discuss the role of the circulator and the demodulator. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

An RFID reader consists of **five functional blocks**:

1. **Microcontroller / DSP Unit** — orchestrates the EPC Gen2 protocol state machine, issues *Query*, *Ack*, and *Req_RN* commands, and processes received *EPC + CRC16* replies.
2. **RF Transmitter / Synthesiser** — generates the local oscillator carrier at 865–867 MHz (for India) and modulates it with PIE-coded commands.
3. **Power Amplifier (PA)** — boosts the modulated signal to the regulatory EIRP of **+33 dBm (2 W)**.
4. **Circulator (or Directional Coupler)** — a three-port ferrite device that **routes the transmitted signal from the PA to the antenna (port 1 → 2)**, and routes the received backscatter from the antenna to the LNA (port 2 → 3), while isolating the PA from the receiver (port 3 → 1). This isolation (typically **> 20 dB**) prevents the strong transmitted signal from saturating the sensitive receiver.
5. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA) + Demodulator** — amplifies the weak backscatter (often **−80 dBm** or below) and recovers the baseband Miller-modulated subcarrier. The demodulated bitstream is handed to the microcontroller for CRC validation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw the reader as a single "black box" and forget to label the **circulator's three ports**. This alone costs **2 marks**. Always annotate TX, ANT, and RX ports.

---

> **Part (b)** A UHF RFID reader transmits at **+30 dBm** with a 6 dBi antenna. The tag antenna gain is 2 dBi, $\lambda = 0.34$ m, the power transmission coefficient $\tau = 0.5$, and the chip sensitivity is **−15 dBm**. Compute the maximum forward-link read range. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to linear scale.**

$$P_t = 10^{30/10} = 1000 \text{ mW} = 1.0 \text{ W}$$

$$G_t = 10^{6/10} = 3.98$$

$$G_{tag} = 10^{2/10} = 1.585$$

$$P_{th} = 10^{-15/10} = 0.0316 \text{ mW} = 3.16 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$

$$\tau = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Apply the forward-link read-range equation.**

$$R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_{tag} \cdot \tau}{P_{th}}}$$

$$R_{max} = \frac{0.34}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 3.98 \times 1.585 \times 0.5}{3.16 \times 10^{-5}}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator inside the square root.**

$$1.0 \times 3.98 \times 1.585 \times 0.5 = 3.154$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{3.154}{3.16 \times 10^{-5}} = 99{,}810 \approx 9.98 \times 10^4$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root.**

$$\sqrt{9.98 \times 10^4} = 315.9$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by the wavelength pre-factor.**

$$R_{max} = \frac{0.34}{12.566} \times 315.9 = 0.02705 \times 315.9 \approx 8.55 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{R_{max} \approx 8.55 \text{ m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert **dBm to watts** before substitution. Always do the linear conversion first.
> **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the pre-factor $\frac{\lambda}{4\pi}$. Students often write $R = \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_{tag} \tau \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 P_{th}}}$ but then compute $\sqrt{...}$ without multiplying by 1.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Part (a)** Compare the working of **inductive coupling** and **electromagnetic backscatter coupling** in RFID systems. Mention at least four comparative parameters. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Inductive Coupling (LF/HF) | Electromagnetic Backscatter (UHF) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Frequency** | 125 kHz / 13.56 MHz | 860–960 MHz (India: 865–867 MHz) |
| **Field Region** | Near field ($d < \lambda/2\pi$) | Far field ($d > \lambda/2\pi$) |
| **Physical Model** | Transformer (primary–secondary coil) | Radar (illumination & reflection) |
| **Read Range** | ≤ 50 cm | 1–15 m (passive) |
| **Antenna Type** | Wound coil | Dipole or patch |
| **Sensitivity to Metal/Water** | Low (works near metal & liquid) | High (detunes, absorbs) |
| **Anti-Collision** | Limited (1–30 tags) | Excellent (> 1000 tags via Q-algorithm) |
| **Standards** | ISO 14443, ISO 15693 | EPC Gen2 (ISO 18000-63) |
| **Tag Power** | Energy from magnetic flux $\Phi$ | Energy from RF-to-DC rectifier |
| **Data Rate** | 1–26 kbps | 40–640 kbps |

**Key Conceptual Difference:**
- In **inductive coupling**, the *magnetic field* $H$ between the two coils induces a voltage $V_2 = j\omega M I_1$ in the tag's coil, where $M$ is the mutual inductance.
- In **backscatter coupling**, the *electromagnetic wave* propagates, hits the tag antenna, and the tag modulates the *radar cross-section* $\sigma$ by varying its load impedance.

---

> **Part (b)** Explain the **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 anti-collision Q-algorithm**. Show that the maximum throughput of slotted ALOHA is **0.368**. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Q-Algorithm Description:**

The EPC Gen2 protocol uses a **dynamic-slotted ALOHA** scheme. The reader announces a parameter $Q \in [0, 15]$, and tags pick a slot number from a range $[0, 2^Q - 1]$ using a 16-bit random number (RN16).

- If the reader sees **exactly one** tag reply in a slot, it sends *ACK* and singulates that tag.
- If the reader sees **no reply**, it decrements $Q$ (fewer slots, faster cycle).
- If the reader sees **a collision** (multiple tags), it increments $Q$ (more slots, fewer collisions).
- The adjustment uses $Q_{fp} = \text{round}(Q_{fp} \times C)$ with $C \in (0.1, 0.5)$.

**Mathematical Derivation of Maximum Throughput = 0.368:**

Let $G$ = average number of transmission attempts per slot (offered load). The probability that exactly **one** tag transmits in a given slot follows a Poisson distribution:

$$P(\text{success in one slot}) = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

This is the **throughput per slot**, $S(G)$:

$$S(G) = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

Differentiate to find the maximum:

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0$$

Solving: $G = 1$.

Substitute $G = 1$:

$$S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679$$

$$\boxed{S_{max} = 0.368 \text{ at } G = 1}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with an infinite number of slots, slotted ALOHA can never read more than **36.8%** of the population per cycle. This is why EPC Gen2's Q-algorithm continuously *adapts* $Q$ to keep the system near the $G=1$ sweet spot.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **offered load $G$** with **population $n$**. They are equal **only** when $n = 2^Q$ (one tag per slot on average). In practice, $G$ is the average number of *attempts*, and $n$ is the *number of tags* — these are distinct concepts.

---

## 5.3 📝 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> The following bullet points are the **minimum** you must internalise before entering the KTU exam hall.

**📌 Core Concepts:**
- ✅ RFID reading = *Reader energises → Tag harvests → Tag backscatters → Reader decodes*.
- ✅ Passive tags have **no battery**; they harvest all energy from the reader's CW.
- ✅ **Backscatter modulation** is the energy-efficient way to encode data in a reflected wave.
- ✅ LF and HF use **inductive (near-field)** coupling; UHF and microwave use **radiative (far-field)** coupling.
- ✅ India permits UHF RFID operation only in the **865–867 MHz** band at **+33 dBm (2 W) EIRP**.

**📌 Key Equations (Memorise!):**
- ✅ Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f$.
- ✅ Friis path loss: $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$.
- ✅ Read range: $R_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_{tag} \tau}{P_{th}}}$.
- ✅ Slotted ALOHA max throughput: $S_{max} = 1/e = 0.368$ at $G = 1$.

**📌 Block-Level Knowledge:**
- ✅ Reader blocks: **Microcontroller → Synthesiser → PA → Circulator → LNA → Demodulator**.
- ✅ Tag blocks: **Antenna → Rectifier → Voltage Regulator → Modulator → Memory (EPC/TID/User)**.
- ✅ Anti-collision: **Q-algorithm with dynamic slotted ALOHA**.

**📌 Common Numerical Conversions (often tested):**
- ✅ $0 \text{ dBm} = 1 \text{ mW}$.
- ✅ $+30 \text{ dBm} = 1 \text{ W}$.
- ✅ $+33 \text{ dBm} = 2 \text{ W}$ (India's UHF limit).
- ✅ $-15 \text{ dBm} = 31.6 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$ (typical chip sensitivity).
- ✅ $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3 \text{ dB}$; $10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77 \text{ dB}$.

**📌 Standards to Remember:**
- ✅ **EPC Class-1 Gen-2 (ISO 18000-63)** — global UHF standard.
- ✅ **ISO 14443** — NFC (proximity, ≤ 10 cm).
- ✅ **ISO 15693** — Vicinity cards (≤ 1 m).
- ✅ **ISO 11784/85** — LF animal tagging.
- ✅ **3GPP TS 38.305** — Ambient IoT / passive IoT (emerging).

**📌 Examiner's Last-Minute Tips:**
- ✅ Always draw the **circulator with 3 labelled ports** in reader architecture questions.
- ✅ Always state the **boundary conditions** (e.g., free-space, line-of-sight, far-field) before applying the Friis equation.
- ✅ Always state **units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ When comparing technologies, build a **table** — it scores better than prose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Getting Location from a GPS
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > **GPS (Global Positioning System)** is a satellite-based navigation system consisting of a constellation of **at least 24 satellites** orbiting the Earth, transmitting precise microwave signals that enable a GPS receiver to determine its **location (latitude, longitude, altitude)**, **velocity**, and **time synchronization** anywhere on or near the Earth's surface. In the **IoT context**, "Getting Location from a GPS" refers to the process by which an IoT node (sensor/edge device) equipped with a **GPS receiver module** captures positioning data from satellite signals, decodes the navigation message (typically in the **NMEA 0183** standard format), and uses it for **geotagging, asset tracking, fleet management, precision agriculture, and location-aware automation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBITT604 – Module 4):** > Students must understand *how* a GPS receiver acquires satellite data, the **trilateration principle**, **NMEA sentence parsing**, and how this data is integrated into an IoT application (e.g., ESP32 + NEO-6M module). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **lost in a city** and you call three friends who each tell you: - *Friend A*: "You are exactly **5 km** from my house." - *Friend B*: "You are exactly **3 km** from my house." - *Friend C*: "You are exactly **4 km** from my house." If you draw three circles with those radii centered on each friend's house, **the single point where all three circles intersect is your exact location**. **GPS works exactly like this**, except the "friends" are satellites in space, and the "distance" is calculated by measuring **how long a radio signal takes to travel** from the satellite to your receiver. > **Speed of Light in Vacuum:** > $$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$ > This is the physical constant that makes GPS time-based ranging possible. --- ## 1.3 Why GPS is Critical in IoT | IoT Application | Role of GPS Location Data | |---|---| | **Smart Agriculture** | Tractor auto-steering, livestock tracking | | **Vehicle Fleet Management** | Real-time route optimization | | **Smart Logistics** | Package traceability, cold-chain monitoring | | **Wearable Health Devices** | Emergency location for elderly/patients | | **Wildlife Conservation** | Animal migration tracking collars | | **Disaster Management** | Geofencing, rescue beacon localization | > [!NOTE] > In KTU examinations, frequently tested sub-topics include the **3-segment architecture of GPS**, **trilateration math**, **NMEA sentence format** (especially `$GPGGA`), and **error sources** in GPS readings. --- ## 1.4 Key Metrics to Remember - **Minimum Satellites Required for 2D Fix:** **4** (3 for position + 1 for clock correction) - **Minimum Satellites for 3D Fix (with altitude):** **4** (3 for x,y,z + 1 for time bias) - **Standard GPS Accuracy:** **±5 to 10 meters** (civilian L1 signal) - **NMEA Sentence Refresh Rate:** Typically **1 Hz** (once per second) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Segments of GPS Architecture The GPS system is operationally divided into three major segments: ### 2.1.1 Space Segment - Constellation of **31 active satellites** (as of 2024) orbiting at an altitude of **~20,200 km**. - Each satellite completes one orbit in **~12 hours**. - Satellites are arranged in **6 orbital planes**, each inclined at **55°** to the equator, with **4 satellites per plane**. - Each satellite broadcasts on **two carrier frequencies**: - **L1 (1575.42 MHz)** – civilian use - **L2 (1227.60 MHz)** – military use (encrypted) ### 2.1.2 Control Segment - Consists of a **Master Control Station (MCS)** in Colorado Springs, USA, plus **monitoring stations** worldwide. - Responsibilities: track satellite orbits, maintain atomic clocks on satellites, and upload navigation data. ### 2.1.3 User Segment - The **GPS receiver** (e.g., NEO-6M, u-blox) in your IoT device. - Receives satellite signals and computes position using **trilateration**. --- ## 2.2 Operational Steps — How a GPS Module Obtains Location 1. **Signal Acquisition:** The receiver's antenna picks up weak L1 signals (~**-130 dBm**) from multiple satellites. 2. **Pseudorange Calculation:** The receiver measures the **time delay** between signal transmission and reception. Multiplying by `c` gives the **apparent range** (pseudorange). 3. **Decoding Navigation Message:** The receiver extracts: - Satellite **ephemeris data** (precise orbital position) - **Almanac data** (coarse orbit info for all satellites) - **Time of transmission** 4. **Trilateration Algorithm:** Using pseudoranges from at least 4 satellites, the receiver solves a system of equations to compute `(x, y, z, t_bias)`. 5. **Coordinate Conversion:** The Cartesian ECEF coordinates are converted to **latitude (φ), longitude (λ), altitude (h)**. 6. **NMEA Output:** The data is streamed as ASCII text sentences over **UART (4800–9600 baud)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind 4 satellites:** A GPS receiver's clock is a cheap quartz crystal (not an atomic clock like on satellites). This introduces a **clock bias error** `t_b`. To resolve `(x, y, z, t_b)`, we need **4 independent equations**, hence **4 satellites minimum**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---| | $$d = c \cdot \Delta t$$ | Distance = Speed of light × Time delay | meters | | $$\rho = c \cdot (\Delta t + \delta t)$$ | Pseudorange (with clock bias) | meters | | $$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot t_b$$ | Trilateration equation for satellite `i` | meters | | $$\phi = \arctan\left(\frac{z + e'^2 b \sin^3(\mu)}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} - e^2 a \cos^3(\mu)}\right)$$ | Latitude from ECEF (Bowring's method, approximate) | radians | | $$\lambda = \arctan_2(y, x)$$ | Longitude from ECEF | radians | | $$h = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}}{\cos(\phi)} - N$$ | Geodetic height | meters | | $$\text{HDOP} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} / \sigma_{range}$$ | Horizontal Dilution of Precision (lower = better) | dimensionless | | $$\text{Accuracy} \approx \text{HDOP} \times \text{UERE}$$ | UERE = User Equivalent Range Error (~3–5 m) | meters | | $$a = 6378137.0 \text{ m}, \quad b = 6356752.3142 \text{ m}$$ | WGS-84 ellipsoid semi-major & semi-minor axes | meters | | $$e^2 = 1 - \frac{b^2}{a^2} \approx 0.00669438$$ | First eccentricity squared of WGS-84 | dimensionless | > **Caution on LaTeX Pipes:** The absolute-value and determinant symbols in the formulas above are written using `\vert` and `\arctan_2` to ensure they do not break the markdown table layout. --- ## 2.4 NMEA 0183 Sentence Format The most important sentence in KTU exams is **`$GPGGA`** (Global Positioning System Fix Data). **Example:** ``` $GPGGA,123519,4807.038,N,01131.000,E,1,08,0.9,545.4,M,46.9,M,,*47 ``` **Field-by-Field Decoding:** | Field Index | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 0 | `$GPGGA` | Sentence identifier | | 1 | 123519 | UTC time: 12:35:19 | | 2 | 4807.038 | Latitude: 48° 07.038' | | 3 | N | North (latitude direction) | | 4 | 01131.000 | Longitude: 11° 31.000' | | 5 | E | East (longitude direction) | | 6 | 1 | Fix quality: 0=invalid, 1=GPS, 2=DGPS | | 7 | 08 | Number of satellites in use | | 8 | 0.9 | HDOP | | 9 | 545.4 | Altitude above mean sea level (meters) | | 10 | M | Meters (altitude unit) | | 11 | 46.9 | Height of geoid above WGS84 ellipsoid | | 12 | (empty) | Time since last DGPS update | | 13 | (empty) | DGPS station ID | | 14 | *47 | Checksum | > **Conversion to Decimal Degrees:** > $$\text{DD} = \text{DMM} + \frac{\text{MM}}{60}$$ > where `DMM` is the raw value and `MM` is the minutes portion. Multiply by `-1` for `S` or `W`. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Telematics** | Vehicle insurance (Usage-Based Insurance models) | | **Civil Engineering** | Surveying using RTK-GPS (cm-level accuracy) | | **Aviation / Drones** | Autopilot navigation, geofencing | | **Maritime** | Fishing vessel monitoring, anti-piracy systems | | **IoT Edge Computing** | Triggering location-aware actuators (e.g., turn on lights at home) | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Pseudorange Equation The fundamental observable in GPS is the **pseudorange** `ρ`, which is the apparent distance between a satellite and the receiver, contaminated by clock errors. ### Step 1 — Ideal Range from Travel Time In an ideal world, if the satellite transmits a signal at time `t_s` and the receiver captures it at time `t_r`, the true geometric range is: $$R = c \cdot (t_r - t_s)$$ ### Step 2 — Introducing Receiver Clock Bias The receiver's clock has an error `δt_r` (it does not know exact GPS time). So the measured time is: $$t_r^{measured} = t_r + \delta t_r$$ The satellite clock has a much smaller error `δt_s` (corrected by the control segment, but residual error remains): $$t_s^{measured} = t_s + \delta t_s$$ ### Step 3 — Measured Travel Time The receiver's measured time of flight is: $$\Delta t_{measured} = (t_r + \delta t_r) - (t_s + \delta t_s) = (t_r - t_s) + (\delta t_r - \delta t_s)$$ ### Step 4 — Definition of Pseudorange Multiplying both sides by `c`: $$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t_{measured} = c(t_r - t_s) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_s)$$ Since `c · (t_r - t_s) = R` (true geometric range), and `δt_s` is small (broadcast in navigation message), we simplify to: $$\rho = R + c \cdot \delta t_r$$ This is the **pseudorange equation** — it's "pseudo" because it's contaminated by the receiver's clock bias. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Trilateration (3D Position Fix) Let: - `(x_r, y_r, z_r)` = unknown receiver position in **ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed)** coordinates - `(x_i, y_i, z_i)` = known satellite `i` position (from ephemeris) - `t_b = δt_r` = unknown receiver clock bias - `R_i` = true geometric range to satellite `i` - `ρ_i` = measured pseudorange to satellite `i` ### Step 1 — Geometric Range The true Euclidean distance from receiver to satellite `i` is: $$R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2}$$ ### Step 2 — Pseudorange Relation $$\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot t_b$$ $$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot t_b$$ ### Step 3 — System of Equations for 4 Satellites For satellites `i = 1, 2, 3, 4`: $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_1 &= \sqrt{(x_1 - x_r)^2 + (y_1 - y_r)^2 + (z_1 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot t_b \\ \rho_2 &= \sqrt{(x_2 - x_r)^2 + (y_2 - y_r)^2 + (z_2 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot t_b \\ \rho_3 &= \sqrt{(x_3 - x_r)^2 + (y_3 - y_r)^2 + (z_3 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot t_b \\ \rho_4 &= \sqrt{(x_4 - x_r)^2 + (y_4 - y_r)^2 + (z_4 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot t_b \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Linearization (Newton-Raphson / Bancroft's Algorithm) These are **non-linear** in `(x_r, y_r, z_r)`. We linearize around an initial guess `(x_0, y_0, z_0, t_{b,0})`. Let: $$\Delta x = x_r - x_0, \quad \Delta y = y_r - y_0, \quad \Delta z = z_r - z_0, \quad \Delta t_b = t_b - t_{b,0}$$ For satellite `i`, the partial derivatives of `R_i` with respect to the unknowns are: $$\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x_r} = \frac{-(x_i - x_0)}{R_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y_r} = \frac{-(y_i - y_0)}{R_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z_r} = \frac{-(z_i - z_0)}{R_{i,0}}$$ The linearized system becomes: $$\Delta \rho_i = -\frac{(x_i - x_0)}{R_{i,0}} \Delta x - \frac{(y_i - y_0)}{R_{i,0}} \Delta y - \frac{(z_i - z_0)}{R_{i,0}} \Delta z + c \cdot \Delta t_b$$ In matrix form, with **H** = geometry matrix: $$ \mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{-(x_1 - x_0)}{R_{1,0}} & \frac{-(y_1 - y_0)}{R_{1,0}} & \frac{-(z_1 - z_0)}{R_{1,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{-(x_2 - x_0)}{R_{2,0}} & \frac{-(y_2 - y_0)}{R_{2,0}} & \frac{-(z_2 - z_0)}{R_{2,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{-(x_3 - x_0)}{R_{3,0}} & \frac{-(y_3 - y_0)}{R_{3,0}} & \frac{-(z_3 - z_0)}{R_{3,0}} & 1 \\ \frac{-(x_4 - x_0)}{R_{4,0}} & \frac{-(y_4 - y_0)}{R_{4,0}} & \frac{-(z_4 - z_0)}{R_{4,0}} & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 5 — Least-Squares Solution The least-squares estimate of the corrections is: $$\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$ The final position is `x_r = x_0 + Δx`, and the iteration repeats until `Δx` is small (convergence). --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example A GPS receiver sees 4 satellites with pseudoranges and ECEF positions: | Satellite | `x_i` (m) | `y_i` (m) | `z_i` (m) | `ρ_i` (m) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 15,600,000 | 12,000,000 | 21,000,000 | 24,820,000 | | 2 | 20,000,000 | 8,000,000 | 18,000,000 | 22,150,000 | | 3 | 18,500,000 | 16,000,000 | 14,000,000 | 21,330,000 | | 4 | 10,000,000 | 18,000,000 | 20,000,000 | 25,710,000 | Assume initial guess `(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (0, 0, 0)` and `t_{b,0} = 0`. **Step 1 — Compute initial ranges `R_{i,0}`:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{1,0} &= \sqrt{15.6^2 + 12^2 + 21^2} \times 10^6 \\ &= \sqrt{243.36 + 144 + 441} \times 10^6 \\ &= \sqrt{828.36} \times 10^6 \\ &\approx 28.782 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ Similarly: - `R_{2,0} ≈ √(400 + 64 + 324) × 10^6 ≈ 28.089 × 10^6 m` - `R_{3,0} ≈ √(342.25 + 256 + 196) × 10^6 ≈ 28.184 × 10^6 m` - `R_{4,0} ≈ √(100 + 324 + 400) × 10^6 ≈ 28.636 × 10^6 m` **Step 2 — Form residual vector `Δρ_i = ρ_i - R_{i,0}`:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta \rho_1 &= 24.820 - 28.782 = -3.962 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \\ \Delta \rho_2 &= 22.150 - 28.089 = -5.939 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \\ \Delta \rho_3 &= 21.330 - 28.184 = -6.854 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \\ \Delta \rho_4 &= 25.710 - 28.636 = -2.926 \times 10^6 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Form the **H** matrix and solve** (omitted here for brevity; covered in advanced KTU problems). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sources of GPS Error | Error Source | Typical Magnitude (m) | Mitigation Strategy | |---|---|---| | **Ionosphere Delay** | 1 – 5 | Dual-frequency receivers (L1 + L2) | | **Troposphere Delay** | 0.5 – 2 | Atmospheric modeling (Saastamoinen) | | **Multipath** | 0.5 – 3 | Choke-ring antennas, RAIM algorithms | | **Satellite Clock** | 0.5 – 2 | Corrected via navigation message | | **Ephemeris Error** | ~1 | Corrected via control segment updates | | **Receiver Noise** | 0.3 – 1 | Better RF front-end design | | **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** | Multiplier | Use more satellites, better geometry | > **Total UERE (User Equivalent Range Error):** **~3.5 m** for civilian single-frequency receivers. # 4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define GPS. List the three segments of the GPS architecture with their functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** GPS (Global Positioning System) is a satellite-based navigation system that provides **location, velocity, and time** information to a receiver anywhere on Earth. The three segments are: 1. **Space Segment** – Constellation of **24+ satellites** broadcasting L1/L2 signals from ~20,200 km altitude. 2. **Control Segment** – Master control station + monitor stations that track satellites and upload corrections. 3. **User Segment** – The GPS receiver in the IoT device that decodes signals and computes position. *(Award 1 mark each for definition and each segment with correct function.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Explain the importance of the NMEA 0183 protocol in GPS-based IoT systems. What is the significance of the `$GPGGA` sentence?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **NMEA 0183** is a standard ASCII serial communication protocol used by GPS receivers to output navigation data to a host device (microcontroller/PC) over UART. Significance of **`$GPGGA`** (Global Positioning System Fix Data) sentence: - Provides **time, latitude, longitude, fix quality, number of satellites, HDOP, and altitude** in a single sentence. - It is the **most commonly parsed sentence** in IoT applications for acquiring position. **Example fields:** `$GPGGA,123519,4807.038,N,01131.000,E,1,08,0.9,545.4,M,46.9,M,,*47` *(Award 1 mark for NMEA definition, 1.5 marks for GPGGA fields, 0.5 mark for example.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** #### **(a) Explain the working principle of GPS-based location determination using the trilateration method. Derive the pseudorange equation and explain why a minimum of 4 satellites is required for a 3D position fix.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Working Principle of Trilateration (3 marks):** - The GPS receiver measures the **time taken by a signal to travel from a satellite to the receiver**. - The time delay is multiplied by the **speed of light** to compute the **distance (pseudorange)** to each satellite. - Each pseudorange defines a **sphere** (in 3D) or **circle** (in 2D) on which the receiver must lie. - The intersection of multiple such spheres from known satellite positions gives the receiver's location. **2. Derivation of Pseudorange Equation (3 marks):** Let `t_s` = satellite signal transmission time, `t_r` = receiver reception time, `δt_r` = receiver clock bias, `δt_s` = satellite clock bias. The measured travel time is: $$\Delta t = (t_r + \delta t_r) - (t_s + \delta t_s)$$ Multiplying by speed of light `c`: $$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t = c(t_r - t_s) + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_s)$$ Since satellite clock error is small and broadcast in the navigation message (corrected by `δt_s`), we get the simplified form: $$\rho = R + c \cdot \delta t_r$$ where `R` is the true geometric range and `c · δt_r` is the clock bias error. **3. Why 4 Satellites are Required (1 mark):** - The unknown receiver position has 3 components `(x, y, z)`. - An additional unknown — **receiver clock bias** `t_b` — arises because consumer receivers use inexpensive quartz clocks, not atomic clocks. - Each satellite provides **one equation**: `ρ_i = √((x_i - x)² + (y_i - y)² + (z_i - z)²) + c · t_b`. - Hence, **4 satellites are required** to solve 4 unknowns `(x, y, z, t_b)`. --- #### **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the three segments of the GPS architecture. Also, list any 4 sources of error in GPS positioning.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Three Segments Diagram (4 marks):** ``` SPACE SEGMENT CONTROL SEGMENT 31 Satellites Master Control 6 Orbital Planes Stations 20200 km altitude Monitor Stations (broadcasts L1/L2) (tracks & uploads) | | | Radio Link | v v USER SEGMENT IoT GPS Receiver + ESP32 (Computes Position via Trilateration) ``` | Segment | Function | |---|---| | **Space** | Transmits navigation signals; contains atomic clocks | | **Control** | Monitors satellite health; uploads orbital corrections | | **User** | Decodes signals; computes (x, y, z, t_b) | **2. Four Sources of GPS Error (3 marks):** 1. **Ionosphere Delay** — Signals slow down traversing ionized layers; mitigated by dual-frequency receivers. 2. **Troposphere Delay** — Water vapor and pressure cause refractive delay; modeled using Saastamoinen equation. 3. **Multipath Error** — Signals reflect off buildings/terrain; mitigated using choke-ring antennas. 4. **Satellite Clock Errors** — Residual drift in atomic clocks; corrected via navigation message parameters. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** #### **(a) Explain the structure of the NMEA 0183 protocol. Decode the following `$GPGGA` sentence completely and calculate the latitude and longitude in decimal degrees:** **`$GPGGA,134512.000,0948.1234,N,07636.9876,E,1,06,1.2,15.3,M,-7.5,M,,*64`** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. NMEA 0183 Structure (2 marks):** - All NMEA sentences begin with `**$**` and end with `**\r\n**` (carriage return + line feed). - Format: `**$TTSSS,field1,field2,...,fieldN*CC**` where `CC` is a **2-character XOR checksum** for error detection. - The prefix `**TT**` is the talker ID (e.g., `GP` for GPS), `**SSS**` is the sentence type. **2. Decoding the GPGGA Sentence (3 marks):** | Field | Value | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `$GPGGA` | — | Sentence type: GPS Fix Data | | 1 | 134512.000 | UTC Time: 13:45:12.000 | | 2 | 0948.1234 | Latitude: 9° 48.1234' | | 3 | N | North | | 4 | 07636.9876 | Longitude: 76° 36.9876' | | 5 | E | East | | 6 | 1 | GPS Fix (valid) | | 7 | 06 | 6 satellites used | | 8 | 1.2 | HDOP = 1.2 | | 9 | 15.3 | Altitude = 15.3 m above MSL | | 10 | M | Meters | | 11 | -7.5 | Geoid height = -7.5 m | | 12 | (empty) | Time since last DGPS | | 13 | (empty) | DGPS station ID | | 14 | *64 | Checksum | **3. Decimal Degree Conversion (2 marks):** **Latitude:** $$\phi = 9 + \frac{48.1234}{60} = 9 + 0.80206 = 9.80206° \text{ N}$$ **Longitude:** $$\lambda = 76 + \frac{36.9876}{60} = 76 + 0.61646 = 76.61646° \text{ E}$$ --- #### **(b) Describe how a GPS module is interfaced with an IoT node (e.g., ESP32 + NEO-6M) to obtain real-time location. Include the hardware connections and the algorithm to parse the GPGGA sentence.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Hardware Setup (3 marks):** | NEO-6M Pin | Connection | |---|---| | **VCC** | 3.3V (or 5V with regulator) | | **GND** | Common Ground | | **TX** | ESP32 GPIO 16 (RX2) | | **RX** | ESP32 GPIO 17 (TX2) | | **PPS** | Optional — for time sync | **2. Algorithm — Parsing GPGGA (4 marks):** ``` Step 1: Initialize UART2 at 9600 baud on ESP32 Step 2: Wait for a line starting with "$GPGGA" Step 3: Read the entire line into a buffer until '\n' Step 4: Validate the checksum: XOR all chars between '$' and '*' Step 5: If valid, split the string by commas into fields Step 6: Extract field[1] = UTC Time Step 7: Extract field[2] = Latitude, field[3] = N/S Step 8: Extract field[4] = Longitude, field[5] = E/W Step 9: Convert to decimal degrees: decimal = deg + min/60 Apply sign: -1 if S or W Step 10: Publish JSON to MQTT broker: {"lat": 9.80206, "lon": 76.61646, "time": "13:45:12", "sat": 6} Step 11: Repeat with delay (1 sec typical) ``` **3. Software Outline:** - **Libraries:** `TinyGPS++` (Arduino) or custom parser. - **Key functions:** `encode()`, `location.lat()`, `location.lng()`, `location.age()`. - **Power optimization:** Put NEO-6M in **periodic mode** (1 fix per minute) to reduce power to **<5 mA**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Pseudorange with True Range** — Students often forget to mention the **clock bias term** `c · t_b` in the pseudorange equation. This alone causes a loss of **2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting the 4th Satellite Justification** — The "why 4 satellites?" question is frequently asked; the correct reasoning is **clock bias**, not "for better accuracy." > 3. **NMEA Checksum Omission** — When asked to validate a sentence, students often skip the XOR checksum calculation. > 4. **Mixing up `t_b` and `t_s`** — Clock bias is the **receiver's** error, not the satellite's. > 5. **Decimal Conversion Errors** — Use the correct formula: `Decimal = Degrees + Minutes / 60`; do NOT divide by 100. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **GPS Architecture:** **3 segments** — Space, Control, User. - **Satellite Constellation:** **24+ satellites** at **20,200 km** in **6 orbital planes** (55° inclination). - **Carrier Frequencies:** L1 = **1575.42 MHz** (civilian), L2 = **1227.60 MHz** (military). - **Trilateration Principle:** Intersection of **3 spheres** for 2D + altitude; needs **4 satellites** to also solve **clock bias**. - **Pseudorange Formula:** $\rho = R + c \cdot t_b$ (true range + receiver clock bias error). - **NMEA Standard:** ASCII sentences at **4800–9600 baud**; format `$TTSSS,...*CC`. - **Key Sentence:** `$GPGGA` = UTC time, lat, long, fix quality, satellite count, HDOP, altitude. - **Decimal Conversion:** $\text{DD} = \text{Degrees} + \frac{\text{Minutes}}{60}$ (sign by N/S, E/W). - **Min. Satellites:** **4 for 3D fix**, **3 for 2D fix** (assumes no clock bias). - **GPS Accuracy:** Civilian ~**±5–10 m**; improved to **cm-level** with RTK. - **Error Sources (UERE):** Ionosphere, Troposphere, Multipath, Clock, Ephemeris, DOP. - **WGS-84 Ellipsoid:** `a = 6,378,137 m`, `b = 6,356,752.3142 m`. - **HDOP:** Lower = better; **Ideal < 2**, Acceptable < 5. - **Popular IoT GPS Module:** **NEO-6M (u-blox)** — UART, 1 Hz update, 3.3V logic. - **Microcontroller Interface:** ESP32 / Arduino Uno / Raspberry Pi via **UART (TX/RX)**. - **IoT Cloud Output:** Publish as **JSON** over **MQTT/HTTP** to AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, or ThingSpeak. - **Power Saving:** Use **AGPS (Assisted GPS)** to reduce Time-To-First-Fix (TTFF) to **<1 sec** vs. cold start **30–60 sec**.

topics.title: Visual Output - Connecting and Using LEDs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Visual Output - Connecting and Using LEDs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a solid-state semiconductor pn-junction device that emits visible, infrared, or ultraviolet light when forward-biased. In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)** and embedded systems, an LED serves as the most fundamental **visual output actuator** — a transducer that converts electrical energy into photons, providing immediate human-readable feedback from a microcontroller (such as an Arduino Uno, NodeMCU ESP8266, or ESP32).

In the KTU 2024 PBITT604 syllabus (Module 4 – *IoT Physical Devices & Endpoints*), LEDs form the foundational output block used to demonstrate GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pin actuation, digital logic signaling, and PWM-based brightness modulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBITT604 – Module 4):** The module explicitly trains students to interface basic output peripherals (LEDs, buzzers) with Arduino-compatible boards. Mastering LED interfacing is a *prerequisite* for more complex actuators like relays, motors, and OLED/LCD displays introduced in later modules.

### 1.2 Key Technical Parameters of an LED

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Standard Red LED) |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Voltage | $V_f$ | **1.8 V – 2.2 V** |
| Forward Current | $I_f$ | **20 mA** (max) |
| Reverse Breakdown Voltage | $V_r$ | 5 V |
| Wavelength (Red) | $\lambda$ | **620 – 750 nm** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
