throughput drops to **\leq 0.3 kbps** at long range. #### 2.3.5 NFC / RFID (ISO 14443) - **Module:** PN532 (I²C/SPI/UART) — used for contactless card reading, **range \leq 10 cm**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = \dfrac{S}{R}$ | Time to transfer file of size $S$ at rate $R$ | seconds | | 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{payload}}{P_{payload} + P_{header}}$ | Protocol efficiency (header overhead) | dimensionless (%) | | 3 | $BDP = R \times d_{prop}$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product (bits in flight) | bits | | 4 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$ | **Friis Free-Space Equation** (path loss) | dBm | | 5 | $t_{frame} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_{bit}}$ | Air-time of one frame | seconds | | 6 | $N_{attempts} = \dfrac{1}{1 - p_{err}}$ | Avg. ARQ retransmissions (BER = $p_{err}$) | dimensionless | | 7 | $E_{total} = P_{tx}\,t_{tx} + P_{rx}\,t_{rx} + P_{sleep}\,t_{sleep}$ | Energy budget for a duty-cycled node | Joules | | 8 | $L_{path} = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}\!\left(f_{MHz}\right) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(d_{km}\right)$ | Free-space path loss (engineering form) | dB | | 9 | $SF_{LoRa} = \dfrac{R_c}{R_s}$ | Spreading Factor (LoRa, 6 to 12) | dimensionless | | 10 | $R_{s,LoRa} = \dfrac{BW}{2^{SF}}$ | LoRa symbol rate | symbols/s | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL formatting note for KTU board answer sheets:** Always quote Friis equation in **dB** form when solving numerical problems — examiners allocate 2 marks for the *exact* logarithmic expansion. ### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice - **Smart Agriculture:** LoRa nodes on a 50-acre farm can push soil-moisture data 8 km back to a gateway — **impossible with Wi-Fi** due to range. - **Industrial 4.0:** RS-485 + Modbus still dominates PLC networks because of its **1200 m reach and noise immunity**. - **Wearables & Healthcare:** BLE is chosen because peak current is **\leq 15 mA** vs. Wi-Fi's **\geq 200 mA** — a coin cell must last months. - **Automotive:** Every modern car carries **3–8 CAN buses** for ECU communication — the protocol was *invented* by Bosch for this exact use case. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — File Transfer Time over Ethernet Shield > **Problem:** An Arduino+W5500 shield uploads a **32 KB sensor log** to a cloud server over a 100 Mbps Ethernet link. Compute the **theoretical minimum upload time** assuming zero protocol overhead and zero propagation delay. Then compute the **actual time** if HTTP+TCP+IP+MAC headers add 54 bytes of overhead per packet, and the data is fragmented into 1460-byte payloads (Ethernet MTU − headers). **Step 1 — Compute the "ideal" transmission time.** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 262\,144 \text{ bits} \\ R &= 100 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s} \\ T_{ideal} &= \frac{S}{R} = \frac{262\,144}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 2.6214 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Determine the number of packets required.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{payload} &= 1460 \text{ bytes} \\ N_{packets} &= \left\lceil \frac{32 \times 1024}{1460} \right\rceil = \lceil 22.466 \rceil = 23 \text{ packets} \end{aligned} $$ The last packet carries a partial payload of $32\,768 - 22 \times 1460 = 648$ bytes. **Step 3 — Compute the total bytes on the wire (including headers).** Each packet carries 54 bytes of headers (Ethernet 14 + IP 20 + TCP 20 = 54), so: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{total} &= N_{packets} \times 54 + 32\,768 \\ &= 23 \times 54 + 32\,768 \\ &= 1242 + 32\,768 = 34\,010 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the actual transmission time.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{actual} &= \frac{34\,010 \times 8}{100 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= \frac{272\,080}{10^{8}} \\ &= 2.7208 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the protocol efficiency.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{32\,768}{34\,010} \times 100\% = 96.35\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** The header overhead costs us only **0.0994 ms extra**, and the link is 96.35 % efficient. This is why Ethernet is preferred for **bulk data** IoT gateways. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Range of an ESP8266 Wi-Fi Link (Friis Equation) > **Problem:** An ESP8266 node transmits at $P_t = +20$ dBm with an antenna gain of $G_t = +2$ dBi. The AP has $G_r = +5$ dBi. The receiver sensitivity is $P_{rx,min} = -91$ dBm at 2.4 GHz. Compute the **maximum line-of-sight range** in metres. **Step 1 — Rearrange Friis equation to solve for distance $d$.** Friis equation in dB form: $$ P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} $$ $$ L_{path} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda) $$ **Step 2 — Compute wavelength $\lambda$ at 2.45 GHz.** $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.45 \times 10^{9}} = 0.1224 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the maximum allowable path loss.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{rx,min} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{max} \\ -91 &= 20 + 2 + 5 - L_{max} \\ L_{max} &= 27 + 91 = 118 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $d$.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{max} &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) \\ \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} &= 10^{L_{max}/20} = 10^{118/20} = 10^{5.9} = 794\,328 \\ d &= \frac{794\,328 \times 0.1224}{4\pi} = \frac{97\,225.7}{12.566} \approx 7\,737 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Apply a real-world safety margin.** > [!IMPORTANT] > Free-space Friis assumes **no obstacles, no ground reflection, no multipath**. In an *urban* environment, path loss is typically **20–30 dB higher** due to walls, foliage, and interference. Applying a **fade margin of 25 dB**: $$ L_{practical} = 118 - 25 = 93 \text{ dB} $$ $$ d_{practical} = \frac{10^{93/20} \times 0.1224}{4\pi} = \frac{0.1224 \times 44\,668}{12.566} \approx 435 \text{ m} $$ > **Conclusion:** Indoors/urban, expect **\leq 500 m**; outdoors LOS, up to **7–8 km** *theoretically* (limited by Earth's curvature and Fresnel clearance). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LoRa Air-Time and Battery Life Estimation > **Problem:** A soil-moisture node uses **SX1276** with $SF = 9$, $BW = 125$ kHz, $CR = 4/5$, payload = 20 bytes. Compute: (i) symbol rate, (ii) total air-time per transmission, (iii) battery life from a 2000 mAh / 3.7 V Li-Po if the node transmits once every 15 minutes and sleeps at 5 µA between transmissions. **Step 1 — Symbol rate.** $$ R_s = \frac{BW}{2^{SF}} = \frac{125\,000}{512} = 244.14 \text{ symbols/s} $$ **Step 2 — Symbol duration.** $$ T_s = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{1}{244.14} = 4.096 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Preamble duration (standard 8 symbols + 4.25 sync).** $$ T_{preamble} = (8 + 4.25) \times T_s = 12.25 \times 4.096 = 50.18 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 4 — Payload symbol count (LoRa packet symbol formula from Semtech).** $$ n_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left\lceil \frac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20H}{4(SF - 2DE)}\right\rceil (CR + 4), \text{ symbols} $$ For $PL = 20$, $SF=9$, $BW=125\text{kHz}$, $CR=1$ (4/5), $H=0$, $DE=1$ (enabled for SF\geq 6), $CRC=1$: $$ n_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left\lceil \frac{160 - 36 + 28 + 16 - 0}{4 \times 7}\right\rceil \times (1+4) = 8 + 6 \times 5 = 38 \text{ symbols} $$ **Step 5 — Total air-time.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{tx} &= T_{preamble} + n_{payload} \times T_s \\ &= 50.18 + 38 \times 4.096 \\ &= 50.18 + 155.65 = 205.83 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Energy per transmission (TX current = 90 mA at +14 dBm).** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{tx} &= V \times I \times t = 3.7 \times 0.090 \times 0.2058 = 0.0685 \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Number of transmissions per day.** $$ N_{day} = \frac{24 \times 60}{15} = 96 \text{ transmissions/day} $$ **Step 8 — Daily energy budget.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{day,tx} &= 96 \times 0.0685 = 6.58 \text{ J} \\ E_{day,sleep} &= 3.7 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 86\,400 = 1.60 \text{ J} \\ E_{day,total} &= 6.58 + 1.60 = 8.18 \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9 — Battery capacity in Joules.** $$ E_{bat} = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 2.0 \text{ Ah} = 3.7 \times 7200 \text{ C} = 26\,640 \text{ J} $$ **Step 10 — Days of operation.** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{life} &= \frac{26\,640}{8.18} = 3256 \text{ days} = \mathbf{8.92 \text{ years}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** With LoRa's high sensitivity and low duty-cycle, the node can run **nearly a decade** on a single Li-Po charge — a key reason LoRa dominates agricultural and remote-sensor IoT. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Picking the Right Network | Parameter | Ethernet (W5500) | Wi-Fi (ESP8266) | BLE (HM-10) | LoRa (SX1276) | KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]** **Q1.** *With a neat block diagram, explain the function of the **Wiznet W5500** Ethernet controller chip. Why is it preferred over W5100 in modern IoT designs?* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The W5500 is a **hardwired TCP/IP embedded Ethernet controller** that integrates the full TCP/IP stack (TCP, UDP, IPv4, ICMP, ARP, IGMP, PPPoE) and a 10/100 Ethernet MAC + PHY in a single 48-pin LQFP package. It communicates with a host MCU over an **SPI bus** at up to **80 MHz** and supports **8 simultaneous socket connections**. It is preferred over the older W5100 because: 1. **Higher SPI clock** (80 MHz vs. 4 MHz) — frees MCU cycles. 2. **More sockets** (8 vs. 4). 3. **Lower power** (~128 mA vs. ~180 mA at 100 Mbps). 4. **Auto-MDIX** — eliminates the need for crossover cables. **Key lines to draw in exam:** SPI bus lines (SCK, MOSI, MISO, CS), RJ45 jack, and the four-layer stack (PHY → MAC → IP → Socket). --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]** **Q2.** *Compare **Bluetooth Classic (SPP)** and **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** in terms of current consumption, data rate, connection time, and typical IoT use cases.* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Bluetooth Classic (HC-05) | BLE (HM-10 / nRF52) | |---|---|---| | Current (peak) | **30–40 mA** | **5–15 mA** | | Data Rate | 1–3 Mbps | 0.27–2 Mbps | | Connection Time | ~100 ms | **7.5 ms** | | Profile | SPP (Serial Port) | **GATT** (Services + Characteristics) | | Use case | Audio, file transfer | **Sensors, beacons, wearables** | BLE is preferred for **battery-powered IoT** because of its **sub-15 mA peak** and instant-connect advertising model. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With a block diagram, describe the architecture of the **Arduino Ethernet Shield** and explain how the **TCP/IP stack is offloaded** to the W5500 chip. Discuss the role of the **SPI bus** in this communication. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An ESP8266 module operates on the 2.4 GHz ISM band. The transmitter output power is **+18 dBm**, the transmitter antenna gain is **+2 dBi**, and the receiver antenna gain is **+3 dBi**. If the receiver sensitivity is **−89 dBm**, calculate: (i) Maximum free-space path loss permissible. (ii) Maximum line-of-sight range in metres. (iii) Practical indoor range assuming an additional fade margin of 20 dB. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Block Diagram:** ``` ┌──────────────┐ SPI (SCK, MOSI, MISO, CS=D10) ┌──────────────┐ RJ-45 ┌──────────┐ │ Arduino Uno │ <----------------------------->│ W5500 Chip │ <------> │ Ethernet │ │ Application │ │ TCP/IP + │ │ LAN │ │ Layer │ │ MAC + PHY │ │ │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────┘ ``` **Explanation (7 marks breakdown):** - **1 mark** — Correct diagram with SPI labels. - **2 marks** — Explanation of W5500's integrated stack (TCP, UDP, IP, ICMP, ARP, MAC, PHY) — 4 hardware sockets expandable to 8. - **2 marks** — Role of SPI bus: clock speed 80 MHz, full-duplex, 4 signal lines, CS pin (D10) for slave select. Library: `SPI.h` and `Ethernet.h`. - **2 marks** — Working flow: `Ethernet.begin(mac, ip)` → DHCP or static IP → `client.connect()` → TCP 3-way handshake → `client.print()` (HTTP) → `client.read()` → `client.stop()`. ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Given:** - $f = 2.4$ GHz → $\lambda = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125$ m - $P_t = +18$ dBm, $G_t = +2$ dBi, $G_r = +3$ dBi, $P_{rx,min} = -89$ dBm **(i) Maximum path loss:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{max} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{rx,min} \\ &= 18 + 2 + 3 - (-89) \\ &= 23 + 89 = \mathbf{112 \text{ dB}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Maximum free-space range:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{max} &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) \\ \dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda} &= 10^{112/20} = 10^{5.6} = 398\,107 \\ d &= \dfrac{398\,107 \times 0.125}{4\pi} \\ &= \dfrac{49\,763}{12.566} \\ &\approx \mathbf{3\,960 \text{ m}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Practical indoor range with 20 dB fade margin:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{practical} &= 112 - 20 = 92 \text{ dB} \\ d_{practical} &= \dfrac{0.125 \times 10^{92/20}}{4\pi} \\ &= \dfrac{0.125 \times 39\,811}{12.566} \\ &\approx \mathbf{396 \text{ m}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** In theory ~4 km, but in a real indoor environment with walls and interference, expect **~400 m or less**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Compare **LoRa, BLE, and Wi-Fi** in terms of range, data rate, power consumption, and suitable IoT applications. Justify why **LoRa is preferred for agricultural IoT deployments** covering 50+ acres. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A wireless sensor node running on a 3.7 V / 2500 mAh Li-Po battery uses an **XBee S2C ZigBee module**. The module draws **40 mA in TX mode** (5 ms per transmission), **35 mA in RX mode** (10 ms per cycle), and **3 µA in sleep mode**. The node wakes once every 5 minutes. Calculate: (i) Total daily energy consumption in Joules. (ii) Estimated battery life in days (assume 90% depth-of-discharge). **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | LoRa (SX1276) | BLE (HM-10) | Wi-Fi (ESP8266) | |---|---|---|---| | Range | **8–15 km** | 10–30 m | 50–100 m | | Data Rate | 0.3–50 kbps | 0.27 Mbps | 72 Mbps | | TX Current | 90 mA | 15 mA | 250 mA | | Topology | Star | Piconet | Star | | Best For | **Field sensors** | Wearables | Indoor nodes | **Justification for agriculture (2 marks):** A 50-acre farm has **\geq 200 m** field-to-far edges; Wi-Fi APs cannot cover this without many hops, and BLE range is insufficient. **LoRa's 8 km range and 8+ year battery life** make it ideal for sparsely placed soil-moisture, pH, and weather nodes. The low data rate (kbps) is acceptable because **sensor payloads are < 30 bytes**, and duty cycling (e.g., once per 15 min) keeps energy minimal. ##### **Model Solution — Part (b)** **Given:** - $V = 3.7$ V, $C = 2500$ mAh = 2.5 Ah - $I_{tx} = 40$ mA, $t_{tx} = 5$ ms - $I_{rx} = 35$ mA, $t_{rx} = 10$ ms - $I_{sleep} = 3$ µA - Cycle = 5 min = 300 s - DoD = 90 % **(i) Daily energy consumption:** Number of cycles per day: $$ N = \frac{24 \times 3600}{300} = 288 \text{ cycles/day} $$ Energy per cycle: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{cycle} &= V(I_{tx}t_{tx} + I_{rx}t_{rx} + I_{sleep}t_{sleep}) \\ &= 3.7 \times (40 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \\ &\quad + 35 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \\ &\quad + 3 \times 10^{-6} \times (300 - 0.005 - 0.010)) \\ &= 3.7 \times (0.0002 + 0.00035 + 0.0009) \\ &= 3.7 \times 0.00145 = 5.365 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ Daily energy: $$ E_{day} = 288 \times 5.365 \times 10^{-3} = 1.545 \text{ J/day} $$ **(ii) Battery life:** Usable battery energy (90 % DoD): $$ E_{bat} = 3.7 \times 2.5 \times 3600 \times 0.90 = 29\,970 \text{ J} $$ Battery life: $$ t_{life} = \frac{29\,970}{1.545} \approx 19\,399 \text{ days} \approx 53.15 \text{ years} $$ > **Conclusion:** The figure is unrealistically high because we ignored the **always-on MCU baseline current** (Arduino Uno = 45 mA). In practice, **replace Uno with a sleep-enabled MCU (e.g., ATtiny84 or ESP32 in deep-sleep = 10 µA)** to achieve realistic **2–3 years** of operation. **This nuance is what examiners love to test.** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common marks-losing mistakes in this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion in Friis equation** — $f$ must be in **Hz**, not MHz, when plugged into $\lambda = c/f$. Carrying wrong units is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > 2. **Mixing up "dBm" and "dBi"** — dBm is *power*, dBi is *antenna gain*. Adding them is dimensionally correct (both are dB) but the **sign convention** (gain is positive, loss is negative) trips students. > 3. **Confusing BLE GATT terminology** — "Service" and "Characteristic" are **not interchangeable**; Services group Characteristics. Examiners deduct 1 mark for loose wording. > 4. **Forgetting the 3-way TCP handshake** in HTTP client questions — always mention `SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK` before sending the HTTP GET. > 5. **In LoRa questions**, students often forget that **increasing SF doubles the symbol time** and therefore **doubles the air-time** — but improves sensitivity by **~2.5–3 dB**. > 6. **Wired vs. wireless confusion in OSI layers** — Ethernet covers layers **1 + 2 only** on the Arduino side; the TCP/IP stack sits **on top** of the W5500, *not inside* the Arduino. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **MAC address** is 48-bit, factory-burned (e.g., `0xDE:AD:BE:EF:FE:ED`); **IP address** is 32-bit, network-assigned (static or DHCP). - **W5500** is the modern Ethernet shield of choice — supports **8 sockets**, **80 MHz SPI**, and **Auto-MDIX**. - **SPI pins** on Arduino Uno for shields: **D10 (CS), D11 (MOSI), D12 (MISO), D13 (SCK)**. - **ESP8266** = Wi-Fi only; **ESP32** = Wi-Fi + BLE + Classic BT + dual-core; both run Arduino sketches. - **HC-05/HC-06** are **UART bridges** to Bluetooth Classic; **HM-10/nRF52** are **BLE GATT** modules. - **LoRa** = long range (8–15 km), low data rate (\leq 50 kbps), payload ≤ 255 bytes — **best for periodic sensor data**. - **ZigBee (XBee)** supports **mesh networking** of up to 65,000 nodes per PAN. - **Friis equation** in dB form: $L_{path} = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ dB — *memorise this.* - **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** = $R \times d_{prop}$ — determines how many bits can be "in flight" simultaneously; critical for TCP window sizing. - **LoRa air-time** grows as $2^{SF}$ — **SF12 is 64× slower than SF6** but **+15 dB more sensitive**. - **Arduino Uno CANNOT do TCP/IP natively** — the protocol stack must be offloaded to a co-processor (W5500, ESP, etc.). - **For exam**: Always state **assumptions** (LOS vs. NLOS, antenna gains, fade margin) before solving Friis problems.

topics.title: Raspberry Pi - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Raspberry Pi - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the **Raspberry Pi Foundation** (a UK-based charity) primarily to promote the teaching of basic computer science in schools and developing countries. From an engineering perspective, it is a **System on Chip (SoC)** based embedded computing platform that integrates a **Broadcom ARM Cortex processor**, **GPU**, **RAM**, and a rich set of **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pins onto a single credit-card-sized Printed Circuit Board (PCB).

In the context of KTU's **PBITT604 - Internet of Things** syllabus, the Raspberry Pi is positioned as a **single-board controller / edge-computing node** capable of running a full operating system (typically a Linux distribution such as **Raspberry Pi OS**, formerly Raspbian) while simultaneously interfacing with physical sensors, actuators, and networked IoT devices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife of Mini-Computers"**

Imagine the **brain of a desktop computer** has been carefully extracted, shrunk down to the size of a credit card, and soldered onto a single green board along with USB ports, HDMI, Wi-Fi, and dozens of tiny metal "fingers" that you can wire to the real world.

- **Arduino** (studied in earlier modules) is like a **calculator** — brilliant for one specific job, but limited.
- **Raspberry Pi** is like a **full smartphone/computer** — it can browse the web, play video, run Python, host a web server, **and** still control LEDs and read temperature sensors at the same time.

In simple terms: **Raspberry Pi = A full Linux computer + the ability to talk to physical electronic components.**

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **SoC** | System on Chip | Broadcom BCM2711 / BCM2712 |
| **CPU Architecture** | Instruction set | ARM Cortex-A72 / A76 |
| **Clock Speed** | Processor frequency | **1.5 GHz – 2.4 GHz** |
| **RAM** | Volatile memory | 1 GB / 2 GB / 4 GB / 8 GB LPDDR4 |
| **GPIO Voltage** | Logic level | **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant on most pins) |
| **Operating Voltage** | Power input | **5 V DC** via USB-C |
| **Power Budget** | Typical current draw | **3 A (15 W)** under load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Always remember the GPIO logic level is **3.3 V**. Applying **5 V** to a GPIO pin will permanently damage the Broadcom SoC. This is one of the most common hardware failures reported by beginners.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown — The Heart of Raspberry Pi

The Raspberry Pi is engineered around a **Broadcom VideoCore / ARM SoC**. The system can be decomposed into the following logical layers:

- **Processing Subsystem**
  * **CPU:** Quad-core **ARM Cortex-A72** (64-bit) in Pi 4 / Cortex-A76 in Pi 5. Executes general-purpose instructions, runs the OS kernel, and manages processes.
  * **GPU:** **VideoCore VI / VII** — a powerful multimedia processor that handles HDMI output, H.265/H.264 hardware video decode (Pi 4: 4K@60fps; Pi 5: 4K@60fps + dual HDMI), and OpenGL ES graphics.
  * **Why both?** Modern workloads are *heterogeneous* — the CPU handles sequential logic, while the GPU offloads parallel pixel/polygon work. This is called **heterogeneous computing**.

- **Memory Subsystem**
  * **LPDDR4 / LPDDR4X RAM:** Low-Power Double Data Rate memory, sharing the package with the SoC (Package-on-Package, PoP).
  * **SD Card Interface:** Acts as the primary **non-volatile storage** holding the OS, user files, and swap space.

- **Connectivity Subsystem**
  * **GPIO Header:** 40-pin (Pi 2/3/4/5) gold-plated male header — the IoT interface.
  * **USB Ports:** USB 2.0 and USB 3.0 (Pi 4+) for peripherals and storage.
  * **Networking:** Onboard Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz / 5 GHz) and **Bluetooth 5.0**.
  * **Ethernet:** Gigabit Ethernet (Pi 3 B+ onwards).
  * **Camera / Display Serial Interface (CSI / DSI):** Ribbon-cable connectors for camera modules and touchscreen displays.

- **Power Management**
  * **PMIC (Power Management IC):** On Pi 5, a dedicated **Raspberry Pi RP1** southbridge and a Dialog/DM PMIC regulate the multiple voltage rails (3.3 V, 1.8 V, 1.0 V) required by the SoC.

### 2.2 GPIO Pins — The IoT Interface

The 40-pin GPIO header exposes the following signal types:

| Pin Type | Count | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Pins** | 4 | 5 V (2 pins), 3.3 V (2 pins) |
| **Ground Pins** | 8 | GND reference |
| **General GPIO** | 26 | Digital I/O (3.3 V logic) |
| **I²C** | 2 (SDA, SCL) | Two-wire serial bus (ID_EEPROM on Pi 4/5) |
| **SPI** | 5 | High-speed synchronous serial (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CE0, CE1) |
| **UART** | 2 (TXD, RXD) | Asynchronous serial debug console |
| **PWM** | Up to 4 software + 2 hardware | Pulse Width Modulation output |
| **PCM / I²S** | 2 | Audio digital interface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the pin count matter for KTU exams?** Most KTU 14-mark questions ask you to draw the GPIO pinout. Always label pin **1** (top-left, nearest SD card slot) and pin **40** (top-right when held with USB ports facing you).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Engineering Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a hardware-introduction topic, the numerical relations below are the most frequently tested. Master them.

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = V \times I$ | $P$ = power (W), $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A) | PSU sizing for Pi 4 = 5 V × 3 A = **15 W** |
| 2 | $I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_{pin,i}$ | Sum of currents drawn by all GPIO pins | Must not exceed **50 mA** aggregate |
| 3 | $f_{CPU} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}}$ | $f$ = clock (Hz), $T$ = clock period (s) | 1.5 GHz → 0.667 ns per cycle |
| 4 | $R_{limit} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}}$ | Series resistor for LED safety | $R = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = 130\ \Omega$ |
| 5 | $BW_{mem} = f_{mem} \times W_{bus} \times 2$ | DDR doubles data rate per cycle | LPDDR4-3200, 64-bit → 25.6 GB/s |
| 6 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy (J) = Power × time | Battery sizing for off-grid IoT nodes |
| 7 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | PWM percentage | LED brightness / motor speed control |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rendering Rule Applied:** All absolute-value and conditional bars inside the table above are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the pipe-delimited table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Edge AI / ML:** Running **TensorFlow Lite** or **ONNX Runtime** for on-device inference (e.g., object detection on a camera feed).
- **Industrial IoT Gateways:** Acting as a **protocol bridge** between Modbus (RS-485) sensors and MQTT brokers in the cloud.
- **Home Automation Hubs:** Hosting **Home Assistant** to orchestrate Zigbee, Z-Wave, and Wi-Fi devices.
- **Network Monitoring:** Running packet capture tools (Wireshark, ntopng) at the network edge.
- **Digital Signage & Kiosks:** Driving HDMI displays with Chromium in *kiosk* mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical & Numerical Derivations

> Since this is an *introduction* topic, the derivations below are **engineering computations and design analyses** commonly asked in KTU board examinations, rather than formal mathematical proofs.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Clock Period from CPU Frequency

**Problem:** The Raspberry Pi 4 Model B has a default CPU clock of **1.5 GHz**. Calculate the **clock period** and the **number of cycles** executed in 1 millisecond.

**Step-by-step solution:**

The fundamental relationship between frequency and time period is the inverse:

$$
f_{CPU} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}}
$$

Rearranging for the period:

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{CPU}}
$$

Substituting the numerical value (1.5 GHz = 1.5 × 10⁹ Hz):

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Hz}}
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 0.6667 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{cycle} \approx 0.667\ \text{ns}}
$$

**Textual Interpretation:** Every single clock tick on the Pi 4 is roughly **two-thirds of a nanosecond** long. This means the processor can theoretically perform about **1.5 billion elementary operations per second**.

**Number of cycles in 1 ms:**

$$
N_{cycles} = f_{CPU} \times t = 1.5 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Hz} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{cycles} = 1.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{cycles} = 1.5\ \text{Million cycles}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Current-Limiting Resistor for a GPIO-Driven LED

**Problem:** You are interfacing a red LED ($V_f$ = 2.0 V, desired $I_{LED}$ = 10 mA) to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin (logic HIGH = 3.3 V). Calculate the **series current-limiting resistor** to protect both the LED and the SoC.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:**

$$
V_{GPIO} = V_{R} + V_{LED}
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the voltage drop across the resistor:**

$$
V_{R} = V_{GPIO} - V_{LED}
$$

$$
V_{R} = 3.3\ \text{V} - 2.0\ \text{V} = 1.3\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law:**

$$
R = \frac{V_{R}}{I_{LED}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1.3\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{A}}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 130\ \Omega}
$$

**Textual Interpretation:** A **130 Ω** (or the next common standard value, **150 Ω** or **220 Ω**) resistor must be placed in series. This keeps the current at a safe **10 mA**, well below the per-pin maximum of **16 mA**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Practice:** Designers use **220 Ω** to be conservative — the LED is slightly dimmer, but the SoC is more protected and power dissipation is reduced.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Budget Calculation for a Pi-Based IoT Node

**Problem:** A Raspberry Pi 4 is powered by a 5 V supply and draws an average current of **2.4 A** while running an IoT workload. Calculate (a) the **electrical power consumed** and (b) the **energy used over 24 hours**.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Part (a) — Power consumed:**

$$
P = V \times I = 5\ \text{V} \times 2.4\ \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 12\ \text{W}}
$$

**Part (b) — Energy over 24 hours:**

$$
t = 24\ \text{hours} = 24 \times 3600\ \text{s} = 86{,}400\ \text{s}
$$

$$
E = P \times t = 12\ \text{W} \times 86{,}400\ \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 1{,}036{,}800\ \text{J} \approx 1.037\ \text{MJ} \approx 0.288\ \text{kWh}}
$$

**Textual Interpretation:** This is the energy a single Pi 4 consumes in a day under typical IoT gateway load. For **solar-powered** off-grid deployments (a common KTU viva question), this defines your **solar panel wattage** and **battery Ah rating**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Raspberry Pi vs. Arduino (Mega 2560)

| Feature | Raspberry Pi 4 B | Arduino Mega 2560 |
|---|---|---|
| **Processor** | ARM Cortex-A72 Quad-core @ 1.5 GHz | ATmega2560 AVR 8-bit @ 16 MHz |
| **RAM** | 1 / 2 / 4 / 8 GB LPDDR4 | 8 KB SRAM |
| **Storage** | microSD card (16 GB+) | 256 KB Flash |
| **OS** | Full Linux (Raspberry Pi OS) | None (bare-metal firmware) |
| **GPIO Voltage** | **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant) | **5 V** |
| **ADC** | **None** on board (external required) | 10-bit, 16 channels |
| **Best For** | Complex tasks, networking, AI, multimedia | Real-time control, simple sensor reading |
| **Boot Time** | 10 – 30 seconds | < 1 second |
| **Power** | 5 V × 3 A = 15 W | 5 V × 0.2 A = 1 W |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ₹4,000 – ₹6,000 | ₹900 – ₹1,200 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "Pi Thinks, Arduino Acts":** The Pi is a *decision-maker* (processes data, runs ML), the Arduino is a *muscle* (controls motors, reads analog sensors in real time). Many industrial designs use **both** together.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Raspberry Pi Internal Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below uses **alphanumeric node IDs** (no reserved keywords) and **plain uppercase labels** (no markdown formatting) to ensure clean compilation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[POWER INPUT 5V 3A USB C] --> PMIC[PMIC POWER MANAGEMENT IC]
    PMIC --> RAIL33[3V3 RAIL]
    PMIC --> RAIL18[1V8 RAIL]
    PMIC --> RAIL11[1V1 RAIL]

    SOC[BROADCOM BCM2711 SoC]:::soc
    CPU[QUAD CORE ARM CORTEX A72 CPU] --> SOC
    GPU[VIDEOCORE VI GPU] --> SOC
    ISP[IMAGE SIGNAL PROCESSOR] --> SOC
    H264[H264 H265 HW VIDEO DECODE] --> SOC

    RAM[LPDDR4 RAM 1GB TO 8GB PACKAGE ON PACKAGE] --> SOC

    SOC --> BUS[INTERNAL HIGH SPEED BUS]
    BUS --> GPIO[40 PIN GPIO HEADER]
    BUS --> USB[USB 2.0 AND USB 3.0 PORTS]
    BUS --> ETH[GIGABIT ETHERNET]
    BUS --> WIFI[WIFI 2.4 AND 5 GHZ]
    BUS --> BT[BLUETOOTH 5.0 BLE]
    BUS --> CSI[CSI CAMERA CONNECTOR]
    BUS --> DSI[DSI DISPLAY CONNECTOR]
    BUS --> HDMI[2X MICRO HDMI PORTS 4K 60FPS]

    GPIO --> I2C[I2C BUS SDA SCL]
    GPIO --> SPI[SPI BUS MOSI MISO SCLK CE0 CE1]
    GPIO --> UART[UART TXD RXD]
    GPIO --> PWM[PWM HARDWARE AND SOFTWARE]
    GPIO --> ADC_EXT[EXTERNAL ADC REQUIRED]

    SD[MICRO SD CARD SLOT] --> STORAGE[BOOTABLE STORAGE ROOTFS]
    STORAGE --> SOC

    RAIL33 --> GPIO
    RAIL33 --> SOC

    classDef soc fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 GPIO Pinout Reference (Text Schematic)

```
 +-----+-----+---------+------+---+---Pi 4 B---+---+------+---------+-----+-----+
 | BCM | wPi |   Name  | Mode | V | Physical  | V | Mode | Name    | wPi | BCM |
 +-----+-----+---------+------+---+----++----+---+------+---------+-----+-----+
 |     |     |    3V3  |      |   |  1 || 2  |   |      | 5V      |     |     |
 |   2 |   8 |   SDA.1 | ALT0 | 1 |  3 || 4  |   |      | 5V      |     |     |
 |   3 |   9 |   SCL.1 | ALT0 | 1 |  5 || 6  |   |      | GND     |     |     |
 |   4 |   7 | GPIO. 7 |   IN | 1 |  7 || 8  | 0 | IN   | TXD     | 15  | 14  |
 |     |     |    GND  |      |   |  9 || 10 | 1 | IN   | RXD     | 16  | 15  |
 |  17 |   0 | GPIO. 0 |   IN | 0 | 11 || 12 | 0 | IN   | GPIO. 1 | 1   | 18  |
 |  27 |   2 | GPIO. 2 |   IN | 0 | 13 || 14 |   |      | GND     |     |     |
 |  22 |   3 | GPIO. 3 |   IN | 0 | 15 || 16 | 0 | IN   | GPIO. 4 | 4   | 23  |
 |     |     |    3V3  |      |   | 17 || 18 | 0 | IN   | GPIO. 5 | 5   | 24  |
 |  10 |  12 |    MOSI | ALT0 | 0 | 19 || 20 |   |      | GND     |     |     |
 |   9 |  13 |    MISO | ALT0 | 0 | 21 || 22 | 0 | IN   | GPIO. 6 | 6   | 25  |
 |  11 |  14 |    SCLK | ALT0 | 0 | 23 || 24 | 1 | OUT  | CE0     | 10  | 8   |
 |     |     |    GND  |      |   | 25 || 26 | 1 | OUT  | CE1     | 11  | 7   |
 |   0 |  30 |   SDA.0 |   IN | 1 | 27 || 28 | 1 | IN   | SCL.0   | 31  | 1   |
 |   5 |  21 | GPIO.21 |   IN | 1 | 29 || 30 |   |      | GND     |     |     |
 |   6 |  22 | GPIO.22 |   IN | 1 | 31 || 32 | 0 | IN   | GPIO.26 | 26  | 12  |
 |  13 |  23 | GPIO.23 |   IN | 0 | 33 || 34 |   |      | GND     |     |     |
 |  19 |  24 | GPIO.24 |   IN | 0 | 35 || 36 | 0 | IN   | GPIO.27 | 27  | 16  |
 |  26 |  25 | GPIO.25 |   IN | 0 | 37 || 38 | 0 | IN   | GPIO.28 | 28  | 20  |
 |     |     |    GND  |      |   | 39 || 40 | 0 | IN   | GPIO.29 | 29  | 21  |
 +-----+-----+---------+------+---+----++----+---+------+---------+-----+-----+
 | BCM | wPi |   Name  | Mode | V | Physical  | V | Mode | Name    | wPi | BCM |
 +-----+-----+---------+------+---+---Pi 4 B---+---+------+---------+-----+-----+
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Booting Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[POWER ON 5V APPLIED] --> B[PMIC STABILIZES VOLTAGE RAILS]
    B --> C[BCM2711 SoC RELEASES FROM RESET]
    C --> D[VIDEO CORE GPU LOADS BOOTCODE.BIN FROM SD]
    D --> E[START ELF FILE BOOTLOADER BIN]
    E --> F[LOAD KERNEL IMG AND INITRD]
    F --> G[MOUNT ROOT FILESYSTEM FROM SD CARD]
    G --> H[EXECUTE SYSTEMD INIT PROCESS]
    H --> I[USER LOGIN PROMPT OR GUI DESKTOP]
    I:::user
    classDef user fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---
