## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

> **Q: Differentiate between a microcontroller and a microprocessor. Which category does the Raspberry Pi belong to and why? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Microcontroller | Microprocessor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O **on a single chip** | **Only CPU** on chip; RAM/ROM/I/O external |
| 2 | Designed for **embedded control** tasks | Designed for **general-purpose computing** |
| 3 | Runs a single program in a tight loop | Runs a full OS (Windows, Linux) with multitasking |
| 4 | Examples: ATmega328P (Arduino), PIC16, STM32 | Examples: Intel Core i7, ARM Cortex-A72 |

The **Raspberry Pi** uses the **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC** containing an **ARM Cortex-A72 CPU** plus a separate **GPU, RAM, and peripherals** off-chip. It runs the **Linux OS**. Therefore, it is based on a **microprocessor**, not a microcontroller. *(Full 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2022]`

> **Q: List any three features of the Arduino IDE that make it suitable for beginners. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cross-platform, single-click install** — runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux without complex toolchain setup.
2. **Built-in libraries and example sketches** — for common peripherals (Servo, Wire/I2C, SPI, LiquidCrystal, SD), enabling rapid prototyping.
3. **Simplified C/C++ dialect** with hardware abstraction functions like `digitalWrite()`, `analogRead()`, and `delay()` — hiding AVR register-level complexity.
4. **Serial Monitor and Plotter** integrated — real-time debugging of sensor data. *(Any 3 → 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A `[14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]`

> **Q (A): (a)** Explain the **architecture of the Arduino Uno board** with a neat block diagram. List its important specifications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (A): (b)** Compare Arduino and Raspberry Pi on at least **six parameters**. Justify a scenario where both are used together in an IoT application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Arduino Uno R3** is built around the **ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller. Its block-level architecture consists of the following functional units:

**1. Microcontroller Core (ATmega328P):**
- 8-bit RISC CPU, 16 MHz clock, 131 instructions (most single-cycle).
- 32 KB Flash (program), 2 KB SRAM (data), 1 KB EEPROM.

**2. Power Supply Section:**
- **USB barrel** (5 V from PC) or **DC jack** (7–12 V recommended).
- **Voltage regulator** drops to stable 5 V (logic) and 3.3 V (auxiliary).
- Reverse-polarity protection diode.

**3. USB-to-Serial Bridge:**
- **ATmega16U2** (or CH340 in clones) converts USB to UART for programming and serial monitor.
- An on-board LED on pin 13 (L) is hard-wired for the "blink" test.

**4. I/O Headers:**
- **14 digital pins** (D0–D13), of which D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11 support **8-bit PWM**.
- **6 analog input pins** (A0–A5) feeding a 10-bit successive-approximation **ADC**.
- I2C on A4/A5, SPI on D10–D13, UART on D0/D1.

**5. Reset Circuit:**
- Reset button + auto-reset on serial DTR open.

**Specifications (minimum 5 required for 7 marks):**

| Spec | Value |
|---|---|
| MCU | ATmega328P |
| Clock | 16 MHz |
| Flash / SRAM / EEPROM | 32 / 2 / 1 KB |
| Digital I/O | 14 (6 PWM) |
| Analog Input | 6 × 10-bit |
| Operating Voltage | 5 V |
| Input Voltage (recommended) | 7–12 V |
| DC Current per I/O pin | 40 mA |

*(7 marks: 3 for architecture explanation + diagram, 2 for block diagram labels, 2 for specs)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

A **detailed comparison** of Arduino and Raspberry Pi on six key parameters:

| Parameter | Arduino (Uno) | Raspberry Pi 4B |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Microcontroller (bare-metal) | Microprocessor + OS (Linux) |
| **Clock Speed** | 16 MHz | 1.8 GHz (quad-core) |
| **RAM** | 2 KB SRAM | 1–8 GB LPDDR4 |
| **Operating System** | None (loop) | Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) |
| **GPIO Voltage** | 5 V tolerant | 3.3 V (NOT 5 V tolerant) |
| **Power Consumption** | ~0.25 W | ~3–6 W |
| **Real-time Behavior** | Hard real-time (deterministic) | Soft real-time (jitter from scheduler) |
| **Connectivity** | None built-in (needs shields) | Wi-Fi, BT, Ethernet, USB built-in |
| **Best For** | Sensor/actuator control, embedded logic | Web servers, ML, vision, edge gateway |

**Hybrid IoT Scenario — Smart Greenhouse:**

- The **Arduino Nano** reads temperature (DHT22), humidity, and soil-moisture sensors. It also drives a relay-controlled 12 V water pump. Its deterministic loop guarantees 1 Hz sampling **without OS-induced jitter**.
- The **Raspberry Pi 4** acts as the **IoT gateway**. It receives the Arduino's UART data, runs a **TensorFlow Lite model** on USB-camera frames for plant-disease detection, publishes everything to **AWS IoT Core** over **MQTT/TLS**, and serves a local web dashboard.
- They communicate over **UART** (or **I2C** for tighter integration). The Arduino acts as a watchdog — if the Pi crashes or reboots (20–60 s), the Arduino keeps the pump safe and logs data to its EEPROM.

*(7 marks: 4 for comparison table with at least 6 parameters, 3 for hybrid scenario with justification)*

---

### Question B `[14 Marks — Alternative Choice]`

> **Q (B): (a)** Describe the **architecture of the Raspberry Pi 4B** with a block diagram. Explain its **boot sequence** step by step. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (B): (b)** Explain the **analogWrite() and analogRead()** functions in Arduino with their internal workings. An Arduino Uno uses a 5 V reference and reads ADC = 678. Calculate the input voltage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Raspberry Pi 4B Architecture:**

The RPi 4B is a **Single Board Computer (SBC)** centered on the **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC**, which integrates:

- **Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72** (64-bit, ARMv8-A) — application processor @ 1.8 GHz.
- **VideoCore VI GPU** — supports 4K HDMI dual display, H.265 hardware decode.
- **LPDDR4 SDRAM** (1/2/4/8 GB) in a **PoP (Package-on-Package)** stack atop the SoC.
- **Peripheral controllers** — USB 3.0/2.0, Gigabit Ethernet, Wi-Fi 5, Bluetooth 5.0, I2C, SPI, UART, PWM, SD/eMMC, HDMI × 2.

**Block Diagram (functional):**

```
[BCM2711 SoC]
    ├── CPU Cluster (4 × Cortex-A72)
    ├── GPU (VideoCore VI)
    ├── RAM Controller → LPDDR4
    ├── USB 3.0 + USB 2.0 ports
    ├── Ethernet Controller → RJ45
    ├── Wi-Fi/BT module
    ├── HDMI × 2
    ├── GPIO Controller → 40-pin header
    └── SD/eMMC Controller → microSD
```

**Boot Sequence (step-by-step):**

1. **Power-on** — the BCM2711 SoC's **mask ROM** (firmware hard-coded in silicon) executes first.
2. The **GPU** (yes, the GPU boots first!) initializes and reads `bootcode.bin` from the **microSD card's FAT32 boot partition**.
3. `bootcode.bin` loads `start.elf` (second-stage bootloader + firmware blob) into RAM.
4. `start.elf` reads `config.txt` for system parameters, then loads `kernel.img` (the Linux kernel) and the device tree blob (`.dtb`).
5. The GPU **releases the ARM CPU from reset**. The ARM CPU begins executing the Linux kernel.
6. Linux mounts the **root filesystem** (second EXT4 partition on the SD card), starts `systemd`, and brings up user-space services.
7. The system is ready — login prompt or auto-launched GUI appears.

> **Why a corrupt SD card bricks the Pi:** everything from `bootcode.bin` onward lives on the SD card. Only the tiny first-stage mask ROM is in silicon.

*(7 marks: 3 for architecture, 2 for block diagram, 2 for boot sequence explanation)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**`analogRead(pin)` — Internal Working:**

- The Arduino's ATmega328P has a **successive-approximation-register (SAR) ADC** with **10-bit resolution** (1024 levels: 0 to 1023).
- When `analogRead(A0)` is called, the function:
  1. Selects the ADC channel corresponding to the pin.
  2. Sets the reference voltage (`DEFAULT` = 5 V; `INTERNAL` = 1.1 V; `EXTERNAL` = AREF).
  3. Starts a conversion (takes **~13 ADC clock cycles** = ~104 µs at 16 MHz / 128 prescaler).
  4. Waits for the conversion to complete.
  5. Returns the **integer count** from 0 (0 V) to 1023 (≈ Vref).
- Conversion: $V_{in} = \text{ADC} \times \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$.

**`analogWrite(pin, value)` — Internal Working:**

- Despite the name, **Arduino has no true analog output**. The function generates **PWM** (Pulse Width Modulation).
- Pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 each connect to a **hardware timer** on the ATmega328P.
- `value` is an 8-bit number (0 to 255) that sets the **duty cycle**:

$$ D_{\%} = \frac{\text{value}}{255} \times 100 $$

- Default PWM frequency is **~490 Hz** (pins 5 and 6 run at ~980 Hz).
- The output is a square wave toggling between 0 V and 5 V at that duty cycle. A motor or LED (with averaging filter or mechanical inertia) "sees" an average voltage.

**Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$, $n = 10$, ADC = 678.

**Step 1 — Apply the ADC voltage formula**

$$ V_{in} = \text{ADC} \times \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$ V_{in} = 678 \times \frac{5.0}{1023} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the LSB (least significant bit) size**

$$ \text{LSB} = \frac{5.0}{1023} = 4.8876 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} $$

**Step 4 — Multiply**

$$ V_{in} = 678 \times 4.8876 \times 10^{-3} \approx 3.314 \text{ V} $$

### Final Answer
$$ \boxed{V_{in} \approx 3.31 \text{ V}} $$

*(7 marks: 2 for analogRead internal steps, 2 for analogWrite/PWM, 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final numerical result)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes (worth 1–2 marks each, repeated across cohorts):**
>
> 1. **Writing "Arduino is a microprocessor"** — wrong. Arduino is a **microcontroller board**. Only the Pi (with its ARM Cortex-A72) is microprocessor-based.
> 2. **Stating "Raspberry Pi has 5 V GPIO"** — wrong. RPi GPIO is **3.3 V**. Applying 5 V physically damages the SoC.
> 3. **Forgetting the SD card dependency** of the Pi's boot — examiners expect you to mention that **all boot stages (except the mask ROM) live on the SD card**.
> 4. **Confusing `analogWrite()` with true analog output** — there is NO DAC on the Arduino Uno. It is **PWM**.
> 5. **Using $2^n$ in the ADC formula instead of $2^n - 1$** — the full-scale value $N=1023$ corresponds to $V_{ref}$, so the denominator must be **1023**, not **1024**. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 6. **Forgetting the $V_f$ (forward voltage) of the LED** in resistor calculation — using $V_{CC}$ alone is wrong.
> 7. **Stating "Arduino runs Linux"** — never. Arduino is bare-metal. The Pi is the Linux one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this checklist and revise the night before the exam. It captures 90% of likely questions.

### 🔹 Arduino Essentials
- Arduino = **open-source microcontroller platform** (ATmega328P on Uno, 16 MHz, 5 V).
- Two mandatory functions: **`setup()` runs once**, **`loop()` runs forever** → real-time, deterministic.
- `digitalRead / digitalWrite` — binary 0 or 1.
- `analogRead` — **10-bit ADC**, returns **0–1023**, uses $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ by default.
- `analogWrite` — **PWM on pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11**, value **0–255**, default **~490 Hz**.
- No OS, no multitasking, no preemption → **hard real-time** behavior.
- Programming: **C/C++ dialect** compiled via **avr-gcc**, uploaded via **avrdude** over **USB-UART**.
- Current limits: **40 mA per pin**, **200 mA total package**.
- Best for: sensors, actuators, simple embedded control, low-power battery applications.

### 🔹 Raspberry Pi Essentials
- Raspberry Pi = **Single Board Computer (SBC)** with **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC**.
- CPU: **Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz (64-bit)**.
- RAM: **1–8 GB LPDDR4**.
- Runs a **full Linux OS** (Raspberry Pi OS) → multitasking, networking, ML.
- **40-pin GPIO header**, all GPIO at **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant).
- **Bootloader on microSD** — corrupt SD = bricked Pi. The GPU boots before the ARM CPU.
- Power: **5 V @ 3 A** recommended (USB-C on Pi 4).
- No real-time guarantee; OS scheduling introduces jitter.
- Best for: web servers, image processing, ML inference, cloud gateway, multimedia.

### 🔹 Key Numerical Formulas
- **ADC Voltage:** $V_{in} = N \times \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$
- **ADC Step Size (LSB):** $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV (5 V, 10-bit)}$
- **PWM Duty:** $D_{\%} = \dfrac{\text{analogWrite value}}{255} \times 100$
- **PWM Average Voltage:** $V_{avg} = D_{\%} \times V_{CC}$
- **LED Resistor:** $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_{LED}}$
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$

### 🔹 The Golden Rule for Hybrid Design
> **Use the Pi for "thinking" (network, ML, OS) and the Arduino for "reacting" (sensors, motors, timing-critical I/O).** They talk over **UART / I2C / SPI**.

### 🔹 Five Words You Must Use Correctly in Answers
1. **Microcontroller** (Arduino) vs **Microprocessor** (Pi) — never swap.
2. **Bare-metal** (Arduino) vs **General-Purpose OS** (Pi).
3. **Hard real-time** (Arduino) vs **Soft real-time** (Pi).
4. **ADC** (analog-to-digital converter) — Arduino has 6; Pi has 0.
5. **PWM** (pulse width modulation) — Arduino hardware PWM; Pi uses mixed hardware + DMA.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — ARDUINO AND RASPBERRY PI**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Input and Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Input and Output

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the Internet of Things (IoT), **Basic Input and Output (I/O)** refers to the fundamental mechanism by which an embedded device or IoT node interacts with the physical environment. It encompasses two primary directions:

- **Input (Sensing)**: The acquisition of physical, chemical, or environmental parameters (such as temperature, light, motion, pressure) from the surroundings using **sensors** and their conversion into electrical signals interpretable by a microcontroller.
- **Output (Actuation)**: The generation of physical actions or signals (such as light emission, motor rotation, buzzer activation, relay switching) by **actuators** in response to processed data.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBITT604**, Basic I/O is the cornerstone of the **perception-execution layer** of the IoT architecture, typically implemented through **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pins, **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)**, and **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Every IoT node must possess a minimum of one input mechanism (sensor) and one output mechanism (actuator) to qualify as a "thing" in the IoT paradigm. The simplest IoT device (e.g., a smart light) uses a light sensor (input) and an LED (output).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an IoT device as a **human body**:
- **Sensors (Input)** are the **five senses** — eyes (camera/light sensor), skin (temperature/touch sensor), ears (microphone/sound sensor), nose (gas sensor), and tongue (chemical sensor).
- **Actuators (Output)** are the **muscles and voice** — they perform actions: motors (move limbs), LEDs/Displays (show expressions), speakers (speak), and buzzers (alert).
- The **microcontroller** is the **brain**, the **GPIO pins** are the **nerves**, and **voltage levels** are the **neural signals** that carry information back and forth.

When you press a button on a remote (Input) and the TV turns on (Output), the system has performed a basic I/O operation. An IoT device does the same, but the button press might be replaced by a temperature sensor, and the TV turning on might be replaced by an automatic cooling fan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Digital I/O in microcontrollers (Arduino Uno, ESP8266, ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico) typically operates at **3.3 V** or **5 V** logic levels, with current sourcing/sinking limits of **20 mA to 40 mA** per pin.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Overview

| Term | Definition | Role |
|------|------------|------|
| **Sensor** | A device that detects physical changes and converts them into electrical signals. | Input |
| **Actuator** | A component that converts electrical signals into physical action. | Output |
| **GPIO** | General Purpose Input/Output — programmable pin on a microcontroller. | I/O Bridge |
| **ADC** | Analog-to-Digital Converter — converts continuous analog signals to discrete digital values. | Input Path |
| **DAC** | Digital-to-Analog Converter — converts digital data to analog voltage. | Output Path |
| **PWM** | Pulse Width Modulation — a technique to simulate analog output using digital pulses. | Output Path |
| **Transducer** | A generic term for any device that converts one form of energy to another. | Both |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Basic I/O System

Every I/O operation in an IoT node follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Physical Phenomenon Sensing** → The sensor detects a change (e.g., a thermistor's resistance changes with temperature).
2. **Signal Conditioning** → The raw signal is amplified, filtered, or linearized using external circuitry.
3. **Signal Conversion** → If analog, the signal is passed through an ADC; if digital, it is read directly.
4. **Microcontroller Processing** → The CPU executes firmware logic (C/C++/MicroPython) to interpret the data.
5. **Decision & Actuation** → Based on thresholds, the controller drives an actuator (LED, motor, relay).
6. **Output Conditioning** → Drivers, transistors, or H-bridges condition the output to match actuator requirements.

## 2.2 Digital I/O — Operational Logic

Digital I/O deals with **two discrete voltage levels**: LOW (0 V) and HIGH (3.3 V or 5 V).

### 2.2.1 Input Mode
- The pin is configured as **INPUT** (high-impedance state).
- It reads whether the connected device is pulling the voltage to HIGH or LOW.
- **Pull-up / Pull-down resistors** (typically **10 kΩ**) are used to prevent floating states.

### 2.2.2 Output Mode
- The pin is configured as **OUTPUT**.
- It actively drives the line to HIGH (source current) or LOW (sink current).
- **Current Limitation**: Standard GPIO pins can source/sink only **20 mA** safely; exceeding this may damage the MCU.

## 2.3 Analog I/O — Operational Logic

### 2.3.1 Analog Input (ADC)
- The microcontroller's **ADC** samples a continuous voltage (e.g., 0 V to 3.3 V).
- Resolution is determined by the **bit-depth** of the ADC (commonly **10-bit** on Arduino, **12-bit** on ESP32).

### 2.3.2 Analog Output
- True DACs are rare in low-cost MCUs. Instead, **PWM** simulates analog behavior by varying the duty cycle of a square wave.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | ADC Digital Output | $D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$ | Converts input voltage to digital count; $n$ = ADC bit-depth. |
| 2 | ADC Resolution (Voltage per LSB) | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Smallest detectable voltage step. |
| 3 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D\% = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100$ | Percentage of time the signal is HIGH. |
| 4 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = D\% \times V_{max}$ | Equivalent DC voltage of a PWM signal. |
| 5 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. |
| 6 | Pull-up Current | $I_{pullup} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{IL}}{R_{pullup}}$ | Current through a pull-up resistor in active-low logic. |
| 7 | LED Current (Series Resistor) | $R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{f}}{I_{LED}}$ | Resistor value to limit LED current. |
| 8 | Sampling Period | $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Time between two consecutive ADC samples. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** In industrial IoT (IIoT), analog I/O is critical for interfacing legacy **4–20 mA current loop sensors** used in process automation, while digital I/O dominates in modern smart-home devices for cost and noise immunity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Conversion Mathematics

The fundamental operation of an ADC is to map a continuous analog voltage into a discrete integer value representable by a binary word of $n$ bits.

### Step 1: Define the Input Range
The analog input voltage $V_{in}$ lies within the ADC's reference range:

$$V_{in} \in [0, V_{ref}]$$

where $V_{ref}$ is the reference voltage supplied to the ADC (typically equal to the MCU's supply voltage, e.g., **3.3 V** for ESP32).

### Step 2: Determine the Number of Discrete Levels
For an $n$-bit ADC, the total number of quantization levels is:

$$L = 2^n$$

This means the input range is divided into $L - 1$ equal intervals.

### Step 3: Compute the Resolution (Step Size)
The voltage corresponding to **1 LSB (Least Significant Bit)** is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref} - 0}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

For a **10-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$:

$$\Delta V = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

### Step 4: Derive the Digital Output
The digital count $D$ corresponding to input $V_{in}$ is the ratio of the input voltage to the step size, truncated (floored):

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref} / 2^n} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \times 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor$$

### Step 5: Worked Example
**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$ reads a sensor voltage of $V_{in} = 1.65\text{ V}$. Find the digital output.

**Solution:**

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{1.65 \times 2^{12}}{3.3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.65 \times 4096}{3.3} \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{6758.4}{3.3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 2048 \right\rfloor = 2048$$

**Interpretation:** The output is exactly **half of full-scale (2048 out of 4095)**, which is expected since 1.65 V is half of 3.3 V.

## 3.2 Derivation: PWM Average Voltage

PWM does not produce a true analog voltage; instead, it produces a high-frequency square wave whose **average DC value** simulates analog behavior.

### Step 1: Define the Period
The total period of one PWM cycle is:

$$T = T_{on} + T_{off}$$

### Step 2: Define the Duty Cycle
The duty cycle is the fraction of the period for which the signal is HIGH:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Average Voltage
The average voltage delivered to a load is the area under the waveform over one period, divided by the period:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt = \frac{V_{max} \cdot T_{on} + 0 \cdot T_{off}}{T} = V_{max} \cdot D$$

### Step 4: Worked Example
**Problem:** An LED is driven by a PWM signal with $V_{max} = 5\text{ V}$ and duty cycle $D = 30\%$. Find the average voltage across the LED.

**Solution:**

$$V_{avg} = 5 \times 0.30 = 1.5 \text{ V}$$

**Result:** The LED perceives an effective voltage of 1.5 V, dimming it appropriately.

## 3.3 Derivation: LED Series Resistor Selection

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)
For a circuit with supply $V_{supply}$, LED forward voltage $V_f$, and series resistor $R$:

$$V_{supply} = V_R + V_f$$

### Step 2: Express Resistor Voltage
$$V_R = V_{supply} - V_f$$

### Step 3: Apply Ohm's Law
$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_{LED}}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example
**Problem:** A red LED ($V_f = 2.0\text{ V}$, $I_{LED} = 20\text{ mA}$) is connected to a $5\text{ V}$ GPIO pin. Find the required series resistor.

**Solution:**

$$R = \frac{5 - 2.0}{0.020} = \frac{3.0}{0.020} = 150 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Standard Practice:** Use the next higher commercial value, **220 Ω**, to be safe.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common I/O Sensors & Actuators

| Category | Component | Type | Physical Principle | Typical Application |
|----------|-----------|------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Input | DHT22 | Digital | Capacitive humidity, thermistor temperature | Weather stations |
| Input | LM35 | Analog | Temperature-dependent voltage | Industrial monitoring |
| Input | LDR (Photoresistor) | Analog | Photoconductivity | Automatic streetlights |
| Input | PIR (HC-SR501) | Digital | Infrared radiation | Motion detection |
| Input | MQ-2 | Analog | Gas resistance change | Smoke/gas alarms |
| Input | Soil Moisture Sensor | Analog | Conductivity variation | Smart agriculture |
| Output | LED | Digital | Electroluminescence | Visual indicators |
| Output | Buzzer | Digital | Piezoelectric vibration | Alarms |
| Output | DC Motor | Analog (PWM) | Electromagnetic induction | Robotics, fans |
| Output | Servo Motor | PWM | Pulse-position control | Robotic arms |
| Output | Relay Module | Digital | Electromagnetic switch | High-voltage appliances |
| Output | 16x2 LCD | Digital (I2C/Parallel) | Liquid crystal modulation | Display interfaces |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Basic IoT I/O Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the complete flow of data from physical world perception to physical world action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Phenomenon] --> B[Sensor Input]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D{Analog or Digital}
    D -->|Analog| E[ADC Conversion]
    D -->|Digital| F[Direct GPIO Read]
    E --> G[Microcontroller Processing]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Decision Logic}
    H -->|Action Required| I[PWM or Digital Write]
    H -->|No Action| J[Idle or Sleep Mode]
    I --> K[Driver Circuit]
    K --> L[Actuator Output]
    L --> M[Physical Action]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: GPIO Pin Internal Structure

The internal architecture of a typical microcontroller GPIO pin shows the multiplexed capabilities.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Pin] --> B[Input Buffer]
    A --> C[Output Driver]
    B --> D[Pull-up Resistor]
    B --> E[Pull-down Resistor]
    B --> F[Schmitt Trigger]
    F --> G[Input Data Register]
    G --> H[CPU Peripherals]
    C --> I[Output Data Register]
    I --> C
    H --> I
    A --> J[Alternate Function Mux]
    J --> K[ADC Channel]
    J --> L[PWM Timer]
    J --> M[Communication Peripherals]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Analog Input Signal Path

This shows how a real-world analog voltage reaches the digital core of the MCU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Sensor] --> B[Anti-aliasing Filter]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    C --> D[Comparator Array]
    D --> E[Successive Approximation Register]
    E --> F[Digital Output Register]
    F --> G[CPU Firmware]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: PWM Output Generation

This illustrates how a digital timer generates a PWM waveform for analog-like output control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Timer Counter] --> B[Comparator]
    C[Duty Cycle Register] --> B
    D[Period Register] --> A
    B -->|Match| E[Set Output HIGH]
    B -->|Overflow| F[Set Output LOW]
    E --> G[PWM Pin Output]
    F --> G
    G --> H[RC Low-pass Filter optional]
    H --> I[Smoothed Analog Voltage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to draw a "block diagram of an IoT node," always include the **sensor block**, **processing block (MCU)**, **actuator block**, and **communication block** — even if the question focuses only on I/O. Examiners award partial credit for this holistic view.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the terms Sensor, Actuator, and Transducer. How do they differ from each other in the context of IoT?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Sensor:** A device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity and converts it into an electrical signal. *Example:* DHT11 temperature sensor, LDR.
- **Actuator:** A device that converts an electrical signal into a physical action. *Example:* DC motor, LED, relay.
- **Transducer:** A general term for any device that converts energy from one form to another. Both sensors and actuators are types of transducers.

**Key Difference:** Sensors operate in the **input direction** (world → device), while actuators operate in the **output direction** (device → world). All sensors and actuators are transducers, but not all transducers are sensors.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)? State two of its applications in IoT.**

**Model Answer:**

**PWM** is a digital signaling technique in which the width (duration) of a HIGH pulse is varied while keeping the frequency constant, thereby controlling the average power delivered to a load.

The **duty cycle** is given by:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$$

**Two IoT Applications:**
1. **LED Brightness Control** — Varying the duty cycle changes the perceived brightness of an LED.
2. **DC Motor Speed Control** — The average voltage applied to a motor determines its rotational speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) used in IoT microcontrollers. Derive the formula to compute the digital output for a given analog input voltage. An ESP32 board has a 12-bit ADC with a reference voltage of 3.3 V. Compute the digital output value when an analog input of 1.24 V is applied.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the basic I/O architecture of an IoT node. List four common sensors and four common actuators used in IoT systems.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Working of ADC (Successive Approximation Type — SAR):**

1. The analog input voltage is held constant by a **sample-and-hold** circuit.
2. A **successive approximation register (SAR)** performs a binary search across the voltage range.
3. An internal **DAC** generates a trial voltage, which is compared to the input using a **comparator**.
4. Based on the comparator result, the SAR narrows down the binary value, bit by bit, from MSB to LSB.
5. After $n$ clock cycles (for an $n$-bit ADC), the final digital value is latched into the output register.

**Derivation of Digital Output Formula:**

The analog input $V_{in}$ is mapped to a digital count $D$ as:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $n = 12$, $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$, $V_{in} = 1.24\text{ V}$.

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{1.24}{3.3} \times (2^{12} - 1) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.24}{3.3} \times 4095 \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \left\lfloor 0.3758 \times 4095 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1538.86 \right\rfloor = 1538$$

**Result:** The digital output is **1538** (in decimal). In binary, this is **011000000010**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Block Diagram of IoT Node I/O Architecture:**

```
[Physical World]
      |
      v
[ Sensors ] ---> [ Signal Conditioning ] ---> [ ADC / Digital Input ] ---> [ Microcontroller ]
                                                                                  |
                                                                                  v
                                                                          [ Decision Logic ]
                                                                                  |
                                                                                  v
[Physical World] <--- [ Actuator ] <--- [ Driver Circuit ] <--- [ PWM / Digital Output ]
```

**Four Common Sensors:**
1. **DHT22** — Temperature and humidity.
2. **PIR Motion Sensor** — Human presence detection.
3. **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** — Light intensity.
4. **MQ-2 Gas Sensor** — Smoke and LPG detection.

**Four Common Actuators:**
1. **LED** — Visual indication.
2. **DC Motor** — Rotational motion (fans, robots).
3. **Relay Module** — Switching high-voltage AC appliances.
4. **Buzzer** — Audible alarms.

---

### Question B

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)? Derive the expression for the average voltage of a PWM signal. A 5 V PWM signal with a duty cycle of 45% is fed to a DC motor. Calculate the average voltage applied to the motor and explain how PWM helps in controlling motor speed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between Analog and Digital I/O in IoT systems. Explain the role of pull-up and pull-down resistors in digital input circuits with suitable examples.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Definition of PWM:** PWM is a modulation technique where the width of a rectangular pulse is varied proportionally to the amplitude of the message signal (or control value). The frequency remains constant.

**Derivation of Average Voltage:**

Let $T_{on}$ be the HIGH duration and $T_{off}$ be the LOW duration. The total period is $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$. The duty cycle is:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

The average voltage over one period is the area under the curve divided by the period:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_{max} \cdot T_{on} + 0 \cdot T_{off} \right] = V_{max} \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T} = V_{max} \cdot D$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_{max} = 5\text{ V}$, $D = 45\% = 0.45$.

$$V_{avg} = 5 \times 0.45 = 2.25 \text{ V}$$

**Speed Control Explanation:**

A DC motor's rotational speed is approximately proportional to the applied average voltage. By varying the duty cycle from 0% to 100%, the effective voltage to the motor varies from 0 V to 5 V smoothly. The motor's mechanical inertia averages out the rapid switching, resulting in smooth speed control. This method is far more power-efficient than using a variable resistor (rheostat), which wastes excess energy as heat.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Comparison: Analog vs Digital I/O**

| Parameter | Analog I/O | Digital I/O |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Signal Type | Continuous voltage | Discrete HIGH/LOW |
| Resolution | Depends on ADC bit-depth | 1 bit (0 or 1) |
| Noise Immunity | Low | High |
| Typical Use | Sensors (temperature, light) | Buttons, LEDs, relays |
| Cost | Higher (requires ADC/DAC) | Lower |
| Speed | Slower (sampling overhead) | Faster |

**Pull-up and Pull-down Resistors:**

When a digital input pin is **not connected** to anything, it is said to be in a **floating state**. The voltage on the pin can randomly fluctuate, causing false triggers.

- **Pull-up Resistor:** Connects the pin to $V_{cc}$ through a resistor (typically 10 kΩ). When the button is not pressed, the pin reads HIGH. When pressed, it connects to GND and reads LOW. This is **active-low logic**.
- **Pull-down Resistor:** Connects the pin to GND through a resistor. When the button is not pressed, the pin reads LOW. When pressed, it connects to $V_{cc}$ and reads HIGH. This is **active-high logic**.

**Example:** A push-button connected to a Raspberry Pi GPIO pin uses an internal pull-up resistor (configurable in software) to default the pin to HIGH, preventing floating state readings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ADC resolution with accuracy** — Resolution is the smallest step ($\Delta V$); accuracy depends on the reference voltage stability and noise.
> 2. **Forgetting the floor function** — ADC outputs are always integers; the digital value $D$ is the floor of the ratio, not the exact ratio.
> 3. **Mixing up pull-up and pull-down** — Pull-up defaults to HIGH, pull-down defaults to LOW. Examiners frequently test this.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — Always state the unit (V, mA, %) for full marks.
> 5. **Drawing pin diagrams without labels** — Every block in a block diagram must have a clear label; unlabeled diagrams receive partial credit only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Sensors** convert physical phenomena → electrical signals (Input); **Actuators** convert electrical signals → physical actions (Output).
- **GPIO** pins are programmable as either input or output and operate at logic levels of **3.3 V** or **5 V**.
- **Digital I/O** uses two discrete states (HIGH/LOW); **Analog I/O** uses continuous voltage ranges.
- **ADC Formula (must memorize):** $D = \left\lfloor V_{in} \cdot 2^n / V_{ref} \right\rfloor$.
- **ADC Resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$ — smaller $\Delta V$ means higher precision.
- **PWM Duty Cycle:** $D = T_{on} / (T_{on} + T_{off})$ — controls average power delivered.
- **PWM Average Voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \times V_{max}$ — used to control motor speed and LED brightness.
- **Pull-up resistors** default a pin to HIGH; **Pull-down resistors** default a pin to LOW. Both prevent floating inputs.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** Sampling frequency must be at least twice the maximum signal frequency to avoid aliasing.
- **LED Safety:** Always use a current-limiting resistor ($R = (V_{supply} - V_f) / I_{LED}$) to prevent burnout.
- **Current Limit:** Standard GPIO pins can source/sink only **~20 mA**; use transistors or relay modules for higher loads.
- **Common Sensors to Remember:** DHT11/22, LM35, LDR, PIR, MQ-2, Soil Moisture, Ultrasonic HC-SR04.
- **Common Actuators to Remember:** LED, DC Motor, Servo Motor, Stepper Motor, Relay, Buzzer, LCD Display.
- **PWM Frequency Matters:** Higher frequencies (>1 kHz) are needed for motor control to avoid audible whining; lower frequencies suffice for LED dimming.
- **I/O Block Diagram** must always show: Sensor → Conditioning → MCU → Driver → Actuator chain.
- **I2C, SPI, UART** are advanced digital communication protocols for connecting multiple I/O devices — covered in subsequent modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Inputs and Outputs with Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming Inputs and Outputs with Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)** and the **Python programming language**, **Programming Inputs and Outputs (I/O)** refers to the standardized mechanisms by which a Python program exchanges data with the *external world* — which may be a human user (via the keyboard/monitor), a file stored on persistent storage, a network socket, a hardware sensor, or an actuator connected to a microcontroller (e.g., Raspberry Pi GPIO pins).

Formally, Python treats I/O as a stream of **bytes/characters** flowing between a **program** and an **I/O device**, mediated by built-in functions (`input()`, `print()`), file objects (`open()`), and the **standard streams** defined in the `sys` module (`sys.stdin`, `sys.stdout`, `sys.stderr`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *"I/O programming enables a Python script running on an IoT edge device (Raspberry Pi, ESP32, etc.) to acquire sensor data as **input**, process it, and produce control signals, log files, or dashboard updates as **output**."*

The three canonical I/O channels in any Python program are:

| Channel | Direction | Default Device | KTU Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard Input (`stdin`)** | External $\rightarrow$ Program | Keyboard | Channel 0 |
| **Standard Output (`stdout`)** | Program $\rightarrow$ External | Monitor/Terminal | Channel 1 |
| **Standard Error (`stderr`)** | Program $\rightarrow$ External | Monitor/Terminal | Channel 2 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post-Office" Model

Imagine your Python program is a **post-office clerk** sitting inside a locked room:

- The **mailbox slot on the door (stdin)** is where customers (users, sensors) drop letters — the clerk reads them using `input()`.
- The **clerk's desk printer (stdout)** prints receipts and responses — this is `print()`.
- The **red emergency alarm (stderr)** is reserved for "something went wrong" messages — it is never used for normal conversation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy matters for IoT:** In a real IoT node (say, a DHT11 temperature sensor connected to a Raspberry Pi), the "mail slot" is the GPIO pin or `serial` port, and the "printer" is the cloud MQTT broker or an LED. The same I/O logic applies — only the *device* changes.

## 1.3 Why I/O is the *Heart* of any IoT Python Script

A typical IoT pipeline follows the **SENSE $\rightarrow$ PROCESS $\rightarrow$ ACTUATE** loop. Every arrow in this loop is, underneath, a Python I/O operation.

- **SENSE** $\equiv$ Reading from a sensor (input)
- **PROCESS** $\equiv$ Pure CPU computation (no I/O)
- **ACTUATE** $\equiv$ Sending a command to a motor/relay (output)
- **LOG** $\equiv$ Writing telemetry to a file or cloud (output)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** In the End Semester Examination, nearly every IoT programming question — whether on Raspberry Pi, Arduino-over-serial, or MQTT — is fundamentally a question about *which* Python I/O mechanism you would use and *why*.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Default newline character** in Python's `print()` = `'\n'` (ASCII code **10**)
- **Default encoding** for text I/O in Python 3 = **UTF-8**
- **Default buffering** for interactive text streams = **Line-buffered**
- **Standard prompt string** for `input()` is an *empty* string by default; the argument you pass is the *prompt*, not the data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `input()` Function — Pulling Data *Into* the Program

The `input()` function is Python's built-in bridge for reading a single line of text from **standard input** (`sys.stdin`).

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. Python encounters `input()`.
2. If an optional **prompt argument** is supplied, it is written to `stdout` first.
3. The program **pauses execution** (blocks) and waits for the user to press the **Enter** key.
4. The entered line is read, the trailing newline `'\n'` is **stripped off**.
5. The result is **always returned as a `str` (string)**, regardless of what the user typed.
6. Execution resumes; the calling code receives the string.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall (2024 Scheme):** `input()` **always** returns a `str`. To get a number, you must explicitly cast: `int(input("Enter age: "))`. Forgetting this conversion is the **#1 most common error** in KTU Python viva/practical exams.

### 2.1.2 Syntax and Signature

```python
variable = input(prompt)
```

- `prompt` (optional): A `str` displayed before reading.
- **Return value**: A `str`.
- **Raises**: `EOFError` if end-of-input is reached unexpectedly; `KeyboardInterrupt` on `Ctrl+C`.

### 2.1.3 Worked Mini-Examples

```python
name = input("Enter your name: ")          # "Alice"
age  = int(input("Enter age: "))           # 25
temp = float(input("Sensor reading: "))    # 24.75
```

## 2.2 The `print()` Function — Pushing Data *Out* of the Program

The `print()` function writes one or more objects to the **standard output** stream (`sys.stdout`) in human-readable form.

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

1. Convert each non-string object to its `str()` representation.
2. Insert the **separator** (`sep`, default `' '`) between objects.
3. Append the **end** string (`end`, default `'\n'`) at the very end.
4. Write the resulting string to `stdout`.

### 2.2.2 Full Syntax

```python
print(*objects, sep=' ', end='\n', file=sys.stdout, flush=False)
```

| Parameter | Type | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `*objects` | Any | — | Values to print. |
| `sep` | `str` | `' '` | Separator inserted *between* objects. |
| `end` | `str` | `'\n'` | String appended *after* the last object. |
| `file` | file-like | `sys.stdout` | Output destination (can be a file or `sys.stderr`). |
| `flush` | `bool` | `False` | Force buffer flush (vital for real-time IoT logs). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** On an IoT edge node, the `flush=True` argument is **mandatory** when logging sensor data that must appear in real time on a remote console (e.g., over SSH or MQTT), because Python buffers output for performance.

## 2.3 String Formatting — The Three "KTU-Mandated" Methods

Python offers three ways to format strings for output. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with all three.

### 2.3.1 Method 1: **f-strings** (Recommended; Python 3.6+)

```python
sensor = "DHT11"
value  = 24.7
print(f"Sensor {sensor} reports {value:.2f} °C")
```

### 2.3.2 Method 2: `str.format()` Method

```python
print("Sensor {} reports {:.2f} °C".format(sensor, value))
```

### 2.3.3 Method 3: **%-formatting** (Legacy, but still in KTU questions)

```python
print("Sensor %s reports %.2f °C" % (sensor, value))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Format Specifier Mini-Dictionary (KTU Must-Know):**
> - `%s` $\rightarrow$ string
> - `%d` $\rightarrow$ decimal integer
> - `%f` $\rightarrow$ float (default 6 decimals)
> - `%.2f` $\rightarrow$ float rounded to **2 decimal places**
> - `%x` $\rightarrow$ hexadecimal integer
> - `%e` $\rightarrow$ scientific notation

## 2.4 File I/O — The Persistent Output Channel

For IoT applications that must **log telemetry** or **store configuration**, file I/O is essential.

### 2.4.1 The `open()` Function

```python
file_object = open(file, mode, encoding=None)
```

### 2.4.2 KTU Mode Table (High-Yield)

| Mode | Symbol | Read | Write | Create | Truncate | Cursor Starts At |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Read | `'r'` | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Beginning |
| Write | `'w'` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Beginning |
| Append | `'a'` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | End |
| Read + Write | `'r+'` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Beginning |
| Binary Read | `'rb'` | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Beginning |
| Binary Write | `'wb'` | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Beginning |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Opening a file in `'w'` mode **silently destroys** its existing contents. For sensor logs, always use `'a'` (append) to preserve history.

### 2.4.3 The `with` Statement (Best Practice)

```python
with open("sensor_log.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write(f"{time.time()},{temp}\n")
# File is automatically closed when 'with' block exits.
```

## 2.5 Command-Line Arguments — The IoT "Boot Configuration" Channel

When a Raspberry Pi boots an IoT script via `systemd` or `cron`, it cannot use `input()`. Instead, it reads **command-line arguments** via `sys.argv` or `argparse`.

```python
import sys
device_id = sys.argv[1]      # First argument after script name
port      = int(sys.argv[2])
```

For structured parsing, the **`argparse`** module is the KTU-recommended approach:

```python
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("--device", required=True)
parser.add_argument("--port",   type=int, default=1883)
args = parser.parse_args()
```

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule of Thumb | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `input()` return type | $\text{type}(\texttt{input()}) = \text{str}$ | Always string. |
| Numeric cast | $x = \text{type}(\text{input}())$ | Use `int()`, `float()`. |
| `print` default `sep` | $\text{sep} = '\text{ }'$ (single space) | Single blank space. |
| `print` default `end` | $\text{end} = '\backslash n'$ | Newline character. |
| File modes | $M \in \{r, w, a, r+, rb, wb\}$ | 6 KTU-required modes. |
| f-string format | $\texttt{f"\{val:.Nd\}"}$ | `N` decimal places. |
| Buffer flush | $\text{print}(..., \text{flush}=\text{True})$ | Required for real-time logs. |
| Encoding default | $\text{encoding} = \text{UTF-8}$ | Python 3 standard. |

## 2.7 Real-World IoT Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** A Raspberry Pi reads soil-moisture `input()`, formats an MQTT payload, and `print()`s to a local log.
- **Industrial SCADA:** A Python gateway `read()`s from a Modbus sensor and `write()`s to a CSV file for trend analysis.
- **Health Wearables:** `argparse` selects the BLE device; `print(flush=True)` streams ECG data to a clinician's screen in real time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Exhaustive Code Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations treat each Python I/O operation as a *mathematical transformation* on a data stream, showing exactly how bytes flow from one entity to another.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From User Keystrokes to a Python Integer

Suppose the user types `25` and presses Enter on a keyboard.

### Step 1: The OS captures raw bytes

The OS keyboard driver produces a byte stream on **stdin (file descriptor 0)**:

$$B_{\text{raw}} = \{ 0x32,\ 0x35,\ 0x0A \}$$

Here, $0x32$ is ASCII `'2'`, $0x35$ is ASCII `'5'`, and $0x0A$ is the newline (`\n`) produced by the Enter key.

### Step 2: Python's `input()` reads the stream

Python reads $B_{\text{raw}}$ until it sees the delimiter `0x0A` (newline). It then **discards** the delimiter and **decodes** the remaining bytes as UTF-8:

$$S = \text{decode}(B_{\text{raw}} \setminus \{0x0A\}) = \text{"25"}$$

The type of $S$ is `str`.

### Step 3: Explicit type casting

To obtain a numeric value, the user code must apply:

$$N = \text{int}(S) = 25$$

The full transformation chain is therefore:

$$
\text{Keyboard} \xrightarrow{B_{\text{raw}}} \text{stdin} \xrightarrow{\text{input}()} S \in \text{str} \xrightarrow{\text{int}()} N \in \text{int}
$$

### Step 4: Failure case derivation

If the user had typed `abc`, the chain becomes:

$$
\text{"abc"} \xrightarrow{\text{int}()} \text{ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'abc'}
$$

This is why KTU expects **try/except** blocks in production IoT code.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: How `print()` Builds the Output String

Given the call `print("DHT11", 24.7, sep=" | ", end=";\n")`:

### Step 1: Convert each object to a string

$$
\text{str}("DHT11") = \text{"DHT11"}
$$
$$
\text{str}(24.7) = \text{"24.7"}
$$

### Step 2: Join with separator

$$
J = \text{sep.join}(\{ \text{"DHT11"},\ \text{"24.7"} \}) = \text{"DHT11"}\,\|\,\text{"24.7"} = \text{"DHT11 | 24.7"}
$$

### Step 3: Append the end string

$$
O = J + \text{end} = \text{"DHT11 | 24.7"} + \text{";\textbackslash n"} = \text{"DHT11 | 24.7;\textbackslash n"}
$$

### Step 4: Write to the target stream

The string $O$ is encoded as UTF-8 and written to `sys.stdout` (fd 1):

$$
O \xrightarrow{\text{encode}} \{0x44, 0x48, 0x54, 0x31, 0x31, 0x20, 0x7C, 0x20, 0x32, 0x34, 0x2E, 0x37, 0x3B, 0x0A\}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right I/O Mechanism

The following matrix maps a **use-case** to the **optimal Python I/O function** — a frequent KTU question type.

| Use-Case | Best I/O Function | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Interactive user prompt on PC | `input()` | Blocks until Enter; returns string. |
| Real-time sensor log to terminal | `print(..., flush=True)` | Forces immediate display. |
| Error / warning message | `print(..., file=sys.stderr)` | Keeps error stream separate. |
| Append telemetry to a daily CSV | `open(..., "a")` + `write()` | Preserves history, no overwrite. |
| Read configuration once at boot | `open(..., "r")` + `read()` | Whole-file loading is fine for small configs. |
| Boot the script with a port number | `argparse` / `sys.argv` | No human in the loop. |
| Format a JSON MQTT payload | `json.dumps()` + `print()` | Standard IoT data interchange. |
| Read from a network socket | `socket.recv()` | Not a file, but follows the same `read()`-style API. |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Walkthrough: A Complete IoT Logging Script

Consider the following program, which is a **canonical KTU 2024 practical-style question**.

```python
import sys
import time
import argparse

def parse_args():
    p = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="IoT Temperature Logger")
    p.add_argument("--device", default="DHT11", help="Sensor model")
    p.add_argument("--out",    default="log.txt", help="Output log file")
    p.add_argument("--n",      type=int, default=5, help="Number of readings")
    return p.parse_args()

def read_sensor():
    # Simulated sensor value (real code: import Adafruit_DHT)
    return 20.0 + (time.time() % 10)

def log_reading(f, device, value):
    line = f"{time.time():.0f},{device},{value:.2f}\n"
    f.write(line)
    print(f"[{device}] {value:.2f} °C logged.", flush=True)

def main():
    args = parse_args()
    print(f"Starting logger for {args.device}", file=sys.stderr)
    try:
        with open(args.out, "a") as f:
            for i in range(args.n):
                t = read_sensor()
                log_reading(f, args.device, t)
                time.sleep(1)
    except IOError as e:
        print(f"File error: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

### Step-by-step logical trace

1. **`parse_args()`** — Parses `--device DHT11 --out log.txt --n 5` from the command line. The `argparse` module uses `sys.argv` internally; if any required argument is missing, it auto-prints usage to **stderr** and exits.
2. **Initial `print(..., file=sys.stderr)`** — Announces startup on the error stream so it does not pollute the data log.
3. **`with open(args.out, "a") as f:`** — Opens (or creates) the log file in **append mode**, ensuring previous runs are preserved. The `with` block guarantees `f.close()` is called even on exception.
4. **`read_sensor()`** — Simulates a hardware read. In production, this is replaced by e.g. `Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(Adafruit_DHT.DHT11, 4)` on a Raspberry Pi.
5. **`log_reading()`** — Constructs a CSV line using an **f-string** with two format specifiers:
   - `{time.time():.0f}` $\rightarrow$ Unix timestamp, 0 decimals
   - `{value:.2f}` $\rightarrow$ temperature, 2 decimals
6. **`print(..., flush=True)`** — Forces the log message onto the terminal *immediately*; without `flush`, output might sit in a buffer for up to several seconds.
7. **`except IOError`** — Catches file-system errors (disk full, permission denied) and reports them on **stderr**, then exits with status code `1` (Unix convention for "failure").
8. **`if __name__ == "__main__":`** — Idiomatic guard allowing the file to be imported as a module *or* executed as a script — a KTU-favored best practice.

### Failure-mode matrix for the above script

| Failure | Trigger | Caught By | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| `PermissionError` | Log file is read-only | `except IOError` | Prints to `stderr`, exits 1. |
| `KeyboardInterrupt` | User hits `Ctrl+C` | (Not caught) | Program terminates; partial line may be in file. |
| `ValueError` in `read_sensor` | Sensor returns `None` | Not caught in this stub | Program crashes. |
| `OSError: No space left` | Disk full | `except IOError` | Graceful exit. |

## 3.5 Derivation: `input()` Block-Time and the "Real-Time" Constraint

For an IoT edge node that polls a sensor every $T$ seconds, the blocking behaviour of `input()` is fatal. We can derive a **consistency condition**:

If $T_{\text{input}} = $ time spent blocked in `input()` and $T_{\text{period}} = $ desired sampling period, then to avoid queue build-up we require:

$$
T_{\text{input}} < T_{\text{period}}
$$

In a real IoT pipeline, `input()` is therefore **never used for sensor reads** — the hardware driver (e.g., GPIO library) provides a *non-blocking* alternative. The KTU expects you to articulate this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Data Flow of a Python I/O Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Python Source Code] --> B{Parser Sees input or print?}
    B -- input --> C[Read from sys.stdin]
    C --> D[Strip trailing newline]
    D --> E[Decode bytes as UTF-8]
    E --> F[Return str to program]
    F --> G[Program stores in variable]

    B -- print --> H[Convert objects to str]
    H --> I[Join with sep argument]
    I --> J[Append end argument]
    J --> K[Encode as UTF-8 bytes]
    K --> L[Write to sys.stdout]
    L --> M[Display on monitor]

    G --> N[Program performs computation]
    N --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The IoT Python I/O Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External_World[External World]
        K1[Keyboard]
        S1[DHT11 Sensor]
        F1[config.json]
        NET1[MQTT Broker]
    end

    subgraph Python_Runtime[Python Runtime]
        STDIN[sys.stdin]
        STDOUT[sys.stdout]
        STDERR[sys.stderr]
        INPUT_FN[input builtin]
        PRINT_FN[print builtin]
        OPEN_FN[open builtin]
        ARGPARSE[argparse module]
    end

    subgraph Edge_Device[Edge Device eg Raspberry Pi]
        GPIO[GPIO Pin 4]
        LED[Status LED]
        LOGFILE[log.txt on SD card]
    end

    K1 --> STDIN --> INPUT_FN
    S1 --> GPIO --> INPUT_FN
    F1 --> OPEN_FN
    NET1 --> ARGPARSE
    INPUT_FN --> COMPUTE[Computation]
    COMPUTE --> PRINT_FN --> STDOUT --> LED
    COMPUTE --> PRINT_FN --> NET1
    COMPUTE --> OPEN_FN --> LOGFILE
    COMPUTE -. errors .-> STDERR
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Sensor Read, Process, Log

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor
    participant App as Python App
    participant File as log.txt
    participant Term as Terminal

    loop Every 1 second
        Sensor->>App: read_sensor() returns 24.7
        App->>App: format line = f"{ts},{val:.2f}"
        App->>File: f.write(line)
        App->>Term: print("[DHT11] 24.70 C", flush=True)
    end

    Note over App,File: with open(...) ensures f.close() is called
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — File Open Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Closed
    Closed --> ReadMode: open with r
    Closed --> WriteMode: open with w
    Closed --> AppendMode: open with a
    ReadMode --> Reading: file.read or file.readline
    Reading --> Closed: file.close or with exit
    WriteMode --> Writing: file.write
    Writing --> Closed: file.close or with exit
    AppendMode --> Writing: file.write
    Writing --> AppendMode: more writes
    AppendMode --> Closed: file.close or with exit
    Closed --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
