## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice Style)

---

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q4 (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete architecture of an IoT-based weather station**. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture follows the standard **5-layer IoT reference model** as shown in the Mermaid diagram (Section 4.1).

**Layer 1 — Field Sensing Layer (Sensors):**
- DHT22 / SHT31 for temperature and humidity.
- BMP280 for pressure (used to compute altitude).
- Anemometer (3-cup) for wind speed; wind vane for direction.
- Tipping bucket rain gauge (0.2 mm per tip).
- BH1750 for light intensity; ML8511 for UV index.
- Role: Convert physical environmental phenomena into electrical signals (analog voltage or digital I²C/SPI).

**Layer 2 — Edge Processing Layer (ESP32):**
- Performs ADC conversion (12-bit on ESP32).
- Applies calibration constants (sensor-specific offsets).
- Implements moving average filter to remove sensor noise.
- Computes derived quantities: Dew Point, Altitude (Hypsometric), Heat Index.
- Packages data as a JSON object: `{"T":32.1,"RH":75,"P":850,"wind":4.2,...}`
- Buffers locally on SD card if network is unavailable.

**Layer 3 — Communication Network Layer:**
- Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) for short-range.
- LoRa (SX1276) for long-range, low-power remote deployments.
- NB-IoT for cellular wide-area coverage.
- Application protocol: **MQTT** with topic like `weather/kerala/idukki/data`.
- TLS encryption for security (port 8883).

**Layer 4 — Cloud Storage and Analytics Layer:**
- MQTT broker (HiveMQ, Mosquitto) ingests messages.
- Time-series database (InfluxDB) stores historical data.
- Analytics engine runs anomaly detection (e.g., pressure drop > 3 hPa/hour → storm warning).
- ML models (LSTM, ARIMA) for short-term weather forecasting.

**Layer 5 — Application Visualization Layer:**
- Web dashboard using Grafana or ThingSpeak for real-time graphs.
- Mobile app (Blynk / custom React Native) with push notifications.
- SMS alerts via Twilio for critical thresholds.
- Public REST API for researchers and government agencies.

---

**Q4 (b)** Design an **IoT weather station for a hilly, remote village in Wayanad, Kerala**. Justify your choice of microcontroller, sensors, communication protocol, and cloud platform. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Microcontroller: ESP32 (DevKit V1)**
- Justification: Built-in Wi-Fi + Bluetooth, 240 MHz dual-core, 12-bit ADC, low power (~80 mA active, 10 µA deep sleep), 34 GPIO pins, and Arduino IDE support. Better than Arduino Uno (no Wi-Fi) and cheaper than Raspberry Pi (lower power, more rugged).

**Sensors Selected (with reasoning):**
- **DHT22** for temperature and humidity (range −40 to 80°C, ±0.5°C accuracy, single-wire digital).
- **BMP280** for pressure (±1 hPa accuracy, I²C interface) — also gives temperature.
- **Anemometer (cup-type, 3-cup)** with reed-switch pulse output for wind speed.
- **Wind vane** with 10 kΩ potentiometer for direction (8 cardinal/inter-cardinal directions).
- **Tipping bucket rain gauge** (0.2 mm per tip) — robust, low maintenance.
- **BH1750** for light intensity (lux, I²C, 16-bit).

**Communication Protocol: LoRaWAN + MQTT fallback to Wi-Fi**
- Justification: Wayanad has dense forest cover and uneven terrain — Wi-Fi range (~50 m) is insufficient. LoRa (SX1276) gives 5–10 km range with very low power (suitable for solar + Li-ion battery operation). Data is sent to a **LoRa gateway** located at the village panchayat office, which then forwards via **MQTT over 4G** to the cloud.

**Cloud Platform: ThingSpeak + InfluxDB + Grafana**
- Justification: ThingSpeak offers free MATLAB analytics for time-series IoT data. InfluxDB is a high-performance time-series DB. Grafana provides a rich, customizable dashboard accessible from any browser. Free-tier and open-source — perfect for a low-budget village deployment.

**Power System: 10 W Solar Panel + 3.7 V 6000 mAh Li-ion Battery + TP4056 Charging Module**
- Justification: Off-grid operation, ~5–7 days autonomy during cloudy monsoon days.

**Enclosure: IP65-rated weatherproof plastic box + radiation shield (Stevenson screen style)**
- Justification: Protects electronics from Kerala's high humidity (>90%) and monsoon rain.

**Alert System: SMS via Twilio when wind speed > 50 km/h or rain > 50 mm/h**
- Justification: Critical for landslide and flash-flood early warning in Wayanad.

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q5 (a)** Derive the **formula for computing altitude from barometric pressure** measured by a BMP280 sensor. Use standard atmospheric constants and simplify to a form suitable for an embedded MCU. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

See full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Recap of Key Steps:**
- Start: International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) equation
- Substitute: $P_0 = 1013.25$ hPa, $L = 0.0065$ K/m, $T_0 = 288.15$ K
- Compute exponent: $\dfrac{g \cdot M}{R^* \cdot L} = 5.255$
- Rearrange: $h = \dfrac{T_0}{L} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{1/5.255}\right]$
- Final: $h = 44330 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P}{1013.25}\right)^{1/5.255}\right]$ meters
- Numerical example: $P = 900$ hPa → $h = 988$ m

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Implementation Hint (Pseudocode for MCU, conceptual only):**
> `float altitude = 44330.0 * (1.0 - pow(pressure_hPa / 1013.25, 0.1903));`
> *(Embedded code shown for completeness — actual coding is not required in ESE answers, only the algorithm/math.)*

---

**Q5 (b)** A field-deployed IoT weather station records $T = 28$ °C and $RH = 85$ % during peak monsoon in Alappuzha. Calculate the **dew point** and **air density** at sea level. State the meteorological significance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Step 1: Dew Point Calculation (Magnus-Tetens)

Constants: $a = 17.67$, $b = 243.5$ °C.

$$
\gamma = \ln\left(\dfrac{85}{100}\right) + \dfrac{17.67 \times 28}{243.5 + 28}
$$

$$
\gamma = \ln(0.85) + \dfrac{494.76}{271.5} = -0.1625 + 1.8223 = 1.6598
$$

$$
T_d = \dfrac{243.5 \times 1.6598}{17.67 - 1.6598} = \dfrac{404.16}{16.0102}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_d \approx 25.24 \text{ °C}}
$$

#### Step 2: Air Density

Convert units: $T_K = 28 + 273.15 = 301.15$ K, $P_{Pa} = 101325$ Pa (sea level).

$$
\rho = \dfrac{P}{R_d \cdot T} = \dfrac{101325}{287.05 \times 301.15} = \dfrac{101325}{86450.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho \approx 1.172 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

#### Step 3: Meteorological Significance

1. **Dew point 25.24 °C** is only **2.76 °C below ambient 28 °C** — extremely saturated air. This explains Alappuzha's persistent fog, high humidity, and "sticky" feel during monsoon.
2. **Dew point above 24 °C** is classified by NOAA as **"oppressive / severely uncomfortable"** for human comfort.
3. **Air density 1.172 kg/m³** is lower than ISA standard (1.225 kg/m³) due to the high temperature. Hot air is less dense — this affects aircraft lift at coastal Alappuzha airstrip and small drone operations.
4. **Fog and visibility:** With such a high dew point, even a 2°C temperature drop will cause **radiation fog** formation, important for the Kochi international airport flight warnings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Student Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-ups:** Forgetting to convert °C to K in the air density formula (very common — costs 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Magnus Constant Confusion:** Using $a = 17.27$, $b = 237.7$ (Aug-Roche-Magnus) vs $a = 17.67$, $b = 243.5$ (Sonntag). KTU expects the **Sonntag** version for water in 0–50°C.
> 3. **Wrong Exponent in Hypsometric:** Writing $1/5.255$ as $0.1903$ is correct, but students often use $1/5.5$ or $1/5$ by mistake — check.
> 4. **Dew Point Below Zero Edge Case:** If $T < 0$ °C, use the **ice version** of Magnus with $a = 22.46$, $b = 272.6$. Forgetting this is a KTU common pitfall.
> 5. **Pressure Unit:** BMP280 returns Pa by default; convert to hPa by dividing by 100 before using $P_0 = 1013.25$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — High-Density Bullets:**

- ✅ IoT Weather Station = **Sense → Process → Connect → Analyze → Visualize** pipeline.
- ✅ Minimum 5 sensors required for a "real" weather station: **T, RH, P, Wind, Rain**.
- ✅ **DHT22** = combined T+RH; **BMP280** = combined T+P (altitude via hypsometric).
- ✅ **ESP32** is the KTU-recommended MCU: dual-core, 240 MHz, 12-bit ADC, Wi-Fi+BT.
- ✅ **MQTT** is the gold-standard application protocol — broker-based pub/sub, low overhead.
- ✅ **LoRa (SX1276)** for long-range rural deployments; **NB-IoT** for cellular scale.
- ✅ Hypsometric Altitude formula: $h = 44330 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P}{1013.25}\right)^{1/5.255}\right]$ m.
- ✅ Dew Point via Magnus-Tetens (Sonntag constants: $a=17.67$, $b=243.5$).
- ✅ Air Density: $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R_d \cdot T}$, with $R_d = 287.05$ J/(kg·K).
- ✅ Power: **Solar + Li-ion + TP4056** is the standard off-grid power pack.
- ✅ Enclosure: **IP65 weatherproof box + radiation shield** for sensor accuracy.
- ✅ Cloud: **ThingSpeak / InfluxDB / Grafana** (open-source, KTU-friendly).
- ✅ Alerts: **Twilio SMS / Blynk push notifications** on threshold breach.
- ✅ Security: **MQTT over TLS (port 8883)** — plain MQTT port 1883 is insecure.
- ✅ **5-Layer IoT Reference Model** must be drawn as a labelled block diagram for full marks.
- ✅ Always convert °C → K and hPa → Pa before substituting into SI formulas.
- ✅ Tipping bucket rain gauge: 1 tip = 0.2 mm; anemometer: 1 pulse = rotation count.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects students to be able to **design + defend** the choice of every component, not just list them.

---

> **END OF NOTES — IoT-based Weather Station (Module 5, PBITT604)**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) — Outcome-Based Education, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L6) — for B.Tech CSE / ECE / EEE students.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Remote Surveillance System with Camera Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 6
> - **Topic:** Remote Surveillance System with Camera Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Remote Surveillance System with Camera Control** is an Internet of Things (IoT) application architecture that integrates image acquisition hardware, electromechanical actuation, network communication protocols, and cloud-edge computing to enable real-time visual monitoring and remote directional/orbital manipulation of a camera unit from geographically distant locations.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBITT604 – Module 6), this system is classified as a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** where the *cyber* domain (network, software, analytics) interacts continuously with the *physical* domain (camera optics, servo motors, sensors) through a closed feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The system is built on four pillars: **(1) Sensing** (camera + PIR/ultrasonic), **(2) Actuation** (servo/stepper motors for pan-tilt), **(3) Connectivity** (Wi-Fi/MQTT/HTTP/RTSP), and **(4) Intelligence** (motion detection, AI-based object recognition).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Watchman"

Imagine hiring a security guard for a large warehouse. Now upgrade this guard with:
- A **head that can rotate 360°** to look at any direction on your command.
- **Eyes (camera)** that record everything in high definition.
- A **smartphone app** in your pocket that lets you see what the guard sees *live* — and lets you *rotate the head* with a swipe of your finger.
- An **automatic alarm** that calls you the moment an unknown object enters the room.

That is precisely what a *Remote Surveillance System with Camera Control* does — except the guard never sleeps, never takes a break, and sends you a video feed over the **Internet** instead of a walkie-talkie.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Components

| Subsystem | Hardware Example | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Vision Sensor | ESP32-CAM, OV2640, Raspberry Pi Camera | Captures still/video frames |
| Processing Unit | ESP32, Raspberry Pi 4, NodeMCU | Runs algorithms, hosts web server |
| Actuator | SG90 / MG996R Servo Motor | Pan and tilt motion |
| Communication | Wi-Fi module (802.11 b/g/n) | Wireless IP connectivity |
| Protocol Layer | MQTT, HTTP, RTSP, WebSocket | Data transport and control signalling |
| Cloud Platform | AWS IoT, Blynk, ThingSpeak, Firebase | Storage, analytics, notifications |
| User Interface | Web dashboard, Mobile app | Live view and control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used by KTU Examiners:**
> Servo PWM pulse width standard — **1.0 ms = 0°**, **1.5 ms = 90°**, **2.0 ms = 180°**, with a repetition period of **20 ms** (50 Hz refresh rate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Tiers (Layered View)

The Remote Surveillance System is modelled as a **five-tier IoT reference architecture**:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors + Actuators)**
   - Camera module (CMOS/CCD), PIR motion sensor, servo motors.
   - Converts light → electrical signal → digital pixel data.
2. **Network Layer (Connectivity)**
   - Wi-Fi router, gateway, MQTT broker (e.g., Mosquitto).
   - Carries both **control commands** (uplink) and **video stream** (downlink).
3. **Edge/Fog Layer (Local Processing)**
   - On-device frame buffering, motion detection (frame differencing), JPEG compression.
4. **Cloud Layer (Storage + Analytics)**
   - Video archival, AI inference, alert generation, dashboard hosting.
5. **Application Layer (User Interface)**
   - Web UI, mobile app, REST APIs.

### 2.2 Communication Protocol Selection (KTU Hot Topic)

| Protocol | Transport | Use Case in Surveillance | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP/HTTPS | TCP | Snapshot capture, RESTful control | High overhead, not for live stream |
| **MQTT** | TCP | Lightweight telemetry + motor control | Low bandwidth, pub-sub model |
| RTSP | TCP/UDP | Live video streaming (H.264) | High bandwidth, real-time |
| WebSocket | TCP | Bi-directional control + stream in browser | Persistent connection needed |
| CoAP | UDP | Low-power sensor reporting | Not suitable for video |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MQTT dominates IoT surveillance:** It uses a **publish/subscribe** model with a fixed header of just **2 bytes**, compared to HTTP's **~200 bytes** header. For a system pushing 10 frames/second of motor position updates, MQTT consumes **>90% less bandwidth** than equivalent HTTP polling.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **non-pipe-safe** — absolute value and conditional bars use `\vert` or `\mid` to comply with the KTU-PREMIER markdown table safeguard.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raw Uncompressed Frame Size | $F_s = W \times H \times b$ | $W$ = width (px), $H$ = height (px), $b$ = bit depth (bits/px) | Memory sizing for frame buffer |
| 2 | Uncompressed Video Data Rate | $R_{raw} = F_s \times \text{FPS}$ | $\text{FPS}$ = frames per second | Bandwidth planning before compression |
| 3 | Compressed Video Data Rate | $R_c = \dfrac{R_{raw}}{\text{CR}}$ | $\text{CR}$ = compression ratio (e.g., 50 for MJPEG) | Actual network throughput required |
| 4 | Required Storage | $S = R_c \times t \times 8^{-1}$ (bits → bytes) | $t$ = recording duration (s) | SD card / cloud storage sizing |
| 5 | One-way End-to-End Latency | $T_{lat} = t_{cap} + t_{enc} + t_{tx} + t_{dec} + t_{disp}$ | $t$ in **milliseconds (ms)** | Real-time control feasibility check |
| 6 | Servo PWM Duty Cycle | $D\% = \dfrac{t_{pulse}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | $T_{period} = 20\,\text{ms}$ | Motor angle generation |
| 7 | Servo Angle (Linear Interpolation) | $\theta = \dfrac{(t_{pulse} - 1.0\,\text{ms})}{1.0\,\text{ms}} \times 180°$ | $t_{pulse} \in [1.0, 2.0]\,\text{ms}$ | Maps pulse width to physical angle |
| 8 | Power Budget | $P_{total} = \sum_i P_i \times \delta_i$ | $\delta_i$ = duty cycle of component $i$ | Battery life estimation |
| 9 | Frame Differencing (Motion) | $D(x,y) = \sum_{x,y} \vert I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$ | $I_t$ = current frame, $I_{t-1}$ = previous | Motion detection threshold test |
| 10 | Nyquist Sampling for Control | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = max motor oscillation freq | Anti-aliasing for servo command stream |

### 2.4 Why This System Matters in Engineering

- **Smart Cities:** Traffic monitoring, crowd density estimation.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Remote inspection of hazardous zones (nuclear, mining).
- **Healthcare:** Tele-consultation, elderly fall detection.
- **Agriculture:** Crop disease monitoring via drone-mounted pan-tilt-zoom (PTZ) cameras.
- **Defence:** Perimeter intrusion detection in forward bases.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Spec Anchor:** A typical ESP32-CAM streams **640×480 MJPEG at 15 FPS**, producing ≈ **1.5 Mbps** compressed bitrate — well within a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi link budget of 54 Mbps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Uncompressed Frame Size for HD Video

**Given:**
- Resolution: $W = 1920$ px, $H = 1080$ px
- Bit depth: $b = 24$ bits/px (RGB888)
- Frame rate: $\text{FPS} = 30$

**Step 1:** Compute the number of pixels per frame.

$$
N_{px} = W \times H = 1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by bit depth to get bits per frame.

$$
F_s = N_{px} \times b = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 = 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to bytes (dividing by 8).

$$
F_s = \frac{49{,}766{,}400}{8} = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes} \approx 6.22 \text{ MB/frame}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by FPS to get raw data rate.

$$
R_{raw} = F_s \times \text{FPS} = 6{,}220{,}800 \times 30 = 186{,}624{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Megabits per second.

$$
R_{raw} = 186{,}624{,}000 \times 8 = 1{,}492{,}992{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 1.49 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A single uncompressed 1080p@30fps stream demands **~1.49 Gbps** — this is **why compression is non-negotiable** in IoT surveillance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compressed Bitrate, Bandwidth and Storage

**Given:** Same HD parameters, MJPEG compression ratio $\text{CR} = 20$, recording duration $t = 24$ hours.

**Step 1:** Compressed data rate in bits/sec.

$$
R_c = \frac{R_{raw}}{\text{CR}} = \frac{1{,}492{,}992{,}000}{20} = 74{,}649{,}600 \text{ bps} \approx 74.65 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2:** Required TCP/IP bandwidth with 10% protocol overhead.

$$
B_{req} = R_c \times 1.10 = 74.65 \times 1.10 \approx 82.12 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 3:** Total storage for 24 hours.

$$
S = R_c \times t = 74{,}649{,}600 \times (24 \times 3600) \text{ bits}
$$

$$
S = 74{,}649{,}600 \times 86{,}400 = 6.45 \times 10^{12} \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert bits → Gigabytes.

$$
S = \frac{6.45 \times 10^{12}}{8 \times 10^9} \approx 806.25 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For 24×7 HD recording, you need **>800 GB storage per camera per day** — driving the need for either H.264 (CR ≈ 100), motion-triggered recording, or cloud-tiered archival.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Servo Angle to PWM Pulse Mapping

**Goal:** Move a pan-tilt servo to $\theta = 135°$.

**Step 1:** Use the linear mapping (Formula 7):

$$
\theta = \frac{(t_{pulse} - 1.0\,\text{ms})}{1.0\,\text{ms}} \times 180°
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for pulse width.

$$
t_{pulse} = \left(\frac{\theta}{180°}\right) \times 1.0\,\text{ms} + 1.0\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\theta = 135°$.

$$
t_{pulse} = \left(\frac{135}{180}\right) \times 1.0 + 1.0 = 0.75 + 1.0 = 1.75 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute duty cycle for a 20 ms period.

$$
D\% = \frac{1.75}{20} \times 100 = 8.75\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> This 8.75% duty cycle is what the microcontroller's PWM peripheral is programmed to produce — directly translating a *logical angle* into a *physical actuation*.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Suitability Scoring

| Criterion (Weight) | HTTP | MQTT | RTSP | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth Efficiency (30%) | 2/5 | 5/5 | 3/5 | 4/5 |
| Real-time Suitability (25%) | 2/5 | 4/5 | 5/5 | 5/5 |
| Motor Control Latency (20%) | 2/5 | 5/5 | 1/5 | 4/5 |
| Browser Compatibility (15%) | 5/5 | 2/5 | 2/5 | 5/5 |
| Security (TLS Support) (10%) | 5/5 | 5/5 | 3/5 | 5/5 |
| **Weighted Total /25** | **2.65** | **4.35** | **2.85** | **4.45** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from Matrix:** For a *full* surveillance-and-control system, the optimal architecture is a **hybrid** — **WebSocket** for browser-based live video + **MQTT** for low-latency motor control signalling. This is the exact architecture KTU examiners expect in design questions.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — Motion Detection Threshold

A system uses frame differencing with threshold $\tau = 5 \times 10^6$ (sum of absolute differences). Two consecutive frames differ by $D(x,y) = 6.2 \times 10^6$. Determine if motion is detected.

**Step 1:** Compare $D$ against $\tau$.

$$
D(x,y) = 6.2 \times 10^6 > \tau = 5 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 2:** Decision rule.

$$
\text{Motion Detected} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{YES}, & \text{if } D(x,y) \geq \tau \\
\text{NO}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3:** Result.

$$
\boxed{\text{Motion is DETECTED. Trigger alarm and start recording.}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Hardware-Software Co-Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PIR Motion Sensor] --> B[ESP32 Microcontroller]
    C[OV2640 Camera Module] --> B
    B --> D[SG90 Pan Servo]
    B --> E[MG996R Tilt Servo]
    B -->|Wi-Fi 802.11n| F[Local Router]
    F -->|MQTT Publish| G[Cloud MQTT Broker]
    F -->|RTSP Stream| H[Video Server]
    G --> I[Cloud Database]
    I --> J[Web Dashboard]
    H --> J
    J -->|User Swipe/Click| K[Mobile App or Browser]
    K -->|MQTT Subscribe| G
    G -->|Command Topic| B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows clockwise from sensors (left) to user (right), while control commands flow counter-clockwise from user back to actuators. This forms the **closed cyber-physical loop** required by the KTU syllabus.

---

### 4.2 Data Flow Diagram — Frame Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Light enters lens] --> F2[CMOS sensor photodiodes]
    F2 --> F3[Raw Bayer pattern buffer]
    F3 --> F4[ISP colour interpolation]
    F4 --> F5[JPEG encoder MJPEG]
    F5 --> F6[TCP socket send]
    F6 --> F7[Wi-Fi MAC layer]
    F7 --> F8[Router and ISP]
    F8 --> F9[Cloud video server]
    F9 --> F10[Browser WebSocket receive]
    F10 --> F11[HTML5 canvas decode]
    F11 --> F12[Display on user screen]
```

---

### 4.3 MQTT Topic Architecture for Camera Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pub1[User Mobile App] -->|pub| t1[surveillance/cam01/cmd/pan]
    pub1 -->|pub| t2[surveillance/cam01/cmd/tilt]
    pub1 -->|pub| t3[surveillance/cam01/cmd/snapshot]
    t1 --> sub1[ESP32 CAM]
    t2 --> sub1
    t3 --> sub1
    sub1 -->|pub| t4[surveillance/cam01/status/angle]
    sub1 -->|pub| t5[surveillance/cam01/status/motion]
    t4 --> sub2[Cloud Dashboard]
    t5 --> sub2
    t5 -->|trigger| alarm[Push Notification]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topic Naming Convention (KTU Best Practice):** Hierarchical and dotted — `<application>/<deviceID>/<datatype>/<action>`. This enables granular ACLs and easy routing in brokers like Mosquitto or HiveMQ.

---

### 4.4 Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Angle desired] -->|error| SUM((+ -))
    SUM -->|control signal| C[Microcontroller PWM]
    C --> A[Servo Motor]
    A --> P[Camera Pan Tilt]
    P --> F[Feedback via Image]
    F --> SUM
    R --> SUM
```

> [!TIP]
> This is a **classic negative feedback loop**. In a real surveillance system, the *feedback* is visual (the user sees the new frame and decides the next angle) — hence the system is **human-in-the-loop**, a key concept in cyber-physical system design.

---

### 4.5 Power Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSU[5V 2A DC Adapter] -->|5V rail| V1[ESP32 VIN]
    PSU -->|5V rail| V2[Servo Motor 1 VCC]
    PSU -->|5V rail| V3[Servo Motor 2 VCC]
    V1 -->|3.3V LDO| V4[Camera OV2640]
    V1 -->|3.3V| V5[PIR Sensor VCC]
    BAT[3.7V LiPo Backup] -->|via boost converter| PSU
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define a *Remote Surveillance System with Camera Control* and list its four functional pillars as per the IoT reference architecture.

**Model Answer:**
A Remote Surveillance System with Camera Control is an IoT application that integrates cameras, actuators, and network connectivity to enable real-time visual monitoring and remote directional control of cameras from any internet-enabled client.
The four functional pillars are:
1. **Sensing** (camera + motion sensors)
2. **Actuation** (servo motors for pan/tilt)
3. **Connectivity** (Wi-Fi, MQTT, RTSP)
4. **Intelligence** (motion detection, analytics, alerts)

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for pillars)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2.** Why is the MQTT protocol preferred over HTTP for transmitting motor control commands in a camera surveillance system? Justify with at least two technical reasons.

**Model Answer:**
MQTT is preferred over HTTP for motor control commands because:
1. **Low overhead:** MQTT's fixed header is only **2 bytes**, while HTTP carries ~200–800 bytes of headers per request. For a system publishing angle updates 10 times/second, MQTT saves >95% bandwidth.
2. **Pub/Sub decoupling:** The publisher (user) and subscriber (ESP32) need not maintain a persistent request-response connection, enabling low-latency fan-out to multiple devices.
3. **Quality of Service levels:** MQTT offers QoS 0, 1, 2, allowing tuned reliability for critical control commands.

> *(3 marks: 1 point + brief explanation each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 6 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IoT-based **Remote Surveillance System with Camera Control**. Identify and describe the role of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A security camera streams **VGA resolution (640×480)** at **15 FPS** using **MJPEG compression with CR = 15**. Calculate:
- (i) The raw uncompressed frame size in KB.
- (ii) The compressed video data rate in kbps.
- (iii) The storage required for **8 hours** of continuous recording in **MB**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The system architecture consists of five subsystems:

1. **Sensing Subsystem:** OV2640 camera + PIR motion sensor. Captures visual and presence data.
2. **Processing Subsystem:** ESP32 microcontroller — runs motion detection (frame differencing), JPEG encoding, and hosts the web/MQTT control logic.
3. **Actuation Subsystem:** Two SG90 servo motors — one for horizontal **pan (X-axis)** and one for vertical **tilt (Y-axis)**, controlled via PWM signals.
4. **Communication Subsystem:** Wi-Fi module (802.11 b/g/n) + MQTT broker (Mosquitto) + RTSP/WebSocket for video transport.
5. **Application Subsystem:** Cloud dashboard (Blynk/Firebase) + mobile app — provides live view, joystick control, and alert push.

> *(Block diagram as in Section 4.1 must accompany — 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for description)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**(i) Raw uncompressed frame size:**

$$
F_s = W \times H \times b = 640 \times 480 \times 24 = 7{,}372{,}800 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
F_s = \frac{7{,}372{,}800}{8 \times 1024} = 900 \text{ KB/frame}
$$

**(ii) Compressed data rate:**

$$
R_{raw} = 7{,}372{,}800 \times 15 = 110{,}592{,}000 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
R_c = \frac{110{,}592{,}000}{15} = 7{,}372{,}800 \text{ bps} = 7{,}372.8 \text{ kbps} \approx 7.37 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**(iii) Storage for 8 hours:**

$$
S = R_c \times t = 7{,}372{,}800 \times (8 \times 3600) = 212{,}336{,}640{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
S = \frac{212{,}336{,}640{,}000}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} = 25{,}312.5 \text{ MB} \approx 24.72 \text{ GiB}
$$

> *(2 marks for setup, 3 marks for calculation, 2 marks for units and final answer)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **MQTT-based communication architecture** for a remote camera surveillance system. Draw the topic hierarchy and describe the publish/subscribe flow between a mobile app, cloud broker, and the ESP32-CAM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SG90 servo is required to position the camera at **45° tilt**. Using the standard 50 Hz PWM, calculate:
- (i) The required pulse width in **ms**.
- (ii) The duty cycle as a **percentage**.
- (iii) The minimum PWM timer resolution (in **μs**) needed for **1° angular precision** across a 180° range. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**MQTT Architecture Overview:**
MQTT is a lightweight pub/sub protocol operating over TCP port **1883** (or **8883** for TLS). The three logical components are:
1. **Publisher** — Mobile app publishing control commands.
2. **Broker** — Central server (Mosquitto, HiveMQ) maintaining topic subscriptions.
3. **Subscriber** — ESP32-CAM receiving commands and publishing telemetry.

**Topic Hierarchy:**

```
surveillance/
    ├── cam01/
    │   ├── cmd/
    │   │   ├── pan   (0–180)
    │   │   ├── tilt  (0–180)
    │   │   └── snapshot (trigger)
    │   └── status/
    │       ├── angle
    │       ├── motion
    │       └── battery
    └── cam02/ ...
```

**Flow:** App → `surveillance/cam01/cmd/tilt` → Broker → ESP32 subscribes → Servo rotates → ESP32 publishes `surveillance/cam01/status/angle` → Broker → App updates UI.

> *(3 marks for architecture, 2 marks for topic hierarchy, 2 marks for flow description)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**(i) Pulse width for 45°:**

$$
t_{pulse} = \left(\frac{45}{180}\right) \times 1.0 + 1.0 = 0.25 + 1.0 = 1.25 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Duty cycle:**

$$
D\% = \frac{1.25}{20} \times 100 = 6.25\%
$$

**(iii) Timer resolution for 1° precision:**

The total pulse-width range is 1 ms (from 1.0 ms to 2.0 ms) covering 180°.

$$
\Delta t = \frac{1.0 \text{ ms}}{180°} = 5.56 \text{ μs per degree}
$$

> *(2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with units)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing Mbps instead of MBps (or vice versa) in storage/bandwidth problems. 1 Byte = 8 bits. Always convert explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting Protocol Overhead:** Pure payload calculation is *not* the bandwidth answer. Add **~10% for TCP/IP headers** and **~5% for Wi-Fi MAC**.
> 3. **MQTT vs HTTP Mix-up:** Examiners *will* deduct marks if you claim MQTT uses request/response — it is **publish/subscribe**, period.
> 4. **Servo Pulse Range:** Never assume a 1.5 ms pulse is "0°". For SG90, **1.0 ms = 0°**, **1.5 ms = 90°**, **2.0 ms = 180°**. Some servos (e.g., MG996R) are 1.0–2.0 ms; others (continuous rotation) use 1.5 ms as *stop*.
> 5. **Missing Block Diagram:** A 7-mark architecture question **without a diagram** will fetch ≤ 4 marks. Always draw and label.
> 6. **Compression Ratio Direction:** Higher CR = more compression = *lower* bitrate. Many students invert this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A Remote Surveillance System is a **cyber-physical system** integrating sensing, actuation, communication, and intelligence.
- [x] **Four pillars:** Sense → Process → Act → Communicate, governed by user feedback.
- [x] **Camera sensors** (OV2640, Raspberry Pi Cam) provide raw Bayer data; ISP converts to RGB.
- [x] **Servo motors** use **PWM at 50 Hz** with **1–2 ms pulse** for **0–180°** angle.
- [x] **MQTT** is preferred for control signalling (2-byte header, pub/sub); **RTSP/WebSocket** for video.
- [x] **Raw frame size** $= W \times H \times b$ bits; raw bitrate $= \text{size} \times \text{FPS}$.
- [x] **Compressed bitrate** $= R_{raw} / \text{CR}$; practical HD needs $\geq$ 80 Mbps bandwidth.
- [x] **Storage** = compressed bitrate $\times$ duration — convert bits to bytes to GB.
- [x] **Latency budget** = capture + encode + transmit + decode + display (target < 300 ms for live).
- [x] **Motion detection** via frame differencing with threshold $\tau$.
- [x] **MQTT topic hierarchy** must be hierarchical and device-scoped for scalability.
- [x] **Power budget** must include peak inrush of servos (SG90 stalls at ~650 mA).
- [x] **Hybrid WebSocket + MQTT** is the gold-standard architecture for browser-based surveillance.
- [x] KTU expects **block diagrams** in every 7-mark design question — draw or lose marks.
- [x] **Security** in surveillance: TLS on MQTT, RTSP digest auth, on-device encryption for archives.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SPACM":** **S**ense (camera + PIR) → **P**rocess (encode/motion) → **A**ctuate (servo PWM) → **C**ommunicate (MQTT/RTSP) → **M**onitor (dashboard/alerts). This is the lifecycle every KTU essay answer should walk through.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health Monitoring System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 7
> - **Topic:** Health Monitoring System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

An **IoT-based Health Monitoring System (HMS)** is a cyber-physical ecosystem that integrates biomedical sensors, wireless communication protocols, edge computing nodes, and cloud-based analytics platforms to continuously acquire, transmit, and analyze physiological parameters of a human subject in real-time, enabling remote diagnostics, chronic disease management, and predictive healthcare intervention.

Per the KTU 2024 PBITT604 syllabus (Module 7), a Health Monitoring System is characterized by three mandatory functional layers:

1. **Perception Layer (Wearable/Implantable Sensor Tier)** — Acquires biosignals such as ECG, PPG, SpO₂, body temperature, blood pressure, glucose, and galvanic skin response (GSR).
2. **Network Layer (Communication Tier)** — Forwards the digitized biosignals using low-power protocols such as BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy), Zigbee, LoRaWAN, or NB-IoT.
3. **Application Layer (Cloud + Analytics Tier)** — Performs feature extraction, anomaly detection, threshold alerting, and visualization for clinicians/caregivers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *“A Health Monitoring System in IoT is a real-time, sensor-driven, networked framework that monitors vital signs of patients and transmits the data to remote servers for storage, processing, and clinical decision support.”*

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your body is a **factory** running 24×7. The **health monitoring IoT system is the supervisor standing at the control room**. Tiny workers (sensors) are placed on every machine (lungs, heart, skin) to record how hard the machine is working. These workers (sensors) whisper their readings to a **foreman** (microcontroller like ESP32 / Arduino), who then radios (BLE / Wi-Fi) the information to the **head office** (cloud server). If a machine starts overheating, the head office immediately calls the **factory manager** (doctor) on his phone via SMS/app push notification.

- **Wearable sensors** = workers on machines
- **Microcontroller (e.g., ESP32, Raspberry Pi, Arduino Uno)** = foreman
- **Wireless protocol (BLE/Zigbee/Wi-Fi)** = radio
- **Cloud / Edge server** = head office
- **Mobile / Web dashboard** = manager's screen

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics You Must Know (Bold Highlights):**
> - Normal Heart Rate: **60–100 bpm** (beats per minute)
> - Normal SpO₂: **95–100%**
> - Normal Body Temperature: **36.1°C – 37.2°C** (oral)
> - Normal Systolic BP: **120 mmHg**
> - Normal Diastolic BP: **80 mmHg**
> - Sampling Frequency for ECG: **250–500 Hz**
> - Sampling Frequency for PPG: **50–100 Hz**

## KTU-Mandated Components of an IoT HMS

| Component | Role | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Biosensor | Analog signal acquisition | AD8232 (ECG), MAX30102 (PPG/SpO₂), DS18B20 (temperature) |
| MCU | Signal conditioning + ADC | Arduino Uno, ESP32, STM32, Raspberry Pi Pico |
| Wireless Module | Data transmission | HC-05, nRF24L01, ESP32 built-in BLE, SIM800 (GSM) |
| Gateway | Protocol bridging | Smartphone, Raspberry Pi, Edge router |
| Cloud Platform | Storage + analytics | AWS IoT, ThingSpeak, Firebase, Ubidots |
| Application | Visualization + alerts | Android app, Web dashboard, SMS gateway |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The end-to-end data lifecycle of an IoT Health Monitoring System follows a deterministic 7-stage pipeline:

1. **Signal Acquisition (Stage 1)** — The biosensor transduces a physiological phenomenon (e.g., heart contraction) into an analog electrical signal. The amplitude of this signal is typically in the order of **mV (millivolts)**, which is why amplification is mandatory.

2. **Signal Conditioning (Stage 2)** — The raw analog signal is passed through:
   - **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — boosts μV/mV signals to 0–3.3V range.
   - **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** — removes baseline wander (< 0.5 Hz) and high-frequency noise (> 40 Hz for ECG).
   - **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz)** — eliminates powerline interference.

3. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Stage 3)** — The conditioned signal is digitized by the MCU's **ADC** (e.g., ESP32 has 12-bit ADC; resolution = 3.3V / 4096 ≈ **0.806 mV/LSB**).

4. **Local Processing (Stage 4)** — Edge MCU performs:
   - **Peak detection** (R-peak in ECG).
   - **Thresholding** (alert generation if HR > 120 bpm).
   - **Data compression** (reduces wireless payload).

5. **Wireless Transmission (Stage 5)** — Digitized packets are transmitted using BLE/Wi-Fi. For long-range, **LoRaWAN** offers ranges up to **10–15 km** (rural) with low power.

6. **Cloud Ingestion (Stage 6)** — MQTT/HTTP POST pushes JSON payloads to a cloud broker (e.g., AWS IoT Core, Mosquitto).

7. **Application Layer Analytics (Stage 7)** — ML/DL models (LSTM, Random Forest) perform classification (e.g., arrhythmia, sleep apnea) and push alerts to caregivers.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield and frequently tested in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $HR = \frac{60}{T_{R-R}}$ | Heart Rate (bpm) from R-R interval | bpm |
| 2 | $SpO_2 = \frac{C_{HbO_2}}{C_{HbO_2} + C_{Hb}} \times 100$ | Oxygen saturation ratio | % |
| 3 | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ (Beer-Lambert) | Light absorbance through tissue | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R = \frac{AC_{red}}{AC_{ir}}$ | Ratio of AC components (PPG) | ratio |
| 5 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Minimum sampling frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot t_i$ | Total energy consumption of wearable | Joules (J) |
| 7 | $Resolution_{ADC} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | ADC quantization step | V/LSB |
| 8 | $I_{avg} = \frac{I_{active} \cdot t_{on} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{off}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Average current (battery life) | mA |
| 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 10 | $P_{tx} = P_{rx} + 20\log_{10}(d) + PL_0$ | Path loss (wireless link budget) | dBm |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology** — Real-time ECG telemetry for post-MI (myocardial infarction) patients reduces hospital readmission by **~38%** (per Mayo Clinic trials).
- **Diabetology** — Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGM) like Dexcom G7 use subcutaneous enzymatic sensors.
- **Elderly Care** — Fall detection via accelerometer + gyroscope fusion (Madgwick filter).
- **Sports Physiology** — VO₂ max estimation using heart rate + accelerometer data.
- **Pandemic Response** — COVID-19 SpO₂ remote monitoring reduced ICU burden during 2020–2022.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Heart Rate from R-R Interval (ECG)

The **R-peak** in an ECG is the most prominent feature corresponding to ventricular depolarization. The time between two consecutive R-peaks is called the **R-R interval** ($T_{R-R}$), measured in seconds.

**Step 1:** Record ECG signal and isolate R-peaks using Pan-Tompkins algorithm.

**Step 2:** Compute the time difference between two consecutive R-peaks:

$$
T_{R-R} = t_{R_{n+1}} - t_{R_{n}}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert this inter-beat interval to beats per minute (bpm). Since $T_{R-R}$ is the time for **one** beat, the number of beats in 60 seconds is:

$$
HR = \frac{60}{T_{R-R}}
$$

**Step 4:** For n beats, average the R-R intervals to improve robustness:

$$
\overline{T}_{R-R} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} T_{R-R}(k)
$$

$$
HR_{avg} = \frac{60}{\overline{T}_{R-R}}
$$

**Numerical Example (Board-Standard):**
If $T_{R-R} = 0.86$ seconds, then:

$$
HR = \frac{60}{0.86} \approx 69.77 \approx 70 \text{ bpm}
$$

This is in the **normal resting range (60–100 bpm)**.

---

## Derivation 2: SpO₂ Calculation Using Beer-Lambert Law

Pulse oximetry exploits the differential light absorption of **oxygenated hemoglobin (HbO₂)** and **deoxygenated hemoglobin (Hb)** at two wavelengths: **660 nm (Red)** and **940 nm (Infrared)**.

**Step 1:** Apply Beer-Lambert Law to light transmission through tissue:

$$
I_{out} = I_{in} \cdot e^{-\varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l}
$$

where $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient, $c$ = concentration, $l$ = path length.

**Step 2:** The absorbance $A$ is:

$$
A = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{in}}{I_{out}}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l
$$

**Step 3:** In PPG (photoplethysmography), the signal has two components: a large DC component (tissue/bone) and a small AC component (pulsatile arterial blood):

$$
A_{total} = A_{DC} + A_{AC}
$$

**Step 4:** The ratio of AC components at the two wavelengths is the **R value**:

$$
R = \frac{A_{AC}^{red}}{A_{AC}^{ir}}
$$

**Step 5:** Empirically, SpO₂ is calibrated (per the manufacturer's curve) as:

$$
SpO_2 \approx 110 - 25 \cdot R
$$

**Step 6:** For healthy adult, typical $R \approx 0.4$ to $0.7$, giving $SpO_2 \approx 96\%$ to $100\%$.

---

## Derivation 3: Battery Life of a Wearable IoT HMS

**Step 1:** Identify current draws in each operating state (ESP32 with BLE example):

$$
I_{active} = 80 \text{ mA}, \quad I_{sleep} = 10 \text{ \mu A}
$$

**Step 2:** Define duty cycle. Suppose MCU is active for $t_{on} = 1$ second and sleeps for $t_{off} = 9$ seconds (cycle $T_{cycle} = 10$ s).

**Step 3:** Compute average current:

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{I_{active} \cdot t_{on} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{off}}{T_{cycle}}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{80 \times 1 + 0.01 \times 9}{10} = 8.009 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4:** For a 1000 mAh battery, battery life in hours is:

$$
t_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{1000}{8.009} \approx 124.86 \text{ hours} \approx 5.2 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to days: $124.86 / 24 \approx 5.2$ days.

---

## Derivation 4: Nyquist Sampling Theorem for ECG

The ECG signal contains clinically relevant frequencies up to **40 Hz** (some applications up to 150 Hz for pediatric / high-resolution).

**Step 1:** Per Nyquist theorem:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $f_{max} = 40$ Hz:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3:** However, KTU/medical standards mandate a **safety factor of 2.5× to 3×**, hence:

$$
f_s = 250 \text{ Hz to } 500 \text{ Hz}
$$

This is why the AD8232 ECG module is typically configured with $f_s = 500$ Hz.

---

## Derivation 5: ADC Resolution Calculation

For an ESP32 with 12-bit ADC and $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V:

$$
Resolution_{ADC} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

This means the smallest detectable voltage change is ~0.8 mV — adequate for ECG which has mV-range amplitudes.

---

## Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Health Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heart Rate | HR | 60–100 bpm | Cardiovascular fitness |
| R-R Interval | $T_{R-R}$ | 0.6–1.0 s | Beat-to-beat variability (HRV) |
| SpO₂ | SaO₂ | 95–100% | Pulmonary function |
| Body Temp | T | 36.1–37.2°C | Metabolic / infection indicator |
| Systolic BP | SBP | 120 mmHg | Arterial pressure peak |
| Diastolic BP | DBP | 80 mmHg | Arterial pressure trough |
| Sampling Rate | $f_s$ | 250–500 Hz | Signal fidelity (Nyquist) |
| Battery Life | $t_{life}$ | 5–7 days | Usability of wearable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
