# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define a Smart Waste Management System (SWMS). List the four layers of the IoT architecture as applied to SWMS.**

**Model Answer (concise, 3-mark weight):**

A Smart Waste Management System is an IoT-driven municipal infrastructure that uses **sensor-equipped dustbins** to monitor waste levels in real time, transmitting the data to a cloud platform for **dynamic route optimization** and **predictive collection**.

The four layers:

1. **Perception Layer** — ultrasonic, weight, and gas sensors mounted on bins.
2. **Network Layer** — LoRaWAN / NB-IoT carrying **MQTT** packets.
3. **Processing Layer** — cloud engines for thresholding, storage, and route optimization.
4. **Application Layer** — dashboards, mobile apps, and SMS alerts.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four layers with one example each — matches KTU's 3-mark valuation rubric.)*

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Why is MQTT preferred over HTTP for transmitting smart bin data over LoRaWAN? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer (concise, 3-mark weight):**

1. **Low overhead:** MQTT's fixed 2-byte header vs. HTTP's ~200-byte header. Critical when LoRaWAN airtime is *purchased per byte* and battery is *limited per mAh*.
2. **Publish/Subscribe model:** The bin does not need to know the dispatcher's IP — it just **publishes** to a topic on the broker. This decouples producers and consumers, ideal for **many-to-many** smart city traffic.
3. **Quality of Service (QoS) levels:** QoS 1 and 2 guarantee delivery — important for **alert messages** that must not be lost.

*(Any 2 of the above for full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A.** [KTU ESE Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end architecture of a Smart Waste Management System**, identifying the sensors, communication protocol, cloud platform, and application interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **ultrasonic sensor HC-SR04** is mounted on the inner lid of a dustbin whose **total internal height is 1.2 m**. The **ambient temperature is 35 °C**. The sensor measures an **echo time of 3.5 ms**. Determine:
- (i) the speed of sound at 35 °C,
- (ii) the one-way distance from the sensor to the waste surface,
- (iii) the **fill-level percentage** of the bin, and
- (iv) whether an alert will be triggered for the **default threshold of 75%**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The SWMS architecture has **four canonical layers**:

1. **Perception Layer** (1.5 marks):
   - **HC-SR04** ultrasonic sensor (lid-mounted) → fill level
   - **Load cell + HX711** (under floor) → weight
   - **MQ-4** (gas sensor) → methane detection
   - **DHT22** → temperature, humidity
   - **NEO-6M GPS** → bin geolocation
   - All wired to an **ESP32** microcontroller.

2. **Network Layer** (1.5 marks):
   - **LoRaWAN (SX1276)** for bins-to-gateway uplink.
   - **MQTT** as the application protocol.
   - Wi-Fi/4G backhaul from gateway to cloud.

3. **Processing Layer** (2 marks):
   - **AWS IoT Core** or open-source **ThingsBoard** ingests MQTT messages.
   - **InfluxDB** stores time-series data.
   - A **threshold engine** fires when fill level ≥ 75%.
   - A **route optimizer** (Google OR-Tools / nearest-neighbour) reorders bins.

4. **Application Layer** (2 marks):
   - **Web dashboard** with a city heat-map of bin fill levels.
   - **Mobile app** for truck drivers showing optimized route.
   - **SMS/push** to citizens and ward councillors.

**Diagram:** *(reuse the Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram; in written exam, draw a clean 4-row block diagram with arrows showing data flow from bin → gateway → cloud → app.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Speed of sound at 35 °C:**

$$
v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 35 = 331 + 21 = 352 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) One-way distance from sensor to waste surface:**

$$
d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2} = \frac{352 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{1.232}{2} = 0.616 \ \text{m} = 61.6 \ \text{cm}
$$

**(iii) Fill-level percentage:**

$$
\text{Fill (\%)} = \frac{H_{bin} - d}{H_{bin}} \times 100 = \frac{1.2 - 0.616}{1.2} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Fill (\%)} = \frac{0.584}{1.2} \times 100 \approx 48.67\%
$$

**(iv) Alert decision:**

Since $48.67\% < 75\%$, **no alert is triggered**. The bin can wait for the next collection cycle.

---

### **Question B-B.** [KTU ESE Model — 14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **role of sensors, microcontrollers, and communication protocols** in a smart dustbin node. Justify why **LoRaWAN + MQTT** is preferred over **Wi-Fi + HTTP** for large-scale municipal deployment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traditional waste-collection ward makes **12 trips per week** using blind fixed routes. After deploying an SWMS, the same ward requires only **7 trips per week**. If each trip costs the municipality **₹1,500** in fuel and wages:
- (i) compute the **percentage reduction** in trips,
- (ii) compute the **weekly monetary savings**,
- (iii) compute the **annual savings**, and
- (iv) comment on the **environmental impact** of the smart system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Component | Role in Smart Bin | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Ultrasonic / Load cell** | Perceive physical state of bin | Battery-friendly, privacy-preserving |
| **ESP32** | Aggregate, package, sleep-cycle | Low-power dual-core, Wi-Fi + BT built-in |
| **LoRaWAN SX1276** | Long-range uplink | 2–5 km urban range; <50 mA TX current |
| **MQTT broker** | Pub/sub messaging | 2-byte header; decouples clients; QoS levels |

**Why LoRaWAN + MQTT > Wi-Fi + HTTP for a city-scale deployment:**

- **Power:** LoRaWAN TX current ≈ 40 mA vs. Wi-Fi TX ≈ 250 mA → 6× longer battery life.
- **Range:** LoRaWAN 2–5 km vs. Wi-Fi ~30 m → **one gateway covers 1000s of bins**.
- **Cost:** No need for Wi-Fi hotspots at every bin; **saves municipal CAPEX**.
- **Header efficiency:** MQTT 2 B vs. HTTP ~200 B → 100× less airtime per message.
- **Scalability:** Pub/sub model of MQTT handles **fan-out to multiple consumers** (dashboard, route engine, analytics) without the bin knowing.

*(7 marks = 2 marks for component roles + 5 marks for the comparative justification.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Percentage reduction in trips:**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{12 - 7}{12} \times 100 = \frac{5}{12} \times 100 \approx 41.67\%
$$

**(ii) Weekly monetary savings:**

$$
\text{Savings}_{weekly} = (12 - 7) \times 1500 = 5 \times 1500 = \text{₹ } 7{,}500
$$

**(iii) Annual savings:**

$$
\text{Savings}_{annual} = 7500 \times 52 = \text{₹ } 3{,}90{,}000
$$

**(iv) Environmental impact comment (model 2-mark answer):**

A 41.67% reduction in trips translates to a **proportional reduction in diesel consumption**, hence **lower CO₂ emissions** and **reduced air pollution** in the ward. Additionally, **fewer overflowing bins** mean **less leachate** contaminating groundwater, and **faster response to hazardous gas accumulation** (methane) prevents **fire/explosion risk** in densely populated Kerala wards. The smart system thus contributes to **UN Sustainable Development Goal 11 (Sustainable Cities)** and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in SWMS Questions (Module 10, PBITT604):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting temperature compensation in $v_{sound}$.** Many students use the constant 343 m/s in numericals. If the problem states a non-25 °C temperature, you **must** apply $v = 331 + 0.6T$. Using 343 m/s at 35 °C loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing *fill level* with *fill volume*.** Fill level is a dimensionless percentage; fill volume is litres × 100. KTU valuation keys explicitly differentiate these. **Read the question carefully.**
> 3. **Justifying MQTT only with "it is lightweight".** A complete answer must mention the **pub/sub architecture**, the **2-byte header**, and **QoS levels** to secure full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring privacy when suggesting camera-based fill detection.** Examiners will deduct at least **1 mark** for this.
> 5. **Skipping the deep-sleep / duty-cycle discussion** when asked about battery life. Always mention **ESP32 deep sleep (10 µA) + RTC wake** for full credit.
> 6. **Not converting echo time correctly.** Echo time is the *round-trip* time. Forgetting to divide by 2 in the distance equation is a classic 1-mark error.
> 7. **Writing the JSON payload structure incorrectly** in 14-mark design questions. A correct answer includes fields like `{ "binId": "...", "fill": 87, "weight": 34, "lat": 9.98, "lng": 76.28, "ts": 1717000000 }`.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Punch-List for SWMS — Module 10, PBITT604:**
>
> - **SWMS** = IoT-based real-time monitoring of municipal solid waste using smart bins.
> - **Architecture:** 4 layers — Perception, Network, Processing, Application.
> - **Core sensors:** HC-SR04 (ultrasonic), HX711 (load cell), MQ-4 (methane), NEO-6M (GPS), DHT22 (T/RH).
> - **Microcontroller of choice:** **ESP32** (Wi-Fi + BT, deep sleep, low cost).
> - **Communication:** **LoRaWAN (long-range, low-power) + MQTT (lightweight pub/sub).**
> - **Avoid:** HTTP, cameras, Wi-Fi for static bins, continuous transmission.
> - **Key formulas (must memorize):**
>   - $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$  (one-way distance)
>   - $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 T$  (temperature-compensated)
>   - $\text{Fill \%} = \dfrac{H_{bin} - d}{H_{bin}} \times 100$
>   - $\text{Trip Reduction \%} = \dfrac{N_{trad} - N_{smart}}{N_{trad}} \times 100$
> - **Default alert threshold:** **75% fill level** (configurable 60–85%).
> - **Battery target:** **3–5 years** with deep sleep + RTC wake.
> - **Cloud platform examples:** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, ThingsBoard (open-source), Node-RED + Mosquitto.
> - **Privacy mantra:** **No cameras in dustbins.**
> - **SDG alignment:** SWMS supports **SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities)** and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**.
> - **Real-world Kerala context:** Suchitwa Mission + Smart City Kochi + Thiruvananthapuram Corporation pilot deployments.
> - **Exam tip:** Always draw a **clean 4-layer block diagram** in every 14-mark SWMS question — examiners award **1–2 marks** purely for the diagram.
> - **JSON payload must contain:** `binId`, `fill`, `weight`, `lat`, `lng`, `timestamp`, `battery`.
> - **MQTT QoS for alerts:** use **QoS 1 (at-least-once)** to ensure the dispatcher never misses a pickup alert.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT-based Smart Parking System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 11
> - **Topic:** IoT-based Smart Parking System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT-based Smart Parking System (ISPS):** A cyber-physical system that integrates **physical sensing infrastructure** (IR, ultrasonic, magnetic, RFID sensors), **edge/embedded controllers** (Arduino, ESP8266, ESP32, Raspberry Pi), **wireless communication protocols** (Wi-Fi, LoRa, MQTT, HTTP/CoAP), and **cloud-edge analytics** to perform **real-time detection, monitoring, reservation, and dynamic allocation** of vehicular parking slots in a defined geographic region.

From the KTU 2024 OBE perspective, the system is classified under the **"Smart City / Smart Environment"** application vertical of IoT, mapping directly to **Course Outcome CO5** — *Design and develop IoT applications for real-world use cases using appropriate hardware, protocols, and cloud services.*

### Key Standardized Metrics (KTU Board Weight)
| Metric | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Slot Detection Accuracy | **% (percentage)** | 95 – 99 % |
| Latency (Sensor → Cloud → App) | **ms (milliseconds)** | 200 – 1500 ms |
| Sensor Sampling Rate | **Hz (Hertz)** | 1 – 10 Hz |
| Communication Range | **m (meters)** | 10 – 500 m |
| Power Consumption | **mW (milliwatts)** | 50 – 500 mW |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Mall Directory + Walkie-Talkie + Smart Map" Analogy**

Imagine you walk into a giant multi-floor shopping mall. Before IoT, you had to **drive around randomly**, hunting for an empty spot, wasting fuel and time (this is the *traditional sensor-less parking* model).

Now imagine three upgrades combined:
1. **A digital "Availability Board" at every entry** → showing green/red lights per slot (this is the **sensor layer** — ultrasonic/IR detectors).
2. **A walkie-talkie connecting every board to a central control room** → updating the board in real time (this is the **network layer** — MQTT/HTTP over Wi-Fi).
3. **A live map on your smartphone** that tells you "Slot B-7 is free, drive 120 m, then turn left" (this is the **application layer** — cloud dashboard + mobile app).

The **IoT-based Smart Parking System = the digital board + walkie-talkie + smart map working together as one automated system.** You do not "search" anymore — the system *finds and reserves the slot for you.*

---

## 1.3 Why This System Matters (Engineering Motivation)

- **Traffic congestion reduction:** Studies (e.g., SFpark pilot) show up to **30 %** reduction in cruising-for-parking traffic.
- **Fuel economy:** Drivers save an average of **7.8 minutes** per trip → ~**0.45 L of fuel saved per parking event.**
- **Emission control:** Direct reduction in CO₂, NOx, and PM2.5 emissions.
- **Revenue optimization:** Dynamic pricing models increase parking lot revenue by **15 – 25 %.**
- **Scalability:** One cloud dashboard can manage **hundreds of geographically distributed lots.**

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> This topic falls under **Module 11 – IoT Applications (Smart Use Cases)** and contributes to the following Course Outcomes:
> - **CO5:** Design IoT-based solutions for real-world problems.
> - **CO6:** Evaluate the performance and security of deployed IoT systems.
> - **Bloom's Levels Covered:** L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — 4-Layer IoT Reference Model

The ISPS is conventionally broken into **four logical layers** as per the KTU 2024 IoT reference architecture:

### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer
This is the **physical contact layer** with the parking environment.

**Core Function:** Convert physical phenomena (vehicle presence, distance, identification) into electrical signals.

**Components:**
- **Ultrasonic sensors** (HC-SR04) — measure distance to vehicle by echoing 40 kHz pulses.
- **Infrared (IR) proximity sensors** — detect object presence via IR reflection.
- **Inductive loop detectors** — buried under pavement; detect metallic mass.
- **RFID readers + tags** — vehicle identification, automated billing.
- **IP cameras (with CV)** — license plate recognition, occupancy counting.

**Why this layer matters:** Without accurate sensing, the entire pipeline becomes unreliable. KTU 2024 emphasizes this as the *Data Acquisition* foundation.

---

### Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer
This is the **data transport layer**, ferrying sensor data to the edge/cloud.

**Core Function:** Reliable, low-latency transmission of structured packets.

**Protocols Used:**
| Protocol | Layer | Use Case | Payload Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MQTT** | App (TCP) | Lightweight pub/sub, ideal for constrained devices | Min 2 bytes |
| **HTTP/REST** | App (TCP) | Web dashboards, slow but universal | ~ KB |
| **CoAP** | App (UDP) | Constrained RESTful, very low overhead | Min 4 bytes |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11)** | Link | Short-range, high bandwidth | 54 Mbps+ |
| **LoRaWAN** | Link | Long-range, low-power (km range) | 0.3 – 50 kbps |
| **Zigbee (802.15.4)** | Link | Mesh network inside parking structure | 250 kbps |

---

### Layer 3 — Processing / Edge-Cloud Layer
This is the **decision-making brain** of the system.

**Core Function:** Aggregate, store, analyze, and act on sensor data.

**Components:**
- **Edge gateway** (Raspberry Pi, ESP32) — pre-processes data, runs lightweight ML.
- **Cloud platform** (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, ThingSpeak, Blynk) — long-term storage, analytics, dashboards.
- **Database** (InfluxDB, MongoDB, Firebase RTDB) — time-series and real-time data.
- **Analytics engine** — occupancy prediction, dynamic pricing, anomaly detection.

---

### Layer 4 — Application / User Layer
This is the **human interaction layer**.

**Core Function:** Present processed information to end-users and operators.

**Components:**
- **Mobile applications** (Android/iOS) — slot search, reservation, payment.
- **Web dashboards** — admin view, lot analytics, revenue reports.
- **Digital display boards** at lot entry/exit.
- **APIs** for third-party integration (Google Maps, payment gateways).

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8-Step Data Flow (High-Yield KTU Board Topic)**

1. **Sensor Activation:** Ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz burst via the trigger pin.
2. **Echo Reception:** The echo pin receives the reflected wave after time *t*.
3. **Distance Calculation:** Microcontroller computes distance using speed of sound.
4. **Threshold Check:** If distance < threshold (e.g., 30 cm), the slot is marked **OCCUPIED**.
5. **Data Packaging:** The MCU creates a JSON payload `{slot_id: "B-07", status: 1, timestamp: ...}`.
6. **Network Transmission:** Published to MQTT broker topic `parking/lot1/slot/B07`.
7. **Cloud Ingestion:** Cloud subscriber updates the database and pushes notification via FCM/APNs.
8. **User Action:** Mobile app shows updated map; user reserves and navigates to the slot.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Board Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most-tested formulas** for IoT parking problems in KTU 2024 scheme.

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | distance, speed of sound, echo time | m, m/s, s | Ultrasonic distance measurement |
| 2 | $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | speed of sound at temperature T | m/s, °C | Temperature compensation |
| 3 | $\eta_{detection} = \dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | detection accuracy | % | System performance |
| 4 | $\tau_{end-to-end} = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{cloud} + \tau_{app}$ | total latency | ms | Real-time validation |
| 5 | $E_{node} = P_{active} \cdot t_{active} + P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}$ | node energy budget | J, mW, s | Battery life estimation |
| 6 | $C_{park} = N_{slots} \cdot P_{hourly} \cdot T_{avg}$ | parking revenue | ₹, hrs | Dynamic pricing model |
| 7 | $D_{user} = \sqrt{(x_u - x_s)^2 + (y_u - y_s)^2}$ | user-to-slot distance | m | Navigation |
| 8 | $L_{path} = \alpha \cdot d + \beta \cdot N_{hops}$ | path loss model | dB | LoRa/Wi-Fi coverage |
| 9 | $T_{throughput} = \dfrac{N_{msgs}}{t_{window}}$ | system throughput | msg/s | Network capacity |
| 10 | $S_{utilization} = \dfrac{N_{occupied}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | slot utilization | % | Lot efficiency |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart City Deployments:** Barcelona, Singapore, San Francisco (SFpark).
- **Airports & Transit Hubs:** Real-time slot booking, EV charging integration.
- **Hospitals:** Priority slots for emergency vehicles.
- **Universities/IT Parks:** Reserved faculty/student slots with RFID-based access.
- **Commercial Complexes:** Integration with loyalty apps, dynamic surge pricing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Distance Calculation (Board-Favorite)

**Problem Context:** A KTU exam asks you to derive the distance formula used in HC-SR04 ultrasonic parking sensors and compute a numerical example.

### Given Information
- Speed of sound in air at room temperature: $v = 343$ m/s
- Measured echo time: $t = 580$ µs $= 580 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- Temperature: $T = 25$ °C

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Establish the physical principle.**
An ultrasonic sensor transmits a sound pulse that travels to the nearest obstacle (vehicle), reflects, and returns to the receiver. The pulse therefore covers the **same distance twice** (there and back).

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{d_{total}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the basic kinematic relation.**
Total distance traveled by the sound wave is speed multiplied by time.

$$
d_{total} = v \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two equations to isolate one-way distance.**

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

This is the **governing equation** for the ultrasonic parking sensor.

**Step 4 — Apply temperature compensation.**
Speed of sound depends on air temperature as:

$$
v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T
$$

At $T = 25$ °C:

$$
v = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331 + 15 = 346 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{346 \times 580 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify numerator.**

$$
346 \times 580 = 200{,}680
$$

$$
200{,}680 \times 10^{-6} = 0.20068 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7 — Final division.**

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{0.20068}{2} = 0.10034 \text{ m} \approx 10.03 \text{ cm}
$$

### Final Result

$$
d_{one-way} \approx 10.03 \text{ cm}
$$

Since this is **less than the typical 30 cm threshold**, the slot is classified as **OCCUPIED**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Detection Accuracy (System Reliability Metric)

**Problem Context:** During a 24-hour monitoring window, 12,400 detection events were recorded. 12,030 were correctly classified. Compute the detection accuracy.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the accuracy formula.**

$$
\eta_{detection} = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$
\eta_{detection} = \frac{12{,}030}{12{,}400} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{12{,}030}{12{,}400} = 0.97016
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
\eta_{detection} = 0.97016 \times 100 = 97.02 \,\%
$$

### Final Result

$$
\eta_{detection} \approx 97.02 \,\%
$$

This exceeds the KTU-acceptable threshold of **95 %**, hence the system is **deployment-ready.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Latency Budget

**Problem Context:** A KTU 2024 question asks you to estimate the total latency from sensor trigger to mobile app update. Given:
- Sensor sampling time: $\tau_{sense} = 50$ ms
- Wi-Fi transmission time: $\tau_{tx} = 120$ ms
- Cloud processing time: $\tau_{cloud} = 300$ ms
- App push notification: $\tau_{app} = 80$ ms

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the total latency equation.**

$$
\tau_{end-to-end} = \tau_{sense} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{cloud} + \tau_{app}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute all four components.**

$$
\tau_{end-to-end} = 50 + 120 + 300 + 80
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sum.**

$$
\tau_{end-to-end} = 550 \text{ ms}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\tau_{end-to-end} = 550 \text{ ms} = 0.55 \text{ s}
$$

This is well within the **human-perceivable real-time threshold of 1 second**, so the user experience will feel "live."

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parking Lot Revenue Optimization

**Problem Context:** A lot has $N = 200$ slots, hourly rate $P = 50$ ₹, and average occupancy duration $T = 2.5$ hrs. Compute daily revenue and revenue gain at 80 % utilization.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall revenue equation.**

$$
C_{park} = N_{slots} \cdot P_{hourly} \cdot T_{avg} \cdot U
$$

where $U$ is utilization.

**Step 2 — Compute full-utilization revenue ($U = 1$).**

$$
C_{full} = 200 \times 50 \times 2.5 = 25{,}000 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute 80 %-utilization revenue ($U = 0.8$).**

$$
C_{80} = 200 \times 50 \times 2.5 \times 0.8 = 20{,}000 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute revenue gain if smart system pushes utilization from 60 % to 85 %.**

Old revenue: $C_{60} = 25{,}000 \times 0.6 = 15{,}000$ ₹
New revenue: $C_{85} = 25{,}000 \times 0.85 = 21{,}250$ ₹

$$
\Delta C = 21{,}250 - 15{,}000 = 6{,}250 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\Delta C = 6{,}250 \text{ ₹/day} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \approx 22.8 \text{ Lakh ₹/year}
$$

This is the **business case** for deploying ISPS — a frequent Part-B KTU question theme.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison for ISPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is a **high-yield KTU exam matrix** — directly testable as "compare and contrast."

| Parameter | Ultrasonic (HC-SR04) | IR Proximity | Inductive Loop | Camera + CV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 2 cm – 4 m | 10 – 80 cm | Vehicle-on-loop only | 5 – 50 m |
| Accuracy | ± 1 cm | ± 5 mm | ± 99 % | ± 95 % |
| Cost | ₹ 80 – 150 | ₹ 40 – 100 | ₹ 3000 – 8000 | ₹ 4000+ |
| Weather Robust | Medium | Low | High | Medium |
| Power | 15 mA | 20 mA | Passive | 200 – 500 mA |
| KTU Use Case | Indoor lots | Gate barriers | Outdoor lots | Premium lots |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Communication Protocol Selection

| Parameter | MQTT | HTTP/REST | CoAP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | TCP | UDP |
| Header Size | 2 bytes | ~ 200 bytes | 4 bytes |
| Power Efficiency | **High** | Low | **High** |
| Reliability | QoS 0/1/2 | Built-in | Confirmable / Non-confirmable |
| KTU Verdict | **Best for ISPS** | Web dashboards only | Constrained sensors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — End-to-End Slot Detection Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant US as Ultrasonic Sensor
    participant MC as ESP32 Microcontroller
    participant MQ as MQTT Broker
    participant CL as Cloud Platform
    participant DB as Database InfluxDB
    participant AN as Analytics Engine
    participant AP as Mobile App
    participant USR as End User

    US->>MC: Trigger 10us pulse, receive echo
    MC->>MC: Compute distance d using d = v*t/2
    MC->>MC: Compare d with threshold 30cm
    MC->>MQ: Publish JSON {slot_id, status, ts} to parking/lot1/slot
    MQ->>CL: Forward payload via subscribe
    CL->>DB: Write to time-series database
    CL->>AN: Run occupancy prediction algorithm
    AN-->>AP: Push updated availability via FCM
    AP->>USR: Display real-time map with nearest free slot
    USR->>AP: User reserves slot B-07
    AP->>CL: Reservation request POST /api/reserve
    CL->>DB: Mark slot B-07 as RESERVED
    CL-->>AP: Confirmation with navigation path
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Parking Slot Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FREE
    FREE --> OCCUPIED: Vehicle detected by sensor
    OCCUPIED --> FREE: Vehicle departs timeout exceeded
    FREE --> RESERVED: User reserves via app
    RESERVED --> OCCUPIED: Reserved vehicle arrives within window
    RESERVED --> FREE: Reservation timeout exceeded
    OCCUPIED --> MAINTENANCE: Admin flags for maintenance
    MAINTENANCE --> FREE: Maintenance complete
    MAINTENANCE --> [*]: Slot decommissioned
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each question carries **3 marks**. Answer in 80 – 100 words.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an IoT-based Smart Parking System. List its four functional layers.**

**Model Answer:**
An IoT-based Smart Parking System (ISPS) is a cyber-physical system that uses sensors, microcontrollers, communication protocols, and cloud platforms to perform **real-time detection, monitoring, reservation, and dynamic allocation** of parking slots.

The four functional layers are:
1. **Perception/Sensing Layer** — sensors (ultrasonic, IR, RFID).
2. **Network/Communication Layer** — Wi-Fi, LoRa, MQTT, HTTP/CoAP.
3. **Processing/Cloud Layer** — edge gateways, databases, analytics.
4. **Application/User Layer** — mobile apps, dashboards, display boards.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for layers with examples.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain why MQTT is preferred over HTTP for parking sensor data transmission.**

**Model Answer:**
MQTT is preferred because:
1. **Lightweight header** (~2 bytes) vs HTTP (~200 bytes) — saves bandwidth.
2. **Publish/Subscribe model** decouples sensors from apps, ideal for many-to-one IoT topology.
3. **QoS levels (0, 1, 2)** provide tunable reliability.
4. **Low power consumption** — extends battery life of sensor nodes.
5. **Works over TCP** ensuring reliable delivery for critical occupancy updates.

HTTP/REST is reserved for less-frequent admin requests (reservations, payments).

*(3 marks: 3 of the 5 points explained.)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question has internal choice. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks.**

---

### **🔷 QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **architecture of an IoT-based Smart Parking System** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of each layer.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
An IoT-based Smart Parking System (ISPS) follows the standard 4-layer IoT reference architecture — Perception, Network, Processing, and Application — to enable real-time, automated parking management.

**Layer 1 — Perception/Sensing Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Ultrasonic sensors (HC-SR04)** detect vehicle presence by emitting 40 kHz pulses and measuring echo time.
- **IR sensors** provide low-cost binary detection.
- **Inductive loops** detect metallic vehicles for outdoor lots.
- **Cameras with CV** enable license plate recognition and occupancy counting.
- **Role:** Convert physical world (vehicle presence) into electrical/digital signals.

**Layer 2 — Network/Communication Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Wi-Fi (802.11)** for indoor lots with line-of-sight to gateway.
- **LoRaWAN** for large outdoor/multi-floor lots with km range.
- **MQTT** as the messaging protocol — lightweight pub/sub, QoS-enabled.
- **HTTP/REST** for occasional reservations and payment API calls.
- **Role:** Reliable, low-latency transport of sensor data from edge to cloud.

**Layer 3 — Processing/Edge-Cloud Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Edge gateway** (ESP32, Raspberry Pi) preprocesses and filters noise.
- **Cloud platform** (AWS IoT, ThingSpeak, Blynk) stores, analyzes, and runs ML models for prediction.
- **Time-series database** (InfluxDB) for historical analysis.
- **Role:** Aggregate, analyze, and decide on data (e.g., which slot to allocate).

**Layer 4 — Application/User Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Mobile app** — slot search, reservation, navigation, payment.
- **Admin web dashboard** — lot analytics, revenue, fault monitoring.
- **Digital display boards** at lot entry.
- **Role:** Human-machine interface and value delivery.

**Conclusion (Optional):** A well-designed ISPS reduces search time by ~40 % and increases revenue by 15 – 25 %.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **ultrasonic distance formula** used in HC-SR04 sensors. An echo time of 850 µs is measured at 30 °C. Compute the distance to the vehicle and state whether the slot is **occupied or free** (threshold = 35 cm).

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Step 1 — Sound pulse travels to vehicle and back, covering twice the one-way distance:

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{d_{total}}{2}
$$

Step 2 — Total distance is speed × time:

$$
d_{total} = v_{sound} \cdot t
$$

Step 3 — Combine:

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}
$$

Step 4 — Speed of sound depends on temperature:

$$
v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T
$$

**Numerical Computation (3.5 marks):**

Step 5 — Compute speed of sound at 30 °C:

$$
v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 6 — Substitute into distance formula (convert µs to s):

$$
t = 850 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{349 \times 850 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

Step 7 — Compute numerator:

$$
349 \times 850 = 296{,}650
$$

$$
296{,}650 \times 10^{-6} = 0.29665 \text{ m}
$$

Step 8 — Final division:

$$
d_{one-way} = \frac{0.29665}{2} = 0.148325 \text{ m} \approx 14.83 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 9 — Decision:**
Since $d_{one-way} = 14.83$ cm $< 35$ cm threshold, the slot is **OCCUPIED.**

---

### **🔷 QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **ultrasonic, IR, inductive loop, and camera-based** parking sensors across the parameters: range, accuracy, cost, weather robustness, and best-fit use case.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Sensor selection is the most critical design decision in ISPS. Each sensor type has trade-offs across range, accuracy, cost, and environmental resilience.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Ultrasonic | IR | Inductive Loop | Camera + CV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 2 cm – 4 m | 10 – 80 cm | Vehicle-on-loop | 5 – 50 m |
| Accuracy | ± 1 cm | ± 5 mm | ± 99 % | ± 95 % |
| Cost (₹) | 80 – 150 | 40 – 100 | 3000 – 8000 | 4000+ |
| Weather Robust | Medium | Low | **High** | Medium |
| Power | 15 mA | 20 mA | Passive | 200 – 500 mA |
| Best Use | Indoor | Gate barriers | Outdoor lots | Premium lots |

**Analysis (1.5 marks):**
- **Ultrasonic** is the KTU board favorite for **indoor multi-floor lots** — cheap, accurate at short range, easy to interface with Arduino/ESP32.
- **IR** is **not suitable outdoors** due to sunlight interference, but works well for entry gates.
- **Inductive loops** are the **gold standard for outdoor lots** — weatherproof, high accuracy, but expensive to install (pavement cutting).
- **Cameras with CV** add **license plate recognition** but require GPU/edge inference and night lighting.

**Conclusion (Optional):** Hybrid deployments (ultrasonic + camera) are common in real-world smart cities.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** A parking lot has **300 slots, ₹ 60 hourly rate, and 2-hour average stay**. With a smart system, utilization increases from **55 % to 82 %**. Compute the **daily and annual revenue gain.** Also explain how **MQTT protocol** is used in this system with a publish-subscribe flow.

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Revenue Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Recall revenue formula:

$$
C_{park} = N_{slots} \cdot P_{hourly} \cdot T_{avg} \cdot U
$$

Step 2 — Full-utilization revenue:

$$
C_{full} = 300 \times 60 \times 2 = 36{,}000 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

Step 3 — Revenue at 55 % utilization (old system):

$$
C_{old} = 36{,}000 \times 0.55 = 19{,}800 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

Step 4 — Revenue at 82 % utilization (smart system):

$$
C_{new} = 36{,}000 \times 0.82 = 29{,}520 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

Step 5 — Daily gain:

$$
\Delta C_{daily} = 29{,}520 - 19{,}800 = 9{,}720 \text{ ₹/day}
$$

Step 6 — Annual gain:

$$
\Delta C_{annual} = 9{,}720 \times 365 = 35{,}47{,}800 \text{ ₹/year} \approx ₹ 35.48 \text{ Lakh/year}
$$

**Part 2 — MQTT Publish-Subscribe Flow (3 marks):**

- **Sensor node (Publisher):** ESP32 publishes JSON payload `{slot_id: "B-07", status: "occupied", ts: ...}` to topic `parking/lot1/slot/B07`.
- **MQTT Broker (Mosquitto/HiveMQ):** Receives the publish, filters subscribers by topic pattern.
- **Cloud subscriber:** AWS IoT Core subscribes to `parking/lot1/slot/+` (wildcard) and ingests data.
- **Mobile app (Subscriber):** Subscribes to user-specific topics and receives push updates via FCM.
- **QoS levels:** QoS 1 is used for parking status to ensure at-least-once delivery.

This decouples publishers from subscribers, allowing the system to **scale to thousands of sensors** without redesign.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in ultrasonic distance formula — students often write $d = v \cdot t$ instead of $d = v \cdot t/2$. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Not converting µs to seconds** — units are checked strictly. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Mixing up MQTT and HTTP** — students write "HTTP is lightweight" which is wrong. MQTT is the lightweight one. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in architecture questions** — KTU mandates a "neat block diagram." Without it, **lose up to 2 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — write "14.83 cm" not just "14.83." **Lose 0.5 mark per question.**
> 6. **In revenue problems** — not multiplying by utilization factor $U$. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** ISPS = real-time, IoT-enabled, sensor + cloud + mobile system for parking slot management.
- **Four Layers:** Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- **Most-used Sensor:** **Ultrasonic HC-SR04** for indoor; **Inductive Loop** for outdoor.
- **Governing Formula:** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ (always divide by 2 for round trip).
- **Speed of Sound:** Temperature-dependent: $v = 331 + 0.6T$ m/s.
- **Best Protocol:** **MQTT** — lightweight, pub/sub, QoS-enabled, low power.
- **Detection Accuracy Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ — must exceed **95 %**.
- **End-to-End Latency Budget:** Sum of sense + transmit + cloud + app latency. Should be **< 1 s.**
- **Slot Lifecycle States:** FREE → OCCUPIED → RESERVED → MAINTENANCE → DECOMMISSIONED.
- **Revenue Equation:** $C = N \cdot P \cdot T \cdot U$ — always include utilization factor.
- **Cloud Platforms (KTU accepts):** AWS IoT, ThingSpeak, Blynk, Firebase, Azure IoT Hub.
- **CO Mapping:** ISPS maps to **CO5 (Design IoT applications)** and **CO6 (Evaluate performance).**
- **Bloom's Levels Tested:** L3 (Apply — calculations), L4 (Analyze — comparison), L5 (Evaluate — system design).
- **Common Board Question Types:** (i) Distance derivation, (ii) Sensor comparison table, (iii) Architecture diagram + explanation, (iv) Protocol justification, (v) Revenue optimization numerical.
- **Key Numerical:** 580 µs echo at 25 °C → ~ 10 cm → slot occupied (threshold 30 cm).
- **Pin Configuration (HC-SR04):** VCC, GND, **TRIG** (input), **ECHO** (output).
- **MQTT Topic Naming Convention:** `parking/<lot_id>/<floor>/<slot_id>` — KTU examiners love this pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Door Lock System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 12 > - **Topic:** Smart Door Lock System** # 🔐 Module 12: Smart Door Lock System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition A **Smart Door Lock System** is an Internet of Things (IoT) application architecture that integrates sensing, processing, actuation, and cloud-based communication modules to enable electronic, credential-driven, and remotely controllable access management of physical doors — replacing or augmenting traditional mechanical key-based locking mechanisms. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (Course Outcome mapping under **CO5: Develop IoT-based applications for real-world domains**), a Smart Door Lock System is defined as a cyber-physical system comprising: - A **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)** (e.g., ESP32, Arduino Uno, NodeMCU). - **Authentication peripherals** (Keypad, RFID reader, biometric sensor, smartphone). - **Actuator hardware** (Solenoid lock, Servo motor, Relay-driven electromagnetic lock). - **Communication transceivers** (Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, MQTT broker). - **Monitoring/Logging services** (Cloud dashboards, Blynk, ThingSpeak, AWS IoT). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Smart Door Lock is an **embedded IoT subsystem** that authenticates a user through electronic means (PIN, RFID tag, biometric, mobile app) and grants physical access via an electromechanical actuator, with all events logged to a network endpoint for traceability and remote management. --- ### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine the **traditional lock-and-key** system you use every day: | Real World Analogy | Smart Door Lock Equivalent | |---|---| | 🔑 Physical metal key | Digital credential (PIN/RFID/Fingerprint) | | 👁️ You recognizing who's at the door | Authentication sensor reading input | | 🖐️ Your hand turning the key | Microcontroller triggering an actuator | | 🧠 Your memory of who came and went | Cloud database logging events | | 📞 Calling someone to open the door | Remote unlocking via smartphone app | > 🧠 **Think of it this way:** A Smart Door Lock is essentially giving your door a **"brain" (microcontroller)** and a **"voice" (wireless module)** so it can listen to instructions, verify identity, decide who enters, remember everything, and tell you about it — all without a physical key. --- ### 🎯 Key Engineering Metrics for Smart Door Lock Systems The following standard metrics are universally used in KTU-evaluated smart lock designs: - **Authentication Latency:** **< 500 ms** (time from credential input to lock actuation) - **False Acceptance Rate (FAR):** **≤ 0.001 %** for biometric locks - **False Rejection Rate (FRR):** **≤ 0.1 %** for capacitive fingerprint sensors - **Power Consumption:** **15 mW – 250 mW** (depending on Wi-Fi/BLE active state) - **Battery Backup:** **≥ 2000 mAh Li-ion** (supports ~30 days standby) - **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V (logic) / 5 V / 12 V (actuator)** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When describing a smart door lock, always mention the **microcontroller (brain)**, **sensors (senses)**, **actuators (muscles)**, and **communication module (voice)**. This 4-layer mental model is exactly how KTU examiners award marks for the "system overview" question. --- ### 🏗️ Functional Subsystems of a Smart Door Lock 1. **Input/Sensing Layer** — Captures user credentials. 2. **Processing/Control Layer** — Validates credentials and runs decision logic. 3. **Actuation Layer** — Physically locks/unlocks the door. 4. **Communication Layer** — Sends notifications, accepts remote commands. 5. **Power Management Layer** — Battery + voltage regulation. 6. **Security/Encryption Layer** — Protects data in transit and at rest. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 12):** The Smart Door Lock System is the *case study* application for the **IoT Application Layer**, demonstrating how perception → network → processing → actuation layers converge in a single deployable product. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown ### 🔹 Layer 1: Authentication / Input Subsystem The system must decide **"Who are you?"** before granting access. Common authentication methods include: - **PIN-based authentication:** User enters a 4–8 digit code via matrix keypad. - **RFID-based authentication:** 125 kHz EM4100 tags or 13.56 MHz MIFARE tags. - **Biometric authentication:** Fingerprint (R307, GT-521F), Face (ESP32-CAM), IRIS. - **Bluetooth/Wi-Fi proximity:** Smartphone as the trusted token (BLE 4.0+ pairing). - **Hybrid (Multi-Factor):** PIN + RFID, or Fingerprint + OTP. ### 🔹 Layer 2: Processing / Control Logic The MCU follows a finite decision tree: 1. **Initialize** all peripherals (keypad, RFID, lock relay, Wi-Fi). 2. **Wait** for an interrupt from any input device. 3. **Capture** the credential. 4. **Compare** the credential against an encrypted whitelist in EEPROM/Flash. 5. **Decide:** - ✅ Match → Energize the relay (unlock) for *N* seconds → Log event. - ❌ Mismatch → Increment failure counter → Trigger alarm after threshold. 6. **Repeat.** ### 🔹 Layer 3: Actuation Subsystem Two dominant locking mechanisms exist: | Lock Type | Operating Voltage | Current Draw | Holding Force | Best Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Solenoid Lock** | 12 V DC | 500 mA – 2 A | 50 – 200 N | Cupboard/light doors | | **Electromagnetic Lock** | 12 V / 24 V DC | 300 mA – 500 mA | 280 – 1200 N | Heavy wooden/glass doors | | **Servo Motor (SG90/MG996R)** | 4.8 V – 6 V | 100 – 350 mA | Torque-based | Deadbolt rotation | | **Motorized Bolt Lock** | 12 V DC | 1 A | 500 N | Premium commercial | ### 🔹 Layer 4: Communication & Cloud Layer Communication protocols are classified by range and power: | Protocol | Range | Power | Use Case in Smart Lock | |---|---|---|---| | **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** | 50 m | High | Direct cloud, no hub needed | | **BLE 4.2 / 5.0** | 10 m | Very Low | Phone-as-key, indoor use | | **Zigbee 3.0** | 100 m | Low | Multi-door mesh in hotels | | **LoRaWAN** | 5 km | Ultra Low | Farm-gate / warehouse locks | | **MQTT over TLS** | Internet | Medium | Secure cloud command channel | ### 🔹 Layer 5: Power Management Power budgeting is critical because smart locks must never lose access. $$T_{standby} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{standby}}$$ Where: - $C_{battery}$ = battery capacity in **mAh** - $I_{standby}$ = current consumed in sleep mode in **mA** - $T_{standby}$ = total standby hours > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** For battery-only smart locks, designers use **ESP32 deep-sleep mode** (10 µA) instead of active Wi-Fi mode (160 mA). The Wi-Fi is only woken by an external interrupt (e.g., keypad press), reducing power consumption by **~99.9 %**. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Standby Time | $T_{stby} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{sleep}}$ | hours | Battery life estimation | | 2 | Active Energy per Unlock | $E_{act} = V_{cc} \cdot I_{act} \cdot t_{unlock}$ | Joules | Per-event energy | | 3 | Total Daily Energy | $E_{day} = N \cdot E_{act} + 24 \cdot V_{cc} \cdot I_{sleep}$ | Joules | Sizing the battery | | 4 | Relay Coil Current (Ohm's Law) | $I_{coil} = \frac{V_{cc}}{R_{coil}}$ | A | Sizing driver transistor | | 5 | FAR (Biometric) | $FAR = \frac{N_{FA}}{N_{total}}$ | ratio (unitless) | Security metric | | 6 | FRR (Biometric) | $FRR = \frac{N_{FR}}{N_{total}}$ | ratio (unitless) | Usability metric | | 7 | EER (Equal Error Rate) | $EER$ at $FAR = FRR$ | % | Biometric sensor quality | | 8 | Latency Budget | $T_{lat} = T_{sensor} + T_{proc} + T_{act}$ | ms | End-to-end response time | | 9 | Wi-Fi Throughput | $R = \frac{P_{tx}}{T_{frame}}$ | bits/s | Notification bandwidth | | 10 | Data Encryption Key Size | $K \in \{128, 192, 256\}$ bits | bits | AES security level | | 11 | Solenoid Pull-in Current | $I_{pull} \approx 2 \cdot I_{hold}$ | A | Inrush current design | | 12 | PWM Duty for Servo Angle | $D = \frac{\theta}{180^{\circ}} \cdot 5\% + 2.5\%$ | % | Servo positioning | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize formulas **#1, #2, #3, and #8** — these are the most commonly asked in 14-mark design problems where students must size the battery, calculate energy, or determine actuator sizing. --- ## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering Smart Door Lock Systems represent a **USD 4.8 billion global market (2024)** and are deployed in: - 🏨 **Hospitality** — Hotel rooms (RFID + mobile key) - 🏢 **Corporate offices** — Audit-trail access control - 🏥 **Hospitals** — Hygienic, contactless biometric entry - 🏠 **Smart homes** — Integrated with Alexa/Google Home - 🚚 **Logistics** — Warehouse & cold-storage access - 🎓 **Academic labs** — Restricted equipment rooms The engineering challenge spans **embedded systems, cybersecurity, ergonomics, and UX** — making it a *perfect* KTU case study that connects hardware, software, and networking. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Battery Sizing for a Smart Door Lock **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** > A smart door lock runs on a 3.7 V, 2000 mAh Li-Po battery. The ESP32 sleeps at 10 µA and wakes 20 times per day, each wake lasting 3 seconds at an active current of 160 mA. The 12 V solenoid lock draws 600 mA for 2 seconds per unlock, stepped up via a boost converter (90 % efficiency). Find the **battery life in days**. --- ### 🔸 Step 1: Compute Standby Energy Per Day $$I_{sleep,day} = I_{sleep} \times 24 \text{ h} = 10\,\mu A \times 24 = 0.00024 \text{ Ah/day}$$ > *Logic:* Multiply the sleep current by 24 hours to find the daily standby charge consumption. ### 🔸 Step 2: Compute Active MCU Energy Per Day $$I_{MCU,day} = I_{active} \times \frac{t_{active}}{3600} \times N_{events}$$ $$I_{MCU,day} = 160\,\text{mA} \times \frac{3}{3600} \times 20$$ $$I_{MCU,day} = 0.160 \times 0.000833 \times 20 = 0.00267 \text{ Ah/day}$$ > *Logic:* Convert the active duration to hours, then multiply by current and number of events per day. ### 🔸 Step 3: Compute Solenoid Energy (Considering Boost Converter Loss) The solenoid runs at 12 V, 600 mA, for 2 s, 20 times per day. Input power must account for converter efficiency. $$P_{solenoid} = V \times I = 12 \times 0.6 = 7.2 \text{ W}$$ $$E_{solenoid,event} = P_{solenoid} \times t = 7.2 \times 2 = 14.4 \text{ J}$$ $$E_{solenoid,day} = 14.4 \times 20 = 288 \text{ J/day}$$ Convert Joules to Ah at 3.7 V (battery side): $$I_{solenoid,day} = \frac{E_{day}}{V_{bat} \times 3600} \times \frac{1}{\eta_{boost}}$$ $$I_{solenoid,day} = \frac{288}{3.7 \times 3600} \times \frac{1}{0.90}$$ $$I_{solenoid,day} = \frac{288}{13320} \times 1.111 = 0.02402 \text{ Ah/day}$$ > *Logic:* Energy is conserved, but the boost converter loses 10 %, so input current must be higher by a factor of $1/\eta$. ### 🔸 Step 4: Total Daily Consumption $$I_{total,day} = I_{sleep,day} + I_{MCU,day} + I_{solenoid,day}$$ $$I_{total,day} = 0.00024 + 0.00267 + 0.02402 = 0.02693 \text{ Ah/day}$$ ### 🔸 Step 5: Compute Battery Life $$T_{battery} = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{total,day}} = \frac{2000\,\text{mAh}}{26.93\,\text{mAh/day}}$$ $$\boxed{T_{battery} \approx 74.3 \text{ days}}$$ > *Engineering Interpretation:* The lock operates nearly 2.5 months on a single charge — a commercially acceptable value. If more events are expected, a **solar trickle charger** or **USB-C backup** is recommended. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Authentication Trade-offs | Authentication Method | Security Level | Cost (USD) | User Convenience | Power Demand | KTU Exam Weightage | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **4-digit PIN Keypad** | Low–Medium | $2 | High | Very Low | ⭐⭐ | | **RFID (125 kHz)** | Medium | $4 | Very High | Low | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **MIFARE 13.56 MHz (Crypto)** | High | $6 | High | Low | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Fingerprint (capacitive)** | Very High | $10–$15 | High | Medium | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Face Recognition (ESP32-CAM)** | High | $8 | Very High | High | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **BLE Smartphone Key** | High | $3 (chip only) | Very High | Low | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Hybrid PIN + RFID** | Very High | $6 | High | Low | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Trend:** The board has increasingly favored **hybrid authentication** and **multi-factor** questions because they test the student's ability to combine subsystems — exactly the design philosophy of real IoT products. --- ## 3.3 Validation Logic — Pseudocode Explanation Although this is an embedded-IoT topic (not pure programming), the **decision flow** is exam-relevant: **Authentication Flow (logical steps, not source code):** 1. System boot → Initialize all modules. 2. Wi-Fi connects to home network. 3. MQTT client subscribes to topic `home/lock/cmd`. 4. Loop: - Listen for credential input event. - Capture input. - Compare against stored whitelist. - **If match →** Energize relay → Lock open for 5 s → Publish "UNLOCKED" to `home/lock/status` → Log timestamp. - **If mismatch →** Increment fail counter. - **If fails ≥ 3 →** Trigger buzzer + publish "TAMPER_ALERT" → Lock disable for 60 s. 5. On MQTT message "LOCK" → De-energize relay → Publish "LOCKED". > [!IMPORTANT] > **Important for KTU:** When asked "explain the working", present it as a **numbered flow** or a **state machine** — never as raw code. The board awards marks for *system design logic*, not for syntax. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End Smart Door Lock Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Input
PIN or RFID or Fingerprint] --> B[Authentication Module
Keypad or RFID Reader or R307 Sensor] B --> C{MCU Decision Engine
ESP32 Microcontroller} C -->|Valid Credential| D[Driver Circuit
NPN Transistor or MOSFET] C -->|Invalid Credential| E[Buzzer + LED Alert] D --> F[Relay Module
5V SPDT Switch] F --> G[Electromagnetic Lock
12V Solenoid] C -->|Wi-Fi Event| H[MQTT Broker
Cloud Server] H --> I[Mobile App
Blynk or Custom Android] I -->|Remote Unlock Command| H H --> C C --> J[OLED Display
Status Feedback] C --> K[EEPROM
Whitelist Storage] C --> L[Li-Po Battery
3.7V 2000 mAh] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The user provides input at `A`, the authentication module `B` captures it, the MCU `C` decides validity, the driver `D` and relay `F` actuate the lock `G`, the cloud `H` and mobile app `I` provide remote control, while `J`, `K`, and `L` form the local support subsystems. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Remote Unlock via Mobile App ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User as User participant App as Mobile App participant Cloud as MQTT Cloud Broker participant ESP as ESP32 MCU participant Lock as Solenoid Lock participant Buzzer as Alert Buzzer User->>App: Tap "Unlock Door" App->>Cloud: Publish "UNLOCK" to topic home/lock/cmd Cloud->>ESP: Forward MQTT message ESP->>ESP: Validate token signature ESP->>Lock: Energize relay for 5 seconds Lock-->>ESP: Confirmation pulse ESP->>Cloud: Publish "UNLOCKED" event with timestamp Cloud->>App: Update status to "Door Open" ESP->>Buzzer: Single beep (success tone) ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Lock State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Locked Locked --> Authenticating: Credential Detected Authenticating --> Unlocked: Valid Match Authenticating --> Locked: Invalid / Fail Count < 3 Authenticating --> TamperLocked: Fail Count = 3 Unlocked --> Locked: 5-Second Timer Expires TamperLocked --> Locked: 60-Second Cooldown Elapsed Locked --> Locked: Remote "LOCK" Command Unlocked --> Locked: Remote "LOCK" Command ``` > **State Interpretation:** > - **Locked** → default secure state. > - **Authenticating** → temporary state while credential is being validated. > - **Unlocked** → only entered upon successful authentication. > - **TamperLocked** → security penalty state after 3 failed attempts. --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Li-Po Battery
3.7V 2000mAh] --> B[TP4056 Charging IC
with USB-C input] A --> C[AMS1117-3.3V LDO Regulator] C --> D[ESP32 MCU + Sensors
3.3V Logic] A --> E[MT3608 Boost Converter
3.7V to 12V] E --> F[Relay Module
12V Coil] F --> G[Electromagnetic Lock
12V 600mA] ``` > **Power Flow Logic:** The battery feeds two parallel rails — a **3.3 V logic rail** for the MCU/sensors and a **12 V power rail** for the electromagnetic lock via a boost converter. This dual-rail design is the standard architecture for any battery-driven IoT actuator system. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **List and briefly explain the four major subsystems of an IoT-based Smart Door Lock System.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four major subsystems are: 1. **Sensing/Input Subsystem (1 mark):** Captures user credentials using devices like keypads, RFID readers, or biometric sensors (e.g., fingerprint module R307). 2. **Processing/Control Subsystem (1 mark):** A microcontroller such as ESP32 or Arduino processes the credential, runs authentication logic, and decides whether to actuate the lock. 3. **Actuation Subsystem (0.5 mark):** Electromechanical components like a solenoid lock, electromagnetic lock, or servo motor physically lock/unlock the door based on the MCU's signal via a relay driver. 4. **Communication Subsystem (0.5 mark):** Wireless modules (Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee) transmit events to a cloud server, enabling remote monitoring and control via mobile apps. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Compare RFID-based and biometric (fingerprint) authentication in smart door locks with respect to security, cost, and user convenience.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | RFID Authentication | Fingerprint Authentication | |---|---|---| | **Security** (1 m) | Moderate; tags can be cloned if not using crypto (MIFARE 13.56 MHz is more secure than 125 kHz) | High; FAR ≤ 0.001 %, hard to forge | | **Cost** (1 m) | Low ($3–$5) | Higher ($8–$15) | | **User Convenience** (1 m) | Very High — tap and go; no contact needed | High — single touch; not ideal in wet/dirty conditions | **Conclusion:** Fingerprint offers superior security at higher cost, while RFID is more economical and user-friendly but less secure against cloning. --- ## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Design-Oriented Question `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Design a complete IoT-based Smart Door Lock System using **ESP32, RFID (RC522), and a 12 V solenoid lock**. Draw the block diagram and explain the working. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain how **MQTT protocol** is used in this system for remote unlocking and event logging. Discuss the security considerations. **(7 marks)** --- #### 🔹 Part (a) — System Design (7 Marks) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RFID Card
13.56 MHz] --> B[MFRC522 Reader
SPI Interface] B --> C[ESP32 MCU
Validates UID] C --> D[ULN2003 Driver
or NPN Transistor] D --> E[Relay Module
5V Coil, 230V/10A contacts] E --> F[12V Solenoid Lock
Door Bolt] C --> G[OLED 0.96 inch Display
I2C Status] C --> H[Buzzer
Audio Feedback] C --> I[EEPROM 24C256
Stores Authorized UIDs] C --> J[Wi-Fi Module
Built into ESP32] J --> K[MQTT Broker
Cloud Server] ``` **Working Explanation (4 marks):** 1. **Initialization:** The ESP32 initializes the MFRC522 RFID reader over SPI, connects to Wi-Fi, subscribes to the MQTT topic, and loads authorized UIDs from EEPROM. 2. **Card Detection:** When an RFID card is placed near the reader, the MFRC522 reads its Unique Identification (UID) number. 3. **Authentication:** The ESP32 compares the read UID with the whitelist stored in EEPROM. If matched, it proceeds; otherwise, the buzzer beeps twice and the failure counter increments. 4. **Actuation:** On successful match, the ESP32 sends a HIGH signal to a transistor/MOSFET driver, energizing the relay coil. The relay's Common-Open contact closes the 12 V circuit, energizing the solenoid lock which retracts the bolt. 5. **Timer + Re-lock:** A 5-second software timer holds the door open, after which the relay de-energizes and the bolt re-engages. 6. **Cloud Notification:** The unlock event with timestamp and UID is published to the MQTT broker. --- #### 🔹 Part (b) — MQTT Protocol and Security (7 Marks) **MQTT Architecture (3.5 marks):** MQTT is a **lightweight publish-subscribe messaging protocol** designed for low-bandwidth IoT devices. The Smart Door Lock uses it as follows: - **Broker:** A central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, AWS IoT Core) running on the cloud. - **Publishers:** The ESP32 publishes to topics like `home/lock/status` and `home/lock/event`. - **Subscribers:** The mobile app subscribes to `home/lock/status` to receive real-time updates and publishes commands to `home/lock/cmd`. **Sample Topic Structure:** ``` home/lock/cmd → Remote unlock/lock commands home/lock/status → Current lock state (LOCKED / UNLOCKED) home/lock/event → Timestamped access logs home/lock/alert → Tamper or low-battery warnings ``` **Security Considerations (3.5 marks):** 1. **TLS/SSL Encryption:** All MQTT traffic must use MQTT over TLS (port 8883) to prevent eavesdropping. 2. **Authentication:** Use username/password or X.509 client certificates for the ESP32 to authenticate to the broker. 3. **Access Tokens (JWT/OAuth2):** The mobile app must obtain a short-lived token before publishing commands. 4. **Topic ACLs:** Restrict topics so only authorized clients can publish to `home/lock/cmd`. 5. **Replay Attack Prevention:** Include a unique nonce and timestamp in every command; reject duplicates older than 60 s. 6. **Firmware Signing:** Prevent malicious firmware updates by signing the binary. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **authentication mechanisms** available for smart door locks. Compare biometric vs. PIN-based methods in terms of FAR, FRR, and EER. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A smart door lock uses a **3.7 V 2500 mAh battery**, with sleep current 8 µA, active MCU current 120 mA for 4 s, and a 12 V/500 mA solenoid for 2 s per unlock event. The system has 15 unlock events per day. **Calculate the battery life in days**, assuming a 90 % efficient boost converter. **(7 marks)** --- #### 🔹 Part (a) — Authentication Mechanisms (7 Marks) **Authentication Methods (3 marks):** 1. **Knowledge-based:** PIN, password, pattern — easy to implement but can be shoulder-surfed. 2. **Possession-based:** RFID cards, NFC tokens, smartphones via BLE — convenient, but tokens can be lost or cloned. 3. **Biometric:** Fingerprint, face, iris — high security, hard to forge, but costlier and may fail in harsh conditions. 4. **Multi-factor:** Combines two or more of the above for higher assurance. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Metric | PIN (4-digit) | Fingerprint (Capacitive) | |---|---|---| | **FAR (False Acceptance)** | 1 in 10,000 (brute force risk) | ≤ 0.001 % (1 in 100,000) | | **FRR (False Rejection)** | ~0 % (deterministic match) | 0.1 % – 1 % (depends on skin condition) | | **EER (Equal Error Rate)** | Not applicable (binary match) | 0.1 % – 0.5 % (high-quality sensors) | | **Security Level** | Low to Medium | High to Very High | | **Cost** | Very Low | Medium to High | | **Usability** | High (no physical contact) | High but requires clean finger | > **EER Explanation:** The Equal Error Rate is the point at which FAR equals FRR. A **lower EER** indicates a better biometric sensor. --- #### 🔹 Part (b) — Battery Life Calculation (7 Marks) **Given:** - $C_{bat} = 2500$ mAh - $I_{sleep} = 8\,\mu A$ - $I_{MCU} = 120$ mA, $t_{MCU} = 4$ s - $V_{lock} = 12$ V, $I_{lock} = 500$ mA, $t_{lock} = 2$ s - $N = 15$ events/day - $\eta_{boost} = 0.90$ **Step 1: Sleep consumption per day (0.5 mark)** $$I_{sleep,day} = 8\,\mu A \times 24 = 0.000192 \text{ Ah/day}$$ **Step 2: MCU active consumption per day (1.5 marks)** $$I_{MCU,day} = 120 \times \frac{4}{3600} \times 15 = 0.002 \text{ Ah/day}$$ **Step 3: Solenoid energy per day (2.5 marks)** $$P_{lock} = 12 \times 0.5 = 6 \text{ W}$$ $$E_{lock,event} = 6 \times 2 = 12 \text{ J}$$ $$E_{lock,day} = 12 \times 15 = 180 \text{ J/day}$$ $$I_{lock,day} = \frac{180}{3.7 \times 3600} \times \frac{1}{0.90} = 0.01500 \text{ Ah/day}$$ **Step 4: Total daily consumption (1 mark)** $$I_{total,day} = 0.000192 + 0.002 + 0.01500 = 0.01719 \text{ Ah/day}$$ **Step 5: Battery life (1.5 marks)** $$T_{battery} = \frac{2500}{17.19} \approx 145.4 \text{ days} \approx 4.8 \text{ months}$$ $$\boxed{T_{battery} \approx 145 \text{ days}}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** The 12 V lock dominates energy use (≈87 % of daily energy). Designers often mitigate this with a **supercapacitor bank** that delivers the inrush current, allowing a smaller battery. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Drawing an Arduino with a lock directly** — Examiners expect a **driver transistor + flyback diode** in between; missing this loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the boost converter efficiency** — Always multiply the solenoid current by $1/\eta$ in battery-life problems. > 3. **Confusing FAR with FRR** — FAR = intruder accepted; FRR = genuine user rejected. Mixing them up costs 0.5–1 mark. > 4. **No mention of MQTT QoS levels** — For critical lock commands, use **QoS 1 or 2**, not QoS 0. > 5. **No security discussion in MQTT question** — The board specifically tests for TLS, ACLs, and authentication; skipping these loses 2–3 marks. > 6. **Wrong unit conversion** — Converting seconds to hours: divide by 3600, not 60. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔑 A Smart Door Lock has **4 critical layers**: Sensing → Processing → Actuation → Communication. - 🧠 The **microcontroller (ESP32/Arduino)** is the brain; never drive a lock directly from a GPIO — always use a **relay + driver + flyback diode**. - 🔋 Battery life is governed by $T = C_{bat} / I_{total,day}$ — include boost-converter efficiency in all calculations. - 📡 **MQTT** is the dominant protocol for cloud integration; always use **TLS + authentication**. - 👆 **Authentication trade-off:** PIN = cheap but low security; Biometric = high security but costlier; **Hybrid** is the industry trend. - 🛡️ Security metrics: **FAR (intruder accepted)**, **FRR (genuine user rejected)**, **EER (crossover point)** — memorize all three. - ⏱️ **Latency budget** for a smart lock should be **< 500 ms**; this is the time from credential read to bolt movement. - 🔌 **Power rails:** 3.3 V for logic, 12 V for the lock — use an **LDO + boost converter** architecture. - 📋 **EEPROM whitelist** is the local credential store; **cloud log** is the master audit trail. - 🔁 **State machine:** Locked → Authenticating → Unlocked → Locked, with a **TamperLocked** penalty state after 3 failed attempts. - 📱 **Mobile app** uses **publish-subscribe** to receive status updates and send lock/unlock commands. - 🏨 **Industry deployments:** Hotels (RFID + mobile), Offices (Biometric + RFID), Homes (BLE + Wi-Fi), Warehouses (LoRa + keypad).

topics.title: IoT-based Personal Safety Device
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 13
> - **Topic:** IoT-based Personal Safety Device

# IoT-based Personal Safety Device

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

An **IoT-based Personal Safety Device (PSD)** is a wearable or portable cyber-physical system that integrates heterogeneous sensors, wireless communication modules, and cloud/edge computing resources to continuously monitor an individual's environmental and physiological context, autonomously detect hazardous conditions, and trigger multi-modal safety responses (alerts, location broadcasts, emergency calls).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> An IoT-based personal safety device is a compact, sensor-rich, network-connected hardware platform designed to safeguard an individual by detecting threats (physical assault, medical emergencies, fall, fire, gas leak, vehicle accident) and transmitting real-time distress signals along with geo-spatial coordinates to predefined guardians or emergency response services.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine carrying a **digital guardian angel** on your wrist or in your pocket.

- **Old way:** A girl walking alone at night had only a loud whistle — useless if no one is nearby to hear it.
- **IoT way:** The same girl wears a smart band. The moment the accelerometer detects a sudden jerk (a grab), the GPS immediately locks the location, the GSM module dials **112 (India's emergency number)**, and an SMS containing a Google Maps link is sent to her parents — all in under **3 seconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> The device is not merely a sensor box; it is a **Context-Aware Safety Ecosystem** combining **Sense → Decide → Communicate → Respond** in a closed loop.

### Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **Response Latency (t_r):** Typically **< 5 seconds** from trigger to alert dispatch.
- **GPS Accuracy:** **± 3 to 5 meters** under open sky (civilian L1 band, 1575.42 MHz).
- **Battery Endurance:** **48–72 hours** on a single 3.7 V Li-Po charge.
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **-10 °C to +55 °C** (industrial grade).
- **Wireless Range:** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) up to **100 meters**; GSM/4G coverage-bound.

### Primary Threat Categories Detected

1. **Physical Assault** — sudden jerk + audio spike
2. **Fall Detection** — free-fall accelerometer signature
3. **Medical Emergency** — heart rate anomaly, SpO₂ drop
4. **Environmental Hazard** — gas leak, fire, smoke
5. **Vehicle Accident** — high-G impact + tilt inversion
6. **Man-down / Immobility** — prolonged horizontal posture
7. **Geo-fence Breach** — exit from safe zone

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture — Layered Breakdown

The device is engineered as a **four-layer IoT stack**, often called the **MCPN Model** (Monitor-Compute-Process-Network):

#### Layer 1: Perception / Sensor Layer (The "Senses")
- **Accelerometer + Gyroscope (IMU):** MPU6050, ADXL345 — captures linear acceleration `a` (m/s²) and angular velocity `ω` (rad/s).
- **GPS Module:** NEO-6M, u-blox — provides latitude `φ`, longitude `λ`, altitude `h`.
- **Heart-Rate / SpO₂ Sensor:** MAX30102 — photoplethysmography (PPG) based.
- **Gas Sensor:** MQ-2 / MQ-135 — detects LPG, methane, smoke (ppm).
- **Temperature/Humidity:** DHT11/DHT22.
- **Microphone/Sound Sensor:** detects screams / gunshots (dB threshold).
- **Panic Button:** hardware interrupt (GPIO).

#### Layer 2: Edge / Processing Layer (The "Brain")
- Microcontroller: **ESP32** (dual-core, 240 MHz, Wi-Fi + BLE) or **Arduino Nano 33 IoT**.
- Runs a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** with states: `IDLE → MONITOR → ALERT → TRANSMIT → SAFE`.
- Threshold comparison in real-time (firmware-level decisioning).

#### Layer 3: Network / Communication Layer (The "Voice")
- **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** for indoor/cloud relay.
- **GSM/GPRS (SIM800/SIM900)** for outdoor SMS/calls.
- **BLE 5.0** for smartphone companion app.
- **LoRa (optional)** for off-grid areas.

#### Layer 4: Application / Cloud Layer (The "Helper")
- **Cloud Platform:** ThingSpeak, AWS IoT Core, Blynk, Firebase.
- **Mobile App (Android/iOS):** receives alerts, shows real-time map, logs history.
- **Emergency Services Interface:** automated 112/911 dialer.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — "The Why and The How"

| Step | Action | Why It Matters |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Sensor continuously samples physical signals | Enables real-time situational awareness |
| 2 | Microcontroller computes **magnitude of resultant acceleration** | Single scalar captures motion intensity |
| 3 | Compare against pre-calibrated **threshold** | Filters noise vs genuine event |
| 4 | On breach → activate **GPS fix** (cold-start ~30 s, warm-start < 5 s) | Adds geo-context to the alert |
| 5 | Compose SMS → `AT+CMGS` command via UART to GSM | Leverages ubiquitous cellular coverage |
| 6 | Send via MQTT (if Wi-Fi) or HTTP POST to cloud | Enables dashboard & multi-recipient alerts |
| 7 | Trigger buzzer + LED strobe | Deterrent + visual SOS locator |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $$a_{\text{mag}} = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$$ | Resultant acceleration magnitude from 3-axis IMU | m/s² |
| 2 | $$g_{\text{threshold}} = 1.0\,g \pm \Delta g$$ | Fall detection: deviation from gravity (1 g = 9.81 m/s²) | m/s² |
| 3 | $$d = R \cdot c \cdot \Delta\lambda$$ | Haversine simplified — small-distance East-West offset | meters |
| 4 | $$\text{GPS Accuracy} \approx 2.5 \cdot \text{HDOP} \text{ (m)}$$ | Horizontal Dilution of Precision impact on fix | meters |
| 5 | $$P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$ | Total system power = sum of sensor + MCU + radio power | mW |
| 6 | $$t_r = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{proc}} + t_{\text{net}}$$ | End-to-end response latency | seconds |
| 7 | $$E = V \cdot I \cdot t$$ | Battery energy budget (Li-Po: 3.7 V typical) | Wh |
| 8 | $$\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$ | Audio trigger reliability metric | dB |
| 9 | $$\phi_{H} = \frac{\sum a_i}{n}$$ | Mean heart rate from PPG peak interval (BPM) | beats/min |
| 10 | $$\text{Battery Life (h)} = \frac{C_{\text{mAh}}}{I_{\text{mA}}}$$ | Endurance estimation for the wearable | hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Threshold:** For fall detection, the literature standard is a free-fall signature where $a_{\text{mag}} < 0.6\,g$ for $\geq 40$ ms, followed by an impact spike $a_{\text{mag}} > 2.5\,g$ within 1 second.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Smart Cities:** Integrated with CCTNS / 112 India emergency dispatch.
- **Elderly Care:** Reduces "long-lie" fatalities (lying > 1 hour after fall → 50% mortality).
- **Women Safety:** Compliance with **POSH Act 2013** workplace mandates.
- **Industrial Workers:** Oil & gas, mining, construction PPE 4.0.
- **Defence & Border Patrol:** Soldier health telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Resultant Acceleration Magnitude (IMU Fusion)

The 3-axis accelerometer measures accelerations along orthogonal X, Y, Z body-fixed axes. The **Euclidean norm** gives total dynamic acceleration including gravity contribution:

$$
\vec{a} = a_x\,\hat{i} + a_y\,\hat{j} + a_z\,\hat{k}
$$

Computing the magnitude using the Pythagorean extension in 3D space:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{\text{mag}} &= \sqrt{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}} \\
&= \sqrt{(a_x\,\hat{i} + a_y\,\hat{j} + a_z\,\hat{k}) \cdot (a_x\,\hat{i} + a_y\,\hat{j} + a_z\,\hat{k})} \\
&= \sqrt{a_x^2\,(\hat{i}\cdot\hat{i}) + a_y^2\,(\hat{j}\cdot\hat{j}) + a_z^2\,(\hat{k}\cdot\hat{k}) + 2a_x a_y(\hat{i}\cdot\hat{j}) + 2a_x a_z(\hat{i}\cdot\hat{k}) + 2a_y a_z(\hat{j}\cdot\hat{k})}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the unit vectors are orthogonal, $\hat{i}\cdot\hat{j} = \hat{i}\cdot\hat{k} = \hat{j}\cdot\hat{k} = 0$, and $\hat{i}\cdot\hat{i} = \hat{j}\cdot\hat{j} = \hat{k}\cdot\hat{k} = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\,a_{\text{mag}} = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}\,}
$$

**Decision Logic:** If $a_{\text{mag}} > 2.5\,g$ (impact) **AND** tilt $> 60°$ from vertical **AND** immobility $> 10$ s → trigger **FALL_DETECTED** event.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Haversine Distance Between Two GPS Coordinates

For a panic alert, the device sends coordinates to a guardian. The recipient's app computes distance from the sender. The **Haversine formula** on a sphere of radius $R = 6{,}371$ km (Earth's mean radius):

Let $\phi_1, \lambda_1$ be the device's location, and $\phi_2, \lambda_2$ be the responder's location. Differences:

$$
\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 \quad,\quad \Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1
$$

The **Haversine** of the central angle is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{\text{hav}} &= \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cdot\cos(\phi_2)\cdot\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) \\
c &= 2 \cdot \arctan 2\!\left(\sqrt{a_{\text{hav}}},\,\sqrt{1 - a_{\text{hav}}}\right) \\
d &= R \cdot c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Device at (10.0261° N, 76.3125° E) — KTU Campus, Trivandrum — and guardian at (10.0150° N, 76.3600° E) — Thampanoor:

- $\Delta\phi = 10.0150 - 10.0261 = -0.0111° = -0.0001937$ rad
- $\Delta\lambda = 76.3600 - 76.3125 = 0.0475° = 0.0008290$ rad
- $\sin^2(\Delta\phi/2) \approx (9.685\times 10^{-5})^2 \approx 9.38 \times 10^{-9}$
- $\cos(10.0261°) \approx 0.9846$, $\cos(10.0150°) \approx 0.9847$
- $a_{\text{hav}} \approx 9.38\times10^{-9} + 0.9846 \times 0.9847 \times (4.145\times10^{-4})^2$
- $a_{\text{hav}} \approx 9.38\times10^{-9} + 1.67 \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.76 \times 10^{-7}$
- $c \approx 2\sqrt{a_{\text{hav}}} \approx 2 \times 4.20\times10^{-4} \approx 8.39\times10^{-4}$ rad
- $d \approx 6371 \times 8.39\times10^{-4} \approx \mathbf{5.35\ \text{km}}$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Power Budget & Battery Life

Let a typical PSD use these components:

| Component | Active Current $I_i$ | Duty Cycle $D_i$ |
|----------|----------------------|------------------|
| ESP32 (MCU) | 80 mA | 1.00 (always on) |
| MPU6050 (IMU) | 3.5 mA | 1.00 |
| NEO-6M (GPS) | 45 mA | 0.10 (10% — pulsed) |
| SIM800 (GSM) | 200 mA (tx burst) | 0.02 (2% — only on alert) |
| MAX30102 (PPG) | 1.2 mA | 0.20 (sampled 5 s/min) |
| MQ-2 (Gas) | 150 mA (heater) | 0.05 (pulsed) |
| BLE Radio | 8 mA | 0.30 |

**Effective average current** (linear weighted sum):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= \sum I_i \cdot D_i \\
&= (80)(1.0) + (3.5)(1.0) + (45)(0.10) + (200)(0.02) + (1.2)(0.20) + (150)(0.05) + (8)(0.30) \\
&= 80 + 3.5 + 4.5 + 4.0 + 0.24 + 7.5 + 2.4 \\
&= \mathbf{102.14\ \text{mA}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **1000 mAh Li-Po battery** (typical 3.7 V, 3.7 Wh):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{life}} &= \frac{C}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1000\ \text{mAh}}{102.14\ \text{mA}} \\
&\approx 9.79\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

To meet the **48-hour benchmark**, we need:

$$
C_{\text{required}} = 102.14 \times 48 \approx \mathbf{4903\ \text{mAh}} \quad (\approx 5000\ \text{mAh power bank})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **aggressive duty cycling**, sleep modes (ESP32 deep-sleep at 10 µA), and event-driven wake-up are **non-negotiable design constraints** for wearables.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Event | Primary Sensor | Threshold | Secondary Confirm | Action |
|-------|----------------|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Fall | IMU $a_{\text{mag}}$ | < 0.6 g → > 2.5 g | Tilt + Immobility | SMS + Call |
| Assault / Grab | IMU jerk | $\Delta a/\Delta t > 15$ g/s | Sound > 90 dB | SMS + Siren |
| Heart Attack | PPG / ECG | HR > 140 or < 40 BPM | SpO₂ < 90% | SMS + Hospital API |
| Gas Leak | MQ-2 | > 300 ppm | Temp > 50 °C | SMS + Buzzer |
| Fire | Flame + Temp | IR detected OR T > 70 °C | Smoke ppm | SMS + Water pump (IoT) |
| Geo-fence Exit | GPS | Distance > 500 m from "Home" | Time of day check | SMS to parent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User / Victim] --> B[Wearable PSD Hub]
    B --> C1[IMU Accel Gyro]
    B --> C2[GPS Module]
    B --> C3[PPG Heart Rate]
    B --> C4[Gas and Smoke Sensor]
    B --> C5[Mic Sound Sensor]
    B --> C6[Panic Button]
    C1 --> D[ESP32 MCU Edge Processor]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E{Threshold FSM}
    E -- Normal --> F1[Sleep Mode LED Green]
    E -- Threat --> G[Alarm Activator]
    G --> G1[Buzzer 95 dB]
    G --> G2[LED Strobe SOS]
    G --> H[Comms Layer]
    H --> H1[GSM SIM800 SMS]
    H --> H2[WiFi MQTT to Cloud]
    H --> H3[BLE to Mobile App]
    H1 --> I1[Guardian Phone]
    H1 --> I2[Emergency 112]
    H2 --> I3[AWS IoT Dashboard]
    H3 --> I4[Companion App]
```

### 4.2 Operational State Machine — Firmware Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> IDLE : Sensors Calibrated
    IDLE --> MONITOR : Loop 50 Hz
    MONITOR --> ALERT : Threshold Breach
    MONITOR --> IDLE : No Threat
    ALERT --> TRANSMIT : GPS Lock Acquired
    TRANSMIT --> SAFE : Acknowledgement Received
    TRANSMIT --> SAFE : Timer 60 s Elapsed
    SAFE --> IDLE : Reset Switch Pressed
    SAFE --> MONITOR : Auto Re-Arm after 5 min
    ALERT --> SAFE : False Positive Dismissed
```

### 4.3 Data Flow Sequence — SMS Alert Pathway

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant S as Sensor Cluster
    participant M as ESP32 MCU
    participant G as GPS Module
    participant T as GSM SIM800
    participant R as Guardian
    participant C as Cloud Server

    U->>S: Triggering event occurs
    S->>M: Raw data (ax, ay, az, HR, gas)
    M->>M: Compute amag and check threshold
    M->>G: AT command to fix location
    G-->>M: NMEA lat, long
    M->>T: AT+CMGS with formatted SMS
    T->>R: SMS via cellular network
    T->>C: HTTP POST backup payload
```
