```mermaid
    M->>U: Buzzer and LED SOS
    R-->>C: Acknowledge endpoint hit
    C-->>M: ACK signal via MQTT
```

### 4.4 PCB / Hardware Pin Allocation Map (ESP32 DevKit V1)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerRail["Power Subsystem 3.3V and 5V"]
        P1[Li-Po 3.7V 2000mAh]
        P2[TP4056 Charger IC]
        P3[AMS1117 3.3V LDO]
    end
    subgraph SensorBus["I2C Bus GPIO21 SDA and GPIO22 SCL"]
        P4[MPU6050 IMU addr 0x68]
        P5[MAX30102 PPG addr 0x57]
    end
    subgraph UARTBus["UART2 GPIO16 RX GPIO17 TX"]
        P6[NEO-6M GPS]
    end
    subgraph UARTBus2["SoftwareSerial GPIO25 GPIO26"]
        P7[SIM800 GSM]
    end
    subgraph AnalogIn["ADC GPIO34"]
        P8[MQ-2 Gas Sensor]
    end
    subgraph GPIOMisc["Digital IO"]
        P9[GPIO35 Panic Button]
        P10[GPIO18 Buzzer]
        P11[GPIO19 LED Red]
        P12[GPIO5 LED Green]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Define an IoT-based Personal Safety Device. List any four sensors used in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An IoT-based Personal Safety Device is a wearable cyber-physical system that integrates sensors, microcontrollers, and wireless communication to monitor an individual's surroundings, detect threats, and transmit real-time distress signals with GPS location to guardians or emergency services. **(2 marks)**

Four sensors used:
1. **Accelerometer/Gyroscope (MPU6050)** — fall and motion detection.
2. **GPS Module (NEO-6M)** — location tracking.
3. **Heart-rate sensor (MAX30102)** — cardiac emergency.
4. **Gas sensor (MQ-2)** — LPG/leak detection. **(½ mark each)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Explain the role of the GSM module in a personal safety device. Why is it preferred over Wi-Fi alone?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **GSM/GPRS module (e.g., SIM800)** enables the device to send SMS alerts and make voice calls over the **cellular network**, ensuring communication even in outdoor areas without Wi-Fi. **(1.5 marks)**

Wi-Fi alone is limited to hotspot range (~50 m indoors); GSM leverages nationwide cellular coverage, making it suitable for **outdoor mobility scenarios** like walking on roads, public transport, or remote areas. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – July 2024 / Sample Paper Module 13]**
> **(a)** Design the complete architecture of an IoT-based Women Safety Device. Explain each layer with suitable components. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the resultant acceleration equation from a 3-axis accelerometer and explain how it is used for fall detection with appropriate threshold values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

The device is structured into **four layers**:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors):**
   - MPU6050 IMU for motion
   - NEO-6M GPS for location
   - MAX30102 for heart rate
   - Analog microphone for scream detection
   - Panic button (hardware interrupt) **(2 marks)**

2. **Network/Communication Layer:**
   - GSM SIM800 (primary, outdoor)
   - Wi-Fi ESP32 inbuilt (indoor backup)
   - BLE to companion app **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Processing Layer (Edge):**
   - ESP32 dual-core runs FSM: IDLE → MONITOR → ALERT → TRANSMIT
   - Threshold-based decision firmware **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Application/Cloud Layer:**
   - ThingSpeak / Firebase dashboard
   - Android app showing live map
   - Automated 112 dialler API **(2 marks)**

**(b) Derivation & Fall Detection (7 marks):**

Step 1: Acceleration vector along three axes:
$$\vec{a} = a_x\hat{i} + a_y\hat{j} + a_z\hat{k}$$

Step 2: Magnitude using dot product:
$$a_{\text{mag}} = \sqrt{\vec{a}\cdot\vec{a}} = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$$ **(2 marks)**

Step 3: Free-fall phase — when $a_{\text{mag}} < 0.6\,g$ (i.e. < 5.88 m/s²) for at least **40 ms**, it indicates near-weightlessness. **(1.5 marks)**

Step 4: Impact phase — within 1 second, $a_{\text{mag}}$ rises above **2.5 g** (24.5 m/s²) confirming collision. **(1.5 marks)**

Step 5: Post-fall — tilt angle > 60° from vertical AND no motion for 10 s confirms "man-down." SMS sent. **(2 marks)**

---

> **Q3B. [Internal Choice – Alternative Question]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an IoT-based accident detection system for vehicles. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A wearable PSD uses a 1000 mAh battery and draws the following average currents: MCU 80 mA (100% duty), IMU 5 mA (100%), GPS 50 mA (10%), GSM 250 mA burst (2% duty). Calculate the average current and the battery life in hours. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Vehicle Accident Detection (7 marks):**
- **Sensors:** MPU6050 (high-G impact ≥ 4 g), vibration sensor, GPS, GSM. **(2 marks)**
- **Working:** At impact, the IMU detects sudden deceleration; GPS fetches coordinates; GSM auto-sends SMS to emergency contacts and to the **IRAI 112** gateway with timestamp. **(3 marks)**
- **Optional cloud:** ThingSpeak dashboard with accident hotspot heatmap for civic planning. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= (80)(1.0) + (5)(1.0) + (50)(0.10) + (250)(0.02) \\
&= 80 + 5 + 5 + 5 = \mathbf{95\ \text{mA}}
\end{aligned}
$$ **(3 marks)**

$$
t_{\text{life}} = \frac{1000\ \text{mAh}}{95\ \text{mA}} \approx \mathbf{10.53\ \text{hours}}
$$ **(2 marks)**

Conclusion: With a single 1000 mAh cell, the device operates only ~10.5 hours; to achieve 48-hour endurance, a higher capacity battery (~4600 mAh) and aggressive sleep-mode duty cycling are required. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert g to m/s²** in fall-detection thresholds (1 g = 9.81 m/s²).
> 2. **Confusing Wi-Fi and GSM** — Wi-Fi is short-range; GSM is wide-area. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Not drawing the four-layer architecture** explicitly (Perception → Network → Processing → Application). Skipping layers loses up to 3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the FSM (state machine)** when explaining firmware logic — it is a high-weightage KTU keyword.
> 5. **Calculation errors in power budget** — forgetting to multiply by duty cycle.
> 6. **Failing to mention cloud platform by name** (e.g., AWS IoT, Firebase) — generic "cloud" wording loses a mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A wearable CPS integrating sensors + comms for personal safety.
- **Four Layers:** Perception → Processing → Network → Application (MCPN).
- **Core Sensors:** IMU, GPS, PPG, gas, mic, panic button.
- **Key MCU:** ESP32 (Wi-Fi + BLE + dual core).
- **Critical Threshold:** Free-fall < 0.6 g → Impact > 2.5 g → Tilt > 60° → Immobility > 10 s.
- **Primary Formula:** $a_{\text{mag}} = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$
- **Latency Standard:** End-to-end alert dispatch < 5 s.
- **GPS Accuracy:** ± 3–5 m; degrades with HDOP > 2.
- **Power Rule:** Battery life = Capacity (mAh) / Average current (mA); always multiply sensor currents by their duty cycle.
- **Haversine Distance:** Standard for GPS point-to-point distance on Earth's surface.
- **Communication Preference:** GSM for outdoor mobility, Wi-Fi for indoor/cloud, BLE for smartphone pairing.
- **Standards/Benchmarks:** 1 g = 9.81 m/s², $R_{\text{Earth}} = 6371$ km, 112 (India emergency).
- **Design Constraint:** Event-driven wake-up + deep sleep to extend battery to 48 h.
- **Regulatory Hooks:** POSH Act 2013 (women safety), AIS-140 (vehicle tracking), IEC 60601 (medical wearables).
- **Kerala/KTU Context:** Devices like "Suraksha" by Kerala Police, KTU student projects, and IEEE SB Kerala Section have published reference designs.
- **Examiner Loves:** State diagrams, threshold tables, numerical derivations, cloud-platform names.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Irrigation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 14
> - **Topic:** Smart Irrigation System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Irrigation System (SIS):** A *cyber-physical agricultural control system* that integrates **soil-moisture sensors**, **ambient environmental sensors**, **microcontroller-based edge nodes**, and **cloud/IoT communication protocols** to automatically regulate the actuation of water-delivery mechanisms (pumps, solenoid valves, drip lines) based on real-time field telemetry, without continuous human intervention.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus for *PBITT604 – Internet of Things*, the Smart Irrigation System is positioned as a flagship **Level-3 IoT application** (per the ITU-T Y.2060 reference model), where:
- **Perception Layer** = Sensors (soil moisture, temperature, humidity, rain)
- **Network Layer** = Wi-Fi / LoRa / ZigBee / GSM
- **Middleware / Edge Layer** = Microcontroller (ESP32 / Arduino / Raspberry Pi)
- **Application Layer** = Cloud dashboard + Mobile App
- **Actuation Layer** = Relay → Motor pump / Solenoid valve

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automatic Plant Babysitter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a gardener who never sleeps. Every 30 minutes, he walks into the field, **digs his finger into the soil** (moisture check), **looks at the sky** (rain sensor), and **feels the air** (temperature & humidity). If the soil is *too dry* AND it's *not raining*, he **turns on the tap**. If the soil is *already wet* OR it's *raining*, he **turns it off**. He then **texts the farmer** the exact moisture percentage and water usage. A Smart Irrigation System is exactly this tireless gardener, except the "finger" is a **capacitive soil-moisture probe**, the "eyes" are **environmental sensors**, and the "tap" is a **solenoid valve driven by a relay**.

## 1.3 Core Entities & Standard Metrics

| Entity | Standard Metric | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Soil Moisture | **Volumetric Water Content (VWC)** | **%** |
| Soil Temperature | Temperature | **°C** |
| Ambient Humidity | Relative Humidity (RH) | **% RH** |
| Rainfall Intensity | Rainfall rate | **mm/hr** |
| Water Flow | Volumetric flow rate | **L/min** or **m³/hr** |
| Irrigation Decision Threshold | Soil moisture setpoint | **% VWC** |
| Energy | Power consumption of pump | **Watts (W)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The most common threshold parameter a student must remember is the **soil-moisture setpoint** (typically **30% – 60% VWC** for loamy soils), below which the relay is triggered ON, and above which it is turned OFF — this is called the **hysteresis band** to prevent relay chattering.

## 1.4 Why Smart Irrigation? — Engineering Motivation

- **Water scarcity:** Agriculture consumes ~**70%** of global freshwater.
- **Over-irrigation losses:** Traditional flood irrigation wastes up to **60%** of water.
- **Energy savings:** Pump runtime is reduced by **30%–50%** when demand-driven.
- **Crop yield:** Optimal moisture improves yield by **20%–40%** for many crops.
- **Remote monitoring:** A farmer can supervise **10+ acres** from a smartphone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Layered Decomposition

A Smart Irrigation System is decomposed into **five functional layers**:

### Layer 1 — Perception (Sensor) Layer
- **Capacitive Soil Moisture Sensor v1.2** → Outputs analog voltage proportional to dielectric permittivity of soil.
- **DHT11 / DHT22** → Digital temperature + humidity.
- **Rain Drop Sensor (FC-37)** → Detects presence of rainfall on sensing pad.
- **Water Flow Sensor (YF-S201)** → Hall-effect pulse counter (pulses/Litre ≈ **450 pulses/L**).

### Layer 2 — Edge / Processing Layer
- Microcontroller (**ESP32**, **NodeMCU ESP8266**, **Arduino Uno**, **Raspberry Pi 4**).
- Runs the **control algorithm** (threshold + hysteresis).
- Publishes telemetry to the cloud via **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) over **TCP/IP**.

### Layer 3 — Network / Communication Layer
- **Short range:** Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz), ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4), BLE.
- **Long range:** **LoRaWAN** (up to 15 km line-of-sight), **GSM/GPRS**, **NB-IoT**.
- **Protocols:** MQTT (lightweight pub/sub), HTTP/REST, CoAP.

### Layer 4 — Application / Cloud Layer
- **Cloud platforms:** ThingSpeak, AWS IoT Core, Blynk, Firebase, Ubidots.
- **Dashboard widgets:** Real-time moisture graph, pump status, water usage charts.
- **Mobile app:** Push notifications, manual override.

### Layer 5 — Actuation Layer
- **Relay Module (5V or 12V SPDT)** → Isolates the 230 V AC pump from the 5 V DC logic.
- **Submersible / Centrifugal Pump** → Delivers water.
- **Solenoid Valve (12V DC or 24V AC)** → Controls drip / sprinkler zones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a relay and not a direct GPIO drive?** A microcontroller GPIO pin sources only **~20 mA at 3.3 V**, while a pump motor draws **0.5 A – 5 A at 12 V/230 V**. The relay is a **galvanic isolator and current amplifier**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 numerical/descriptive questions. The vertical bar `|` symbol is **strictly avoided** inside the table to preserve markdown syntax — `\vert` is used instead.

| # | Formula / Expression | Variable Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $VWC = \dfrac{V_{water}}{V_{soil}} \times 100$ | VWC = Volumetric Water Content | **%** | Defines soil moisture metric |
| 2 | $\theta = \theta_{FC} - \theta_{WP}$ | $\theta$ = Available water; $\theta_{FC}$ = Field Capacity; $\theta_{WP}$ = Wilting Point | **% VWC** | Readily Available Water (RAW) |
| 3 | $ET_c = ET_0 \times K_c$ | Crop evapotranspiration; $ET_0$ = Reference ET; $K_c$ = Crop coefficient | **mm/day** | Determines daily water need |
| 4 | $I = \dfrac{(FC - \theta_{current})}{100} \times d \times A$ | $I$ = Irrigation depth; $d$ = root depth; $A$ = field area | **Litres** | Volume of water to apply |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | $Q$ = Flow rate; $V$ = Volume; $t$ = time | **L/min** | Pump / valve capacity check |
| 6 | $V_{analog} = \dfrac{ADC_{val}}{2^{n} - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | ADC output voltage, $n$-bit ADC | **Volts (V)** | Maps sensor reading |
| 7 | $VWC_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{dry} - V_{read}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100$ | Calibrated moisture map | **%** | Sensor-to-physics mapping |
| 8 | $Hysteresis\ Band = \theta_{ON} - \theta_{OFF}$ | $\theta_{ON}$ \vert $\theta_{OFF}$ are thresholds | **% VWC** | Prevents relay chattering |
| 9 | $Water\ Saved\ (\%) = \dfrac{W_{trad} - W_{smart}}{W_{trad}} \times 100$ | Comparative savings metric | **%** | KTU descriptive answer |
| 10 | $P_{pump} = V_{pump} \times I_{pump} \times \cos\phi$ | Electrical power of pump | **Watts (W)** | Energy audit |

## 2.3 The Hysteresis Control Logic (Core Algorithm)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most asked concept** in KTU 2024 ESE for Smart Irrigation. The controller uses **two thresholds** instead of one to prevent the relay from oscillating ON/OFF when the moisture value hovers near a single setpoint.

**State Machine Logic:**

1. **Initial State:** Pump = OFF.
2. **Read** soil moisture $M(t)$ from sensor.
3. **IF** $M(t) \le \theta_{OFF}$ (lower threshold, e.g., 30% VWC) **THEN** Pump = ON.
4. **IF** $M(t) \ge \theta_{ON}$ (upper threshold, e.g., 60% VWC) **THEN** Pump = OFF.
5. **IF** $\theta_{OFF} < M(t) < \theta_{ON}$ **THEN** retain previous state.
6. **IF** Rain sensor = HIGH **THEN** Pump = OFF (override).
7. **Publish** telemetry to MQTT broker every $T$ seconds.
8. **Loop** with sleep interval to save power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two thresholds (hysteresis) and not one?** With a single threshold (say, 45%), the moisture value could oscillate around 45% due to ADC noise (typically $\pm 2\%$), causing the relay to **switch 50+ times per hour**, drastically reducing its mechanical lifespan (rated for ~100,000 cycles).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision agriculture** (vineyards, greenhouses, tea/coffee estates in Kerala).
- **Urban rooftop gardens** and vertical farming.
- **Defence cantonments** with limited water supply.
- **Disaster-resilient farms** (drought-prone regions of Marathwada, Rayalaseema).
- **Smart-city pilots** under the **India Smart Cities Mission**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Water Content (VWC) from Analog Sensor

A capacitive soil moisture sensor outputs a DC voltage inversely proportional to the dielectric constant of the soil (water has $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$, dry soil $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$). We derive the calibration equation.

### Given Data
- $V_{dry}$ = output voltage when sensor is in **completely dry** soil.
- $V_{wet}$ = output voltage when sensor is in **saturated** soil.
- $V_{read}$ = current analog reading.
- **Assumption:** Linear response between dry and wet extremes (first-order approximation valid for **loamy soils**).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the calibration end-points.**

$$V_{dry} = f(0\%\ VWC) \quad \text{and} \quad V_{wet} = f(100\%\ VWC)$$

**Step 2 — Construct a linear mapping using two-point form.**

For a linear sensor, voltage varies from $V_{dry}$ (dry) to $V_{wet}$ (wet). Since more water → higher capacitance → lower frequency → specific voltage mapping (sensor-dependent), we adopt a generalized linear inverse mapping:

$$VWC_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{dry} - V_{read}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Show the units and bounds.**

- When $V_{read} = V_{dry}$: $VWC_{\%} = 0\%$ (dry).
- When $V_{read} = V_{wet}$: $VWC_{\%} = 100\%$ (saturated).
- For all intermediate $V_{read}$: $0\% \le VWC_{\%} \le 100\%$.

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

Suppose $V_{dry} = 3.2\ V$, $V_{wet} = 1.0\ V$, $V_{read} = 2.5\ V$.

$$VWC_{\%} = \dfrac{3.2 - 2.5}{3.2 - 1.0} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.7}{2.2} \times 100 \approx 31.82\%$$

**Interpretation:** The soil has **31.82% VWC** — moderately dry, irrigation may be triggered if threshold is 30%.

## 3.2 Derivation: Irrigation Water Requirement (Volume)

For a field of area $A$ and effective root depth $d$, the volume of water needed to refill the soil from current moisture $\theta_{current}$ to field capacity $\theta_{FC}$ is:

$$I = \dfrac{(\theta_{FC} - \theta_{current})}{100} \times d \times A$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define moisture deficit.**

The moisture gap is $(\theta_{FC} - \theta_{current})$, expressed as a **decimal** of soil volume:

$$Deficit = \dfrac{\theta_{FC} - \theta_{current}}{100}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by soil volume.**

The volume of soil in the root zone is $V_{soil} = d \times A$ (depth $\times$ area).

**Step 3 — Water volume equals the deficit fraction of the soil volume.**

$$I = Deficit \times V_{soil} = \dfrac{(\theta_{FC} - \theta_{current})}{100} \times d \times A$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

Given: $\theta_{FC} = 35\%$, $\theta_{current} = 20\%$, $d = 0.3\ m$, $A = 100\ m^2$.

$$I = \dfrac{(35 - 20)}{100} \times 0.3 \times 100 = 0.15 \times 30 = 4.5\ m^3 = 4500\ Litres$$

**Interpretation:** The pump must deliver **4500 L** of water to bring the top 30 cm of this 100 m² field to field capacity.

## 3.3 Derivation: Water Savings Percentage (Comparative Analysis)

To quantify the benefit of Smart Irrigation over traditional flood irrigation:

$$Water\ Saved\ (\%) = \dfrac{W_{trad} - W_{smart}}{W_{trad}} \times 100$$

**Numerical example.** A traditional flood system uses $W_{trad} = 10,000\ L$/day. A smart system uses $W_{smart} = 4,500\ L$/day.

$$Water\ Saved = \dfrac{10000 - 4500}{10000} \times 100 = \dfrac{5500}{10000} \times 100 = 55\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensors Used

| Sensor | Parameter | Output Type | Range | Accuracy | KTU Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitive Soil Moisture v1.2 | Soil moisture | Analog (0–3 V) | 0–100% VWC | $\pm 3\%$ | Corrosion-resistant (preferred) |
| Resistive Soil Moisture | Soil moisture | Analog | 0–100% | $\pm 5\%$ | Corrodes fast, short life |
| DHT11 | Temp + Humidity | Digital (single-wire) | 0–50 °C, 20–90% RH | $\pm 2\ ^\circ C$, $\pm 5\%$ RH | Low-cost, common |
| DHT22 (AM2302) | Temp + Humidity | Digital | -40 to 80 °C, 0–100% RH | $\pm 0.5\ ^\circ C$, $\pm 2\%$ RH | Higher accuracy |
| FC-37 Rain Sensor | Rainfall presence | Digital + Analog | 0 / 1 (binary) | — | Surface conduction |
| YF-S201 Flow Sensor | Water flow | Pulse (Hall effect) | 1–30 L/min | $\pm 5\%$ | 450 pulses/L constant |
| DS18B20 | Soil temperature | Digital (1-Wire) | -55 to 125 °C | $\pm 0.5\ ^\circ C$ | Waterproof probe |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End System Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Rain Drop Sensor FC37] --> M1[ESP32 Microcontroller Edge Node]
    B[Capacitive Soil Moisture v1.2] --> M1
    C[DHT11 Temp and Humidity] --> M1
    D[DS18B20 Soil Temp] --> M1
    E[YF-S201 Water Flow Sensor] --> M1
    M1 --> F{Hysteresis Decision Engine}
    F -->|Pump ON when VWC less than 30 percent| G[Relay Module SPDT 5V]
    F -->|Pump OFF when VWC greater than 60 percent| H[Solenoid Valve 12V]
    G --> I[Submersible Pump 12V DC]
    H --> J[Drip Irrigation Lines]
    I --> J
    M1 -->|MQTT Publish over Wi-Fi| K[Cloud Broker ThingSpeak]
    M1 -->|HTTP REST API| L[Firebase Database]
    K --> M[Farmer Mobile App Blynk]
    L --> N[Web Dashboard Analytics]
    M --> O[Push Notification and Manual Override]
    N --> O
    O -->|Command| M1
```

## 4.2 Sensor-to-Actuation Signal Flow (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S1 as Soil Moisture Sensor
    participant S2 as DHT11
    participant S3 as Rain Sensor
    participant MCU as ESP32 Edge Node
    participant RY as Relay Module
    participant PM as Pump Motor
    participant CL as Cloud MQTT Broker
    participant APP as Mobile App

    loop Every 30 seconds
        S1->>MCU: Analog voltage value
        S2->>MCU: Digital temperature and humidity
        S3->>MCU: Digital rain flag HIGH or LOW
        MCU->>MCU: Compute VWC percent
        MCU->>MCU: Apply hysteresis rule
        alt VWC less than 30 percent and Rain LOW
            MCU->>RY: GPIO HIGH
            RY->>PM: Energize coil
            PM-->>MCU: Flow pulses from YF-S201
        else VWC greater than 60 percent or Rain HIGH
            MCU->>RY: GPIO LOW
            RY->>PM: De-energize coil
        end
        MCU->>CL: Publish JSON telemetry
        CL->>APP: Update dashboard
    end
    APP->>MCU: Optional manual override command
```

## 4.3 MQTT Topic Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Root[Farm IoT Network] --> T1[Topic farm1 zoneA moisture]
    Root --> T2[Topic farm1 zoneA temperature]
    Root --> T3[Topic farm1 zoneA humidity]
    Root --> T4[Topic farm1 zoneA rainstatus]
    Root --> T5[Topic farm1 zoneA pumpstatus]
    Root --> T6[Topic farm1 zoneA flowrate]
    Root --> T7[Topic farm1 zoneA command]
    T7 --> C1[ON]
    T7 --> C2[OFF]
    T7 --> C3[AUTO]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Draw the architecture of a Smart Irrigation System"*, always label the **five layers** (Perception, Network, Edge, Application, Actuation) explicitly on the diagram. Marks are awarded for layer identification, not just boxes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define a Smart Irrigation System. List any four sensors used in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Smart Irrigation System is an IoT-based automated watering system that uses real-time soil and environmental data to control water pumps/valves without manual intervention. *(1 mark)*

Four sensors used: *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. Capacitive soil moisture sensor — measures VWC.
2. DHT11/DHT22 — temperature and humidity.
3. Rain drop sensor (FC-37) — detects rainfall.
4. Water flow sensor (YF-S201) — measures water delivered.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is hysteresis control in irrigation, and why is it preferred over single-threshold control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hysteresis control uses **two thresholds** — a lower one ($\theta_{OFF}$, e.g., 30% VWC) to turn the pump ON, and a higher one ($\theta_{ON}$, e.g., 60% VWC) to turn it OFF. *(1.5 marks)*

It is preferred because:
- A single threshold causes the relay to oscillate ON/OFF rapidly when moisture hovers near the setpoint, due to **ADC noise** (typically $\pm 2\%$). *(1 mark)*
- This rapid switching reduces relay lifespan (rated ~100,000 cycles) and causes pump wear. Hysteresis creates a **dead-band** that prevents chattering. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the layered architecture of an IoT-based Smart Irrigation System. Describe the function of each layer with suitable components.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A Smart Irrigation System is a 5-layer IoT application as per the ITU-T Y.2060 reference model.

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer (1.5 marks):**
- Consists of physical sensors that sense field conditions.
- **Capacitive soil moisture sensor** → soil VWC.
- **DHT11** → ambient temperature and humidity.
- **Rain sensor (FC-37)** → detects precipitation.
- **DS18B20** → soil temperature.
- **YF-S201** → water flow measurement.

**Layer 2 — Network/Communication Layer (1.5 marks):**
- Wireless protocols: **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11n)** for local gateway; **LoRaWAN** for long-range farms; **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** for meshed sensor clusters; **GSM/NB-IoT** for cellular reach.
- Application protocols: **MQTT** (lightweight pub/sub on TCP port 1883), **HTTP/REST**, **CoAP**.

**Layer 3 — Edge/Processing Layer (1.5 marks):**
- Microcontroller (**ESP32 dual-core, 240 MHz, 520 KB SRAM**) runs the hysteresis algorithm.
- Reads sensors, computes VWC, decides ON/OFF, publishes to cloud.
- Supports OTA firmware updates.

**Layer 4 — Application/Cloud Layer (1 mark):**
- **ThingSpeak / AWS IoT Core / Firebase** stores time-series data.
- **Blynk / custom Android app** displays graphs and sends push notifications.
- Triggers SMS/email alerts via **Twilio** if pump runs >30 min continuously.

**Layer 5 — Actuation Layer (0.5 marks):**
- **5V SPDT Relay** isolates 230V AC pump.
- **Solenoid valve** + **submersible pump** deliver water to drip lines.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Layered design enables **scalability, modularity, and fault isolation** — failure of one sensor does not crash the system.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] A farmer has a field of 500 m² with effective root depth 0.4 m. The current soil moisture is 18% VWC and field capacity is 32% VWC. (i) Calculate the volume of water needed to refill the soil to field capacity. (ii) If the pump delivers 20 L/min, how long must it run? (iii) Compare water saved against a traditional flood system using 12,000 L.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Volume of water required (3 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$I = \dfrac{(\theta_{FC} - \theta_{current})}{100} \times d \times A$$

$$I = \dfrac{(32 - 18)}{100} \times 0.4 \times 500$$

$$I = \dfrac{14}{100} \times 200 = 0.14 \times 200 = 28\ m^3$$

Converting: $28\ m^3 = 28{,}000\ Litres$ *(0.5 mark for unit conversion)*

**(ii) Pump runtime (2 marks):**

$$t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{28{,}000\ L}{20\ L/min} = 1400\ min = \dfrac{1400}{60} \approx 23.33\ hours$$

**(iii) Water saved vs traditional (2 marks):**

$$Water\ Saved = \dfrac{W_{trad} - W_{smart}}{W_{trad}} \times 100 = \dfrac{12000 - 28000 \ ?}{\text{re-check}}$$

> *Correction — re-evaluate using same units. Traditional = 12,000 L. Smart = 28,000 L. Since smart uses more in this scenario (dry soil needs heavy refill), the result would be negative — this is expected for the first refill cycle. The savings occur in **subsequent cycles** where smart applies only the deficit, not flooding.*

For steady-state weekly comparison: assume smart applies 28,000 L once per week, traditional applies 12,000 L **per day** (flooding daily) = 84,000 L/week.

$$Water\ Saved = \dfrac{84000 - 28000}{84000} \times 100 = \dfrac{56000}{84000} \times 100 \approx 66.67\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert m³ to litres. **1 m³ = 1000 L** is a common 1-mark deduction point. Also, sign convention on the "savings" formula — write it out fully with units; do not leave Q (flow) without a numerical check.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

#### (a) [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an IoT-based Smart Irrigation System using ESP32 and MQTT protocol. Discuss the role of the cloud platform.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** Sensors → ESP32 ADC → Algorithm → Relay → Pump. (Refer to Section 4 Mermaid diagram.)

**Working — Step by Step (4 marks):**

1. **Initialization:** ESP32 connects to Wi-Fi AP and the MQTT broker (e.g., `broker.hivemq.com` on port 1883).
2. **Sensor Reading:** Every 30 s, the ESP32 reads:
   - Capacitive soil moisture via **ADC1_CH0 (GPIO36)**.
   - DHT11 via **GPIO4** (single-wire digital).
   - Rain sensor via **GPIO5** (digital).
3. **Data Conversion:** Analog voltage → VWC% using calibration formula:

$$VWC_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{dry} - V_{read}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100$$

4. **Decision:** Hysteresis rule — ON if VWC < 30% AND rain = 0; OFF if VWC > 60% OR rain = 1.
5. **Actuation:** ESP32 sets **GPIO26 HIGH** → relay energizes → pump runs.
6. **Publish:** ESP32 publishes JSON to MQTT topic `farm1/zoneA/telemetry`:

```json
{"moisture": 28.5, "temp": 32, "humidity": 65, "pump": "ON", "ts": 1716301200}
```

7. **Cloud Role (1 mark):** ThingSpeak stores and visualizes the data, triggers alerts, and supports historical analytics (water usage trends, crop growth correlation).
8. **Manual Override:** Farmer sends MQTT message `{"cmd":"OFF"}` to `farm1/zoneA/command` topic.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** MQTT's lightweight pub/sub model is ideal for low-bandwidth agricultural IoT.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] Compare traditional irrigation with Smart Irrigation across any **five** parameters. Justify the role of IoT in sustainable agriculture.

**Model Answer (table format — 5 marks, 1 per row):**

| Parameter | Traditional Irrigation | Smart Irrigation |
|---|---|---|
| Water Usage | Flooding: 8000–15000 L/day | Demand-based: 3000–6000 L/day |
| Labour | Manual valve operation | Fully automated + remote |
| Energy | Pump runs on fixed schedule | Pump runs only when deficit exists |
| Crop Yield | Sub-optimal due to over/under-watering | Optimal moisture → 20–40% yield boost |
| Data Logging | None / paper-based | Time-series cloud analytics |
| Decision-making | Experience-based | Data-driven ML/AI on cloud |

**Justification — Role of IoT in Sustainable Agriculture (2 marks):**

IoT enables:
1. **Precision resource use** → minimizes water waste, conserves aquifers.
2. **Real-time decision support** → alerts farmer to anomalies (pump failure, soil salinity).
3. **Climate-smart farming** → adapts irrigation to weather forecasts via API integration (OpenWeatherMap).
4. **Scalability** → one dashboard can monitor 100+ acres.
5. **Traceability** → blockchain-integrated IoT for organic produce certification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in 14-mark answers:** Students write the table but **forget the justification paragraph**. A table alone is worth only ~5 of 7 marks. The 2-mark justification on "sustainability" is mandatory. Also, do not write only 3–4 comparison rows; the question says **five** — examiners will deduct 1 mark per missing row.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Smart Irrigation System (KTU 2024 PBITT604 — Module 14):**

- **Definition:** IoT-driven automated watering using sensor-actuator feedback loop.
- **5 Layers:** Perception → Network → Edge → Application → Actuation.
- **Key Sensors:** Capacitive soil moisture (VWC), DHT11/22, FC-37 rain, YF-S201 flow, DS18B20.
- **Microcontroller:** ESP32 (most common — Wi-Fi + BLE, dual-core).
- **Key Protocol:** **MQTT** — publish/subscribe, broker-based, port **1883** (8883 for TLS).
- **Core Algorithm:** **Hysteresis with two thresholds** (e.g., 30% ON / 60% OFF).
- **Relay Spec:** **5V SPDT**, isolates high-voltage pump from low-voltage logic.
- **Volume formula:** $I = \dfrac{(\theta_{FC} - \theta_{current})}{100} \times d \times A$.
- **Flow formula:** $Q = V / t$ (L/min).
- **Savings formula:** $Water\ Saved\ (\%) = \dfrac{W_{trad} - W_{smart}}{W_{trad}} \times 100$.
- **Threshold Units:** Always **% VWC** (Volumetric Water Content), not "% moisture" loosely.
- **Calibration:** $VWC_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{dry} - V_{read}}{V_{dry} - V_{wet}} \times 100$.
- **Cloud Platforms:** ThingSpeak, AWS IoT Core, Blynk, Firebase, Ubidots.
- **Power:** Solar panel + 18650 Li-ion battery for off-grid deployments.
- **Security:** Use **TLS** on MQTT and **device certificates** in AWS IoT to prevent spoofed pump commands.
- **Scalability:** Use **LoRaWAN gateways** for farms >2 km from Wi-Fi.
- **Kerala-specific relevance:** Smart irrigation pilots in **Kuttanad** (paddy below sea level) and **Wayanad** (pepper/ cardamom estates).
- **Exam weightage:** Typically **14 marks** (full Part-B question) every semester — high priority.
- **Numericals to practice:** Volume of water, pump runtime, water saved %, VWC calibration, power consumption.
- **Diagrams to memorize:** 5-layer architecture, MQTT topic tree, hysteresis state machine.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart City Infrastructure Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT604 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 15
> - **Topic:** Smart City Infrastructure Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart City Infrastructure Monitoring** is the systematic deployment of heterogeneous IoT-enabled sensor networks, edge computing nodes, and cloud-based analytics platforms to continuously observe, measure, and manage the critical physical, environmental, and operational assets of an urban environment — including water supply networks, power grids, transportation corridors, structural health of buildings/bridges, waste management systems, and air quality.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBITT604 – Module 15)** syllabus, this topic sits at the convergence of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, **Urban Informatics**, and **M2M (Machine-to-Machine) communication**, where real-world urban services are digitized using the **感知层–网络层–平台层–应用层 (Perception–Network–Platform–Application)** four-layer IoT reference architecture defined by the **ITU-T Y.2060** recommendation.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **CPS** | Cyber-Physical System — tight coupling of computation, networking, and physical processes |
| **WSN** | Wireless Sensor Network — spatially distributed autonomous sensors |
| **SCADA** | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (legacy industrial monitoring) |
| **LPWAN** | Low-Power Wide-Area Network (LoRa, NB-IoT, Sigfox) |
| **Edge Computing** | Computation performed near the data source, reducing cloud latency |
| **Digital Twin** | Virtual replica of a physical urban asset used for simulation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The City's Nervous System":**
> Imagine a human body. Your **nerves** detect pain, temperature, and pressure; your **spinal cord** makes quick decisions (edge); your **brain** (cloud) analyses long-term patterns. A Smart City works the same way — **sensors** are the nerves, **gateways** are the spinal cord, and the **cloud platform** is the brain. When a water pipe leaks, a pressure sensor "feels pain," the gateway "decides" it's abnormal, and the cloud "diagnoses" the failure and dispatches a repair crew — all without human intervention.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are **bold-marked** as they are universally tested in KTU examinations:

- **PM2.5 / PM10** — Particulate Matter concentrations (µg/m³) measured for air quality.
- **Noise Level (dB)** — Decibel scale, permissible urban limit is **55–65 dB** during day.
- **Water Turbidity (NTU)** — Nephelometric Turbidity Units, drinking water standard is **<1 NTU**.
- **Structural Strain (µε)** — Microstrain, monitored using strain gauges on bridges/buildings.
- **LoRaWAN Spreading Factor (SF7–SF12)** — Trade-off between range and data rate.
- **NB-IoT Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL) = 164 dB** — defined by 3GPP Release 13.

---

## 1.5 Four-Layer IoT Reference Architecture (ITU-T Y.2060)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every Smart City infrastructure monitoring system in KTU questions is mapped onto this **four-layer architecture**:
> 1. **Perception Layer (Device Layer)** — Physical sensors and actuators.
> 2. **Network Layer (Transmission Layer)** — Communication (LoRa, NB-IoT, Wi-Fi, 5G).
> 3. **Platform Layer (Middleware / Service Layer)** — Data ingestion, storage, analytics.
> 4. **Application Layer** — End-user dashboards, alerts, control actions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — "How a Smart City Sensor Node Works"

The end-to-end pipeline of a smart city monitoring node is logically broken into **seven sequential stages**:

- **Step 1 — Physical Sensing:** Transducers (electrochemical, MEMS, piezoelectric) convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.
- **Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:** Analog front-end amplifies and filters (e.g., low-pass at 10 Hz) the raw signal to remove noise.
- **Step 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Typically **12-bit / 16-bit** resolution; sampling rate governed by Nyquist theorem.
- **Step 4 — Local Processing:** An MCU (ESP32, STM32, Arduino) processes the digital sample — calibration, thresholding, edge filtering.
- **Step 5 — Wireless Transmission:** Data is packetized and sent via LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, or Zigbee depending on range/power budget.
- **Step 6 — Gateway / Edge Aggregation:** A gateway (e.g., Raspberry Pi, Kerlink) aggregates many nodes and forwards to the cloud over MQTT/HTTPS.
- **Step 7 — Cloud Analytics & Visualization:** Time-series DB (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) + dashboard (Grafana, Power BI) for operators.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" Insight:** The reason **LPWAN** dominates smart city deployments is because municipal infrastructure is geographically spread over **10–100 km²**, where Wi-Fi's ~100 m range and cellular 4G's high power (>1 W) are infeasible for battery-powered nodes expected to last **5–10 years**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested formulas** in KTU ESE questions on smart city infrastructure. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** — minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | Hz |
| 2 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | dB |
| 3 | $L_{fs} = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | dB |
| 4 | $E_b / N_0 = \dfrac{S/R}{N/B} = \dfrac{S}{N}\cdot\dfrac{B}{R}$ | **Bit-energy to noise spectral density** (link budget) | dB |
| 5 | $T_{air} \approx 20 + 0.6\,T_{dry}$ | **Wet-bulb approximation** (environmental monitoring) | °C |
| 6 | $P_{tx} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{tx} \cdot t_{tx}$ | **Energy per transmission** | Joules |
| 7 | $T_{battery} = \dfrac{C_{mAh} \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{avg}}$ | **Node battery lifetime** | hours |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{SF \cdot \dfrac{4}{5}}{2^{SF} / BW}$ | **LoRaWAN data rate (bit/s)** approximation | bps |
| 9 | $Q = C_{d}\,A\,\sqrt{2gH}$ | **Pipe flow (Torricelli-based discharge)** for water monitoring | m³/s |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A\,E}$ | **Hooke's Law (Strain)** for structural health monitoring | dimensionless (µε) |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, **never use the pipe symbol `|` inside cells** as it breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` for absolute value, e.g., $SNR = \vert V_{signal} \vert / \vert V_{noise} \vert$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Utilities (e.g., Kerala Water Authority):** Deploy LoRaWAN ultrasonic flow sensors in underground pits to detect **Non-Revenue Water (NRW)** and pipe bursts.
- **Smart Grids (KSERC-regulated):** Smart meters on every transformer use NB-IoT for **Automated Meter Reading (AMR)**.
- **Bridge Health (e.g., Kochi Metro viaducts):** Vibrating wire strain gauges + accelerometers feed **Modal Analysis** to detect structural fatigue.
- **Air Quality (Smart City Mission — Kochi, Bengaluru):** PM2.5 + NO₂ + O₃ sensor pods on streetlights.
- **Smart Parking (KSRTC bus depots):** Magnetometer + ultrasonic sensors report slot occupancy via MQTT to a central dashboard.

## 2.4 Communication Protocol Selection Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Which protocol for a given smart city use case?"* Use this decision matrix:

| Range Required | Power Budget | Recommended Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| < 100 m | Battery | BLE / Zigbee |
| 100 m – 5 km | Battery | **LoRaWAN** |
| 5 – 20 km, deep indoor | Battery | **NB-IoT** (LTE Cat-NB1) |
| Real-time, high bandwidth | Mains powered | **Wi-Fi / 5G** |
| Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) | Mains powered | **DSRC / C-V2X** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Link Budget for an Outdoor LoRaWAN Smart City Node

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU-Style):** A water-level sensor is deployed inside a covered underground sump in Thiruvananthapuram municipality. We need to verify whether a LoRaWAN class-A node (TX power = 14 dBm, antenna gain = 2 dBi) can communicate with a rooftop gateway 6 km away at 868 MHz.

### Given Parameters

- Transmit power: $P_{tx} = 14\ \text{dBm}$
- TX antenna gain: $G_{tx} = 2\ \text{dBi}$
- RX antenna gain: $G_{rx} = 6\ \text{dBi}$
- Frequency: $f = 868\ \text{MHz}$
- Distance: $d = 6\ \text{km}$
- Cable + connector loss: $L_{c} = 1.5\ \text{dB}$
- Required receiver sensitivity: $S_{rx} = -137\ \text{dBm}$ (for SF12, BW 125 kHz)
- Fade margin (urban clutter): $M_{fade} = 15\ \text{dB}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert frequency to a usable log form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(f) &= 20\log_{10}(868) \\
&= 20 \times 2.9385 \\
&\approx 58.77\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute distance term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(d) &= 20\log_{10}(6000) \\
&= 20 \times 3.7782 \\
&\approx 75.56\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) using the standard formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &= 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) \\
&= 32.4 + 75.56 + 20\log_{10}(868) \\
&= 32.4 + 75.56 + 58.77 \\
&\approx 166.73\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: We used $20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ since $d = 6$ km. If you prefer the metre-based formula $32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_m) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$, the result is identical (you can verify: $20\log_{10}(6000) = 75.56$).

**Step 4 — Compute Received Signal Strength (RSSI) using the link-budget equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rx} &= P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - L_{fs} - L_{c} \\
&= 14 + 2 + 6 - 166.73 - 1.5 \\
&\approx -146.23\ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with sensitivity and apply fade margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= P_{rx} - S_{rx} \\
&= -146.23 - (-137) \\
&= -9.23\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply urban fade margin (15 dB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Margin} &= -9.23 - 15 \\
&= -24.23\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The link **fails** by ~24 dB. The design must be modified — either (a) reduce SF from 12 to 9 (sacrificing range for sensitivity), (b) raise the gateway antenna, or (c) add a repeater. This is the **typical KTU analytical trap**: students forget to subtract the **fade margin** at the end.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Lifetime of a Smart Streetlight Node

**Given:**
- Battery capacity: $C = 2400\ \text{mAh}$ at $V_{nom} = 3.7\ \text{V}$ (single 18650 Li-ion cell)
- Active TX current: $I_{tx} = 90\ \text{mA}$ for $t_{tx} = 1.2\ \text{s}$
- Active RX current: $I_{rx} = 10\ \text{mA}$ for $t_{rx} = 2\ \text{s}$
- Sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 8\ \mu\text{A}$
- Reporting cycle: every $T = 15\ \text{min} = 900\ \text{s}$
- Self-discharge per month: $\eta = 2\%$

### Step 1 — Energy per Reporting Cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cycle} &= V_{nom}\,(I_{tx}\,t_{tx} + I_{rx}\,t_{rx} + I_{sleep}\,(T - t_{tx} - t_{rx})) \\
&= 3.7 \times (90 \times 1.2 + 10 \times 2 + 0.008 \times (900 - 1.2 - 2))\ \text{mC} \\
&= 3.7 \times (108 + 20 + 7.17)\ \text{mC} \\
&= 3.7 \times 135.17\ \text{mC} \\
&\approx 500.13\ \text{mC} = 0.500\ \text{As}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Charge Consumed per Cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cycle} &= \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{V_{nom}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.500}{3.7} \\
&\approx 0.135\ \text{C per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Number of Cycles per Day

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{day} &= \dfrac{24 \times 3600}{T} \\
&= \dfrac{86400}{900} \\
&= 96\ \text{cycles/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Daily Charge Consumption

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{day} &= N_{day} \times Q_{cycle} \\
&= 96 \times 0.135 \\
&\approx 12.97\ \text{C/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to mAh:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{day,mAh} &= \dfrac{Q_{day}}{3.6} \\
&\approx 3.60\ \text{mAh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Theoretical Battery Lifetime (ignoring self-discharge)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{batt} &= \dfrac{C}{Q_{day,mAh}} \\
&= \dfrac{2400}{3.60} \\
&\approx 666.7\ \text{days} \\
&\approx 1.83\ \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Adjust for Self-Discharge (η = 2 %/month ⇒ ~24 %/year)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} &= \dfrac{T_{batt}}{1 + \eta \cdot T_{batt\ years}} \\
&\approx \dfrac{1.83}{1 + 0.24 \times 1.83} \\
&\approx \dfrac{1.83}{1.439} \\
&\approx 1.27\ \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The node lasts ~**15 months**, which is **below the 5-year industrial target**. The remedy: add a small **solar harvester (0.5 W panel + 1 F super-capacitor)** so the system is energy-neutral.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LPWAN Technologies for Smart Cities

| Parameter | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | Sigfox |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spectrum | Sub-GHz ISM (868/915 MHz) | Licensed LTE in-band | Sub-GHz ISM |
| Bandwidth | 125 / 250 / 500 kHz | 180 kHz | 100 / 600 Hz (UNB) |
| Max Data Rate | 50 kbps | 200 kbps | 0.6 kbps |
| Range (urban) | 2–5 km | 4–10 km | 3–10 km |
| Max Payload | 243 B | 1600 B | 12 B (UL) |
| Battery Life | 5–10 yr | 10+ yr | 5–7 yr |
| Mobility | Limited | Yes (LTE handover) | No |
| KTU-City Use-Case | Water meters | Smart streetlights | Static bin sensors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Four-Layer Smart City IoT Reference Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["PERCEPTION LAYER - Sensor Field"]
        N1["Water Flow Sensor"]
        N2["PM25 Air Quality Sensor"]
        N3["Strain Gauge on Bridge"]
        N4["Ultrasonic Bin Level Sensor"]
        N5["Smart Energy Meter"]
    end

    subgraph A2["NETWORK LAYER - Communication"]
        N6["LoRaWAN Class A"]
        N7["NB-IoT LTE Cat NB1"]
        N8["Zigbee Mesh"]
        N9["MQTT Broker over TLS"]
    end

    subgraph A3["PLATFORM LAYER - Edge and Cloud"]
        N10["Edge Gateway - Raspberry Pi"]
        N11["Kafka Stream Ingestor"]
        N12["InfluxDB Time Series Store"]
        N13["Anomaly Detection - LSTM"]
    end

    subgraph A4["APPLICATION LAYER - User Services"]
        N14["Operator Dashboard - Grafana"]
        N15["Citizen Mobile App"]
        N16["Municipal Control Room Alert"]
        N17["Digital Twin Simulator"]
    end

    N1 --> N6
    N2 --> N6
    N3 --> N7
    N4 --> N8
    N5 --> N7
    N6 --> N9
    N7 --> N9
    N8 --> N9
    N9 --> N10
    N10 --> N11
    N11 --> N12
    N12 --> N13
    N13 --> N14
    N13 --> N15
    N13 --> N16
    N13 --> N17
```

## 4.2 End-to-End Data Flow for a Water Pipeline Burst Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Pressure Sensor in Pipe"] -->|Analog mV| S2["Signal Conditioner - Amplifier + Low-Pass"]
    S2 -->|12-bit ADC| S3["MCU - Threshold Check"]
    S3 -->|LoRa packet| S4["LoRaWAN Gateway 868 MHz"]
    S4 -->|TCP/IP| S5["MQTT Broker - Mosquitto"]
    S5 -->|Subscribe topic pipe/zone3/pressure| S6["Cloud - AWS IoT Core"]
    S6 -->|Time-series write| S7["InfluxDB"]
    S7 -->|Anomaly flag| S8["Alert Engine - PagerDuty"]
    S8 -->|SMS + Dashboard| S9["KWA Field Engineer"]
    S8 -->|Valve close command| S10["Actuator - Solenoid Valve"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Smart City Monitoring Hub

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph B1["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        B11["Multi-Gas Sensor"]
        B12["MEMS Accelerometer"]
        B13["Acoustic Emission Sensor"]
        B14["GPS Module"]
    end

    subgraph B2["PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM"]
        B21["ARM Cortex M4 MCU"]
        B22["Real-Time OS - FreeRTOS"]
        B23["Crypto Accelerator - AES 256"]
    end

    subgraph B3["POWER SUBSYSTEM"]
        B31["Solar Panel 2W"]
        B32["Li-Po Battery 6600 mAh"]
        B33["Power Management IC - BQ25570"]
    end

    subgraph B4["COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        B41["LoRa Modem SX1276"]
        B42["NB-IoT Modem BC95"]
        B43["BLE for Local Config"]
    end

    B11 --> B21
    B12 --> B21
    B13 --> B21
    B14 --> B21
    B21 --> B23
    B21 --> B41
    B21 --> B42
    B21 --> B43
    B31 --> B33
    B33 --> B32
    B33 --> B21
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any three real-time infrastructure assets monitored in a Smart City using IoT, and name the sensor used for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Water pipelines** → Ultrasonic flow sensor / pressure transducer (1 mark)
2. **Air quality** → Electrochemical NO₂ sensor / laser-scattering PM2.5 sensor (1 mark)
3. **Structural health of bridges** → Vibrating-wire strain gauge / MEMS accelerometer (1 mark)

*(Alternative correct examples: smart streetlight → LDR + PIR; smart parking → ultrasonic HC-SR04; waste bin → capacitive level sensor.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between LoRaWAN and NB-IoT in the context of smart city deployments (any 3 points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Spectrum:** LoRaWAN uses unlicensed ISM sub-GHz bands; NB-IoT uses licensed LTE in-band spectrum (1 mark).
2. **Data rate:** LoRaWAN offers 0.3–50 kbps; NB-IoT up to 200 kbps (1 mark).
3. **Mobility / Handover:** NB-IoT supports seamless LTE handover; LoRaWAN does not support seamless mobility (1 mark).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-layer ITU-T Y.2060 IoT reference architecture** as applied to a Smart City water distribution monitoring system. Label **all sensors, gateways, protocols, and cloud services** used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LoRaWAN node transmits 20 bytes every 10 minutes at SF9, BW = 125 kHz. Compute the **average current draw** and **battery lifetime**, given $I_{tx} = 90\ \text{mA}$, $t_{tx} = 0.9\ \text{s}$, $I_{sleep} = 8\ \mu\text{A}$, battery = 2400 mAh at 3.7 V. Neglect self-discharge. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks

| Component | Marks Awarded | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Block diagram with 4 labelled layers | 2 | One mark for each correctly labelled layer (Perception/Network/Platform/Application) |
| Sensors named correctly | 1 | e.g., Electromagnetic flow meter, pressure transducer |
| Communication protocols specified | 1 | e.g., LoRaWAN on 868 MHz + MQTT over TLS |
| Cloud platform named | 1 | e.g., AWS IoT Core, InfluxDB, Grafana |
| Application services listed | 1 | Citizen app, dashboard, alert engine |
| Logical flow arrows drawn | 1 | Complete end-to-end data path |

**Detailed Explanation (for full 7 marks):**
1. **Perception Layer:** Electromagnetic flow meters (sizes DN50–DN300) installed in zonal District Metered Areas (DMAs) of KWA; pressure transducers at pump stations; water-quality probes (pH, chlorine, turbidity).
2. **Network Layer:** Sensors → LoRaWAN Class-A modems (Semtech SX1276) → rooftop gateways → backhaul over 4G/fibre → MQTT broker.
3. **Platform Layer:** Mosquitto broker, InfluxDB for time-series, Apache Kafka for stream processing, anomaly-detection ML model (Isolation Forest).
4. **Application Layer:** React.js dashboard for KWA operators, SMS alerts to citizens during shut-downs, mobile app for leak-reporting.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify time on per cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 10\ \text{min} = 600\ \text{s} \\
t_{tx} &= 0.9\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Charge per cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cycle} &= I_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + I_{sleep} \cdot (T_{cycle} - t_{tx}) \\
&= 90 \times 0.9 + 0.008 \times 599.1\ \text{mC} \\
&= 81 + 4.79\ \text{mC} \\
&= 85.79\ \text{mC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mAh:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cycle,mAh} &= \dfrac{85.79}{3600}\ \text{mAh} \\
&\approx 0.0238\ \text{mAh per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cycles per day:**

$$
N_{day} = \dfrac{24 \times 60}{10} = 144\ \text{cycles/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily consumption:**

$$
Q_{day} = 144 \times 0.0238 \approx 3.43\ \text{mAh/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Battery lifetime:**

$$
T_{batt} = \dfrac{2400}{3.43} \approx 699.7\ \text{days} \approx 1.92\ \text{years}
$$

**Conclusion:** With 144 transmissions per day, the node lasts ~**1.9 years**, falling short of the 5-year industrial target — students must recommend energy harvesting or duty-cycle reduction as the design improvement. *(1 mark for conclusion + valid recommendation.)*

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **architecture and working of a Smart Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) system** for a long-span bridge using IoT. Include the type of sensors, data acquisition, and the role of **digital twins** in predictive maintenance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** and received signal strength for a smart-parking gateway operating at **2.4 GHz** placed **500 m** from a sensor node. Given: $P_{tx} = 10\ \text{dBm}$, $G_{tx} = G_{rx} = 2\ \text{dBi}$, $S_{rx} = -95\ \text{dBm}$, cable loss = 1 dB. Comment on link feasibility. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Sensor subsystem (2 marks):**
- **Strain gauges (fibre Bragg gratings):** Mounted on bridge girders to measure microstrain (µε).
- **MEMS 3-axis accelerometers (e.g., ADXL345):** Capture vibration signatures for modal analysis.
- **Tiltmeters / Inclinometers:** Detect pylon rotation.
- **Temperature + humidity probes:** Compensate for thermal expansion.

**Data acquisition (2 marks):**
- Synchronous sampling at 100–200 Hz on each pier.
- Time-synchronization via **IEEE 1588 PTP** or GPS PPS.
- Local buffering on industrial gateways (e.g., Advantech ADAM-3600) with edge filtering.

**Digital twin role (3 marks):**
- A digital twin is a **virtual replica** of the bridge built in a software platform (e.g., ANSYS Twin Builder, Azure Digital Twins).
- It receives **real-time telemetry** from the SHM sensors and runs **Finite Element Model (FEM)** updates.
- The twin predicts **remaining useful life (RUL)**, **fatigue cycles**, and **load capacity** — enabling predictive maintenance before visible damage occurs.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute FSPL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &= 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) \\
&= 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(0.5) + 20\log_{10}(2400) \\
&= 32.4 + (-6.02) + 67.60 \\
&\approx 93.98\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Received signal strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rx} &= P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - L_{fs} - L_{c} \\
&= 10 + 2 + 2 - 93.98 - 1 \\
&\approx -80.98\ \text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Link margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Margin} &= P_{rx} - S_{rx} \\
&= -80.98 - (-95) \\
&= 14.02\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply urban fade margin (≈10 dB):**

$$
\text{Net margin} = 14.02 - 10 \approx 4.02\ \text{dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** The link is **marginally feasible** (positive 4 dB). It will work in open sight but is **fragile under heavy rain or vehicular obstruction**. Recommendation: use **directional patch antenna** with $G_{rx} = 8\ \text{dBi}$ to gain 6 dB additional margin. *(1 mark for valid conclusion + 1 mark for design improvement.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the FSPL equation ($d$ in **km** vs **m**). One slip ⇒ zero marks for the entire numerical.
> 2. **Subtracting fade margin at the wrong stage** — always subtract it *after* the receiver comparison.
> 3. **Confusing SF (Spreading Factor) with Sensitivity** — in LoRaWAN, higher SF ⇒ higher sensitivity but lower data rate. Examiners explicitly test this trade-off.
> 4. **Writing `|` (pipe) inside LaTeX tables** — will break the markdown table layout. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 5. **Skipping self-discharge adjustment** in battery-life numericals. KTU key includes a -0.5 mark penalty for this.

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Smart City Infrastructure Monitoring**

- **Architecture:** Always map the system onto **ITU-T Y.2060 four layers** (Perception, Network, Platform, Application).
- **Sensors (must memorize):** Ultrasonic (distance), MEMS accelerometer (vibration), FBG strain gauge (µε), electrochemical (gas), electromagnetic (flow), LDR (light), PIR (motion).
- **Protocols & Range:** BLE < Zigbee < Wi-Fi < LoRa < NB-IoT < 5G. Match with use-case range.
- **LoRaWAN trade-off:** SF7 → SF12 ⇒ range ↑, data rate ↓, time-on-air ↑, battery ↓.
- **FSPL formula:** $L_{fs} = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ where d is in **km** and f in **MHz**.
- **Battery lifetime:** $T_{batt} = C_{mAh} / Q_{day,mAh}$; **always adjust for self-discharge**.
- **Digital Twin:** Real-time virtual replica used for **predictive maintenance**, not just visualization.
- **MQTT vs CoAP:** MQTT = TCP, pub/sub, ideal for cloud; CoAP = UDP, request/response, ideal for constrained nodes.
- **Security:** Use **TLS 1.3** + **AES-256** + **device certificates (X.509)** in production deployments.
- **KTU 2024 buzzwords:** "Cyber-Physical System", "Edge Analytics", "Green IoT", "Energy Neutral Operation", "5G URLLC".
- **Standards to remember:** **ITU-T Y.2060** (IoT reference model), **IEEE 1451** (smart transducer interface), **3GPP Release 13/17** (NB-IoT), **LoRaWAN 1.0.4** (MAC spec).
- **Indian context:** Smart Cities Mission (100 cities), KSERC smart-grid regulations, KWA NRW reduction targets, BBNL BharatNet for backhaul.
- **Common numerical traps:** Unit mismatches, ignoring fade margin, omitting self-discharge, sign errors in dB subtraction.
